# Mage: The Ascension — Storypath Ultra Edition

> **AI/Agent Reference File** — generated from source JS modules
> Markdown spec for LLM/agent ingestion. All rules, examples, and tables are present.
> Structure: `#` = Chapter/Appendix &nbsp; `##` = Section &nbsp; `###` = Subsection

---

# Chapter One: Introduction

*A Complete Conversion Rulebook*

Reality is a lie agreed upon by the masses, and a few souls — called mages — know the truth. They Awaken to the fact that everything, from the trajectory of a bullet to the formation of a storm to the slow creep of entropy across a dying star, can be bent, redirected, and rewritten by a sufficiently enlightened Will backed by sufficient understanding. These are not stage magicians or comic-book sorcerers. They are philosophers made dangerous, scientists who work in a lab bounded only by imagination, artists whose medium is the fabric of existence itself.

This book places those mages into the Storypath Ultra (SPU) system — a sleek, momentum-driven engine that rewards dramatic action, clever thinking, and collaborative storytelling. If you are already familiar with Storypath Ultra, this book teaches you how to layer the rich cosmology of Mage: The Ascension on top of that engine. If you are new to SPU, the first chapters of this book walk you through every mechanical concept you need.

## What This Book Contains

This book is divided into nine core chapters followed by a full suite of appendices and a standalone Index.

Chapter One: Introduction — You are reading it. Background on the setting, the design philosophy, and how to use this book.

Chapter Two: Core Mechanics — The complete Storypath Ultra dice engine adapted for Mage: attributes, skills, the dice pool, hits, complications, tags, and the Botch rule. Returning M20 players: a sidebar explains how Arete maps to the new system.

Chapter Three: Character Creation — Step-by-step character building, including Tradition selection, Sphere allocation, Backgrounds, Merits, starting Quintessence, and the Resonance trait.

Chapter Four: Magic — The Tapestry of Reality — The full casting system: Sphere Skill + Casting Attribute pools, Spell Factors, freeform magic, Rotes, Coincidental vs. Vulgar effects, Quintessence spending rules, and the Spell Parameter Scaling table.

Chapter Five: Paradox — The Price of Power — How Paradox accumulates, the Paradox dice pool, Backlash, Quiet, Paradox Spirits, and the creative dice-based backlash system that makes every Vulgar effect feel genuinely dangerous.

Chapter Six: The Nine Spheres — Detailed rules for all nine Spheres (Correspondence, Entropy, Forces, Life, Matter, Mind, Prime, Spirit, Time), each with dot-level breakdowns, Sphere Tricks, and five fully worked casting examples from dot-1 through dot-5.

Chapter Seven: Combat — Violence in the World of Mage — The complete combat system: initiative, the Phase system, attack and defense rolls, damage and the Health track, armor, healing, and the full intersection of spellcasting and violence. Multiple worked examples including a three-round encounter.

Chapter Eight: Factions — The Ascension War — The Nine Traditions, the Technocratic Union, the Nephandi, the Marauders, and the Disparate Crafts. Faction histories, internal politics, the Ascension War, and mechanical support for faction-based play.

Chapter Nine: Storyguide — Advice on running Mage chronicles: building scenes, handling Quiet, using Paradox as a dramatic tool, managing the Consensus, and the unique challenges of running a game where the players' characters can rewrite reality.

Appendix A: Avatars — Optional, modular rules for the inner spiritual being that drives a mage's Awakening. Seeking rules, Avatar ratings, and the Avatar's mechanical relationship to Quintessence and Paradox.

Appendix B: Paradigm, Practice & Instruments — Optional rules for the flavor layer of magic: how a mage's worldview shapes what their spells look and feel like, and how Instruments (foci) interact with the casting system.

Appendix C: Avatar Essences — The Shape of the Soul — Detailed profiles for the four primary Essences (Dynamic, Pattern, Primordial, Questing) and their rare variants, with full Invoke/Compel mechanics and Seeking examples.

Appendix D: Entities and Antagonists — Full stat blocks and narrative profiles for spirits, Umbrood, Paradox entities, Tradition rivals, Technocratic operatives, and the most dangerous supernatural threats a Mage chronicle is likely to produce.

Appendix E: Chantries — Sanctums of the Awakened — Rules for Chantry creation and management: location, defenses, Nodes, Resonance, factional politics, and the mechanical benefits a well-run Chantry provides to its cabal.

Appendix F: The Nine Traditions — Deep Lore — Extended history, internal politics, signature magical approaches, common Rotes, and mechanical detail for all nine Traditions.

Appendix G: The Technocratic Union — Deep Operations — The Union from the inside: the five Conventions, their paradigms, their Constructs, their relationship to the Consensus, and the moral complexity that makes the best Technocracy stories genuinely ambiguous.

Appendix H: The Phase Initiative System — The full Phase-and-Action-Dice combat timing engine, including Movement in Combat and the Range Band system (Close/Short/Medium/Long/Extreme) used for both physical positioning and spell targeting, with detailed examples of how spellcasting, Rotes, and mundane violence interact across a multi-participant round.

Appendix I: The Armor System — Complete rules for armor as a mechanical system: Soak values, Sturdy ratings, the Condition Track, armor construction, and fully worked armor examples from street leather to combat exoskeleton.

Appendix J: Tags Reference — A comprehensive reference for all Tags used in the system: Combat Action Tags, Weapon Tags, Armor Tags, Character Condition Tags, Environmental Tags, Magical Tags, and Phase System Tags, with Invoke and Compel examples for each.

Appendix K: Sanity — The Fraying Edge of the Awakened Mind — Optional rules for psychological damage: the ten-point Sanity track, Status Tags, Paradox Insulation, and recovery procedures.

Appendix L: Bringing It All Together — A complete worked example following Sasha through a three-round combat encounter against Technocracy operatives, with every mechanical decision — casting, damage, Paradox, Botch — shown in real time.

Appendix M: The Cosmos — The Tellurian and Its Otherworlds — A comprehensive cosmological reference: the Physical World, the Penumbra, the Gauntlet, the Near Umbra, the Horizon Realms, the Deep Umbra, and the entities that inhabit every layer.

Appendix N: Master Rotes — Recorded Workings of the Awakened — The full Rotes library organized by Sphere, including Technocracy Procedures, with SF values, casting pools, and effect descriptions for each.

Appendix O: Quick Reference — One-Page Cheat Sheets — Five printable sheets covering core dice rules, magic and casting, Paradox, the Coincidental vs. Vulgar decision tree, and combat. Keep these at the table.

Appendix P: Character Sheet — A complete, fillable character sheet formatted for Mage: The Ascension — Storypath Ultra Edition.

Appendix Q: Quick Reference Card — A condensed single-card summary of the most frequently referenced rules.

Appendix R: Items and Gear — Rules and stat blocks for mundane weapons, armor, equipment, and Wonders: Periapts, Talismans, Devices, Fetishes, and Legendary Items.

Appendix S: Crafting — Rules for creating mundane items with Enlightened techniques and forging Wonders through magical crafting: Nodes, Fetish Binding, and the full Master Work system.

Appendix T: Vehicles — Vehicle stat blocks, chase and pursuit rules, vehicle combat, and Mage-modified vehicle options.

Appendix U: The Nephandi — Descent, the Caul, and the Fallen — The three Paths of Falling, the Descent Track, Nephandic Paradigms and Practices, key Fallen Rotes, and the mechanical and narrative tools for running Nephandi as antagonists or player characters.

Appendix V: Merits, Flaws, and Wonders — The complete Merit and Flaw catalogue (Physical, Mental, Social, Supernatural) plus Selected Wonders, Wonder Crafting reference tables, and the full Backgrounds list.

Appendix W: Streamlining Combat — Speed Tiers and Group Rules for Large Fights — Optional tools for running fights with large numbers of non-named NPCs without a large bookkeeping count: the Speed Tier initiative system (a small set of colored dice replaces individual Initiative Pools for mooks, scaled to the fastest thing in the fight rather than headcount) and formal Group Rules for resolving mob attacks as a single Volley. For positioning and movement, see Appendix H.

Index — A full alphabetical index of game terms, subsystems, rules concepts, and setting elements, with live hyperlinks to each relevant section.

## A Note on Sources

This conversion draws on Mage: The Ascension 20th Anniversary Edition (M20) for setting, sphere definitions, factions, and Paradox flavor, and on Storypath Ultra Core Manual for all mechanical frameworks. Where the two systems conflict, Storypath Ultra rules take precedence. Where M20 offers richer narrative detail (Quiet stages, Paradox spirits, Marauder psychology), that detail has been adapted and preserved.

## How to Read This Book

Each major mechanical concept is explained first in plain language, then given a formal rule block, and then illustrated with at least one worked example — and frequently two or three. Combat examples are especially numerous because the intersection of magic and violence is where systems most often become confusing. Read the examples. They are not optional flavor; they are the rules made visible.

When a rule has a shortcut (a quick way to play that ignores nuance), that shortcut is labeled "Quick Play." The full rule immediately follows. You do not need to use every option in this book at every table. Pick the depth that serves your story.

```
Design Philosophy
-----------------
Storypath Ultra rewards bold choices. Mage rewards deep belief. Together, they create a game where a character's worldview — their Paradigm — is not just backstory but a mechanical resource that changes what the dice do.

This rulebook assumes that magic is personal. Two mages can achieve the same effect through entirely different means, and both are right. The rules exist to adjudicate conflict, not to police imagination.

Complexity is modular. A table that loves crunchy subsystems can activate Sphere Tricks, Paradox Backlash Tables, and the full Avatar rules. A table that prefers narrative speed can use Freeform Magic, a simple Paradox track, and skip Appendix A entirely. Both tables are playing the same game.
```

# Chapter Two: Core Mechanics — Storypath Ultra

Storypath Ultra is a dice-pool game. Whenever a character attempts something risky or interesting, you gather a handful of ten-sided dice (d10s), roll them, and count how many show 8 or higher. Each such die is a hit. Hits are currency: they tell you how well you succeeded, what bonus effects you can buy, and whether you had enough power to accomplish something difficult.

This chapter explains every core concept you need to play. It is dense but complete. You can return to any section as a reference.

## Dice Pools

A dice pool is assembled from two sources: one Skill and one Attribute. Skills describe what your character does; Attributes describe how naturally gifted they are at categories of action. Together they form the pool.

The Storyguide (the person running the game) decides which Skill and Attribute are relevant to a given action. Some combinations come up constantly and are listed in Chapter Three. Others are called on the fly. A player can always suggest a pool; the Storyguide makes the final call, but should be generous when a suggestion makes narrative sense.

### Skills

Every character has ratings from 0 to 5 in each of the following sixteen Skills. A rating of 0 means no formal training — you can still try, but you roll fewer dice. A rating of 5 represents world-class mastery.

| Skill | Typical Uses in Mage |
| --- | --- |
| Artistry | Creating or appreciating art, music, writing; certain creative magic expressions |
| Athletics | Running, climbing, fighting unarmed, feats of raw physicality; initiative |
| Close Combat | Fighting with melee weapons or in brawl; magical melee |
| Culture | Knowledge of history, customs, languages, social norms; Tradition lore |
| Empathy | Reading emotions, sensing lies, social intuition; some Mind Sphere effects |
| Enigmas | Solving puzzles, riddles, and metaphysical problems; understanding paradoxes |
| Esoterica | Occult knowledge, ritual lore, understanding magical symbols and traditions |
| Larceny | Stealing, lock-picking, sleight of hand, stealth, deception through action |
| Leadership | Inspiring groups, commanding respect, rallying allies; some social magic |
| Medicine | Healing, diagnosing illness, forensics; Life Sphere medical effects |
| Persuasion | Convincing others through argument, charm, or negotiation |
| Pilot | Operating vehicles, spacecraft, Technocratic constructs |
| Ranged Combat | Firearms, throwing, archery; magical ranged attacks |
| Science | Physics, chemistry, biology, mathematics; Technocratic paradigm |
| Survival | Wilderness navigation, foraging, tracking, enduring the elements |
| Technology | Electronics, computers, mechanics, Technocratic Wonders |

### Attributes

Attributes are grouped into three categories of three. Each Attribute is rated 1–5 (no character has a 0 in an Attribute; even a newborn has a 1).

**Mental Attributes**

- Intellect — Raw reasoning power, memory, academic acuity. Used for analysis, research, and most Science-based pools.
- Cunning — Lateral thinking, pattern recognition, improvisation under pressure. Used for deception, quick plans, and traps.
- Resolve — Mental endurance, willpower, resisting mental intrusion. Used to maintain concentration, push through fatigue, and resist Mind magic.

**Physical Attributes**

- Might — Raw physical strength. Used for lifting, breaking, and dealing extra damage in close combat.
- Dexterity — Speed, coordination, fine motor control. Used for ranged attacks, piloting, and dodging.
- Stamina — Toughness and endurance. Used for soaking damage, resisting poison and disease, and long physical exertion.

**Social Attributes**

- Presence — Commanding attention; personal magnetism. Used for first impressions, intimidation, and leadership.
- Manipulation — Subtle influence, misdirection, reading and exploiting others' desires.
- Composure — Emotional control, grace under pressure, hiding feelings. Used to resist social attacks and maintain a facade.

## Building a Pool

When you want to do something, you and the Storyguide agree on a Skill and an Attribute. You add the two ratings together; that is your pool size. Roll that many d10s.

> Example: Isabeau (Cultist of Ecstasy) wants to read the emotional state of a nervous bureaucrat.
> The Storyguide calls for Empathy + Cunning.
> Isabeau has Empathy 3, Cunning 2. Pool = 5 dice.
> She rolls: 3, 7, 8, 9, 1. Three dice show 8 or higher — three hits.

## Hits and What They Mean

Roll your dice pool. Count every die showing 8, 9, or 10. Each is one hit. Each 10 counts as two hits. Hits above the minimum needed become Trick Hits — extra currency you can spend to buy bonus effects called Tricks.

| Hits | Outcome |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | Failure. The action does not succeed. The Storyguide adds 1 Momentum to the shared pool. |
| 0 hits AND more than half the dice show 1 | Botch (Disaster). The action fails catastrophically. See Botch Rule below. The Storyguide gains 2 Momentum. |
| 1+ | Success. You accomplish the basic goal. |
| Extras | Trick Hits. Spend these on Tricks for bonus effects (see Tricks, below). |

### Botch Rule — When Failure Becomes Catastrophe

A standard failure (0 hits) is simply an unsuccessful action — the attempt is made, it does not work, and play moves on. A Botch is different: it is a catastrophic failure where the action actively backlashes against the character. Botches are rare but meaningful, and in Mage they carry unique additional consequences through the Paradox system.

A Botch occurs when ALL of the following are true:

- The roll produces zero hits (no die shows 8, 9, or 10).
- More than half the dice in the pool show a 1. On a 6-dice pool: at least 4 ones. On a 5-dice pool: at least 3 ones. On an 8-dice pool: at least 5 ones. Rounding: "more than half" means strictly more than half, rounded down — so on a 7-dice pool, more than 3.5 means at least 4 ones.
If only one condition is met — zero hits but not enough 1s, or plenty of 1s but at least one hit — it is a normal failure, not a Botch. Both conditions together produce the Botch.

| Dice Pool | Minimum 1s Required for Botch (given 0 hits) |
| --- | --- |
| 3 dice | At least 2 ones (2 of 3 = 67%) |
| 4 dice | At least 3 ones (3 of 4 = 75%) |
| 5 dice | At least 3 ones (3 of 5 = 60%) |
| 6 dice | At least 4 ones (4 of 6 = 67%) |
| 7 dice | At least 4 ones (4 of 7 = 57%) |
| 8 dice | At least 5 ones (5 of 8 = 63%) |
| 10 dice | At least 6 ones (6 of 10 = 60%) |
| 12 dice | At least 7 ones (7 of 12 = 58%) |

Botch probability at common pool sizes:

| Pool Size | Approximate Botch Chance (given any roll) |
| --- | --- |
| 4 dice | ~3.7% (about 1 in 27 rolls) |
| 5 dice | ~2.5% (about 1 in 40 rolls) |
| 6 dice | ~1.5% (about 1 in 65 rolls) |
| 8 dice | ~0.5% (about 1 in 185 rolls) |

The design intent: building your dice pool through experience, gear, and preparation genuinely reduces your catastrophic failure risk in proportion to your investment. A starting mage rolling 4 dice botches roughly three times as often as a skilled mage rolling 8 dice. Expertise matters.

> BOTCH EXAMPLES — MUNDANE ACTIONS
> 
> EXAMPLE 1 — BOTCH: Elena (Athletics + Dexterity pool of 5) attempts to leap a gap between rooftops.
> She rolls: 1, 1, 1, 3, 5. Zero hits. Three 1s out of 5 dice (60% — more than half). BOTCH.
> She misjudges the leap entirely and falls. The Storyguide narrates her catching a ledge two floors down — she takes 2 Lethal from the impact and is now dangling in plain sight.
> 
> EXAMPLE 2 — FAILURE (NOT a botch): Same roll, different dice: 1, 1, 2, 4, 5. Zero hits. Two 1s out of 5 (40% — not more than half). Normal failure.
> She hesitates at the edge and cannot commit to the jump. She simply does not make it. No catastrophic consequence — she needs another approach.
> 
> EXAMPLE 3 — BOTCH: Marcus (Technology + Cunning, 6 dice) attempts to hotwire a car under pressure.
> He rolls: 1, 1, 1, 1, 2, 4. Zero hits. Four 1s out of 6 dice (67%). BOTCH.
> He crosses the wrong wires and triggers the car's anti-theft system — it locks down completely and starts blaring an alarm. He has maybe 90 seconds before someone investigates.
> 
> EXAMPLE 4 — SUCCESS overrides 1s: Rosamund (Crafts + Dexterity, 6 dice) rolls: 1, 1, 1, 3, 5, 9. Three 1s — but she got 1 hit (the 9). Not a botch. A close call with a hit: she succeeds, but barely, and the Storyguide might describe the workmanship as rougher than intended.

### Botch Consequences

A Botch is more than a failure. When a Botch occurs:

- The action fails completely, with an active negative consequence determined by the Storyguide. This is not "nothing happens" — something goes wrong.
- The Storyguide gains 2 Momentum (vs. 1 for a normal failure).
- For magical Botches, additional consequences apply (see Chapter Four: Magic and Chapter Five: Paradox).
The Storyguide should tailor Botch consequences to the fiction. A Botch on a social roll is not the same as a Botch on a combat roll. The common element is that the character did not merely fail — they made the situation actively worse. A Botch on an Investigation roll does not mean the character fails to find a clue; it might mean they find a false clue and become convinced it is real. A Botch on a stealth roll does not mean they are noticed; it might mean they leave something behind that will incriminate them later.

### Enhancement

Enhancement is a flat bonus to hits that applies whenever you roll at least one hit — it does not help a failure become a hit, but it makes a hit better. Enhancement typically comes from good gear, a superior position, a well-crafted Rote, or a helpful tag. Enhancement is rated +1, +2, or +3, and the total hits (roll + enhancement) cannot exceed +5 above the base.

> Example: Silas (Son of Ether) fires a Tesla coil pistol (Enhancement +2 weapon tag).
> His Ranged Combat + Dexterity pool is 5 dice.
> He rolls: 8, 8, 10, 3, 5. That is 2 + 2 (the 10) = 4 hits from the dice.
> Adding Enhancement +2 = 6 hits total... but Enhancement caps bonus hits at +5 above base.
> His first base hit was 4 dice-hits. Enhancement adds +2 = 6 total. That is fine (no cap triggered).
> He has 6 hits. Difficulty was 1. He has 5 Trick Hits to spend.

## Difficulty

Every action has a Difficulty from 0 to 5. This is the minimum number of hits required to succeed at all. If your hits do not reach Difficulty, you fail even if you rolled ones.

| Difficulty | Typical Task |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | Trivial. Any success works. (Roll anyway if outcome is uncertain.) |
| 1 | Straightforward for a trained individual. |
| 2 | Challenging even for a professional. |
| 3 | Very hard; would tax an expert. |
| 4 | Bordering on impossible for ordinary humans. |
| 5 | Superhuman feat; possible only for the exceptional. |

For magic, Difficulty is often set by the scope and Sphere rating of the effect. See Chapter Four.

## Complications

A Complication is an obstacle layered on top of a roll's Difficulty. Complications represent things going partially wrong: the lock opens but triggers an alarm, the spell works but backlashes, the punch lands but you leave yourself open. Complications come in three severities.

| Severity | Hits to Buy Off |
| --- | --- |
| Minor Complication | 1 hit |
| Moderate Complication | 2 hits |
| Major Complication | 3 hits |

If you do not buy off a Complication, it triggers — the Storyguide narrates what goes wrong. You may choose which Complications to address. A single roll can impose multiple Complications.

> Example: Maya (Virtual Adept) attempts to hack a Technocratic server.
> Difficulty 2. The Storyguide also sets a Major Complication (3 hits): "Your intrusion triggers an automated tracer."
> Maya's Technology + Cunning pool yields 4 hits. She needs 2 for success = 2 Trick Hits left.
> The Complication costs 3 hits to buy off — she only has 2 spare. She cannot afford it.
> She succeeds in the hack, but the tracer fires. Now the Technocracy knows someone poked the server.
> 
> Alternatively: if Maya had rolled 5 hits, she could spend 2 (Difficulty) + 3 (Complication) = all 5. Clean hack.

## Momentum

Momentum is a shared pool of narrative energy maintained between all players (including the Storyguide, who has a separate pool). Players spend Momentum for extra advantages; the Storyguide spends theirs to make scenes harder or more dramatic. Momentum resets at the start of each session.

### Player Momentum

Players share a single pool of Momentum, starting at 0. It accumulates during play:

- A failed roll: the Storyguide adds 1 Momentum to the player pool.
- A Disaster: the Storyguide adds 2 Momentum to the player pool.
- Roleplaying milestone (Storyguide discretion): +1 Momentum.
Players spend Momentum on:

- Add a die to a roll (before rolling): 1 Momentum.
- Add 1 hit to a completed roll (after seeing dice): 2 Momentum.
- Activate a Trick that requires Momentum: cost varies.
- Introduce a beneficial fact into the scene: 1-2 Momentum (Storyguide approval).

### Storyguide Momentum

The Storyguide has a separate pool starting at the session's recommended amount (usually 3-5). They spend it to:

- Add Complications to a scene.
- Activate NPC Tricks.
- Represent environmental hazards escalating.

## Tricks

Tricks are specific bonus effects purchased with Trick Hits (hits in excess of Difficulty). Each Trick lists its hit cost and what it does. Tricks are presented throughout this book for each type of action. Sphere Tricks — the magical analogs — appear in Part Two.

General Combat Tricks available to all characters:

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Knockdown (1) | Target is knocked prone. Standing from prone costs 1 action. |
| Disarm (1) | Target drops one held item of your choice. |
| Stagger (1) | Target loses their next Minor Action. |
| Extra Damage (1) | Add +1 damage to the attack. |
| Precise Strike (2) | Ignore 1 point of the target's Armor for this hit. |
| Called Shot (2) | Strike a specific location; impose a contextually appropriate Complication. |
| Overwhelm (2) | Target is Staggered and Disarmed simultaneously. |
| Setup (2) | Create an advantageous circumstance: next ally who attacks this target gains +1 die. |
| Finishing Move (3) | If target is at 0 Health, they are immediately incapacitated or dead. |

## Tags

Tags are descriptive keywords attached to characters, equipment, locations, and magical effects. Tags do not have numerical ratings by themselves; instead they are invoked in context to justify a bonus die, a Trick, or an automatic capability.

A tag is written as a bracketed keyword, sometimes with a qualifier: [Precise], [Armored 2], [Fire], [Linked to Correspondence 3], [Paradigm: Sacred Geometry].

### Equipment Tags

Weapons, tools, and gear carry tags that define what they are especially good at. When a tag directly applies to the current action, you gain Enhancement +1 (stacking with other relevant tags up to the +5 cap).

> Example: Silas's Tesla coil pistol has tags: [Electrical], [Ranged], [Precise], [Enhancement +2].
> He is shooting at a target inside a Faraday cage. The Storyguide rules that [Electrical] is at a disadvantage — the cage grounds the charge. The Enhancement +2 still applies (it is inherent to the weapon's craftsmanship), but the [Electrical] tag cannot be invoked for contextual bonuses in this scene.
> 
> Against a target in standing water, however, [Electrical] could justify the Storyguide granting a bonus Complication for free (the water conducts the shock to nearby targets).

### Character Tags

Characters can earn or select tags during character creation and advancement. These represent reputation, supernatural connections, or innate qualities. Examples: [Known to the Progenitors], [Former Soldier], [Seer of the Akashic Brotherhood].

A character tag can be invoked when relevant just as an equipment tag can — to justify a bonus, explain a capability, or create a narrative hook. Tags can also be invoked against you: [Wanted by the Technocracy] means that Iteration X agents may recognize you.

**Tags vs. Merits:** Tags are free-form descriptors — they can be created on the fly, assigned by the Storyguide, or earned through play, and carry no point cost. Merits are a structured, point-purchased subset of Tags: they are Tags with a defined cost, a specific Invoke condition, and explicit mechanical weight. Every Merit is a Tag, but not every Tag is a Merit. If you are reading the Merits section and wondering how it connects to Tags, this is the relationship: Merits are the official, costed version of the same system. See the Character Creation chapter for the full Merit rules.

## Initiative: The Spotlight System

Storypath Ultra does not use a fixed numerical initiative order. Instead, the Storyguide determines who acts first based on the fiction — who was already moving, who had a prepared action, who has the tactical advantage. This is called the Spotlight.

If the starting order is genuinely unclear, all characters in the scene make an Initiative Roll:

Athletics + Cunning — Pool: Add these two ratings. The number of hits determines when your Spotlight falls in the first round. Highest hits act first; ties are broken by Dexterity, then by mutual agreement or coin flip.

After the first round, order continues in the same sequence unless a character's action meaningfully changes their position. The Storyguide can reassign Spotlight as the scene evolves.

### Actions in a Round

Each Spotlight, a character gets:

- One Major Action: An action requiring meaningful effort — an attack, a spell, a complex task.
- One Minor Action: A quick supplemental action — drawing a weapon, moving up to your Speed, ducking behind cover, shouting a command.
- Reflexive Actions: Trivial physical responses that require no meaningful effort — speaking a sentence, dropping a held item, looking around.
Some situations allow Extended Actions, where a character takes multiple rounds to complete a task. Each round's roll contributes hits to a total; once the cumulative hits reach a threshold, the task completes.

## Health and Damage

Characters have a Health track equal to 2 + Stamina. When you take damage, fill in boxes on the track. When all boxes are filled, you are incapacitated or dead depending on context.

| Damage Type | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Bashing | Blunt force, electrical shock, most non-lethal harm. Recovers at 1 box per hour of rest. |
| Lethal | Bullets, blades, fire. Recovers at 1 box per day without medical care; 1 box per scene with care. |
| Aggravated | Severe mystical harm, high-powered magic, supernatural virulence. Recovers at 1 box per week. |

When combining damage types on a track, Aggravated is always on the right, then Lethal, then Bashing. If an attack adds Lethal to a track that already has Bashing, the Bashing shifts left to make room.

### Armor

Armor reduces incoming damage. When you take a hit, subtract your Armor rating from the damage dealt before recording it on your Health track. Armor does not help against Aggravated damage unless it has the [Mystical] tag.

> Example: Ezra (Iteration X agent, Armor 2) is shot by a Cultist for 3 Lethal damage.
> 3 - 2 (Armor) = 1 Lethal damage recorded.
> 
> If Ezra had been caught in a Paradox backlash instead (Aggravated), his Armor would do nothing — Paradox damage tears through mundane protection.

## Advantage

Advantage is a measure of supernatural power, extreme situational dominance, or the edge that equipment and preparation can provide over an opponent. It is rated by levels and applies differently depending on who has the higher rating in a contested situation.

| Advantage Difference | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Equal or lower | No special effect. |
| 1 level higher | +2 Enhancement on rolls against the weaker opponent. |
| 2 levels higher | Weaker opponent suffers +1 Difficulty on rolls against you. |
| 3+ levels higher | Automatic success; no roll required for direct conflict. Storyguide may still call for rolls involving circumstance. |

For mages, Advantage is usually set by their Arete rating relative to their opponent's. A mage with Arete 4 dealing with an unAwakened human effectively has a 4-level advantage — they can reshape that person's reality as a trivial act.

## Status Effects

These conditions represent specific temporary states a character can be in, typically imposed by Tricks, spells, or environmental hazards. Each lists how it is resolved.

| Status Effect | Effect and Resolution |
| --- | --- |
| Blinded | Cannot make visual perception rolls; -2 dice on all combat actions. Resolves: end of scene or medical treatment. |
| Burning | Take 1 Lethal at start of each turn. Resolves: full action to extinguish, or ally spends Minor Action to help. |
| Confused | Cannot take Major Actions for one round; can only act defensively. Resolves: end of next turn. |
| Dazed | Lose next Minor Action. Resolves: automatically at start of next turn. |
| Deafened | Cannot hear; fail auditory perception rolls. Resolves: end of scene. |
| Drowning | Take 1 Bashing per round; escalates to Lethal if unrescued. Resolves: reach air. |
| Exhausted | -1 die to all physical pools. Resolves: full scene of rest. |
| Frightened | Must flee or cower on first action; afterward, -1 die on actions against source. Resolves: Composure + Resolve, Difficulty 2. |
| Grappled | Cannot move freely; -2 dice on ranged attacks. Resolves: Might + Athletics vs. grappler's Might + Athletics. |
| Immobilized | Cannot move at all. Resolves: stated duration ends or ally frees you. |
| Infected | Escalating disease track (see Medicine). Resolves: Medicine + Intellect, Difficulty 2, or magical healing. |
| Poisoned | Take 1 Lethal per scene until treated. Resolves: Medicine + Intellect, Difficulty 2. |
| Prone | -1 die on attacks; +1 die on ranged defense (lower profile). Resolves: Minor Action to stand. |
| Staggered | Lose next Minor Action. Resolves: automatically next turn. |
| Stunned | Cannot act; lose entire next Spotlight. Resolves: Stamina + Resolve roll, Difficulty 2. |
| Unbalanced | -1 die to all actions until the character re-grounds themselves (Minor Action + Athletics, Difficulty 1). |
| In Quiet | Mage-specific: see Paradox chapter. Resolves: Quiet track must be cleared. |

## Extended Tasks

When a task takes significant time or multiple attempts — researching an arcane library, constructing a Wonder, brewing a complex ritual — the Storyguide sets a cumulative hit threshold. Each roll (usually one per hour, day, or narrative segment) adds hits. Once the total meets or exceeds the threshold, the task is complete.

> Example: Priya (Euthanatos) is deciphering an ancient Vedic text to find a banishment ritual.
> The Storyguide sets Threshold 8, using Culture + Intellect per hour of study.
> Hour 1: She rolls 3 hits. Running total: 3.
> Hour 2: She rolls 2 hits. Running total: 5.
> Hour 3: She rolls 4 hits. Running total: 9. Threshold reached.
> The ritual is decoded. It took 3 hours of game-time.

## Opposed Rolls

When two characters directly contest an action — arm-wrestling, a staring contest, a race, or a mage trying to counter another mage's spell — both roll their relevant pools. The character with more hits wins. Ties favor the initiating character, or are re-rolled at the Storyguide's discretion.

Trick Hits from an opposed roll can still be spent normally by the winner, buying effects from the outcome of the contest.

> Example: Dominic (Mind 3 Verbena) attempts to push a false memory into Samuel (Technocrat, Resolve 4).
> Dominic rolls Esoterica + Manipulation (his casting pool) = 7 dice. Gets 4 hits.
> Samuel rolls Resolve + Composure to resist = 6 dice. Gets 3 hits.
> Dominic wins with 4 vs 3. He plants the memory. He has 1 Trick Hit to spend (4 - 3).
> He spends it on the "Depth" Trick: the memory feels indistinguishable from a real one.

# Chapter Three: Character Creation

A mage in this game is defined by three interlocking layers: who they were before Awakening (their Paths), who they became after (their Awakened Template), and who they are becoming (advancement). This chapter walks through all three.

## Step One — Concept

Before touching a single number, decide on a character concept in a single sentence. This sentence should capture your character's paradigm (what they believe magic is), their faction (where they belong in the Awakened world), and their mundane identity (the life they lead between magical crises).

> Concepts:
> "Kaito is a Technocratic NWO agent who believes that a unified global surveillance network will end all suffering, and who is slowly realizing that the Conventions may be the ones causing it."
> "Rosamund is a Verbena hedge-witch from rural Wales who heals the sick with blood and bone and cannot understand why the Traditions fear her methods."
> "Vikram is a Virtual Adept who cracked reality's source code at sixteen and has been high on possibility ever since — the Consensual reality is a bug, not a feature."

## Step Two — Paths (The Storypath Ultra Framework)

Every character is defined by one Major Path and two Minor Paths. Paths represent the most formative chapters of your character's life — not just a job or a skill set, but a way of moving through the world that left deep marks.

### Major Path

Your Major Path is the defining identity that shapes your character most deeply. It provides:

- 6 skill dots distributed among the Path's associated skills (no more than 3 in any single skill at creation).
- 10 attribute dots distributed freely across any three attributes associated with the path.
- 3 Contacts (named NPCs with tags defining their specialty and relationship to you).
- A Bond rated at 2 (a deep personal connection — a person, place, or ideal — that can be invoked for Momentum bonuses and tested in dramatic scenes).
- 1 Special Ability (a unique Trick, immunity, or passive power tied to the Path's nature).

### Minor Paths

Each Minor Path is a secondary facet of identity — a past career, a subculture, a formative relationship. Each provides:

- 3 skill dots (no more than 2 in any single skill).
- 4 attribute dots (freely distributed among path-associated attributes).
- 1 Contact.
- A Bond rated at 1.

### Major Paths by Faction

Each Awakened faction — Tradition, Convention, or Disparate Craft — offers one or more Major Paths. These are listed in full in the Factions chapter. Below is a summary of the template:

| Faction / Group | Major Path Name |
| --- | --- |
| Akashic Brotherhood | The Warrior-Philosopher |
| Celestial Chorus | The Voice of the Divine |
| Cult of Ecstasy | The Seeker of the Bleeding Edge |
| Dreamspeakers | The Walker Between |
| Euthanatos | The Good Death |
| Order of Hermes | The Hermetic Scholar |
| Sons of Ether | The Mad Genius |
| Verbena | The Blood-and-Bone Witch |
| Virtual Adepts | The Reality Hacker |
| Iteration X | The Living Weapon (Technocracy) |
| NWO | The Invisible Hand (Technocracy) |
| Progenitors | The Architect of Life (Technocracy) |
| Syndicate | The Markets That Move the World (Technocracy) |
| Void Engineers | The Pioneer Beyond the Horizon (Technocracy) |
| Nephandi | The Hollowed One (see Antagonists chapter) |
| Marauder | No path — Marauders are NPCs by default (see Antagonists chapter) |
| Disparate Crafts | Craft-specific paths (see Factions chapter) |

## Designing and Customizing Paths

The Tradition and Convention Paths listed in Chapter Eight are strong defaults, but Storypath Ultra is explicitly designed to support custom Paths. A player might want an Akashic Brotherhood martial artist who spent years as a field medic before finding the Brotherhood, or a Verbena witch who grew up in an urban underground rather than a rural tradition. The Path system supports both. The rules below are drawn directly from the Storypath Ultra core manual and govern how to create new Major and Minor Paths.

As Storyguide, you can also use this framework to build Paths for new factions, Disparate Crafts, or unique character concepts that fall outside the canonical Traditions. Any Path you build with these rules is fully compatible with the rest of the system.

### Major Path Design Template

A Major Path represents the single most defining formative experience in the character's life — the calling, culture, or identity that shapes everything else. To build a custom Major Path, define each of the following five elements:

```
MAJOR PATH TEMPLATE
-------------------
1. SKILLS — Choose 3 to 4 Skills that practitioners of this Path use most often. The player distributes 6 dots freely among those Skills (no more than 3 dots in any single Skill at creation). List the available Skills clearly; the player's distribution is their choice within that set.

   Example: A Path of the Investigator might offer Enigmas, Persuasion, and Awareness. The player could put 3 in Enigmas, 2 in Persuasion, and 1 in Awareness — or 2 in each of the first two and 2 in the third, etc.

2. ATTRIBUTES — Choose which Attribute Arenas (Mental, Physical, Social) the Path emphasizes and assign a total of 10 dots distributed across all three Arenas, with at least 1 dot in each Arena. State the Arena breakdown (e.g., Mental 5, Physical 3, Social 2) so the player knows how many dots they can allocate per Arena. Within each Arena, the player assigns dots to any individual Attributes they choose.

   Mental Arena: Intellect, Cunning, Resolve
   Physical Arena: Might, Dexterity, Stamina
   Social Arena: Presence, Manipulation, Composure

   Example: A Scholar-Mage Path might give Mental 6, Physical 2, Social 2. The player puts their 6 Mental dots however they like among Intellect, Cunning, and Resolve.

3. CONTACTS — List 3 to 4 example Contact types that someone on this Path would naturally know. Use the format [adjective or role], not specific names (e.g., "beat cop," "research archivist," "underground herbalist"). These are suggestions; players may define their own contacts if the type fits the Path's world.

4. BOND — The player defines a Bond at rating 2 with another player character, reflecting a relationship forged through this Path. The nature of the Bond is collaborative storytelling, not a mechanical formula.

5. SPECIAL ABILITY — One unique ability, Trick, passive power, or immunity that practitioners of this Path possess by virtue of their training or background. This is the mechanical fingerprint of the Path. Scope it narrowly: an ability that works in almost any situation is too broad; one that applies to a single rare edge case is too narrow. Once per scene, in a defined context, is the right register.

   Example: The Verbena Path of the Blood grants "The Witch's Mark — Once per scene, may spend 1 Momentum to diagnose any natural illness or wound by touch with no roll required." Narrow, thematic, and tied to the Path's identity.
```

### Minor Path Design Template

A Minor Path represents a secondary formative experience — a past career, a subculture, a family heritage, a stint in an institution. It gives depth without defining the character as completely as the Major Path. To build a custom Minor Path:

```
MINOR PATH TEMPLATE
-------------------
1. SKILLS — Choose 3 to 4 Skills. The player distributes 3 dots among them (no more than 2 dots in any single Skill at creation).

2. ATTRIBUTES — Choose Attribute Arenas and assign 4 dots total. Unlike Major Paths, one Arena may have 0 dots — a Minor Path does not need to touch every Arena.

   Example: A Path of the Street Brawler might give Physical 3, Mental 1, Social 0. The player puts 3 dots into Physical Attributes and 1 into a Mental Attribute.

3. CONTACTS — List 1 example Contact type relevant to the Path.

4. BOND — The player defines a Bond at rating 1 with another player character.
```

### Sphere Affinity — Mage-Specific Addition

Beyond the standard Storypath Ultra Path elements, every Tradition Path also carries a Sphere Affinity — a notation of which Spheres most naturally express the paradigm of a mage who walks this Path. Sphere Affinity is not a mechanical bonus at character creation; it is a narrative signal. A mage on the Path of the Scholar-Mage will find that Forces and Prime present themselves most readily to their magical style. Storyguides may use Sphere Affinity to inform which coincidences feel natural to a character, which effects encounter resistance from the Consensus, and which Rotes a mentor would offer during training.

When designing a custom Tradition Path, choose two Spheres as Affinity Spheres. If your character's paradigm centers powerfully on one Sphere above others — as Dreamspeakers center on Spirit, or Verbena on Life — list that Sphere first. For Convention Paths, Sphere Affinity reflects the Convention's approved methodologies.

### Are Tradition Paths Hard-Coded?

The Storypath Ultra core manual distinguishes between hard-coded Paths (always the Major Path, regardless of player preference) and flexible Paths (the player chooses which Path is Major based on their concept). In this game, Tradition Paths are soft hard-coded. Each Tradition offers one canonical Major Path as the expected default. A player who departs meaningfully from that archetype may, with Storyguide approval, either use a custom Major Path built with these rules, or re-designate one of the listed Minor Paths as their Major if it better fits their concept.

Approval should be easy to grant and hard to abuse. The question is not "does this custom Path give too much?" (the template keeps all Paths mechanically equivalent) but "does this Path concept make sense for this mage's life?" A Dreamspeaker who was raised in a city and whose formative experience was as a paramedic rather than a wilderness walker has a genuine reason to customize. A player who simply wants to avoid their Tradition's canonical skill set without narrative justification does not.

### Design Principles (from the SPU Core Manual)

When building a suite of Paths — a full Tradition's or Convention's canonical Major + two Minors, or a new faction — the Storypath Ultra core manual provides the following guidance:

- Skill variety: Count how often each Skill appears across all Paths in the suite. If one Skill appears on every Path, players who focus on it gain no distinctive identity from their Path choice. Consolidate overlapping Paths or swap in different Skills to create contrast.
- Attribute Arena diversity: Across a suite of Paths, ensure players wanting Social, Mental, or Physical characters all have viable options. If every Path gives Mental 6, Physical 2, Social 2, the Tradition plays as a monoculture regardless of Path choice.
- Special Ability scope: Major Path specials should feel distinctive without being broadly overpowered. Use the following as a rough guide: a passive, always-on awareness (no roll needed to sense X) is worth one use of "once per scene." An active triggered ability that replaces a roll is worth one Momentum spend. If an ability would work in the majority of scenes a character participates in, scale it back.
- Contacts: Path contacts are useful acquaintances, not powerful patrons. Keep suggested Contact types evocative of the Path's world. "University librarian" is useful; "archmage with access to all Tradition resources" is not a Contact — it is a story problem.
- Untethered builds (optional): The SPU core manual allows Storyguides to untether Attribute dots from specific Arenas, giving a flat pool of 10 (Major) or 4 (Minor) dots for the player to place anywhere. This suits highly flexible character concepts. For most Mage characters, keeping Attributes tethered to Arenas reinforces Path identity and reduces the choice paralysis of a blank-slate allocation.

## Step Three — The Awakened Template

On top of the Path framework, mages receive the Awakened Template: a set of additional ratings that define their magical capabilities.

### Arete (Traditions) / Enlightenment (Technocracy)

This stat represents the breadth and depth of a mage's awakened consciousness — how far their awareness has expanded beyond the Sleepers' Consensus. It is rated 1–5 and functions as the overall cap on magical capability.

Tradition mages, Independents, and Disparates call this stat Arete — a classical Greek term for excellence and the full realization of potential. Technocracy Operatives call the same stat Enlightenment — reflecting their paradigm of rational, scientific advancement rather than mystical awakening. The mechanics are identical; only the name differs, reflecting each faction's fundamental disagreement about what magic actually is.

| Rating | Traditions (Arete) | Technocracy (Enlightenment) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Sparked — Newly Awakened; magic unpredictable and small in scope. | Initiated — Fresh operative; procedures unreliable; working under supervision. |
| 2 | Initiated — Magic more reliable; meaningful effect on the world. | Field Agent — Procedures solid; cleared for independent fieldwork. |
| 3 | Adept — Significant power; Paradox more dangerous; magic more potent. | Specialist — Formidable; pushing the edges of approved methodologies. |
| 4 | Master — Reshapes significant aspects of reality; Consensus resists. | Senior Operative — Reality-shaping capability; Union oversight increases. |
| 5 | Archmage — Presence warps local reality; few reach this and remain sane. | Extraordinary Citizen — Legend within the Union; reality bends to their methodology. |

All new player characters begin at Arete/Enlightenment 2. Rating 1 is available for chronicles focused on the Awakening or Initiation itself. Rating 3+ is an advancement milestone, not a starting point.

```
Converting from M20: What Happened to Arete and Enlightenment?
--------------------------------------------------------------
If you have played M20 before, you know Arete (Traditions) or Enlightenment (Technocracy) — the single most important number on a mage's sheet, rated 1-10, used directly as the casting dice pool.

In this conversion, both stats have been restructured into three separate functions. Nothing was lost — the functions were redistributed to fit the Storypath Ultra engine more naturally.

FUNCTION 1 — THE CAP: In M20, Arete/Enlightenment capped all Sphere ratings. This function is preserved unchanged. Arete/Enlightenment 1-5 caps all Sphere ratings at the same number.

FUNCTION 2 — THE CASTING POOL: In M20, you rolled Arete/Enlightenment dice to cast magic. In SPU, casting rolls are Sphere Skill + Casting Attribute. A mage with Life 3 and Resolve 5 rolls 8 dice to heal; the same mage with Forces 2 and Dexterity 3 rolls only 5 dice to throw lightning. Characters are stronger in their primary Sphere.

FUNCTION 3 — THE SCALE: M20 ran 1-10. SPU runs 1-5. Conversion:
  M20 Arete/Enlightenment 1-2  →  SPU Rating 1
  M20 Arete/Enlightenment 3-4  →  SPU Rating 2 (starting characters)
  M20 Arete/Enlightenment 5-6  →  SPU Rating 3
  M20 Arete/Enlightenment 7-8  →  SPU Rating 4
  M20 Arete/Enlightenment 9-10 →  SPU Rating 5

TRADITION OR TECHNOCRACY? The stat is called Arete for Traditions, Independents, and Disparates. It is called Enlightenment for Technocracy Operatives. Same mechanics, different name — reflecting each faction's understanding of what magic is.
```

### Sphere Skills

Each of the nine Spheres is rated from 0 to 5 independently, functioning mechanically as a Skill. Your Arete (or Enlightenment) rating is the hard cap on any individual Sphere rating — you cannot have a Sphere rated higher than your Arete/Enlightenment.

At character creation, a beginning Arete/Enlightenment 2 character receives:

- 1 Sphere at rating 2 (their primary Sphere, the one their Tradition emphasizes).
- 2 Sphere dots to distribute freely (could raise a second Sphere to 1 or 2, or raise the first to 3 if Arete 3, etc.).
- The nine Spheres: Correspondence, Entropy, Forces, Life, Matter, Mind, Prime, Spirit, Time.
> Example: Rosamund (Verbena, Arete 2) builds her Sphere array.
> Her Tradition emphasizes Life. She sets Life at 2.
> She distributes her 2 free Sphere dots: +1 to Prime (now 1) and +1 to Spirit (now 1).
> Result: Life 2, Prime 1, Spirit 1, all others at 0.

### Paradox Pool

Each mage has a Paradox Pool — a growing collection of paradox dice that represent reality's accumulated hostility toward their vulgar magic. This pool starts at 0 and grows during play. See the Paradox chapter for full rules.

### Quiet Track

The Quiet Track has five boxes. It represents the mage's accumulating metaphysical instability — the creeping madness that comes from spending too long outside the Consensus. Filling the Quiet Track has escalating consequences. See the Paradox chapter.

### Willpower

Willpower is a rated trait (1–10) representing the force of a mage's trained mind, self-mastery, and psychological resilience. It is one of the most personal numbers on the sheet — it reflects who the character actually is, not what they've learned.

Permanent Willpower rating is set at character creation. The standard starting value is Resolve + 3, maximum 10. The Storyguide may allow higher ratings for characters whose entire concept centers on extraordinary inner discipline (Akashic Brotherhood monks, Euthanatoi death-walkers, Order of Hermes ritualists of long practice).

Current Willpower is a pool that fluctuates during play. It begins equal to the permanent rating and is spent and recovered as follows.

```
Willpower
---------
SPENDING WILLPOWER:
Spend 1 point from your current pool to gain +2 dice (Enhancement) on any single roll — physical, mental, magical, or social. This may only be done once per roll.

WILLPOWER AND MAGIC:
A mage may spend 1 Willpower instead of accepting a Complication on a failed or complicated casting roll. The spell still works at the cost of personal will rather than metaphysical backlash. The Consensus notices this ego-fueled forcing of reality — the Storyguide may add 1 Paradox die to the pool as a consequence.

WILLPOWER AND RESISTANCE:
When resisting mental influence, social coercion, supernatural compulsion, or Paradox-induced Quiet progression, a mage may spend 1 Willpower to add their Resolve to the resistance roll as Enhancement dice rather than as part of the base pool. This stacks with other bonuses.

RECOVERY:
• Spend a scene acting in accordance with your Nature (the core drive that defines your character): recover 1 Willpower.
• Complete a full night of uninterrupted sleep: recover 1d3 Willpower.
• Complete a major story arc that resolves one of your core Bonds or advances a personal goal: recover to full.
• Successfully complete a Seeking: recover to full.

DEPLETION:
A mage at 0 current Willpower cannot spend Willpower for Enhancement. They gain the [Rattled] tag: Compel to hesitate, defer, or avoid conflict. The [Rattled] tag clears when Willpower recovers to 2+.
```

> Example — Willpower in Combat:
> Rosamund is attempting to cast a Life 3 healing effect on a critically wounded ally while under fire. Her pool is Life 3 + Resolve 4 = 7 dice, Difficulty 2 (complex effect under duress).
> 
> She rolls and gets only 1 hit — a Complication. She could accept the Complication (partial heal with a side effect) or spend 1 Willpower to negate it, treating the roll as 2 hits (enough to clear 2 damage boxes). She spends the Willpower. The Storyguide notes her Paradox pool gains 1 die for the ego-fueled forcing.

### Quintessence

Quintessence is the raw substance of magic — the Prime energy that flows through Nodes, crystallizes in Tass, and surges through a mage's Avatar connection to the Tellurian. Every mage carries a personal reservoir of Quintessence that empowers their magic and insulates them from Paradox.

```
OPTIONAL RULE — The Avatar (Appendix A)
---------------------------------------
Avatar is an optional, modular subsystem. A mage's Avatar is the inner spiritual being that drives their Awakening — it has its own Essence, its own agenda, and its own mechanics for Seekings and advancement. Groups can play without it entirely: remove Appendix A, use Arete + 1 for Quintessence maximum, and treat all Avatar-dependent Merits as unavailable. The game functions completely without this subsystem. It is included for groups who want a deeper exploration of the mage's inner spiritual life. Full rules in Appendix A: The Avatar.
```

Maximum Quintessence equals Avatar rating + 3. For mages without an Avatar (not using Appendix A), use Arete + 1. Quintessence begins at half maximum (rounded up) at character creation.

```
Quintessence
------------
SPENDING QUINTESSENCE:
Spend 1 Quintessence before rolling to add 1 Enhancement die to a casting roll. You may spend up to (Arete ÷ 2, rounded up) Quintessence per casting.

Example: Sasha (Arete ●●●●) can spend up to 2 Quintessence per cast, gaining +2 Enhancement dice on top of his base pool.

QUINTESSENCE AS INSULATION:
Once per scene, a mage may spend 1 Quintessence to reduce incoming Paradox by 1 die (to a minimum of 0) before the Paradox roll is made. This represents the Avatar's instinctive buffering of reality's backlash.

QUINTESSENCE AND PRIME:
Mages with Prime ●● or higher may channel Quintessence in additional ways: shaping raw Prime energy, charging Tass, and empowering other mages. See Chapter Six (Prime dot levels) for details.

RUNNING DRY — THE [STRAINED] TAG:
A mage at 0 Quintessence gains the [Strained] tag immediately.
Invoke [Strained]: Push through fatigue for one final effort, spending a Willpower point in place of Quintessence.
Compel [Strained]: The next casting roll suffers -2 dice as the mage's connection to the Tapestry flickers. Magical effects that normally cost no Quintessence become unreliable.

RECOVERY:
• Meditate at a Node: regain 1 Quintessence per hour, up to the Node's rating per day.
• Absorb Tass: regain 1 Quintessence per point stored in the Tass.
• Successful Seeking: restore Quintessence to full.
• Dawn meditation (Akashic practice, Verbena dawn rite, etc.): regain 1 Quintessence per scene of uninterrupted practice.
```

> Example — Quintessence in Play:
> Sasha (Arete ●●●●, Avatar ●●●) has a maximum Quintessence of 6. He begins the session at 4 (starting Quintessence after a night's rest).
> 
> Round 1: He casts Iron Skin (Life ●● + Resolve ●●●● = 6 dice). He spends 2 Quintessence for +2 Enhancement dice, rolling 8 total. He gets 4 hits — strong armor effect. Quintessence drops to 2.
> 
> Round 2: Paradox fires. He spends 1 Quintessence to reduce incoming Paradox by 1 die before the roll. Quintessence drops to 1.
> 
> Round 3: He is at 1 Quintessence, not yet [Strained] (that triggers at 0). He conserves, casts without spending Quintessence.
> 
> After the encounter, the Crossroads Dojo Node is within reach. One hour of meditation restores 2 Quintessence (Node rating 2). He is back to 3, enough for the next scene.

## Step Four — Attributes and Skills from Paths

Total your attribute and skill dots from your Major and Minor Paths:

- Attributes: 10 (Major) + 4 + 4 (Minors) = 18 total attribute dots, distributed per path guidelines.
- All attributes start at 1; dots are added on top. No attribute can exceed 4 at creation.
- Skills: 6 (Major) + 3 + 3 (Minors) = 12 total skill dots, distributed per path guidelines.
- All skills start at 0; dots are added. No skill can exceed 3 at creation.

```
CALLOUT — Arena Distribution Rules (Frequently Asked Question)
--------------------------------------------------------------
All nine Attributes start at 1 before any dots are added from Paths.
The three Arenas are: Mental (Intellect, Cunning, Resolve),
                      Physical (Might, Dexterity, Stamina),
                      Social (Presence, Manipulation, Composure).

MAJOR PATH rule: You must place at least 1 dot in each Arena. You cannot
  ignore an Arena entirely when distributing your 10 Major Path attribute dots.

MINOR PATH rule: Minor Paths may allocate 0 dots to one Arena if the path
  concept warrants it (e.g., a purely social path need not invest in Physical).

CREATION CAP: No Attribute may exceed 4 at character creation regardless of
  how many dots your Paths would allow. Excess dots are lost — plan accordingly.

EXAMPLE: Major Path gives 10 dots. You place 4 in Mental, 4 in Social, 2 in
  Physical. All Arenas covered ✓. No single attribute exceeds 4 ✓.
```
## Step Five — Finishing Touches

Step Five is where the character becomes fully individualized. Your Paths have established your core competencies; now you spend 7 Creation Points to define the edges — the advantages, quirks, resources, and personal connections that make this mage specifically yours.

Spend 7 Creation Points freely across the following options. Unspent points are lost; you cannot bank them as XP. The Storyguide may increase this pool to 10 for more experienced or powerful chronicle tones, or reduce it to 5 for grittier, more resource-scarce games. 7 is the standard.

```
Step Five — Creation Point Costs
--------------------------------
1 point — 1 dot in any Background (Contacts, Resources, Library, Allies, etc.)
1 point — 1 additional Contact (beyond Path-granted Contacts)
2 points — 1-point Merit (minor advantage; a single invoke-style bonus)
4 points — 3-point Merit (significant advantage; a meaningful ongoing mechanical benefit)
6 points — 5-point Merit (major advantage; a rare ability with broad mechanical weight)
2 points — 1 additional starting Rote (beyond the three granted in Step Four)
2 points — Upgrade a Bond by +1 dot (max 3 at creation)
1 point — 1 dot in Quintessence (starting Quintessence, max equal to Arete)

Creation Points cannot be spent on Attributes, Skills, or Sphere ratings. Those are fixed by Paths and Tradition selection.
```

Merits in this system are effectively named Tags with mechanical weight. If you have not read the Tags section in the Core Mechanics chapter, do so first — Merits are built on that foundation. The short version: a Tag is any descriptive label attached to a character, object, or situation that can be invoked for a bonus or compelled for a complication. A Merit is a Tag you paid for at character creation — it comes with a defined point cost and a specific Invoke condition, making it more structured than a Tag the Storyguide assigns on the fly or one you earn through play. A 1-point Merit gives you one Invoke-style bonus — a specific die added in a specific circumstance, or a narrative permission. A 3-point Merit gives a sustained mechanical advantage or a meaningful narrative ability with real game impact. A 5-point Merit is a rare, powerful capability that defines a significant aspect of the character.

When writing a Merit for your character, describe it as a Tag: name it, give it an Invoke condition, and give it a Compel condition if appropriate. The Invoke tells you when you get the bonus. The Compel tells the Storyguide when they can complicate your life with it.

> Example Merit as a Tag

Backgrounds purchased with Creation Points work like Backgrounds purchased during Path selection — they provide narrative resources and occasionally mechanical benefits (see Appendix V: Merits, Flaws, and Wonders for the full Background list). The most commonly purchased Backgrounds at creation are Resources (money and liquid assets), Library (access to magical research material), Node (personal access to a Quintessence source), and Wonder (a specific magical item). A Wonder purchased with Creation Points costs points equal to its Background dot rating.

### Contacts

You have 3 (Major) + 1 + 1 (Minors) = 5 Contacts total. Each Contact is a named NPC. Assign 2–4 tags to each one. Tags define what they know, what they can do for you, and the nature of your relationship. Invoking a Contact's tag in a relevant situation allows you to add 1 die to a related roll or bypass an information-gathering roll entirely.

> Contact: "Dr. Anaya Krishnamurthy" — Tags: [Progenitor Scientist], [Genetics Expert], [Owes You a Debt], [Paranoid About Tradition Agents].
> When Rosamund needs to understand a mutagenic disease, she calls Anaya. The [Genetics Expert] tag means Rosamund can ask a direct question without rolling — Anaya tells her what she needs.
> But [Paranoid About Tradition Agents] is also in play: Anaya keeps the conversation short and refuses to meet in person.

### Bonds

You have a Bond at 2 from your Major Path and a Bond at 1 from each Minor Path. A Bond is a deep personal connection — a person, a place, an ideal, an organization.

A Bond can be invoked once per scene to add a number of dice equal to its rating to a roll that directly relates to it. However, Bonds can be Stressed — when the Storyguide frames a scene that puts the Bond in danger, jeopardy, or conflict, failing to act on it costs 1 dot temporarily. A fully Stressed Bond (reduced to 0) is a lost Bond — a permanent, character-defining wound that must be addressed in future stories.

> Rosamund's Major Path Bond (2): "The Healing Grove of Cwm Glas" — a sacred place in Wales where her grandmother initiated her.
> When Rosamund is pursuing healing magic or protecting the natural world, she can invoke this Bond for +2 dice.
> 
> The Storyguide frames a scene: an Iteration X cleanup crew has burned the grove to root out "biohazardous organic entities." Rosamund must choose between the mission and flying to Wales to mourn.
> If she ignores it without narrative resolution, the Bond is Stressed to 1. If it happens again without resolution, the Bond breaks — and Rosamund's healing magic pool is permanently reduced by 1 until she rebuilds the grove or finds a new anchor.

### Derived Statistics

Calculate the following:

- Health track: four fixed bands. BRUISED band = 2 + Stamina boxes (no penalty). IMPAIRED = 2 boxes (−1 die). CRITICAL = 2 boxes (−2 dice). INCAPACITATED = 1 box. Total = 7 + Stamina boxes. Write your Stamina score on the character sheet and fill in your band sizes.
- Speed: Dexterity + Athletics + 3. This is measured in meters per action on foot.
- Initiative Dice: Athletics + Cunning.
- Defense: The relevant contested roll for dodging; no fixed number — this is always an active roll.

### Starting Gear and Tags

Work with the Storyguide to establish starting gear. Each piece of significant equipment should have 1–3 tags. Standard items need only a name. Items that provide mechanical advantage need tags. At minimum, a starting mage should have:

- One weapon or defensive tool appropriate to their tradition (unarmed, a knife, a wand, a sidearm).
- One focus object appropriate to their paradigm (see Appendix B).
- Situational gear appropriate to their concept.

### Starting Rotes

Every newly Awakened mage begins play with three Rotes already in their repertoire. Rotes are not abilities improvised in the moment; they are the specific magical procedures a mage has rehearsed until they are instinctive — the core toolkit of their magical identity. At character creation, three Rotes are established for free, representing the foundational workings developed during apprenticeship, initiation, and early practice. They have no XP cost at creation. Additional Rotes beyond the starting three cost 3 XP each during advancement.

When selecting starting Rotes, apply these constraints:

- Sphere Requirement: Each starting Rote must use only Sphere ratings the character actually has at creation. A Life 2 character cannot begin with a Life 3 Rote. A character with no Entropy cannot begin with any Entropy working.
- Paradigm Consistency: Each Rote must fit the character's Paradigm, Practice, and Tradition. A Verbena's Rotes use blood, earth, herbs, and spoken invocation. A Virtual Adept's Rotes look like code, algorithms, and signal manipulation. An Akashic Brother's Rotes are forms, kata, and meditative states. The Sphere mechanics are identical across all three; the expression is wholly different.
- Tradition Signature: At least one starting Rote must draw from the character's Tradition's signature Sphere. The nine Tradition signature Spheres: Verbena — Life; Akashic Brotherhood — Mind; Celestial Chorus — Prime; Cult of Ecstasy — Time; Dreamspeakers — Spirit; Euthanatoi — Entropy; Order of Hermes — Forces; Sons of Ether — Matter; Virtual Adepts — Correspondence. Technocratic conventions have their own signature Sphere per convention. (If a character has zero dots in their Tradition's signature Sphere — unusual but not forbidden — this requirement is waived.)
- Utility: At least one starting Rote should be practically useful in regular play: a healing working, an information-gathering effect, a defensive ability, or a social enhancement. A starting mage whose three Rotes are all offense-only or all situational is harder to play and less satisfying to run.
Record each starting Rote with: Sphere(s) and rating required, casting attribute, Instruments, effect description, mechanical summary, and Difficulty. This becomes the mage's magical identity in shorthand. The Storyguide and other players will come to associate these workings with the character just as much as their physical appearance.

> SIGNATURE SPHERES BY TRADITION AND CONVENTION:
> 
>   Akashic Brotherhood ..... Mind
>   Celestial Chorus ........ Prime
>   Cult of Ecstasy ......... Time
>   Dreamspeakers ........... Spirit
>   Euthanatoi .............. Entropy
>   Order of Hermes ......... Forces
>   Sons of Ether ........... Matter
>   Verbena ................. Life
>   Virtual Adepts .......... Correspondence
> 
>   Iteration X ............. Forces
>   NWO ..................... Mind
>   Progenitors ............. Life
>   Syndicate ............... Entropy
>   Void Engineers .......... Correspondence
> 
> At least one starting Rote must use the character's Tradition or Convention signature Sphere.
> Full Rote creation and learning guidelines are in Chapter 4.

## Sample Character: Rosamund Ashby (Verbena)

```
Rosamund Ashby — Blood-and-Bone Witch
-------------------------------------
CONCEPT: Verbena hedge-witch; healer and guardian of the natural world; fears that the Traditions' bureaucracy is as bad as the Technocracy's sterility.
ENLIGHTENMENT: 2

PATHS:
  Major Path — The Blood-and-Bone Witch: Skills: Esoterica 3, Medicine 2, Survival 1. Attributes: Resolve 3, Stamina 2, Composure 2, Presence 2, Might 1.
  Minor Path — The Healer's Apprentice: Skills: Medicine 2, Empathy 1. Attributes: Intellect 1, Cunning 1, Dexterity 1, Composure 1.
  Minor Path — The Daughter of the Moors: Skills: Survival 2, Athletics 1. Attributes: Stamina 2, Dexterity 1, Resolve 1.

FINAL ATTRIBUTES: Intellect 2, Cunning 2, Resolve 5, Might 2, Dexterity 3, Stamina 5, Presence 3, Manipulation 1, Composure 4.
FINAL SKILLS: Esoterica 3, Medicine 4, Survival 3, Athletics 1, Empathy 1.

SPHERES: Life 2, Prime 1, Spirit 1.
STARTING ROTES: (1) "The Grandmother's Knitting" Life 2 — heal Bashing/Lethal by touch; (2) "Reading the Green" Life 1 — diagnose any living thing by touch; (3) "The Silver Thread" Spirit 1 — perceive Umbral entities and spiritual scars. Full write-ups in Chapter 4.
PARADOX POOL: 0. QUIET TRACK: 0/5.

HEALTH: 12 boxes total (Stamina 5: 7 Bruised + 2 Impaired + 2 Critical + 1 Incap). SPEED: 7 (Dexterity 3 + Athletics 1 + 3).

BONDS: "The Healing Grove of Cwm Glas" (2); "Her sister Iona" (1); "The Verbena council of elders" (1).
CONTACTS: Anaya Krishnamurthy [Progenitor Scientist, Genetics Expert, Owes Debt], Brother Matthias [Celestial Chorus, Hospital Chaplain, Neutral], Old Mab [Dreamspeaker Elder, Spirit Lore, Cryptic].

SPECIAL ABILITY (Major Path): The Witch's Mark — Once per scene, Rosamund can spend 1 Momentum to diagnose any natural illness or wound by touch with no roll required.
```

## Advancement

Between sessions — or between major story arcs — characters may advance. Advancement is narrative-driven: you must have demonstrated or practiced the capability you are buying. Spending experience points (XP) on a Sphere requires that you used that Sphere meaningfully in the story, or sought tutelage.

| Advancement | XP Cost |
| --- | --- |
| New Skill dot (1→2 or 2→3) | 2 XP |
| New Skill dot (3→4 or 4→5) | 3 XP |
| New Attribute dot | 3 XP |
| New Sphere dot (first dot) | 4 XP |
| New Sphere dot (additional) | 3 XP × new rating |
| Arete/Enlightenment increase | 10 XP + a major narrative milestone (Seeking or equivalent) |
| Learn a new Rote | 3 XP + narrative justification (tutelage, decoded text, independent research) |
| Add a new Instrument | 1 XP + narrative justification (practice, study, experience) |
| Transcend one Instrument (no penalty without it) | 3 XP + qualifying narrative beat; requires Arete/Enlightenment 3+ |
| Instrument-Free Casting (no penalty without any) | 5 XP; requires Arete/Enlightenment 6+ |
| New Contact | 1 XP |
| New equipment tag | 1 XP (must be justified in fiction) |

The Storyguide awards 2–5 XP per session depending on accomplishment, role-playing quality, and story progression. Arete increases require both XP and a significant in-game experience — a near-death revelation, the completion of a major magical working, or an encounter that fundamentally changes the mage's understanding of the Spheres.

---

## Three Ready-to-Play Characters

The following three characters are complete and ready to play at the start of their first session. Each represents a different Tradition, magical specialty, and approach to play. New players: choose the one whose description resonates with you and hand the sheet to your Storyguide before session one. Experienced players: use these as templates or reference for your own builds.

All three are built on the standard starting template: Arete 2, 7 Sphere dots (no Sphere above 2), 25 Attribute points, 20 Skill points, 3 Willpower, Quintessence equal to (Avatar dot + 3) or 4 if not using the Avatar subsystem, Paradox Pool 0.

### Character 1: Kenji Watanabe — The Akashic Warrior

"I do not fight to win. I fight to understand. The difference is everything."

Kenji is a third-generation Japanese-American martial artist who Awakened during a near-death experience in a tournament fight. His paradigm is Akashic: the universe is a pattern of Do (the Way), and violence — when practiced with perfect mindfulness — is a form of prayer. He is the simplest of the three pregens to play: minimal spellcasting, maximum physical presence, and clear moment-to-moment decisions.

Best for: New players who want to feel powerful immediately without managing complex spell timing. Kenji punches things and occasionally bends reality in subtle ways.

```
KENJI WATANABE — Akashic Brotherhood
------------------------------------
CONCEPT: Martial artist who Awakened to Do (the Way) through combat near-death experience.
TRADITION: Akashic Brotherhood  |  PATH: The Eight-Fold Way (Mind/Life specialists)
ARETE: 2  |  WILLPOWER: 4  |  QUINTESSENCE: 4  |  PARADOX POOL: 0

ATTRIBUTES
  Might 4      Dexterity 4     Stamina 3
  Presence 2   Manipulation 2  Composure 3
  Intellect 2  Cunning 3       Resolve 3

SKILLS
  Close Combat 4   Athletics 3   Stealth 2
  Awareness 3      Medicine 2    Enigmas 2
  Occult 2         Empathy 2

SPHERES
  Life 2   Mind 1
  Casting Attribute: Life = Composure. Mind = Resolve.

TAGS
  [Akashic Training] — Invoke: +2 dice on any Close Combat or Athletics roll.
  [Read the Flow] — Invoke: +2 dice to read an opponent's intent or incoming attack.
  [Unwavering Composure] — Invoke: +2 dice on any Composure roll under pressure.

HEALTH: 5   (Stamina 3 + 2)
ARMOR: 0 (mundane) / Life 2 can grant +2 Armor when enhanced.

TYPICAL SPELLS
  Enhanced Body (Life 2, Coincidental): spend 1 Quintessence + cast Life 2 + Composure.
    On success: +2 dice to all physical actions for the scene; Armor +2.
    SF 2 (Spotlight: resolve same round). Paradox: 0 (Coincidental).
  Still the Mind (Mind 1, Coincidental): cast Mind 1 + Resolve.
    On success: +2 dice to Willpower rolls vs fear, pain, or mental influence for scene.
    SF 1 (Spotlight: resolve same round). Paradox: 0.
  Read Intent (Mind 1, Coincidental): cast Mind 1 + Cunning.
    On success: ask Storyguide one honest question about the target's next action.
    SF 1. Paradox: 0.
```

HOW TO PLAY KENJI: In combat, declare your action early — Close Combat with high Might and Dexterity means you are almost always the most dangerous person in the room. Use Enhanced Body on round one if you expect a prolonged fight. Still the Mind is excellent when facing Quiet Track damage or supernatural fear. Read Intent is situational but can be decisive: learn exactly what the enemy plans before it happens.

Outside combat, Kenji is introspective and observant. Lean on Awareness (3) and Empathy (2) for social reads. His Code is strict: he does not use lethal force unless genuinely necessary, and he prefers resolution over escalation. When the Storyguide puts him in a situation with no clean solution, play into that tension — it is where Akashic characters shine.

### Character 2: Miriam Cole — The Verbena Hedge Witch

"Magic is not a tool. It is a relationship. You ask; the world answers."

Miriam is a third-generation herbalist and midwife from rural Appalachia whose Awakening happened quietly, over years — the first time a poultice cured something it had no right to cure, the first time she called rain and it came. She is a Life and Prime specialist with a deep connection to the natural world. She heals, blesses, and reads the threads of fate. She is moderately complex: her spells have clear payoffs but require some Paradox management.

Best for: Players who enjoy support roles, nature themes, and the satisfaction of solving problems through preparation rather than reaction. Miriam is the character you want when someone in the party is bleeding.

```
MIRIAM COLE — Verbena
---------------------
CONCEPT: Appalachian hedge witch and midwife; heals, blesses, and reads the land.
TRADITION: Verbena  |  PATH: The Root and Branch (Life/Prime hedge witchcraft)
ARETE: 2  |  WILLPOWER: 3  |  QUINTESSENCE: 5  |  PARADOX POOL: 0

ATTRIBUTES
  Might 2      Dexterity 2     Stamina 3
  Presence 3   Manipulation 3  Composure 3
  Intellect 3  Cunning 3       Resolve 3

SKILLS
  Medicine 4   Empathy 3    Occult 3
  Survival 3   Awareness 3  Persuasion 2
  Academics 2

SPHERES
  Life 2   Prime 1
  Casting Attribute: Life = Stamina. Prime = Resolve.

TAGS
  [Healing Hands] — Invoke: +2 dice on any Medicine or Life-based healing roll.
  [Read the Land] — Invoke: +2 dice to Awareness or Survival checks in natural settings.
  [Green Blessing] — Invoke: once per scene, treat one failed Medicine roll as one hit.

HEALTH: 5   (Stamina 3 + 2)
ARMOR: 0

TYPICAL SPELLS
  Knit Flesh (Life 2, Coincidental if subtle): cast Life 2 + Stamina, spend 1 Quintessence.
    On success: heal 1 Health band on any living target. On 3+ hits: heal 2 bands.
    SF 2 (Spotlight). Paradox: 0 Coincidental / 2 Vulgar (dramatic flourish).
  Bless the Threshold (Prime 1, Coincidental): cast Prime 1 + Resolve.
    On success: a location gains Quintessence resonance. Next Awakened spell cast there
    costs 1 fewer Quintessence (min 0). Lasts one scene.
    SF 1. Paradox: 0.
  Poison Read (Life 1, Coincidental): cast Life 1 + Cunning.
    On success: detect toxins, disease, or corruption in any organic matter by touch.
    SF 1. Paradox: 0.
```

HOW TO PLAY MIRIAM: Miriam wins fights by making sure her side is healthier, better-prepared, and better-informed than the opposition. Before any expected confrontation, use Bless the Threshold on the location if possible — free Quintessence fuels future spells. In combat, hold action until someone takes damage; Knit Flesh on your turn restores Health bands that would otherwise cost a scene to recover naturally.

Poison Read is underrated: it tells you whether food, water, or a wound is corrupted, and in urban Mage games that information is frequently life-saving. Her Tags scale well — Healing Hands pairs with her high Medicine to make her the party's definitive medical resource. Play into her rural, earthy paradigm: she does not cast "spells," she makes preparations, applies poultices, and asks favors of the land.

### Character 3: Dex Nguyen — The Virtual Adept

"Reality is a consensus simulation. I just found the debug console."

Dex is a second-generation Vietnamese-American freelance security consultant who breaks into systems professionally. His Awakening happened at 19, the night he patched a zero-day vulnerability in a power-grid control system and realized he had not used a computer. He is a Correspondence and Mind specialist: he can reach information anywhere, perceive things he has no right to perceive, and subtly restructure how people think about problems. He is the most complex pregen and rewards players comfortable with lateral problem-solving.

Best for: Players who enjoy information warfare, social influence, and puzzle-solving over direct combat. Dex rarely throws a punch; he arranges the situation so no one needs to.

```
DEX NGUYEN — Virtual Adepts
---------------------------
CONCEPT: Freelance security consultant who debugs consensus reality.
```
