```
TRADITION: Virtual Adepts  |  PATH: The Signal Path (Correspondence/Mind specialists)
ARETE: 2  |  WILLPOWER: 3  |  QUINTESSENCE: 4  |  PARADOX POOL: 0

ATTRIBUTES
  Might 2      Dexterity 3     Stamina 2
  Presence 2   Manipulation 3  Composure 3
  Intellect 4  Cunning 4       Resolve 3

SKILLS
  Technology 4  Academics 3   Awareness 3
  Stealth 3     Persuasion 2  Occult 2
  Empathy 2

SPHERES
  Correspondence 2   Mind 1
  Casting Attribute: Correspondence = Cunning. Mind = Resolve.

TAGS
  [Signal Ghost] — Invoke: +2 dice to Stealth or any attempt to go undetected digitally.
  [Pattern Recognition] — Invoke: +2 dice to Academics or Awareness when analysing data.
  [Social Engineer] — Invoke: +2 dice to Persuasion or Manipulation using false pretenses.

HEALTH: 4   (Stamina 2 + 2)
ARMOR: 0

TYPICAL SPELLS
  Distant Reach (Correspondence 1, Coincidental): cast Correspondence 1 + Cunning.
    On success: perceive or interact with a remote location as if physically present.
    Range: any place Dex has a strong mental image of. Duration: one scene.
    SF 1. Paradox: 0.
  Data Intercept (Correspondence 2, Coincidental): cast Correspondence 2 + Cunning,
    spend 1 Quintessence.
    On success: intercept all electronic comms in a one-block radius for a scene.
    Storyguide summarises the most relevant data.
    SF 2 (Spotlight). Paradox: 0.
  Reframe (Mind 1, Coincidental if subtle): cast Mind 1 + Resolve.
    On success: one target receives a gentle emotional nudge — not compulsion, just a
    slight shift in mood. Useful for de-escalation or reducing suspicion.
    SF 1. Paradox: 0 Coincidental / 1 Vulgar (obvious mental influence).
```

HOW TO PLAY DEX: Dex's power is information. Before any mission, ask the Storyguide if you can spend 10-15 minutes of in-game time running reconnaissance with Distant Reach — learning the layout, identifying threats, counting guards. That information pays dividends for the entire party.

In social situations, Pattern Recognition plus high Academics lets Dex pick up on contradictions and deceptions faster than almost anyone. Reframe is not a win button — it shifts mood, not commands action — but used subtly it can turn a hostile NPC toward neutrality before a confrontation starts. Dex is physically fragile (Health 4, no Armor). Keep him out of direct melee and use Stealth (3) with [Signal Ghost] to avoid situations where his frailty matters.

# Chapter Four: Magic — The Tapestry of Reality

Magic in Mage: The Ascension — Storypath Ultra Edition is the expression of Will upon the fabric of reality. When a mage wishes to change something about the world, they do not petition a higher power, activate a pre-loaded spell, or expend a spell-slot. They decide what they want reality to do, they focus their Will through their Practice and their understanding of the relevant Spheres, and they roll.

The result is either Coincidental (something that could be explained away by mundane observers) or Vulgar (something that cannot). This distinction is not merely flavor — it is mechanical, because the Consensus itself responds to vulgar magic, generating Paradox.

```
Quick Reminder — Arete and Enlightenment
-----------------------------------------
Throughout this chapter, "Arete/Enlightenment" refers to a single stat with two names.
Tradition mages (and Independents/Disparates) call it Arete.
Technocracy Operatives call it Enlightenment.
The rating, the cap function, the advancement cost, and every mechanical reference are
identical. Only the name changes — reflecting each faction's different understanding of
what magic fundamentally is.

If you see "Arete" in a rule, read it as "Enlightenment" if your character is a
Technocratic Operative, and vice versa.
```

## The Casting Roll

To cast any magical effect, a mage makes a Casting Roll:

```
The Casting Roll
----------------
POOL: Sphere Skill + Casting Attribute

The Sphere Skill is the rating in whichever Sphere (or combination of Spheres) is primarily invoked.
The Casting Attribute varies by Sphere and style; the most common pairings are listed below.
Difficulty is set by the scope and nature of the effect (0-5).

On Success: The effect occurs as intended.
On Failure: The effect does not manifest. The attempt may have been noticed. Add 1 Momentum to the player pool.
On Botch (0 hits AND more than half dice show 1): Effect fails catastrophically AND backlash occurs; add 2 dice to Paradox Pool immediately. Paradox dice count toward the botch 1s threshold — see Magical Botches.
```

### Casting Attributes by Sphere

| Sphere | Primary Casting Attribute (and why) |
| --- | --- |
| Correspondence | Cunning — Spatial reasoning, lateral thinking, perception of simultaneous locations. |
| Entropy | Resolve — Staring into dissolution requires unwavering will; also Composure for subtle decay effects. |
| Forces | Might or Dexterity — Raw kinetic manipulation uses Might; precision targeting of energy uses Dexterity. |
| Life | Resolve or Stamina — Commanding living tissue requires endurance; healing uses Resolve. |
| Matter | Intellect — Understanding molecular and structural relationships is fundamentally academic. |
| Mind | Manipulation or Composure — Imposing thought uses Manipulation; receiving or shielding uses Composure. |
| Prime | Resolve — Quintessence is the raw fabric of magic; only iron will channels it. |
| Spirit | Composure — The spirit world rewards stillness and presence; panic closes the Gauntlet. |
| Time | Cunning — Navigating temporal possibility requires the same lateral cognition as Correspondence. |

The Storyguide may override these defaults for particularly creative casting approaches, always in favor of the richer story.

## Setting Difficulty

Difficulty for a magical effect is calculated directly from the Spheres involved — no Storyguide negotiation required. The formula differs between Rotes and freeform casting, reflecting the difference between practiced precision and real-time improvisation.

| Mode | Difficulty Formula |
| --- | --- |
| Rote | Highest Sphere dot rating used (unchanged). Note: this is Difficulty only, not SF. SF = highest dot − 1 (min 1). A Life ●●● Rote = Difficulty 3, SF 2. A Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●● Rote = Difficulty 3, SF 2. |
| Freeform | Highest Sphere dot rating + number of Spheres involved (minimum 1). A Life ●●● freeform = Difficulty 4 (3+1). A Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●● freeform = Difficulty 5 (3+2). |

Additional modifiers that apply to both modes:

| Condition | Modifier |
| --- | --- |
| Using Instruments (your practice's focus tools) | +1 Enhancement die to pool (see Appendix B) |
| No Instruments available | +1 Difficulty |
| Sympathetic link to target | −1 Difficulty |
| Rushing the cast (reduce time category by one) | +1 Difficulty |
| Under severe duress (active combat, serious injury) | +1 Difficulty (Storyguide discretion) |

> Example 1 — Rote, Single Sphere:
> Rosamund (Life 3) casts her "Grandmother's Knitting" healing Rote.
> Difficulty = highest sphere = 3.
> Pool: Life (3) + Resolve (5) = 8 dice. Using Instruments (+1 die) = 9 dice.
> 
> Example 2 — Rote, Two Spheres:
> Silas (Forces 3, Correspondence 2) uses a practiced Rote to teleport lightning across the room.
> Difficulty = highest sphere = 3.
> Pool: Forces (3) + Dexterity (4) = 7 dice.
> 
> Example 3 — Freeform, Single Sphere:
> Viktor improvises a Life 2 healing effect without a Rote.
> Difficulty = 2 + 1 = 3.
> Pool: Life (2) + Resolve (4) = 6 dice. Needs 3 hits — challenging but viable.
> 
> Example 4 — Freeform, Two Spheres:
> Mara improvises Forces 3 + Correspondence 2 on the fly.
> Difficulty = 3 + 2 = 5.
> Pool: Forces (3) + Dexterity (4) = 7 dice. Needs 5 hits — a desperate gamble.
> Spending Willpower (+2 dice) and Instruments (+1 die) brings pool to 10 dice,
> improving odds to roughly 12%. Still hard. That is the cost of not having a Rote.

## Coincidental vs. Vulgar Magic

Every magical effect is either Coincidental or Vulgar. This is the most important distinction in the game.

### Coincidental Magic

A coincidental effect is one that could, in principle, be explained by mundane means. The bullet curves because of air currents. The patient healed quickly because of their remarkable immune response. The fire spread faster because of unusual drafts. Coincidental magic does not generate Paradox, because the Consensus can absorb it — it finds a way to rationalize what happened.

Coincidental magic is harder to achieve, because the mage must frame their working within the bounds of the possible. This often means more preparation, a better Rote, or a more creative approach. The payoff is safety from Paradox.

### Vulgar Magic

A vulgar effect is one that cannot be explained. The mage raises their hand and a pillar of fire erupts from the sky. They speak a word and someone's bones become rubber. They rip open a gate in mid-air and step through. These things simply do not happen in the Consensus-approved world, and when they do, reality notices.

Performing vulgar magic in front of Sleepers (ordinary unAwakened humans) is called Vulgar with Witnesses and carries the heaviest Paradox penalties. Performing it where only other mages, supernatural beings, or the fundamentally unhinged can see is Vulgar without Witnesses — still Paradox-generating, but less so.

### The Witness Rule

| Magic Type | Paradox Dice Added |
| --- | --- |
| Coincidental (any audience) | 0 — No Paradox |
| Vulgar without Witnesses | 1 die per level of Sphere involved (max 3) |
| Vulgar with Sleeper Witnesses | 2 dice per level of Sphere involved (max 5) |

These dice go into the mage's Paradox Pool immediately after the effect resolves (even if it fails). See Chapter Five: Paradox for what happens with that pool.

## Freeform Magic

Freeform magic is the core of the system. The mage describes what they want to do; the Storyguide sets the Difficulty and notes what Complications might apply; the mage rolls and spends hits. No pre-defined spell is needed.

The mage's description should specify:

- What Sphere or Spheres are involved.
- What the intended effect is.
- How the mage is working — what they are doing physically, mentally, or spiritually to cause this (even if abstract).
The Storyguide then:

- Sets Difficulty based on scope and Sphere rating required.
- Determines Coincidental vs. Vulgar.
- Notes any Complications.
- Optionally narrates what a failure would look like before the dice fall (good practice).
> Example of Freeform Casting:
> 
> Setup: Vikram (Virtual Adept, Mind 2, Correspondence 1) is chasing a Technocratic agent through a crowded subway station. The agent has ducked into the crowd and Vikram cannot see them.
> 
> Vikram's Player: "I want to send a pulse of my consciousness through the digital overlay of the station — Vikram thinks of physical space as a network — and ping any node that's moving against the crowd flow. Basically using Correspondence + Mind to triangulate the agent's position through spatial awareness of bodies-as-data-packets."
> 
> Storyguide: "That's a two-Sphere effect (Correspondence 1 + Mind 1). Base Difficulty is 1, +1 for the second Sphere = Difficulty 2. It's in a public space but the effect is invisible — it's Coincidental. No Paradox. Roll Correspondence (1) + Cunning (3) = 4 dice."
> 
> Vikram rolls: 8, 9, 3, 6 — 2 hits. Just reaches Difficulty 2. Success.
> 
> Storyguide: "You feel the station as a web of motion. One node near the north exit is moving with mathematical precision — too smooth, too counter-intuitive for a panicked civilian. You have a bead on the agent."

## Rotes

A Rote is a carefully practiced magical procedure — a formula, a ritual sequence, a coded algorithm — that the mage has rehearsed until it is second nature. Rotes represent the intersection of theory and practice. Using a Rote does not change the magic fundamentally; it makes it more reliable, safer, and often subtler.

### Rote Benefits

- Enhancement +1 on the casting roll (the practice and preparation pay off).
- Coincidental threshold is slightly more generous — effects that would normally border on Vulgar may be ruled Coincidental if the Rote is beautifully executed.
- The mage can cast the Rote faster — under normal circumstances, a Rote can be invoked as a Minor Action instead of a Major Action for small effects.

### Starting Rotes

Every newly Awakened mage begins play with three Rotes already in their repertoire. These represent the fundamental workings the mage learned during their apprenticeship, initiation, or early post-Awakening experimentation — the magical vocabulary they have practiced enough to trust under pressure. Starting Rotes are not improvised on the fly; they are well-worn grooves in the mage's paradigm, as instinctive as a musician's scales.

At character creation, choose three starting Rotes subject to the following requirements:

- Each Rote must use only Sphere ratings the character actually possesses at their starting dot values. A character with Life 2 cannot start with a Life 3 Rote. A character with no Entropy dots cannot start with any Entropy Rote.
- Each Rote must be consistent with the character's Paradigm, Practice, and Tradition. A Virtual Adept's starting Rotes will look like algorithms and code rather than spoken incantations. A Verbena's starting Rotes involve blood, earth, and botanical reagents. An Order of Hermes initiate uses High Enochian formulae and precise geometric inscriptions. The magic is the same; the expression is wholly different.
- At least one of the three starting Rotes must draw from the character's Tradition's signature Sphere. Every Tradition has a Sphere it emphasizes above all others — the Verbena's Life, the Virtual Adepts' Correspondence, the Akashic Brotherhood's Mind, the Order of Hermes' Prime. A mage who began their magical education within a Tradition will have at least one core working rooted there. (If a character has no dots in their Tradition's signature Sphere — unusual but not forbidden — this requirement is waived.)
- At least one starting Rote should be practical and repeatable in normal play: a healing working, an information-gathering effect, a defensive ability, or a social enhancement. Starting mages who have nothing they can routinely deploy are harder to play and less satisfying to run.
Work with the Storyguide to name, describe, and record each starting Rote. Each one needs: a Sphere and rating, a casting attribute, a list of Instruments, a brief description of how it manifests through the mage's paradigm, its mechanical effect, and a Difficulty value (equal to the highest Sphere dot used).

> STARTING ROTES EXAMPLE: ROSAMUND ASHBY (Verbena, Life 2, Prime 1, Spirit 1)
> 
> Rote 1: "The Grandmother's Knitting" (Life 2) -- TRADITION SIGNATURE [Verbena: Life]
>   Effect: Heals up to [hits] boxes of Bashing or Lethal damage on a single touched target.
>   Casting Attribute: Resolve
>   Pool: Life 2 + Resolve 5 + Enhancement +1 = 8 dice  |  Difficulty 2  |  Coincidental
>   Instruments: Rhythmic hand motions mimicking knitting; sustained focus of 10 seconds.
>   Paradox: None. Medical observers describe it as spontaneous coagulation.
>   Why this Rote: Rosamund's grandmother was a hedge-healer. This was the first working she was ever
>   shown, and she has practiced it more than any other -- the most instinctive thing she knows.
> 
> Rote 2: "Reading the Green" (Life 1)
>   Effect: Rosamund touches any living plant or animal and reads its health, stress, recent injury
>   or toxin exposure, and whether it has been magically altered. Information arrives as a felt sense
>   rather than words -- she knows what the oak knows, not what a biologist would report.
>   Casting Attribute: Resolve
>   Pool: Life 1 + Resolve 5 + Enhancement +1 = 7 dice  |  Difficulty 1  |  Coincidental
>   Instruments: Physical touch and quiet attention.
>   Paradox: None. Indistinguishable from a skilled naturalist's observation.
> 
> Rote 3: "The Silver Thread" (Spirit 1)
>   Effect: Opens Rosamund's awareness to the spirit world. She can perceive Umbral entities at
>   her location, detect whether a location has been spiritually scarred or blessed, and receive
>   impressions from nearby spirits (not full communication -- that requires Spirit 2).
>   Casting Attribute: Resolve
>   Pool: Spirit 1 + Resolve 5 + Enhancement +1 = 7 dice  |  Difficulty 1  |  Coincidental
>   Instruments: Silence; a small reflective surface (mirror shard); closing the mundane eyes.
>   Paradox: None alone. Vulgar with Sleeper Witnesses who see her respond to invisible things.
> 
> Note: Rosamund has Prime 1 but no Prime Rote. She is aware of Quintessence flows but has not
> yet formalized a working around them. That will be her first advancement purchase.

### Learning New Rotes

Between sessions and during downtime, mages develop new Rotes by crystallizing improvised effects they have performed successfully in play. Learning a new Rote costs 3 XP and requires a qualifying narrative justification. A mage who has never cast a given effect cannot simply buy a Rote for it because the XP is available; they must have performed the effect (or received formal tutelage in it), and the Rote represents the codification of hard-won understanding.

Qualifying narrative justifications for a new Rote:

- Tutelage: The mage received formal instruction from a more experienced practitioner. The working must have been demonstrated and explained, not merely described. A Chantry master, a Tradition mentor, or a captured grimoire worked through with a translator all qualify.
- Independent Research: The mage performed the effect successfully at least three times in past sessions through improvised casting, and between sessions spent time writing it down, refining the Instruments, and testing variations. The Storyguide may call for a downtime extended roll (Esoterica + Intellect, Threshold 5) for complex or multi-Sphere Rotes.
- Decoded Text: The mage obtained a grimoire, formula, or recorded working from another mage's tradition and translated it into their own paradigm. This requires either a Mentor to assist with translation or a solo roll (Esoterica + Intellect, Difficulty 3 for same-tradition text, Difficulty 4 for cross-tradition, Difficulty 5 for Technocratic or Marauder workings).
- Witnessed and Reverse-Engineered: The mage observed another mage perform the working, was able to perceive the Sphere-level effects (requires appropriate Sphere knowledge to recognize), and spent downtime reconstructing the procedure. The three-successful-casts criterion still applies before the Rote is formally recorded.
What does NOT qualify: buying a Rote because it would be convenient, or because the mage "probably would have figured it out off-screen." Rotes are hard-won. The narrative justification should be at least a sentence of actual play history that the Storyguide can confirm.

Some traditions treat Rotes as cultural artifacts with lineages. Hermetic mages use formal designations in High Enochian; Virtual Adepts name algorithms with version numbers and author handles; Verbena rites carry generational names passed mother-to-daughter, grandmother-to-grandchild. A mage who inherits a named Rote from a slain Chantry-mate carries something more than a mechanical advantage.

> LEARNING A NEW ROTE -- WORKED EXAMPLE
> 
> Marcus (Order of Hermes, Life 3, Prime 2) wants to learn "Mending the Vessel" --
> a multi-Sphere working that uses Prime 2 to channel raw Quintessence into a Life 3
> restoration, healing Aggravated damage.
> 
> Step 1: Does Marcus have the required Spheres? Life 3 and Prime 2. He does.
> 
> Step 2: Does he have narrative justification?
>   Session 14 -- Marcus improvised this effect on a dying Verbena ally. It worked (1 hit
>   on Difficulty 3). Attempt one on record.
>   Session 16 -- Controlled conditions in the Chantry infirmary. Two hits. Attempt two.
>   Session 19 -- Used it on himself under field pressure. Three hits. Attempt three.
> 
> Step 3: Between Sessions 19 and 20, Marcus writes the Rote in his grimoire in High Enochian.
>   No extended roll required -- three successful improvised castings on record, same-Sphere
>   combination he understands well. A complex cross-tradition Rote might require the roll.
> 
> Step 4: Player spends 3 XP. Marcus now has "Mending the Vessel" as a formal Rote.
>   Future castings gain Enhancement +1. Instruments: his silver-wound athame and a brief
>   Latin recitation from the Clavicula Salomonis.

Mages under fire while casting have a third option beyond absorbing the hit or hoping the Concentration check succeeds: Sphere Defense. A reactive magical working that deflects, negates, or interferes with an incoming attack before it lands. A successful Sphere Defense eliminates the Concentration check entirely. Full rules — including Quintessence costs, Paradox interactions, Willpower burns, and multiple worked examples — appear in Chapter 7: Combat, under Sphere Defense — Defensive Casting.

## Magical Botches — When Reality Rejects the Working

The standard Botch rule (see Chapter Two: Mechanics) applies to all rolls — zero hits and more than half the dice showing 1. For magical effects, the consequences are more severe and the rule has a critical extension: Paradox dice count toward the botch threshold.

### Paradox Dice and the Botch Threshold

When a mage casts a magical effect, they roll their casting pool. If they are carrying Paradox, the Paradox Pool dice are rolled separately (they do not add hits). However, when determining whether a Botch has occurred, the 1s from Paradox dice are counted alongside the 1s from the casting pool. Hits come only from the casting pool.

This mechanic exists for a specific reason: a mage carrying significant Paradox is operating with a corrupted pattern. The Paradox in their system actively works against their magic — the 1s it generates represent reality resisting the working. The more Paradox a mage carries, the lower the threshold for catastrophic failure, even if their casting skill remains the same.

```
MAGICAL BOTCH RULE
------------------

A magical roll BOTCHES when:
  1. The casting pool produces ZERO hits (no 8s, 9s, or 10s), AND
  2. More than half of ALL dice rolled (casting pool + Paradox pool combined) show 1.

Hits come from casting pool only.
Ones for the botch threshold: both pools combined.

Example: 6 casting dice + 3 Paradox dice = 9 total dice.
Botch threshold: more than 4.5 = at least 5 ones across all 9 dice.
```

> MAGICAL BOTCH EXAMPLES
> 
> EXAMPLE 1 — CLEAN FAILURE (no botch): Devorah casts Forces ●●● (6-die casting pool). No Paradox.
> Roll: 1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Zero hits. Two 1s out of 6 (33%). Not more than half. Normal failure.
> The fireball fizzles. The Paradox Pool is not affected. The scene continues.
> 
> EXAMPLE 2 — BOTCH, no Paradox: Same scenario.
> Roll: 1, 1, 1, 1, 2, 4. Zero hits. Four 1s out of 6 (67%). More than half. BOTCH.
> Consequences: The fire effect inverts — the mage herself ignites momentarily, taking 2 Lethal. The Paradox Pool gains 2 dice. The area is now lit by the mage's flaming coat.
> 
> EXAMPLE 3 — BOTCH with Paradox (the dangerous scenario):
> Nikolai has 3 dice in his Paradox Pool (from earlier Vulgar casting). He casts Mind ●●● freeform (6 casting dice).
> Casting pool: 1, 1, 1, 4, 5, 6. Zero hits.
> Paradox pool: 1, 1, 3.
> 
> Total dice rolled: 9. 1s found: 5 from casting + 2 from Paradox = 5 ones total.
> Botch threshold: more than 4.5 = at least 5. Exactly 5 ones. BOTCH.
> 
> Without Paradox, this would have been: 3 ones out of 6 = 50%. Not more than half. Just a failure.
> The Paradox dice pushed him over the threshold. His invasive Mind working collapses catastrophically — the thoughts he was trying to read reflect back into him. He takes 2 dice of Shock damage to his Quiet Track and gains a temporary Quiet condition for the scene.
> 
> EXAMPLE 4 — PARADOX saves an otherwise near-botch:
> Rosamund rolls: 1, 1, 1, 4, 5, 9. One hit (the 9). Three 1s.
> She succeeds — one hit. No botch is possible because she has at least one hit. Paradox dice don't matter here: hits override the botch condition entirely.
> The rule is clean: any hit = no botch, regardless of 1s.
> 
> EXAMPLE 5 — HIGH PARADOX escalation:
> Marcus carries 5 Paradox dice (a serious buildup — he's been casting Vulgar effects all session).
> He rolls Forces ●●● (6 casting dice) + 5 Paradox dice = 11 total.
> Botch threshold: more than 5.5 = at least 6 ones across all 11 dice.
> Casting pool: 1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Zero hits. Two 1s.
> Paradox pool: 1, 1, 1, 2, 4. Three 1s.
> Total 1s: 5. Threshold: 6. Not a botch — barely.
> 
> If his Paradox pool had been 6 dice instead of 5, and he had rolled one more 1: botch.
> This illustrates why managing Paradox is a survival skill, not a cosmetic concern. Carrying 5+ Paradox dice means your botch threshold is dramatically more accessible, even on rolls where your casting pool was "almost fine."

### Magical Botch Consequences

A magical Botch is worse than a mundane one because the energy of the failed working does not simply dissipate — it goes somewhere. The Storyguide chooses from the following consequence categories, escalated in severity by how many dice are in the Paradox Pool:

| Paradox Level at Time of Botch | Botch Consequence Severity |
| --- | --- |
| 0–1 Paradox dice | Minor: The effect inverts or rebounds onto the caster. 1–2 Bashing or Shock damage. Paradox Pool gains 2 dice. The environment may show a brief weird symptom (lights flicker, metal rusts, air smells wrong). |
| 2–3 Paradox dice | Moderate: The effect partially manifests in a harmful or uncontrolled way. 2–3 Lethal or Trauma damage. Paradox Pool gains 2 dice and immediately triggers a Paradox roll (see Chapter Five). A new Quiet condition or environmental anomaly. |
| 4–5 Paradox dice | Major: Full Paradox backlash. The effect discharges violently — all targets within Short range (including allies) are affected. Paradox Pool gains 3 dice and immediately triggers a full Paradox event. The mage is Impaired until the end of the next scene. |
| 6+ Paradox dice | Catastrophic: The Paradox Pool fully discharges as a Paradox Storm. The area around the mage (Group area, up to 10 people) experiences a severe Paradox event. The mage is Incapacitated (Quiet Track) and cannot cast magic until the Paradox is reduced below 3 dice. |

Storyguides should always tie the botch consequence thematically to the Sphere and the specific effect attempted. A failed Forces fireball botch should produce fire-based consequences. A failed Mind reading botch should produce cognitive/psychological consequences. The category above sets the severity; the fiction sets the form.

## Sphere Tricks

Sphere Tricks are named magical effects — specific capabilities that become available as a mage's Sphere rating increases. They function exactly like Combat Tricks: they are purchased with Trick Hits from a casting roll. Some Tricks have a base hit cost of 1, others 2 or 3.

Sphere Tricks are the "crunchy" layer of the magic system. They are not exhaustive — a mage can always attempt effects not on the Trick list using Freeform Magic — but the Tricks represent the most common, most well-understood applications of each Sphere at each dot level. Using a Trick instead of freeform magic is faster (no negotiation of scope) and often more reliable.

Full Sphere Trick lists appear in Chapters Six through Fourteen, one chapter per Sphere. Below is an example from the Forces Sphere:

> Forces Sphere Trick Example — "Directed Flame" (Forces 2, cost 1 Trick Hit):
> When you successfully cast a Forces effect involving fire, spend 1 Trick Hit to designate a precise direction and radius for the fire. Bystanders within 3 meters who are not your target must make Athletics + Dexterity rolls (Difficulty 1) or suffer 1 Lethal damage from proximity heat.
> Note: If you have Forces 3, you can instead spend 2 Trick Hits to make the fire not harm designated allies at all — your control is precise enough to burn selectively.

## Spell Parameter Scaling

Every magical effect has three spatial-temporal parameters: Range (how far away the effect can reach), Area (how large a volume or how many targets the effect covers), and Duration (how long the effect persists). A fourth parameter — Time Reach — applies specifically to effects using the Time Sphere, governing how far into the past or future the mage can perceive or intervene. These parameters are governed by unified band tables. Extra hits from the casting roll can be spent to move any parameter up one band per hit.

A spell's default parameter values are set by the Sphere dot level used. Higher dot ratings grant longer range, larger area, and greater duration at no extra cost. Spending free hits on parameters is an alternative to spending them on Sphere Tricks — you are trading precision or special effects for raw scope.

The Storyguide always has final say on whether a requested parameter increase is narratively coherent. You cannot extend a Forces fireball to Extreme range (1,000+ meters) and then claim it is coincidental because no one can see you casting.

### Range Bands

Range governs the maximum distance between the caster and the target or point of effect. Range is measured from the caster's current position, not from an intermediary target.

This project's standing rule is that a genuine conflict between a house rule and an actual Storypath Ultra core mechanic is resolved in favor of the SPU core mechanic. Storypath Ultra already provides a fully worked-out positional abstraction — Range Bands — and Appendix H's "Movement in Combat" section defines exactly one such table for tracking where combatants physically stand relative to each other. Spellcasting range uses that same table. There are not two separate band systems to reconcile in play; a mage's sense of "how far away is that target" does not change depending on whether their next move is a punch or a Forces bolt.

| Range Band | Distance | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Close | 0–2 meters | Arm's reach, grappling distance, point-blank. |
| Short | 3–20 meters | Across a small room, a short hallway, close-quarters combat distance. |
| Medium | 21–60 meters | Across a street, a large room, standard outdoor engagement range. |
| Long | 61–1,000 meters | A city block, a field, a warehouse floor, rooftop to rooftop. |
| Extreme | 1,000+ meters | Hilltop sniping distance, the visible horizon in clear terrain — situational, and only reachable at all by effects built or extended to reach that far. |

Default range by Sphere dot level: ● = Close; ●● = Short; ●●● = Medium; ●●●● = Long; ●●●●● = Extreme. Each free hit spent on Range moves the effect up one band — the identical "1 hit = 1 band" conversion used for mundane weapons and movement in Appendix H. Spending two hits on Range moves it two bands, and so on.

**Touch — a requirement Tag, not a band.** Some effects are not merely short-ranged, they demand actual physical contact: pressing a hand to a wound to knit it shut, gripping a target's temples to walk through their thoughts, laying a palm flat against a wall to feel its history through Life's residue in the stone. These effects carry a **[Touch]** Tag rather than occupying their own Range Band. [Touch] requires two things simultaneously: the caster must be at Close range with the target, *and* the Storyguide must be satisfied that actual physical contact is fictionally possible and being attempted (a target braced against a wall with a weapon leveled at the caster is at Close range but may not be reachable for a [Touch] effect without first winning a grapple or forcing an opening). Being at Close range is necessary for a [Touch] effect but is not automatically sufficient — it is the tighter of the two conditions, layered on top of the Close band rather than replacing it. Most Life healing/harming Tricks, several Mind Tricks that read or alter a specific mind directly, and any effect explicitly described as requiring the caster's hands on the target carry this Tag; see each Sphere's Trick list in Chapters Six through Fourteen for which of their Tricks are tagged [Touch].

**Sympathetic range — a Correspondence exception, not a sixth band.** A mage with Correspondence ●●●+ who holds a genuine sympathetic link to a target (a lock of hair, a drop of blood, a true name spoken in binding, a possession of deep personal significance) can ignore the Range Band track entirely for effects targeting that specific person or object, reaching them at any distance the link can plausibly span. This is not a band on the ladder that anyone can reach by spending enough hits — it is a Correspondence-specific capability, gated behind the dot rating and requiring an actual link to exist in the fiction, not merely being declared. A Forces ●●●●● mage with no Correspondence training cannot buy their way to unlimited range no matter how many hits they spend; Extreme (1,000+ meters, and only situationally reachable even there) is the hard ceiling for every Sphere except Correspondence's sympathetic-link exception. See the Correspondence Sphere chapter for the full Sympathetic Range Trick and its Paradox implications — reaching across an unlimited gap on a thread of hair is powerful, and vulgar, magic.

> RANGE SCALING EXAMPLE — THE FORCES ●●● FIREBALL
> 
> Devorah (Forces ●●●) hurls a fire bolt. Default range: Medium (21–60m). She rolls 5 hits vs. Difficulty 3 — two free hits.
> 
> Option A: She spends both free hits on Range. Medium → Long (1 hit) → Extreme (2 hits). The fireball can now reach a target more than a kilometer distant. This is extremely Vulgar at that range.
> 
> Option B: She spends 1 hit on Range (Medium → Long) and 1 hit on the Shaped Charge Trick (spares her allies). The bolt reaches Long range and burns selectively.
> 
> Option C: She spends both free hits on Area (see below). The fireball's range stays at Medium but it expands from Single Target to Group (up to 10 people / 5m radius). Useful in a crowd.
> 
> Default (no free hits spent on Range): Range stays at Medium. That band is the baseline for Forces ●●●, no cost required.
>
> Contrast — a [Touch] effect: if Devorah instead cast a Life ●● Trick tagged [Touch] to cauterize her own wound, no amount of hits spent on Range would let her do it from Medium range. [Touch] effects are pinned to Close range plus actual contact regardless of the caster's Sphere rating or free hits; the fix for "I want to heal someone across the room" is to close distance (see Appendix H's Movement in Combat), not to spend hits on this table.
>
> Contrast — a sympathetic effect: if Devorah instead had Correspondence ●●●● and a lock of the target's hair, she could reach the target at any distance the link could span — across the city, in principle — without touching this Range Band table at all. That capability comes from Correspondence specifically, not from spending hits on Range.

### Area Bands

Area governs how many targets or how large a volume the effect covers. The default for all spells is Single Target (one person, one object, one precisely defined point). Spending free hits on Area expands coverage. Note that hitting more targets with a damaging effect does not increase damage per target — each target in an area effect is hit for the same base damage the spell would deal to a single target. Area attacks are generally Vulgar unless framed carefully.

| Area Band | Coverage / Description |
| --- | --- |
| Single target | One specific person, object, or point. Default for all spells. No hit cost. |
| Small group | 2–3 people within 2 meters of each other, or a small object cluster. 1 free hit. |
| Group | Up to 10 people within a 5-meter radius, or a room's worth of objects. 2 free hits. |
| Large group | Up to 30 people within a 10-meter radius — a crowded bar, a small office floor. 3 free hits. |
| Building | An entire structure and all within it. 4 free hits. Vulgar without strong justification. |
| City block | An open city block or equivalent open-air area (~100m radius). 5 free hits. Always Vulgar. |

Area expansion requires the caster to be able to conceivably reach all targets within the band. You cannot cast a "Group" area effect and then exclude everyone except one specific person — if you want precision within an area, use the Shaped Charge Trick (Forces) or equivalent per-Sphere precision Tricks. Area costs are cumulative with Range costs — a Long-range Group-area fireball costs 1 hit for Range (Medium → Long) + 2 hits for Area (Single → Group) = 3 free hits total.

### Duration Bands

Duration governs how long a magical effect persists after the initial casting action. Instant effects apply their consequence and end (a fire bolt ignites something; the bolt itself is gone). Sustained effects require ongoing magical presence — they can usually be dispelled, and they degrade if the caster is incapacitated. Permanent effects are rare and expensive; they require spending Quintessence and are subject to the specific Sphere's maximum sustained complexity.

The default Duration for most effects is determined by the type of spell: attack spells are Instant by default; transformation, enhancement, and ward spells are Scene by default; perception and awareness spells are Scene by default. The Storyguide may adjust defaults for unusual effects.

| Duration Band | Length / Notes |
| --- | --- |
| Instant | The effect applies and ends immediately. Default for attacks, damage, one-shot transformations. No hit cost. |
| Concentration | Lasts as long as the caster concentrates (Minor Action each round to maintain). Interruption ends the effect. No hit cost — but concentration takes up your Minor Action. |
| Scene | Lasts approximately one hour or the current dramatic scene. Default for most sustained effects. No hit cost if appropriate for the effect type; 1 free hit to extend an Instant effect to Scene. |
| Day | Lasts 24 hours. 1 free hit beyond Scene default (or 2 hits from Instant). |
| Week | Lasts 7 days. 2 free hits beyond Scene default. |
| Month | Lasts 30 days. 3 free hits beyond Scene default. |
| Permanent | The effect does not fade. Requires spending 1 Quintessence per additional Sphere dot involved, plus the effect must be within the Sphere's permanent-change capability (typically requires ●●●● or ●●●●●). Cannot be dispelled without matching Sphere knowledge. |

A caster can choose to spend hits on Duration instead of Tricks. An effect that would normally last a Scene can be extended to a Week for 2 free hits — useful when you need a ward or disguise to persist without ongoing attention.

### Time Reach Bands (Time Sphere Only)

The Time Sphere uses a separate scaling table for how far into the past or future the mage can perceive, communicate, or intervene. This is distinct from Duration (which governs how long the effect lasts) and distinct from Range (which governs spatial distance). Time Reach governs temporal distance — measured in the chart below. The direction (past vs. future) is governed by the Time Sphere dot level as described in Chapter Nine: higher dots unlock the ability to look forward rather than merely backward.

Reaching further into the past or future is increasingly Vulgar and increasingly subject to Paradox. Viewing the distant past is generally coincidental if framed correctly (visions, memory echoes); intervening in it is always Vulgar. Viewing the near future is Vulgar; changing it approaches chronicle-level Paradox.

| Time Reach Band | Distance / Minimum Sphere |
| --- | --- |
| Moments (seconds) | The immediate past/present/future — what just happened or is about to happen within the same action. Time ●. |
| Minutes | Events of the last or next few minutes. Time ●. |
| Hours | Events of the last or next several hours. Time ●●. |
| Days | Events of the last or next day or two. Time ●●. |
| Weeks | Events of the last or next month. Time ●●●. |
| Months | Events of the last or next several months. Time ●●●. |
| Years | Events of the last or next decade. Time ●●●●. |
| Decades | Events of the last or next century. Time ●●●●. |
| Centuries | Historical and near-mythological time. Time ●●●●●. |
| Millennia | Deep history or remote future — the limits of the sphere's reach. Time ●●●●●, with free hits spent on Time Reach. Paradox cost is severe. |

Extra free hits spent on Time Reach extend the accessible range one band per hit, within the limits set by the mage's Time dot rating. A Time ●●● mage can reach back Days by default; spending 2 free hits extends this to Months. They cannot reach Years without Time ●●●●, regardless of how many hits they spend.

> TIME REACH SCALING EXAMPLE — LOOKING INTO THE PAST
> 
> Amara (Time ●●●) investigates a murder scene. She wants to see what happened. Her Time ●●● default reach is Weeks.
> 
> She rolls 4 hits vs. Difficulty 2 (reading recent past impressions — coincidental) = 2 free hits.
> 
> The murder was three weeks ago — right at the edge of her default range. She gets a clear but distant vision.
> 
> Spend 1 free hit on Time Reach: Weeks → Months. Now she can reach back several months with this casting. She sees not only the murder but the meeting three months earlier that set it in motion.
> 
> Spend the remaining free hit on Duration (Scene → Day): her vision-trance lasts a full day instead of one scene, giving her time to watch the full sequence of events at whatever pace she chooses.
> 
> She cannot spend hits to reach Years — that requires Time ●●●●. But two hits bought her a significantly more useful reading.

### Default Parameters by Sphere Dot Level

This table summarizes the default Range, Area, and Duration granted by each Sphere dot level. These defaults apply when the mage spends no free hits on parameters. Spending free hits steps the parameter up one band per hit from the default, not from zero.

| Sphere Rating | Default Range — Default Area — Default Duration |
| --- | --- |
| ● (one dot) | Close (0–2m) — Single Target — Instant (attacks) / Scene (sustained) |
| ●● (two dots) | Short (3–20m) — Single Target — Instant / Scene |
| ●●● (three dots) | Medium (21–60m) — Single Target — Instant / Scene |
| ●●●● (four dots) | Long (61–1,000m) — Single Target — Instant / Scene. Permanent effects become accessible. |
| ●●●●● (five dots) | Extreme (1,000+m) — Small Group (free) — Instant / Scene. Permanent effects are routine. |

Note: a Sphere dot level's default Range is a starting point on the shared Close/Short/Medium/Long/Extreme table (see Range Bands, above), not a separate scale — this is the same table Appendix H uses for combat positioning. [Touch]-tagged effects are an exception layered on top of Close range (see above) rather than a band of their own, and Correspondence's sympathetic-link capability bypasses this table entirely rather than sitting at the top of it.

At five dots, a mage automatically covers a Small Group with any effect at no hit cost — their mastery of the Sphere is so complete that precision at that scale requires no additional effort. The Storytguide can rule that truly grand masters (Arete ●●●●●●+ in chronicle-scale stories) extend these defaults further, but this is not part of standard play.

## The Role of Time in Casting

Not all magic happens instantly. The time required to cast an effect scales with its ambition:

| Cast Time | Typical Application |
| --- | --- |
| Reflexive (free) | Passive Sphere awareness: sensing magic, detecting Paradox, general perception effects. |
| Minor Action | Simple Rotes; small freeform effects already practiced; boosting ongoing effects. |
| Major Action | Standard freeform casting; most Sphere Tricks; active magical attacks. |
| Full Round | Two-Sphere combinations; significant healing; direct transformation of matter. |
| Extended (minutes) | Complex multi-Sphere workings; rituals requiring precise symbolism. |
| Extended (hours/days) | Wonders, Nodes, long-term enchantments, Ascension workings. |

Rushing a casting (reducing time by one category) adds +1 Difficulty to the roll.

## Aiding Magic: Sympathy, Tools, and Collaboration

### Magical Sympathy

Sympathetic magic is the practice of working effects on a target through something connected to them — a lock of hair, a photograph, a possession of great personal significance. Using sympathetic links reduces Difficulty by 1 for Sphere effects targeting that specific person or object. The strength of the link determines how long it remains viable.

| Sympathetic Link | Reduction and Duration |
| --- | --- |
| Powerful (blood, bone, deep personal item) | -1 Difficulty; lasts until link is destroyed or severed. |
| Moderate (photograph, handwriting, common possession) | -1 Difficulty; lasts for the scene. |
| Weak (recent touch, brief proximity) | -1 Difficulty; expires after one casting. |

### Collaborative Casting

Two or more mages can cooperate on a single magical working. The mage with the highest Sphere rating leads the casting; each additional mage who has at least 1 dot in the relevant Sphere adds 1 die to the leader's pool (maximum +3 bonus dice from collaboration). The Paradox from the working is divided among all participating mages, reducing the dice each mage adds to their Paradox Pool by 1 (minimum 1 each).

> Example: Three mages collaborate on a Correspondence 3 working to open a permanent portal.
> 
> Maya (Correspondence 3) leads. Vikram (Correspondence 2) assists. Priya (Correspondence 1) assists.
> Maya's base pool: Correspondence 3 + Cunning 3 = 6 dice.
> Vikram adds +1 die (Correspondence 2 >= 1). Priya adds +1 die.
> Total pool: 8 dice.
> 
> The working is Vulgar without Witnesses (the three of them are alone). The Sphere rating is 3.
> Base Paradox: 3 dice. Divided by 3 mages: 1 die each added to their Paradox Pools.

## Dispelling and Counter-Magic

Mages can attempt to unravel another mage's working. This is called Dispelling. To dispel an active magical effect, a mage must:

- Know (or sense) that the effect exists — a Prime 1 effect grants magical awareness automatically.
- Have at least 1 dot in the primary Sphere of the effect they are trying to dispel.
The dispelling mage makes a Casting Roll using their Sphere rating + Resolve (or another appropriate Attribute), against Difficulty equal to the original effect's Sphere rating. On success, the effect is ended. On failure, the effect persists. On disaster, the dispelling mage accidentally feeds the effect extra Quintessence and it becomes more powerful (+1 to its effective Sphere level for the remainder of the scene).

Dispelling an opponent's active working in the same round (counter-magic) is an opposed roll and requires both a Major Action and a clear line of magical perception (usually Prime 1 or relevant Sphere 1).

> Example: Silas's electromagnetic cage (Forces 2 Rote) has trapped Priya inside an energy lattice.
> 
> Rosamund (Forces 1, barely enough) attempts to unravel it.
> Pool: Forces (1) + Resolve (5) = 6 dice. Difficulty 2 (the original effect was Forces 2).
> She rolls: 8, 9, 4, 2, 7, 1. Two hits. Exactly Difficulty 2. Success.
> 
> The lattice dissolves. Priya drops to the floor, gasping.
> 
> Note: Had Rosamund rolled a Disaster, Silas's cage would surge — perhaps dealing 1 Lethal to Priya before Rosamund could try again.

---

## Maintaining and Sustaining Effects

When a mage casts an effect at Concentration duration, they are not done with the spell when the casting roll resolves. The effect is active and real — but it persists only as long as the mage keeps a thread of will, attention, and Quintessence flowing through it. The moment that thread drops, the effect collapses.

### The Concentration Cost — One Minor Action Per Round

Maintaining a Concentration-duration effect requires spending one Minor Action per combat round — or, outside of combat, one meaningful moment of divided attention per scene. That Minor Action represents the mage keeping a portion of their awareness locked onto the sustained effect: continuously re-channeling, adjusting, and holding the pattern in place against the Tapestry's natural tendency to unravel what does not belong.

This Minor Action is unavoidable. It cannot be skipped or abbreviated. If the mage does not spend it — because they were Staggered, Dazed, Incapacitated, or simply chose not to — the effect ends immediately. There is no grace round. The spell drops the instant the mage's attention does.

Outside of combat, the cost is less granular: maintaining a sustained effect through a conversation, a meal, or a brief journey requires periodic re-focusing — the Storyguide may call for a brief check-in each scene, or simply rule that the mage is visibly distracted and socially penalised (−1 die to Social rolls) while holding something active. The Minor Action mechanic becomes relevant again the moment combat begins.

> EXAMPLE — Concentration in combat:
> 
> Kenji has cast a Forces 2 effect that generates a sustained electromagnetic pulse suppressing technology within 5 metres. It is running at Concentration duration.
> 
> Round 1: Kenji's Spotlight arrives. He spends his Minor Action maintaining the pulse (step, draw weapon, or cover would normally go here — he gives that up). His Major Action is a Close Combat attack on the approaching operative.
> 
> Round 2: Kenji is Staggered by a return hit. He loses his next Minor Action. The pulse collapses at the start of Round 3 — not because he was hit, but because the Stagger consumed the Minor Action he needed to sustain it.
> 
> Round 3: Without the pulse, the operative's smartgun comes back online. Kenji must re-cast if he wants the effect back — full SF cost, full casting roll.

### Sustaining Multiple Spells

A mage may sustain more than one Concentration-duration effect simultaneously. This is not forbidden — experienced mages are quite capable of holding several threads at once. But the costs compound directly:

- Each additional sustained effect costs its own Minor Action per round. A mage sustaining two spells spends two Minor Actions. Three spells costs three Minor Actions. Since a Spotlight normally provides one free Minor Action (or two if the mage sacrifices their Major Action entirely), sustaining three or more spells simultaneously effectively locks the mage out of any other supplemental activity.
- Each active sustained effect imposes a cumulative −1 die penalty to all new casting rolls. A mage sustaining two effects rolls −2 dice on any new working. Three effects means −3 dice. This represents the partitioned awareness and divided Quintessential flow — the mage is not fully present in any single working because their attention is distributed across multiple active patterns.
In practice, most mages find two sustained effects to be the comfortable limit. Three is possible but punishing — the mage is spending nearly their entire Spotlight on maintenance, rolling with a significant penalty on anything new, and presenting a high-value disruption target (see below). Four or more sustained effects is theoretically possible but operationally ruinous. A mage holding four active effects in combat is rolling −4 dice to cast anything new, spending all their Minor Actions on maintenance, and one Stagger away from losing everything simultaneously.

> EXAMPLE — Two sustained effects:
> 
> Rosamund is sustaining two spells: a Mind 2 effect reading the surface thoughts of everyone in the room (Concentration), and a Forces 1 effect keeping the lights on despite the blown fuses (Concentration).
> 
> Each round she spends both her Minor Actions on maintenance (step, cover, and draw are all gone). Her Major Action is free — she can still attack, cast, dodge, or take any Major Action.
> 
> But if she wants to cast a third spell, her pool is at −2 dice before she begins. A Forces 3 offensive blast she would normally roll at 8 dice now rolls at 6. And if she is Staggered, she loses a Minor Action — which means one of her two sustained effects drops. Which one drops is her choice, made immediately.

### Disruption and Concentration Checks

Taking damage while sustaining an effect does not automatically drop it. The effect persists unless the mage fails to spend the required Minor Action. However, taking damage while in the middle of casting a new working — or while holding an effect that requires active re-casting (such as a Full-Round working) — does trigger a Concentration roll. See Chapter 7: Combat, Concentration Under Fire, for the full rules on Concentration rolls, Difficulty, and consequences for failure.

Opponents who know a mage is sustaining effects have strong tactical reason to target that mage's Minor Actions specifically — through Stagger, Daze, or any effect that denies Minor Actions. A well-timed Stagger against a mage holding three effects is catastrophically effective: all three drop at once, no saving roll, no partial preservation. This is one of the core reasons experienced Technocracy operatives are trained in disruption protocols.

> EXAMPLE — Disruption via Stagger:
> 
> Yuki is sustaining three effects: a Mind 3 telepathic link to her cabal (Concentration), a Prime 2 Quintessence ward around the door (Concentration), and a Forces 1 sound-dampening field (Concentration).
> 
> An NWO agent fires a burst attack and scores enough hits to trigger a Stagger on Yuki (she loses her next Minor Action).
> 
> Next round: Yuki has no Minor Action available. She cannot spend three Minor Actions she does not have. All three sustained effects collapse simultaneously — the telepathic link goes dark, the ward drops, the sound dampening ends. The Storyguide narrates the alarms going off and her cabal going silent.
> 
> The NWO agent spent one action and erased three active spells. This is why the NWO trains for disruption before targeting.

### Dropping a Sustained Effect Deliberately

A mage can always choose to drop a sustained effect voluntarily. This costs nothing — no action, no roll, no penalty. It simply ends. A mage who decides their wall of fire is no longer serving its purpose can release it as a free action at any point in the round, before or after their other actions. Dropping all sustained effects voluntarily at once is sometimes the correct tactical move — reclaim those Minor Actions and casting dice for something more immediately useful.

---

## Rotes — Prepared Magical Workings

A Rote is a prepared, rehearsed magical working. Unlike improvised freeform casting, a Rote has been developed through practice, refined through repetition, and anchored in the mage's paradigm with a specific set of Instruments, symbolic actions, and internal visualizations. When a mage casts a Rote, they are not inventing the effect from scratch — they are running a deeply grooved mental and physical program that their Avatar and their Will already know by heart.

The mechanical benefit of Rotes is substantial: lower Difficulty (highest Sphere dot only, no penalty for additional Spheres), a Speed Factor reduction, and the ability to use them under stress with fewer penalties. The philosophical benefit is that Rotes express the mage's Tradition and paradigm — a Rote is not just a spell, it is a statement about how the mage understands reality.

### What Makes a Rote

A Rote has three components: a specific effect (defined by its Sphere or Sphere combination and the precise nature of what it does), a set of Instruments (physical or mental foci that the mage uses to cast it), and a paradigmatic frame (the tradition's language for why this works — "the fire obeys my voice," "the electrical differential equalizes through my intent," "the pattern of life flows to where it is needed").

A Rote cannot be a catch-all. "Anything with Forces" is not a Rote. "Redirect an existing fire source to burn in a direction I choose" is a Rote. The specificity is part of what makes it reliable. When a mage devises a Rote, they commit to a defined scope — and that scope is what the reduced Difficulty applies to. Trying to use a Fire Redirect Rote to, say, generate fire from nothing is not covered: the mage would be improvising, and Freeform penalties apply.

### How Rotes Work Mechanically

When casting a Rote:

- Difficulty equals the highest Sphere dot rating involved (not the sum). A Life ●●● Rote has Difficulty 3. A Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●● Rote still has Difficulty 3.
- Speed Factor (SF) = highest Sphere dot − 1 (minimum 1). The same Life ●●● Rote has SF 2; it activates in Phase 3 of the initiative order (see Appendix F).
- Instruments grant +1 Enhancement die to the casting pool when used. Working without Instruments adds +1 Difficulty.
- Rotes can be cast under duress without the standard +1 Difficulty for stress, unless the Storyguide rules the situation is truly extreme (combat, serious injury, active threat to concentration all remain as possible Storyguide discretion penalties).

### Creating and Recording Rotes

Starting mages begin with a number of Rotes equal to their Arete rating. These are recorded in the character's Grimoire (a physical or digital record of their magical workings). A Rote in the Grimoire specifies: the effect, the Sphere(s) and dot levels required, the Instruments used, the paradigmatic frame, and the Difficulty and SF.

Learning a new Rote during play requires: a period of development (at minimum one full session spent practicing the effect), a successful casting of the effect at least three times in varied circumstances, and recording it in the Grimoire. The Storyguide may require additional steps for particularly complex Rotes. A Rote cannot be learned from another mage's Grimoire without first spending time with that mage or being taught — the paradigmatic frame is non-transferable without translation.

Rotes can be lost. If a mage's Grimoire is destroyed and they have not memorized the Rote deeply enough (Storyguide discretion based on how long they have used it), the Rote degrades to Freeform over time. Senior mages know their Rotes will outlast their Grimoires.

> EXAMPLE: Rosamund has used her Correspondence 2 Ward Rote for five years. It is as natural to her as breathing. The Storyguide rules it is memorized: even if her Grimoire burned tomorrow, the Rote would persist.
> 
> Compare: Yuki learned a new Mind 3 Rote last session from a recovered Hermetic text. If the text is destroyed before she has practiced it extensively, the Storyguide may rule she must re-learn it from scratch.

---

SIDEBAR — When to Use a Rote vs. Freeform Casting
====================================================

One of the most common questions new mages ask their Mentors — and new players ask their Storyguides — is: "When should I cast a Rote instead of just improvising?" The answer depends on the situation, and experienced mages learn to read those situations instinctively.

**Use a Rote when:**

You need RELIABILITY. A Rote reduces Difficulty to the highest Sphere dot involved (rather than adding all Sphere dots together). If your most-used combat effect is Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●●, freeform casting has Difficulty 5. That same effect as a Rote has Difficulty 3. Under pressure, that gap is the difference between success and catastrophe.

You need SPEED. Rote Speed Factor = highest Sphere dot − 1 (minimum 1). Freeform SF = the sum of all Sphere dots. A Forces ●●● / Correspondence ●● Rote activates at SF 2 (Phase 3 of initiative). The same improvised effect activates at SF 4 (Phase 5). In a firefight, acting earlier can mean the Technocrat does not get a shot off at all.

You are under STRESS. Freeform casting under physical duress adds +1 Difficulty (injured, distracted, in danger). Rotes are rehearsed to the point of reflex and carry no stress penalty unless the Storyguide rules the situation is truly extreme. A mage who knows their Rotes can keep casting through chaos that would paralyze a less disciplined practitioner.

You want to MINIMIZE PARADOX RISK. Both Rotes and freeform effects that qualify as Vulgar generate Paradox, but a lower-Difficulty Rote is more likely to succeed on the first roll — and a single success avoids Botches, which generate additional Paradox. A mage with a refined Rote generates less Paradox over time simply because they fail less often.

**Use Freeform when:**

The situation demands FLEXIBILITY. Rotes are specific. "Electrify the water in this room" is a Rote. "Electrify this specific piece of machinery in a way that overloads only its left output circuit and leaves its main board intact" may go beyond what the Rote covers. Freeform lets the mage shape the effect precisely to circumstances that no Rote could anticipate.

The target effect is NOVEL. If you have never done something before — a new Sphere combination, an improvised solution to an unexpected problem, a creative synthesis under time pressure — there is no Rote. You improvise. That is what freeform casting is for.

You have TIME and HIGH ARETE. At high Arete ratings (4+), freeform casting difficulty becomes more manageable, and the extended ritual system (see Appendix F) lets mages roll multiple times to accumulate hits, making even difficult freeform effects achievable. A high-Arete mage working an extended ritual freeform is often MORE effective than a lower-Arete mage using a Rote.

Your PARADIGM FORBIDS the structure. Some mages — particularly Chaos Mages, Hollow Ones, and certain Verbena — find that rigidly structured Rotes actually conflict with their understanding of magic. A Chaos Mage may refuse to use a Rote on philosophical grounds: "If I already know what the magic is going to do, it isn't alive anymore." Freeform is not just a mechanical choice — it is sometimes a character choice.

**The Practical Rule of Thumb:**

If you have a Rote for it — use the Rote. If you do not — improvise, but know your Paradox risk. Experienced mages maintain a library of Rotes for their most common effects and reserve freeform for the unexpected. The battle-tested formula is: *Rotes for the routine, Freeform for the moment.*

> EXAMPLE — Same Mage, Two Approaches:
>
> Esperanza (Verbena, Life ●●●, Prime ●●) faces a wounded ally in a firefight. She has two options:
>
> OPTION A — ROTE: Her "Canticle of Rapid Mending" (Life ●●●) Rote heals one level of Lethal damage. Difficulty 3, SF 2. She rolls her Resolve + Life pool at Difficulty 3. Clean, fast, reliable. She can do this while staying in cover.
>
> OPTION B — FREEFORM: She improvises a broader healing effect, trying to restore both Lethal AND Bashing damage simultaneously AND suppress the poison affecting her ally. This would require Life ●●●● effects (healing aggravated isn't on the table, but the scope expansion matters). Difficulty rises. SF rises. Paradox risk rises. But the effect could do far more if she succeeds.
>
> The ally has four Lethal wounds and one Bashing wound and is about to pass out. The poison is slow-acting. In this moment, the Rote wins: get them stable NOW. The poison can be treated later with a deliberate ritual.
>
> The lesson: when survival is the question, Rotes provide the answer. When nuance is the question, Freeform opens the door.

**A Note for Storyguides:**

Encourage players to develop Rotes for their mage's signature effects. A mage's Rote list is a portrait of who they are — what they have practiced, what they believe in, what they reach for under pressure. When a player names their Rote and describes its Instruments and paradigmatic frame, reward that investment narratively. The Etherite who has a Rote called "Galvanic Restoration Field (v.3)" is telling you something about their character that "I heal with Life 3" never could.

At the same time, do not let Rotes become a comfort blanket that removes tension. Situations should arise where the Rote does not fit — where improvisation is required. That gap is where character emerges.

---

# Chapter Five: Paradox — The Price of Power

Magic has a cost. Not in blood or mana — the Consensus itself fights back. When a mage's Will is too naked, too large, too defiant of what the majority of humanity believes is possible, reality notices and retaliates. This retaliation is called Paradox.

In this system, Paradox is a dice pool that grows as the mage defies the Consensus. The pool does not immediately hurt you — but it hangs over every session like a storm cloud. When Paradox triggers, it rolls, and the dice read like an augury of calamity.

## The Paradox Pool

Every mage has a Paradox Pool that starts at 0 each chronicle (though some beginning scenarios may start mages with a few dice). The pool is a collection of d10s that grows each time the mage performs Vulgar magic. Dice in the pool do not go away on their own — they must be deliberately burned off through ritual cleansing, extended rest in a Node, or the Storyguide triggering a Paradox event that "spends" some of them.

### Adding to the Pool

The Paradox Pool gains dice in the following situations:

- Vulgar magic without witnesses: +1 die per level of the highest Sphere used (minimum 1).
- Vulgar magic with Sleeper witnesses: +2 dice per level of the highest Sphere used (minimum 2).
- Magical Botch (0 hits AND more than half all dice — casting pool + Paradox pool — show 1): +2 dice to the Paradox Pool immediately. See Chapter Four: Magic for the full Botch rule and Paradox dice interaction.
- Attempting to break a truly fundamental law (causing death from nothing, violating Prime causality without Prime 5): +1 die at Storyguide discretion.
- Paradox Spirits targeting the mage (see below): the Spirit may add dice as part of its attack.
> Example — Pool growth:
> 
> Kaito (NWO, Mind 3) blasts a barroom full of Sleepers with a Wave of Confusion — a vulgar mental attack that causes everyone in the room to forget the last two minutes.
> 
> He is in a room with approximately twenty Sleeper witnesses.
> The effect uses Mind 3. Vulgar with Witnesses: +2 dice × 3 Sphere levels = +6 dice to Kaito's Paradox Pool.
> 
> Before this scene, Kaito had 3 dice in his pool. He now has 9.
> "Control that feeling," Kaito thinks. "The math is still good."
> The math is not good.

## Paradox Dice and the Botch Threshold

The relationship between Paradox and Botches is circular and intentional: Paradox makes Botches more likely, and Botches generate more Paradox. A mage who allows their Paradox Pool to grow large enough is not just risking a Paradox event — they are actively destabilizing every future magical roll they make.

When determining whether a magical roll has Botched, the 1s from the Paradox Pool dice are counted alongside the 1s from the casting pool. The two pools are rolled separately (Paradox dice do not contribute hits), but their 1s combine for the Botch threshold check.

Concretely: if a mage has a 6-die casting pool and a 4-die Paradox Pool, they are effectively rolling 10 dice for the purpose of the Botch threshold. A Botch requires more than half of all 10 dice — at least 6 dice — to show 1. That threshold is dramatically more accessible than the same mage with no Paradox (where 4 out of 6 = 67% required).

| Casting Pool / Paradox Pool | Total Dice / 1s Needed for Botch (given 0 hits) |
| --- | --- |
| 6 casting / 0 Paradox | 6 total — at least 4 ones needed (67%) |
| 6 casting / 2 Paradox | 8 total — at least 5 ones needed (63%) |
| 6 casting / 4 Paradox | 10 total — at least 6 ones needed (60%) |
| 6 casting / 6 Paradox | 12 total — at least 7 ones needed (58%) |
| 6 casting / 8 Paradox | 14 total — at least 8 ones needed (57%) |

The pattern is clear: each Paradox die added to the pool reduces the absolute number of 1s needed from the casting pool to trigger a Botch. A mage with 8 Paradox dice could Botch on just 2 casting-pool 1s (if the Paradox pool also contributes 6 ones) — whereas a clean mage with the same casting pool needs 4 casting-pool 1s.

This is why veteran mages treat Paradox management as a core survival skill, not an afterthought. The Paradox Pool is not merely a resource tracking your "debt" to reality. It is a loaded gun sitting inside every casting roll, growing more dangerous with each die added.

> PARADOX POOL ESCALATION EXAMPLE
> 
> Takeshi has accumulated 5 Paradox dice over the course of a hard session (two Vulgar-with-Witnesses effects and one earlier Botch). He now attempts a Forces ●●●● freeform attack — a lightning bolt in an enclosed space, clearly Vulgar.
> 
> Casting pool: 7 dice (Forces 4 + Dexterity 3).
> Paradox pool: 5 dice.
> Total dice for Botch threshold: 12.
> Botch threshold: more than 6 ones across all 12 dice.
> 
> Takeshi rolls the casting pool: 1, 1, 1, 2, 4, 5, 7. Zero hits. Three ones.
> Paradox pool: 1, 1, 1, 3, 6. Three ones.
> Total ones: 6. Threshold: more than 6 = at least 7. Six does not exceed 6.5. NOT a Botch.
> 
> ...barely. One more 1 on either pool and this becomes catastrophic.
> 
> The roll fails. The lightning bolt does not fire. And because it was Vulgar (regardless of failure), the Paradox Pool gains +4 dice (2 dice × Force 4 for Vulgar without Witnesses — he is in an empty building).
> 
> Takeshi now has 9 Paradox dice. His next Botch threshold on a 7-die pool will be:
> 16 total dice. More than 8 ones needed. He needs just 1 from his casting pool if the Paradox pool contributes 8.
> 
> "I should probably find a Node," Takeshi thinks.
> Yes. He really should.

## When Paradox Triggers

The Paradox Pool is rolled in the following circumstances:

- At the end of each scene in which the mage used Vulgar magic.
- Whenever the pool reaches 7 or more dice (triggered immediately on reaching that threshold).
- When a Paradox Spirit deliberately "calls the debt" (rare, but possible — see Spirit Paradox below).
- When the Storyguide spends Storyguide Momentum to trigger a Paradox event for dramatic effect.
When triggered, roll ALL dice in the Paradox Pool. Do not count hits normally. Instead, read the dice for specific result categories:

## Reading the Paradox Dice

This is the creative heart of the Paradox system. When the pool rolls, each die falls into a category based on its face value:

```
Paradox Dice Results
--------------------
1 — The Unraveling: The mage's last Vulgar effect is partially undone. One aspect of the effect (Storyguide's choice) reverses or fails. If multiple 1s appear, the undoing is total.

2 — Quiet Whisper: Add 1 box to the mage's Quiet Track. If the Quiet Track fills, a Quiet episode begins (see Quiet, below).

3 — Backlash Wound: The mage suffers 1 Bashing damage per 3 rolled. Multiple 3s stack.

4 — Sensory Bleed: The mage experiences a vivid hallucination tied to their paradigm — lasting one scene. Mechanically: -1 die to all actions this scene as their senses lie.

5 — Paradox Flare: The mage's next magical effect, whether they intend it or not, is automatically Vulgar and adds +1 die to the pool when it resolves. The mage cannot make it Coincidental until this clears.

6-7 — Backwash: The current Vulgar effect has a twisted side-effect of the Storyguide's invention. The magic worked, but it brought something unexpected.

8 — Paradox Drain: Remove 2 dice from the Paradox Pool. Reality partially "forgives" a small piece of the debt — perhaps the effect was less witnessed than thought, or the mage's paradigm was unusually coherent.

9 — Spirit Touch: A Paradox Spirit is drawn toward the mage (see below). The Spirit may manifest fully within 1-3 scenes.

10 — Catastrophe: Choose one: the mage suffers 2 Aggravated damage, OR a Major Paradox Backlash occurs (Storyguide invents something terrible and permanent about the world that the mage caused), OR 3 dice are added to the pool.
```

After rolling the Paradox Pool, all 6-7 results are consumed (the pool loses 1 die per Backwash result). All 8 results drain 2 dice each. All other results persist — the pool retains its current size minus the consumed dice.

### Clearing the Paradox Pool

The pool does not reduce on its own except through triggered results. Mages can actively reduce their pools through:

- Spending a full scene in a Node (a place of concentrated Quintessence) meditating: -1 die from pool.
- A full week of magical inactivity and mundane living: -1 die from pool.
- A cleansing ritual with at least one other mage (Prime 2 required): -2 dice from pool per hour-long ritual.
- Deliberate Paradox Expenditure: A mage may intentionally "burn off" their Paradox Pool by inviting a Backlash — they roll the pool voluntarily, accept all consequences, and reduce the pool to 0. This is dangerous but sometimes necessary.

## The Quiet Track and Quiet Episodes

Every mage has a Quiet Track with five boxes. Quiet represents the accumulation of metaphysical instability — the growing gap between the mage's personal reality and the Consensus, and between the mage's inner world and their outer perception. Too much Quiet and the mage begins to lose their grip on what is real.

### Filling the Track

Quiet boxes are added through:

- Paradox dice showing 2 (Quiet Whisper): +1 box.
- A Paradox episode that goes badly unresolved: +1-2 boxes (Storyguide discretion).
- Extended isolation from the Consensus (days alone in the spirit world, deep in a Node, etc.): +1 box per 48 hours.
- Witnessing reality-breaking events while having no framework to process them: +1 box (Storyguide discretion, used sparingly).
| Quiet Track Level | Effect on the Mage |
| --- | --- |
| 1 box — Static | Minor perceptual glitches. The mage sees things out of the corner of their eye. No mechanical penalty, but the Storyguide should describe small wrongnesses. |
| 2 boxes — Distortion | The mage's paradigm leaks into their perception. A Hermetic might see mathematical formulae overlaid on everything; a Verbena might hear the land speaking. -1 die on all Empathy rolls (difficulty reading what is real vs. paradigmatic overlay). |
| 3 boxes — Unmooring | The mage is clearly Not All There. Other mages can sense the Quiet on them. -1 die on all Social rolls with Sleepers; they feel something is wrong. Storyguide may invoke one brief hallucinatory scene per session. |
| 4 boxes — Breaking | The mage experiences regular involuntary episodes. Once per session, the Storyguide may trigger a Quiet vision mid-scene; the mage is effectively Confused (loses Major Action) for one round as they respond to something that is not there. -2 dice on all rolls until a box is cleared. |
| 5 boxes — Quiet | Full Quiet. The mage is lost in their inner reality. They may function, but they are no longer fully in the same world as their companions. They become an NPC for the duration of the Quiet episode (usually 1 scene to 1 session). They may, however, cast magic freely — without Paradox — because their Quiet world is internally consistent. Marauder-adjacent behavior. |

### Resolving Quiet

Quiet boxes are removed through:

- Meaningful human connection (a true conversation, a reconciliation, a shared meal): -1 box per scene, once per session.
- Successfully completing a goal that reconnects the mage to their paradigm: -1-2 boxes.
- Extended time in normal Consensus reality (a full week of mundane life): -1 box.
- The Verbena tradition has specific rites for clearing Quiet in others; so do the Celestial Chorus.

## Paradox Backlash: The Full Table

When a 10 appears in a Paradox roll (Catastrophe), or when the Storyguide invokes a Major Backlash, consult the following for inspiration. These are not random roll results — the Storyguide selects the one most dramatically appropriate for the character and scene. The mage's paradigm should flavor every backlash.

| Backlash Type | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Sphere Feedback | The mage's affected Sphere "misfires" for a scene: all Casting Rolls with that Sphere take +1 Difficulty. At higher Quiet levels, the Sphere may be temporarily locked. |
| Reality Scar | The area where the magic was cast carries a minor but permanent mark — plants die, electronics glitch, the temperature drops 5 degrees. Other mages can sense it. |
| Temporal Hiccup | Time stutters around the mage. They experience 1-10 seconds of lost time per 10 rolled. Actions taken during that time are retroactively decided by the Storyguide. |
| Paradigm Bleed | The mage's effects begin looking like a different tradition's magic for the scene. A Virtual Adept's effects start looking mystical; a Verbena's look technological. Enemies may misidentify them. |
| Quintessence Hemorrhage | Raw magical energy bleeds from the mage visibly. Sleepers see glowing cracks in their skin, sparks from their eyes, an aura of wrong light. Extremely vulgar until the scene ends. |
| Twist | The mage's last Vulgar effect manifests, but with a cruel irony baked in. The door they opened leads somewhere else. The target they healed becomes allergic to mundane medicine. |
| Paradox Spirit Summons | Automatically draws a Paradox Spirit (see below) that manifests fully within moments, not scenes. |
| Aggravated Wound | The mage suffers 1-3 Aggravated damage. This cannot be soaked by mundane Armor. |

## Paradox Spirits

Paradox Spirits are manifestations of the Consensus's immune system — semi-sentient entities that exist to punish and correct mages who tear too large a hole in reality's fabric. They are not evil, but they are implacable and they are smart enough to know which leverage points will hurt a mage most.

### Types of Paradox Spirits

| Spirit Type | Behavior and Specialty |
| --- | --- |
| Banality Hound | Hunts mages who use magic to avoid mundane consequences. Attacks with forced mundanity — magic fails in its presence (Difficulty +1 to all Casting Rolls while the Hound is within 10 meters). |
| Witness | A Paradox Spirit that only watches — but everything it observes is reported to... something. Having a Witness in a scene means the next Paradox trigger is more severe. |
| Scourge | An aggressive Spirit that attacks the mage physically. Deals Aggravated damage (Might 4, Stamina 4, attacks with Close Combat + Might, pool 8 dice, Damage 3). |
| Wyrm-Whisper | Subtle, psychological. Implants false memories, deepens Quiet, and makes the mage doubt their own Paradigm. Attacks through opposed Mind + Resolve rolls; success adds 1 Quiet box. |
| Reality Anchor | Latches onto the mage and prevents further Vulgar magic. All Vulgar attempts automatically become Vulgar-with-Witnesses in terms of Paradox generation until the Anchor is dispelled (Spirit + Resolve, Difficulty 3, opposed by Anchor's Stamina 5). |

### Dealing with Paradox Spirits

Paradox Spirits are Spirit-world entities (Spirit Sphere to perceive and communicate with them), but their physical manifestations can be fought with mundane means — they just regenerate quickly. The best solutions:

- Negotiation (Spirit + Composure vs. Spirit Resolve 4): Some Spirits will accept alternative punishments — a mage choosing to suffer 2 Aggravated rather than losing their magic for a scene. They are implacable but not unreasonable.
- Banishment (Prime 3 or Spirit 3 working, Difficulty 3): Force the Spirit back to its realm. Does not prevent it from returning if the Paradox Pool remains high.
- Reduce the Paradox Pool: Spirits typically leave on their own when the pool drops below 3 dice.
- Full Quiet: If the mage enters Full Quiet (5 boxes), Paradox Spirits lose interest — a mage in their own personal reality is no longer a threat to the Consensus. This is cold comfort.

## Marauder Magic and Paradox

Marauders — mages consumed by Full Quiet — generate no Paradox at all. Their magic is internally consistent with their personal reality, and the Consensus cannot find an angle from which to punish it.
