> The NWO interrogator has a piece of information Sasha needs — the location of a safehouse.  The interrogator has been conditioned against telepathic probing (he has a basic mental shield, which Sasha must overcome).  Sasha does not have time for subtlety.
> 
> Pool: Mind ●●●● (4) + Resolve ●●●● (4) = 8 dice.  SF: 4 (Freeform).  Difficulty: 4 (base, matching Mind ●●●●'s casting scope — this does not change because of the shield; see Opposition, below).  Opposition: the interrogator's Mental Shield (Mind 1, per the Mental Shield Rote above) banked 1 hit on the casting that raised it. Per that Rote's own rule, a Mental Shield never adds to the intruder's Difficulty or SF — Sasha's hits must simply exceed the Shield's 1 banked hit for the intrusion to get through at all; if they don't, the compulsion fails outright and the interrogator immediately senses the attempt.
> Roll: [10, 9, 8, 7, 5, 3, 2, 1] = 6 hits (10 = 2).  Sasha's 6 hits comfortably exceed the Shield's 1 banked hit — the shield fails to stop her.  Per the Mental Shield Rote, the interrogator would only have sensed the attempt if his own shield-casting had scored 3+ hits; his banked 1 hit falls well short of that, so he notices nothing.  Against her own base Difficulty 4, that leaves 2 net hits for the compulsion itself.  The compulsion takes hold: for the next hour, the interrogator believes he has already given the safehouse location willingly and feels no conflict about it.  He tells Sasha the address without hesitation.
> Result: Implants a single complex belief or command that overrides the target's free will for up to one hour.  The target acts on the compulsion as if it were their own idea.  After the effect ends, they may notice a gap in their memory (Intellect + Awareness, Difficulty 4, to notice).
> VULGAR against a resisting target.  Even against a willing one, altering conscious thought is not coincidental.  Sasha does not perform this lightly — it leaves psychic fingerprints that a skilled Mind mage can detect.
> 
> FREE HIT SPEND: 2 free hits.  First hit extends duration to 24 hours (the interrogator continues to believe he acted voluntarily, giving no alert to his superiors).  Second hit scrubs the memory entirely — no gap to notice, no Intellect roll possible.

> MIND ●●●●● FREEFORM — MASS BROADCAST
> 
> The Technocracy is rolling out a new Consensus-reinforcement broadcast through the local cell network.  It will dampen Awakening potential for everyone in a six-block radius.  Sasha cannot stop the signal — but he can counter it.  He will broadcast a single, wordless emotional state to every person in the area: wonder.  The specific flavour of wonder that precedes questioning what you have been told to believe.
> 
> Pool: Mind ●●●●● (5) + Resolve ●●●● (4) = 9 dice.  SF: 5 (freeform).  Difficulty: 5.
> Roll: [10, 9, 9, 8, 7, 5, 3, 2, 1] = 7 hits (each 10 = 2), 2 net hits.  The broadcast radiates outward — not through technology but through the Tapestry itself.  Every person within a 500m radius experiences a sudden, sourceless moment of open curiosity.  Some stop walking.  Some put down their phones.  For about ninety seconds, the Technocracy's reinforcement signal has nothing to reinforce: the minds it targets are briefly, genuinely elsewhere.
> Result: Broadcasts a single emotion or simple (non-verbal) impulse to every sapient being within Extreme range (500m+, scaled to ●●●●●).  Cannot convey complex thoughts — only raw emotional states or simple urges (stop, look, feel joy, feel fear).  Duration: one turn per net hit (2 turns here, roughly 90 seconds).
> HIGHLY VULGAR — no natural phenomenon makes hundreds of people simultaneously experience the same emotion.  Any Technocracy instruments monitoring the area will detect the mental resonance spike immediately.  Sasha performs this from a rooftop, using a prepared ritual chalk array as a focus.  He is moving before the effect even settles.
> 
> FREE HIT SPEND: 2 free hits.  First hit adds a secondary emotional layer — the wonder is accompanied by a vague sense of being watched over, which subtly undermines the alienating effect of the Technocracy's broadcast.  Second hit collapses the duration into a single overwhelming instant — 90 seconds compressed into 3 seconds, a flash of pure wonder that is harder for the Technocracy to trace because it is already over.

## Prime — The Sphere of Quintessence and Magic Itself

Prime is the most fundamental Sphere because it deals with the raw substance of magic itself — Quintessence, the divine fire that underlies all things. Every other Sphere works by reshaping patterns of Quintessence. Prime lets the mage perceive, channel, store, and ultimately create or destroy those patterns directly.

Tradition Affinity: Order of Hermes, Celestial Chorus. Technocracy Affinity: None officially — Technocrats deny Quintessence exists. However, their paradigm includes what they call "Dynamic Energy" and "Primal Utility," which are mechanically identical.

## Prime Dot Levels

### Prime 1 — Sense Quintessence

The mage perceives the flow and concentration of Quintessence in all things. They can see magical auras, identify Nodes (places of concentrated magical energy), detect active enchantments, and sense when another mage has cast recently.

**Passive Sense:**

The mage can always perceive magical effects, enchanted objects, Nodes, and active castings. This is the most important passive sense in the game — a mage without Prime 1 is operationally blind to the magical world.

#### Sphere Tricks — Prime 1

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Aura Read (1) | You see the complete magical history of an object or the current magical state of a mage. This reveals Sphere ratings (roughly), recent castings, and Paradox levels. |
| Trace Magic (1) | You can follow the magical "scent" of a specific mage through areas they have cast in, up to 24 hours after the casting. |
| Node Sense (2) | You know the exact location, strength, and available Quintessence of all Nodes within Extreme range (1km). |

### Prime 2 — Channel Quintessence

The mage can channel Quintessence from Nodes, Wonders, and their own reserves, using it to empower magical effects, create Tass (physical solidified Quintessence), or grant temporary magical charge to enchanted objects.

**Active Effects (Freeform):**

- Draw Quintessence from a Node to reduce Paradox Pool dice (1 Quintessence = 1 die removed, maximum 3 per scene).
- Charge an object with Quintessence, making it a Wonder temporarily (see Wonders section).
- Create Tass — physical Quintessence that others can use.

#### Sphere Tricks — Prime 2

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Draw Quintessence (1) | You pull Quintessence from an available Node. Remove 1 die from your Paradox Pool. Requires being at or near the Node. |
| Fuel the Fires (2) | You empower an ally's next Casting Roll with raw Quintessence. They gain Enhancement +2 on that roll. |
| Enchant Object (2) | You charge one object with Quintessence. It gains the tag [Magical] and can be used as a focus for magic with +1 Enhancement for the scene. |
| Create Tass (2) | You solidify Quintessence into a small physical object (~50g). This Tass can be consumed by another mage later to remove 2 dice from their Paradox Pool. |

### Prime 3 — Create and Destroy Patterns

Now the mage can create Quintessence from nothing (very Vulgar) or destroy patterns entirely — unraveling magical effects, destroying enchanted objects, and most significantly, creating new magical patterns that persist independently.

**Active Effects (Freeform):**

- Create raw Quintessence from nothing. Extremely Vulgar; generates +3 dice Paradox automatically.
- Destroy any magical effect by unraveling its Quintessence pattern (enhanced Dispel: +3 to dispelling rolls).
- Create Wonders — persistent enchanted objects that work without the mage present.
- Create a ward that prevents Quintessence from flowing into an area (blocks Nodes from being tapped; prevents magical healing).

#### Sphere Tricks — Prime 3

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Unravel (2) | Permanently destroy one magical effect or enchanted item. No dispelling roll required for effects of Sphere rating lower than your Prime. |
| Prime Shield (2) | Create a ward that absorbs the next 3 hits of Paradox backlash that would affect any target within Short range (up to 20m). The shield collapses once spent. |
| Wonder-Craft (ext.) | Begin an extended ritual (Threshold 10+) to create a Wonder. See Wonder rules below. |

### Prime 4 — Advanced Quintessence

The mage can permanently alter the Quintessence flow of an area, create or destroy Nodes, and grant the Awakening to others (a rare, morally complex ability that requires the target to be spiritually ready — a mage cannot simply Awaken an unwilling Sleeper).

#### Sphere Tricks — Prime 4

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Kindle the Spark (Special) | Under precisely the right narrative conditions (Storyguide discretion), you can trigger another's Awakening. This requires Prime 4 + a scene-length ritual + the target being on the cusp of Awakening narratively. |
| Node Creation (Extended) | You create a small Node (Quintessence 3 per day) in a prepared location. Threshold 20; requires Prime 4 + Spirit 3 + days of ritual work. |
| Drain Node (3) | You permanently destroy a Node by siphoning all its Quintessence. The Node becomes a dead place. This action is not reversible without years of magical work. |
| Quintessence Reservoir (3) | Store up to [hits × 3] points of Quintessence in a created Periapt or prepared object. The reservoir can be accessed by anyone you designate — useful for fueling a team of mages. |
| Pattern Armor (2) | Infuse your own pattern with raw Quintessence, granting a magical Soak of [hits] against all damage types including Aggravated, for one scene. Unlike physical armor, this works against pattern-level attacks. |
| Unweave Permanent (4) | Dispel a permanently enchanted object, sustained ward, or self-sustaining magical effect. These normally cannot be unwoven — Prime 4 makes it possible. Opposed roll against the original casting's effective hits. |
| Imbue Quintessence (2) | Transfer Quintessence directly into another mage's reserves, a Tass object, or a Node, charging it beyond its normal capacity temporarily. Each hit transferred adds 1 point above the recipient's normal maximum for one scene. |

### Prime 5 — Prime Mastery

The mage exists in direct communion with the pattern-nature of reality. They can rewrite the fundamental rules of how magic works in a local area, create self-sustaining magical systems without ongoing maintenance, and ultimately touch the Great Consensus — the source of all Quintessence.

#### Sphere Tricks — Prime 5

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Rewrite Magic Laws (5) | For one scene, in an area up to Medium range (up to 60m) across, you redefine how magic works: choose one rule of the Sphere system and invert or alter it (all Vulgar magic becomes Coincidental; all Forces magic costs double; all spells gain +2 hits). Paradox +5. Chronicle-event level. |
| Self-Sustaining System (4) | Create a magical effect that perpetuates itself without ongoing maintenance or Quintessence input. The effect continues until dispelled. Maximum complexity: any Effect you could create in one scene. Paradox +3. |
| Quintessence Absorption (3) | Permanently convert a Tass object, Node, or free Quintessence zone into personal fuel — siphoning all available Quintessence into your reserves instantly. No casting roll per batch after the first. |
| Null Zone (4) | In a zone up to Short-to-Medium range (20m) across, all magic is suppressed — no new Effects can be created or sustained, existing Effects are dispelled, and Paradox dice cannot accumulate. Duration: one scene. Paradox +4 (from the paradox of suppressing paradox). |
| Pattern Genesis (5) | Create a new, persistent Pattern — an object, a creature, a place — from pure Quintessence and will. This is the mage as sub-creator. Requires Spirit 4 for living Patterns, Matter 5 for objects. Paradox +5. |

### Worked Examples — Prime

> PRIME ● ROTE — SENSE QUINTESSENCE (Celestial Chorus)
> 
> Brother Thomas (Celestial Chorus, Prime ●●●, Resolve ●●●●) enters an unfamiliar city neighbourhood and wants to know whether any Nodes, Tass, or active magical workings are nearby before he sets up a safe house.
> 
> Pool: Prime ● (1) + Resolve ●●●● (4) = 5 dice.  SF: 1 (Rote: highest dot 1 − 1 = 0, minimum 1).  Difficulty: 1.
> Roll: [9, 8, 6, 4, 2] = 3 hits, 2 net.  Thomas closes his eyes for a moment of quiet prayer.  He senses a faint Node three blocks west — a church garden, small but real.  A Technocracy Reality Anchor is active two streets north, its Quintessence-dampening field humming at the edge of his awareness.
> Result: Detects all significant Quintessence sources (Nodes, Tass, enchanted objects) and active magical workings within Short range (up to 20m).  Does not reveal the nature of the magic — only its presence and rough power level.
> COINCIDENTAL — reads as deep spiritual attentiveness, which it is.  No Paradox risk.

> PRIME ●● ROTE — DRAW QUINTESSENCE (Celestial Chorus)
> 
> Brother Thomas (Celestial Chorus, Prime ●●●, Resolve ●●●●) enters a Node and draws on its radiance in prayer.
> 
> Pool: Prime ●● (2) + Resolve ●●●● (4) = 6 dice.  SF: 1 (Rote).  Difficulty: 2.
> Roll: [9, 7, 6, 5, 2, 1] = 3 hits, 1 net.  Quintessence flows.
> Result: Thomas gains 3 Quintessence (1 per hit, up to his Avatar rating maximum).
> COINCIDENTAL — prayer at a power site is explicable.  No Paradox.

> PRIME ●●● FREEFORM — PRIME WARD
> 
> Brother Thomas raises a ward to disrupt incoming magical effects for the scene.
> 
> Pool: Prime ●●● (3) + Resolve ●●●● (4) = 7 dice.  SF: 3.  Difficulty: 3.  Costs 1 Quintessence to prime the ward.
> Roll: [10, 8, 7, 6, 5, 3, 2] = 5 hits (10 = 2), 2 net.  The ward settles into place.
> Result: Any magical effect targeting Thomas or allies in the same room must meet or beat 2 hits on its own roll to penetrate the ward.  Lasts the scene.
> VULGAR — a visible halo of golden light surrounds the group (visible to Awakened).  With no Sleeper witnesses: minimal Paradox.  In public: Paradox roll immediately.

> PRIME ●●●● FREEFORM — DISENCHANT
> 
> An enemy mage has crafted a Wonder — a prototype Reality Emitter that passively suppresses coincidental magic in a two-block radius.  The device is protected by a Prime Ward (Prime ●●●).  Brother Thomas must dismantle both the ward and the device's enchantment before the cabal can operate freely.
> 
> Pool: Prime ●●●● (4) + Resolve ●●●● (4) = 8 dice.  SF: 4 (Freeform).  Difficulty: 4 (base, matching Prime ●●●●'s casting scope — this does not change because of the Ward; see Opposition, below).  Opposition: the Prime Ward (rating 3) protecting the device.  A Ward never inflates the caster's Difficulty or SF — it contests directly, on its own separate roll of dice equal to its rating, exactly as described under the Prime ●●● Ward Trick above. Thomas's hits must exceed the Ward's hits before any of his hits can affect the Wonder underneath.
> Opposed Roll: Thomas rolls his full 8-dice pool: [10, 9, 8, 7, 5, 3, 2, 1] = 6 hits (10 = 2).  The Ward rolls its rating (3 dice): [8, 5, 2] = 1 hit.  Thomas's hits exceed the Ward's by 5 (6 − 1 = 5) — the Ward collapses.  He turns 2 of those 5 net hits against the Wonder itself, stripping 2 Enhancement points permanently, and holds the remaining 3 in reserve for the Free Hit Spend below.
> Result: Dismantles active magical workings.  Each net hit removes one Enhancement point or one active enchantment from the target.  Against a Ward, the Ward must be overcome first (a separate opposed roll, never a Difficulty or SF penalty) before the Wonder beneath can be reached.
> VULGAR — the disenchantment is visible to Awakened sight as Quintessence threads unravelling.  To Sleepers it looks like the device simply powers down.  SF 4 means moderate Paradox risk; Thomas conducts this as a formal prayer-exorcism, which his tradition's Focus allows.
> 
> FREE HIT SPEND: The 3 remaining net hits strip the device of its remaining Enhancement entirely — the Reality Emitter is now an inert, non-magical piece of electronics that the cabal can safely discard.

> PRIME ●●●●● FREEFORM — WEAVE TASS
> 
> The Chantry's Quintessence reserves are critically low after sustaining a Paradox storm.  Brother Thomas needs to create physical Tass — raw, storable Quintessence in solid form — to replenish the cabal's reserves.  He cannot draw on a Node (there is none accessible).  He will weave it from ambient Quintessence threads in the Tapestry itself.
> 
> Pool: Prime ●●●●● (5) + Resolve ●●●● (4) = 9 dice.  SF: 5 (freeform).  Difficulty: 5.
> Roll: [10, 9, 9, 8, 7, 5, 3, 2, 1] = 7 hits (10 = 2), 2 net hits.  Thomas spends an hour in deep prayer, drawing ambient Quintessence from the Tapestry and condensing it into a physical form — in his case, small wafers of golden bread that glow faintly to Awakened sight.  Each wafer holds 1 point of Quintessence.  He creates 5 wafers (base 3 from effect + 2 from net hits).
> Result: Creates physical Tass from ambient Quintessence.  Base yield: Prime dots (5) ÷ 2 = 3 points, rounded down.  Each net hit adds +1.  Total: 5 Tass.  The Tass holds indefinitely and can be used by any mage as a Quintessence source.
> HIGHLY VULGAR — creating matter ex nihilo from magical energy is one of the most overt magical acts possible.  Witnesses see golden light coalesce into physical objects.  SF 5 is maximum Paradox pressure.  Thomas performs this in the Chantry's sanctified inner room with every ward and protection the cabal can muster.  Even so, the act leaves a Quintessence signature that a skilled Prime-user could track for days.
> 
> FREE HIT SPEND: 2 free hits.  First hit makes the Tass sympathetically linked to the Chantry's prime ward — the wafers passively reinforce the Chantry's wards while stored inside it.  Second hit gives the Tass a secondary property: any mage who consumes one recovers 1 Quintessence AND gains one free hit on their next Sphere roll (a brief resonance boost).

## Spirit — The Sphere of the Invisible World

Everything has a spirit. Every rock, every tree, every parking garage and corporate server farm has a counterpart in the spirit world called the Penumbra — a reflection of the material world, twisted by meaning and metaphor. Spirit mages learn to see that world, enter it, speak to its inhabitants, and ultimately reshape the membrane between the worlds.

Tradition Affinity: Dreamspeakers, Verbena. Technocracy Affinity: Void Engineers (the Deep Universe is full of spirits they call "Dimensional Anomalies").

## The Gauntlet

The Gauntlet is the spiritual barrier between the material world and the Penumbra. It has a rating from 1 (nearly nonexistent, in places of great spiritual power) to 5 (nearly impenetrable, in the heart of a modern city). Stepping into or out of the spirit world requires working against the Gauntlet.

| Gauntlet Rating | Typical Location |
| --- | --- |
| 1 — Thin | Ancient sacred sites, deep wilderness Nodes, active Caerns. |
| 2 — Permeable | Parks, rural areas, spiritual gathering places. |
| 3 — Average | Suburbs, small towns, college campuses. |
| 4 — Thick | Urban centers, industrial zones, Technocratic facilities. |
| 5 — Impenetrable | Technocratic Nodes, certain Iteration X facilities. |

## Spirit Dot Levels

### Spirit 1 — Sense Spirit

The mage perceives the spirit world overlaid on the material world. They see the Penumbra's reflection of their surroundings, notice where spirits are active, and feel the Gauntlet's thickness.

**Passive Sense:**

The mage always perceives spirits that are manifested or "peeking through" from the Penumbra. They feel the Gauntlet's rating in their current location.

#### Sphere Tricks — Spirit 1

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Spirit Sight (1) | You see fully into the Penumbra for one scene, perceiving all spirits in the vicinity clearly. |
| Gauntlet Read (1) | You know the exact Gauntlet rating at your location and within Long-to-Extreme range (500m) in any direction. |
| Sense Haunting (2) | You detect any spirits attached to a person, object, or location, and know their rough power and disposition. |

### Spirit 2 — Communicate with Spirits

The mage can speak to spirits directly, understand their language, and make simple requests. They can also channel a spirit's influence into the material world briefly — a fire spirit's warmth, a storm spirit's static charge, a death spirit's chill.

**Active Effects (Freeform):**

- Hold a full, genuine conversation with any spirit in your vicinity. The spirit can hear, respond, and negotiate. It is not compelled to cooperate.
- Make a formal Request — ask a spirit to perform a service in exchange for Quintessence, Essence, or a promise. The spirit evaluates the offer by its own values.
- Channel a spirit's essence: a willing spirit lends you one of its defining abilities (a Jaguar spirit's speed, a River spirit's flow) for one round. Coincidental if subtle.
- Detect and identify spirits within Medium range (50m): you know their type, rough power level, and current emotional state.

#### Sphere Tricks — Spirit 2

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Speak to Spirit (1) | You communicate with any spirit in your vicinity. The conversation is genuine — spirits are not compelled to cooperate, but they can hear and respond. |
| Channel (2) | A willing spirit channels its essence through you briefly — you gain one of its abilities (a fire spirit's flame, a storm spirit's lightning) for one round. |
| Bind Spirit (2) | A spirit with lower Power than your Spirit rating cannot flee your presence for one scene. It can still refuse to cooperate. |

### Spirit 3 — Enter and Exit the Penumbra

The mage can step sideways into the spirit world and back out, bring companions with them, and open passages through the Gauntlet for spirits to enter the material world.

**Active Effects (Freeform):**

- Step sideways: Spirit + Composure vs. Gauntlet rating. On success, enter the Penumbra.
- Bring a willing companion through the Gauntlet (adds +1 Difficulty per additional person).
- Open a Gauntlet rift for a spirit to enter the material world.

#### Sphere Tricks — Spirit 3

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Step Sideways (1) | Enter or exit the Penumbra. Difficulty = Gauntlet rating. Vulgar to Sleeper observers. |
| Bind (2) | Compel a spirit to remain in a location or object for [hits] hours. The spirit fights back (opposed Spirit + Resolve vs. your Spirit + Composure). |
| Umbral Ally (2) | A spirit you have previously communicated with agrees to serve as an ally for one scene. It manifests and can act on your behalf. |

### Spirit 4 — Command Spirits

The mage commands spirits with authority. They can compel spirits to action, create Fetishes (objects with bound spirits), and seal the Gauntlet temporarily to prevent spiritual incursion. Where Spirit 3 negotiates, Spirit 4 commands.

**Active Effects (Freeform):**

- Compel a spirit to act against its will — contest Spirit + Composure vs. the spirit's Resistance. On success, the spirit obeys for one scene, though hostile spirits may seek revenge afterward.
- Begin the Fetish-creation process: bind a willing or defeated spirit into a prepared physical object. The bound spirit's power becomes a usable ability of the object. Extended action; see Fetish rules.
- Seal the Gauntlet within Medium range (up to 60m): no spirits may cross in either direction for one scene. This affects Paradox Spirits as well — a powerful defensive use when Paradox is approaching.
- Destroy a spirit's anchor — the object, concept, or place that ties it to a location. A destroyed anchor causes the spirit to fade back into the Umbra unless it has other anchors.

#### Sphere Tricks — Spirit 4

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Create Fetish (Extended) | Bind a spirit willingly into an object (Threshold 10). The spirit's power becomes a usable ability of the object. |
| Banish (2) | Expel a spirit from the material world or from the Penumbra in your vicinity. Opposed: Spirit + Composure vs. Spirit's Resolve. Difficulty = Spirit's rating. |
| Seal Gauntlet (3) | Prevent all spirits from crossing the Gauntlet within Medium range (up to 60m) for one scene. |

### Spirit 5 — Spirit Mastery

The mage can reshape the Penumbra itself, create permanent spirit realms (Horizon Realms), destroy spirits permanently, and touch the Deep Umbra — the vast, strange regions beyond the near spirit world.

#### Sphere Tricks — Spirit 5

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Create Realm (5) | Create a persistent Horizon Realm — a pocket dimension in the Umbra with its own rules and geography. Size: roughly one square kilometer per hit scored. Requires Spirit 5 + Prime 4. Chronicle-event level. |
| Destroy Spirit (4) | Permanently unmake a spirit, erasing it from existence. The spirit's Essence is released as free Quintessence. Opposed: Spirit + Composure vs. spirit's Resistance. Higher-rating spirits may require extended actions. |
| Deep Umbra Access (3) | Step into the Deep Umbra — the vast regions beyond the Near Umbra. Standard Gauntlet crossing does not reach this far; Spirit 5 opens the way. What the mage finds there is Storyguide territory. |
| Reshape Penumbra (4) | Permanently alter the Penumbra reflection of a physical location — changing what spiritual landscape corresponds to a place, what spirits are drawn there, and what metaphysical rules apply locally. |
| Apotheosis (Special) | Under precisely the right narrative conditions, a mage with Spirit 5 and Avatar 5 may attempt Ascension — becoming one with a higher pattern of existence. This is a chronicle-ending event for the character, not a tactical option. Storyguide discretion required. |

### Worked Examples — Spirit

> SPIRIT ● ROTE — SPIRIT WHISPER (Dreamspeaker)
> 
> Ahanu (Dreamspeaker, Spirit ●●●, Resolve ●●●●) walks into a new location and wants to know whether it has spiritual presences before he acts.  He whispers a quiet acknowledgement to whatever may be present and listens for a response in the texture of the air.
> 
> Pool: Spirit ● (1) + Resolve ●●●● (4) = 5 dice.  SF: 1 (Rote: highest dot 1 − 1 = 0, minimum 1).  Difficulty: 1.
> Roll: [9, 8, 6, 3, 1] = 3 hits, 2 net.  Ahanu senses two spirits in the area: one small and territorial (a place-spirit claiming the alley), one larger and more watchful (a spirit of secrets, recently disturbed).  Neither is hostile — but the spirit of secrets is agitated.
> Result: Detects the presence, rough power level, and emotional state of any spirits within Short range (up to 20m), including spirits in the Penumbra.  Cannot communicate or interact — only sense.
> COINCIDENTAL — reads as spiritual attentiveness or cultural practice.  No Paradox risk.

> SPIRIT ●● ROTE — SPIRIT SIGHT (Dreamspeaker)
> 
> Ahanu (Dreamspeaker, Spirit ●●●, Resolve ●●●●) opens his perception to the Penumbra without leaving his body.
> 
> Pool: Spirit ●● (2) + Resolve ●●●● (4) = 6 dice.  SF: 1 (Rote).  Difficulty: 2.
> Roll: [8, 6, 5, 4, 3, 1] = 2 hits, 0 net above Difficulty — exactly meets it.  Sight opens.
> Result: Ahanu sees and can communicate with spirits in the immediate area for the scene.  He does not Umbra-step; he remains physically present.
> COINCIDENTAL — to Sleepers, he appears to be meditating.  No Paradox.

> SPIRIT ●●● FREEFORM — SPIRIT BINDING
> 
> A fire spirit has been causing accidents in an apartment building.  Ahanu binds it before it harms someone.
> 
> Pool: Spirit ●●● (3) + Resolve ●●●● (4) = 7 dice.  SF: 3.  Difficulty: 4 (uncooperative spirit of moderate power).
> Roll: [9, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 2] = 5 hits, 1 net above Difficulty.  The binding holds.
> Result: The spirit is bound and cannot act freely for [hits] days.  Ahanu may negotiate its release or have it bound into a fetish vessel (see Appendix S: Crafting).
> VULGAR — spirit fire visibly constrained in a binding circle.  With no Sleeper witnesses (the building is temporarily cleared): 1 Paradox die.

> SPIRIT ●●●● FREEFORM — UMBRA STEP
> 
> Ahanu needs to enter the Penumbra — the spirit reflection of a warehouse where a corrupted Bane spirit has taken up residence.  The Gauntlet here is thick (rating 4) because the area is heavily industrialised.  He must push through it alone.
> 
> Pool: Spirit ●●●● (4) + Resolve ●●●● (4) = 8 dice.  SF: 4 (Freeform).  Difficulty: 4 (= the local Gauntlet rating).  Opposition: none beyond the Difficulty itself — per the Step Sideways Sphere Trick (Spirit 3, above), crossing the Gauntlet is a standard hits-vs-Difficulty roll where Difficulty always equals the Gauntlet's rating at that location. This is not a separate opposed dice pool and not automatic successes subtracted afterward; it is simply this roll's Difficulty, exactly like any other Freeform effect.
> Roll: [10, 9, 8, 7, 5, 3, 2, 1] = 6 hits (10 = 2).  Against Difficulty 4, that leaves 2 net hits.  He pushes through — the physical world blurs and the Penumbra assembles itself around him.  The warehouse, in spirit-space, is a rusted hulk of grinding gears and poisoned light.
> Result: Crosses the Gauntlet and enters the Penumbra (or returns to the physical world).  The crossing is solo — bringing others requires additional Sphere dots or a prepared crossing point.
> VULGAR — from the physical world's perspective, Ahanu simply vanishes.  Any Sleeper watching would have no framework for what they just saw.  SF 4 means Paradox risk is meaningful.  Ahanu leaves a bundle of tobacco and cedar at the crossing point — a traditional offering to ease the spirit-road.
> 
> FREE HIT SPEND: 2 free hits.  First hit makes the crossing silent and without any visible shimmer — no physical sign at all.  Second hit creates a temporary "door" at the crossing point that Ahanu can use to return quickly without rolling again (lasts one scene).

> SPIRIT ●●●●● FREEFORM — REALM TEAR
> 
> The cabal is under attack from a Bane-possessed hunter who cannot be harmed by physical means.  Ahanu decides to drag the entire confrontation into the Penumbra — he will tear the Gauntlet open wide enough for everyone present (four cabal members, two antagonists) to cross simultaneously.
> 
> Pool: Spirit ●●●●● (5) + Resolve ●●●● (4) = 9 dice.  SF: 5 (Freeform).  Difficulty: 3 (= the local Gauntlet rating — thinner than average here, because the area sits near a small natural Node).  Opposition: none beyond the Difficulty itself, per the same Gauntlet-crossing rule as Umbra Step above: Difficulty always equals the Gauntlet's local rating, with no separate opposed roll or automatic-successes step layered on top.
> Roll: [10, 10, 9, 8, 6, 4, 2, 1, 1] = 7 hits (each 10 = 2).  Against Difficulty 3, that leaves 4 net hits.  The Gauntlet tears open — a ragged aperture roughly 3m wide and 2m tall, edges crackling with spirit-energy.  The Penumbra is visible through it.  Everyone within 10m is drawn into the crossing unless they actively resist (Resolve + Awareness, Difficulty 4).
> Result: Opens the Gauntlet for mass crossing.  The tear remains open for [net hits] turns (4 turns here, about 30 seconds).  Anyone who enters the aperture crosses to the Penumbra.  The tear is visible from both sides.
> HIGHLY VULGAR — a visible hole in reality that pulls people through it is beyond any mundane explanation.  SF 5 in a Gauntlet area means serious Paradox exposure.  Ahanu does not celebrate his own audacity; he knows exactly what this costs him.
> 
> FREE HIT SPEND: Ahanu uses 2 net hits (above the minimum 2 needed to hold the tear open) to specify that the crossing is selective — he can choose which beings are drawn through and which are not.  The Bane-possessed hunter is pulled across; the cabal's uninitiated ally stays on the physical side.

## Time — The Sphere of Temporal Perception and Manipulation

Time is a river, and most people are carried by its current. Time mages learn to stand on the bank and look both ways. Then they learn to swim upstream. Then — at the highest levels — they learn that upstream and downstream are labels they invented.

Tradition Affinity: Euthanatos, Virtual Adepts. Technocracy Affinity: Void Engineers. Time magic generates some of the most interesting (and headache-inducing) Paradox in the game, because the Consensus believes time is fixed and sequential.

## Time Dot Levels

### Time 1 — Sense Time

The mage perceives time with unusual clarity. They can always sense the exact time without a clock, know how long events lasted, feel approaching significant events as a general unease, and read the temporal "echo" of a place (what happened here recently).

**Passive Sense:**

The mage never needs a clock. They also always sense if they are approaching a significant event (combat, a major decision point) 1-2 rounds before it begins — a general warning, not specifics.

#### Sphere Tricks — Time 1

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Echo Read (1) | You perceive the last major event that occurred in a location (up to 24 hours ago) as a vivid impression. |
| Warning (1) | Once per scene, before initiative is rolled, you sense an ambush or attack coming and may act on that warning (you are never truly surprised). |
| Deadline Sense (2) | You know exactly how much time you have before a significant event occurs — a bomb detonates, an ally arrives, a negotiation deadline passes. |

### Time 2 — Alter Time Locally

The mage can speed up or slow down time in a small area around themselves or a target. This does not create paradoxes of causality — it is local temporal dilation, the kind of thing that can be rationalized as adrenaline or perception.

**Active Effects (Freeform):**

- Haste: You act twice as fast for one round — gain an additional Minor Action. Coincidental (observers think you're simply quick).
- Slow: One target acts at half speed — they lose their Minor Action this round. Borderline Vulgar.
- Freeze a small object in time: it becomes impervious to change for [hits] rounds.

#### Sphere Tricks — Time 2

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Haste (1) | You gain one additional Minor Action this round. |
| Slow (1) | One target loses their next Minor Action. |
| Freeze Object (2) | One non-living object is frozen in time for [hits] rounds. It cannot be moved, damaged, or changed during this time. |
| Time Sense (2) | You know the exact future timeline of a single mundane process (how long this car chase will last, when the guard changes, when the storm arrives). |

### Time 3 — See Through Time

The mage can view the past or possible futures of a location, object, or person. This is vision only — no interaction — but the clarity and detail are remarkable. This is the level where Time magic starts generating significant Paradox because the Consensus is not comfortable with precognition.

**Active Effects (Freeform):**

- Retrocognition: See the past of an object or location. How far back depends on hits (1 hit = 1 day; 3 hits = 1 month; 5 hits = 1 year; 7 hits = 10 years, etc.).
- Precognition: See a possible future of an event or choice. The Storyguide shows one probable future based on current trajectories. It is not guaranteed — circumstances can change. Vulgar.

#### Sphere Tricks — Time 3

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Retrocognition (1) | See the past of a location or object. Clarity and distance depend on hits. |
| Probability Vision (2) | See the most likely outcome of a specific decision or action (one question answered by the Storyguide based on current probabilities). |
| Time Loop (2) | You may redo your last Major Action once per scene — you literally experience the moment again and choose differently. Vulgar. +2 dice Paradox. |

### Time 4 — Manipulate Time

The mage can send objects or small amounts of information backwards or forwards in time. They can also freeze time in a small area, creating a moment that exists outside the flow for everyone except themselves. This is the level at which Time magic stops being perception and becomes intervention — and the Consensus responds accordingly.

**Active Effects (Freeform):**

- Timestop: suspend time for a localized area for one round. The mage acts freely during the frozen moment; everyone else is perfectly still. Extremely Vulgar; always adds Paradox regardless of witnesses.
- Send a small object (up to 1kg) a few hours into the past or future. The receiving-end implications (the object appears somewhere it wasn't) create timeline inconsistencies that the Consensus fights back against. Paradox +3 minimum.
- Send information backward in time — a simple message (a few words) to yourself at an earlier point in the session. You receive the message as a sudden memory you 'already had.' This cannot be used to prevent your own death, only to pass information.
- Create a temporal pocket: a zone where time moves at a different rate (twice as fast or half as fast) for [hits] rounds. People inside experience subjective time at the altered rate; from outside, they appear to move in slow or fast motion.

#### Sphere Tricks — Time 4

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Timestop (2) | Freeze time for everyone except yourself for 1 round. Take one additional Major Action during the frozen moment. When time restarts, no one is aware of the interruption. Vulgar; +2 Paradox minimum. |
| Send Object (3) | Send a small object (~1kg) up to 24 hours into the past or future. The object arrives in a location the mage specifies. Extremely Vulgar. Paradox +4 dice. The object's absence in the intervening period creates a minor timeline scar. |
| Temporal Stasis (2) | Freeze a single non-resisting target in time for [hits] rounds. They are perfectly preserved — cannot be harmed, cannot act, are not aware of the passage of time. Releases automatically at duration's end or if the mage chooses. |
| Time Echo (2) | Send a brief message (up to 30 words) back in time to yourself at a point earlier in the current scene. You 'remember' the message now, as if you always knew it. Cannot change decisions already made — only supplements knowledge. |
| Accelerated Healing (2) | Fast-forward the biological healing process in a willing target. They heal [hits] Bashing or 1 Lethal damage as time rushes through their body (bruises forming and fading in seconds). Vulgar if witnessed. |

### Time 5 — Time Mastery

The mage can travel through time personally, create stable time loops, or alter historical events. These are chronicle-defining powers that should be used very sparingly. The Paradox generated by Time 5 effects is extreme, and the Consensus fights back with everything it has. A Time 5 mage who casually time-travels will accumulate a Paradox Pool that could destroy them within the session.

#### Sphere Tricks — Time 5

| Trick (Cost) | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| Personal Time Travel (5) | Travel personally to a specific past or future moment. The mage arrives as an observer or participant — changing events creates paradox branches. Paradox +5 per major change made. Chronicle-event level. |
| Stable Loop (4) | Create a time loop in a defined area — events reset at the end of each cycle. The mage remembers all cycles; other participants do not (unless they have Time 3+). Maximum loop duration: one scene. Paradox +4. |
| History Revision (5) | Alter a specific past event within the last month. The change propagates forward through the timeline. Extremely Vulgar; generates a Paradox backlash immediately regardless of witnesses. Chronicle-defining; requires Storyguide approval. |
| Temporal Sanctuary (3) | Remove yourself from the flow of time for [hits] rounds. You cannot be detected, targeted, or affected by any event during this time. You observe but cannot interact. When you re-enter time, it is as if you never left. |
| Age or Restore (4) | Advance or reverse a target's age by up to [hits × 10] years. Aging beyond biological maximum is fatal. Reversing aging restores physical condition to that period — healing injuries that occurred since that age. Paradox +3. |

### Worked Examples — Time

> TIME ● ROTE — TEMPORAL SENSE (Virtual Adepts)
> 
> Professor Chen (Virtual Adepts, Time ●●●, Intellect ●●●●) always knows the exact time — not from a watch, but because she can feel the flow of moments against her awareness like current against skin.  More practically: she can sense when something time-sensitive is approaching, even if she cannot see it.
> 
> Pool: Time ● (1) + Intellect ●●●● (4) = 5 dice.  SF: 1 (Rote: highest dot 1 − 1 = 0, minimum 1).  Difficulty: 1.
> Roll: [9, 7, 5, 3, 2] = 2 hits, 1 net.  Chen senses that something significant will occur within the next twenty minutes — a threshold she can feel as a temporal density, like a knot in the flow.  She cannot see what, only when: approximately seventeen minutes from now, and it will be sudden.
> Result: Precise awareness of the current time to the second, and detection of any significant temporal event (a planned action, a trap trigger, a scheduled arrival) within Short range in time (within the next hour).  Cannot reveal the nature of the event, only its timing.
> COINCIDENTAL — reads as exceptional time-awareness or professional punctuality.  Virtual Adepts train this rote as a basic interface with the temporal substrate.

> TIME ●● ROTE — ECHO READ (Virtual Adepts)
> 
> Professor Chen (Virtual Adepts, Time ●●●, Intellect ●●●●) places her hand on a crime scene wall and reads temporal echoes.
> 
> Pool: Time ●● (2) + Intellect ●●●● (4) = 6 dice.  SF: 1 (Rote: 2 − 1 = 1).  Difficulty: 2.
> Roll: [9, 8, 6, 5, 3, 2] = 3 hits, 1 net.  A 12-hour-old impression surfaces.
> Result: Clear sensory impression of the last major event in this location up to 24 hours ago.  She sees the courier's face.
> COINCIDENTAL — Chen has a sensor glove prop that sells the interaction as a forensics device to any Sleepers watching.

> TIME ●●● FREEFORM — COMBAT PRECOGNITION
> 
> Professor Chen reads a few seconds forward during combat to predict where an attacker's blade will land.
> 
> Pool: Time ●●● (3) + Intellect ●●●● (4) = 7 dice.  SF: 3.  Difficulty: 3.
> Roll: [9, 8, 7, 5, 4, 3, 2] = 4 hits, 1 net.  The immediate future flickers into view.
> Result: +2 dice to her next dodge or parry roll against a declared attacker.  The effect lasts one action.
> COINCIDENTAL — appears as extraordinary reflexes and predictive movement.  No Paradox.
> NOTE: Viewing further forward (hours, days) steps up the Difficulty — see Time ●●● dot description for the retrocognition/precognition scale.

> TIME ●●●● FREEFORM — TEMPORAL ANCHOR
> 
> The NWO extraction team is about to reach a door that, once opened, will trigger a cascade Chen cannot stop.  She has 8 dice and one idea: freeze the team leader in place long enough for the cabal to reach him first.
> 
> Pool: Time ●●●● (4) + Intellect ●●●● (4) = 8 dice.  SF: 4 (freeform).  Difficulty: 4.
> Roll: [10, 9, 8, 7, 5, 3, 2, 1] = 6 hits (10 = 2), 2 net hits.  Chen anchors the team leader in a personal temporal loop — from his perspective, nothing passes.  From everyone else's perspective, he simply stops: mid-stride, one hand reaching for the door handle, completely motionless.  He experiences zero subjective time and will not remember these moments at all.  Duration: 2 rounds (one per net hit).
> Result: Freezes one non-resisting target in personal temporal stasis for [net hits] rounds.  The target is effectively removed from the initiative order for that duration.  They cannot act, be harmed (the stasis also prevents damage from entering the frozen moment), or communicate.  When they unfreeze, they have no memory of missing time.
> HIGHLY VULGAR — a person standing perfectly motionless while the world moves around them is not explainable.  SF 4 means meaningful Paradox risk.  Chen performs this from around a corner; no Sleepers have line of sight to the frozen agent.  She counts the rounds quietly and moves.
> 
> FREE HIT SPEND: 2 free hits.  First hit extends stasis to 3 rounds total.  Second hit allows Chen to move the frozen target (like a statue) without breaking the effect — she rotates him away from the door so that when he unfreezes, he is facing the wrong direction.

> TIME ●●●●● FREEFORM — REWIND
> 
> The cabal made a critical error three minutes ago: they triggered an alarm that has now brought Technocracy reinforcements.  Professor Chen proposes the most dangerous thing she has ever done: rewind those three minutes of local time for everyone present, allowing the cabal to act differently.
> 
> Pool: Time ●●●●● (5) + Intellect ●●●● (4) = 9 dice.  SF: 5 (Freeform).  Difficulty: 5 (base, matching Time ●●●●●'s casting scope — the same pattern used for every other Sphere's top-dot Freeform effect; see Chapter Four).  Opposition: none — Rewind targets local time itself, not a resisting person or ward, so there is nothing to oppose the roll.
> Roll: [10, 9, 9, 8, 7, 5, 3, 2, 1] = 7 hits (10 = 2), 2 net hits (7 hits − Difficulty 5).  Chen collapses the last three minutes of local time.  The reinforcements have not arrived.  The alarm has not triggered.  The cabal stands where they were three minutes ago — but with their memories of what happened intact.  They know exactly what went wrong and can choose differently.
> Result: Rewinds local time by up to [net hits × 2] minutes (4 minutes here) for all beings within Short range.  Memory of the rewound events is preserved for the caster only, unless they spend a Quintessence point to share the memory with each additional target.  The timeline branches — the original timeline is sealed off.
> HIGHLY VULGAR — temporal reversal is one of the most profound violations of the Consensus possible.  SF 5 means the maximum Paradox pressure.  The Storyguide draws five cards.  If even one is a botch card, reality backlashes immediately.  Chen has spent two Tass beforehand as a Paradox buffer, and she is very pale when she finishes.  "We have four minutes," she says quietly.  "Do not make the same mistake."
> 
> FREE HIT SPEND: 2 free hits.  First hit extends the rewind to 6 minutes (enough to preempt the entire failed approach).  Second hit keeps the rewound period's memories intact for all cabal members simultaneously — no Quintessence surcharge.

# Chapter Seven: Combat — Violence in the World of Mage

Mages do not want to fight. Fighting is loud, witnesses are everywhere, and nothing generates Paradox faster than a visible magical duel in a parking garage full of Sleepers. And yet the world of Mage is a world at war — a slow, invisible, ideological war between the Traditions and the Technocracy, punctuated by moments of sudden, desperate, brutal violence. The Traditions battle the Technocracy for the soul of consensus reality. Nephandi twist the darkest corners of that war toward annihilation. Marauders tear holes in the fabric of the world wherever they wander. Even within a Chantry, power struggles and philosophical rifts sometimes boil past words into something uglier.

When violence erupts, it is fast and consequential. Storypath Ultra's combat system reinforces this reality: there is no grinding attrition of abstract hit points, no rounds of trading blows until one side runs out of numbers. Fights are over in seconds. A single decisive strike from a trained soldier can drop a mage who spent all her effort on preparation and none on defense. A mage who commits to magical violence is one bad roll away from a Paradox spiral that ends the encounter before it begins — and possibly ends the mage with it. This chapter lays out everything you need to resolve that violence cleanly, dramatically, and with the full weight of consequence that Mage demands.

This chapter covers: the structure of a violent scene, Initiative and the Spotlight system, Attack rolls and how they interact with defense, Cover and positioning, the three damage types and the Health track, Armor and its limits, the Tag system as it applies to weapons and combat situations, Status Conditions in full, Healing and recovery, Death and Incapacitation, Wonders in combat, Magical defenses, Special circumstances from vehicles to grappling, and six worked combat examples of progressive complexity. Cross-references to Appendix H (Phase Initiative — a tactical variant for groups who want granular action economy) and Appendix I (Armor in Depth — construction rules, enchantment, and special materials) are noted throughout.

A note before we begin: the combat rules in this chapter are designed to be dramatic tools, not simulation engines. Every mechanic here exists to create moments of choice, tension, and consequence at the table. The Storyguide is always empowered to bend a rule in service of a better story. The dice are instruments of drama, not arbiters of physics.

---

```
WHICH COMBAT SYSTEM DO I USE? — Default Flow at a Glance
--------------------------------------------------------
DEFAULT: SPOTLIGHT SYSTEM (this chapter)
  ┌─ Round starts: Roll Initiative (Athletics + Cunning). Highest goes first.
  ├─ Your Spotlight: one Major Action + any Minor Actions.
  ├─ Declare → Roll → Apply hits vs. Difficulty → spend Trick Hits.
  └─ Cycle through all Spotlights. Repeat until fight ends.

MAGIC IN SPOTLIGHT:
  Cast on your Spotlight. No timing conflict — your spell resolves when you act.
  Casting Roll = Sphere Rating + Casting Attribute. Paradox: see Chapter Five.

SWITCH TO PHASE INITIATIVE (Appendix H) WHEN:
  • 6+ combatants and you want granular action economy.
  • Multiple mages cast in the same Round and timing matters.
  • The table enjoys simulation-style play for set-piece encounters.

MAGIC SPEED FACTORS (for Phase Initiative only):
  Rote SF     = highest Sphere dot rating used.   (SF 2 for a Forces 2 Rote)
  Freeform SF = total Sphere dots used.            (SF 5 for Corr 2 + Forces 3)
  Lower SF resolves first. Rote SF = highest dot − 1 (min 1). Freeform SF = sum of dots. Being hit before your spell resolves: Concentration check or Sphere Defense.

QUICK ANSWER: 95% of sessions use Spotlight. Phase Initiative is a powerful option,
  not a requirement. When in doubt, use Spotlight.
```

---

## Two Combat Systems — How They Fit Together

This book contains two combat resolution systems that work at different levels of granularity. Understanding both — and knowing which to use — prevents confusion at the table. They are not in conflict. They are the same underlying mechanics running at different zoom levels.

### The Spotlight System — Default (This Chapter)

The Spotlight System is the standard Storypath Ultra combat framework. It is narrative-forward, fast, and designed for dramatic storytelling. In the Spotlight System:

- Each Round, every participant gets one Spotlight — their turn in the scene's center.
- Spotlights happen in Initiative order, highest to lowest (roll Athletics + Cunning at the start of a scene).
- During your Spotlight: one Major Action (attack, cast a spell, dodge) and any number of Minor Actions (speak, draw a weapon, step one pace, use a Quick-Draw item).
- There are no phases within a Round. Actions in the same Spotlight are simultaneous from a fiction perspective.
- This is the system most tables will use most of the time.
```
OPTIONAL RULE — Phase Initiative System (Appendix H)
----------------------------------------------------
The Phase Initiative System is a complete tactical variant and is NOT required for play. Most sessions — including sessions with significant combat — work perfectly with the standard Spotlight system. Phase Initiative is recommended for groups who enjoy granular action economy, multi-participant fights with complex timing, or who want full Speed Factor mechanics for spellcasting. If your table is new to the system or prefers narrative-forward play, skip this section entirely and use Spotlights throughout. You can always add Phase Initiative later when the table is ready.
```

### The Phase Initiative System — Tactical Variant (Appendix H)

The Phase Initiative system was custom-built for groups who want granular action economy — particularly tables that enjoy tactical magic interactions, multi-participant fights with complex timing questions, or the full weight of fast vs. slow actions. In the Phase Initiative System:

- Each Round is divided into numbered Phases (1 through 10+). Actions declare and resolve across specific Phases based on their Speed Factor (SF).
- Everyone draws Action Dice (AD) equal to their Initiative roll result. Spending 1 AD lets you act in a Phase.
- Rotes resolve at Phase 1 + highest Sphere rating (SF). Improvised magic resolves at Phase 1 + total Sphere dots used.
- Faster actions happen before slower ones — a punch (SF 1) beats a heavy spell (SF 5) every time, unless the spellcaster started first.
- This system is used in Appendix L (the complete example of play) to demonstrate how the two systems translate to real table play.

### Which System Is Primary?

The Spotlight System is primary. The Phase Initiative system is a tactical variant. For most sessions — including sessions with significant combat — use Spotlights. Switch to Phase Initiative when:

- The group specifically wants granular action economy for a climactic fight.
- Multiple mages are casting in the same Round and timing matters (does the Force bolt land before the Mind shield goes up?).
- You want the full simulationist experience for a high-stakes encounter.
Nothing in the core rules requires Phase Initiative. Appendix H provides it as a tool; use it when it serves the story.

```
SPU Combat Terminology — Quick Reference
----------------------------------------
ENHANCEMENT DICE: Bonus dice added to a pool from any source — Tags, Quintessence, Willpower, circumstance.
  Example: Spending 1 Quintessence adds 1 Enhancement die to the casting pool.

TRICK HITS: Excess hits on an attack (hits beyond the Difficulty threshold) that can be spent on Combat Tricks
  instead of damage. Not the same as Enhancement — Trick Hits are the result of a successful roll.
  Example: Need 2 hits, roll 5 hits = 3 Trick Hits to spend on Disarm, Extra Damage, Knockdown, etc.

COMPLICATIONS: Narrative disadvantages or mechanical penalties that can arise from failed rolls or tag Compels.
  Example: A [Broken Arm] Complication gives -2 dice to all Might actions until healed.

MOMENTUM: A shared resource pool that players build up and spend. +1 Momentum on a failed roll; spend 1
  Momentum to invoke a Tag, buy an extra Minor Action, or enable certain Tricks.
  Storyguide also has a Momentum pool used for NPC actions and narrative twists.

TAGS: Descriptive keywords on characters, items, or situations. Invoke (spend 1 Momentum) for +2 dice or
  automatic narrative effect. Compel (SG offers a complication) for +1 Momentum to the player pool.
  Example: [Fast] weapon -> invoke for +2 dice when speed is the deciding factor.

SPOTLIGHT: Your turn in the combat Round (Spotlight System). One Major + Minor Actions.

ACTION DICE (AD): Resource drawn at start of each Round (Phase Initiative system only).
  = Initiative roll result. Spend 1 AD to act in a Phase.

SPEED FACTOR (SF): How many Phases after declaration before a spell resolves (Phase Initiative only).
  Rote SF = highest Sphere rating. Improvised SF = total Sphere dots used.
```

---

## Scene Structure: How a Fight Unfolds

A violent scene in Mage is organized into Rounds. Each Round represents approximately three to five seconds of real time — long enough for a punch, a shot, a dodge, a shouted warning, and the first half of a spell. At the start of a violent scene, the Storyguide announces that combat is beginning and calls for Initiative. This is the moment when everything slows down and becomes deliberate: every action counts, every decision has weight, and the fiction shifts into the sharp focus of a life-or-death situation.

Within each Round, every participant gets one Spotlight — their moment of focused action. The Spotlight is more than a turn. It is a brief period of narrative time in which that character is the center of attention. The camera is on them. Their choices drive what happens next. Spotlights happen in Initiative order, highest to lowest. Once every participant has had their Spotlight, the Round ends and a new Round begins. There is no fixed "end" to a combat scene — it ends when one side is incapacitated, surrenders, flees, when dramatic circumstances intervene, or when the Storyguide determines that the outcome is no longer in doubt.

This structure — Rounds divided into Spotlights — means that combat is fundamentally a series of dramatic beats rather than a clock-tick simulation. The Storyguide should treat each Spotlight as a moment of collaborative storytelling: describe what is happening, invite the player to act, describe the consequences, and move on. Mechanical resolution should take seconds at the table, not minutes.

### Major and Minor Actions

During their Spotlight, a character can take one Major Action and one or more Minor Actions. The Major Action is the main event of the Spotlight — the thing the character is doing that matters most. Minor Actions are small, quick supporting activities that can realistically be accomplished while also doing the Major Action.

Minor Actions do not have a fixed numerical limit, but they should pass a common-sense test: can you realistically do all of these things in five seconds while also doing the Major Action? Opening a door and drawing a weapon? Yes. Opening a door, drawing a weapon, reloading it, and also treating a wound? No. The Storyguide is the final arbiter.

| Action Type | Examples |
| --- | --- |
| Major Action | Attack, cast a spell, use a Wonder, full dodge, administer first aid, extended sprint (full movement), disarm, initiate or break a grapple, pick a complex lock under pressure |
| Minor Action | Draw or holster a weapon, speak a sentence or short command, step one pace, glance around a corner, take cover behind available terrain, open an unlocked door, activate a pre-charged Wonder, pass an object to an adjacent ally |
| Free Action | Glance at a visible object, drop a held item, shout a single word, brace against a surface already adjacent |

A character may convert their Major Action into two Minor Actions if they need to do several small things in sequence and have no need for a full attack or spell that round. This is useful when repositioning, preparing equipment, or setting up for the next round's major play. They give up all offensive capability for that round in exchange for flexibility.

### The Spotlight and Narrative Momentum

The Spotlight system differs philosophically from traditional initiative in one important way: it is not just about who acts when. It is about who the story is focused on in any given moment. When it is your Spotlight, you are not simply "taking a turn." You are the protagonist of that three-to-five-second slice of fiction. Describe what your character does with physical and emotional specificity. What does it feel like to cast that spell under fire? What does Rosamund smell when the NWO agent's weapon discharges two feet from her head? What does Vikram say when he realizes his teleportation attempt just painted a glowing target on his back?

The Storyguide should reinforce this by narrating the results of actions in the same vivid register. Not "Cyborg 1 takes 3 Lethal" but "the electromagnetic pulse from Silas's Tesla gauntlet punches through Cyborg 1's chassis at the shoulder joint; the arm hangs wrong, spark-stuttering, and its targeting reticle jitters across three targets at once before locking." Numbers matter, but story matters more.

*See Appendix H: Phase Initiative for an optional tactical variant that divides each Round into micro-phases — Declare, Fast Actions, Standard Actions, Slow Actions, End of Round. This variant adds granularity and is ideal for groups who enjoy detailed tactical play. The standard Spotlight system presented here is recommended for narrative-forward tables.*

---

## Initiative

Initiative determines the order in which characters act during a combat Round. It reflects not just physical speed but situational awareness, combat instinct, the ability to read a chaotic environment and act decisively within it. A highly perceptive mage with modest physical ability can still act before a faster but less aware opponent — because she saw what was coming before he did.

Initiative is not simply reflexes. It represents the full cognitive and physical picture of combat readiness: the trained soldier who scans the room on entry, the paranoid mage who never stops tracking exits, the spirit-touched Dreamspeaker who feels the violence in the air before it manifests. All of these inform when and how a character enters the flow of battle.

### The Initiative Roll

When combat begins, every participant rolls Initiative. The formula is:

**Athletics + Cunning (add any relevant Enhancement from cyborg implants, magical augmentation, a prepared tactical advantage, or situational bonuses)**

The number of hits — not the total of the dice, but the count of results at or above the target number — determines your Initiative score. Characters act in descending order: highest scores act first. This Initiative order is established at the start of the scene and does not change from round to round unless a specific effect (a Time magic working, a Wonder, a dramatic narrative shift) alters it.

The distinction between Athletics and Cunning in this roll is important and intentional. Athletics represents the body's speed and physical readiness. Cunning represents the mind's ability to read a situation. Both matter equally. A slow but perceptive character can outmaneuver a fast but oblivious one. This is mechanically true and narratively satisfying — it rewards players who build conceptually coherent characters rather than min-maxing pure speed.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Initiative roll: Three Tradition mages are confronted in an alley. Rosamund (Verbena): Athletics 1 + Cunning 3 = 4 dice. Vikram (Virtual Adept): Athletics 2 + Cunning 4 = 6 dice. Silas (Son of Ether): Athletics 2 + Cunning 2 = 4 dice.

Rosamund rolls: 9, 8, 4, 2 = 2 hits.
Vikram rolls: 10, 8, 5, 3, 2, 1 = 3 hits (10 counts as 2 hits in SPU).
Silas rolls: 7, 4, 3, 1 = 0 hits — Silas is always the last to notice trouble, a fact his companions have learned to plan around.

Initiative order: Vikram (3), Rosamund (2), Silas (0). Vikram acts first, giving him the opening move. In a fight where the first spell determines whether there is a second round, this matters enormously.

### Ties in Initiative

When two characters have the same Initiative score, the following tiebreaker order applies: player characters go before Storyguide characters. This is a deliberate narrative choice — the protagonists get the benefit of the doubt. If two player characters tie, they discuss and choose their own order freely. If two Storyguide characters tie, the Storyguide decides, typically choosing the order that creates the most dramatic tension.

Some groups prefer a secondary tiebreaker using Cunning alone (higher Cunning goes first). This is a reasonable house rule and does not break any other mechanic. It rewards perceptive characters and differentiates the Athletics-heavy brawler from the Cunning-heavy tactician even within a tie.

### Surprise and Ambush

If one side prepares an ambush and the ambushed party has no reason to suspect an attack — no psychic warning, no sound cue, no obvious tell — the ambushers automatically go first in the first Round without rolling Initiative. This is not about being faster; it is about being ready when the other side is not. The ambushed party then rolls Initiative normally and acts starting in Round 2, recovering their situational awareness as quickly as their dice allow.

If the ambush is imperfect — the target heard something, received a fragment of mental impression, noticed a reflection in a window — the Storyguide may call for an opposed roll to determine whether the target is truly surprised. The ambusher rolls Dexterity + Stealth. The target rolls Cunning + Alertness. If the target wins or ties, they are not fully surprised and Initiative is rolled normally by all parties. If the ambusher wins, the target is surprised and the ambushers go first as above.

A mage with active magical perception — Time 1 sense of immediate futures, a Mind 1 alertness enhancement, a Spirit 1 awareness of hostile presences — is almost impossible to genuinely surprise. These effects do not automatically eliminate ambush, but they typically grant the mage a free Initiative roll even when surprised, potentially letting them act in Round 1 despite the ambush.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Ambush resolution: Two NWO agents have set up in a parking garage. They prepared this ambush; Rosamund and Vikram do not know they are there. The agents go first in Round 1 automatically.

But Vikram has a persistent Mind 1 background awareness running (Coincidental, no Paradox in private space). The Storyguide calls for a Cunning + Alertness roll: 4 + 4 = 8 dice for Vikram. Results: 9, 9, 8, 7, 4, 3, 2, 1 = 4 hits. "You feel it before you see it — a wrongness in the air, attention focused on you from above left." He is not surprised. He rolls Initiative normally and joins Round 1.

Rosamund has no such protection. She acts in Round 2. The agents still go first, but now Vikram can respond immediately.

### Held Actions

A character can choose to delay their action, holding their Spotlight until a specific trigger occurs. This is declared at the start of what would be their Spotlight: state the trigger clearly ("I act when the door opens," "I fire when I see a target emerge from the smoke") and your Spotlight shifts to immediately after that trigger. A held action always resolves before the triggering event fully plays out — you interrupt it at the last moment.

If the trigger never happens before the Round ends, the held action is lost. You cannot "bank" held actions into the next Round. This creates meaningful tension: holding for the perfect moment is powerful, but committing to a trigger that never comes is a wasted Spotlight.

A character who holds their action and is attacked before their trigger occurs may still defend normally. Holding does not make you passive — it makes you patient, waiting for exactly the right moment to act.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Held action: Rosamund holds her action with the trigger "I act when the Syndicate banker reaches for his phone." Two other characters act. The banker begins to move his hand toward his jacket pocket. Rosamund's held action fires: she steps forward and grabs his wrist before he completes the motion. The phone stays in his pocket.

---

## The Advantage Mechanic

Storypath Ultra uses a consistent Advantage mechanic throughout all conflict resolution. In combat, Advantage represents a meaningful positional, tactical, or situational edge that one character holds over another. Advantages are not simply about physical position — they encompass preparation, information, terrain, psychological state, and the accumulated tactical benefit of good play.

When a character has Advantage over a specific opponent in the current situation, they add +2 dice to all rolls made against that opponent. This is a significant bonus. It represents the difference between fighting on equal terms and fighting from a position of genuine superiority. Advantages are earned, not given — they come from successful setup moves, environmental exploitation, good tactical choices, and clever use of abilities.

### Establishing Advantage

Advantage is established through narrative and mechanical action. Common ways to establish Advantage in combat:

- Higher ground: Moving to an elevated position above your opponent gives you Advantage on ranged attacks against them for as long as you hold it. They have Advantage on attacks against you in melee (they can exploit the awkward angle of your downward strikes).
- Flanking: Coordinating with an ally to attack an opponent from opposite sides. Both attackers gain Advantage on attacks against the flanked target.
- Setup moves: Spending a Spotlight on a non-attacking action specifically designed to create advantage for a follow-up. Throwing sand in someone's face, kicking furniture into their path, using a Minor Action to feint — these can establish Advantage if narrated well and approved by the Storyguide.
- Successful Trick Hits: The "Setup" Combat Trick gives your next ally who attacks the target +1 die, effectively creating a partial Advantage.
- Magical enhancement: Many spells specifically grant Advantage — a Time 2 combat sense, a Mind 1 emotional reading, a Correspondence 2 spatial awareness in a familiar location.
- Information superiority: Knowing your opponent's capabilities, vulnerabilities, and intentions before the fight begins. Detailed preparation provides a narrative Advantage the Storyguide may ratify mechanically.

### Losing and Negating Advantage

Advantage is situational and can be lost. If the character who established Advantage through high ground moves away from that position, they lose the Advantage. If a flanking pattern is broken — one of the flankers is forced to reposition — the Advantage ends. Magical Advantages tied to sustained effects end when the effect ends.

An opponent can spend their Spotlight actively working to negate your Advantage: repositioning to break flanking, closing distance to negate a height advantage, using a spell to counter the information edge you hold. This is a valid and often important tactical choice — giving up offensive action to deny your opponent a powerful edge.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Advantage in play: Silas and Rosamund have flanked a Nephandi cultist. Both have Advantage (the flanking bonus) on their next attacks against him. Silas rolls his Forces bolt with +2 dice. Rosamund rolls her staff strike with +2 dice. The cultist's Spotlight arrives — his first action is not an attack but a reposition, pressing his back against a wall. He sacrifices his attack to break the flank. Next round, only one of them has a clear line to him. Advantage lost, but so is one round of the cultist's offense — a fair trade from his perspective.

---

## Divided Attention — Splitting Focus Between Two Tasks

Most actions in combat demand your full attention for a brief moment. You swing a sword, then the swing is done and your mind is free. You fire a gun, then the trigger pull is complete. These are sequential — one after another — even if they happen within the same Spotlight.

Divided Attention is different. Some tasks require continuous, simultaneous focus throughout their entire duration. Driving a car while shooting out the window is not sequential: the steering wheel demands your hands and spatial awareness at the exact same moment you are lining up the shot. Holding a grapple while casting a spell is not sequential: your physical grip must stay active while your mind builds the magical working. Both tasks are live at once, both can fail independently, and failure in one can cascade directly into the other.

The rule is deliberately simple. When two tasks genuinely require simultaneous, continuous focus, both dice pools take a flat −2 dice penalty. There is no "primary" action — you are equally committed to both throughout.

```
DIVIDED ATTENTION — THE RULE
----------------------------
When two tasks require truly simultaneous, continuous focus:

  • Both pools take −2 dice from their own base pools.
  • No priority designation — neither action is primary.
  • Maximum two simultaneous tasks without a special Merit or magical enhancement.
  • Each task generates its own consequences independently (damage, Paradox, etc.).
  • If either task fails, the Storyguide may rule it bleeds into the other.
  • Tasks that are purely reflexive or Minor Actions do NOT trigger Divided Attention when paired with a Major Action — only two simultaneous Major-level tasks qualify.

QUICK PLAY: Need two things at once? Take −2 to both pools.

If a pool would drop to 0 or below after the −2 penalty, that task cannot be done simultaneously. Choose one, or use Phase Initiative to sequence them.
```

### Sequential vs. Divided Attention — Knowing the Difference

The critical skill is recognizing which situation applies. If an action has a defined moment of commitment that completes before the next begins, it is sequential. If both tasks require you to be actively present to both throughout the round — no pause, no handoff, both live simultaneously — it is Divided Attention.

| Scenario | How to Handle It |
| --- | --- |
| Draw a weapon (Minor Action) then attack | Sequential. Minor Actions are free alongside a Major Action. No penalty. |
| Cast two spells at different SF slots in Phase Initiative | DIVIDED ATTENTION. Both magical constructs are live in your mind from declaration onward. Both casting pools −2. Phase Initiative still sequences when they fire, but the mental load is split the whole round. |
| Drive a car while shooting out the window | DIVIDED ATTENTION. Wheel and gun require simultaneous physical and spatial attention. Both pools −2. |
| Grapple an opponent while casting a spell | DIVIDED ATTENTION. Physical grip and magical working are live at the same time. Both pools −2. |
| Attack, then sprint to cover in the same Spotlight | Sequential. The attack resolves; then movement is a Minor Action. Not Divided Attention. |
| Two weapon strikes against two different targets | DIVIDED ATTENTION. Both hands engaged simultaneously on independent actions. Both pools −2. (See Ambidextrous Tag, below.) |
| Negotiate with a hostage-taker while a teammate flanks | Sequential. Your teammate acts on their own Spotlight. You are doing one thing. |
| Negotiate while actively scanning for a hidden sniper | DIVIDED ATTENTION. Your voice runs the negotiation; your eyes and psychic senses run a live scan. Both pools −2. |
| Pilot a vessel alone while operating its weapon systems | DIVIDED ATTENTION. Navigation and targeting both need you simultaneously. Both pools −2. |
| Maintain a sustained spell while attacking | Covered by the Sustained Spell / Concentration rule (Chapter Four). That system already prices in the mental split. Use that rule, not this one. |

### Failure Bleed

When a character acts under Divided Attention and one task fails, the Storyguide should consider whether that failure bleeds into the other task. This is not automatic — it depends on whether the two tasks share physical, mental, or spatial overlap.

Drive and shoot: if the shot goes wild and the recoil twists the shooter's torso, the Storyguide may call for a follow-up Pilot roll at +1 Difficulty to correct course. If the car hits a pothole first and the driver lurches violently, the next shot happens at +1 Difficulty from the flinch. The tasks inform each other in both directions.

Grapple and cast: if the casting roll fails and the working collapses, the physical grip is not necessarily affected — one is mental, one is physical. But a botch on the casting roll might deliver a psychic backlash to the mage's concentration, imposing −1 die on the grapple check for the next action. Storyguide discretion applies. The test: would a reasonable observer watching this scene say "of course one affected the other — one demand overloaded the other"? If yes, apply failure bleed. If the tasks draw on genuinely separate resources, failure in one stays isolated.

### Multiple Examples

> EXAMPLE 1 — DRIVE AND SHOOT
> 
> Yusuf (Son of Ether) is driving the cabal's car through a parking structure at speed while a Black Dog Security vehicle pulls alongside. He wants to swerve to block their line on Priya while simultaneously firing out his window.
> 
> Storyguide: "That is Divided Attention — steering and shooting simultaneously. Both pools take −2."
> 
> Pilot pool: Pilot 3 + Cunning 4 = 7 dice → 7 − 2 = 5 dice.
> Ranged pool: Ranged 2 + Dexterity 3 = 5 dice → 5 − 2 = 3 dice.
> 
> Pilot roll (5 dice, Difficulty 2 — tight quarters and speed): 9, 8, 7, 4, 2 = 3 hits. Swerve succeeds. Priya's cover lane opens.
> Ranged roll (3 dice, Difficulty 1): 9, 5, 3 = 1 hit. Barely connects — 2 Lethal on the security driver. Enough to distract but not stop.
> 
> The alternative: Yusuf takes the swerve as his full Major Action (7 dice). Priya fires on her own Spotlight. No penalty — sequential. The Divided Attention option trades dice for simultaneity.

> EXAMPLE 2 — GRAPPLE AND CAST
> 
> Rosamund (Verbena) has a Syndicate courier pinned against a wall — left arm across his throat. She wants to use her free right hand and Life 2 to accelerate his pulse to unconsciousness while the hold stays active.
> 
> Player: "Left arm holds him. Right hand casts. Both are live at once — I need the hold to keep him in range while the magic takes effect."
> Storyguide: "Physical grapple and active casting simultaneously. Divided Attention. Both −2."
> 
> Grapple (hold): Might 2 + Close Combat 3 = 5 dice → 5 − 2 = 3 dice. Difficulty 1.
> Life 2 casting: Life 2 + Resolve 4 = 6 dice → 6 − 2 = 4 dice. Difficulty 2.
> 
> Grapple (3 dice): 9, 7, 3 = 2 hits. Hold maintained.
> Casting (4 dice): 8, 8, 7, 4 = 3 hits. Effect lands. Pulse spikes at 220 bpm. The courier goes limp within seconds.
> 
> Note: If Rosamund dropped the hold and stepped back before casting, she rolls Life 2 at full pool (6 dice, no penalty) — but the courier can run. Divided Attention trades dice for the ability to do both at once.

> EXAMPLE 3 — CAST TWO SPELLS IN ONE ROUND (Phase Initiative)
> 
> Priya (Virtual Adept) needs a Forces 1 scatter-pulse at SF 1 to disrupt the mercs' aim AND a Correspondence 2 teleport at SF 2 to extract her fallen companion. Both in the same round.
> 
> Player: "Forces 1 pulse at SF 1 and Correspondence 2 teleport at SF 2. Both this round."
> Storyguide: "You are holding two magical constructs in your mind simultaneously — the pulse builds while the teleport framework assembles. That is Divided Attention. Phase Initiative still sequences when they fire: pulse goes at SF 1, teleport at SF 2. But your mental load is split from the moment of declaration. Both casting pools take −2 dice. EACH GENERATES ITS OWN PARADOX INDEPENDENTLY."
> 
> Forces 1 + Cunning 4 = 5 dice → 5 − 2 = 3 dice. Difficulty 1. Coincidental. Zero Paradox.
> Correspondence 2 + Cunning 4 = 6 dice → 6 − 2 = 4 dice. Difficulty 2 (Vulgar, no witnesses). +2 Paradox.
> 
> The reduced pools tighten both rolls. At Cunning 4, both are still viable — the teleport clears Difficulty 2 on 4 dice with a good roll. At Cunning 2, the teleport pool would be 4 − 2 = 2 dice against Difficulty 2, which is barely possible. At that point, the player might choose to cast one spell only. Divided Attention is always a conscious trade: do you need both now enough to pay the cost?

> EXAMPLE 4 — NEGOTIATE WHILE SCANNING FOR A SNIPER
> 
> Brother Agios (Order of Hermes) is negotiating the release of a captive with a Technocracy field commander. He suspects a sniper on the roof and wants to run an active Mind 1 scan for hostile intent while keeping the negotiation alive.
> 
> Player: "Can I keep talking while my Mind 1 is actively reading the emotional field for kill-intent?"
> Storyguide: "A background passive awareness running on the edge of your focus is Sustained Spell — Minor Action cost, −1 die to new castings (see Chapter Four). But an active, directed scan for a specific signal while simultaneously managing a high-stakes conversation? That is Divided Attention. You are genuinely split between two tasks that both need you present to them."
>
