> Negotiation: Empathy 4 + Presence 3 = 7 dice → 7 − 2 = 5 dice.
> Mind 1 scan: Mind 1 + Cunning 4 = 5 dice → 5 − 2 = 3 dice. Difficulty 1.
> 
> Negotiation (5 dice): 9, 8, 7, 4, 3 = 3 hits. The commander is genuinely wavering.
> Mind scan (3 dice): 8, 6, 3 = 1 hit. Agios catches a flicker — someone nearby has spiked anxiety. Not a kill-order yet, but someone is very nervous about something.
> 
> The key distinction: a hum-in-the-background passive is Sustained Spell. An active, attention-demanding second task is Divided Attention. When in doubt, the Storyguide asks: "Does this second thing require you to be consciously attending to it this round?" If yes: Divided Attention.

> EXAMPLE 5 — TWO-WEAPON FIGHTING AND THE AMBIDEXTROUS TAG
> 
> Valentina (Akashayana) fights with a blade in her right hand and a weighted chain in her left. She wants to attack two different targets simultaneously.
> 
> Player: "Blade on the agent in front of me. Chain on the one at my left. Same instant."
> Storyguide: "Two simultaneous attack actions — both hands engaged on independent tasks. Divided Attention. Both pools −2."
> 
> Blade: Close Combat 4 + Dexterity 4 = 8 dice → 8 − 2 = 6 dice.
> Chain: Close Combat 4 + Dexterity 4 = 8 dice → 8 − 2 = 6 dice.
> 
> PLAYER: "This is her entire combat style. She has trained dual-simultaneous attacks for years. Can I take a Tag to represent that? Ambidextrous?"
> 
> STORYGUIDE: "Good call. Here is how the Tag works: when both Divided Attention tasks are physical and both-hands-engaged — dual attacks, strike plus deflect, grapple plus strike, drive plus shoot — you reduce the penalty from −2 to −1 on both pools. Not eliminated; you are still splitting focus. But your training makes the split cost you less. It does not apply when one task is magical or mental — you cannot Ambidextrous your way out of a combat-and-cast split."
> 
> PLAYER: "And the Compel?"
> 
> STORYGUIDE: "Your hands want to be doing different things at all times. Situations requiring total single-unit commitment of both hands — surgery, threading something under pressure, catching a fragile object with both arms at once — your trained dual-task reflex fires at the wrong moment. +1 Difficulty on the affected roll."
> 
> With [Ambidextrous] invoked: Blade 8 − 1 = 7 dice. Chain 8 − 1 = 7 dice.
> The Tag is narrow but real. It rewards a specific archetype without being broadly applicable — it does not help when one task is a spell, a social action, or anything mental.

> EXAMPLE 6 — SOLO PILOT AND GUNNER
> 
> Yusuf is alone in an Etherite ornithopter during a chase. He needs to fly evasive maneuvers while firing the craft's weapon array — there is no copilot.
> 
> Player: "I fly and fire simultaneously. Solo."
> Storyguide: "If the vessel had dedicated stations, the gunner would be a separate character acting on their own Spotlight. But you are alone. Both tasks need you simultaneously. Divided Attention."
> 
> Pilot: Pilot 3 + Cunning 4 = 7 dice → 7 − 2 = 5 dice. Difficulty 2 (evasive flight).
> Weapon Array: Technology 2 + Cunning 4 = 6 dice → 6 − 2 = 4 dice. Difficulty 1.
> 
> Pilot (5 dice, Diff 2): 8, 8, 7, 4, 2 = 3 hits. Evasion holds.
> Weapon (4 dice, Diff 1): 9, 7, 4, 3 = 2 hits. 1 Trick Hit. Weapon connects; Breach hit damages the target's hull.
> 
> Failure bleed check: the Pilot roll succeeded, so no cascade. Had the Pilot roll failed, the Storyguide might rule: "The lurch off-course threw your weapon alignment. Your next weapons roll comes at +1 Difficulty as you reacquire the target." Failure in one task reaching into the other is the signature risk of Divided Attention.

> EXAMPLE 7 — HACKING WHILE SUSTAINING AN ILLUSION (Non-Combat)
> 
> Zara (Virtual Adept, Mind 2, Data 3) has conjured a sustained Mind 2 illusion of an empty server rack in a secure lab — a convincing fabrication that keeps a security camera's patrol loop running on a false visual. While the illusion holds, she wants to actively hack the lab's data vault.
> 
> Player: "I maintain the illusion and start pulling the encryption keys at the same time. The illusion needs my active attention to respond to the camera's panning, and the hack needs me fully in the system."
> Storyguide: "Maintaining a static, unchanging illusion would be a Sustained Spell — Minor Action cost, background focus. But you're describing a dynamic illusion that adapts to the camera's motion in real time. That is active attention. So is the hack. Both tasks need you simultaneously — Divided Attention. Both pools −2."
> 
> Mind 2 (adaptive illusion): Mind 2 + Cunning 4 = 6 dice → 6 − 2 = 4 dice. Difficulty 2 (sustained, dynamic). Coincidental in a tech lab.
> Hacking (data intrusion): Technology 4 + Cunning 4 = 8 dice → 8 − 2 = 6 dice. Difficulty 2 (encrypted vault).
> 
> Illusion (4 dice, Diff 2): 9, 8, 4, 3 = 2 hits. The illusion holds — the camera sees a stable empty rack.
> Hack (6 dice, Diff 2): 9, 8, 8, 7, 3, 2 = 4 hits. 2 Trick Hits. Encryption falls. Spend 1 Trick Hit → clean extraction, no access log entry. Spend 1 Trick Hit → bonus data layer unlocked.
> 
> Paradox: 0 for both (both Coincidental in context). No bleed — both tasks succeeded cleanly.
> 
> Note the key distinction the Storyguide applied: a static illusion is Sustained Spell (see Chapter Four). The moment the illusion needs to react and adapt — answering the environment rather than just existing in it — it becomes a genuine second task. The same logic applies to Node defense: holding a Ward passively is Sustained Spell; actively reshaping it in response to probing is Divided Attention.

The Ambidextrous Tag noted in Example 5 is a Character Condition Tag that can be created collaboratively with Storyguide approval when a player's combat style consistently demonstrates dual-simultaneous physical mastery. It applies only when both divided-attention tasks are physical and both-hands-engaged. It does not reduce the penalty when one task is magical, mental, or verbal. See Appendix J for full Tag creation guidelines.

Magical solutions to Divided Attention penalties exist. A Time 1 effect giving heightened reaction time, a Mind 2 working that optimizes cognitive load, or a Life 3 enhancement accelerating neural processing can each reduce a −2 penalty to −1 or remove it for a scene — at the cost of a casting action and Paradox accumulation.

## Attacks

Making an attack is a Major Action. You declare the attack, name the target, describe the method with at least a sentence of physical specificity (what are you doing? how? where is the weapon going?), and roll. The number of hits on the roll is compared against the Difficulty. If hits meet or exceed Difficulty, the attack lands. The target applies Armor to the damage and marks what remains on their Health track.

The declaration requirement is not bureaucratic — it serves the fiction. "I attack" is a slot machine pull. "I drive my elbow into his solar plexus while he's still processing the dodge and then step behind him to use him as a shield" is a combat scene. The Storyguide rewards specificity with narrative richness, and occasionally with mechanical benefit when the described action creates a genuine tactical opening.

### Attack Pools by Type

| Attack Type | Pool |
| --- | --- |
| Unarmed strike (power focus) | Athletics + Might |
| Unarmed strike (precision focus) | Athletics + Dexterity |
| Melee weapon (power) | Close Combat + Might |
| Melee weapon (precision) | Close Combat + Dexterity |
| Thrown object | Athletics + Dexterity |
| Pistol / light firearm | Ranged Combat + Dexterity |
| Rifle / heavy firearm | Ranged Combat + Dexterity |
| Bow / crossbow | Ranged Combat + Dexterity |
| Magical attack (direct) | Relevant Sphere + Casting Attribute (see Chapter 4) |
| Wonder (targeted effect) | Relevant Sphere + Casting Attribute OR flat dice pool specified by Wonder |

The choice between power and precision in melee is a meaningful tactical decision, not an afterthought. Power attacks deal more damage when they land because Might contributes more to raw harm. Precision attacks are more reliable against defended or moving targets because Dexterity contributes more to accuracy. A high-Might brawler who cannot hit a moving target is useless; a high-Dexterity knife fighter who cannot break through armor is equally frustrated.

### Attack Difficulties

Every attack has a base Difficulty of 1 — representing an unobstructed shot or strike at a visible, stationary target in adequate lighting with no unusual environmental factors. Reality almost never provides this baseline, so most attacks are made at higher Difficulties. Modify the base Difficulty based on the situation:

| Situation | Difficulty Modifier |
| --- | --- |
| Target in open, well-lit, stationary | Base Difficulty 1 (no modifier) |
| Target moving at walking speed | +0 (standard combat movement already factored) |
| Target sprinting or making dramatic evasive moves | +1 |
| Target behind partial cover (hip-level barrier, doorway) | +1 |
| Target behind heavy cover (full vehicle, concrete wall) | +2 |
| Target fully concealed (estimated position only) | +3 |
| Attacker moving faster than a step | +1 |
| Attacker prone attempting ranged attack | +1 |
| Attacker on unstable ground (moving vehicle, slick ice) | +1 |
| Attacker in darkness or effectively blinded | +2 |
| Called shot to specific body part | +1 to +2 depending on target size |
| Long range (past weapon's optimal bracket) | +1 per range bracket beyond optimal |
| Point blank range (<1m) with firearm | +1 (cannot aim properly at this distance) |
| Point blank range (<1m) with melee | -1 (opponent cannot dodge properly) |
| Multiple attackers flanking (coordinated) | -1 per flanker beyond first, max -2 |
| Target is Prone (ranged attack) | +1 (smaller profile) |
| Target is Prone (melee attack) | -1 (easily accessible) |

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Difficulty stacking: Agent Carver is firing a handgun at Vikram, who is behind a concrete pillar (heavy cover, +2) and sprinting toward a stairwell (+1). Carver is also standing on the hood of a moving car (+1). Base Difficulty 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 = Difficulty 5. Carver has Ranged Combat 3 + Dexterity 4 = 7 dice. He needs 5 hits. This is possible but not reliable — exactly as it should be when firing from a moving car at a sprinting target behind cover.

### Trick Hits

When an attacker scores more hits than the Difficulty threshold, the excess hits become Trick Hits. Each Trick Hit is a discrete narrative resource that can be spent on Combat Tricks rather than converting directly into damage. The attacker declares how they spend Trick Hits immediately after the roll, before the target responds. Trick Hits represent the attacker's superior performance in that exchange — they did not just hit, they hit well, and they can choose what "hitting well" means in this context.

Combat Tricks are not abstract mechanical bonuses — they are specific narrative outcomes that the attacker achieves through superior execution. A Disarm is a specific physical motion: hooking a thumb under the opponent's trigger guard, slapping the wrist at precisely the right moment, driving a knee into the weapon hand. Narrate the Trick as part of the Spotlight description. Numbers alone are never enough.

- Extra Damage: +1 damage die to the attack. This stacks — spending three Trick Hits on Extra Damage adds 3 dice to the damage roll. Represents a particularly clean, powerful, or precisely targeted hit.
- Disarm: The target drops whatever they are holding in one hand. It falls to the ground at their feet or (on a particularly dramatic hit, at the Storyguide's discretion) is sent skittering across the floor out of easy reach. Recovering a dropped weapon requires a Minor Action.
- Knockdown: The target is knocked Prone. They are immediately on the ground and must spend a Minor Action to stand. See Status Conditions for the full mechanical effects of Prone.
- Stagger: The target loses their next Minor Action. They are momentarily disrupted, their footing broken, their next move slightly slower. Does not affect their Major Action.
- Wound: A specific hit to a meaningful location — the weapon hand, the dominant eye, the knee, the throat. The Storyguide determines an appropriate and specific penalty. A slashed weapon hand means -2 dice to all weapon attacks until treated. A blow to the knee means movement reduced to one step per Spotlight. This is a powerful option but requires vivid narration.
- Setup: The next ally who attacks this target gains +1 die on that attack. Represents an opening created by your attack — you drew their defense, exposed a flank, distracted their guard.
- Pin: The target cannot move from their current position until they spend a Major Action to break free. Represents a specific physical restraint — a knife driven through a sleeve into a wall, a tackle that traps legs, a spell-anchoring effect.
- Disrupt: Against a mage maintaining an active spell, this forces a Concentration roll (Composure + Resolve, Difficulty equal to hits scored) or the active spell collapses. This is the main mundane counter to sustained magical effects and is extremely valuable.
- Expose: Remove one point of the target's active dodge bonus for the rest of the round. Represents breaking through their defensive form.
---

## Defense

Unlike attack rolls, defense is not automatically rolled on every incoming attack. The baseline assumption of Storypath Ultra combat is that characters in a fight are already moving, shifting, angling, and doing everything their body knows how to do to stay alive. This baseline evasiveness — the ducked head, the slight sidestep, the reflexive flinch — is already factored into the Difficulty of attack rolls. A base Difficulty 1 attack is hard to make precisely because the target is a moving, breathing, combat-aware creature, not a stationary target.

Characters who want to do better than this baseline can spend actions to improve their defense beyond what their bodies provide automatically. Active defensive choices are powerful but they come at the cost of offensive capability. This is one of the core tactical tensions in Mage combat: do you hit hard this round, or do you make sure you survive to hit hard next round?

### Active Dodge — Major Action

A character who devotes their entire Major Action to actively dodging rolls Athletics + Dexterity. Each hit on this roll adds +1 Difficulty to all incoming attacks against them for the remainder of the Round. This is the most powerful defensive option available without magic — it can make a character nearly impossible to hit — but it sacrifices all offensive capability. A character who fully dodges is not attacking, not casting, not doing anything else of mechanical consequence. They are surviving.

Active Dodge is the right choice when the character is outgunned, outnumbered, or waiting for help. It is the tactical equivalent of holding ground under fire rather than advancing. Verbena practitioners with high Athletics can become extremely difficult targets while maintaining this discipline, particularly if they are also benefiting from nature-attuned speed enhancement.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Active dodge: Rosamund (Athletics 1, Dexterity 3) devotes her Spotlight to dodging as three cultists close in. She rolls 4 dice and scores 2 hits. All attacks against her this round are at +2 Difficulty. The cultists have Close Combat 2 + Might 3 = 5 dice, normally needing 1 hit. With +2 Difficulty they need 3 hits. Suddenly manageable becomes difficult. She survives the round without a scratch — and next round her allies act first.

### Parry — Minor Action (Melee Only)

Against a melee attack, a defender who is aware of the attack and has a free hand or a weapon to parry with can roll Close Combat + Dexterity as a Minor Action. This does not require spending the Major Action. Each hit on the parry roll cancels one hit from the incoming attack. You cannot parry firearms, explosions, or area effects. You can parry magical melee attacks if you have an appropriate magical weapon.

The key limitation of parry is that it is reactive — it only works against one specific incoming attack, and only if you are aware of it and capable of physically interposing your defense. A character who is Blinded cannot parry. A character who is grappled cannot parry effectively (Storyguide's judgment on whether the mechanics even allow it). A character who has already spent their Minor Action cannot parry.

### Counterattack

If a parry is fully successful — the parry roll scores more hits than the attacker's excess hits, meaning the attack is completely negated — the defender may immediately make a free unarmed strike or Close Combat attack against their attacker at no action cost. This counterattack occurs at the end of the same Spotlight, before Initiative moves on. The counterattack uses the standard attack pool and damage, but cannot itself be parried (the attacker's momentum was just broken; they are not in position to defend).

This rule rewards skillful defensive play and creates swings in narrative momentum. The attacker thought they were making a move; instead they handed the defender an opening. Skilled practitioners of defensive martial arts — Akashic Brothers, trained Euthanatos, military-trained Technocrats who have drilled defensive counters — exploit this constantly.

### Cover

Physical cover is always available as a Minor Action and does not require a roll. Duck behind a car, drop below a windowsill, press behind a doorframe. Cover adds Difficulty to incoming attacks passively and automatically, without requiring the defended character to do anything more than occupy the right position.

| Cover Type | Difficulty Added to Attacker \| Notes |
| --- | --- |
| Partial cover — hip-level barrier, edge of doorway, scattered furniture | +1 \| Defender can still act freely; cover only works from one direction |
| Heavy cover — full vehicle body, concrete pillar, heavy desk | +2 \| Firing from heavy cover: expose self briefly for +1 Diff window |
| Full cover — completely hidden behind barrier | No direct attack possible; estimated-position attack at +3 Diff |

Cover protects from specific directions. A character behind a parked car has cover from the street side. If an attacker flanks around the back of the car, the cover no longer applies. This creates tactical movement as a meaningful consideration: finding cover that protects all angles, or forcing opponents out of cover by threatening their flanks.

Taking cover and attacking from cover simultaneously is possible. Firing from behind partial cover imposes no penalty on the defender's attack roll. Firing from behind heavy cover requires exposing briefly — during the round the character fires from heavy cover, the Difficulty bonus drops to +1 (not +2) as they lean out. A character who is fully behind cover and wishes to attack must first spend a Minor Action to expose, then fire.

---

## Damage Types

Every source of harm in Mage deals one of three damage types. These types differ in how they interact with Armor, how quickly they heal, and what they represent in the physical and spiritual fiction of the World of Darkness. Choosing the right damage type for a magical attack is not just a mechanical decision — it is a statement about the nature of the power being wielded and its relationship to consensus reality.

### Bashing Damage

Bashing damage represents blunt trauma, physical impact, concussive force, pain, and the kind of injury the human body is actually designed to withstand and recover from. Most unarmed strikes deal Bashing. A character can survive an enormous amount of Bashing damage because Bashing represents the body being punished and stressed rather than fundamentally broken. The bruised rib, the black eye, the concussive headache, the muscle that cannot quite work at full efficiency — all of this is Bashing.

Bashing heals quickly and completely: a full night of rest clears all Bashing damage. Light medical attention — bandages, ice packs, rest, basic first aid — can speed this to a few hours. There are no long-term consequences for Bashing damage that is fully healed. You took a beating; you recovered; you move on. This is mechanically significant: a mage who uses only Bashing-dealing attacks is less dangerous in the short term but forces enemies to track a damage type that evaporates by morning.

Against Armor: Armor reduces Bashing damage normally, at the full armor rating. A leather jacket (Armor 1) reduces an unarmed punch from 1 Bashing to 0 Bashing — no damage at all. Heavy armor against unarmed opponents is essentially impenetrable.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Bashing in context: Tobias punches a corrupt police officer three times in a scuffle, marking 3 Bashing on the officer's Health track. The officer is Impaired (3+ boxes filled) and cannot fight effectively. After the encounter, the officer sleeps it off in the precinct break room. By morning, all 3 boxes are clear. There is no lasting record of the injury in his pattern. Tobias's choice to use fists rather than magic left no trace that any Sphere could detect.

### Lethal Damage

Lethal damage represents cuts, gunshot wounds, stab wounds, burns, falls onto hard surfaces, and other injuries that physically damage tissue and threaten life if untreated. Most weapons deal Lethal. A person can survive significant Lethal damage, but it requires medical attention and time — the body does not simply absorb and dismiss a bullet wound the way it dismisses a bruise. A character who reaches the end of their Health track with Lethal damage is actively dying and needs immediate help.

Lethal heals slowly: 1 box per day of rest without medical care; 2 boxes per day with competent medical attention and proper conditions; up to 3 boxes per day with Life magic applied daily. This slow rate means that a character who takes serious Lethal damage in one fight carries that damage into the next. Combat consequences compound. A mage who takes 4 Lethal in a Monday fight and faces another encounter on Tuesday is starting that encounter already Impaired, with all the mechanical penalties that implies.

Against Armor: Armor reduces Lethal damage normally. A standard tactical vest (Armor 3) against a handgun (4 Lethal) reduces the damage to 1 Lethal — still painful and still dangerous, but survivable in a way that an unarmored hit is not.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Lethal consequences: Vikram takes 5 Lethal from an NWO sniper during a rooftop pursuit. His Health track has 6 boxes; 5 are now filled. He is Critical (-2 dice to all rolls) and has one box left. He crashes through a skylight to escape and takes 1 Bashing from the fall — that Bashing converts to Lethal because his track is full, bringing him to Incapacitated. He is hospitalized for two days, receiving competent medical care. He heals 2 Lethal per day: by day 3, he has cleared 4 Lethal and is functional again. The remaining 1 Lethal takes another day. Four days to fully recover from a single sniper hit. That is the weight of Lethal damage.

### Aggravated Damage

Aggravated damage represents harm that the body fundamentally cannot ignore through its natural processes — supernatural harm that attacks the pattern of a being's existence rather than merely its physical tissue. Paradox backlash burning out the resonance of a mage's soul. Werewolf claws that tear through spiritual flesh as well as physical. Fire applied by a being whose nature is fire. Ancient curses. Direct Prime strikes that dissolve the energetic pattern underlying physical form. The spiritual toxin of a Bane's claws. All of these deal Aggravated damage.

Aggravated damage is horrifying because most things that protect against mundane harm — armor, ordinary healing, low-level Life magic — cannot touch it. The body does not know how to heal itself from a wound in its fundamental pattern. Standard healing processes repair tissue; they cannot repair a hole in the soul's blueprint.

Aggravated heals very slowly: 1 box per week of rest; 1 box per day with sustained Life 4+ healing magic applied daily; 2 boxes per day with Life 5 in a Node-connected space filled with Quintessence. A mage who has taken significant Aggravated damage is carrying that wound for weeks or months. It is a permanent character-level consequence.

Against Armor: Standard physical armor does NOT reduce Aggravated damage. It passes through body armor, magical tactical weaves, and mundane protection as if they were not there. Only specially enchanted armor — constructed with Matter 4 at minimum, Life 4 for biological integration, or Prime 3 for pattern-level resistance — provides any protection against Aggravated damage, and even then only at a reduced rate (see Appendix I).

Converting Damage: The Health track converts damage types when it fills. If all boxes fill with Bashing and more Bashing arrives, the overflow converts to Lethal (the body cannot absorb more impact without tissue damage). If all boxes fill with Lethal and more Lethal arrives, the overflow converts to Aggravated (wounds this severe begin attacking the pattern itself). This conversion mechanic means that sustained beatings, even with ostensibly non-lethal strikes, can become life-threatening.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Aggravated from Paradox: Maya pushed her Paradox Pool to 9 dice over the course of a combat. The Paradox explodes during the scene's resolution. Among the results: a Catastrophe — 3 Aggravated damage to Maya directly. Her Health track is already showing 4 Lethal from the fight. Now it shows 3 Aggravated on top of that. She is Incapacitated, her last boxes filled with Aggravated. She will be unconscious for days and needs Life 4 healing to make meaningful recovery. Without it, she heals 1 box per week — and she has 3 boxes of Aggravated to clear. Three weeks of healing time from a single Paradox backlash.

---

## The Health Track

Every character has a Health track representing their capacity to absorb damage before being unable to continue functioning. This is not a measure of how much punishment a body can theoretically absorb — it is a dramatic pacing tool that determines when a character is forced out of action and what mechanical penalties accumulate as they approach that threshold.

**Health Track — SPU Injury Band Structure**

The Health track uses a fixed four-band structure mirroring the Storypath Ultra injury level design. This guarantees that every character — regardless of Stamina — experiences all four threshold stages. Stamina does not simply add more boxes to an undifferentiated pool; it extends the width of the first and most forgiving band, the Bruised band, giving tougher characters more breathing room before penalties begin.

The four bands are: BRUISED (2 + Stamina boxes, no penalty), IMPAIRED (2 boxes, −1 die to all rolls), CRITICAL (2 boxes, −2 dice to all rolls), and INCAPACITATED (1 box, cannot act). Total Health boxes = 7 + Stamina. A character with Stamina 1 has 8 boxes total: three Bruised boxes, two Impaired, two Critical, and one Incapacitated. A character with Stamina 3 has 10 boxes: five Bruised, two Impaired, two Critical, one Incapacitated. A character with Stamina 5 has 12 boxes: seven Bruised, two Impaired, two Critical, one Incapacitated. Record your band sizes on the character sheet at character creation. Damage fills left to right across all bands in sequence.

### Marking Damage and Track Penalties

Damage is marked on the Health track from left to right. The track holds both Bashing (marked as B) and Lethal (marked as L) in the same boxes. Aggravated (marked as A) occupies boxes just like the others but cannot be reduced by armor, takes the longest to heal, and converts all remaining Lethal to Aggravated when it begins filling the track at the end.

As boxes fill, the character accumulates physical penalties that affect everything they do. These penalties are cumulative and automatic — they do not require the Storyguide to remind players, though players sometimes forget them in the heat of play.

| Injury Band | Boxes \| Penalty \| Description |
| --- | --- |
| BRUISED | 2 + Stamina boxes \| No penalty \| Scratched, winded, bleeding — but fully functional. The body absorbs punishment here before any mechanical cost. |
| IMPAIRED | 2 boxes \| −1 die to all rolls \| Genuinely hurt. Pain intrudes on every action. The body is working against the mind. |
| CRITICAL | 2 boxes \| −2 dice to all rolls \| Seriously wounded. Every movement costs effort the character does not have. Survival is now a conscious act. |
| INCAPACITATED | 1 box \| Cannot take voluntary actions \| Down. Unconscious, collapsed, or unable to act. Removed from the fight. |

```
HEALTH TRACK — VISUAL BAND REFERENCE
------------------------------------
Total boxes = 7 + Stamina.  The Bruised band grows with Stamina; the other three bands are always fixed.

STAMINA 3  (10 boxes total)
  [ ][ ][ ][ ][ ]  |  [ ][ ]  |  [ ][ ]  |  [ ]
   ←——— BRUISED ×5 ———→  IMPAIRED  CRITICAL  INCAP
   No penalty              −1 die     −2 dice   Cannot act

STAMINA 5  (12 boxes total)
  [ ][ ][ ][ ][ ][ ][ ]  |  [ ][ ]  |  [ ][ ]  |  [ ]
   ←—————— BRUISED ×7 ——————→  IMPAIRED  CRITICAL  INCAP
   No penalty                        −1 die     −2 dice   Cannot act

Damage marks fill left to right. B = Bashing  L = Lethal  A = Aggravated.
Your penalty is determined by which BAND your total damage has pushed you into — not a raw box count.
```

These penalties apply regardless of damage type. Three boxes of Bashing gives the same -1 die as three boxes of Lethal. The body does not distinguish between types when assessing its current functional capacity — it only knows how much of its reserve has been consumed.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Track progression: Rosamund has Stamina 2. Her Health track is: [B][B][B][B] | [I][I] | [C][C] | [X] — 4 Bruised boxes, 2 Impaired, 2 Critical, 1 Incapacitated, 9 total. Round 1: 1 Bashing (punch to the ribs) — first Bruised box filled. No penalty. Round 2: 2 Lethal (knife) — Bruised boxes 2 and 3 filled. Still no penalty; she is still in the Bruised band. Round 3: 1 Lethal (second knife thrust) — the fourth and final Bruised box fills. Still no penalty. Round 4: 2 more Lethal — she enters the Impaired band (box 5 fills, −1 die to all rolls), then the second Impaired box fills (box 6). Round 5: 1 Lethal — she enters the Critical band (box 7 fills, −2 dice to all rolls). Two Critical boxes remain. She is badly hurt but still upright. It took seven points of damage across five rounds to push Rosamund from fully functional to Critical. The Bruised band gave her four boxes of punishment before any penalty touched her. That is what high Stamina buys.

### Incapacitation

When the last box of the Health track is filled, the character is Incapacitated. They fall unconscious, collapse in agony, or are simply no longer capable of voluntary action. They cannot take actions. They cannot defend. They are, for all combat purposes, removed from the fight.

What happens next depends on the nature of the damage. An Incapacitated character whose track is filled mostly or entirely with Bashing will regain consciousness on their own at the end of the scene — bruised, aching, and furious, but functional. An Incapacitated character whose last boxes are Lethal or Aggravated needs immediate medical attention. Without it, their condition deteriorates: treat as 1 Lethal box per hour converting to Aggravated as wounds go untreated. A character who reaches full Aggravated damage on all boxes dies.

The Storyguide should narrate Incapacitation vividly. This is a dramatic moment — the moment when a player character is no longer in control of their own fate. What do the enemies do? What do the allies do? Does an NWO agent pick up the incapacitated mage's phone? Does a fleeing Nephandi stop to ensure the kill? These choices define the story that follows.

### Death

A character dies when their Health track fills completely with Aggravated damage. This is not automatic on reaching Incapacitation from Lethal damage — it requires either sustained untreated conversion of Lethal to Aggravated over hours, direct Aggravated damage piled onto a track already at the limit, or deliberate killing action by an opponent against an already-Incapacitated character.

Player character death should be a dramatic moment, not an arithmetic surprise. The Storyguide should always signal clearly when death is a realistic possibility: describe the severity of wounds, remind players of their current track state, and let the fiction carry the weight of what is happening. Death in Mage is meaningful. It ends a character's story at the table, but it does not end their story in the world — former players can choose to have their character's death have lasting narrative consequences, and mages who die with significant Paradox pools have a tendency to make their exits spectacular.

---

```
OPTIONAL DEPTH — The Armor System (Appendix I)
----------------------------------------------
The armor rules in this chapter cover standard play: Armor rating reduces Bashing and Lethal damage, and does not reduce Aggravated damage unless enchanted. This is all you need for most chronicles. Appendix I: The Armor System is an optional deep-dive for groups who want full armor construction rules, Soak vs. Resistance variants, Sturdy ratings, Condition Tracks for armor, and detailed coverage of magical and exotic materials. It can be adopted mid-chronicle without affecting existing characters. Skip Appendix I if you want lean, fast-playing armor rules — the core coverage here is complete.
```

## Armor

Armor reduces incoming Bashing and Lethal damage by its rating. This is a flat subtraction applied before the damage is marked on the Health track: if armor has a rating of 3 and a weapon delivers 5 Lethal, the target takes 2 Lethal and marks 2 boxes. If the armor rating meets or exceeds the damage rating, the attack deals no damage — the armor absorbed it entirely. This simple mechanic creates a clear reward for preparation: wearing armor in a world of guns is not paranoia, it is survival.

Armor does NOT reduce Aggravated damage unless it has been specifically enchanted to do so — a process requiring significant magical investment and described in full in Appendix I. This means that even the heaviest mundane protection is worthless against the most dangerous attacks in the supernatural world. Werewolf claws, Paradox backlash, Prime pattern strikes, Bane spirit toxin — all of these flow through physical armor as if it were gauze. Planning to survive in the supernatural world requires supernatural protection.

| Armor Type | Rating \| Type Protected \| Notes |
| --- | --- |
| Leather jacket / thick clothing | 1 \| Bashing only \| Provides negligible protection vs. firearms; purely social-layer armor |
| Light body armor (concealable vest) | 2 \| Bashing and Lethal \| Can be worn under normal clothing; requires no special adjustment |
| Standard tactical vest with trauma plates | 3 \| Bashing and Lethal \| Visible to anyone who looks; slight movement restriction |
| Military full-body armor (plates + helmet) | 4 \| Bashing and Lethal \| Obvious and distinctive; -1 die to Athletics-based movement rolls |
| Powered exoskeleton or HIT Mark chassis | 5 \| Bashing and Lethal \| Extremely obvious; may restrict Dexterity (Storyguide's judgment) |
| Technocratic smart-weave (worn as suit) | 3 \| Bashing and Lethal \| Visually indistinguishable from business attire; NWO standard issue |
| Iteration X subdermal plating | 3 \| Bashing and Lethal \| Implanted; invisible; does not stack with worn armor (same layer) |
| Mage Armor — Forces 3 or Life 3 sustained | 3 vs. Lethal; 1 vs. Aggravated \| Vulgar if visible; costs 1 die to all actions while maintained |
| Pattern Hardening — Life 3 or Matter 3 | +2 to existing armor \| One scene duration; Vulgar; cannot be maintained, must be reapplied |

*See Appendix I: The Armor System for full rules on constructing enchanted armor, combining armor types, and specific materials (cold iron, orichalcum-weave, Pattern-hardened composites) and their properties against supernatural damage types.*

### Armor vs. Specific Attacks

Some attacks ignore armor entirely or penetrate it more effectively than standard damage:

- Armor-Piercing Ammunition: Specifically engineered to defeat armor. Reduces the target's effective armor rating by 2 for that attack (minimum 0). The Syndicate sells this commercially; the NWO issues it to field agents operating against enhanced targets. Getting your hands on it as a Tradition mage typically requires either exceptional contacts or theft.
- Magical Pattern Strikes (Prime 3): A direct attack on a being's magical pattern rather than their physical body. Bypasses mundane armor entirely. Deals Aggravated damage. One of the most dangerous offensive Sphere combinations available.
- Life or Matter Direct Attacks: A Life 3 working that directly disrupts biological function (cellular breakdown, neurological seizure) bypasses physical armor — the armor is on the outside; the magic is attacking the inside. Similarly, a Matter 3 attack that restructures the molecular bonds of a target's body bypasses their surface protection.
- Called Shots to Unarmored Locations: Targeting the gap at the armpit of a tactical vest, the unprotected back of the knee, the face above a ballistic collar. This ignores armor at +2 Difficulty to the attack roll. Specific location chosen before the roll; Storyguide determines which locations are actually exposed given the armor worn.
- Aggravated Damage Sources: Always bypass standard armor. No exceptions for mundane armor. See magical armor in Appendix I for the limits of enchanted protection.

### Stacking Armor

Armor sources do not add together freely. Physics and geometry impose limits: two things protecting the same location do not double the protection. The rule is: use the highest applicable rating and add 1 for each additional significant layer of protection covering the same location. A character wearing a tactical vest (Armor 3) and also maintaining Mage Armor (Armor 3) has total effective Armor 4, not 6. A character with Iteration X subdermal plating (Armor 3) and a tactical vest (Armor 3) has effective Armor 4.

The exception is magical armor that specifically states it adds to existing protection (like Pattern Hardening, which explicitly adds its bonus) rather than replacing it. Even stacked magical protection has a soft cap at Armor 6 — beyond that point, the Storyguide may rule that attacks find gaps regardless.

---

## Weapons and the Tag System

Every weapon in Mage has more character than a damage rating. The Tag system gives weapons (and many other combat elements) descriptive labels that carry both narrative weight and mechanical consequence. Tags are not passive bonuses — they are active aspects of how a weapon exists in the fiction, aspects that can be invoked to gain advantage or compelled by the Storyguide to create complications.

A Tag is a bracketed label: [Fast], [Brutal], [Loud], [Concealed], [Reload 2], [Area 5m], [Armor-Piercing], [Autofire], [Devastating]. Each Tag has a specific meaning in the fiction and a mechanical framework for invoke and compel.

### How Tags Work in Combat

**Invoking a Tag:**

A player may invoke a relevant Tag on their weapon or piece of equipment to gain +2 dice to a roll where that Tag is genuinely applicable, or to claim an automatic success on a less important task related to the Tag's nature. Invoking a Tag costs 1 Momentum (a shared resource described in Chapter 2) or can be granted freely by the Storyguide when the fiction particularly calls for it. You can only invoke each Tag once per Spotlight.

The key word is "relevant." A [Fast] knife does not give +2 dice to a disarm attempt against a slow opponent — it gives +2 dice when speed is genuinely the decisive factor: acting before a slower weapon can be drawn, striking before a mage completes a verbal component, interrupting an action where a split-second advantage changes the outcome. Players who invoke Tags carelessly (trying to apply them in situations where they do not logically apply) should be gently redirected by the Storyguide.

**Compelling a Tag:**

The Storyguide may compel a Tag — introduce a narrative complication arising naturally from that Tag's nature — in exchange for giving the player 1 Momentum. This is not a penalty; it is an invitation to richer fiction. The player may refuse the compel by spending 1 Momentum to push back, keeping their pool but accepting the simpler story. If they accept the compel, they gain 1 Momentum and deal with the complication.

Compels should feel like natural consequences of what the Tag describes, not arbitrary punishments. A [Loud] weapon being compelled to attract witnesses makes complete sense. A [Reload 2] weapon being compelled to run dry at the worst possible moment is classic drama. A [Concealed] weapon being compelled because the character forgot to re-conceal it after using it makes logical sense. The best compels are things the player looks at and thinks "yeah, of course, that would happen."

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Tag invoke: Rosamund's combat knife is tagged [Fast] and [Concealed]. She is in initiative order against a Syndicate enforcer who is drawing a heavy pistol (tagged [Loud] and [Slow Draw]). Rosamund's player says: "I invoke [Fast] — my knife is already in my hand and I'm striking before his draw clears the holster." The Storyguide agrees: +2 dice to Rosamund's attack roll this Spotlight. She rolls Close Combat + Dexterity + 2. The enforcer's gun is halfway out of the holster when the blade finds him.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Tag compel: Silas fires his elephant gun (tagged [Loud], [Devastating], [Reload 2]) at a Nephandi in an apartment building. He drops the Nephandi. The Storyguide offers a compel on [Loud]: "That shot is going to bring every Sleeper on this floor out of their apartments to investigate. You get 1 Momentum. But you have about fifteen seconds before someone calls the police and you're surrounded by witnesses — which means no more magic, no more gun, and a very complicated conversation." Silas's player accepts, gains the Momentum, and the scene pivots to a frantic exit. The gun solved one problem and created three more. That is a well-constructed compel.

### Edge Cases — Where the Judgment Call Actually Gets Made

The two examples above are the clean cases: an invoke that obviously applies, and a compel that obviously fits. Most tables run into trouble on the messier cases — the moment where a Tag is invoked but doesn't quite fit, or a compel that's technically defensible but feels unfair, or a scene with three Tags all screaming to be invoked at once. Below are worked examples of exactly those moments, because this is where "relies on GM judgment" stops being a throwaway phrase and starts being the actual game being played at the table.

> EDGE CASE — Disputed Invoke, and How to Say No Without Shutting the Player Down: Dex is fighting a Void Engineer technician in a server room. His combat knife is tagged [Fast] and [Concealed]. He's already drawn the knife two Phases ago and is mid-swing. His player says: "I invoke [Fast] for the attack — my knife's just quicker than his reactions." The Storyguide pauses: [Fast]'s actual invoke condition is about *speed being the decisive factor* — beating a slower draw, interrupting an action, seizing initiative advantage — not "my weapon type is generically fast." Dex already has the initiative advantage from the Phase order; nothing about this particular swing is being decided by speed specifically. The Storyguide says: "I don't think [Fast] is doing anything for you here — you're not racing him to anything, you already went first. If he was about to bring his own weapon up and you're beating him to the punch, that's exactly [Fast]. Is that what's happening?" Dex's player reconsiders: "No, he's already down, I'm just finishing him." The Storyguide: "Then it's just a regular attack roll — no Tag needed, and that's fine, not every good moment needs a mechanical bonus attached to it." No Momentum changes hands. The player isn't punished for trying; they're redirected, and the fiction stays honest. This is the failure mode "relies on GM judgment" is actually warning about — a GM who either rubber-stamps every invoke (Tags become free +2 dice on demand, which cheapens them) or who nitpicks every invoke into oblivion (players stop bothering to engage with Tags at all) has broken the subsystem in opposite directions. The fix in both cases is the same habit: ask "what specifically about the fiction right now makes this Tag true," out loud, before ruling.

> EDGE CASE — Refusing a Compel, and What That Costs: Continuing the Silas example above — a session later, Silas fires the elephant gun again, this time inside the Chantry's own warded sanctum during a defensive stand against a Technocracy incursion. The Storyguide offers a compel on [Loud]: "Same deal — the noise draws attention, 1 Momentum." Silas's player looks at the pool: the cabal has zero Momentum banked and is one bad roll from a Nephandi finishing the fight. She decides the Momentum isn't worth the complication this time and spends 1 Momentum to refuse — except she has none to spend. Refusing a compel costs 1 Momentum from the player's own pool; if the pool is empty, the compel cannot be refused, full stop — the Storyguide's offer stands and the complication happens regardless of whether the player "wants" it, though they still take the compensating Momentum if they accept it (which, at zero Momentum, is now clearly the better of two options). This is the mechanical teeth behind Momentum scarcity: a cabal that has been spending Momentum freely all session has less leverage to say no to the Storyguide's complications later in that same session. The lesson for the table: refusing is a resource decision, not a free veto, and a GM should never let a player "just say no" to a compel without spending the Momentum the rule actually requires — otherwise compels stop costing anything and the whole exchange collapses into the GM asking permission before every complication.

> EDGE CASE — Multiple Tags, One Roll, and the Order of Operations: Maya (Order of Hermes) casts a Forces effect through her tagged instrument, a signet ring [Focus: Fire] [Attuned] [Fragile], while standing in a burning building (an environmental Tag the Storyguide has put into play: [Structural Collapse Risk]). Her player wants to invoke [Focus: Fire] (the ring's Focus matches the effect's element) AND [Attuned] (the ring has bonded to her over multiple sessions) on the same casting roll. Two Tags can stack on a single roll as long as each is invoked separately (a separate Momentum spend per Tag, per the "up to the +5 Enhancement cap" rule from Chapter Two) and each is independently justified — the Storyguide should make the player state the justification for each one in turn, not accept "I invoke both" as a single bundled sentence. Here: "[Focus: Fire] — this is a fire effect, that's what the ring is for" (clean, +2 dice, 1 Momentum) and "[Attuned] — the ring and I have been through enough that it responds instantly to what I need" (also clean, +2 dice, 1 Momentum, since Attuned's invoke condition is about the bond itself, not the specific effect type). Total: +4 dice for 2 Momentum. The Storyguide could also compel [Fragile] right back in the same exchange — free-standing, not something the player chose, but a live option given the burning building: "The heat's already stressing the ring's setting. I'm compelling [Fragile] — after this casting, the stone cracks. It still works, but the Focus bonus doesn't apply until you get it repaired. 1 Momentum to you." Multiple Tags in one exchange are normal and expected in an important scene; the discipline is making each one earn its keep individually rather than letting a pile of Tags become a single vague "the vibes are right" bonus.

> EDGE CASE — A Compel That's Too Harsh, and How to Recalibrate Mid-Scene: A newer Storyguide, running the [Autofire] Tag on a PC's SMG, compels it as: "Your gun jams completely and permanently — you can never use it again this chronicle." The player is (understandably) upset — this isn't a complication, it's confiscating a piece of their character sheet with no recourse, and it came out of a Tag that's supposed to produce drama, not attrition. This is a real failure mode worth naming explicitly: a compel should always be *recoverable* within the scene or, at most, the session — a jam that costs a Major Action to clear (per [Autofire]'s actual listed Compel effect, above) is drama; a jam that's permanent is a Difficulty spike disguised as narrative color, and it will make players stop wanting to use tagged gear at all, which defeats the entire point of the Tag system. If a Storyguide realizes mid-scene they've overreached, the correct move is to walk it back openly at the table: "Actually, let me revise that — the gun jams for this scene, clears with a Major Action like the Tag says, I got carried away." This is not a loss of authority; reversing an overcorrected compel in front of the players models exactly the judgment the system asks GMs to exercise, and it costs nothing except a moment's honesty.

> EDGE CASE — Compelling a Character Tag Instead of an Equipment Tag: Character Tags (see above) compel the same way equipment Tags do, but the complications tend to be social or narrative rather than mechanical. Priya has the Character Tag [Wanted by the Technocracy]. She's negotiating with a Syndicate broker in a public café — the broker isn't Technocracy himself, but the Storyguide compels [Wanted by the Technocracy] anyway: "A NWO surveillance drone across the street just ran facial recognition on the café's foot traffic. It got a partial hit on you. You don't know that yet — but in about ten minutes, someone's going to know exactly where you were sitting. 1 Momentum." Nothing about the current roll changed — Priya isn't penalized on her negotiation, and the complication lands as a ticking clock on the scene rather than a dice penalty, which is the normal shape for Character Tag compels: they usually cost future narrative safety rather than present-roll dice, in contrast to equipment Tag compels which more often hit the roll or the gear directly.

### Tag Reference — Combat Weapons

The following Tags appear on weapons throughout this chapter. Each Tag's mechanical effect is described here for reference:

| Tag | Invoke Effect \| Compel Effect |
| --- | --- |
| [Fast] | Invoke: +2 dice when speed is the decisive factor — acting before a slower opponent draws, interrupting an action, establishing initiative advantage \| Compel: Your speed leads you to act before thinking — the Storyguide introduces a consequence of haste (wrong target exposed, ally struck, commitment before full information) |
| [Brutal] | Invoke: +2 dice when intimidation, raw stopping power, or overwhelming force is the operative dynamic \| Compel: The weapon's brutality is witnessed or leaves obvious evidence — forensic trace, traumatized bystanders, Sleepers who cannot rationalize what they saw |
| [Loud] | Invoke: +2 dice on intimidation rolls — the sound alone is a weapon \| Compel: The sound has attracted attention, alerted enemies, or (in Sleeper areas) generated a Paradox witness situation |
| [Concealed] | Invoke: +2 dice when surprise, hidden capability, or a social situation turning violent is the context — the weapon was not expected \| Compel: The concealment fails at a critical moment — the weapon is noticed during a pat-down, the profile is visible under clothing, re-concealment was forgotten |
| [Reload X] | Invoke: Not invokable for benefit — this Tag exists primarily to be compelled \| Compel: The weapon runs dry at the worst possible moment; reloading requires X Minor Actions and the character cannot attack during that time |
| [Area Xm] | Invoke: +2 dice when hitting multiple targets is the goal and all are within the Area radius \| Compel: The area effect catches an ally, an innocent, or a critical piece of the environment the players needed intact |
| [Armor-Piercing] | Invoke: Automatically reduce target armor by 2 for this attack (this is the primary mechanical effect, always active — additional invoke gives +2 dice on called shots targeting armor gaps) \| Compel: Ammunition is limited; the Storyguide announces this was the last armor-piercing round, or that the round passed through the target and hit something beyond |
| [Autofire] | Invoke: +2 dice on suppressing fire; or apply hits to up to 3 targets in a line for 1 hit each \| Compel: Ammunition consumed rapidly; weapon overheats and jams (Major Action to clear); burst fire attracts additional attention in a Sleeper environment |
| [Devastating] | Invoke: +2 dice when the goal is to destroy cover, smash through a barrier, or make the attack psychologically as well as physically overwhelming \| Compel: The devastation is indiscriminate — structural damage, collateral destruction, potential innocent harm |
| [Silent] | Invoke: +2 dice when remaining undetected or operating without sound is critical \| Compel: The silence is incomplete — someone heard something, or the silence itself (no shot heard) is conspicuous to someone who was watching |
| [Reach] | Invoke: +2 dice when the character can attack from outside normal melee range — preventing the opponent from reaching them \| Compel: In tight quarters, the weapon's length becomes a liability — cannot maneuver, cannot bring full force to bear |
| [Two-Handed] | Invoke: +2 dice to damage (the full-body power of a two-handed swing) \| Compel: One hand is now occupied — cannot use a shield, cannot grab or parry with off hand, highly visible and difficult to explain socially |
| [Shock] | Invoke: +2 dice against opponents in metal armor or with significant metal implants \| Compel: Electrical discharge affects unintended conductive surfaces — water, metal railings, wiring — potentially shocking allies or creating environmental hazard |

### Weapons Table — With Tags

The following table presents standard weapons with their damage ratings and assigned Tags. These Tags are not flavor — they are active mechanical elements that players and Storyguides should invoke and compel regularly throughout play. A weapon that is never Tagged is just a number. A weapon that is regularly invoked and compelled is a character in the story.

| Weapon | Damage \| Type \| Tags |
| --- | --- |
| Unarmed punch / kick | 1 \| Bashing \| [Fast] (strikes only; cannot be disarmed) |
| Unarmed (exceptional build, 4+) | 2 \| Bashing \| [Brutal], [Fast] |
| Combat knife / blade | 2 \| Lethal \| [Fast], [Concealed] |
| Improvised weapon (bottle, chair) | 1-2 \| Bashing or Lethal \| [Brutal] (situational) — breaks on Complication |
| Short sword / machete | 3 \| Lethal \| [Brutal], [Reach] (vs. unarmed only) |
| Sword / war axe | 4 \| Lethal \| [Brutal], [Reach], [Two-Handed] (if two-handed: +1 dmg) |
| Heavy weapon (chainsaw, maul) | 5 \| Lethal \| [Brutal], [Devastating], [Two-Handed], [Loud] |
| Light pistol (.22, .32) | 3 \| Lethal \| [Concealed], [Reload 2], [Loud] |
| Handgun (9mm, .45) | 4 \| Lethal \| [Reload 2], [Loud] |
| Heavy pistol (.44 Magnum) | 5 \| Lethal \| [Brutal], [Loud], [Reload 2] |
| Silenced pistol (9mm) | 4 \| Lethal \| [Concealed], [Silent], [Reload 2] |
| Submachine gun (burst) | 4 \| Lethal \| [Autofire], [Loud], [Reload 3] |
| Shotgun | 5 \| Lethal \| [Brutal], [Loud], [Reload 2], [Area 2m at close range] |
| Assault rifle | 5 \| Lethal \| [Autofire], [Loud], [Reload 3], [Reach] |
| Sniper rifle | 6 \| Lethal \| [Devastating], [Loud], [Reload 2], [Reach] (requires aim action) |
| Explosive grenade | 6 \| Lethal \| [Area 5m], [Devastating], [Brutal], [Loud] |
| Shock weapon (taser, baton) | 2 \| Bashing \| [Shock] (2+ hits: apply Stunned condition) |
| Armor-piercing ammunition (mod) | +0 dmg \| Lethal \| [Armor-Piercing] (applied to any standard firearm) |
| Hermetic Dümerang Blade | 3 \| Aggravated \| Returns when thrown; [Fast]; resists mundane damage |
| Verbena Thorn Whip (enchanted) | 3 \| Lethal \| [Reach], [Brutal]; Aggravated vs. beings allergic to natural materials |
| Etherite Stun Array (Wonder) | 3 \| Bashing \| [Shock], [Area 3m]; Requires activation (Minor Action) |

### Burst Fire and Automatic Weapons

When a character fires a burst from an [Autofire] weapon, they gain +1 damage but must declare the burst before rolling. The burst consumes additional ammunition — the Storyguide should track when an [Autofire] weapon's [Reload X] Tag becomes relevant through compel.

On 3+ hits with a burst, the attacker may apply 1 hit to a second target within 2 meters of the primary target, representing the spread of automatic fire. The secondary target takes 1 Lethal and may take cover as a free action in response.

Full-auto suppressing fire is a special case. The attacker rolls normally but instead of damage, every character in the targeted zone (up to the weapon's optimal range) must make an immediate choice: take cover (Minor Action, moving to the nearest cover position) or take 2 Lethal as they are caught in the fire. Characters already behind heavy cover are unaffected. Suppressing fire consumes the entire [Reload X] charge — after suppressing fire, the weapon must be fully reloaded before it can be used again.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Autofire and Tag compel: Agent Moss opens up with her submachine gun ([Autofire], [Loud], [Reload 3]) on a group of three Cultists of Ecstasy in a warehouse. The Storyguide says: "Good moment for a compel on [Reload 3]. You drop all three of them and the gun runs dry — you have no more rounds in the magazine. Spend one Major Action plus two Minor Actions to reload. And the [Loud] Tag? The police scanner at the desk in the corner just activated. You want that Momentum?" Moss's player accepts both compels, gains 2 Momentum (one for each Tag compelled), and now has resources to spend — but is holding an empty gun while a police response is in motion.

---

## Status Conditions

Status Conditions are temporary states that modify a character's capabilities in specific and concrete ways. They are imposed by Combat Tricks, magical effects, environmental hazards, and dramatic circumstances. Each condition has a specific effect and a specific recovery path. Conditions are not abstract penalties — they are descriptions of a physical or psychological state, and they should be narrated with the same vividness as everything else in a fight.

A character can be under multiple conditions simultaneously. They are all tracked separately and all apply simultaneously. A character who is both Prone and Stunned is in a very bad position. A character who is Blinded and Bleeding and Burning is probably moments from death. The accumulation of conditions is one of the ways that combat escalates dramatically rather than grinding mechanically.

### Prone

The character is on the ground — knocked down, tripped, dived behind cover, or otherwise operating from a horizontal position. Prone is not simply bad: it is a mixed tactical state. Against ranged attackers, being Prone makes you a smaller target (+2 Difficulty for ranged attacks against you). Against melee attackers, it gives them an advantage (-2 to your dice on all attacks, and melee attackers have an easier angle). The tactical decision of whether to stay Prone or stand up is genuinely interesting: if you are under fire from a sniper, Prone is correct. If there is a sword-wielding enemy two feet away, you need to get up immediately.

Recovery: Standing up from Prone is a Minor Action in combat. Out of combat, it requires no action — you simply stand.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Prone in play: Silas has been knocked Prone by a Trick Hit from an Iteration X combat drone. He is surrounded: one melee-capable drone to his left, and the sniper drone on a catwalk above. He chooses to stay Prone. Against the sniper, his Prone position adds +2 Difficulty — the sniper needs more hits to connect. The melee drone has an advantage on attacks against Prone Silas, but Silas's player invokes his knife's [Fast] Tag from the ground: "Even down here, I can drive the blade up into its knee joint before it gets its claws into me." The Storyguide agrees. +2 dice to Silas's attack from the ground.

### Stunned

The character has been hit hard enough — physically or mentally — that they cannot act on their next Spotlight. Stunned represents a moment of complete disruption: the blow to the head that turns the world white, the sonic blast that wipes thought away, the unexpected psychic intrusion that scrambles cognition. The character is not unconscious — they are simply unable to organize themselves into action. They can still be attacked and must defend passively (at base Difficulty 1 with no active dodge).

Recovery: Stunned resolves automatically after one full Spotlight passes — at the start of their next Spotlight after the one they missed, they are functional again. No roll required; the brain and body simply reset.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Stunned sequence: An Etherite's electromagnetic pulse grenade ([Area 3m], [Shock]) detonates next to two NWO agents. Both are Stunned. On the Round's Initiative count, both agents' Spotlights arrive and pass without action. In the following Round, both agents act normally. The Etherite used his Spotlight to reposition while they were stunned. That is what Stunned is for: buying time, not securing kills.

### Staggered

The character has been disrupted in a way that costs them flexibility rather than action. A Staggered character loses their next Minor Action — they cannot draw a weapon, take cover, move one step, or speak a tactical command. They can still use their Major Action fully. This condition reflects being knocked off-balance, having your footing compromised, receiving a hit that rattles your equilibrium without fully disrupting your capacity to act.

Recovery: Staggered resolves automatically at the start of the affected character's next Spotlight. It is a brief disruption, not a sustained penalty.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Staggered timing: Rosamund's Trick Hit applies Stagger to an NWO field agent. On his next Spotlight, the agent wants to draw his backup pistol (Minor Action) and then fire (Major Action). He cannot: Stagger costs him his Minor Action. He fires his existing weapon instead, which is almost out of ammunition. He had planned to draw the backup; now he cannot. The lost Minor Action becomes a critical deficit when his primary gun runs dry next round.

### Blinded

The character cannot see — whether from a flash bang, a magical Blindness effect, darkness, or physical damage to the eyes. Blinded imposes +2 Difficulty on all attack rolls (the character is attacking in the direction they believe the target is) and -2 dice to all defense rolls (they cannot see incoming attacks and must rely entirely on sound and instinct). This is a severe condition — a blinded character is at serious disadvantage against any sighted opponent.

Characters with magical sensory enhancement — Spirit sight, Correspondence spatial awareness, enhanced Mind perception — may partially or fully negate the Blinded condition depending on the sense involved. A character who cannot see but can perceive all patterns in a room via Prime sight is effectively not Blinded in combat terms, since their awareness remains complete through a different channel.

Recovery: Depends on the cause. A flash bang causes Blindness for 1 Round. A magical effect lasts until the effect ends. Darkness lasts until light is available. Physical injury to the eyes is a Wound condition with its own duration and healing requirements.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Blindness and magical sense: A Celestial Chorus mage uses a Songs of Purity effect that floods the room with brilliant white light, Blinding everyone without magical protection for 2 Rounds. The NWO agents — purely mundane — are effectively non-functional as attackers. But a Dreamspeaker ally with Spirit 2 active awareness is unaffected: his Otherside sense fills in what his eyes cannot, and he acts normally through the Blindness period.

### Deafened

The character cannot hear — from explosive concussion, a sonic weapon, a targeted Mind effect, or damage. Deafened characters suffer -2 dice on all Alertness and Awareness rolls and cannot use magic that requires spoken words or sound-based Sphere workings as a component. More practically, they cannot hear verbal commands from allies, cannot hear enemies moving behind them, and cannot use any equipment or Wonder that relies on acoustic signals.

Recovery: Like Blindness, depends on cause. Explosive concussion: 1-3 Rounds. Magical effect: until the effect ends. Actual physical damage to hearing: a Wound condition requiring medical treatment.

### Pinned

The character is physically prevented from moving from their current position. This can be from a thrown weapon impaling their clothing to a surface, a grapple focused on restraining movement, a magical anchoring effect, a physical collapse of terrain around them. Pinned does not prevent the character from taking other actions — they can still attack, cast spells, use Wonders, speak, and defend. They simply cannot move from their current location.

Recovery: Spend a Major Action to break free (tearing clothing, wrenching the limb from the restraint, countering the magical anchor). An ally can help free a Pinned character as a Major Action. Magical Pinning effects require counter-magic to remove.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Pinned in tactical play: A Trick Hit pins Vikram against the wall — an NWO agent drove a combat knife through his jacket sleeve, anchoring it to the doorframe. Vikram cannot move. But he can still cast: "I'm pinned, not dead. I reach through Correspondence — teleportation doesn't need me to move to the door, I just need to recalculate my spatial position." He spends his Major Action on a Correspondence 3 effect, leaving the jacket (and the knife) behind and reappearing ten meters away. The jacket stays pinned to the wall. Mechanical limitation, creative solution.

### Disoriented

The character is confused, off-balance, or cognitively disrupted in a way that affects all of their actions. Disoriented applies -1 die to all actions for the current and following Round. It is a mild but pervasive penalty that stacks badly with other conditions — a Disoriented Blinded character is in very serious trouble. Common causes include head trauma, concussive magical effects, rapid teleportation (Correspondence sickness), and the early stages of Quiet.

Recovery: Disoriented resolves at the start of the character's Spotlight in the following Round — a full Round of maintaining a single condition.

### Rattled

The character's mental composure has been disrupted — through shock, psychological attack, witnessing something deeply disturbing, or a low-level Mind effect. Rattled applies -1 die to all Mental actions specifically: casting, perception, social interaction, and any roll primarily driven by Intellect, Cunning, or Composure. Physical actions are unaffected. This condition is common in supernatural encounters where the horror of what characters witness affects their capacity to think clearly under pressure.

Recovery: Spend 1 Momentum to push through the disruption, or wait until the end of the scene when adrenaline and the demands of survival override the shock.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Rattled from horror: The group encounters a Nephandi's handiwork in a basement — the remains of three people who were used in a pattern-unraveling ritual. Annika is Rattled (-1 die to all Mental actions). She is not incapable; she is simply operating with part of her mind devoted to processing what she just saw. Her next spell casting: -1 die. Her next Paradox calculation: -1 die. Her social read on the Nephandi when he appears: -1 die. The horror is mechanically present in the scene.

### Restrained

The character is physically bound — handcuffed, tied, zip-tied, in a magical binding. Restrained characters cannot take offensive physical actions. All their dice pools are at -2. They can still speak, attempt to escape, and (if their hands and voice are free) potentially cast magic at the Storyguide's discretion. Restrained is one of the most debilitating non-damaging conditions because it combines offense denial with a general penalty.

Recovery: Escape attempt as a Major Action — roll Athletics + Might against Difficulty 3 for mundane restraints. Each failure before success causes 1 Bashing from exertion. Magical restraints require counter-magic at the appropriate Sphere Difficulty.

### Burning

The character is on fire. This is as bad as it sounds. Burning causes 1 Aggravated damage at the start of every Spotlight until the fire is extinguished. It also spreads to flammable items in contact with the character: clothing, carried materials, anything combustible. The psychological effect of being on fire — the sheer panic of burning — means most characters prioritize extinguishing the fire above all other actions, even when tactically inadvisable.

Recovery: Stop, drop, and roll — roll Athletics, Difficulty 2. On success, the fire is out. On failure, take 1 Aggravated and try again next Spotlight. Alternatively, submerging in water or having a fire suppressant applied (Major Action by self or ally with appropriate material) extinguishes instantly.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Burning and priority: Silas's coat is on fire from a Nephandi's fire attack. He is Burning. On his Spotlight, his tactical mind says "attack the Nephandi" but the fire says "you are taking 1 Aggravated per round and this coat is going to get to your skin very quickly." He spends his Major Action rolling to extinguish: Athletics 2 + Might 3 = 5 dice. Results: 9, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 2 hits. Difficulty 2. Success. The fire is out. He lost an attack to save himself from 2-3 more Aggravated. Mechanically correct, narratively vivid.

### Bleeding

The character has a wound that is continuing to damage them. Bleeding causes 1 additional Lethal at the end of each Round until the wound is addressed. This condition is particularly dangerous because it is persistent — a character who is Bleeding and is not attended to will continue to deteriorate even after combat ends, potentially converting boxes of Lethal to Aggravated as the track fills. First aid can stop the Bleeding even if it cannot heal the underlying wound.

Recovery: First aid applied as a Major Action — roll Medicine + Intellect, Difficulty 1. On any success, the Bleeding stops. The wound remains; the progression does not. Life 1 magical healing stops Bleeding automatically as a component of any healing working.

### Shocked

The character has been electroshocked — hit with a taser, a magical lightning effect, an [Etherite] shock array, or a static discharge at dangerous levels. Shocked causes total loss of all actions for the current Spotlight — Major and Minor. The character is physically unable to coordinate movement or thought. They may still defend passively. After one Spotlight passes, Shocked resolves automatically and the character acts normally.

Shocked is essentially a more severe and specific version of Stunned. The key difference is that Shocked represents actual neuromuscular disruption, not cognitive disruption — a character who is Shocked is functionally convulsing. Opponents who close with a Shocked target are fighting someone who cannot respond.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Shocked and coup de grace: Rosamund tasers an NWO agent — 2 Bashing and the Shocked condition. On his Spotlight, the agent is Shocked: all actions lost. Rosamund moves to him and places her hand on his forehead — not attacking, but casting a Mind 2 deep memory-read, which requires touch. She gets one full uncontested minute of access to everything he knows about the operation. The taser bought her a window. She used it perfectly.

### The Quiet Track — Sanity Under Pressure

Mages who accumulate Paradox, witness cosmic horror, or endure sustained psychological trauma accumulate damage to a separate track: the Quiet Track. Like the Health track, the Quiet Track is a fixed-band structure mirroring the SPU injury level design — but it measures the erosion of the mage's grip on consensual reality rather than their body's physical integrity. See Appendix K for the full Quiet Track rules, damage types, and recovery. This section summarises the combat-relevant mechanics.

The Quiet Track has four named bands. Composure extends the first and most forgiving band. Band structure: STATIC band = (2 + Composure) boxes, no penalty. DISTORTION band = 2 boxes, −1 die to all Social and Empathy rolls. FRACTURE band = 2 boxes, −1 die to ALL rolls. FULL QUIET = 1 box, the mage enters a Quiet episode and cannot act voluntarily. Total Quiet track size = 7 + Composure.

| Quiet Band | Boxes \| Effect \| Symptoms |
| --- | --- |
| STATIC | 2 + Composure boxes \| No mechanical penalty \| Colors subtly wrong, sounds have faint echoes, minor perceptual glitches. The mage is shaken but functional. |
| DISTORTION | 2 boxes \| −1 die to all Social and Empathy rolls \| Reality noticeably bends around the mage. Sleepers sense wrongness. Social presence is compromised. |
| FRACTURE | 2 boxes \| −1 die to ALL rolls \| Internal experience diverges from consensus. Active hallucinations begin. Some actions require a Composure + Resolve check (Diff 1) just to initiate. |
| FULL QUIET | 1 box \| Cannot take voluntary actions \| Complete break. The mage is physically present but unreachable. They respond to people who are not there. Non-participant until rescued. |

**Quiet Damage Types**

Not all psychological damage is equal. Three types of damage mark boxes on the Quiet Track, exactly as Bashing, Lethal, and Aggravated mark boxes on the Health track. They fill the same boxes — damage type determines how hard it is to heal, not which boxes it occupies.

| Type | Mark \| Cause \| Recovery |
| --- | --- |
| SHOCK | / (forward slash) \| Ordinary human horror: a mutilated corpse, a sudden violent death, unexpected supernatural manifestation \| 1 box per scene of safety and calm, no roll required |
| TRAUMA | X (cross)         \| Severe or sustained horror: witnessing a brutal murder, extended captivity, being the cause of innocent deaths \| 1 box per session with active recovery effort; Composure + Resolve roll, Diff 2 |
| VOID | * (asterisk)      \| Cosmic fracture: Outer Gods, extended Paradox Realm exposure, Nephandi pattern contact, severe Paradox backlash \| 1 box per story arc; requires extraordinary intervention; some Void may be permanent |

**Overflow and Escalation**

When all boxes on the Quiet Track are filled with Shock damage and more Shock arrives, the leftmost Shock box upgrades to Trauma (/ becomes X). When all boxes are filled with Trauma and more Trauma arrives, the leftmost Trauma box upgrades to Void (X becomes *). Chronic low-level horror is genuinely dangerous — enough accumulated Shock will eventually become Trauma, enough Trauma will eventually become Void.

**Paradox and the Quiet Track**

Paradox is the primary source of Void damage. A Paradox Cascade result deals 1 Void to the Quiet Track in addition to its other effects. Spending time in a Paradox Realm deals 1 Void per day inside the Realm. Witnessing another mage's Quiet episode (Fracture band or higher) deals 1 Shock to observers. A Paradox Catastrophe deals 2–3 Void directly. These are the wounds that change a mage permanently — the deep scars of pushing too hard against the Tapestry.

For full rules on the Quiet Track — including detailed band effects, recovery procedures, Paradox Insulation mechanics, and the Marauder Threshold — see Appendix K: Sanity.

---

## Healing

Damage taken in combat can be recovered through mundane medicine, rest, magical healing, or emergency preparedness in the form of pre-charged Tass. The speed and completeness of recovery depends on the damage type, the resources available, and the severity of the original injury. A Tradition mage who survives a serious fight should expect to operate at reduced capability for some time afterward — the health costs of violence compound across sessions.

### Mundane First Aid — During Combat

A character can apply first aid to an injured ally as a Major Action during combat. This requires physical proximity, at least one free hand, and some basic medical supplies (most Tradition mages carry at least a basic kit as standard field preparation). Roll Intellect + Medicine against Difficulty 1.

- Each hit on the Medicine roll stabilizes 1 Lethal box — the wound is bound, the bleeding controlled, the damage frozen where it is rather than worsening. This conversion does not heal the box; it simply prevents it from getting worse.
- First aid cannot heal Aggravated damage under any circumstances. Aggravated is a pattern wound; mundane bandages cannot touch a wound in the soul's blueprint.
- First aid cannot bring an Incapacitated character back to consciousness. It can prevent their condition from deteriorating. The character remains Incapacitated until they receive more substantial care or significant time passes.
- First aid can stop the Bleeding condition immediately on any success, before any stabilization occurs.

### Mundane Healing — After Combat

After the scene ends and characters have time to rest and receive care, healing proceeds at the following rates:

| Damage Type | Natural Healing Rate \| With Medical Care |
| --- | --- |
| Bashing | All Bashing clears after 8 hours of rest \| Reduces to 2-3 hours with basic care |
| Lethal | 1 box per day of rest \| 2 boxes per day with competent medical attention |
| Aggravated | 1 box per week of rest \| Cannot be sped meaningfully by mundane medicine alone |

Medical care means genuine medical attention — a trained physician, a properly equipped clinic, sustained wound management. Washing a wound and wrapping it in a field bandage does not count as "competent medical care" for Lethal healing purposes. It prevents infection (relevant in longer survival scenarios) but does not accelerate the healing rate. Characters who have access to a Chantry with a proper medical station, or an ally with Medicine 3+, qualify for the accelerated rate.

### Magical Healing

Life magic is the primary magical healing tool, and it can dramatically accelerate recovery compared to mundane methods. Life Sphere healing works by directly accelerating and correcting the body's natural repair processes — at lower levels, nudging biological function; at higher levels, fundamentally rewriting tissue patterns. Magical healing is never invisible to those with Sphere sight: a healed wound has a distinctive Quintessence signature that any Life practitioner can detect.

| Life Sphere Level | Healing Capability |
| --- | --- |
| Life 1 | Diagnose injuries with precision; identify all damage types and locations; enhance natural healing rate by 50% (Bashing in 4 hours instead of 8; Lethal at 1.5 boxes per day). |
| Life 2 | Actively heal Bashing and Lethal damage. Roll Intellect + Life (or Medicine + Life): each hit heals 1 box of Bashing or Lethal. Touch required. Major Action. Cannot heal Aggravated. |
| Life 3 | Full Lethal restoration. Heal all Lethal damage from a single wound source in one working. Or: heal (hits) boxes of any combination of Bashing and Lethal. Touch required. Full Round action. |
