| Life 4 | Heal Aggravated damage. Each 2 hits heals 1 box of Aggravated. Touch required. Extended ritual (minimum 10 minutes per box). Exhausting to the caster. |
| Life 5 | Complete restoration of all damage types in a single working. Life 5 + access to a Node can heal Aggravated at 1 box per hour of sustained effort instead of per day. |

Emergency Tass — pre-charged Life 2 Tass objects — represent prepared magical first aid that anyone with basic magical awareness can activate. Examples: a Verbena herb-packet soaked in moonwater (heals 2 Lethal as a Minor Action); an Etherite medical nanite syringe (heals 2 Lethal, Minor Action, slight chance of Paradox if used visibly among Sleepers); a Chorister holy water vial blessed by three Priests (heals 2 Lethal, Minor Action, requires spoken invocation). These objects are consumed on use. Planning mages carry two or three. Unplanning mages die regretting it.

### The Stabilization Rule

Any character at Incapacitated with Lethal damage filling their track can be stabilized — their condition frozen against further deterioration — with a successful Medicine + Intellect roll at Difficulty 2, or any Life 2 magical effect applied to healing. A stabilized character is not healing; they are simply not worsening. They need no further attention to survive the immediate crisis but also do not recover without additional care. Stabilization is the difference between "they'll live if we get them to the Chantry" and "they'll be dead before we reach the car."

---

## Magical Combat

Magic in combat follows the same casting rules as magic in any other context — described fully in Chapter 4 and the individual Sphere entries in Chapter 6. What follows are the combat-specific considerations: the practical realities of trying to cast spells while someone is shooting at you, the specific mechanics for magical attacks, and the ways in which Spheres interact with the violence of a fight.

The most important thing to understand about magical combat is that it is not a power fantasy. Every significant magical attack in a Sleeper-adjacent environment carries Paradox risk. Every sustained effect costs the mage concentration and attention they may need to dodge bullets. And every mage who commits fully to magical offense is, in a very concrete way, gambling their pattern against the returns. The mage who wins a magical duel might still lose everything if their Paradox Pool explodes.

> Running Combat with Magic — Quick Decision Flowchart

### Casting in Combat — The Basic Sequence

1. Declare the effect and all relevant Sphere levels involved. Be specific: name the effect, describe the physical or magical mechanism, identify the target. "I blast him" is not a declaration. "I use Forces 2 to direct a focused electromagnetic pulse at Cyborg 1's targeting system" is a declaration.

2. Determine whether the effect is Coincidental or Vulgar. Coincidental effects fit within the local paradigm of consensus reality — unlikely, but not impossible. Vulgar effects clearly violate consensus and are immediately recognizable as impossible to any Sleeper who witnesses them. In an empty warehouse with no Sleeper witnesses, Vulgar magic carries no immediate Paradox risk from witnessing (though accumulated Paradox from overuse remains).

3. Roll the casting pool: Attribute + Sphere Skill, modified by Enhancement from instruments, Resonance alignment, or situational advantage. Add any relevant Tags from magical instruments (a Hermetic Circle adds [Precise] to workings performed within it; a Verbena ritual knife adds [Attuned] to Life and Spirit workings).

4. Compare hits to Difficulty. Standard Difficulty 1; increase for complex targeting, combined Spheres, or resisted effects.

5. If Vulgar and Sleepers may have witnessed, add Sphere level(s) in dice to the Paradox Pool. If the working combined multiple Spheres at Vulgar level, add each Sphere's level. A Forces 3 + Correspondence 2 Vulgar working adds 5 dice to the Paradox Pool.

6. Apply the effect on success. On failure (insufficient hits but no Complication), the magic simply does not work — no Paradox from the failure itself. On a Complication result, immediately roll the Paradox Pool.

### Speed of Casting

A standard magical effect takes a Major Action to cast. The mage is spending their full Spotlight on the working: concentrating, channeling, directing. This is equivalent in time-cost to making a weapon attack. You cannot both attack with a weapon and cast a spell in the same Spotlight. You can cast a spell and take Minor Actions (speak, step one pace, draw an item) in the same Spotlight.

Complex effects — those combining three or more Spheres at high levels, requiring precise targeting of multiple points simultaneously, or reshaping the environment at a large scale — may require a full Round: the mage's entire Spotlight plus some additional time into the next Round. The Storyguide announces this before the roll. Full-Round castings can be interrupted if the mage takes damage during the Round (see Concentration roll, below).

Rote effects — pre-practiced, highly familiar workings that the mage has performed dozens or hundreds of times in similar conditions — can sometimes be cast as a Minor Action if the Sphere level is significantly below the mage's current rating and no unusual targeting or combining is required. A Forces 3 mage whose signature rote is a basic electromagnetic disruption (Forces 1) can deploy it as a Minor Action while taking cover. The Storyguide makes the final call based on the mage's history with the effect and current conditions.

### Concentration Under Fire

When a mage is in the middle of a casting — particularly a Full-Round or extended effect — and takes damage before the working completes, they must make a Concentration roll to maintain the working. Roll Composure + Resolve + Arete/Enlightenment against a Difficulty equal to the number of damage dice that hit (not the final damage taken, the original attack roll hits). On success, the working proceeds normally. On failure, the working collapses — no effect, no refund of the action, and if the working was Vulgar, the incomplete working still adds Paradox dice to the pool. Alternatively, before taking the hit, the mage may declare a Sphere Defense (see Sphere Defense — Defensive Casting, below) to negate or reduce the damage and protect the working without a Concentration roll.

This rule makes mages in combat highly vulnerable to disruption. A mage channeling a powerful combined-Sphere effect is presenting a high-value target: disrupt the casting and you neutralize a dangerous working before it materializes. NWO agents are specifically trained in disruption protocols — fire a burst at the mage's center mass the moment they begin a complex gesture and the mage must either fail the Concentration roll or spend their action on defense instead of casting.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Concentration under fire: Annika is casting a complex Prime 3 + Forces 2 working (Freeform SF 5, sum of 3+2 dots) that will seal the Nephandi's access to the Node. The working is Full-Round. On the Round she casts, the Nephandi fires two shots at her. Both hit — 4 Lethal total, reduced to 2 Lethal through her Mage Armor. The Concentration Difficulty equals the original attack hits (let's say the attacker scored 3 hits before armor). Annika rolls Composure 2 + Resolve 3 + Arete 2 = 7 dice against Difficulty 3. Results: 8, 7, 4, 3, 2, 1, 1 = 1 hit. She fails. The sealing working collapses. Two Lethal are marked. And 5 Paradox dice are added to her pool from the incomplete Vulgar working. Her next Spotlight begins with a Paradox Pool roll forced by the Complication on the collapsed working.

### Sphere Defense — Defensive Casting

The standard response to being shot while casting a spell is to absorb the hit and roll Concentration. Sphere Defense is the alternative: instead of eating the damage and hoping your Concentration holds, you declare a reactive magical working that deflects, negates, warps, or otherwise interferes with the incoming attack before it lands. A successful Sphere Defense eliminates the Concentration check entirely — there is nothing to concentrate through because the damage never reached you.

Sphere Defense is not a generic magical dodge. It is actual Sphere magic deployed reactively, and it obeys all the rules of Sphere magic: Paradox exposure, Quintessence cost, and the requirement that the effect be narratively justified through the mage's Paradigm. A Verbena mage who hardens her skin with Life ●●, an Akashic who uses Mind ●●● to read the shooter's intent a heartbeat before the trigger is pulled, an Euthanatos who causes the gun to malfunction through Entropy ●● — these are all Sphere Defenses. The player describes how their Paradigm applies their Sphere to the incoming threat. The Storyguide approves the narrative logic, and that is the only gate.

#### Requirements

Any Sphere rated ●● or higher can be used for a Sphere Defense. At ●, most Spheres are limited to sensing and perception — not enough to actively deflect harm. The exceptions are Spirit ● (the mage can partially merge with the Umbra, reducing physical damage by their Spirit rating even at ●) and Entropy ● (minor probability nudges that can cause weapons to malfunction or shots to go slightly wide). These two Spheres are the only ones with meaningful defensive applications at a single dot.

#### Triggering a Sphere Defense

Triggering a Sphere Defense works exactly like any other defensive reaction in the Phase Initiative system: you must spend your Bank Token (see Banking a Die in Appendix H). There is no free reactive defense in this system — mages who want the option to defend must plan for it by banking a die at the right moment. A mage who committed every die to a slow powerful working and never banked has nothing left to react with: they are exposed until the next round gives them a fresh opportunity to bank.

In the Spotlight system, Sphere Defenses work differently: you may declare a Sphere Defense when an attack lands by spending 1 Willpower instead of a Bank Token. This represents burning raw personal essence to perform a split-second magical reaction that the non-phase system cannot otherwise model. The Quintessence and Paradox costs still apply normally.

#### Quintessence Cost

Every Sphere Defense costs 1 Quintessence. This is the raw magical energy required to power an improvised defensive working under combat pressure — there is no preparation time, no deliberate instrument arrangement, no chance to build the effect carefully. The Quintessence is spent at the moment of declaration, before the roll. If the mage has no Quintessence remaining, they cannot declare a standard Sphere Defense. See Willpower Burns below for the emergency fallback.

#### The Defense Roll

Roll [Sphere rating] + [Casting Attribute for that Sphere] against Difficulty 1 if the defense would be Coincidental, or Difficulty 2 if it would be Vulgar. The Casting Attribute is the same one the mage uses when casting offensively with that Sphere: Forces uses Dexterity, Life uses Resolve, Mind uses Intellect, Correspondence uses Cunning, etc. The number of hits determines how effective the defense is.

#### Success Tiers

Compare the hits from the defense roll to the hits from the attacker's roll:

- Full Defense (defense hits equal or exceed attacker hits): The attack is completely negated. The mage takes no damage. If they were mid-cast on a spell, no Concentration check is required — the working proceeds uninterrupted. This is the ideal outcome and the reason Sphere Defense is worth the resource cost.
- Partial Defense (defense hits are between 1 and attacker hits minus 1): Each defense hit cancels one attacker hit. The mage takes damage from the remaining hits only. If they were mid-cast, a Concentration check is still required, but the Difficulty is equal only to the remaining hits after the defense absorbed its share — not the original full damage roll. This is still meaningfully better than absorbing the full attack.
- Failed Defense (0 defense hits): The defense fails entirely. The full attack lands normally. If mid-cast, the Concentration check proceeds at standard Difficulty (equal to the attacker's full hit count). The mage has spent a Bank Token and 1 Quintessence and gotten nothing for it. This is the worst outcome and the risk the player accepts when declaring a defense.
> SPHERE DEFENSE — THREE OUTCOMES
> 
> Setup: Mira (Verbena, Life 3, Arete 3) is casting an SF 2 Life 3 Rote when a merc
> fires a burst (Ranged 3 + Dex 4 = 7 dice). The merc scores 4 hits.
> 
> Mira has a Bank Token. She declares a Life ●● Sphere Defense:
>   "My skin hardens for the instant of impact — the flesh knits ahead of the bullets."
>   Roll: Life 3 + Resolve 4 = 7 dice. Coincidental (internal biological hardening).
>   Difficulty 1.
> 
> OUTCOME A — Full Defense: Mira rolls 5 hits. 5 ≥ 4 (merc hits). Bullets deflect.
>   No damage. No Concentration check. Rote proceeds at SF 2 as planned.
>   Cost: 1 Bank Token + 1 Quintessence. Total safety at resource cost.
> 
> OUTCOME B — Partial Defense: Mira rolls 2 hits. Her defense cancels 2 of 4 merc hits.
>   2 remaining hits deal reduced damage. Concentration check: Difficulty 2 (remaining
>   hits), not Difficulty 4 (full hits). Pool: Composure 3 + Resolve 4 + Arete 3 = 10
>   dice vs Diff 2 — much more survivable than the original Diff 4 check.
>   Cost: 1 Bank Token + 1 Quintessence. Partial success still worth it.
> 
> OUTCOME C — Failed Defense: Mira rolls 0 hits (four 1s and 2s). Defense fails.
>   Full 4 hits land as damage. Concentration check at Difficulty 4. She spent a Bank Token and 1 Quintessence and gained nothing. This is the risk.

#### Sphere Defense and Mid-Cast Spells

The key tactical question is always: does declaring a Sphere Defense interfere with the spell currently in progress? The answer is no — the Bank Token is separate from the dice already committed to the casting's SF window. A mage who banked a die earlier in the round (or is carrying one from last round) can trigger a Sphere Defense mid-cast without disrupting committed dice. However, the mage is splitting their magical awareness in the moment of the defense, which is why the Concentration check is not fully eliminated on a partial defense. Think of it as the difference between deflecting a blow and ignoring it: a full defense means the mage redirected enough attention to stop the threat cleanly; a partial defense means they deflected some of it but not all, and the remainder rattled their concentration.

If the mage has no Bank Token and is mid-cast, they have no active defense available. The only option is to abort the casting (freeing committed dice, losing spent ones), immediately bank one of the freed dice, and spend the fresh token to defend. This is a hard choice: it costs both the casting and the remaining committed dice, but it may be preferable to taking a potentially fatal hit with no recourse at all. A mage who plans to cast long actions in combat should bank a die first, before beginning the working.

#### Paradox and Sphere Defense

**Same-Sphere Rule: Unified Working**

When the Sphere Defense uses the same Sphere as the spell currently being cast, the Storyguide treats both effects as a single unified magical working. There is no second Paradox roll. Instead, the Paradox ceiling for the working rises to the highest Sphere dot involved across both effects.

Example: A mage casting a Life ●● healing Rote (likely Coincidental, zero Paradox) and defending with Life ●●. The combined working is assessed as a Life ●● effect — no change, no Paradox. Clean.

Example: A mage casting a Life ● sensing Rote (Coincidental, zero Paradox) and defending with Life ●●. The ceiling rises to Life ●●. If Life ●● is still Coincidental in this context, still no Paradox. But the Storyguide now assesses the full working at the ●● level — a more powerful effect, with all that implies.

Example: A mage casting a Forces ●●● lightning strike Rote and defending with Forces ●●●●. The ceiling rises to Forces ●●●●. If the defense is Vulgar (visible force barrier materializing), the whole working takes Paradox at the ●●●● level, not just the ●●● level. The defense upgraded the Paradox exposure of the entire working.

This creates a critical strategic insight: using a higher-Sphere same-Sphere defense can make a perfectly clean cast suddenly expensive. A mage casting a Coincidental Life ●● Rote who uses Life ●●● to defend against a Vulgar-context attack has just turned a zero-Paradox cast into a Vulgar working assessed at Life ●●●. The die benefit of the stronger defense has to outweigh the Paradox cost of the upgrade.

**Different-Sphere Rule: Two Independent Workings**

When the Sphere Defense uses a different Sphere than the active cast, it is a second simultaneous magical working. Each working generates its own Paradox assessment, independently. The Storyguide rolls for each separately.

This means a subtle, Coincidental Forces defense (the bullet deflects oddly, within what physics could theoretically permit) generates zero Paradox even if the Life spell being cast at the same time is Vulgar. The two workings are assessed on their own merits. This can make different-Sphere defense surprisingly Paradox-cheap — and sometimes deliberately cheaper than same-Sphere defense.

Example: A mage casting a Vulgar Life ●●● body transformation (Paradox: 3 dice, no witnesses) and defending with Forces ●● to deflect a bullet (Coincidental, zero Paradox). Two rolls: Life ●●● Paradox roll (3 dice), Forces ●● Paradox roll (0 dice — Coincidental). Net Paradox: just the Life ●●● roll. Had she instead defended with Life ●●● (same Sphere), the ceiling would have stayed at ●●● and the Paradox would have been identical — but she would have gotten a stronger defense roll (Life ●●● dice vs. Forces ●● dice). The tradeoff is dice pool vs. Paradox independence.

> SAME vs. DIFFERENT SPHERE PARADOX — DECISION MATRIX
> 
> Mage: Tobias (Hermetic, Life 3, Forces 3, Arete 3). Casting Life 3 transformation
> (Vulgar, no witnesses, +3 Paradox dice to pool). A merc fires at him mid-cast.
> 
> OPTION A: Defend with Life ●●● (same Sphere)
>   Roll: Life 3 + Resolve 4 = 7 dice. Strong pool.
>   Paradox: Combined working = Life ●●●, already Vulgar. No change to Paradox level.
>   Net: Same Paradox as without defense. Best dice pool. Cleanest.
>   Choose this when: you need maximum defense hits and Paradox level won't increase.
> 
> OPTION B: Defend with Forces ●●● (different Sphere)
>   Roll: Forces 3 + Dexterity 3 = 6 dice. One die fewer, but still strong.
>   Paradox: Two separate rolls. Life ●●● Vulgar = +3 dice. Forces ●●● Coincidental
>   (deflecting a bullet via kinetic manipulation = plausibly mundane-adjacent) = +0 dice.
>   Net: Same +3 Paradox dice as without defense. Same as Option A.
>   Choose this when: Sphere pools are similar — no meaningful difference here.
> 
> OPTION C: Defend with Life ●●●●● (same Sphere, higher level)
>   Roll: You only have Life 3. Cannot use ●●●●●. Illustrating the ceiling trap:
>   If Tobias HAD Life 5 and used it, the combined working rises to Life ●●●●●.
>   Paradox: Now +5 dice instead of +3. The stronger defense costs MORE Paradox.
>   Choose this only when: the threat is severe enough that the extra dice are required
>   and you can absorb the Paradox increase.
> 
> OPTION D: Defend with Entropy ●● (different Sphere, subtle)
>   Roll: Entropy 2 + Cunning 3 = 5 dice. Weaker pool.
>   Effect: The gun jams. The bullet doesn't fire. Coincidental — guns jam.
>   Paradox: Life ●●● Vulgar = +3 dice. Entropy ●● Coincidental = +0. Same as A.
>   But: Entropy ●● only generates ~3-4 hits on average vs. Life ●●● 7 dice average.
>   Choose this when: you want to stop the attack at source (no bullet = no damage
>   at all, no partial), AND your Entropy pool is sufficient.

#### Willpower Burns

Willpower interacts with Sphere Defense in three distinct ways, each representing a different kind of sacrifice:

- Willpower as Concentration Insurance: Spend 1 Willpower to automatically pass any single Concentration check this scene, no roll required. This is declared after you see the Difficulty — making it a clutch save that costs your best resource. One use per scene. This is independent of Sphere Defense: you might take a hit, skip the Sphere Defense entirely, and just burn Willpower to keep the casting alive. Sometimes that is the right play. Note: this is the existing rule, reinforced here because its interaction with Sphere Defense matters — if your Sphere Defense partially fails and you face a Concentration check, burning Willpower lets you pass that check automatically, effectively turning a partial defense into a full defense at the cost of both a die and a Willpower.
- Willpower as Quintessence Substitute: When a mage has no Quintessence remaining, they may spend 1 Willpower instead of 1 Quintessence to fuel a Sphere Defense. This represents burning raw personal essence — the mage's life force, conviction, and identity rather than stored magical energy. It is a worse trade (Willpower is harder to recover mid-combat than Quintessence) but it provides a fallback when the mage's Quintessence pool is depleted. A mage who has burned through all her Quintessence on an extended fight and now faces another attack still has options — at personal cost.
- Willpower to Boost the Defense Roll: Spend 1 Willpower to add 3 dice to a Sphere Defense roll. This is the standard Storypath Ultra Willpower bonus applied to a defense. It can be combined with the Quintessence cost — a mage can spend 1 Quintessence AND 1 Willpower in the same declaration, getting both the defense and the +3 dice. When a single hit could mean the difference between full and partial defense, this combination can be decisive.
> WILLPOWER BURNS IN PRACTICE
> 
> Setup: Kenji (Akashic, Mind 3, Life 2, Arete 3, Willpower 3, Quintessence 1).
> He's mid-cast on a Mind 3 Rote (SF 2). A merc scores 5 hits on an attack.
> Kenji has 1 Quintessence and 3 Willpower remaining. He has a Bank Token.
> 
> OPTION 1 — Standard Sphere Defense:
>   Spend Bank Token + 1 Quintessence (his last). Roll Mind 3 + Intellect 4 = 7 dice.
>   On average 3-4 hits. Against 5 merc hits: likely a partial defense.
>   Quintessence pool drops to 0. Next attack cannot be defended with Sphere magic
>   unless he burns Willpower as substitute.
> 
> OPTION 2 — Boosted Defense (Quintessence + Willpower):
>   Spend Bank Token + 1 Quintessence + 1 Willpower. Roll 7 + 3 = 10 dice.
>   Average ~5 hits on 10 dice. Against 5 merc hits: likely a full defense.
>   Cost: Bank Token, last Quintessence, 1 Willpower. Resources nearly depleted.
>   But: the working survives, no damage, no Concentration check.
> 
> OPTION 3 — Eat the hit + Willpower Concentration:
>   Take full damage (5 hits). Then burn 1 Willpower: auto-pass Concentration.
>   Rote survives. But: Kenji takes 5 hits of damage AND spent Willpower.
>   Cost: health damage, 1 Willpower. Quintessence preserved for later.
>   Choose when: the Sphere Defense roll looks too risky to chance a failure,
>   but the damage is survivable.
> 
> OPTION 4 — No Quintessence, use Willpower as substitute:
>   (If he had already burned his last Quintessence before this attack)
>   Spend Bank Token + 1 Willpower (as Quintessence substitute). Roll 7 dice.
>   Same result as Option 1 but costs Willpower instead of Quintessence.
>   Willpower pool drops to 2. He's running out of resources fast.
> 
> KEY INSIGHT: Each option is correct in a different scenario. The player who
> understands all four options will make better decisions than the player who
> reflexively always picks one. That depth of choice is what Sphere Defense adds.

#### Quintessence Investment Options

Beyond the standard 1 Quintessence cost, a mage can invest more Quintessence in a Sphere Defense to increase its effectiveness:

- Standard (1 Quintessence): Powers the defense as described. Roll [Sphere] + [Casting Attribute]. No modifier.
- Enhanced Defense (2 Quintessence): Spend 2 Quintessence to treat the defense as a Rote-quality working — you gain Enhancement +1 on the defense roll. This represents pouring an additional surge of raw magical energy into the working, giving it the precision of a prepared formula even though it is improvised. Most useful when the attacker is powerful and a single extra hit on the defense roll could shift from partial to full.
- Desperate Surge (all remaining Quintessence, maximum +3 extra): Spend all your remaining Quintessence on this one defense. For each Quintessence spent beyond the base 1, add 1 die to the roll (maximum +3 extra dice at 4 total Quintessence spent). This is a last-resort move that leaves the mage magically depleted — no Quintessence for further defenses, no fueling of subsequent spells, no Quintessence-dependent effects. It should feel like the mage pouring everything into one desperate moment.

#### Sphere Defense in the Spotlight System

The Spotlight system does not use stored dice or phase windows, so the triggering mechanism works differently. When an attack lands and the mage is mid-cast (or simply wants to attempt a reactive defense), they may declare a Sphere Defense by spending 1 Willpower in addition to the standard 1 Quintessence. The Willpower replaces the stored-die cost — it represents the sudden surge of focused will that makes the improvised reaction possible.

In Spotlight, the success tiers apply identically: full defense negates the attack and eliminates Concentration; partial reduces the Concentration Difficulty; failure means full damage and normal Concentration. The Willpower spent is separate from the Willpower that can be spent to auto-pass Concentration — a mage could theoretically spend 1 Willpower to declare the defense, fail the defense roll, then spend a second Willpower to auto-pass the resulting Concentration check. Two Willpower, the rote survives, but the mage is burned through her personal reserves.

> COMPLETE SPHERE DEFENSE EXAMPLE — PHASE INITIATIVE
> 
> Characters:
>   Siobhan (Verbena, Life 3, Entropy 2, Arete 3)
>   Quintessence: 4 remaining. Willpower: 3. Stored die: yes (she planned ahead).
>   Mid-cast: Life 3 Rote "The Grandmother's Knitting" — SF 2, healing a fallen ally.
>   The Rote is Coincidental. No Paradox expected.
> 
>   Antagonist: Black Dog Security merc with assault rifle [Autofire] [Loud].
>   Ranged 3 + Dexterity 4 = 7 dice, burst fire +2 = 9 dice total. He rolls 6 hits.
> 
> SIOBHAN'S PLAYER: "Six hits on a burst. I cannot survive that and pass
>   Concentration. I'm declaring a Sphere Defense."
> 
> SPHERE CHOICE: Siobhan has Life 3 and Entropy 2.
>   Option A — Life 3: Harden skin. Strong pool (Life 3 + Resolve 5 = 8 dice).
>     Same Sphere as active Rote. Paradox ceiling stays at Life 3 (no increase —
>     the Rote is already Life 3, defense is also Life 3).
>   Option B — Entropy 2: Cause the rifle to jam at the critical moment.
>     Different Sphere. Pool: Entropy 2 + Cunning 3 = 5 dice. Weaker.
>     Coincidental (weapons malfunction constantly). Zero additional Paradox.
>     Two separate Paradox rolls: Life 3 Rote (Coincidental = 0) + Entropy 2 (0). Both clean.
> 
> SIOBHAN'S PLAYER: "Entropy. I want to jam the weapon before the burst fires.
>   No bullets means no damage at all — even a partial Life defense still hurts me.
>   And both workings are Coincidental."
> 
> STORYGUIDE: "Guns jam. Approved. Roll Entropy 2 + Cunning 3."
> 
> ROLL: Entropy 2 + Cunning 3 = 5 dice. Difficulty 1 (Coincidental).
>   Cost: 1 Bank Token + 1 Quintessence (pool: 3 remaining).
>   Result: 9, 8, 7, 4, 2 = 3 hits.
> 
> COMPARE: Siobhan 3 hits vs. Merc 6 hits.
>   3 < 6 = Partial Defense.
>   Entropy cancels 3 of the merc's 6 hits. The rifle fires a shortened burst —
>   perhaps the jam cleared mid-burst, perhaps only part of the magazine cycled.
>   Remaining hits: 6 − 3 = 3 hits. Those 3 hits deal damage.
> 
> CONCENTRATION CHECK (partial defense):
>   Difficulty = 3 (remaining hits), not 6 (original hits).
>   Roll: Composure 3 + Resolve 5 + Arete 3 = 11 dice vs Difficulty 3.
>   Results: 10, 9, 9, 8, 7, 6, 4, 4, 3, 2, 1 = 7 hits (10=2). Easily passes.
>   The healing Rote continues.
> 
> DAMAGE: 3 hits from the partial burst. Siobhan has Armor 0. Takes 3 Lethal.
>   She marks 3 boxes — Impaired band, no penalty yet.
> 
> PARADOX:
>   Life 3 Rote: Coincidental = 0 Paradox dice.
>   Entropy 2 defense: Coincidental = 0 Paradox dice.
>   Total: 0. Clean fight. Both workings passed through the Consensus without friction.
> 
> OUTCOME: Siobhan took 3 hits instead of 6. Her healing Rote continues and will
> resolve next phase. She spent 1 Bank Token and 1 Quintessence to convert a likely
> catastrophic hit into a manageable wound. The tactical choice — Entropy over Life —
> was correct: stronger Paradox independence, and the possibility of stopping all
> bullets at source (even partial = fewer bullets, not less damage-per-bullet).
> 
> ALTERNATIVE: If Siobhan had rolled 6+ hits on Entropy, the rifle would have jammed
> completely before firing. Zero bullets. Zero damage. Zero Concentration check.
> The Rote resolves cleanly next phase and her fallen ally is healed.
> That is what a full Sphere Defense looks like.

#### Quick Reference: Sphere Defense

| Sphere Defense Element | Rule |
| --- | --- |
| Trigger cost (Phase Init) | Spend Bank Token + 1 Quintessence |
| Trigger cost (Spotlight) | 1 Willpower + 1 Quintessence |
| Minimum Sphere | ●● for most Spheres; Spirit ● and Entropy ● are exceptions |
| Sphere choice | Player's call — any Sphere ●●+ that narrative justifies |
| Roll | [Sphere] + [Casting Attribute] vs Diff 1 (Coincidental) or 2 (Vulgar) |
| Full success (hits ≥ attacker) | Attack negated. No damage. No Concentration check. |
| Partial (1 hit to attacker−1) | Reduce attacker hits by defense hits. Concentration at reduced Difficulty. |
| Failure (0 hits) | Full damage. Normal Concentration check. Resources spent. |
| Mid-cast: success | No Concentration check required. Casting continues. |
| Mid-cast: partial | Concentration check, Difficulty = remaining hits only. |
| Mid-cast: failure | Full Concentration check at original Difficulty. |
| Same Sphere as cast | Unified working. Paradox ceiling = highest Sphere dot involved. |
| Different Sphere | Two separate Paradox assessments, each on own Coincidental/Vulgar merits. |
| Willpower: Concentration save | Auto-pass one Concentration check per scene (declared after seeing Difficulty). |
| Willpower: substitute Quintessence | If Quintessence depleted, spend 1 WP instead of 1 QE to fuel defense. |
| Willpower: boost roll | +3 dice on the defense roll. Can combine with Quintessence cost. |
| Enhanced defense | Spend 2 Quintessence total: gain Enhancement +1 on defense roll. |
| Desperate surge | Spend all remaining QE (max +3 extra): +1 die per extra QE spent. |

### Combining Spheres

Many magical attacks and defenses combine multiple Spheres for greater effect. Each additional Sphere beyond the first adds +1 to the casting Difficulty and adds its own level to the Paradox Pool if the working is Vulgar. This creates a meaningful risk-reward tension in complex workings: more Spheres means more power, but also more Paradox exposure and higher Difficulty.

A practical example: A Life 3 + Forces 2 working that uses Life to accelerate tissue regeneration while simultaneously using Forces to cauterize the wound with directed heat has a Difficulty of 2 (base 1 + 1 for second Sphere). If it is Vulgar (which it probably is in most contexts), it adds 5 dice to the Paradox Pool (Life 3 + Forces 2).

### Common Magical Attack Forms

| Magic Type | Sphere \| Damage \| Notes |
| --- | --- |
| Pattern Strike | Prime 3 \| Aggravated 2 per 2 hits \| Bypasses all mundane armor; directly attacks the magical pattern of a being |
| Forces Bolt | Forces 2-3 \| Lethal (F2); Aggravated at F3 if directed as pattern attack \| Standard magical ranged attack; highly visible, Vulgar in Sleeper areas |
| Mind Assault | Mind 2-3 \| No physical damage \| Each hit inflicts a mental condition (Rattled, Disoriented, Stunned); at Mind 3, can inflict Stunned directly |
| Entropy Curse | Entropy 2-3 \| No direct damage \| +1 Difficulty to all target rolls per 2 hits; stacks up to +3; duration one scene |
| Life Disruption | Life 3 \| Lethal, bypasses armor \| Directly disrupts biological function at the cellular level; requires targeting a specific body system |
| Matter Shaping Attack | Matter 3 \| Environmental Lethal \| Reshape terrain, weapons, or armor — indirect damage from manipulation (floor becomes liquid, weapon becomes useless) |
| Correspondence Port | Correspondence 3 \| No damage \| Remove target from fight by teleporting them; resisted by Stamina + Resolve opposed roll |
| Time Slow | Time 3 \| No damage \| Target acts last in Initiative for remainder of scene; -2 dice to their pools while maintained |
| Spirit Binding | Spirit 3 \| No damage \| Immobilize a materialized spirit; resisted by Spirit's Willpower; useful for spirit enemies only |
| Entropy Catastrophe | Entropy 4 \| Aggravated \| Objects, structures, or complex systems (including cyborg components) begin to fail catastrophically |

### Maintaining Effects in Combat

Sustained magical effects — Mage Armor, a Time Sense combat enhancement, an active Mind connection to an ally — require ongoing concentration to maintain. Each sustained effect costs 1 die from all other dice pools the mage rolls while it is active. A mage maintaining two simultaneous effects has -2 dice to everything else. Three effects: -3 dice. This penalty is continuous and universal — it applies to attacks, defenses, social rolls, everything.

When a mage takes significant damage while maintaining a sustained effect, the Storyguide calls for a Concentration roll: Composure + Resolve + Arete/Enlightenment against Difficulty equal to the number of hits the attacker scored (before armor reduction). Failure means one maintained effect of the mage's choice drops. The mage does not get to choose which one fails if they have no time to think — the Storyguide may determine which drops based on dramatic logic.

This mechanic creates a genuine cost for magical multitasking under fire. A mage who wants to maintain Mage Armor, a Time combat sense, and a Mind link with two allies simultaneously is paying 3 dice off every pool while managing three Concentration checks whenever they take hits. It is possible. It is extremely taxing. It is exactly what powerful combat mages look like: constantly walking a knife-edge between capability and collapse.

### Dispelling and Counter-Magic

A mage can attempt to dispel or counter an ongoing magical effect. This is one of the most important combat tools available: neutralizing a sustained working before it can do further harm. Counter-magic uses the relevant Sphere at the same or higher level than the effect being countered, combined with a Prime component at Prime 2 (to directly disrupt the pattern of the working).

Counter-magic roll: Intellect + Sphere (relevant to the effect) against Difficulty equal to the original caster's hits on the effect. Each hit on the counter-magic roll removes one hit of strength from the effect. When the effect reaches 0 hits of strength remaining, it ends. If the caster is present and maintaining the effect actively, they may attempt to reinforce it: roll their casting pool and add hits to the effect's remaining strength. This becomes a contested magical tug-of-war that can span multiple Rounds.

A particularly skilled counter-magic practitioner can turn an active dispel into an attack: if they score more than enough hits to dispel the effect and have Prime 3, the excess Quintessence released by the dispel can be directed back at the original caster as a pattern-disruption attack (Lethal damage, bypasses mundane armor, hits equal to excess hits above the dispel threshold).

---

## Wonders in Combat

Wonders — enchanted objects, Technocratic Devices, spirit Fetishes, Hermetic Talismans, Etherite Inventions — bring reliable magical capability into combat without requiring the wielder to make a casting roll each time. The magic is encoded in the object during its creation; the wielder simply activates it. Wonders are the great equalizer: a non-mage ally can use a pre-charged Wonder as effectively as the mage who made it, as long as they can activate the trigger. They are also preparation made tangible — the mage who spent three hours last week enchanting a defensive talisman is now the mage who has a defensive talisman when they need one.

Activating a Wonder is a Minor Action for simple effects: firing a charged bolt from a Tesla coil pistol, activating a charm whose trigger is touch, triggering a pre-set ward. It is a Major Action for effects requiring targeting, sustained output, complex intent, or large-scale environmental manipulation. The Wonder's description always specifies which applies.

Wonders do not generate Paradox for their wielder in the moment of use — the Paradox was built into the object at creation time and absorbed by the maker through the construction process. This is one of the primary reasons mages invest so heavily in Wonder-creation: combat Paradox exposure goes to zero, at the cost of significant preparation time. However: if a Wonder's effect is witnessed by Sleepers and is clearly impossible by consensus reality standards, the Storyguide may rule that the witnessing itself generates narrative complications (police attention, civilian panic, Syndicate monitoring flags) even without a direct Paradox roll.

### Wonder Tags in Combat

Wonders can have the same Tags as mundane weapons, plus unique Tags specific to their magical nature:

- [Charged X]: The Wonder has X charges of its primary effect. When charges run out, it must be recharged with Quintessence (Minor Action in a Node; Major Action anywhere else). This functions exactly like [Reload X] for compel purposes.
- [Sustained]: The Wonder produces a continuous effect that lasts as long as it is active. Activating is a Minor Action; the effect persists each Round without further rolls. Deactivating is a free action.
- [Attuned]: The Wonder responds to a specific user or type of user. Others who try to activate it must spend 1 Momentum or make an opposed roll. Attuned Wonders are more reliable in their user's hands and harder for enemies to exploit.
- [Unstable]: The Wonder has a flaw in its construction. The Storyguide may compel [Unstable] at any point — the Wonder misfires, produces an unintended effect, or temporarily ceases to function. Players who accept this compel gain Momentum.

### Magic Compatibility

A mage using a Wonder whose paradigm directly contradicts their own may generate friction at the Storyguide's discretion. A deeply nature-oriented Verbena using an Iteration X neural interface designed to filter sensory data through a machine-logic framework is asking her pattern to process something fundamentally alien to its Resonance. Most Wonders are paradigm-flexible enough to be used by anyone with basic magical awareness, but edge cases create interesting roleplay moments. The Storyguide should use this sparingly and for narrative effect, not as a mechanical punishment for creativity.

---

## Magical Defenses

Beyond physical armor and active dodging, mages have access to magical protective techniques that provide defense not available to any mundane combatant. These require Sphere access, generate Paradox if maintained visibly in Sleeper areas, and carry the maintenance cost of sustained effects. But they can also make a mage nearly impossible to harm through conventional means — which is why the NWO trains specifically in methods to disrupt, dispel, and circumvent magical defenses before committing to direct assault.

| Defense | Sphere \| Armor/Effect \| Paradox Risk \| Maintenance Cost |
| --- | --- |
| Mage Armor | Forces 3 or Life 3 \| Armor 3 vs. Lethal, 1 vs. Aggravated \| Vulgar if shimmering or visible; Coincidental if subtle and in private space \| -1 die to all actions while sustained |
| Pattern Hardening | Life 3 or Matter 3 \| +2 to existing armor for one scene \| Extended cast (1 min); Vulgar \| No ongoing cost — cast once per scene |
| Mind Shield | Mind 2 \| Mental Armor 2 vs. all Mind attacks and psychic intrusion \| Coincidental if not displayed \| Can be maintained without penalty — subtle effect |
| Warding | Prime 2 + relevant Sphere \| Place-based alert and barrier — intruder detected, entry resisted \| Must be set in advance; not combat-speed \| No ongoing cost after setup |
| Counterspell | Any Sphere at same or higher level \| Cancel incoming magical effect \| Depends on effect countered \| Contested roll (see Dispelling) |
| Temporal Dodge | Time 3 \| +2 Difficulty on all attacks against you from seeing 1 second ahead \| Vulgar in Sleeper areas \| -1 die to all actions while sustained |
| Probability Shield | Entropy 3 \| Successful attacks must reroll; take lower result \| Vulgar \| -1 die to all actions while sustained |
| Spatial Dodge | Correspondence 2 \| Microshift position continuously; +1 Difficulty on attacks \| Coincidental in familiar space \| -1 die to all actions while sustained |

Stacking magical defenses is possible but expensive. A mage running Mage Armor, Temporal Dodge, and a Mind Shield simultaneously has -2 dice to all actions (Mage Armor and Temporal Dodge each cost 1 die; Mind Shield is free) plus three separate Concentration checks to manage when hit. This is a powerful defensive posture — effectively impossible to harm through mundane means — but the mage is paying dearly in offensive and cognitive capacity.

---

## Special Circumstances

### Fighting Multiple Opponents

When a single character faces multiple enemies who are all attacking within the same Round, being surrounded is mechanically punishing. For each attacker beyond the first who attacks in the same Round, the defender suffers -1 die to all defense rolls for that Round. Two attackers: -1 die to dodge and parry. Three attackers: -2 dice. Four or more: still capped at -2 dice (at that point, the mathematics of being surrounded are already catastrophic without adding further penalties).

This means that a well-coordinated team that can all attack on the same Round — through similar Initiative scores or through held actions timed to arrive simultaneously — can overwhelm even a very skilled defender. The NWO uses exactly this tactic: two agents draw fire from the front while a third flanks. By the time the third agent fires, the defender is at -2 dice to everything and has probably spent their Major Action on defense. The formation is not about individual agent capability; it is about creating the -2 die penalty.

Conversely, a mage who understands this can prioritize breaking out of encirclement: a Correspondence teleport, a Forces blast that clears the space around them, a sprint through a chokepoint that forces enemies to attack one-at-a-time. Escaping encirclement before it fully materializes is always better than dealing with it once established.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Multiple opponents: Rosamund faces three cultists. They have all acted and all attacked this round. Rosamund chose Active Dodge (Major Action, 2 hits = +2 Difficulty to all attacks). But she is also at -2 dice to her dodge pool because of the three attackers (-1 per attacker beyond first). Her active dodge: Athletics 1 + Dexterity 3 = 4 dice, -2 for multiple opponents = 2 dice. She scores 1 hit. That gives only +1 Difficulty instead of +2. The cultists were harder to lock down than she hoped. She survived this round. She needs to thin the numbers or escape, not wait it out.

### Environmental Hazards

Extreme environments impose ongoing effects that compound with combat damage and create secondary tactical problems. Smart combatants use environmental hazards deliberately: luring enemies into a burning room, fighting near electrical panels, positioning over a water source for Shock weaponry. The environment is always a potential weapon.

| Hazard | Ongoing Effect \| Notes |
| --- | --- |
| Active fire | 2 Aggravated per round to anyone in the fire; adjacent characters risk Burning condition \| Spreads 1m per round on flammable surfaces |
| High-voltage electrical | 3 Lethal per contact; Stunned condition; metal armor becomes an additional hazard (Storyguide's judgment) \| Touching conductive surfaces counts as contact |
| Drowning / submerged | Stamina + Athletics, Difficulty 2 per round; failure = 1 Lethal (asphyxiation) \| Fighting underwater: all physical pools at -2 dice; firearms do not function; most spells function normally |
| Extreme cold | Stamina + Resolve, Difficulty 1 per scene initially; rising to Difficulty 2 after first hour \| Failure = Impaired condition; eventual Lethal from hypothermia |
| Falling | 1 Bashing per 3m; at 10m+ = Lethal; at 20m+ = Aggravated \| Athletics + Might to roll and reduce: each hit reduces damage by 1 |
| Toxic atmosphere | Stamina + Fortitude per round at rising Difficulty (starts 1, adds 1 per failed roll) \| Failure = 1 Lethal cumulative; mask or magical air filtration negates |
| Zero gravity | All physical actions at -1 die; movement requires surface to push from \| Magic functions normally; unexpected tactical implications |

### Vehicles and Mounted Combat

Fighting in, on, or from vehicles introduces the driver's divided attention as a core tactical element. A driver who also wants to attack applies -1 die to both their driving roll and their attack roll — they are doing two demanding physical tasks simultaneously. Passengers suffer no such penalty unless they are also attempting to drive or otherwise interact with the vehicle's controls.

Passengers in a moving vehicle gain partial cover (+1 Difficulty for attackers targeting them) from the vehicle's body. Passengers in a vehicle specifically rated for combat (armored vehicles, helicopter gunships) gain the vehicle's own Armor rating against external attacks.

Attacking a vehicle to disable or destroy it requires either: hitting specific components (engine, tire, fuel tank) at +2 Difficulty with a called shot, or simply dealing enough damage to reduce the vehicle's Structure to 0, at which point it fails catastrophically — the Storyguide determines whether this means a slow stop, a catastrophic engine fire, a crash, or a spectacular explosion based on the vehicle type and damage dealt.

| Vehicle | Structure \| Armor \| Special Notes |
| --- | --- |
| Motorcycle | 4 \| 0 \| Rider takes damage directly if 3+ hits connect with vehicle; extremely maneuverable |
| Civilian car | 6 \| 1 \| Passengers get +1 cover; fuel tank explodes on Aggravated damage to rear |
| SUV / Pickup truck | 7 \| 2 \| Higher ground advantage for bed passengers vs. surrounding pedestrians |
| Armored civilian vehicle | 8 \| 3 \| Technocratic "executive transport"; appears mundane |
| Military armored vehicle | 9 \| 4 \| Personnel inside safe from most small arms; heavy weapon required to threaten |
| Helicopter | 6 \| 1 \| Rotor destruction = crash in 1d6 rounds; altitude determines fall damage |
| Iteration X Hopper | 7 \| 3 \| Technocratic military transport; can verticalize; passengers gain Armor 3 internally |

### Grappling and Restraint

Grappling — attempting to physically seize, hold, and control another person — is a Major Action. To initiate: roll Athletics + Might against the target's Athletics + Might (opposed roll, both roll simultaneously). On success, both characters are Grappling. While Grappling, neither character can use ranged weapons or two-handed weapons effectively. Both characters can still speak, cast spells, and use items small enough to deploy with one hand.

Each round of grapple, both characters roll Athletics + Might. The winner may choose one of the following outcomes: inflict 1 Bashing (a controlled strike within the grapple), improve their position (setting up advantage for next round — +1 die on the next grapple roll), attempt a specific technique (Takedown: Prone + 1 Bashing; Disarm; Choke-hold: target must make Stamina roll or be Stunned), or attempt to break free themselves.

Breaking free from a grapple: Athletics + Might, Difficulty 2. Success ends the grapple. Both characters may act freely next round. Failure maintains the grapple and the initiating character may exploit the failed escape: free Trick Hit.

Mages who are grappled face a specific problem: many magical effects require specific hand gestures (somantic components), vocal components, or access to instruments. A grappled mage who needs both hands for their casting paradigm cannot cast until they escape. A mage whose paradigm allows purely mental or vocal casting is much less disrupted by grapple — which is one reason some mages invest in purely mind-based magical paradigms despite the Prime cost.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Grapple and magic: A Syndicate agent has seized Annika by both wrists — she is Grappled and cannot complete the hand-gestures for her Forces working. However, Annika's Celestial Chorus paradigm allows for purely sung magical workings. She sings — just four words of sacred Latin — and her Mind 2 effect fires through the grapple with no physical component required. The agent's grip loosens as his threat-assessment suddenly prioritizes the wrong target. One round of grapple, one clever paradigm exploitation.

---

## Death, Incapacitation, and Consequences

When a character fills all Health boxes, they are Incapacitated. The story does not stop here. In fact, this is often when the most interesting story choices arrive: What do the enemies do? What do the allies do? Who is watching? What was the character carrying that now falls into other hands? Incapacitation is a story engine, not a curtain.

For player characters specifically, the Storyguide should always consider what makes Incapacitation interesting rather than simply painful. Being captured by the NWO is not just defeat — it is an opportunity: interrogation scene, a rescue, a chance to learn something about the enemy's operation from the inside. Being left for dead in an alley opens completely different story doors than being taken prisoner. Consequences should generate forward momentum, not dead ends.

### Paradox Nova — Death of a Mage

When a mage dies while carrying 3 or more dice in their Paradox Pool, the Pool explodes outward in a phenomenon called a Paradox Nova. The mage's pattern unravels catastrophically, and all that accumulated reality-strain releases at once. A Paradox Nova is one of the most dangerous events that can occur in a magical combat zone — not because the mage intended it, but because it happens regardless of intent.

```
Paradox Nova Rules
------------------
Trigger: Mage dies with 3 or more dice in their Paradox Pool.

All Paradox Pool dice are rolled simultaneously. All results apply to everyone in the immediate area (radius in meters equals the Pool size at time of death). Every result applies — this is not "pick one," it is "all of these happen at once."

  1 — Unraveling: The mage's last active spell is reversed or twisted catastrophically. If they were maintaining Mage Armor, it inverts, briefly drawing attacks rather than deflecting them. If they were mid-teleportation, the destination becomes randomized.
  3 — Backlash Wound: Every living being in range takes 1 Bashing from the reality shockwave.
  5 — Paradox Flare: The area becomes Paradox-charged for the rest of the scene. The next Vulgar magic performed in this space automatically gains +2 dice to its Paradox roll.
  7 — Quintessence Drain: All magical effects within the radius immediately end as the raw Quintessence is consumed by the Nova.
  9 — Spirit Touch: A Paradox Spirit is drawn to the location, arriving within 10 minutes to assess the source of the disruption.
  10 — Catastrophe: Every living being in range takes 1 Aggravated damage as reality tears at the fundamental level. The mage's body is destroyed completely — no remains, no forensic evidence, just a scorched outline.

The Paradox Pool is fully consumed. The mage is gone. And everyone in the area knows exactly why they should have been paying more attention to Paradox management.
```

A Paradox Nova is simultaneously a dramatic payoff and a tactical warning to every mage present. When a mage with a high Paradox Pool is in danger of dying, their allies have a strong incentive to keep them alive — not out of compassion, but out of self-preservation. A mage who dies with 8 Paradox dice is going to make the next 8 seconds very unpleasant for everyone within 8 meters. This creates genuine moral complexity: do you save the reckless mage, or do you get out of range?

---

## Combat Examples — Progressive Complexity

The following eight examples progress from simple mundane combat through full magical engagement, social and political magical confrontation, detailed Tag mechanics, and finally the Phase Initiative system — the tactical variant described in Appendix H. Examples 1 through 6 use the standard Spotlight system. Examples 7 and 8 demonstrate Phase Initiative: first in isolation, then in comparison with Spotlight to help you and your group decide which to reach for at the table. Read them in order to see how the system builds on itself. Each example is designed to be usable as a direct teaching tool at the table — share these with new players before their first combat session.

### Example 1: Simple Mundane Combat — The Alley

> SCENE: Rosamund (Verbena, Health 5) is confronted by a gang enforcer in an alley. She has no intention of using magic — Sleepers are visible at the alley entrance. The enforcer (Might 3, Close Combat 2, Dexterity 2, Stamina 3, Health 5) draws a knife. His knife has the Tags [Fast] and [Concealed] — the same Tags as Rosamund's combat blade.

INITIATIVE:
Rosamund: Athletics 1 + Cunning 3 = 4 dice → 7, 8, 3, 2 = 1 hit.
Enforcer: Athletics 2 + Cunning 1 = 3 dice → 9, 3, 4 = 1 hit.
Tie — Rosamund was here first, her body already reading the situation. She goes first.

ROUND 1 — ROSAMUND (Spotlight):
"I need to be impossible to hit while I assess him." She commits her Major Action to Active Dodge.
Athletics 1 + Dexterity 3 = 4 dice → 8, 9, 3, 2 = 2 hits. All attacks against her this Round: +2 Difficulty.

ROUND 1 — ENFORCER (Spotlight):
Knife attack: Close Combat 2 + Dexterity 2 = 4 dice. Base Difficulty 1 + 2 (active dodge) = Difficulty 3.
Results: 8, 4, 6, 2 = 1 hit. Needs 3. Miss — the knife finds nothing but cold air.

ROUND 2 — ROSAMUND (Spotlight):
"Grab his knife arm. I want to control, not kill."
Grapple: Athletics 1 + Might 2 = 3 dice → 10, 8, 5 = 3 hits (10 = 2 hits). Difficulty 1. 2 Trick Hits.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Disarm: the knife clatters to the wet pavement.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Stagger: enforcer loses his next Minor Action.

ROUND 2 — ENFORCER (Spotlight):
No Minor Action from Stagger — he cannot draw his backup weapon. Unarmed attack only.
Athletics 2 + Might 3 = 5 dice → 9, 3, 4, 2, 7 = 1 hit. Exactly hits Difficulty 1.
1 Bashing. Rosamund marks 1 Bashing. Health: 4/5 boxes empty. No penalty yet.

ROUND 3 — ROSAMUND (Spotlight):
"Heel-strike to the solar plexus." Athletics 1 + Might 2 = 3 dice → 8, 9, 4 = 2 hits. 1 Trick Hit.
1 Bashing base. Spend Trick Hit → Knockdown. Enforcer: 2 Bashing, now Prone.

ROUND 3 — ENFORCER:
Prone, 2 Bashing, facing a trained hedge-mage who has not broken a sweat.
"I'm done," he says, hands flat on the pavement. Rosamund retrieves his knife as an afterthought.

COMBAT RESULT: 3 rounds. Zero magic. Zero Paradox. One bruised rib on Rosamund, one humiliated enforcer, zero witnesses who saw anything impossible.

KEY MECHANICS DEMONSTRATED: Active Dodge as the decisive Round 1 choice. Trick Hits enabling Disarm and Knockdown. Stagger creating a tactical deficit. The clean end state of non-lethal mundane combat.

### Example 2: Initiative, Cover, and the Cost of Armor-Piercing — The Rooftop

> SCENE: Vikram (Health 6) is fleeing two NWO agents across a rain-slicked rooftop. Agent Carver (Ranged Combat 3, Dexterity 3, handgun with [Armor-Piercing] rounds) pursues from behind. Agent Moss (Ranged Combat 2, Dexterity 4, standard SMG [Autofire] [Loud] [Reload 3]) is cutting off a flanking route.

INITIATIVE:
Vikram: Athletics 2 + Cunning 4 = 6 dice → 10, 8, 5, 3, 2, 1 = 3 hits.
Moss: Athletics 3 + Cunning 2 = 5 dice → 8, 8, 4, 2, 1 = 2 hits.
Carver: Athletics 2 + Cunning 3 = 5 dice → 9, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 1 hit.
Order: Vikram (3), Moss (2), Carver (1). Vikram goes first — he saw the ambush pattern and moved before they could close the trap.

ROUND 1 — VIKRAM (Spotlight):
Sprints full-out for a rooftop AC unit (Major Action: full sprint, 12m). Minor Action: ducks into heavy cover position.
Heavy cover: +2 Difficulty for all attacks against him.

ROUND 1 — MOSS (Spotlight):
Moss fires while running — she cannot hold her position while the target moves.
SMG attack: Ranged 2 + Dexterity 4 = 6 dice. Difficulty 1 + 2 (heavy cover) + 1 (Moss moving) = 4.
Results: 9, 8, 7, 6, 4, 3 = 3 hits. Needs 4. Shot sparks off the AC unit. Vikram flinches but takes nothing.

ROUND 1 — CARVER (Spotlight):
Carver stops moving. Minor Action: brace against a ventilation stack (removes +1 Difficulty from next shot).
Handgun with [Armor-Piercing] rounds. Carver invokes [Armor-Piercing]: "My round will punch through whatever cover he's using." The Storyguide confirms: the round penetrates the AC unit — heavy cover drops to +1 Difficulty for this shot.
Ranged 3 + Dexterity 3 = 6 dice. Difficulty 1 + 1 (cover, reduced by AP) - 1 (bracing) = 1.
Results: 10, 9, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 3 hits (10=2). 2 Trick Hits.
Damage: 4 Lethal (handgun). Spend 2 Trick Hits → Extra Damage x2: 6 Lethal.
Vikram has no armor. Health 6 → 0. He is critically hit — last box just filled. Critical.

ROUND 2 — VIKRAM (Spotlight):
6 boxes filled. He cannot function through this wound. Sees a skylight.
"Through the glass." Athletics 2 + Dexterity 3 = 5 dice, -2 (Critical penalty) = 3 dice. Difficulty 2.
Result: 8, 6, 4 = 1 hit. Just barely threads the gap and crashes through.
Fall: 3m drop = 1 Bashing. Track is full — Bashing converts to Lethal. Vikram is Incapacitated, in the stairwell, out of the agents' sight.

The agents must descend through the building — losing their tactical position — to follow. The mission clock has shifted in Vikram's favor even as he lies bleeding.

KEY MECHANICS: [Armor-Piercing] invoke reducing cover effectiveness. Bracing as a tactical Minor Action. Critical wound creating the desperate exit decision. Incapacitated-but-alive creating a story pivot rather than a story end.

### Example 3: Magical Combat with Full Paradox — The Parking Structure

> SCENE: Maya (Virtual Adept, Correspondence 3, Forces 2, Mind 1, Health 6) and Silas (Son of Ether, Forces 3, Matter 2, Health 7, Tesla gauntlets [Electrical][Precise][Charged 5]) face two Iteration X combat cyborgs in an empty parking structure. No civilian witnesses. The cyborgs have: Might 5, Dexterity 4, Stamina 5, Armor 3, Health 9, Ranged Combat 3, SMG [Autofire].

INITIATIVE:
Maya: Athletics 2 + Cunning 4 = 6 dice → 9, 8, 8, 4, 3, 2 = 3 hits.
Silas: Athletics 2 + Cunning 2 = 4 dice → 10, 7, 4, 2 = 3 hits (10=2). Tie with Maya; Maya goes first.
Cyborg 1: Athletics 3 + Cunning 2 = 5 dice → 8, 8, 4, 3, 2 = 2 hits.
Cyborg 2: Athletics 3 + Cunning 2 = 5 dice → 9, 5, 3, 2, 1 = 1 hit.
Order: Maya (3), Silas (3, second), Cyborg 1 (2), Cyborg 2 (1).

─── ROUND 1 ───

MAYA: "I pull Cyborg 2 out of the fight." Correspondence 3 — teleport Cyborg 2 to roof (Vulgar, no witnesses).
Casting: Correspondence 3 + Cunning 4 = 7 dice. Difficulty 3 (large unwilling mass).
Results: 10, 9, 8, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 5 hits (10=2). 2 Trick Hits.
Cyborg 2 resists: Stamina 5 + Resolve 3 = 8 dice → 8, 8, 4, 3, 3, 2, 2, 1 = 2 hits. Maya wins convincingly.
Cyborg 2 vanishes; reappears on the roof 4 stories up. Trick Hit 1: safe landing. Trick Hit 2: roof door is mechanically sealed on the exterior, delaying its return.
Paradox: +3 dice (Correspondence 3 Vulgar). Maya's pool = 3.

SILAS: "Tesla gauntlet. Focused arc on Cyborg 1." Forces 3 (Vulgar). Invokes [Electrical] Tag on gauntlets: +2 dice vs. metal chassis. Invokes [Precise] Tag: +2 dice on called shot to Cyborg 1's targeting system.
Casting: Forces 3 + Might 3 = 6 dice + 2 (Electrical) + 2 (Precise) = 10 dice. Difficulty 1.
Results: 10, 9, 9, 8, 7, 6, 4, 3, 2, 1 = 7 hits (10=2). 6 Trick Hits.
Paradox: +3 dice (Forces 3 Vulgar). Silas pool = 3.
Damage: 3 Lethal + 3 (Trick Hit Extra Damage x3) = 6 Lethal. Cyborg 1 Armor 3: takes 3 Lethal. Health: 9 → 6.
Spend 2 Trick Hits → [Electrical] Trick: targeting system burns out. Cyborg 1 at +2 Difficulty on all attacks next 2 rounds.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Shocked condition: Cyborg 1 loses all actions this round (already acted? No — it hasn't acted yet this round).

CYBORG 1: Shocked — entire Spotlight lost. Cannot act.

CYBORG 2: On the roof. Finds the sealed door. Attempts to breach: Might 5 = 5 dice, Difficulty 3 (reinforced seal + magical working). Results: 8, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 1 hit. Not enough. Still locked out. Round 1 passes without Cyborg 2 in the fight.

─── ROUND 2 ───

MAYA: "Forces 2 — electromagnetic surge. Cheap, Coincidental, no Paradox." Disrupts Cyborg 1's electronics.
Casting: Forces 2 + Cunning 4 = 6 dice. Difficulty 1. Coincidental (electrical surge in industrial space). Zero Paradox.
Results: 9, 9, 8, 4, 3, 2 = 3 hits. 2 Trick Hits.
Electrical surge: -2 dice on Cyborg 1's attack pools. Spend 1 Trick Hit → Duration extended to 3 rounds.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Cyborg 2 also affected (it's on a roof above, still within the surge field). Same penalty applies.
Paradox pool stays at 3. Good. Safe so far.

SILAS: Attacks Cyborg 1 again. Forces 3 + Might 3 = 6 dice + [Charged] Tag (still have 4 charges). No additional invoke. Vulgar: +3 Paradox. Pool = 6.
Results: 10, 8, 7, 6, 4, 3 = 4 hits (10=2). 3 Trick Hits. 3 Lethal + 3 (Tricks) = 6 Lethal. Armor 3: 3 Lethal through. Health: 6 → 3. Impaired.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Knockdown: Cyborg 1 is Prone. Melee attackers now at -1 Difficulty vs. it.

CYBORG 1: Prone, Impaired, +2 Difficulty from targeting failure, -2 from surge. Fires SMG at Silas anyway.
SMG: Ranged 3 + Dexterity 4 = 7 dice, -2 (Impaired) -2 (surge) = 3 dice, +2 Difficulty (targeting burn-out) = Difficulty 3.
Results: 9, 8, 4 = 1 hit. Needs 3. Miss.

CYBORG 2: Breaches roof door on second attempt. Begins descending stairs. Not on combat floor yet.

─── ROUND 3 ───

MAYA: "Cyborg 1 is almost down. Lock it." Correspondence 2 — anchor Cyborg 1 to its current position. Vulgar (it is clearly impossible). +2 Paradox. Pool = 5.
Casting: Correspondence 2 + Cunning 4 = 6 dice. Difficulty 2 (resisted). Results: 9, 8, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 3 hits.
Cyborg 1 resists: Stamina 5 + Resolve 3 = 8 dice → 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 1 = 0 hits. Anchored — Pinned until dispelled.

SILAS: Walks to Cyborg 1. Point blank. Forces 3. Final charge on the gauntlets ([Charged 5] down to 1 charge after this).
Difficulty 1 - 1 (Prone target in melee) = Difficulty 0 minimum 1. Still 1. 10 dice pool total with Tags.
Results: 10, 10, 9, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2 = 8 hits (10s = 2 each). Trick Hits: 7.
Damage: 3 + 7 = 10 Lethal. Armor 3: 7 Lethal through. Cyborg 1 Health: 3 → Incapacitated. Beyond Incapacitated. Systems offline. Not getting up.
Silas pool = 9. High but... he checks the pool. That is a lot of dice.

CYBORG 2: Enters the parking level. Fresh, undamaged. Fires SMG burst at Silas ([Autofire] invoked by the SG: it targets both mages in a sweep).
Autofire compelled by SG: "The burst catches both of you. You each take the hit, and I'm going to compel [Loud] — that sustained automatic fire has now definitely triggered the building's security system. You have 4 minutes before guards arrive." Silas's player accepts. Maya's player accepts. Both gain 1 Momentum.
Silas: Ranged 3 + Dexterity 4 = 7 dice. Results: 9, 9, 8, 7, 4 = 4 hits. 4 Lethal burst. Armor (none): 4 Lethal. Silas Health: 7 → 3. Impaired.
Maya: Same burst, 1 hit applied to secondary target: 1 Lethal. Maya Health: 6 → 5. No penalty yet. But the building security clock is now running.

─── RESOLUTION ───
Silas is Impaired and has a gauntlet with 1 charge remaining. Maya has 5 Paradox dice. Cyborg 2 is fully functional. Security arriving in 4 minutes.
"We run," Maya says. They run. The Paradox pool rolls as they exit.
Maya: 5 dice → results 3, 7, 9, 1, 10. Results: 3 (Backlash Wound: 1 Bashing to Maya), 9 (Spirit Touch: Paradox Spirit inbound), 10 (Catastrophe: 3 Aggravated to Maya — she convulses mid-sprint). Silas catches her. They make it to the car. Maya is Incapacitated from Aggravated damage. Silas drives.

KEY MECHANICS: Tag invokes from magical instruments. Multi-round tactical coordination. Paradox accumulating until it becomes the dominant threat. Compel on [Autofire] creating a time pressure. Paradox Nova risk narrowly avoided (Maya survived). Security clock from [Loud] compel reshaping the post-combat scene.

### Example 4: Mixed Tactical Scene — Spirit Incursion

> SCENE: Tobias (Dreamspeaker, Spirit 3, Life 2, Health 6) and Annika (Celestial Chorus, Prime 2, Forces 1, Mind 1, Health 5) defend a Node from a materialized Bane Spirit. No Sleepers present.

BANE STATS: Might 4, Dexterity 3, Stamina 4, Spirit Armor 2 (immune to non-silver, non-magical, non-Aggravated damage), Health 7. Claws: 2 Aggravated. Spirit Scream: Mind attack, Difficulty 2, inflicts Rattled and Staggered on 2+ hits.

INITIATIVE: Bane (3), Tobias (2), Annika (1).

ROUND 1 — BANE: Spirit Scream at Annika.
Pool: Might 4 + Cunning 2 = 6 dice → 9, 9, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 2 hits. Difficulty 2: exactly hits.
Annika resists: Composure 2 + Resolve 3 = 5 dice → 8, 4, 3, 2, 1 = 1 hit. Fails. Rattled and Staggered.

ROUND 1 — TOBIAS: Iron-tipped rowan-wood staff (Lethal vs. spirits, [Reach], [Attuned] to Spirit-realm entities).
Close Combat 2 + Might 3 = 5 dice → 10, 9, 7, 4, 2 = 4 hits (10=2). 3 Trick Hits.
Invokes [Attuned]: +2 dice retroactively recognized — the hit is clearly attuned, the SG confirms the Tag was worth it.
Damage: 2 Lethal. Spirit Armor 2: 0 through normally.
But: spend 1 Trick Hit → Wound (spirit anatomy via Spirit 3 knowledge): bypass Spirit Armor entirely. 2 Lethal through.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Stagger: Bane loses next Minor Action.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Setup: Annika gains +1 die on her next roll against the Bane.
Bane Health: 7 → 5.

ROUND 1 — ANNIKA: Rattled (-1 die to Mental) and Staggered (no Minor Action). Uses Major Action: Prime 2 counter-ritual — disrupts the Bane's pattern coherence. Coincidental (sacred counter-ritual in a Node space).
Casting: Prime 2 + Intellect 3 = 5 dice -1 (Rattled) + 1 (Tobias's Setup bonus) = 5 dice. Difficulty 2.
Results: 9, 8, 6, 4, 3 = 2 hits. Hits Difficulty 2. Bane: -1 die to all actions until end of next round.

ROUND 2 — BANE (no Minor Action, Staggered): Claws at Tobias.
Might 4 + Dexterity 3 = 7 dice -1 (Annika disruption) = 6 dice. Results: 9, 8, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 3 hits. 2 Trick Hits.
2 Aggravated. Spend 2 Tricks → Extra Damage x2: 4 Aggravated. Tobias: Health 6 → 2. Critical.

ROUND 2 — TOBIAS: Critical (-2 dice). 3 dice for staff attack. Results: 8, 7, 4 = 1 hit. No Tricks. Spirit Armor negates. Nothing.
"Annika — I need healing, not offense."

ROUND 2 — ANNIKA: Life 2 healing on Tobias (touch). Intellect 3 + Medicine 2 = 5 dice. Difficulty 1. Results: 9, 8, 8, 4, 2 = 3 hits. Heals 3 Aggravated (SG ruling: Life 2 can address spirit-Aggravated in Node space). Tobias: Health 2 + 3 = 5. No longer Critical.

ROUND 3 — BANE: Screams at Annika again. 6 dice, Difficulty 2. Results: 9, 7, 4, 3, 3, 2 = 1 hit. Fails Difficulty 2. Annika stands firm.

ROUND 3 — TOBIAS: Full health (5/6). Spends 1 Momentum — calls Node spirits to his aid. +2 dice. Staff: 7 dice. Results: 10, 9, 9, 8, 7, 4, 2 = 6 hits. 5 Trick Hits.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Wound (bypass armor): 2 Lethal through.
Spend 2 Trick Hits → Extra Damage x2: 4 Lethal.
Spend 2 Trick Hits → Banish (Spirit 3 Trick): Spirit + Presence opposed vs. Bane Willpower. Tobias: 6 dice → 9, 9, 8, 6, 4, 2 = 3 hits. Bane: Willpower 5 → 8, 4, 3, 2, 1 = 1 hit. Tobias wins. Bane takes 4 Lethal AND is partially banished — pushed back to the Umbra for the scene.

Bane Health: 5 - 4 = 1. Driven back. The Node is held.

KEY MECHANICS: Spirit Armor as a gear-check requiring Trick Hits to bypass. Conditions stacking (Rattled + Staggered) but not disabling. Healing as a tactical Major Action. Momentum spending for narrative effect. Banish as a Spirit-specific Trick requiring Sphere knowledge.

### Example 5: Social and Political Stakes in Magical Combat — The Conclave

> SCENE: The Traditions Conclave in a Seattle waterfront warehouse. Present: Elara (Verbena Elder, Spirit 4, Life 3, Mind 2, Health 6, significant political authority within the Traditions), Magistra Voss (Hermetic Magister, Forces 4, Matter 3, Prime 3, Health 5, extensive Conclave connections), and the player character mages — Rosamund (Verbena, Life 2, Forces 1), Vikram (Virtual Adept, Correspondence 3, Forces 2), and Annika (Celestial Chorus, Prime 2, Mind 2).

The political situation: Magistra Voss has accused Elara of deliberately sabotaging a Tradition mission that cost three lives. The accusation is false — Voss orchestrated the sabotage and is attempting to cover her tracks by discrediting Elara before evidence surfaces. The player characters know this. Voss has brought three Hermetic Guardians who are loyal to her personally (Close Combat 3, Ranged Combat 2, Health 5 each).

Elara invokes Conclave law: magical combat on consecrated Conclave ground is forbidden. Doing so risks formal censure, loss of Tradition standing, and — in extreme cases — the Rite of Severance (magical excommunication). The player characters face a fight where winning through violence means losing politically. And yet Voss's Guardians are between them and the door.

INITIATIVE (not for fighting — for social maneuvering; the SG rules this as an extended conflict with initiative determining speaking order and positioning):
Vikram: Athletics 2 + Cunning 4 = 6 dice → 9, 8, 7, 4, 3, 1 = 3 hits.
Annika: Athletics 1 + Cunning 2 = 3 dice → 8, 4, 2 = 1 hit.
Rosamund: Athletics 1 + Cunning 3 = 4 dice → 9, 9, 4, 2 = 2 hits.
Voss: Athletics 2 + Cunning 4 = 6 dice → 10, 9, 8, 7, 4, 3 = 4 hits. Goes first.

─── ROUND 1 ───

VOSS (Spotlight): Formally recites the charges against Elara, invoking Conclave procedure. She is not attacking — she is establishing the narrative frame. This is a social attack: Presence + Persuasion roll to establish authority. Results: 9, 9, 8, 7, 4, 2 = 3 hits. Difficulty 2. 1 Trick Hit. Spend Trick Hit → Establish Frame: the Conclave observers (three Tradition members present) are now predisposed to believe her account. -1 die for anyone who tries to contradict her without evidence.

VOSS (ongoing): She also activates a Forces 4 / Mind 2 Effect — an emotional resonance field that makes her words feel more authoritative, more truthful, more compelling. Vulgar in a Conclave space? No — Mind 2 emotional influence is Coincidental if subtle. Forces 4 adds a nearly subsonic frequency to her voice that the humans present cannot identify but respond to physiologically. This is within paradigm for a Hermetic working.

VIKRAM (Spotlight): Goes second. Correspondence 3 — spatial awareness in this room. He already knows where everything is. Minor Action: sends a tiny data-package via his phone to a contact who has evidence of Voss's actual involvement. The evidence will arrive in 8 minutes if the contact responds. Major Action: a very subtle Correspondence 1 / Mind 1 combined effect — he marks Voss's position precisely and slips a query into the room's electronic surveillance system. He now knows exactly where she is even if she becomes invisible, and can read the room's emotional state through the cameras. Coincidental (using his phone, natural background tech). Zero Paradox.

ROSAMUND (Spotlight): She speaks — directly and plainly, citing specifics. Presence + Persuasion = 3 + 3 = 6 dice, -1 (Voss's Establish Frame) = 5 dice. Results: 9, 8, 7, 4, 2 = 3 hits. Difficulty 2. 1 Trick Hit.
Spend Trick Hit → Challenge: she formally challenges Voss's account, citing a specific date and location. This forces Voss to respond in detail — her next Social action must address this specific point or lose credibility with the Conclave observers.

ANNIKA (Spotlight): Mind 2 — reads the emotional truth of Voss's statements. Not reading minds — reading the emotional resonance of what is said versus what is felt. Coincidental (she is a priestess in sacred space; emotional discernment is her vocation). Results: 9, 8, 7, 4, 3 = 3 hits, well above Difficulty 1. "She's afraid," Annika says quietly to Rosamund. "She's afraid of what he found." A nod toward Vikram and his phone.

─── ROUND 2 — VOSS's GUARDIAN MAKES A MOVE ───

Guardian 1 steps physically between Rosamund and Elara — not attacking, but positioning. This is a physical threat assertion. The Guardian has no social authority but has a sword and is between Rosamund and the Elder she is protecting. This is a classic intimidation play: we are not attacking, but we will.

Vikram's player invokes Advantage here: "We established advantage through Vikram's spatial awareness and Rosamund's Challenge. The Guardian's move was expected and we're positioned to counter it." The SG agrees — the player characters have Advantage on the next response.

ROSAMUND: Stands her ground. She does not draw her own weapon. She says, in a voice audible to the Conclave observers: "Guardian. You are standing between an Awakened Elder and those who would defend her, in a Conclave space. Under what authority?" 

Presence + Intimidation = 3 + 3 = 6 dice + 2 (Advantage) = 8 dice. Results: 10, 9, 8, 8, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 6 hits (10=2). Against the Guardian's Composure + Resolve: 2 + 2 = 4 dice → 8, 4, 3, 2 = 1 hit. The Guardian does not retreat, but he takes an involuntary step sideways and glances at Voss for instruction. That glance is noted by every Conclave observer in the room.

─── ROUND 3 — THE EVIDENCE ARRIVES ───

Vikram's contact delivers. His phone vibrates. The file contains mission logs showing Voss's personal communication codes authorizing a specific reroute that left the three mages without support. He transmits this to the Conclave observers' own devices — using Correspondence 2 to send the data packet through the building's wireless infrastructure without anyone physically passing anything. Coincidental. Clean. Zero Paradox.

Voss sees what is happening. Her Guardians begin to move — not to attack the player characters, but to create an exit for Voss.

SG: "The Guardians are going to physically clear a path to the door. This is now a combat situation — they draw weapons. But the moment the first sword clears a scabbard in this Conclave space, the observers react. What do you do?"

Annika: "I speak first. Before the sword is fully drawn." She invokes [Fast] — metaphorically, not a weapon Tag, but the principle applies. The SG nods. "You can speak first before the sword fully clears." Annika addresses the observers: "Witness this. Weapons drawn on Conclave ground. Witness who drew first."

Mind 2 — not an attack, but a clarity effect on the observers. All three observe the scene with perfect emotional clarity for the next minute, unable to misremember what they witness. Coincidental (she is calling for witness, the observers are already paying attention). Results: 9, 9, 8, 4, 2 = 3 hits. Effect secured.

The Guardians freeze. Voss freezes. Drawing steel in a formally witnessed Conclave space, with three Tradition observers in Mind-enhanced witness state, is political suicide.

Voss and her Guardians leave without violence. She is formally called before a Conclave review within the week. Her Tradition standing is suspended pending investigation. Elara is cleared.

COMBAT RESULT: No health damage to anyone. Zero Paradox (all workings Coincidental). Political victory through magical social combat — exactly as consequential as a physical brawl, with entirely different stakes and entirely different tools.

KEY MECHANICS DEMONSTRATED: Initiative used for social positioning, not just violence. Trick Hits in social combat (Establish Frame, Challenge). Advantage from information superiority. The Compel principle applied to political consequences (Guardians drawing = political cost). Mind magic in non-violent application. [Fast] principle applied to narrative speed. The game rewarding creative non-combat solutions.

### Example 6: Tag Invocation and Compel Mechanics in Action — The Safehouse Raid

> SCENE: Rosamund and Silas are raiding an NWO safehouse to recover a kidnapped Tradition initiate. The safehouse has three rooms: entry hall, main room, back room. Four NWO field agents are present, armed with handguns ([Loud], [Reload 2]) and tactical gear (Armor 2). Rosamund is carrying: combat knife ([Fast], [Concealed]) and a short-range communication disruptor Wonder ([Silent] field, [Charged 3], [Sustained]). Silas carries: elephant gun ([Brutal], [Devastating], [Loud], [Reload 2]) and a prepared Matter 2 Wonder — a small device that instantly rusts metal objects in contact range ([Fragile] — only 1 use).

Both players begin with 3 Momentum each. The SG has 2 Momentum. This example focuses entirely on Tag invocations and compels.

─── ENTRY PHASE ───

Silas prepares to breach the entry hall door. He has the elephant gun but considers the Tags. His player looks at [Brutal], [Devastating], [Loud], [Reload 2].

"I'm not using the gun to breach. The [Loud] Tag — if I shoot through this door, every agent in the building knows exactly where I am and has time to prepare. I'll use the Matter Wonder on the lock instead."

SG: "Good call. The Matter Wonder on the lock — that's one use of [Fragile]. The lock dissolves. The door swings open. You have 2 seconds of surprise." This is not a compel — the player avoided the compel by recognizing the Tag and making a smart choice. That IS how Tag awareness pays off.

INITIATIVE:
Rosamund: 3 hits. Silas: 2 hits. Agent A (hall): 1 hit. Agent B (main room): 2 hits. Agent C (main room): 1 hit. Agent D (back room): 0 hits.

Order: Rosamund (3), then Silas and Agent B (2, player goes first), then Agent A and Agent C (1, SG decides Agent A goes first).

─── ROUND 1 ───

ROSAMUND (Spotlight): "I activate the communication disruptor. [Silent] field goes up — no radio calls going in or out of this building." Minor Action: activates Wonder ([Charged 3] — 1 charge used, 2 remaining). Major Action: moves silently into hall.

SG: "The [Silent] field is up. Agents cannot call for backup. But—" The SG considers a compel. "[Charged 3] on your disruptor. If I compel [Charged 3] right now — another device in this building, maybe a Technocratic monitor system, drains one of your remaining charges trying to counter-fight the field — you'd drop to 1 charge. You gain 1 Momentum. Want it?"

Rosamund's player: "Yes. I take it." Gains 1 Momentum (now 4). The Wonder is down to 1 charge. If it gets compelled again, the field drops.

SILAS (Spotlight): "I hold the door and let Rosamund take point. Held action — I fire when I see an agent raise a weapon."

AGENT B (Spotlight): Through a doorway, sees Rosamund entering the hall. Draws and fires. Ranged 3 + Dexterity 3 = 6 dice. Handgun: Difficulty 1. Results: 9, 8, 4, 3, 2, 1 = 2 hits. 4 Lethal - no armor on Rosamund = 4 Lethal. But wait:
