Rosamund's player: "I invoke [Fast] on my knife. I'm not using it to attack — I'm using it to interrupt his draw. I read the shoulder movement and was already moving when his hand went to his weapon."

SG: "That's a solid invoke. [Fast] in an initiative-interruption context — you get to move 1 pace and take your Active Dodge before his shot resolves. Spend 1 Momentum."

Rosamund spends 1 Momentum (now 3). She takes Active Dodge as a free response: Athletics 1 + Dexterity 3 = 4 dice → 9, 7, 4, 2 = 2 hits. Attack Difficulty jumps to 1 + 2 = 3. Agent B has 2 hits. Needs 3. Miss. The [Fast] invoke turned a hit into a miss. Worth every Momentum.

SILAS's held action FIRES: Agent B raised his weapon — the trigger. Silas fires the elephant gun from the doorway.
[Brutal] invoked: "This shot needs to stop him completely. He cannot return fire." +2 dice to attack.
Ranged 2 + Dexterity 3 = 5 dice + 2 [Brutal] invoke = 7 dice. Difficulty 1. Results: 10, 9, 8, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 5 hits (10=2). 4 Trick Hits.
5 Lethal base. Armor 2 = 3 Lethal through. Spend 4 Trick Hits → Extra Damage x4: 7 Lethal. Armor 2 = 5 Lethal. Agent B: Health 5 → Incapacitated.

BUT. The SG immediately offers a compel: "[Loud]. That gunshot in an enclosed hallway. The other three agents know exactly where you are, exactly what weapon you have, and exactly where the threat is. Also — your [Reload 2] is now relevant. You fired. Two Minor Actions to reload. Gain 2 Momentum (one for each Tag compelled)."

Silas's player: "Accepted." Gains 2 Momentum (now 4). Silas is now holding an empty elephant gun in a hallway with two more agents incoming.

AGENT A (Spotlight): Charges Silas from the side — he knows the gun is empty. Close Combat 3 + Might 3 = 6 dice. Difficulty 1. Results: 8, 8, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 3 hits. 2 Trick Hits. Unarmed strike: 2 Bashing. Spend 2 Tricks → Disarm: Silas drops the empty gun, AND Knockdown: Silas is Prone.

Silas: Prone, unarmed (gun on the floor), 2 Bashing. This is the correct consequence of firing [Loud] + [Reload 2] in a building with three more enemies.

─── ROUND 2 ───

ROSAMUND (Spotlight): "I invoke [Concealed] on my knife. Agent C in the main room — he hasn't seen me yet. He's focused on Silas." +2 dice on an attack that relies on the element of surprise.
Close Combat 2 + Dexterity 3 = 5 dice + 2 [Concealed] invoke = 7 dice. Difficulty 1. Results: 10, 9, 8, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 5 hits (10=2). 4 Trick Hits.
2 Lethal. Spend 1 → Extra Damage: 3 Lethal. Spend 1 → Stun: Agent C loses next Spotlight. Spend 1 → Knockdown: Prone. Spend 1 → Wound (knife to weapon hand): Agent C drops his pistol. Agent C: Health 5: 3 Lethal marked. Prone. Stunned. Disarmed. He is comprehensively neutralized.

SG: "The [Concealed] invoke worked perfectly because he wasn't watching the right person. But here's a compel — [Concealed] means you haven't re-concealed the knife since using it last round. Agent D in the back room just saw you through the doorway and knows you have a blade. He's now expecting a knife fighter, not an unarmed combatant. Gain 1 Momentum."

Rosamund's player: "Accepted." Gains 1 Momentum (now 4). Small price. Agent D is warned.

SILAS (Spotlight): Prone, empty gun on the floor. Spends 1 Momentum: "I invoke [Brutal] — not on the gun, on the floor. I grab the gun by the barrel and use it as a club. [Brutal] applies to improvised-weapon use when the goal is to stop someone decisively." SG: "Creative. I'll allow [Brutal] invoke for the improvised weapon — it's the same principle. But note that using the gun as a club means it's probably damaged when you go to reload it." Spend 1 Momentum (now 3). Stand up (Minor Action). Club attack: Athletics 2 + Might 3 = 5 dice + 2 [Brutal] = 7 dice. Agent A: 4 Bashing. Armor 2: 2 Bashing. Agent A Health: 5 → 3. Impaired.

─── ROUND 3 — FINALE ───

Agent D emerges from the back room — the initiate is behind him, hands bound. He is using the initiate as cover. He has a handgun pointed at her head. Standoff.

SG: "He knows about the knife (from the [Concealed] compel). He knows the gun is damaged (from the [Brutal] improvisation). He does not know about the [Silent] disruptor. What does he know about your capabilities?"

Rosamund's player: "He knows I have a knife. He doesn't know I have the disruptor. And the disruptor has 1 charge left — enough for... does the [Silent] field have a short-range stun application if I discharge it directly into his nervous system rather than broadcasting it?"

SG: "That's a creative use. The Wonder is a communication disruptor — the mechanism is electromagnetic interference. Apply it directly to a person's nervous system and you've got a rough-and-ready stun effect. But it kills the last charge. And I'll note: the [Fragile] Tag on the Matter Wonder is gone (already used). The [Charged 3] is at 1 charge — compellable at any moment. Want to risk it?"

Rosamund's player accepts the risk. She approaches, knife visible (keeping his attention on the wrong threat), activates the disruptor at contact range. [Charged 3] last charge depleted — the Wonder goes dark. But the result: Agent D seizes from the electromagnetic pulse. Shocked condition. Weapon discharged into the ceiling. Initiate is unharmed.

Silas recovers the initiate while Rosamund secures Agent D. They leave before anyone else arrives.

FINAL MOMENTUM: Rosamund 4, Silas 3. SG: 2 (unused). The group walks out ahead on resources.

KEY MECHANICS DEMONSTRATED: Every Tag in this example was invoked or compelled at least once. The [Loud]+[Reload 2] double compel created the most dramatic moment of the scene. [Fast] as a defensive invoke (not just offensive). [Concealed] both invoked and immediately compelled — the same Tag creating benefit and complication within two Spotlights. Creative wonder repurposing within mechanical limits. Momentum economy throughout. The safehouse was cleared with zero player character deaths, zero Paradox, and a clear Momentum advantage for the players.

### Example 7: Phase Initiative in Full — The Rail Yard Ambush

> SYSTEM: Phase Initiative (Appendix H). This example assumes the group has switched to the tactical variant for this scene.

PHASE INITIATIVE REMINDER: At start of each Round, all participants DECLARE their actions simultaneously — before anyone acts. Actions then resolve in Speed Factor (SF) order: SF 1 (fast) → SF 2 (moderate) → SF 3 (slow) → SF 4+ (very slow). Rote SF = highest Sphere dot − 1 (min 1). Freeform SF = sum of all Sphere dots. Being hit during a slow action triggers a Concentration check (or Sphere Defense) for spellcasters.

SCENE: Kenji (Euthanatos, Entropy 3, Life 2, Health 6) and Priya (Virtual Adept, Correspondence 2, Forces 3, Health 5) are ambushed in a Seattle rail yard by three Black Dog Security mercs. No civilian witnesses — this is a corporate kill zone cleared before the ambush. The mercs: Merc A (assault rifle [Autofire] [Loud] [Reload 3], Ranged 3, Dexterity 3, Health 5, Armor 2), Merc B (same loadout), Merc C (close combat specialist: Close Combat 4, Might 4, Dexterity 3, Health 6, Armor 2, tactical knife [Fast][Brutal]).

─── ROUND 1 — DECLARE ───

All parties declare simultaneously before any dice are rolled:

KENJI declares: "Entropy 3 — Probability Cascade on all three mercs. I'm targeting their weapon systems and coordination simultaneously. The effect will degrade all their attack pools by 2 dice for the rest of the scene." Entropy 3 improvised effect, no Rote. Speed Factor: 3 (slow — Freeform SF = sum of dots = 3). VULGAR (clearly impossible degradation of physical systems in view of... wait, are there witnesses? No. Vulgar with no witnesses: +3 Paradox dice to his pool). This is a big play and Kenji knows it.

PRIYA declares: "Forces 1 cantrip — electromagnetic flicker around Merc A's eyes. Not blindness, just a momentary disruption of visual acuity." Forces 1, Coincidental (EM interference is everywhere in an industrial rail yard). Speed Factor: 1 (fast — Freeform Forces 1, sum=1, SF 1). Zero Paradox.

MERC A declares: burst fire at Kenji. SF 2 (burst fire = moderate).
MERC B declares: burst fire at Priya. SF 2.
MERC C declares: charge into melee with Kenji. SF 1 (movement + unarmed/blade strike = fast).

─── ROUND 1, SF 1 — FAST ACTIONS RESOLVE ───

All SF 1 actions happen simultaneously, in Initiative order within the phase (Priya rolled highest Initiative: 3 hits; Merc C: 2 hits; Kenji: 1 hit).

PRIYA (SF 1 — cantrip): Forces 1 + Cunning 4 = 5 dice. Difficulty 1. Results: 9, 8, 7, 4, 2 = 3 hits. 2 Trick Hits.
Merc A: EM flicker around his visor optics. -2 dice on all ranged attacks this Round. Spend 1 Trick Hit → Duration extended: -1 die persists next Round as well. Spend 1 Trick Hit → the flicker also catches Merc B (collateral effect of the EM field). Both mercs at -2 dice this Round.
Paradox: 0. Clean. Done.

MERC C (SF 1 — charge and strike): Charges the 6 meters to Kenji. He arrives BEFORE Kenji's Entropy 3 working resolves. This is the core Phase Initiative tactical reality: Kenji's big spell is SF 3 — it has not happened yet. Merc C is already in his face.
Tactical knife [Fast][Brutal]: Close Combat 4 + Dexterity 3 = 7 dice. Invokes [Fast]: "I'm faster than his reaction. He was focused on his spell." +2 dice. Total: 9 dice. Difficulty 1.
Results: 10, 9, 9, 8, 7, 6, 4, 3, 2 = 7 hits (10=2). 6 Trick Hits.
Damage: 2 Lethal base. [Brutal] invoked (second invoke): +2 dice already spent, but SG rules [Brutal] applies as a damage-amplifier: treat the weapon as dealing an additional 2 Lethal. So 4 Lethal base.
Spend 3 Trick Hits → Extra Damage x3: 7 Lethal. Armor (none): 7 Lethal. Kenji: Health 6 → Impaired (5 boxes filled).
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Stagger: Kenji loses 2 dice from Concentration check.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Knockdown: Kenji is Prone.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Wound (to casting hand): Kenji's next physical casting action is at +1 Difficulty.

KENJI — CONCENTRATION CHECK: He was hit mid-declaration of a slow (SF 3) spell. He must pass a Concentration check or the working collapses.
Composure 2 + Resolve 3 = 5 dice, -2 (Stagger) = 3 dice. Difficulty equal to Merc C's hits (7). He needs 7 hits on 3 dice. Maximum possible on 3 dice = 6 (three 10s). This check is mathematically impossible.
KENJI'S PLAYER: "The working collapses. I'm not going to pretend otherwise."
The Entropy 3 cascade falls apart as Kenji hits the gravel, bleeding from the knife wound. His Paradox pool absorbed the dice without releasing — the working was destroyed before it manifested. Paradox pool: +3 (for the attempt) but the SG rules that an unmaterialized working that collapses due to interruption only adds 1 die rather than the full 3. Kenji's pool: 1.

KENJI'S SF 3 SLOT: Empty. The working collapsed at SF 1 due to physical interruption before the SF 3 phase.

─── ROUND 1, SF 2 — MODERATE ACTIONS RESOLVE ───

MERC A (SF 2 — burst fire at Kenji): Kenji is Prone. Ranged 3 + Dexterity 3 = 6 dice, -2 (EM flicker) = 4 dice. Burst: +2 dice = 6 dice. Difficulty 1 - 1 (Prone target) = Difficulty 1 (minimum remains 1).
Results: 9, 8, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 3 hits. 2 Trick Hits. 3 Lethal burst. Spend 2 Tricks → Extra Damage x2: 5 Lethal. Kenji has no armor.
Kenji already at 5 boxes (Impaired). New hit: 5 Lethal pushes him to Critical, then Incapacitated. Beyond Incapacitated — Kenji is dying.

MERC B (SF 2 — burst fire at Priya): Ranged 3 + Dexterity 3 = 6 dice, -2 (EM flicker) = 4 dice. Burst +2 = 6 dice. Difficulty 1.
Results: 9, 7, 4, 3, 2, 1 = 1 hit. Exactly Difficulty 1. 3 Lethal. No Tricks. Priya Health 5 → 2 boxes filled. No penalty. She is still fully functional.

─── ROUND 1, SF 3 — SLOW ACTIONS ───

Nothing resolves here. Kenji's slot is empty. No other SF 3 actions were declared.

─── BETWEEN ROUNDS ───

Kenji is Incapacitated and dying. Priya has 2 Lethal marked. Merc C is in close combat range with an unconscious mage. Two mercs with assault rifles have clear sightlines.

PRIYA'S PLAYER: "I need a fast exit for both of us. I have Correspondence 2. Can I teleport Kenji to the car — right now, on my next declare?"

SG: "Correspondence 2 + Cunning. Short-range known location. You've parked at that rail yard twice before — you know the spot. Improvised Vulgar with no witnesses: +2 Paradox. SF 2 (Correspondence 2). But you're also being shot at. If a burst lands before SF 2 resolves, Concentration check."

PRIYA: "I declare Correspondence 2 teleport on Kenji (SF 2) and a Forces 1 scatter-pulse to disrupt their aim (SF 1). Both in the same Round."

SG: "That is two magical workings with both constructs live in your mind simultaneously — Divided Attention. Phase Initiative still sequences them: the pulse fires at SF 1, the teleport at SF 2. But from the moment you declare, your focus is split between both. Both casting pools take −2 dice. Each generates its own Paradox independently. Still want it?"

PRIYA: "Yes. I need the pulse to make the teleport viable. I will pay the cost."

─── ROUND 2 — DECLARE ───

Priya: Forces 1 scatter-pulse (SF 1) + Correspondence 2 teleport (SF 2) — Divided Attention, both pools at −2.
Merc A: burst fire at Priya (SF 2).
Merc B: burst fire at Priya (SF 2).
Merc C: searches Kenji for documents/data (SF 1 — searching an unconscious target).

─── ROUND 2, SF 1 ───

PRIYA (scatter-pulse — DIVIDED ATTENTION, −2 dice): Forces 1 + Cunning 4 = 5 dice − 2 = 3 dice. Coincidental. Difficulty 1. Results: 9, 7, 4 = 2 hits. 1 Trick Hit. Electromagnetic scatter: +1 Difficulty on all ranged attacks against her this Round. 1 Trick Hit → scatter radius extended, pushing Merc C back 2 meters from Kenji's position and causing his search action to fail (interrupted). Merc C does not get the data. (At full 5-dice pool she would have had 2 Trick Hits; the Divided Attention cost meant she could not also extend the effect into Round 3.)

MERC C (search — SF 1): Interrupted by the scatter-pulse. His action collapses.

─── ROUND 2, SF 2 ───

All three SF 2 actions resolve simultaneously: Priya's teleport, Merc A's burst, Merc B's burst.

SG: "Simultaneous SF 2 actions resolve in Initiative order: Priya first (she rolled highest Initiative overall), then Merc A (2 hits), then Merc B (1 hit). If Priya's teleport goes through before they fire, Kenji is gone. If the mercs hit before the teleport resolves... Concentration check."

SG RESOLUTION: Priya goes first (Initiative superiority within the phase).

PRIYA'S TELEPORT (DIVIDED ATTENTION, −2 dice): Correspondence 2 + Cunning 4 = 6 dice − 2 = 4 dice. Difficulty 2 (Vulgar). Results: 9, 8, 7, 3 = 3 hits. 1 Trick Hit. Kenji vanishes — reappears in the back seat of their car, two blocks from the rail yard. Spend 1 Trick Hit → gentle landing (he does not take additional damage from teleportation). The car door remains closed — she did not have the extra hit to finesse the arrival details. Paradox: +2 dice. Priya's pool: 2. (At full 6-dice pool she would have had 4 hits and 2 Trick Hits. Divided Attention meant the teleport cleared Difficulty 2 by exactly 1 hit — a lower Cunning would have made it too risky to attempt simultaneously.)

KENJI IS GONE. Merc A and Merc B's burst fire at a target who is no longer there. Rounds pass through empty air.

Priya runs.

─── RESOLUTION ───

The mercs have the location (they know where she parked before — her Correspondence 2 working connected to a known location). But she has a head start and a Correspondence mage's understanding of spatial relationships. She is gone before they reorganize.

Kenji is alive. Barely. Life 2 healing will take a scene. Priya's Paradox pool: 2 dice. She rolls them in the car:
Results: 4, 8. 4 = nothing. 8 = Spirit Touch (a Paradox Spirit is now aware of this location). They do not stay in the car.

KEY MECHANICS DEMONSTRATED — PHASE INITIATIVE:
1. DECLARATION RISK: Kenji's big SF 3 spell looked dominant on paper. It never fired. Declaration without knowledge of opponent's actions creates genuine tactical uncertainty.
2. SF TIMING DETERMINES SURVIVAL: Merc C's SF 1 charge reached Kenji before his SF 3 spell could resolve. This is not bad luck — it is the Phase Initiative system working correctly. Fast actions beat slow ones.
3. CONCENTRATION CHECKS UNDER FIRE: Kenji's 7-hit Staggered concentration check was impossible. The player accepted this cleanly. The system does not protect slow spells from fast opposition.
4. SIMULTANEOUS SF RESOLUTION: Within SF 2, Initiative order still determines sequencing. Priya's teleport resolved before the mercs fired. Initiative mattered even inside a Phase.
5. DIVIDED ATTENTION ACROSS SF SLOTS: Priya declared two workings for the same Round — a Divided Attention situation. Both magical constructs were live in her mind from the moment of declaration. Both casting pools took −2 dice (5→3 for the pulse; 6→4 for the teleport). Each generated Paradox independently. The reduced pools tightened both rolls — the teleport cleared Difficulty 2 by exactly 1 hit. A lower Cunning would have made the simultaneous attempt unviable.
6. PHASE INITIATIVE PAYOFF: The system rewarded Priya for thinking in phases. She used a fast action to suppress, then used her slow action to extract. The Spotlight system would have required both to be in the same "turn" with less tactical texture.

### Example 8: Both Systems, One Fight — The Collapsed Warehouse

> This example shows the same combat scene run twice: first under Spotlight, then under Phase Initiative. Same characters, same mercs, same environment. The comparison reveals when each system creates the most interesting play.

SCENE: Lena (Verbena, Life 3, Forces 1, Health 6) and Dario (Hermetic, Prime 3, Forces 2, Health 5) confront two NWO Amalgam agents in a condemned warehouse. The agents: Agent Stone (Ranged Combat 3, Dexterity 4, Health 6, Armor 2, pistol [Armor-Piercing]) and Agent Rivers (Close Combat 4, Might 4, Dexterity 3, Health 7, Armor 3, shock baton [Stun][Charged 4][Non-Lethal]).

No civilian witnesses. Vulgar magic: +Sphere dot Paradox.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
VERSION A: SPOTLIGHT SYSTEM
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Initiative: Lena (3), Agent Stone (2), Dario (1), Agent Rivers (0).

ROUND 1 — LENA (Spotlight):
Lena casts Life 3 — augments her own body. Speed, reflexes, strength all elevated. Coincidental if subtle (she simply moves with uncanny grace). Vulgar if dramatic (she visibly accelerates). She keeps it subtle: Coincidental. Zero Paradox.
Life 3 + Cunning 3 = 6 dice. Difficulty 1. Results: 9, 9, 8, 7, 4, 2 = 4 hits. 3 Trick Hits.
Effect: +2 dice to all Athletics and Close Combat pools this scene. Spend 1 Trick Hit → Duration extends to one scene. Spend 1 Trick Hit → +1 die to damage. Spend 1 Trick Hit → she now reads the heartbeats of everyone in the room (Life 3 awareness).
Lena is now augmented. She feels extraordinary.

ROUND 1 — AGENT STONE (Spotlight):
Fires pistol [Armor-Piercing] at Lena. Ranged 3 + Dexterity 4 = 7 dice. Difficulty 1.
Results: 10, 9, 8, 7, 4, 3, 2 = 5 hits (10=2). 4 Trick Hits.
Lena: no armor. 4 Lethal base. Spend 4 Tricks → Extra Damage x4: 8 Lethal. Lena: Health 6 → Incapacitated in one shot.

Storyguide pauses. "That is mechanically valid but dramatically terrible. This is why Lena spent her Spotlight augmenting instead of seeking cover. In the Spotlight system, Agent Stone gets a full unimpeded shot. Let's see how Phase Initiative handles the same situation."

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
VERSION B: PHASE INITIATIVE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Declaration:
Lena: Life 3 augmentation. SF 3 (Freeform: sum of 3 dots = SF 3).
Dario: Prime 3 — create a Quintessence barrier. SF 3 (Freeform: sum of 3 dots = SF 3).
Agent Stone: single shot at Lena (he declares before seeing what she's doing). SF 1 (pistol single shot = fast).
Agent Rivers: close combat charge at Dario (SF 1 — movement and strike).

─── SF 1 — FAST ───

AGENT STONE fires at Lena. Her Life 3 augmentation is Freeform SF 3 (sum of 3 dots) — it has NOT resolved yet. She has no bonuses. Ranged 3 + Dexterity 4 = 7 dice. Results: 9, 8, 7, 4, 3, 2, 1 = 3 hits. 2 Trick Hits. 4 Lethal. Spend 2 Tricks → Extra Damage x2: 6 Lethal. Lena: Health 6 → Critical (5 boxes marked).

LENA — CONCENTRATION CHECK (she was hit during SF 3 spell): Composure 2 + Resolve 3 = 5 dice. -2 (Critical penalty). = 3 dice. Difficulty = Stone's hits: 3. Results: 8, 8, 7 = 3 hits. Exactly matches Difficulty. BARELY PASSES.
Lena is bleeding and barely standing, but the Life 3 augmentation did not collapse. It is still winding up.

AGENT RIVERS charges at Dario. Close Combat 4 + Dexterity 3 = 7 dice. [Stun] invoked on shock baton: +2 dice. 9 dice. Difficulty 1. Results: 10, 9, 8, 7, 6, 4, 3, 2, 1 = 6 hits (10=2). 5 Trick Hits.
Baton: Non-Lethal, Stun. Dario: takes 3 Bashing. Spend 2 Tricks → Extra Damage: 5 Bashing. Dario: Health 5 → 5 boxes. Critical. Spend 2 Tricks → Shocked (loses all actions this Round — his SF 3 slot goes dark) and Staggered (-2 dice next Round).

DARIO — CONCENTRATION CHECK: He was hit during SF 3 (Prime barrier). Composure 2 + Resolve 2 = 4 dice, -2 (Critical) = 2 dice. Difficulty = Rivers's hits: 6. Maximum on 2 dice = 4 (two 10s). Impossible. The Prime barrier collapses.

─── SF 3 — SLOW ───

Lena's Life 3 augmentation RESOLVES. Despite being at Critical health, barely conscious, bleeding from a gunshot wound — the biological transformation is complete. It is a testament to her focus that the working survived the Concentration check.
Life 3 + Cunning 3 = 6 dice, -2 (Critical) = 4 dice. Difficulty 1. Results: 9, 8, 7, 6 = 4 hits. 3 Trick Hits.
Effect: +2 Athletics, +2 Close Combat, +1 die damage (her body is now operating at non-human efficiency). Her Critical health is now a crisis she is overcoming rather than a crisis that has ended her.
Vulgar? She chose subtle — Coincidental. Zero Paradox.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → she also accelerates her own healing slightly: 1 Bashing healed at end of Round. Spend 1 Trick Hit → heightened pain resistance: Critical penalty reduced to -1 die (not -2) for the rest of the scene via biological override. Spend 1 Trick Hit → her augmented heartbeat awareness now gives her tactical read: she knows exactly how many heart-beats each opponent has left before exhaustion forces a retreat.

Dario's SF 3 slot: empty (Shocked by Rivers).

─── END OF ROUND 1, PHASE INITIATIVE ───

STATE:
Lena: Critical, but augmented. -1 die (not -2) from Critical thanks to Life 3 override. +2 to all relevant pools. Net: better dice than before she was shot.
Dario: Critical, Staggered, no active workings. He needs a Round to recover.
Agent Stone: has fired once. Pistol has [Reload 2] — he spent his standard shot. No bonus action in Phase Init; he will reload as SF 1 next Round.
Agent Rivers: fresh. Dario is down for practical purposes.

─── ROUND 2 — DECLARE ───

Lena: Life 3 — she pushes the augmentation to regenerate: 1 Lethal removed per Phase this Round. Coincidental (internal biological acceleration). SF 2 (she's sustaining an existing effect — SG rules SF 2 for augmentation maintenance (reduced from new-cast Freeform SF 3)).
Lena also declares a close combat charge at Agent Rivers (he is between her and Dario). SF 1 (movement + attack).

SG: "Two actions again — SF 1 and SF 2. Legal. You're a Life 3 mage running on overdrive."

Dario declares: Prime 2 — a basic Quintessence strike at Agent Rivers. No longer attempting the barrier. Fast and cheap. Freeform SF 2 (sum of 2 dots = 2). Still in phase. Staggered (-2 dice) but functioning.

Agent Stone: reloads (SF 1 — two Minor Actions combined as one reload action, SG rules this as an SF 1 action for a prepared professional).
Agent Rivers: attacks Lena with baton as she charges (SF 1 — reacting to her declared charge, SG rules he can counter-declare within the phase if Initiative is higher — Rivers has 2 hits vs. Lena's 3. She goes first within SF 1).

─── ROUND 2, SF 1 ───

LENA CHARGES RIVERS: Athletics 2 + Close Combat 3 = 5 dice, +2 (augmentation) = 7 dice. Difficulty 1. Results: 10, 9, 9, 8, 7, 4, 3 = 6 hits (10=2). 5 Trick Hits.
2 Lethal base + 1 (Trick Hit damage bonus from augmentation) = 3 Lethal. Spend 3 Tricks → Extra Damage x3: 6 Lethal. Rivers: Armor 3 = 3 Lethal through. Health 7 → 4. Impaired.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Knockdown: Rivers is Prone.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Disarm: baton skids across the warehouse floor.

RIVERS (counter): He was going to attack — he's now Knockdown and Prone. His declared SF 1 action collapses. He cannot both be knocked down and complete his baton strike in the same Phase.

AGENT STONE reloads (SF 1). Clean action. Pistol is ready for Round 3.

─── ROUND 2, SF 2 ───

LENA (SF 2 — augmentation maintenance): Life 3 accelerated healing. 1 Lethal removed. Health improves from Critical: 4 boxes filled. She is now Impaired, not Critical. The -1 die penalty replaces -2. She is climbing back.

DARIO (SF 2 — Prime 2 strike): Targets Rivers on the floor. Prime 2 + Intellect 3 = 5 dice, -2 (Stagger) = 3 dice. Difficulty 1. Results: 9, 8, 4 = 2 hits. 1 Trick Hit. 3 Aggravated (Prime energy). Rivers: Armor 3 does NOT reduce Aggravated (standard armor). 3 Aggravated. Health 7 → 1 box remaining. Rivers is Incapacitated.
Spend 1 Trick Hit → Dario's Stagger condition ends early (he channels the Quintessence discharge through his own pattern to purge the bioelectric disruption from the shock baton).

─── ROUND 2 RESOLUTION ───

Agent Rivers: Incapacitated.
Agent Stone: reloaded, alone, facing an augmented Life 3 Verbena who just tanked a gunshot and a freshly functional Hermetic with Prime 2 and nothing to lose.

Agent Stone makes a professional calculation. He retreats through the emergency exit, leaving Rivers behind.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
COMPARISON: WHAT EACH SYSTEM DID
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SPOTLIGHT (Version A):
- Lena used her whole Spotlight to augment. Stone used his whole Spotlight to shoot her. She died.
- Clear, fast, decisive. No ambiguity about who acted when. Took 90 seconds of actual table time.
- Dramatic problem: the Spotlight system rewarded Stone for having more Initiative, full stop. Lena's strategic choice (augment first) was punished purely because she went before Stone in Initiative order.

PHASE INITIATIVE (Version B):
- Lena's SF 3 augmentation was slower than Stone's SF 1 shot. She got hit before her magic resolved. She barely passed the Concentration check and kept the working alive.
- The fight lasted 2 Rounds of meaningful tactical decision-making. Both players engaged with the declaration structure.
- Phase Initiative rewarded Lena for passing a hard Concentration check — a dramatic moment that the Spotlight system cannot produce. It also punished Dario for choosing the same slow option without a contingency.
- Took 8 minutes of actual table time.

WHEN TO USE EACH:

Spotlight System — Reach for it when:
  The combat is 2-4 participants on each side.
  The story needs to move. You have a session to finish.
  New players are at the table.
  The emotional weight is the story happening around the fight, not inside the mechanics of the fight.
  You want to run a fight in 15 minutes.

Phase Initiative — Reach for it when:
  You have 6+ participants and timing between fast/slow actions matters.
  A mage is attempting a complex, slow working in a dangerous environment. You want the risk of interruption to feel real and mechanical.
  The group enjoys tactical optimization. Players are making pre-battle plans that depend on action sequencing.
  You have a fight that IS the dramatic centerpiece of the session. Everyone is focused on this fight.
  You have 30-45 minutes for one encounter.

The hybrid approach — used at many tables: Run most fights under Spotlight. When the narrative weight of a combat warrants it, announce at the start of initiative: "We're doing Phase Initiative for this one." Players who know the system will understand why. Players who are learning will see the difference in real time.

KEY MECHANICS DEMONSTRATED — BOTH SYSTEMS:
The Concentration check is the defining Phase Initiative mechanic for mages. Slow spells are powerful and vulnerable. Fast spells are safe and cheap. The tactical decision at declaration — "how much do I risk for how much payoff?" — is the heart of Phase Initiative magic.
In Spotlight, this decision does not exist in the same form. A mage who goes first casts freely. A mage who goes later risks nothing different for big spells vs. small ones. Phase Initiative makes spell speed a meaningful tactical choice, not just a flavor note.

---

## Quick Reference: Combat Summary

For fast access during play, this summary distills the chapter's core mechanics into reference form.

### Round Structure

- 1. At start of scene: everyone rolls Athletics + Cunning for Initiative.
- 2. Highest Initiative acts first (Spotlight), then descending.
- 3. Each Spotlight: one Major Action + Minor Actions as time permits.
- 4. When all Spotlights complete: new Round begins with same Initiative order.
- 5. Combat ends when one side is incapacitated, surrenders, flees, or narrative circumstances conclude it.

### Attack Summary

- Attack roll: Attribute + Skill, versus Difficulty (base 1, modified by situation).
- Hits at or above Difficulty: attack lands. Excess hits are Trick Hits.
- Trick Hits: spend on Extra Damage, Disarm, Knockdown, Stagger, Wound, Setup, Pin, Disrupt, Expose.
- Damage = weapon rating + Extra Damage Tricks. Reduce by target Armor. Mark on Health track.

### Defense Summary

- Active Dodge (Major Action): Athletics + Dexterity; each hit = +1 Difficulty to all attacks vs. you this Round.
- Parry (Minor Action, melee only): Close Combat + Dexterity; each hit cancels one incoming hit.
- Cover (Minor Action, no roll): Partial +1, Heavy +2, Full — no direct attack possible.

### Damage and Health

- Health track bands: BRUISED (2+Stamina boxes, no penalty) → IMPAIRED (2 boxes, −1 die) → CRITICAL (2 boxes, −2 dice) → INCAPACITATED (1 box). Total = 7+Stamina.
- Quiet track bands: STATIC (2+Composure boxes, no penalty) → DISTORTION (2 boxes, −1 die Social/Empathy) → FRACTURE (2 boxes, −1 die ALL) → FULL QUIET (1 box, episode). Damage types: Shock(/), Trauma(X), Void(*).
- Bashing heals in hours. Lethal heals in days. Aggravated heals in weeks.
- Armor reduces Bashing and Lethal. Armor does NOT reduce Aggravated (unless enchanted).

### Tag Quick Reference

- Invoke: spend 1 Momentum for +2 dice when Tag is relevant. One invoke per Tag per Spotlight.
- Compel: SG offers a narrative complication; player gains 1 Momentum (or spends 1 Momentum to refuse).
- Tags are always active as fiction — even when not formally invoked, [Loud] weapons make noise.

### Magical Combat Quick Reference

- Casting = Major Action. Full-Round for complex combined effects.
- Coincidental: 0 Paradox from witnesses. Vulgar: add Sphere level(s) to Paradox Pool.
- Concentration check when hit during casting: Composure + Resolve vs. attacker's hits. Fail = working collapses.
- Sustained effects: -1 die per maintained effect to all other rolls. Concentration check when hit.
- Counter-magic: Intellect + Sphere vs. Difficulty equal to original caster's hits.
*See Appendix H for Phase Initiative tactical variant. See Appendix I for Armor construction, enchantment, and special materials. See Appendix J for the complete Tag list with invoke and compel examples for all weapon and situation Tags.*

---

Combat in Mage is consequential by design. A single fight can deplete Willpower, build Paradox to dangerous levels, damage Wonders and instruments, compromise relationships with Sleepers who witnessed impossible things, attract the attention of Paradox Spirits, and leave all participants physically and spiritually compromised for the story that follows. Embrace this friction. It makes the moments of violence meaningful. It makes the mages who choose patience, preparation, and cleverness over direct force look very, very smart. And it makes the stories that emerge from combat feel earned — because they are.

Violence in the World of Mage is always a cost. The question is who pays, and how much.

## Worked Example — Combat with a Tagged Wonder (Jovian Thundergloves)

This example demonstrates a character using a named Wonder from Appendix V in combat, showing the full invoke/compel cycle and Phase Initiative interaction.

**The Setup:** Barnabas (Sons of Ether, Arete ●●●, Forces ●●●, Correspondence ●●) has acquired Kid Kimota's Jovian Thundergloves (●●●, Tags: [Enchanted], [Ranged], [Stunning], [Aggravated], [Loud]). He is confronting an NWO extraction team in a parking garage — three agents in body armor, plus a fourth in a vehicle. His Verbena ally Esperanza is behind him. His goal: clear the path.

**The Gloves' Stats (Appendix V):** Enhancement +2 to Forces-based ranged attacks. Lightning discharge, Aggravated damage. Activation: speak the trigger word and strike a grounded surface. Range: 15 meters. Quintessence cost: 1 per activation.

---

**ROUND 1 — DECLARE PHASE**

Barnabas declares: activate the Jovian Thundergloves (free action to speak trigger word), then discharge a thunderbolt arc at all three agents. He targets Agents A, B, and C — all within 12 meters, clustered near their vehicle.

This is a Wonder activation, not a casting roll from his own Sphere. The Gloves use their embedded Effect: Forces ●●● discharge, SF 2 (from the item's Sphere rating, not Barnabas's). Barnabas's dice pool for the attack: Forces 3 + Cunning 4 + Enhancement +2 (Gloves) = 9 dice. Cost: 1 Quintessence.

Barnabas invokes **[Ranged]**: "The arc can jump between conductors. I'm invoking Ranged — the parking garage has metal support columns and wet floor from the rain outside. The bolt can chain between them and hit all three agents."

Storyguide: "+2 dice, making your pool 11. That's a significant invoke — but the [Loud] tag comes into play. The discharge in a concrete garage is going to sound like a cannon shot. Everyone within a block will hear it."

Player: "Accepted."

**PHASE 2 (SF 2 resolves):**

ATTACK ROLL: 11 dice. Results: 10, 10, 9, 8, 7, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 1 → 7 hits (two 10s = 4 hits, plus 9 and 8 = 2 hits, plus 7 = 1 hit). Difficulty 2 (medium range). 7 hits − 2 Difficulty = 5 Trick Hits.

DAMAGE vs. AGENT A (primary target): 7 hits base, Aggravated type. Military Body Armor (Soak 4, Ballistic). [Aggravated] bypasses standard armor entirely. Full 7 Lethal to health track → 7 boxes filled → Critical band crossed. Agent A is down and not getting up without emergency medical intervention.

TRICK HIT SPENDS (remaining 5 Trick Hits):

- 2 Trick Hits → Extra Damage ×2 to Agent B: base 7 + 2 = 9 damage. [Aggravated] again bypasses armor. 9 Aggravated → Incapacitated. Agent B is out.
- 2 Trick Hits → apply [Stunning] tag effect to Agent C: he loses his next Major Action (stunned by the electrical discharge even without full damage contact). The arc clipped him.
- 1 Trick Hit → Knockdown on Agent C: he is prone.

PARADOX: The Gloves are a Wonder. The discharge is Vulgar — there is no natural explanation for a lightning bolt in an indoor parking garage. Witnesses: Agent C (conscious), the vehicle driver (visible through windshield), and potentially Sleeper bystanders. Vulgar with witnesses: +2 dice × Forces ●●● = +6 dice. Barnabas's pool was at 0; now 6.

**THE COMPEL:**

Storyguide: "I'm compelling [Loud]. The shot sounded like artillery. Car alarms are going off throughout the garage. In about 45 seconds, the Sleepers on level 1 — six of them — are going to be on the phone with emergency services. Also, your Verbena ally Esperanza covers her ears and turns away. Her Quiet Track took 1 box from the sensory assault. She has [Noise-Shocked]. Accept the Compel?"

Player: "Yes. This is the problem with the Gloves."

Storyguide: "Take 1 Momentum. And the vehicle is starting. The driver got very motivated."

---

**ANALYSIS — WHAT THIS EXAMPLE DEMONSTRATES:**

The Jovian Thundergloves performed dramatically. Two agents incapacitated and one stunned-prone in a single action is overwhelming firepower. The Enhancement +2 and [Ranged] invoke pushed the pool to 11 dice. At 7 hits against Aggravated-vulnerable targets, the result was decisive.

But the cost was real:

- **+6 Paradox dice** from Vulgar-with-witnesses in a single action. The pool sits at 6 — one more Vulgar casting triggers a forced Paradox roll.
- **The [Loud] compel** created two immediate consequences: a 45-second witness clock and an ally condition ([Noise-Shocked]) that costs Esperanza her next action.
- **Collateral ally damage is real.** Wonders with area effects in enclosed spaces affect allies. The Storyguide was within their rights to apply this — and the Compel gave the player Momentum as compensation.

This is tagged Wonder combat at its most expressive. The item solved the tactical problem completely. The tags then generated exactly the complications that make the next five minutes interesting.

# Chapter Eight: Factions — The Ascension War

The world of Mage is the ordinary world, with a hidden layer that most people cannot perceive. Cities have political structures, economies, and histories — and on top of all that, they have Nodes that mages fight over, Chantries that serve as faction headquarters, and fault lines of metaphysical tension that only the Awakened can feel.

The central conflict is called the Ascension War, and it has been running — mostly cold, sometimes hot — for centuries. At its heart is a disagreement about what reality should be and who gets to decide.

## The Consensus

The Consensus is the collective belief of humanity. It is not a voting system or a philosophy — it is literally the force that determines what is and is not possible in the physical world. When nine billion people believe that objects fall when dropped, gravity functions perfectly. When nine billion people believe that illness is caused by pathogens and treated by medicine, supernatural healing becomes increasingly Vulgar.

The Technocracy controls the Consensus. They have spent four hundred years ensuring that the dominant worldview is materialist, scientific, and rational — not because rationalism is wrong, but because it neatly defines away everything that Traditions mages do. Under a purely materialist Consensus, magic is definitionally impossible. The Traditions exist in the cracks.

This is not entirely cynical. The Technocracy genuinely believes that a unified, stable Consensus protects humanity from the chaos that followed the Renaissance, when competing magical paradigms tore Europe apart. The Order of Reason became the Technocracy because they won — and they won by making people safer, more comfortable, and longer-lived. The problem is what they had to do to maintain that safety.

## The Nine Traditions

The Traditions are an alliance of magical philosophies that share one thing: a rejection of the Technocratic Consensus as the only possible reality. They are not united beyond that. The Traditions alliance is constantly riven by politics, theology, and genuine disagreements about what the Ascension — the fulfillment of magical potential — actually means.

### Akashic Brotherhood

The Akashic Brotherhood holds that the universe is composed of information — the Akashic Record — and that enlightenment is the ability to read, and eventually rewrite, one's own entry. Their primary Sphere is Mind; their secondary is Life and Forces. Their practices are predominantly physical: Do, the martial discipline that is less a fighting art and less a meditation practice than a unified philosophy of body-as-reality.

They appear as monks, martial artists, philosophy professors, and occasionally as ascetics who have clearly been meditating since the Song dynasty. Their focus objects are their own bodies; their paradigm is the Record.

```
Akashic Brotherhood — Tradition Path
------------------------------------
Major Path Name: The Warrior-Philosopher
Associated Skills: Athletics, Close Combat, Empathy, Esoterica, Enigmas, Culture
Associated Attributes: Dexterity, Resolve, Composure
Special Ability: Do — Once per scene, when making an unarmed strike, the mage may substitute any one Sphere rating for Might or Dexterity in the attack pool.
Primary Sphere: Mind
Cultural Context: South and East Asian traditions, though global members have joined in the modern era.
```

### Celestial Chorus

The Celestial Chorus believes that magic is the Word of a Divine Whole — every act of true will is a prayer, every powerful spell a hymn. They are the most internally diverse Tradition: their members include Christian monks, Jewish kabbalists, Islamic Sufi mystics, and followers of pantheons across the globe. What unites them is the conviction that reality's fabric is woven by a Supreme Being's ongoing creative act, and that mages are those who have learned to sing harmony with that act.

Their primary Sphere is Prime; their secondary is Forces and Spirit. Their focus objects are religious symbols, holy texts, and sacred music. Their Rotes are prayers, hymns, and blessings.

```
Celestial Chorus — Tradition Path
---------------------------------
Major Path Name: The Voice of the Divine
Associated Skills: Esoterica, Leadership, Persuasion, Empathy, Medicine, Culture
Associated Attributes: Presence, Resolve, Composure
Special Ability: Litany of Healing — When casting a Life effect to heal, the Chorus mage may treat every Complication on the roll as one severity lower.
Primary Sphere: Prime
```

### Cult of Ecstasy

The Cult of Ecstasy knows that the Consensus is a prison built from boredom, routine, and the fear of sensation. Their path to magic runs through the extremes: pain, pleasure, intoxication, ecstatic dance, sensory overload, and the occasional ego-dissolving drug. They are not hedonists for its own sake — they are sensory researchers who have mapped the hidden geography of consciousness through their bodies.

Their primary Sphere is Time; their secondary is Mind and Spirit. Their focus objects are everything from drugs and music to self-inflicted sensation. Their Rotes involve specific rituals of excess.

```
Cult of Ecstasy — Tradition Path
--------------------------------
Major Path Name: The Seeker of the Bleeding Edge
Associated Skills: Esoterica, Empathy, Artistry, Persuasion, Survival, Athletics
Associated Attributes: Cunning, Presence, Composure
Special Ability: Burn the Veil — Once per session, when in a state of genuine emotional or physical extremity, the Cultist may waive the Paradox dice from one Vulgar casting.
Primary Sphere: Time
```

### Dreamspeakers

The Dreamspeakers are not a unified magical tradition but a collection of traditions that the founding Order of Hermes (and later the other Traditions) lumped together under a single name: practitioners of animism, spirit magic, ancestor veneration, and shamanistic practice from Indigenous, African, and Polynesian backgrounds. The name was and remains colonial. The mages it describes are dealing with that legacy.

Their primary Sphere is Spirit; their secondary is Life and Forces. Their focus objects are sacred animals, ancestor bones, ceremonial regalia. Their Rotes involve calling on specific spirits, ancestors, and natural forces.

```
Dreamspeakers — Tradition Path
------------------------------
Major Path Name: The Walker Between
Associated Skills: Esoterica, Survival, Medicine, Empathy, Enigmas, Culture
Associated Attributes: Composure, Resolve, Cunning
Special Ability: Spirit Ear — The Dreamspeaker hears and understands all spirits without needing Spirit 2. Communication is free (though cooperation still requires skill).
Primary Sphere: Spirit
```

### Euthanatos

The Euthanatos understand that death is not an ending but a transition — and they understand it better than anyone, because they study it, sometimes deliver it, and have long made their peace with it. They are healers who are also executioners, philosophers of endings who collect the debts that karma generates. Their relationship with violence is complex: they are emphatically not assassins, but they have killed, and they will again, when they determine that the Pattern demands it.

Their primary Sphere is Entropy; their secondary is Life and Spirit. Their focus objects are skull-motifs, death-related iconography, certain plants and fungi. Their Rotes involve judgments, final rites, and the reading of fate.

```
Euthanatos — Tradition Path
---------------------------
Major Path Name: The Good Death
Associated Skills: Esoterica, Medicine, Empathy, Close Combat, Enigmas, Survival
Associated Attributes: Resolve, Composure, Cunning
Special Ability: Read the Thread — Once per scene, the Euthanatos may ask the Storyguide one specific yes-or-no question about whether a target will die within the next 24 hours, and receive an honest answer.
Primary Sphere: Entropy
```

### Order of Hermes

The Order of Hermes is the oldest and most codified of the Traditions. They are the mages of formal tradition, of grimoires and circles and precise Latin incantations, of celestial correspondences and alchemical transmutation. If magic is a language, the Order has been writing its grammar for two thousand years. They are also, consequently, among the most politically conservative and internally hierarchical of the Traditions.

Their primary Sphere is Prime; their secondary is Forces and Matter. Their focus objects are wands, circles, grimoires, and alchemical apparatus. Their Rotes have formal designations in Latin.

```
Order of Hermes — Tradition Path
--------------------------------
Major Path Name: The Hermetic Scholar
Associated Skills: Esoterica, Culture, Science, Enigmas, Leadership, Artistry
Associated Attributes: Intellect, Resolve, Cunning
Special Ability: Hermetic Formulation — When casting any Rote, the Order mage gains Enhancement +2 (instead of the standard +1). Their preparation is meticulous.
Primary Sphere: Prime
```

### Sons of Ether

The Sons of Ether (increasingly: the Etherites, or the Society of Ether) are scientists who lost the Paradigm War with the Technocracy in the 1900s. Their physics — based on luminiferous ether, orgone energy, aetheric resonance, and related principles that the Technocracy declared impossible — was literally excised from the Consensus. Undeterred, they kept working, and discovered that sufficiently dedicated belief in an impossible physics makes that physics actually work, at least for you.

Their primary Sphere is Forces; their secondary is Matter and Correspondence. Their focus objects are fantastic scientific apparatus: ray guns, gravity nullifiers, aetheric resonators. Their Rotes are engineering protocols.

```
Sons of Ether — Tradition Path
------------------------------
Major Path Name: The Mad Genius
Associated Skills: Science, Technology, Artistry, Pilot, Enigmas, Athletics
Associated Attributes: Intellect, Cunning, Dexterity
Special Ability: Inspired Device — Once per session, the Etherite may create a single-use Wonder with a Power of up to their highest Sphere rating, requiring only 15 minutes and their laboratory tools. The device works once, spectacularly.
Primary Sphere: Forces
```

### Verbena

The Verbena know what the world is made of: blood and bone, root and rain, the turning of seasons and the howling of something very old in the dark between the stars. They are the oldest tradition — older than the Traditions themselves, older than the Order of Hermes, older perhaps than history. They practice magic through the body, through nature, through the sacrifice and the harvest and the thing that happens when you stand in a circle under the full moon and mean it.

Their primary Sphere is Life; their secondary is Spirit and Prime. Their focus objects are blood, herbs, moonlight, and sacred groves. Their Rotes are rites, some of which predate writing.

```
Verbena — Tradition Path
------------------------
Major Path Name: The Blood-and-Bone Witch
Associated Skills: Esoterica, Medicine, Survival, Athletics, Empathy, Artistry
Associated Attributes: Resolve, Stamina, Composure
Special Ability: The Witch's Mark — Once per scene, the Verbena may diagnose any natural illness or wound by touch, with no roll required. Rosamund's signature.
Primary Sphere: Life
```

### Virtual Adepts

The Virtual Adepts are the newest Tradition, defectors from the Technocracy's Iteration X Convention who realized that the tools they were building to control information could also be used to liberate it. They believe that reality is software — complex, poorly documented, and absolutely open to modification by anyone with enough skill and enough will. Their paradigm is hacking: the exploitation of reality's underlying architecture.

Their primary Sphere is Correspondence; their secondary is Mind and Forces. Their focus objects are technology: phones, laptops, VR systems, custom hardware. Their Rotes are algorithms.

```
Virtual Adepts — Tradition Path
-------------------------------
Major Path Name: The Reality Hacker
Associated Skills: Technology, Science, Enigmas, Larceny, Ranged Combat, Pilot
Associated Attributes: Cunning, Intellect, Dexterity
Special Ability: Root Access — Once per session, the Adept may treat any electronic system as a magical focus, using it as a sympathetic link to any digital infrastructure it is connected to (at Correspondence 2+, this means surveillance-free access to connected networks).
Primary Sphere: Correspondence
```

## The Technocratic Union

The Technocracy is not a monolith. It is five Conventions — four surviving and active, one (Iteration X) increasingly terrifying in its literal interpretation of "human enhancement." They share a unified Consensus and a chain of command that runs through something called the Inner Circle, but at the ground level, individual Technocrats are professionals with genuine beliefs, real fears, and personal ethics that sometimes clash with their orders.

The Technocracy's genius is that they are not wrong. Their medicine saves lives. Their infrastructure feeds billions. The Consensus they maintain is not entirely a cage — for the vast majority of humanity, it is a warm, well-lit house. The horror is in who they sacrifice to maintain it, and what they do to people who notice the cracks.

### Iteration X

The machine-body-and-mind Convention. Iteration X believes that human biology is a prototype and that iterating toward perfection requires hardware upgrades. Their agents carry cybernetic enhancements that give them superhuman strength, targeting systems that make combat unpleasant for anyone trying to shoot at them, and occasionally modifications that their own colleagues find disturbing. The MKIVs — full combat-chassis Iterators — are walking nightmares by any reasonable definition.

```
Iteration X — Convention Path
-----------------------------
Major Path Name: The Living Weapon
Associated Skills: Close Combat, Ranged Combat, Technology, Science, Athletics, Pilot
Associated Attributes: Might, Stamina, Dexterity
Special Ability: Cybernetic Enhancement — The Iterator begins with 2 cybernetic enhancements (each is an equipment-level upgrade with 2-3 tags, e.g. [Targeting System: +1 die on Ranged Combat], [Reinforced Frame: Armor 1]). Additional enhancements can be acquired through advancement.
Primary Sphere: Forces (as "Dimensional Physics")
```

### NWO (New World Order)

The political and informational arm. NWO agents are information brokers, government officials, media personalities, and intelligence operatives. They believe that the world is safest when properly managed — and that management requires surveillance, narrative control, and the strategic deployment of social pressure. They are the Technocracy's visible-but-deniable face in the world's corridors of power.

```
NWO — Convention Path
---------------------
Major Path Name: The Invisible Hand
Associated Skills: Leadership, Persuasion, Culture, Empathy, Enigmas, Technology
Associated Attributes: Manipulation, Presence, Cunning
Special Ability: Narrative Control — Once per session, the NWO agent may retroactively establish a cover story for any event that has already occurred, as long as no player character directly contradicts it publicly within the same session. Mundane records reflect the new narrative.
Primary Sphere: Mind (as "Social Engineering")
```

### Progenitors

Life scientists and biotechnologists. The Progenitors build the living systems that the Technocracy uses to maintain the Consensus biosphere: disease treatments that accidentally make populations more docile, enhancement programs that select for Consensus-compatible traits, ecological monitoring that keeps the natural world "stable." They are also, occasionally, monsters — the experiments that do not make it into public reports are the ones that keep other Technocrats up at night.

```
Progenitors — Convention Path
-----------------------------
Major Path Name: The Architect of Life
Associated Skills: Medicine, Science, Survival, Enigmas, Technology, Leadership
Associated Attributes: Intellect, Resolve, Stamina
Special Ability: Living Lab — The Progenitor may perform any Life Sphere effect on a willing or restrained subject without a Casting Roll, provided they have 15 minutes and their laboratory equipment. The effect is Coincidental (framed as medicine).
Primary Sphere: Life (as "Dimensional Science: Biology")
```

### Syndicate

Money is magic, and the Syndicate knows it literally. They manage the Technocracy's finances, control global markets, and ensure that the economic infrastructure of the modern world remains aligned with Consensus values. Their agents rarely fight — they do not need to. They make sure that anyone who challenges the Technocracy finds their funding gone, their allies purchased, and their assets frozen.

```
Syndicate — Convention Path
---------------------------
Major Path Name: The Markets That Move the World
Associated Skills: Leadership, Persuasion, Culture, Larceny, Enigmas, Pilot
Associated Attributes: Manipulation, Cunning, Presence
Special Ability: Deep Pockets — Once per session, the Syndicate agent may acquire any mundane resource (money, equipment, personnel, information) at a quantity appropriate to their Enlightenment rating, without a roll and without a paper trail.
Primary Sphere: Entropy (as "Primal Utility Theory")
```

### Void Engineers

The space program of the Technocracy, and the most idealistic of the Conventions. The Void Engineers genuinely believe in exploration, in the wonder of the Deep Universe, in the possibility that there is more out there than any Consensus has yet accounted for. They also staff the Reality Deviant Response Teams and maintain the orbital defense platforms that the Technocracy does not officially admit exist.

```
Void Engineers — Convention Path
--------------------------------
Major Path Name: The Pioneer Beyond the Horizon
Associated Skills: Pilot, Science, Technology, Athletics, Survival, Ranged Combat
Associated Attributes: Cunning, Resolve, Dexterity
Special Ability: Deep Universe Training — The Void Engineer ignores Gauntlet ratings when attempting to perceive or enter the Deep Umbra (beyond the spirit world's inner layers). In standard spirit realms, they gain +2 dice on all Spirit-related rolls.
Primary Sphere: Correspondence (as "Dimensional Science: Spatial Mechanics")
```

## The Nephandi

The Nephandi are mages who have chosen to invert their Awakening — to dedicate their will not to Ascension but to the unmaking of reality. They are not simply "evil mages." They are agents of entropy and destruction in the most fundamental sense: they serve Outer Powers (vast, alien entities beyond the Tellurian) whose goals are incomprehensible to human minds, but whose apparent desire is to accelerate reality's end.

A Nephandus is created through a ritual called the Cauls — a process that literally inverts the mage's Avatar, twisting their connection to Quintessence inside out. After the Cauls, a mage is a Nephandus forever. There is no redemption. This is not a philosophical position — it is metaphysical fact in this setting. The Cauls breaks something that cannot be unbroken.

Mechanically, Nephandi generate twice the normal Paradox from Vulgar magic — but they also heal Paradox damage by causing suffering in others. A Nephandus at full Paradox Pool is in no more danger than a Tradition mage at an empty pool, as long as they can find victims. They are antagonists and should never be player characters in standard play.

```
Nephandi — Key Traits (Antagonist)
----------------------------------
Paradox Healing: A Nephandus may reduce their Paradox Pool by 1 die per box of damage they intentionally cause to a Sleeper victim.
Sphere Inversion: Their Spheres function normally, but their effects are always cosmetically terrible — healing comes as writhing flesh-growth, mind effects feel like violation.
Outer Power Bond: Each Nephandus serves a specific Outer Power. They can call on its influence once per session for +3 dice on any roll, but the Power has its own agenda.
Barabbi (Tradition-fallen Nephandi): These are the most dangerous kind — they know Tradition protocols, safehouses, and vulnerabilities.
```

## The Marauders

Marauders are mages who have lost their connection to the shared Consensus entirely. They are not corrupted or evil in the Nephandi sense — they are simply gone, immersed permanently in their own personal reality, which has become more real to them than the world everyone else lives in. A Marauder who believes they are a medieval knight is a medieval knight, for all magical purposes. The people around them exist in their Quiet-world, reshaped by it.

Marauders generate no Paradox for themselves. They generate Paradox for everyone around them. Sleepers near a Marauder begin accumulating Quiet; other mages find their Paradox Pools growing; reality in the area becomes unstable and local Consensus degrades.

Marauders are not trying to destroy the world. They do not understand that there is a world to destroy. They are profoundly, permanently lost, and the most humane response to them is containment — but containment of a being who can rewrite local reality as a reflexive action is not a small project.

```
Marauders — Key Traits (Antagonist)
-----------------------------------
Personal Reality: Marauders always succeed at casting magic that fits within their personal reality. No roll is required for effects that their inner world dictates as normal.
Reality Bleed: Sleepers within 50m take 1 Quiet Track box per scene of exposure. Other mages' Paradox Pools grow by 1 die per scene of exposure.
Immune to Standard Dispelling: Standard dispel rolls have +2 Difficulty against a Marauder's effects, because their reality is internally consistent.
Occasionally Allies: A Marauder can sometimes be a temporary ally if their personal reality happens to align with the mages' goals. They are not evil. They are just... elsewhere.
```

## Disparate Crafts

The Disparates are mages who never joined the Traditions, either by choice or by history. They represent every magical tradition that survived independently outside the great factional wars. Some are ancient beyond reckoning. Some are newly formed responses to modern problems. Each has its own paradigm, its own Sphere affinities, and its own political relationship with the larger Awakened world.

| Disparate Craft | Brief Description and Primary Sphere |
| --- | --- |
| Ahl-i-Batin | The Subtle Ones. Islamic-adjacent mystical order specializing in hidden connections; Correspondence. They once served as neutral brokers between factions. |
| Bata'a | Afro-Caribbean spirit workers; Life and Spirit. They maintain one of the most sophisticated relationships with the spirit world outside the Dreamspeakers. |
| Hollow Ones | Gothic-subcultural nihilists and neo-romantic poets; Entropy and Spirit. They do not believe in Ascension. They believe in beauty and the end. |
| Kopa Loei | Hawaiian guardians of sacred land; Life and Spirit. Their magic is tied explicitly to specific geographic locations. |
| Solificati | Alchemists; Matter and Prime. They were once part of the Order of Hermes and left in a doctrinal dispute. Their gold transmutation actually works. |
| Taftâni | Fire-mages of the Middle East; Forces and Prime. They walk through flame and read the future in smoke. |

## The Ascension War in Play

The Ascension War is not primarily a military conflict. It is a propaganda war, an ideological war, a war for the souls of uncommitted Sleepers and undecided Awakened alike. At the table, this means:

- Missions: Both sides take missions that involve sabotage, intelligence-gathering, protection of assets, and recruitment. These are the scenes that make up most of a chronicle.
- Chantries vs. Constructs: Tradition mages operate from Chantries (hidden safehouses with Nodes). Technocracy agents operate from Constructs (research facilities with their own magical infrastructure). Attacking an enemy base is a major story arc.
- Influence: Characters can accumulate influence in specific communities — academic, criminal, governmental, spiritual — that becomes a resource in the larger war.
- The Gray: Many Awakened are not aligned. Disparate Crafts, isolated mages, and Sleeper-adjacent people who have touched the supernatural but are not full mages all represent potential allies and threats. Recruiting and protecting these people is often more important than fighting.
---

## Tradition Path Tables — Skills and Attributes

Each Tradition is associated with a Major Path and two Minor Paths. The tables below show exactly what each Path provides. See Chapter Three — Designing and Customizing Paths for full Path design rules and how to create custom Paths.

HOW TO READ THESE TABLES: Each Major Path row shows: Attribute Arenas with dot totals summing to 10, a Skill list with suggested allocation summing to 6 (distribute freely among the listed Skills — no more than 3 in any single Skill), a Special Ability, and suggested Contact types. Each Minor Path row shows Attribute dots summing to 4 and Skill dots summing to 3 (no more than 2 in any single Skill). All dot counts are player-allocated; the suggested allocations are examples, not requirements.

# Chapter Nine: Storyguide

```
OPTIONAL RULE — Sanity System (Appendix K)
------------------------------------------
The Sanity system is an optional, modular subsystem for tracking psychological damage as a mechanical resource. Without it, mental and emotional trauma are handled purely through roleplay and Willpower. With it, characters have a ten-point Sanity track that degrades through exposure to the impossible, Paradox backlash, and Quiet onset, expressed through Status Tags ([Unsettled], [Fractured], [Unraveling], [Quiet]) that create mechanical pressure alongside the narrative weight. The Sanity system is particularly valuable for horror-inflected or psychological chronicles. It can be added mid-chronicle without disrupting existing characters — simply establish each character's current Sanity level based on what they have already experienced. Full rules in Appendix K: Sanity.
```

## Running a Mage Chronicle

A Mage chronicle is built on conflict between belief systems, each of which has genuine merit. The Storyguide's job is not to tip the scales toward the Traditions or the Technocracy, but to make both sides feel real, to keep the world consistent, and to ensure that every act of magic — every roll of the dice — feels like it matters.

## The Three Tensions

Every Mage chronicle is animated by at least one of three fundamental tensions:

- Reality vs. Will: The world as it is versus the world as the mages believe it should be. Paradox is the mechanical expression of this tension. Let Paradox be a character, not a punishment. When it triggers, make it interesting, not painful for the sake of pain.
- Freedom vs. Safety: The Traditions want freedom; the Technocracy wants safety. Neither is entirely wrong. The best stories hold both arguments with equal seriousness.
- Personal vs. Universal: Every mage has an inner life — a Paradigm, a Bond, a history. The Ascension War is the backdrop; the mages'own stories are the foreground. The war presses on them; they decide how to respond.

## Structuring Scenes

Storypath Ultra's scene structure works well for Mage. Each scene should have a clear dramatic purpose and at least one meaningful choice for the players. Avoid filler — if a scene doesn't advance the story or reveal character, cut it.

- Opening Hook: Every session begins in the middle of something interesting. The mages are already in a scene with stakes. Explain where they are and what is immediately demanding their attention.
- Complication Escalation: As the session proceeds, add Complications — physical, social, magical — that raise stakes without necessarily requiring combat.
- Paradox as Plot: Whenever Paradox triggers, make it a scene. Do not resolve it as a number on a sheet. Let it be visible, felt, and narratively relevant.
- Breathing Room: Not every scene is high-stakes. Let the mages exist between crises — talking to Contacts, researching, crafting Rotes, managing their Chantry.

## Using the Paradox System as a Storyguide Tool

The Paradox system is your most powerful narrative lever. It rewards subtlety, penalizes recklessness, and provides automatic dramatic escalation as characters grow more powerful. Here is how to use it well:

### Make Results Personal

Every Paradox result should be flavored through the mage's paradigm. A Verbena's Paradox Backlash does not look like a Hermetic mage's. When Rosamund triggers a Backlash Wound, her flesh briefly becomes bark, her blood runs sap-green. When a Hermetic triggers the same result, equations appear on their skin and resolve incorrectly, burning like acid.

This is not just flavor. It tells the player: reality is speaking their language back to them, twisted. That is frightening in a way that generic damage is not.

### Paradox Spirits as Characters

Do not use Paradox Spirits as boss fights. Use them as persistent antagonists with personality. A Banality Hound that follows a specific mage for three sessions, showing up at the worst possible moments, is more effective than one the mages defeat in a single scene.

Let the Spirits communicate, in their fashion. A Witness might leave impressions in the mage's dreams — images of the damage they are doing to the Consensus fabric. A Wyrm-Whisper might start as a barely-there feeling of being watched, before escalating to full-on questioning of the mage's memories.

### The Paradox Pool as a Clock

Experienced Storyguides use the Paradox Pool visually — perhaps poker chips on the table, or a visible counter. When a pool approaches 7, everyone at the table feels it. The mage does too, in-character: a growing wrongness, a tension in the air, a sense that something is about to break.

This is good. Lean into it. Ask the player: "Kaito can feel it building. What does it feel like to him? Does he have a plan?" Getting the player narrating their own doom is excellent.

## Running Different Faction Play

### Traditions Chronicles

Traditions chronicles are about resistance and identity. The characters are outsiders who have found a community among the Awakened. The tension is between their individual magical practices (often messy, personal, and inefficient) and the Technocracy's seamless, industrially-scaled operation. Themes: creativity vs. control, diversity vs. unity, personal truth vs. collective safety.

Good Traditions antagonists are not cartoons. NWO agents have families. Progenitor scientists are genuinely saving lives. Iteration X operatives may have been ordinary humans before the augmentation procedures. The mages are fighting a system, not monsters.

### Technocracy Chronicles

Technocracy chronicles are about service, compromise, and the price of order. The characters are agents of an organization that is genuinely making the world better by most measurable standards. The tension is between doing the job and what the job requires. Themes: loyalty vs. conscience, pragmatism vs. morality, efficiency vs. compassion.

Good Technocracy antagonists are Tradition mages who are, from the Technocracy's point of view, literally generating madness in the population and attracting Nephandic attention with their wild magic. The mages'own colleagues — supervisors, fellow agents — may be the real antagonists, as they pressure the characters to do things they find wrong.

### Cross-Faction Chronicles

The most interesting chronicles often involve characters from different factions — a Verbena and a Progenitor, a Hermetic and a Virtual Adept — who are forced to work together by circumstances neither controls. Use the Paradigm system (Appendix B) to ensure that their magical methods clash interestingly, even when their goals align.

## Managing Quiet

Quiet is not a punishment. It is a character development resource. When a mage's Quiet Track begins to fill, engage with it narratively:

- Ask the player what the Static-level Quiet looks like for their character. What wrongness does Rosamund see in the corners of her vision? What equation does Vikram see in people's faces?
- Use Quiet as motivation. A mage at Distortion level (2 boxes) is fighting to maintain themselves. That fight is interesting. It creates moments of doubt, connection, and resolution.
- Full Quiet is an event, not a penalty. When a character hits 5 boxes, make it a session. Their internal world is a place the other characters can try to enter — using Mind magic, Spirit connections, or just sitting with a person in crisis. Bringing someone back from the edge of Quiet is one of the most meaningful things characters can do for each other.

## NPCs and the Awakened World

Not every NPC needs Sphere ratings. Most Sleepers the characters interact with should be played as fully human beings with goals, fears, and opinions. The magic is in the background; the people are in the foreground.

For named antagonists and significant NPCs, use the full character framework: Paths, Spheres, Paradox Pool. For recurring minor characters (the Chantry administrator, the Contact who always has information, the Technocracy agent who shows up at wrong moments), a few Attributes and one or two Sphere ratings is enough.

### Creating Antagonists

The best antagonists in Mage share the player characters' values and differ in method. A Progenitor who genuinely believes the vaccine program they are running will save ten thousand lives — and is right — is harder to fight than one who is simply cruel. The mages may have to watch them do the right thing for the wrong reasons while looking for a way to stop the long-term harm.

Use the Advantage system to calibrate antagonist power. An Iteration X MKIV with Might 6, Stamina 6, and Forces 3 has a significant Advantage over starting mages. The players should feel the threat.

## The Ascension War at Scale

Over the course of a long chronicle, the war shifts. Traditions gain or lose Chantries. The Technocracy responds to mage activity by tightening surveillance in specific cities. Disparate Crafts make tentative alliances. These shifts should be felt at the table — through the stories characters return from, the news they receive from Contacts, the way the Consensus seems to tighten or loosen in specific domains.

A good tool: every five sessions, the Storyguide picks one Consequence and one Development:

- Consequence: Something that changed because of the characters' actions, for good or ill. A Node they protected is now a Chantry. A Technocracy operative they let go returned with backup.
- Development: Something that changed in the larger world independent of the characters. A major Tradition figure has gone missing. A new NWO program is making Sleepers subtly more resistant to Tradition magic in this city.

# Appendix A: Avatars

This appendix is entirely optional. It adds a layer of spiritual depth to the game: the Avatar, the mage's inner genius, the spiritual entity that drove their Awakening and continues to guide, test, and occasionally torment them.

If your group wants a game focused primarily on political intrigue, magical combat, and the Ascension War without heavy metaphysics, skip this appendix entirely. The core game is complete without it. If your group is drawn to questions of spiritual identity, the meaning of Awakening, and the relationship between personal destiny and personal will — read on.

## What Is the Avatar?

The Avatar is the shard of the Divine Will that inhabits every mage — the fragment of cosmic consciousness that pressed itself into a mortal soul and whispered "wake up." It is not the mage's soul (that is separate), not their will (that is also separate), and not their conscience (though it may feel that way). It is something older than all of those: a purpose, a pattern, a story that wants to be told through this particular life.

Avatars are experienced differently based on the mage's paradigm. A Celestial Chorus mage experiences their Avatar as the voice of God. A Virtual Adept experiences it as their own internal reasoning process, elevated to a higher function. A Verbena experiences it as the Old One who lives in the hollow at the center of their chest.

Mechanically, the Avatar is a rating from 1 to 5. This rating represents how clearly the mage can hear their Avatar, how much guidance they receive, and how much friction exists between the mage's chosen path and the Avatar's essential nature.

## Avatar Ratings

| Avatar Rating | Experience and Capability |
| --- | --- |
| 1 — Whisper | The Avatar communicates in dreams, gut feelings, and synchronicities. The mage may not recognize its voice at all. Once per session, the Storyguide may give the player a cryptic hint about the session's central danger. |
| 2 — Nudge | The Avatar is clearer. It pushes the mage toward their destiny with stronger feelings of rightness or wrongness. Once per session, the player may ask the Storyguide one yes-or-no question about their character's Essence (the Avatar's type) and receive an honest answer. |
| 3 — Voice | The Avatar can hold something like a conversation. It appears in meditations, dreams, and moments of stillness. It provides guidance that is specific though rarely direct. Once per session, the mage may ask the Avatar one question and receive a genuinely useful (if cryptically phrased) answer. |
| 4 — Presence | The Avatar is a palpable companion. Other supernatural beings can sense it. The mage's eyes sometimes shift to show something ancient. Once per scene, the mage may draw on the Avatar's presence for +2 dice on a roll that directly serves their Essence. |
| 5 — Union | The mage and Avatar are in deep alignment. The Avatar's Essence informs everything the mage does. All magical rolls that serve the Avatar's Essence gain Enhancement +1 automatically. The mage also begins to resist Quiet (remove 1 box at the start of each session automatically). |

## Avatar Essence

Every Avatar has an Essence — a fundamental nature that defines what it is seeking through the mage's life. Essences come in four broad types. The Storyguide and player choose an Essence at character creation; it shapes the Avatar's guidance and determines when the mage can draw on its power most effectively.

| Essence | Nature and Guidance Style |
| --- | --- |
| Dynamic | The Avatar seeks change, creativity, and the new. It pushes the mage toward action, innovation, and breaking old patterns. Guidance: "Move. Create. Do not repeat yourself." |
| Pattern | The Avatar seeks order, understanding, and the correct arrangement of things. It guides the mage toward knowledge, structure, and completion. Guidance: "Understand first. Then act. Once only." |
| Primordial | The Avatar seeks primal truth — the things underneath all structure, all change, all order. It is the oldest type and the hardest to work with. Guidance is intuitive, visceral, and often expressed through sensation rather than language. |
| Questing | The Avatar seeks something specific — a goal, a person, an answer — and will not rest until it is found. Guidance: "This is the way. This is always the way." Very focused, occasionally tunnel-visioned. |

## The Avatar in Play

The Avatar should appear in play in at least one of the following ways each session:

- A dream or vision that contains information about the current situation, filtered through the Avatar's Essence.
- A moment of inexplicable certainty: the mage simply knows something, and is right.
- A test: a situation that challenges the mage to act in accordance with (or against) their Essence.

### Avatar Damage

An Avatar can be harmed. Nephandi who complete the Cauls have inverted their Avatar. High levels of Quiet damage the connection to the Avatar. And certain Paradox events — particularly Backlash at the Catastrophe level — can temporarily sever the mage from their Avatar.

When the Avatar is damaged, reduce the Avatar rating by 1. This immediately reduces the mage's effective Arete/Enlightenment cap by 1 for the remainder of the session. A mage whose Avatar rating reaches 0 is in severe spiritual crisis; they cannot advance their Spheres and Quiet heals more slowly. Healing Avatar damage requires the resolution of the specific trauma that caused it — a dedicated story arc, not a numbers exercise.

### The Seeking

When a mage is ready to advance their Arete or Enlightenment, they undergo a Seeking — a metaphysical trial mounted by their Avatar. The Seeking is unique to each mage and each Arete/Enlightenment threshold. It is never combat in the conventional sense; it is a confrontation with an aspect of the mage's own nature, posed as an impossible question that the Avatar demands be answered by action.

Mechanically, the Storyguide designs the Seeking as a special scene — typically one session or a major scene within a session. The mage enters a trance (or is pulled into one involuntarily), navigates a symbolic landscape determined by their paradigm, and must make meaningful choices that reveal what they have learned. Success advances Arete/Enlightenment. Failure sends the mage back — not permanently, but they must wait for the right narrative moment to try again.

> Example Seeking — Rosamund at Arete 2 → 3:
> 
> Rosamund's Avatar Essence: Primordial. Her Bond: "The Healing Grove of Cwm Glas."
> 
> The Seeking manifests during a deep meditation. She finds herself in the grove — but wrong. The trees are standing as charred columns. In the center is a figure: an ancient woman wrapped in thorns, whose face is every dying thing Rosamund has ever failed to save.
> 
> "You heal the wounded," the figure says. "But you do not yet know what healing costs. You do not yet know what the land takes, when you take from it."
> 
> The Storyguide presents a choice: Rosamund can pour her own life-force into the grove (take 2 Aggravated to the Health track, which will persist out of the Seeking), restoring it and paying the cost the Avatar is pointing toward. Or she can refuse, try to solve it with magic — and wake up without the Arete advance.
> 
> If Rosamund chooses the sacrifice, the grove blazes back to life. She exits the Seeking at 2 Aggravated — and at Arete 3.

# Appendix B: Paradigm, Practice & Instruments

This appendix is optional. It provides a simple, flavorful framework for defining how a mage's magic looks, sounds, smells, and feels — without adding mechanical complexity beyond what the tag system already provides.

The Focus system uses three elements: Paradigm (what the mage believes magic is), Practice (how they work it), and Instruments (what they use to work it). Together, these three elements give every mage a unique magical signature that is narratively rich and mechanically useful.
