```
The Chantry's physical layout has been deliberately optimized for defense: chokepoints, fields of fire, lines of sight, planned fallback routes, pre-positioned defensive elements.

Benefit: When the Chantry is under physical attack, its defenders gain +1 die to all combat rolls made while within the Chantry's walls. Attackers must contend with a floor plan that favors the defenders — the Storyguide should impose +1 Difficulty on any attacker attempting to advance through the Chantry's chokepoints.

How Earned: Have the cabal deliberately plan the layout with defense in mind and spend at least one session establishing it in the fiction.
```

### Magical Merits

```
Node Tap (••••  Chantry Merit — requires Node Access •• +)
----------------------------------------------------------
The Chantry's connection to its Node has been deepened and formalized, with physical and magical infrastructure designed to distribute Node energy throughout the Chantry efficiently.

Benefit: The Node's weekly Primal Energy output is increased by 50% (round up). Additionally, a mage working within the Chantry can draw on the Node directly during a magical working, adding up to 3 Primal Energy per scene as Enhancement dice (1 Primal Energy per additional die) without the normal requirement to physically touch the Node. Tass that forms at the Node automatically flows to a designated collection point within the Chantry.

How Earned: Install physical infrastructure (pipes, channels, crystals — matching the paradigm) connecting the Node to the Chantry, and perform a Prime 3 / Spirit 2 extended working (minimum 10 hits) to attune the connection.
```

```
Paradox Sink (••• Chantry Merit)
--------------------------------
Through sustained magical work and careful attunement, the Chantry has become capable of absorbing and slowly dissipating Paradox energy. Paradox that would otherwise discharge explosively within the Chantry is instead drawn into the structure itself, releasing slowly over days rather than all at once.

Benefit: When a mage within the Chantry would trigger a Paradox Discharge (from a Complication on their Paradox Pool), they may choose to have the Chantry absorb the discharge instead. Roll the Chantry's Sanctum Rating as dice against Difficulty 1. Each hit absorbs one Paradox die result — instead of triggering, it is stored in the Chantry structure. The Chantry can hold a number of stored Paradox dice equal to its Sanctum Rating before it must release them (the Storyguide chooses an appropriate and dramatic moment for release). A Paradox Sink cannot protect against a Paradox Nova from a dying mage.

How Earned: The Chantry must have Sanctum 3+. The mages must deliberately attempt to have the Chantry absorb Paradox over multiple sessions rather than allowing it to discharge normally, essentially "training" the structure to manage the energy.
```

```
Spirit Covenant (••• Chantry Merit)
-----------------------------------
The Chantry has a formal, ongoing covenant with one or more powerful local spirits who serve as guardians, advisors, or residents in exchange for Primal Energy, respect, and specific services.

Benefit: The covenant spirits alert the cabal to supernatural threats (functioning as a permanent supernatural early-warning system), provide a pool of knowledge about local spirit politics and history, and can be called upon once per story arc for active assistance (up to Spirit 4 equivalent). The spirits are genuinely powerful beings with their own agendas — they are allies, not servants. Violating the covenant's terms causes it to dissolve and may create enemies.

How Earned: Negotiate and formalize a covenant through Spirit 3 working and extended roleplay. The covenant's specific terms are established in play and the spirits' personalities become NPCs with ongoing relationships.
```

```
Warded Teleportation Point (•••• Chantry Merit)
-----------------------------------------------
The Chantry contains one or more fixed Correspondence anchor points — specific locations within the Chantry that a Correspondence-trained mage can teleport to from anywhere on Earth with reduced difficulty, as long as they have been there before.

Benefit: A mage with Correspondence 3+ who has been to the Chantry can teleport to the anchor point at -1 Difficulty. This makes emergency extraction to the Chantry reliable even under combat stress. Additionally, the anchor point can be designated as a "landing zone" — unauthorized teleportation INTO the Chantry via Correspondence 3+ still requires overcoming the Warding Trait but arriving at the anchor point does not.

How Earned: Establish the anchor point with a Prime 2 / Correspondence 3 extended working (minimum 6 hits) within the Chantry, with all cabal members physically present during establishment.
```

```
Resonance Specialization (•• Chantry Merit — one Sphere)
--------------------------------------------------------
The Chantry has been specifically developed around a single Sphere, with every element — the Ward patterns, the Dedicated Spaces, the Library contents, the Node resonance — focused on amplifying that one type of magic.

Benefit: Magic using the specialized Sphere gains an additional +1 die within the Chantry (on top of the Sanctum Rating bonus). Extended workings using the specialized Sphere are at -1 Difficulty beyond the normal Sanctum reduction. This is the most focused version of magical optimization — a Chantry specialized for Life magic will be extraordinary for healing and transformation but offers no additional benefit for, say, Forces or Correspondence work.

How Earned: Choose the Sphere at Chantry establishment or through sustained single-Sphere focus over a story arc. The specialization becomes embedded in the Chantry's resonance.
```

```
Conclave Space (•••• Chantry Merit)
-----------------------------------
The Chantry has been formally registered with the Council of Nine (or the Technocracy's administrative structure, for Constructs) as a recognized sanctum. This registration grants certain political protections and responsibilities.

Benefit: Attacking a recognized Conclave Space is considered a major violation of Tradition law (or Technocratic protocol), and the perpetrators can be formally sanctioned. The Chantry can host Conclaves — formal meetings of multiple cabals — with all attendees bound by the rules of parlay while within its walls. The Parlay Ward (a Spirit 3 / Prime 2 effect maintained automatically by the Chantry) enforces this: any being who violates parlay within the space suffers immediate backlash (roll Sanctum Rating as automatic Paradox dice against the violator). Additionally, the Chantry gains Allies 1 with the regional Council or Convention structure for free.

How Earned: Apply to the relevant Tradition or Convention administrative body, pay whatever political dues are required, and host at least one formal Conclave event.
```

### Intelligence and Communication Merits

```
Scrying Chamber (•• Chantry Merit)
----------------------------------
A dedicated space within the Chantry has been set up for Correspondence and Time scrying — a dark room with a large mirror or water basin or electronic sensor array, depending on paradigm, with amplifying materials and a comfortable position for the scryer.

Benefit: Scrying performed from the Scrying Chamber gains +2 dice (Sphere bonus plus the Chamber's focused setup). Extended scrying that would normally require the mage to maintain physical concentration can instead be set to run automatically for up to four hours while the mage is elsewhere — the Chamber maintains the parameters and alerts the mage to anything of note.

How Earned: Outfit the room with appropriate materials (Resources 2) and perform a Correspondence 2 / Prime 1 attunement ritual.
```

```
Communications Network (•• Chantry Merit)
-----------------------------------------
The Chantry is the hub of a magical communication network among allied Chantries, mages, and contacts. This might be a Correspondence 2 permanent sending-stone network, a Data 2 encrypted quantum-entangled system, a Spirit 2 message-runner arrangement with bound messenger spirits, or any other paradigm-appropriate form.

Benefit: The cabal can communicate securely with all network members instantaneously from within the Chantry. Messages sent through the network cannot be intercepted by mundane means and require Correspondence 3 or Data 3 to monitor magically. The network also provides an automatic information feed — news of significant events affecting any network member reaches the Chantry within 24 hours.

How Earned: Establish formal communications links with at least three other Chantries or individuals through the appropriate Sphere working.
```

### Historical and Spiritual Merits

```
Ancient History (•••  Chantry Merit)
------------------------------------
The Chantry was established more than a century ago (or occupies a location with deep pre-existing magical history). It carries the accumulated resonance of generations of use.

Benefit: The Chantry's Sanctum Rating counts as 1 higher for the purpose of magical workings (a Sanctum 3 Chantry with Ancient History functions as Sanctum 4 for dice bonuses and Paradox reduction). Additionally, the Library contains historical records going back to the Chantry's founding — information about local supernatural history that is unavailable elsewhere. The Chantry is recognized by local spirits as an ancient institution, granting +1 die to all Spirit-related negotiations conducted within it.

How Earned: Either establish the Chantry in a location with documented magical history, or maintain a Chantry in active use for multiple story arcs until the passage of (game) time establishes this status.
```

```
Consecrated Ground (••• Chantry Merit)
--------------------------------------
The Chantry has been formally consecrated according to a specific spiritual tradition — blessed by a Chorister high priest, dedicated in Verbena blood ritual, purified by Akashic ceremony, aligned in Hermetic sacred geometry, or initialized in Technocratic scientific inauguration ritual. The consecration pervades the physical structure.

Benefit: Beings whose nature is antithetical to the consecration's tradition suffer -2 dice to all rolls while within the Chantry. Nephandi (for any consecration), hostile spirits (for Spirit consecration), and beings of decay (for Life consecration) find the Chantry actively uncomfortable. The Chantry's residents gain +1 die to all Willpower-related rolls (resisting manipulation, maintaining concentration under duress) while within its walls. Healing magic performed within a Consecrated Chantry is at -1 Difficulty.

How Earned: A formal consecration ceremony performed by a mage of Arete 3+ using the appropriate Sphere (Prime 3, Spirit 3, or Life 3 for most consecrations), with the full cabal present.
```

---

## Running and Maintaining a Chantry

A Chantry is not a static resource. It requires ongoing attention, generates its own stories, and responds to the activities of its inhabitants. A Storyguide who treats the Chantry as a living element of the chronicle will find it one of the most productive sources of drama.

### Upkeep

Every Chantry requires routine upkeep:

- Mundane upkeep: Rent or mortgage, utilities, property taxes, repairs. Cost scales with Chantry size and location.
- Magical upkeep: The Wards must be renewed periodically. Standard Wards require refreshing once per season (once every three months). A mage with Prime 2 spends one scene performing the renewal ritual. A Chantry whose Wards are not renewed begins losing Warding dots at the rate of one per missed season.
- Node maintenance: A Node with guardian spirits requires the cabal to maintain their relationship — regular offerings, respect of the spirits' conditions, and response to the spirits' requests. Failing to maintain this relationship causes the Node Access to degrade toward 0.
- Social upkeep: Chantry Allies require cultivation. A cabal that goes months without acknowledging their network will find it weakened when they need it.

### Threats to a Chantry

Chantries attract unwanted attention. As a resource and a base of power, they are targets:

- Rival mages and Technocratic Constructs may attempt to poach the Node, compromise the Wards, or take the Chantry by force.
- Hostile spirits may challenge the Chantry's Umbral presence, especially if the cabal has neglected their spirit relationships.
- Mundane threats — nosy neighbors, building inspectors, law enforcement, fire, flood — are particularly dangerous because the cabal cannot respond to them magically without generating Paradox.
- Paradox contamination: Heavy magical activity at the Chantry can lead to Paradox buildup in the structure. An untended Paradox Sink that overflows, a massive backlash, or a Paradox Nova within the Chantry walls can permanently damage its Sanctum and Warding ratings.
- Nephandi corruption: A Chantry can be spiritually corrupted through sustained Nephandic ritual performed within or near it. The process is slow — it takes months of deliberate effort — but the result is devastating. A corrupted Chantry begins actively harming its former inhabitants while attracting entities of decay and entropy.

### Improving a Chantry

Chantry Traits improve through investment and narrative development:

| Trait Improvement | Requirement |
| --- | --- |
| Sanctum +1 | Sustained magical activity over a story arc; perform a new attunement ritual; or achieve a significant spiritual milestone at the location. |
| Warding +1 | Deliberate magical construction of new defensive systems; formation of new spirit guardian contracts; investment of Resources 2+. |
| Library +1 | Acquire a significant new text collection, translate a rare text, or complete a major research project whose results are archived in the Chantry. |
| Node Access +1 | Clear a blockage in the Node's flow (an extended Spirit + Prime working); negotiate deeper access with the Node's guardians; or perform a major healing or purification of the Node. |
| Allies +1 | Cultivate a new significant relationship over a story arc; resolve a crisis in a way that earns trust from the relevant group. |

---

## Technocratic Constructs

Technocratic Constructs follow the same mechanical framework as Tradition Chantries, with different aesthetics and some different narrative considerations. A Construct is typically:

- Disguised as a legitimate organization: a corporate research facility, a government office, a medical center, a military installation. Its magical nature is invisible to mundane investigation.
- Staffed by extraordinary citizens alongside Enlightened operatives. These unawakened support staff are a resource (and a responsibility — they can be endangered, corrupted, or turned against their employers).
- Connected to the Technocratic administrative structure, providing access to Union resources and intelligence but also subjecting the Construct to Union oversight and political maneuvering.
Constructs have equivalent Traits: Consensus Rating (Sanctum equivalent, representing how thoroughly the local consensus has been managed), Security (Warding equivalent), Data Archives (Library equivalent), Power Supply (Node Access equivalent), and Personnel (Allies equivalent). The mechanical values translate directly.

Construct-specific Merits include: SIGINT Network (intelligence feeds from Union surveillance infrastructure), Social Processing Suite (specialized space for NWO conditioning procedures), Manufacturing Capability (Matter/Life research and production), and Dimensional Monitoring Station (Void Engineer deep-space observation capability).

---

## Example Chantries

### The Hawthorn House — Small Urban Tradition Chantry

A three-story Victorian brownstone in a gentrifying neighborhood, owned by a local professor (a Verbena elder who has aged out of field work but maintains the Chantry with fierce dedication). The building appears from outside to be a normal if slightly eccentric old home — the garden is very well maintained, the windows always have unusual plants in them, and the residents tend to be odd but friendly.

```
Hawthorn House — Chantry Stats
------------------------------
SIZE: Small (•••)
Tradition Focus: Verbena and allied Traditions welcome

Sanctum: ••• (3)
Warding: •• (2)
Library: •• (2)
Node Access: •• (2) — a ley line convergence beneath the basement
Allies: •• (2) — friendly neighbors, a sympathetic police detective, a local herbalist network

Merits:
  Dedicated Workspace (••) — the basement lab is optimized for Life and Spirit workings
  Consecrated Ground (•••) — blessed in a Verbena spring ceremony; Nephandi and death-spirits are uncomfortable here
  Hidden Spaces (••) — a concealed ritual room behind the wine cellar
  Spirit Covenant (•••) — a hawthorn spirit and three land-spirits serve as guardians

Node Resonance: Growth, healing, seasonal change. Tass forms as honey (harvested from spirit-attended hives in the garden), dried herbs, and pressed flowers.

Weekly Primal Energy: 7 (Node ••)

Paradox Reduction: 2 dice for Coincidental, 1 die for Vulgar (Sanctum •••)

History: Established in 1887 by a hedge witch who fled the UK during a Technocratic crackdown. The house has passed through six generations of caretakers, accumulating deep resonance. Local spirits consider it "the old house" and treat it with a respect they wouldn't extend to any newer establishment.
```

> Using Hawthorn House: The cabal returns to the Chantry after a difficult confrontation with an NWO surveillance team. Silas has 4 Lethal damage and is bleeding. Rosamund uses the basement's dedicated workspace (Life working at -1 Difficulty + +1 die from Sanctum + +1 die from Dedicated Workspace = highly favorable conditions). She rolls Intellect 3 + Medicine 3 + 3 bonus dice = 9 dice, Difficulty 1. She rolls 9 dice and gets 5 hits — more than enough to fully heal Silas. The Consecrated Ground's bonus to healing magic (-1 Difficulty, which was already 1, so 0 effective) makes even a difficult working feel straightforward. The spirit guardian manifests briefly to acknowledge the cabal's return — a flicker of light through the hawthorn leaves in the garden. "Welcome home," it doesn't say, but means.

### The Atelier — Medium Urban Chantry (Multi-Tradition)

A converted loft space above an art gallery in a major city, held jointly by a Cult of Ecstasy mage, a Virtual Adept, and two Order of Hermes practitioners. The gallery is legitimate and successful — the art on the lower floor is extraordinary, and the connection to cultural capital is part of the Chantry's strategy. The upper floors are a maze of studios, servers, ritual space, and increasingly strange decor.

```
The Atelier — Chantry Stats
---------------------------
SIZE: Medium (••••)
Tradition Focus: Mixed — Ecstasy, Hermetic, Adept; other Traditions welcome by invitation

Sanctum: •••• (4)
Warding: ••• (3)
Library: •••• (4) — exceptional combination of Hermetic texts and digital archives
Node Access: ••• (3) — an artistic resonance Node; the arts community has been drawing energy to this location for a century
Allies: ••• (3) — the arts world, a city councilwoman's office, three allied Disparate practitioners in the city

Merits:
  Ritual Space (•••) — the top floor is a large circular space with reconfigurable altar architecture, optimized for group workings
  Scrying Chamber (••) — a dark room with a two-way mirror array and Correspondence anchors
  Communications Network (••) — linked to five other Chantries in the region
  Warded Teleportation Point (••••) — the Ritual Space contains a Correspondence anchor known to the cabal and three trusted allies
  Conclave Space (••••) — formally recognized; has hosted three Tradition Conclaves
  Resonance Specialization: Mind (••) — the creative, consciousness-shifting energy of the arts Node emphasizes Mind workings

Node Resonance: Creative inspiration, altered states, boundary dissolution. Tass forms as pigments (used directly in magical paintings), printed digital images that carry embedded information, and occasionally as music that persists on recording without having been played.

Weekly Primal Energy: 15 (Node •••)

Paradox Reduction: 3 dice for any working (Sanctum ••••)

History: Established in 1968 during the height of the arts scene in this city's creative community. Has changed hands three times, each time expanding its Sanctum rating as accumulated resonance built. Currently experiencing political tension as the Hermetic members push for more formal structure while the Ecstasy member argues that systematization kills the Node's creative resonance.
```

> Using The Atelier: The cabal needs to gather intelligence on a Technocratic operation before they act. They convene in the Scrying Chamber. The Adept uses Correspondence 3 + Cunning 4 = 7 dice + 2 (Scrying Chamber) = 9 dice to scout the target location. Difficulty 2 (defended Technocratic Construct). She rolls 6 hits — well over the threshold. Trick Hits spent: one to extend duration (the scrying stays active for 2 hours while she monitors), one for detail (she can hear as well as see), one for a "mark" (she places a Correspondence anchor at the target site for later teleportation). The Sanctum's 3-die Paradox reduction means this Vulgar working only adds 0 dice to her Paradox Pool (Correspondence 3 = 3 dice, minus 3 from Sanctum = 0). Inside their own Chantry, even Vulgar magic is almost safe.

### Cambridge Cybertechnologies — Technocratic Construct (Medium)

A mid-sized corporate campus in Massachusetts, officially a cybertechnology research firm. Publicly known for its AEGIS robot demonstrations. The civilian-facing side is legitimate science; the back half of the campus is Iteration X territory, with a fabrication lab, a Primal Energy extraction system running off a technical Node beneath the server farm, and a comfortable but thoroughly monitored residence facility for its Enlightened staff.

```
Cambridge Cybertechnologies — Construct Stats
---------------------------------------------
SIZE: Medium (••••)
Convention: Iteration X (primary); NWO (liaison)

Consensus Rating (Sanctum): ••• (3)
Security (Warding): •••• (4)
Data Archives (Library): ••• (3) — technical and engineering data; NWO social analysis
Power Supply (Node Access): ••• (3) — technical resonance Node beneath the server room
Personnel (Allies): •••• (4) — Massachusetts tech sector; local government; university partnerships

Construct Merits:
  Manufacturing Capability (•••) — on-site fabrication can produce Iteration X Devices; automated repair nanite production
  SIGINT Network (••) — connected to Union surveillance infrastructure for the greater Boston area
  Social Processing Suite (••) — NWO conditioning facility (officially "employee wellness center")
  AEGIS Command Center (••••) — houses and coordinates the 20 AEGIS units; Enlightened oversight required to authorize field deployment

Node Resonance: Precision, efficiency, mathematical elegance. "Tass" forms as precision-machined components, perfect crystalline structures, and occasionally as compiled code that runs without bugs.

Weekly Primal Energy: 15 (Node •••)

Paradox Reduction: 1 die for Coincidental, 2 dice for Vulgar (Consensus ••• — the local population's acceptance of high tech makes coincidental procedures genuinely coincidental here)

History: Established 2019 over an existing tech startup that Iteration X acquired and converted. The founding team was Social Processed; the remaining employees believe they work for a legitimate (if secretive) robotics firm. The cover is excellent. The ethical complexity of the situation is a source of ongoing tension for the two Enlightened employees who retain enough perspective to notice it.
```

---

---

## Chantry Flaws

Chantry Flaws are the counterweight to Merits. They represent problems, vulnerabilities, or complications built into the Chantry's history or situation. Flaws are not chosen at character creation — they are imposed by circumstance, acquired through play, or inherited from the Chantry's history. The Storyguide may introduce a Flaw at any time when the fiction supports it. A Flaw can be removed through dedicated play effort, narrative resolution, or magical reworking, but never simply purchased away.

Every Flaw has a severity rating (one to three dots) and a specific mechanical consequence. More importantly, every Flaw is a story hook — a built-in source of complications that the Storyguide should use regularly, not save for major moments.

```
[Known Location] — Flaw (••)
----------------------------
The Chantry's location is known to one or more hostile or potentially hostile factions.

Mechanical consequence: The Storyguide may introduce external threats to the Chantry without
  setup. Surveillance, infiltration attempts, or direct assault can occur at any time. The cabal
  cannot assume safety even within their home base.

Story hook: Who knows? How did they find out? Is it one rogue agent who's keeping the
  knowledge to herself, or is it in a file that gets reviewed quarterly? The answer changes
  everything about how the threat feels.

Removal: Physically relocate the Chantry, or conduct a successful disinformation campaign
  (Manipulation + Persuasion extended roll, 10+ hits, over multiple sessions) to make
  hostile observers believe the location is abandoned or a decoy.
```

```
[Compromised Node] — Flaw (•••)
-------------------------------
The Chantry's Node has been damaged, contaminated, or is being siphoned by an outside party.

Mechanical consequence: Node output is halved. Tass that forms is often tainted —
  using tainted Tass for Quintessence requires a Resolve + Composure roll (Difficulty 2)
  or gain 1 die in the Paradox Pool. The Node's resonance bleeds strange energy that
  affects workings performed in the Chantry unpredictably.

Story hook: What happened to the Node? Is it a Nephandi working slowly corrupting it
  from the Umbral side? A Technocratic siphon drawing its power into their infrastructure?
  An old wound from a battle fought here years before the current cabal arrived?

Removal: A Prime 4 / Spirit 3 extended working (minimum 15 hits) to cleanse and
  re-attune the Node. This also requires addressing whatever caused the damage in the
  first place — a purely magical fix will not hold if the source persists.
```

```
[Exposed History] — Flaw (••)
-----------------------------
The Chantry's past includes something that creates present-day vulnerability — a
  previous owner's crimes, a broken covenant with local spirits, a Nephandi who
  was once a member, a blood ritual gone wrong that left a permanent resonance mark.

Mechanical consequence: Certain beings, factions, or spirits are hostile to the Chantry
  by default. The Storyguide assigns specific entities that will react adversely to the
  Chantry's history. Researching the Chantry's past (Library + Occult, Difficulty 2)
  reveals the nature of the problem to anyone who looks.

Story hook: The current cabal may not know what happened here. Learning the history
  is itself an arc, and deciding what to do about it — acknowledge the crime, make
  reparations, cover it up, or let it stand — defines the cabal's character.

Removal: Address the underlying cause through play. Reparations to wronged parties,
  rituals of cleansing, or renegotiation with offended spirits. There is no mechanical
  shortcut — the fiction must be resolved.
```

```
[Mundane Entanglements] — Flaw (•)
----------------------------------
The Chantry's cover requires ongoing, active maintenance. It is a functioning business,
  a family home with real relatives, a religious building with an actual congregation,
  or a registered nonprofit with real members. The cover cannot simply be neglected.

Mechanical consequence: The cabal must spend at least one scene per session arc
  maintaining the mundane cover. If they neglect this, the cover degrades: neighbors
  start asking questions, board members want meetings, relatives demand explanations.
  Full cover collapse costs the Chantry the [Hidden Location] condition.

Story hook: The mundane elements of the cover are people, and people have needs.
  The congregation member who is quietly desperate. The family member who suspects
  something is wrong. The business partner who is actually competent and curious.
  These entanglements are where some of the most human stories in a chronicle live.

Removal: Cannot be removed without either burning the cover or magically managing
  the mundane relationships (Mind magic, with all its ethical complications).
```

```
[Contested Ownership] — Flaw (•••)
----------------------------------
Another group — a rival cabal, an ex-member, a Technocratic Convention, a spirit
  lord with territorial claims — believes they have a legitimate claim to the Chantry
  or its Node.

Mechanical consequence: The Chantry's Ward does not function against the contesting
  party — they can enter freely. The Storyguide may introduce the contesting party
  as an active antagonist in any session. The cabal's Chantry Background dots are
  contested — they cannot spend or improve them until the dispute is resolved.

Story hook: Is the claim legitimate? The answer does not change what the cabal
  will do, but it changes how they feel about it. A Chantry stolen from a murdered
  cabal, with surviving members seeking justice, is a different story than a rival
  cabal making a false territorial claim.

Removal: Resolve the dispute through play — negotiation, confrontation, legal/political
  maneuvering within Tradition law, or (most directly) by ensuring the claimant is no
  longer in a position to make claims.
```

---

## Chantry Tags — SPU Integration

Chantries, like characters and items, can have Tags. Chantry Tags represent persistent qualities of the location that can be Invoked by characters acting within the Chantry or Compelled by the Storyguide to introduce complications. Tags are applied to Chantries at the Storyguide's discretion, often emerging organically from the fiction.

### Invoking Chantry Tags

A character within the Chantry may Invoke a relevant Chantry Tag once per scene for +2 dice on a roll where the Tag genuinely applies. Invoking a Chantry Tag costs 1 Momentum (from the player pool) or is granted free by the Storyguide when the fiction particularly calls for it. Multiple characters cannot invoke the same Tag in the same scene — only the first invocation applies.

### Compelling Chantry Tags

The Storyguide may Compel a Chantry Tag to introduce a complication. The player(s) affected can accept the Compel (gaining 1 Momentum each) or spend 1 Momentum to refuse it. A Compelled Tag should always produce a situation that requires a meaningful decision — not just inconvenience for its own sake.

| Chantry Tag | Invoke / Compel |
| --- | --- |
| [Consecrated] | Invoke: +2 dice to Willpower resistance rolls, morale checks, or spiritual workings performed here.
Compel: Beings with antithetical resonance are drawn to disrupt or defile the Chantry specifically because it is consecrated. |
| [Hidden Node] | Invoke: +2 dice to conceal magical activity from detection; Technocratic scanning attempts are at +2 Difficulty.
Compel: The hidden Node attracts the attention of precisely the entity the cabal least wants finding it. |
| [Ancient History] | Invoke: +2 dice to any research roll, spirit negotiation, or historical knowledge check performed within the Chantry.
Compel: Something from the Chantry's history resurfaces at an inconvenient moment — an old debt, a forgotten enemy, a promise made by previous inhabitants. |
| [Known Location] | Invoke: (Cannot be invoked as a benefit — this is a Flaw Tag only.)
Compel: The Storyguide introduces external observation or threat to the Chantry without setup. The cabal cannot assume they are unobserved. |
| [Ritual Space] | Invoke: +2 dice to any group ritual or extended magical working performed in the designated area.
Compel: The Ritual Space's power draws something uninvited — a spirit curious about the working, a Paradox manifestation, a rival who felt the magic from outside. |
| [Warded] | Invoke: +2 dice to detect supernatural intrusion or unauthorized magical activity within the Chantry's borders.
Compel: The Ward's own resonance interferes with a working the cabal needs to perform — the protection creates an obstacle. |
| [Living Node] | Invoke: +2 dice to any Quintessence-related roll, Node attunement, or Tass-collection activity.
Compel: The Node has a mood today. Its output changes character — the Tass forms in a disquieting shape, or the Node's resonance magnifies exactly the wrong emotion in the Chantry's inhabitants. |
| [Compromised] | Invoke: (Cannot be invoked as a benefit — this is a Flaw Tag only.)
Compel: The compromise manifests in a specific way this scene: a siphon draws away Quintessence at a critical moment, a tainted working produces unexpected side effects. |
| [Community Hub] | Invoke: +2 dice to Persuasion, Presence, or Allies rolls conducted from or about the Chantry.
Compel: The community that relies on the Chantry needs something from the cabal, right now, at the worst possible moment. |
| [Technocratic Design] | Invoke (Constructs only): +2 dice to Technology or science-related workings performed within; Primal Energy distribution is more efficient (+25% Node output).
Compel: Iteration X engineering tolerances and NWO protocols generate bureaucratic friction at a critical moment. The paperwork demands filing. |

> CHANTRY TAG INVOCATION IN PLAY:
> 
> The Hawthorn House has tags: [Consecrated], [Hidden Node], [Ancient History].
> 
> Scene: The cabal is interrogating a Nephandi prisoner in the Ritual Space. The
>   prisoner is resisting mental probing and the cabal's Mind mage is struggling.
> 
> Player: "I want to Invoke [Consecrated]. The Hawthorn House has been a sanctified
>   space for forty years. Beings antithetical to the Light are weakened here. That
>   has to help."
> 
> Storyguide: "Absolutely. Spend 1 Momentum. The consecration presses down on the
>   prisoner like a weight — her resistance feels different, more effortful, less
>   confident. You get +2 dice on your next Mind roll against her."
> 
> Later: The Storyguide Compels [Ancient History].
> 
> Storyguide: "While you're working, something surfaces in the Library's oldest records.
>   The Nephandi you're holding was here before — decades ago, under a different name,
>   as a guest of the previous cabal. She knows this Chantry. She knows its weak points.
>   You can accept this Compel and gain 1 Momentum each, or spend 1 Momentum to
>   suppress it for now."
> 
> Players: "We accept. This is more interesting."

## The Chantry as a Character

The most memorable Chantries in a long-running Mage chronicle develop into characters in their own right — not necessarily sapient, but with personality, history, and narrative weight. The Hawthorn House has moods. The Atelier has preferences. A well-developed Chantry at Sanctum 4 or 5 begins responding to events in small but unmistakable ways: lights flicker when danger approaches, the temperature drops when someone lying enters, specific rooms feel welcoming or forbidding based on circumstances.

This is not magic in the mechanical sense — it does not require rolls or Sphere levels. It is the accumulated resonance of a place that has been loved and worked in and defended and grieved in for years or decades. The Chantry remembers. It responds. It becomes part of the cabal's identity.

Storyguides should lean into this. Give the Chantry its own small moments. The smell of the specific incense that the first cabal member used to use, years after they've gone. The way the lights in the spirit room always come on at sunset, even when no one is there. The library book that always seems to be open to a relevant page when someone needs it. These things don't need mechanical justification — they are the texture of a place that has accumulated meaning.

And when the Chantry is threatened — when it might be lost, damaged, or taken — it should feel like losing a character, not just losing a resource.

---

For rules on acquiring a Chantry as a Background at character creation, see Chapter 3: Character Creation. The Chantry Background (1-5 dots) represents the cabal's share of a pre-existing Chantry and gives them access to proportional Trait ratings. A cabal that begins with a Chantry at Background 3 has a Medium-size Chantry with Traits averaging at 2-3, and two Merits chosen at character creation. Additional development happens in play.

# Appendix F: The Nine Traditions — Deep Lore

This appendix provides Storyguides and players with a comprehensive reference for each of the Nine Traditions of the Council of Nine Mystic Traditions. Content here is drawn from the Lore of the Traditions sourcebook and converted fully to Storypath Ultra mechanics. Each Tradition entry covers its philosophical paradigm, signature practices, preferred instruments, notable rotes (converted to SPU mechanics), special Wonders and Devices, and guidance for integrating Tradition-specific Tags and abilities into play.

These entries are not standalone character creation guides — they supplement the core rules in Chapters 3, 4, and 8 of this book. Cross-reference the Sphere chapter (Ch. 6), the Magic chapter (Ch. 4), and the Focus rules in Appendix B when building Tradition mages. The entries below assume familiarity with SPU's dice mechanics: roll Attribute + Skill + Enhancement, hits on 8+, 10s count double, Complications trigger on 1s.

## The Akashayana (Akashic Brotherhood)

The Akashayana — whose name translates roughly as "those who embody the Akasha," the omnipresent record of all that is — are the most internally diverse Tradition on the Council. Hailing from across Central, South, and East Asia, they unite under a shared cosmological framework: that the universe is conscious, that all beings are connected to that consciousness, and that enlightenment is achieved through mastering the self so completely that the boundary between self and cosmos dissolves. The old nickname "Akashic Brotherhood" is an artifact of Western misunderstanding, and most Akashayana find it mildly embarrassing.

The Akashayana teach that there are no true enemies, only beings in varying stages of remembering their divine nature. This philosophy — called Draladharma, the recognition that opposition is ultimately illusory — makes them patient teachers and relentless combatants simultaneously. An Akashic might spend a decade teaching a Sleeper meditation and another decade disabling that same Sleeper's former master with a finger to the spine. Both acts express the same principle. If an enemy cannot be enlightened, they can at least be stopped from causing further harm.

### Paradigm

The Akashayana hold that Enlightenment comes through mastery of Do — a term meaning both "the path" and "the art" — which encompasses every discipline from martial combat to calligraphy to breath-work to the study of qi flow through landscapes. The universe is not a machine to be manipulated from outside; it is a living, breathing, remembering entity, and the mage's body and mind are microcosms of it. Magic does not come from outside the self but from aligning oneself so completely with the fabric of reality that effects manifest as natural extensions of the practitioner's will.

Akashayana mages typically use one of several internal paradigms: the classical Draladharma (focused on karma and compassion), the Kannagara approach (present-moment perfection, Japanese-influenced), the Lì-Hài approach (consequentialist action, outcomes over ideology), or any number of Pai-specific philosophies. Despite these variations, all Akashayana share the view that the body is the primary instrument of magic.

### Practices & Instruments

The Akashayana's core practice is Do: the martial art of self-mastery. This encompasses not only combat but also calligraphy, meditation, acrobatics, gardening, tea ceremony, and any activity performed with total mindful presence. The practitioner does not divide these activities into "magical" and "mundane" — all of them are expressions of the same underlying discipline.

A newer recognized practice among the Akashayana is Qì Manipulation: the conscious direction of life-energy through the body, through landscapes, and between beings. Where Do focuses on the practitioner's internal alignment, Qì Manipulation extends outward — adjusting the energy flow through a building using feng shui principles, unblocking a patient's meridians through acupuncture, or harmonizing dissonant forces in a location.

Common instruments for Akashayana mages include: bodily discipline (the primary instrument, always), meditation, Do training and martial techniques, body postures (asanas, combat stances), breath control, Qì Tools (luopan compasses for feng shui, acupuncture needles, moxa bundles), Mantras (spoken or chanted syllables that resonate with universal frequencies), Memories (access to past-life experiences stored in the Akashic Record), sacred geometry and calligraphy, tea ceremony, and music (particularly drumming and stringed instruments).

### SPU Mechanics

In Storypath Ultra terms, an Akashayana mage's primary Casting Attribute is almost always Dexterity or Might (for Do-based effects) or Intellect (for Qì Manipulation and record-reading). Their Body skills (Athletics, Brawl, Medicine) double as magical instruments — an Akashic who scores exceptional hits in unarmed combat can spend Momentum to simultaneously channel a Life or Mind effect through the strike, representing the blending of physical and magical mastery.

Akashayana who specialize in Do gain the following Tradition Tag: EMBODIED PRACTITIONER. This Tag means that when using their body as an instrument (unarmed combat, athletic feats, meditative trance), they may treat Complications on the Paradox Dice as one step less severe — a Backlash Wound becomes a Sensory Bleed, a Catastrophe becomes a Backwash. Their magic flows so naturally through physical action that the Paradox backlash finds less purchase.

### Rotes

#### Do Strike (Mind 1 / Life 1)

The foundational technique of Akashic combat. The practitioner channels focused intention through a single blow, causing it to bypass armor and disrupt the target's nervous system simultaneously. This is not simply hitting someone hard — it is a moment of perfect alignment between the striker's will and the fabric of reality that happens to express itself through a punch.

Mechanics: The mage makes a standard Brawl + Dexterity attack. Each hit in excess of the defense threshold may be converted, at the mage's choice, into a Mental Condition (Rattled, Disoriented) rather than physical damage. Additionally, the attack ignores soft armor (Enhancement from clothing, padded protection) for the round. The mage may use Momentum to add +1 Enhancement per point spent, reflecting deeper alignment.

> Example: Sifu Chen faces a Technocratic enforcer in heavy tactical gear. She rolls Brawl + Dexterity (5 dice) and gets 4 hits against the enforcer's Defense of 2. She has 2 excess hits. She converts one into the Disoriented Condition on the enforcer (imposing -1 die to his next action) and applies the second as lethal damage. His armor Enhancement is ignored this round. The enforcer staggers back, confused despite his protection.

#### Reading the Akashic Record (Time 2 / Mind 2)

Every event, thought, and experience in existence is recorded in the Akashic Record — the omnipresent memory of the cosmos. An Akashayana trained in this rote can enter a meditative state and read specific passages of that Record relevant to themselves, a location, or another person. Past-life memories surface as vivid, emotionally resonant experiences rather than abstract information.

Mechanics: The mage rolls Intellect + Esoterica (or Occult) against Difficulty 2. Each hit reveals one significant detail from the past relevant to the query. With 3+ hits, the mage accesses an entire scene of past memory. On a Complication, the mage becomes temporarily lost in a past life and must spend 1 Momentum to reorient — until they do, they apply -1 die to all rolls as their current identity blurs.

#### Qì Adjustment (Life 2 / Matter 1)

Using acupuncture needles, a luopan compass, or pure focused intent, the mage identifies blockages in the flow of qi through a person's body or through a location's feng shui grid, then corrects them. For a person, this accelerates healing and clears mental fog. For a location, it improves the resonance of any magical working performed there.

Mechanics: On a person — the mage rolls Intellect + Medicine, Difficulty 1. Each hit removes one level of a physical Condition (Bruised, Injured) or converts one mental Condition to a milder form. In a location — requires 30 minutes of work and a Difficulty 2 roll. With 2+ hits, all magical workings in that location gain +1 Enhancement for the remainder of the scene. This cannot stack with other Enhancement bonuses.

### Wonders

```
Dragon Pearls (••• Wonder)
--------------------------
Background Cost: 6 | Quintessence: 20 | SPU Primal Pool: 10

These luminous spheres, formed in the heart of the Akashic Record over centuries of practice, serve simultaneously as Quintessence batteries and as aids to focused rote work. No larger than a golf ball, they emit a faint inner light that pulsates in time with the holder's heartbeat. Each Pearl is unique — shaped by the particular enlightenment-journey of the Akashic whose practice caused it to crystallize.

SPU Effect: A Dragon Pearl holds 10 points of Primal Energy. When the holder performs a rote (a pre-planned magical working from their tradition), they may draw on the Pearl to add +1 die to their casting roll. This bonus cannot raise the casting pool above 5 dice beyond the character's Enlightenment rating. Masters do not benefit from additional dice — the Pearl's encouragement is for those still developing their craft. When the Pearl's Primal Energy is depleted, it becomes dormant and must be recharged at a Node.
```

## The Celestial Chorus

The Celestial Chorus holds that beneath all the world's conflicting religions — the prayers of Christians, Muslims, Buddhists, Jews, Hindus, indigenous faith traditions, and countless others — beats a single divine heart. They call this unity the One, the Song, the divine source from which all genuine faith draws power. A Chorister mage does not claim that their own denomination is the correct one; they claim that all genuine spiritual yearning points toward the same transcendent reality, and that the Song connecting all these traditions is itself the source of magical power.

This pluralistic theology makes the Chorus one of the most internally complex Traditions, encompassing Orthodox priests, Sufi mystics, Protestant ministers, Jewish Kabbalists, Islamic scholars, Buddhist monks, Wiccan high priests, and shamans of countless cultures, all working together under the belief that their specific paths are fingers pointing at the same moon. The Five Doctrines — Charity, Comity, Compassion, Foresight, and Harmony — provide the ethical framework that holds this diverse coalition together, offering shared values that transcend specific dogmatic differences.

### The Five Doctrines

Charity requires active giving — not merely of money or goods but of time, knowledge, and magical power in service of those in need. Comity demands respectful coexistence with other faiths and practices, including those of other Traditions. Compassion extends even to enemies — a Chorister who destroys a Nephandus does so with grief, not triumph, recognizing that a soul has been lost rather than a battle won. Foresight requires thinking beyond the immediate moment, considering the long-term consequences of both action and inaction. Harmony calls each Chorister to work toward healing divisions — within the Traditions, within the Chorus itself, and within the world at large.

These Doctrines are not passive ideals. They actively shape Chorister magic: a mage who violates the Doctrines repeatedly finds their connection to the Song weakening, their magic becoming less reliable, their Paradox pool growing faster than it otherwise would.

### Practices & Instruments

The Chorus practices Faith in all its forms as their primary magical framework, supplemented by High Ritual Magic (elaborate ceremonial workings that call on the names and traditions of many faiths simultaneously). Some Choristers practice Invocation and God-Bonding, entering into direct communion with divine presences. Prayer is both practice and primary instrument.

Common instruments include: prayer (spoken, chanted, or silent), sacred music and song, religious texts and scriptures, incense and ritual smoke, sacred objects from any faith tradition, offerings and sacrifices (appropriate to the tradition in question), group ritual (the Chorus gains significant power from collective working), holy sites and consecrated spaces, fasting and other religious ordeals, and the symbolic elements of the practitioner's specific faith.

### Extended Ritual Variant: Distributed Prayer

A uniquely Chorister technique allows extended rituals to be distributed across time and space. Rather than completing a multi-hit working in a single extended session, a Chorister may divide the required working across multiple prayer sessions held at a shared location or focused on a shared object — a specific cathedral, a holy relic, or even a living person who has consented to be the focus.

Mechanics: The mage chooses a regular interval (daily, weekly, etc.) and begins the extended action. Each qualifying session counts toward the total hits needed. Other mages with a genuine faith connection (any faith recognized by their tradition) who are present and actively participating may add 1 automatic hit to the Chorister's roll for that session, representing the power of collective belief. Sessions separated by more than twice the chosen interval do not count.

### Rotes

#### Palimpsest (Matter 3 / Time 2 / Prime 2)

Sacred texts have been burned, lost to flood and war, deliberately destroyed by those who feared their power. The Palimpsest rote calls on the Chorus's connection to the Song — which underlies all genuine religious expression — to restore faded, damaged, or even deliberately erased writings, as long as genuine spiritual intention lay behind their original creation.

Mechanics: The mage rolls Intellect + Occult against the Difficulty of the damage (1 for faded ink, 3 for fire damage, 5 for deliberately destroyed originals). Each hit restores one "section" of text — a paragraph, a page, a chapter, depending on scale. On a Complication, the restored text reflects the emotional state of its original author in overwhelming detail — the mage experiences the author's fear, grief, or exaltation and must succeed on a Composure + Resolve roll or spend 1 Momentum to continue the rote. This restored text is not a copy; it is the original expression, made present again.

#### Dismiss the Discordant (Mind 2 / Correspondence 2 / Entropy 2 / Prime 2)

The Song that Choristers hear is harmonious by nature. Entities, people, and intentions that are genuinely malevolent create discordance in that Song — an almost-audible dissonance that the trained Chorister can identify and act upon. This ward detects and repels presences defined by ill intent, whether that intent comes from a Nephandus, a Marauder, an angry ghost, or simply a person whose heart is poisoned with hatred.

Mechanics: Cast on a location (a room, a building, a property) as an extended ritual requiring Difficulty 3 and at least 3 hits. The ward lasts for one lunar month per 2 excess hits beyond the minimum. Any being that enters the warded area with a genuinely harmful intent toward those within takes 1 die of mental damage per ward hit as the discordance of their intention becomes physically painful. They must succeed on a Resolve + Composure roll or retreat. Beings without hostile intent feel nothing unusual.

## The Cult of Ecstasy (Sahajiya)

The Cult of Ecstasy — now increasingly self-identifying as the Sahajiya, after the Bengali term for natural spontaneity — pursue Enlightenment through the deliberate, disciplined shattering of conventional boundaries. They are the Tradition most associated with drugs, sex, trance states, and transgression, and most of those associations are accurate, though they tell an incomplete story. The truth is more interesting: the Sahajiya understand that ordinary consciousness is a cage, and that liberation requires breaking the bars. They just disagree endlessly about the best tools for the job.

The Cult does not pursue pleasure for its own sake — though some of them certainly enjoy it. They pursue altered states as a pathway to perceiving reality without the filters that Consensus imposes. A mage in a carefully prepared entheogenic trance is not merely high; they are perceiving dimensions of reality that sober Consensus-consciousness cannot access. The danger is real: not all such perception is enlightening, and not all border-crossing leads somewhere worth going.

### Ecstatic Practices

#### Shadow Work

Named for Carl Jung's concept of the psychological shadow, this practice involves deliberate descent into the hidden, repressed, and feared elements of the human psyche — both internal and social. Internally, the practitioner confronts trauma-scars, suppressed impulses, and the parts of themselves they have refused to acknowledge. Externally, shadow work means engaging with the social shadow: the things that cultures prohibit, deny, and project onto marginalized others.

Shadow work magic is uncomfortable by design. It gains power precisely from its willingness to engage what others flee. A shadow-work practitioner does not need permission from consensus reality because shadow work operates in the spaces consensus reality refuses to see. Associated Paradigms include antinomian frameworks, chaos magic approaches, and the Aghori tradition of Hindu transgression mysticism.

Common instruments for shadow work include: artwork (especially dark, disturbing, or subversive art), blood and bodily fluids, body modification, psychoactive substances, eye contact (the "evil eye"), taboo-breaking behavior, group ordeals, meditation on feared subjects, social transgression, and—in its most extreme forms—acts that most practitioners consider unconscionable. Storyguides should use their judgment about how far shadow work instruments are taken, with Nephandi serving as the warning of where that path leads when taken without compassion or wisdom.

#### Tantra

Tantra is far older and more complex than its popular Western reputation suggests. In its classical form, Tantra is a framework for uniting the practitioner with the sacred truth of creation through intensive discipline — both the austerities of the Right-Hand Path and the sensual engagement of the Left-Hand Path. The Vamamarga (Left-Hand) practice is most popular among Sahajiya, who appreciate its integration of pleasure, sensation, and sacred experience.

Western Tantra — the "bag of sexual tricks" that has proliferated in New Age circles — is also a valid (if considerably simplified) version of the practice for Mage purposes. A Tantric practitioner uses physical asanas, shared energy work with partners, invocations of Shiva and Shakti, study of Tantric sutras, and a deep appreciation of sensory experience as a form of prayer. Sensuality, in Vamamarga, becomes sacrament.

Common instruments for Tantra: physical postures and breathwork, shared sensation with consenting partners, ritual invocations, food and drink (including foods prohibited in orthodox practice), meditation on sensation, written sutras and yantras, and—in extreme Aghori practice—deliberate violation of taboos.

### Special Instruments

#### Entheogens

An entheogen (from the Greek "to bring the god within you") is a psychoactive substance used in a specifically ritual context with sacred intention, preparation, and structure. This is not simply recreational drug use — it is a vision quest in chemical form. The substance itself is prepared or selected with care; the practitioner purifies themselves beforehand; the setting is established with intention; and the experience is processed and integrated afterward.

SPU Mechanics: Entheogens used as a magical instrument provide +1 Enhancement to effects involving perception, time-sense, or accessing hidden information (Time, Mind, Spirit effects particularly). However, the practitioner is Impaired for the duration (+1 Difficulty to all purely physical actions requiring coordination or reaction). The GM may add complications based on the specific substance and the mage's preparation quality.

#### Sigils

Chaos Magic sigils — popularized by artist-occultist Austin Osman Spare — create custom magical entities through a specific process: the practitioner formulates their intention as a sentence, reduces it to essential characters, rearranges those characters into an abstract symbol, invests personal energy through intense meditation, and releases the sigil into the world. Where classical Hermetic sigils invoke existing magical entities, a Spare-style sigil creates a new one to suit the specific purpose.

SPU Mechanics: Creating a sigil requires at least 10 minutes of focused work (the roll is Wits + Art or Expression, Difficulty 1). The resulting sigil functions as a one-use stored magical effect that activates when encountered by the intended target or when a trigger condition is met. An Ecstatic with Time 2+ can compress sigil creation to a single combat action by spending 2 Momentum, trading preparation time for intensity.

## The Dreamspeakers (Kha'vadi)

The Dreamspeakers are the Western Occident's deeply inadequate label for an enormous diversity of indigenous, animist, and shamanic traditions from across the globe. The name itself is a Western imposition — these practitioners include the Kha'vadi, the Bata'a, the Madzimbabwe, the Ngoma, the Akinkanju, and dozens of other distinct cultural groups who each have their own terminology, cosmology, history, and practice. What unites them is not a shared doctrine but a shared cosmological recognition: that the world is alive, that spirits are real, and that humans exist in relationship to forces far greater than themselves.

The Dreamspeakers sit uneasily on the Council, aware that the Traditions' structures and assumptions were built around European and Asian magical paradigms. Their presence on the Council was originally political — an alliance of convenience that has gradually, imperfectly, become something more genuine. In the current era, younger Kha'vadi in particular are actively engaging with the Council's decision-making structures, insisting that the Dreamspeaker seat represent them on their own terms.

### Paradigm

The Dreamspeakers hold that all reality is alive and relational. Every stone, river, tree, and storm has a spirit. Every ancestor who has passed is still present in some form. Every action reverberates through the web of relationships connecting all beings. Magic is not the imposition of will on an inert universe — it is the negotiation of relationships with a universe that has its own will, memory, and purpose.

Most Dreamspeaker practices involve forming and maintaining relationships with specific spirits, ancestral presences, and place-spirits. A Dreamspeaker who attempts to command a spirit without first establishing relationship will generally fail — and may offend a being powerful enough to cause serious problems. Respect, reciprocity, and ongoing relationship maintenance are as much "practice" as any ritual working.

### Rotes

#### Bullet-Rider's Blessing (Entropy 1 / Matter 3 / Spirit 3)

Through sustained relationship with specific technology-spirits, a Dreamspeaker can negotiate compact arrangements that cause specific technologies to simply not function against them. Sister Moriah, one of the most skilled practitioners of this rote, makes deals with spirits of specific technological categories — bullets, elevators, cameras — that cause those technologies to fail, miss, or simply decline to operate in her presence.

Mechanics: The mage must first establish an ongoing compact with a specific technology-spirit (requiring a separate Spirit 2 working and a minimum of one scene of negotiation and offering). Once established, the compact persists for one story or until dramatically broken. During that time, the specified technology category has no effect on the mage — bullets veer around her, cameras fail to capture her image, the elevator simply does not move while she stands in it. This cannot affect supernaturally enhanced technology. The compact must be maintained through periodic renewal offerings (once per week of game time).

#### Dwennimmen — The Ram's Horns (Mind 4 / Spirit 4)

This powerful rote, unique to the Akinkanju sect, invokes the adinkra symbol of the Ram's Horns tattooed on the practitioner's body as a portal through which a specific Umbrood spirit — The Voices, a denizen of the Well of Souls — temporarily inhabits the sacred symbols. The practitioner becomes a living fetish-spirit, possessed by ancient wisdom and power.

Mechanics: Activation requires the practitioner to spend 2 Momentum and roll Resolve + Occult at Difficulty 3. On success, for a number of scenes equal to the hits scored, the practitioner gains: immunity to wound penalties (all negatives from health track conditions are suppressed), +3 dice to resist mental coercion (attackers must first overcome this before any other resistance rolls), and regains 1 spent Willpower point at the start of each round. However, the practitioner cannot access their Sphere magic while in this state — previously established effects continue but no new castings are possible. Ending the state prematurely costs 5 Willpower points as the spirit tears itself free of the host.

## The Euthanatos (Chakravanti)

The Euthanatos — formally the Chakravanti, or "those who turn the Wheel" — are the most misunderstood Tradition on the Council, and often the most misunderstood by themselves. They are a Tradition of death-workers: people who have dedicated their lives to ensuring that the Great Wheel of existence turns properly, that the dying pass cleanly into their next stage, that those whose threads have run their full measure are cut cleanly, and that those who hoard Quintessence past their natural span are released. They are executioners, hospice workers, psychopomps, and grief-counselors, often simultaneously.

The Euthanatos acknowledge that their role carries enormous potential for corruption — and they have the Himalayan War as their perpetual reminder of what happens when death-workers lose compassion. The Chodona (their code of compassionate action) and the jhor (the spiritual taint that accumulates from killing without proper intention) exist precisely because the Tradition knows its own darkness. A Chakravanti who kills is supposed to be killing something that needs to end, and they are supposed to grieve for it. Those who stop grieving are in danger.

### Rotes

#### Blood for the Ghosts (Entropy 2 / Prime 2 / Spirit 2)

Ghosts and spirits of the honored dead — orisha, ancestors, guides — can be called across the barrier between worlds with the offering of something precious. In the classical Greek tradition, the substance used was blood; in Celtic practice, it might be mead or sacred objects; in African traditions, specific foods and libations. The Euthanatos use whatever offering carries genuine Quintessential weight and personal meaning.

Mechanics: The mage performs the ritual offering (at least 1 scene of preparation and presentation) and rolls Presence + Occult against Difficulty 2. Each hit calls one willing ghost or spirit to the threshold of the material world. With 3+ hits, the spirit can fully manifest in the material world for a scene. Spirits called in this way are not bound — they are invited. They give information freely and may sometimes act on behalf of the caller, but they cannot be compelled. A Complication means a less friendly spirit answers the call instead.

#### The Last Sacrifice (Entropy 4 / Spirit 2)

When the Euthanatos kills someone, proper tradition demands that the killing is followed by rites ensuring the spirit moves on cleanly — without becoming a ghost, a wraith, or an unquiet presence haunting the living. This is considered an act of profound respect: a gift to the departing soul, ensuring they do not linger in a state of confusion and pain between worlds.

Mechanics: Performed over the body of a recently deceased person (within a scene of death). The mage rolls Presence + Occult against Difficulty 2. With 2+ hits, the spirit of the deceased passes completely to whatever lies beyond — they cannot return as a ghost, cannot be summoned by Spirit magic, and any lingering emotional impressions they left in the material world (haunting phenomena) dissipate within 24 hours. This is considered deeply kind by the spirit traditions that recognize it — Dreamspeakers and Chorister practitioners in particular respect this rote even as they disagree on its precise metaphysical mechanics.

### Supernatural Flaws

#### Scarred Avatar (2 or 4 Point Flaw)

The Himalayan War left marks not just on the Tradition's history but on the Avatars of those who participated in it — and those Avatars have been reincarnating ever since. A mage with this Flaw carries an Avatar that was traumatized by the War, and that trauma manifests as an intense negative reaction to specific triggering circumstances.

Two-Point Version (••): The trigger is uncommon (a specific type of weather, a particular kind of person, the presence of dead bodies) and the reaction is moderate. When in the vicinity of the trigger, the mage must spend 1 Primal Energy point to cast any magical effect. This Primal Energy expenditure does not affect the roll.

Four-Point Version (••••): The trigger is common (a specific time of day, the presence of fire, a common physical characteristic in others) and the reaction is severe. When in the vicinity of the trigger, the mage must spend 1 Primal Energy and 1 Willpower to cast any magical effect. Neither expenditure affects the roll.

This Flaw reflects deep psychological and spiritual wounding. Storyguides should ensure the trigger creates genuinely dramatic situations rather than simply penalizing the player.

### Wonders

```
Antaratma (••• Wonder)
----------------------
Background Cost: 8 | Primal Pool: 10 | Enlightenment Required: 2

Meaning "spirit inside" or "conscience" in Hindi, an Antaratma is a talisman hand-crafted by a Chakravanti Acarya (mentor) for a specific student, bearing recordings of key precepts in the Acarya's own words — tailored to the recipient's particular spiritual needs and challenges. Some Euthanatoi wear the Antaratma as a tattoo inked with magical substances; technologically-oriented mentors might encode it as an app on the student's phone that plays the Acarya's recorded voice at moments of crisis.

SPU Effect: The Antaratma's primary function is resistance to Quiet and Paradox-induced psychological states. When a Paradox discharge, a Complication, or any magical effect would push the mage into a stage of the Quiet Track, the Antaratma activates automatically. Roll the device's Enlightenment (3 dice) against Difficulty 1. Each hit reduces the severity of the Quiet progression by one stage. Two hits can reduce a Stage 2 Quiet entry to Stage 1; three hits can negate a Stage 1 entry entirely. The Antaratma holds 10 Primal Energy and expends 2 per activation.
```

## The Order of Hermes

The Order of Hermes is the most explicitly institutional of the Traditions — a body of mages who have organized their magical practice around a system of formal ranks, Houses, specialized studies, and elaborate ritual structures stretching back through medieval Europe to ancient Egypt, Greece, and the Hermetic philosophical tradition. They are not the oldest mages in the world, but they are among the most systematically organized, and they have done more than any other group to create the shared vocabulary (Arete, Quintessence, Paradox, Sphere) that the entire Council uses.

The Order is also experiencing an identity crisis in the modern era. Their historical dominance of the Council's power structures has eroded, the loss of the Horizon Realms devastated many Houses, and the traditional Apprenticeship/Journeyman/Master hierarchy feels increasingly outdated. Younger Hermetics are pushing for reform; older Masters are resisting. The debates are vigorous, sometimes literally so.

### Wonders

```
Game of Senet (•• Wonder)
-------------------------
Background Cost: 2 | Primal Pool: N/A

An ancient Egyptian board game, originally played to determine fate and communicate with the dead. A Hermetic's Game of Senet is always the original article — handmade from ebony and ivory, with counters carved from alabaster and obsidian. The game has been charged with Time and Entropy energy over centuries of use.

SPU Effect: When two mages play a game of Senet, both players roll Wits + Subterfuge (this is a game of strategy and reading your opponent). The winner of the game may ask one question about a future probability within the next lunar month, receiving a vision-response rather than a direct answer. The vision is always accurate but always symbolic — interpreting it correctly requires a subsequent Intellect + Occult roll at Difficulty 2. If either player cheats, the game answers with a false vision and both players add 1 die to their Paradox Pools.
```

```
Imphepho Wierook (••• Wonder)
-----------------------------
Background Cost: 3 | Primal Pool: 15

A sacred incense blend originating in African Hermetic practice, combining Imphepho (Helichrysum odoratissimum) with other botanicals charged with Prime and Spirit energy. When burned, the smoke creates a boundary-thinning effect that makes spirit communication significantly easier.

SPU Effect: When burned in a ritual space (a slow-burning bundle lasting one scene), the Imphepho reduces the Difficulty of all Spirit-based magical workings by 1, to a minimum of Difficulty 0. Spirits in the immediate area can be perceived by all present (not just mages) as faint impressions, sounds, and temperature changes. Each use expends 3 Primal Energy from the bundle. Once depleted, the remaining ash can be used as a one-time instant-activation reagent for a single Spirit effect, adding +2 dice to the roll.
```

```
Waidan Ding (••••• Wonder)
--------------------------
Background Cost: 5 | Primal Pool: 10

A Chinese Taoist alchemical cauldron, brass-fitted and ancient, charged with a combination of Matter, Prime, and Life energies through centuries of alchemical practice. The cauldron is used for the ancient practice of Waidan (outer alchemy) — literally transmuting materials to produce substances of spiritual power.

SPU Effect: When used in an hour-long alchemical working, the Waidan Ding can produce one of the following: a Tass item with 3 Primal Energy; a restorative substance that heals 2 health track boxes when consumed (Life 3 effect); or an alchemical substance that, when ingested, grants +1 die to a specific Sphere for one scene. The mage rolls Intellect + Science (or Craft) at Difficulty 2; each hit beyond the minimum improves the product (more Tass, more healing, longer effect duration). The cauldron expends 1 Primal Energy per use.
```

```
Dümerang Blade — Device (•• to •••• )
-------------------------------------
Background Cost: 4-12 | Primal Pool: N/A | Enlightenment: 1+

Forged from Etheric alloy extracted from the Hollow Earth, these blades defy conventional metallurgy and conventional probability. They hum with harmonic resonance, return to their wielder's hand when thrown or dropped (bouncing off walls and obstacles in ways that should be impossible), and cannot be damaged by mundane means. Available in any blade configuration, they are often tricked out with additional components — telescoping handles for optics, tesla coil reactors, lithium calipers.

SPU Effect: Dümerang Blades deal Aggravated damage rather than Lethal. When thrown, they automatically return to the thrower's hand at the end of the round (no roll needed; this is a Forces 2 permanent effect). They cannot be broken by mundane force (they treat mundane damage as Enhancement 0 with no additional dice). Additional magical or technological Enhancements can be added normally. Higher-rated versions (•••, ••••) represent more elaborate customization with additional built-in capabilities (expanded optics, additional elemental Enhancement, etc.).
```

```
Kitab al-Alacir (••••• Wonder)
------------------------------
Background Cost: N/A | Primal Pool: N/A

A pre-Socratic grimoire, the Book of Ether predates most Hermetic scholarship and is used to trigger Awakening in genuinely promising intellectuals. Multiple translations exist — from the standard Gregorian to the Haitian Liv Eter (which adds Matter and Spirit effects) to a Kikongo translation that amplifies Avatar potency. The text is among the most treasured in the Order.

SPU Effect: After sustained study (a minimum of one story arc), the Kitab can trigger Awakening in a Sleeper reader who has demonstrated genuine intellectual and spiritual readiness. On success (Intellect + Academics at Difficulty 4), the reader Awakens and begins play at Enlightenment 1. Different translations provide different additional gifts — the Haitian translation's users begin with an affinity for Matter; the Kikongo translation's users begin with Avatar at one dot higher than they would otherwise have. The Kitab cannot Awaken someone against their will or before they are ready.
```

```
The Last Caern — Device (•••••)
-------------------------------
Background Cost: N/A | Primal Pool: 25 | Enlightenment Required: 5

A twenty-six-foot tanker truck containing waters from Ireland's River Barrow where the infant god Mechi was slain — his essence boiling the river in an ancient purifying rite. The Last Caern contains the spirit of a dead triple serpent-hearted deity, preserved in trust for a specific apocalyptic purpose. The vehicle is Edwardian in its enormous brass fittings, armored plating, and crystalline reservoir. It can only be found in the Umbra.

SPU Effect: The truck functions as a heavily armored vehicle (Structure 8, Armor 5). The ignition requires a sword of three parts (pommel, blade, handle) assembled and inserted — Leland deliberately scattered these components when he hid the vehicle. Once the sword is inserted and rotated, a countdown begins (duration unknown — "nobody wrote down the timer"). When it detonates, the vehicle unleashes a Prime 5 / Entropy 5 / Forces 5 effect equivalent to a limited tactical nuclear detonation, simultaneously releasing Mechi from the reservoir. Mechi's disposition and goals are unknown. Storyguide discretion is essential — this is a world-changing event, not a combat tool.
```

## The Sons of Ether (Etherites)

The Sons of Ether are the Tradition that never quite should have been — a group of mad scientists, weird theorists, and visionary engineers who defected from the Technocracy in the 1930s, taking their belief in the luminiferous Ether and their passion for impossible science with them. They have spent the subsequent decades proving that consensus-defying science is valid if you're willing to pay the cost, and that the best science is the kind that makes people say "that's impossible" right up until it demonstrably isn't.

The Etherites are intensely individualistic. Where other Traditions share paradigms, the Sons share a methodology: passionate inquiry, elaborate apparatus, spectacular failure, and the occasional catastrophic success. Two Etherites can hold diametrically opposed theories about the nature of reality and still collaborate happily, because the process matters more than the conclusion. Discovery is the point. The universe is gloriously weird and largely unknown, and the Etherites would like to map all of it.

### Paradigm

Etherite paradigms vary enormously between individual practitioners, but they share certain structural features: the universe operates according to discoverable, if bizarre, principles; those principles can be formalized into reproducible technology; the luminiferous Ether is real and is the medium through which all magical and most physical forces propagate; and the difference between "magic" and "science" is merely consensus — the Etherites know that their Science is as rigorous as anything the Technocracy produces, just differently oriented.

Etherite Wonders always look like technology. Gear, gizmos, elaborate apparatus, analog computing machinery, steam-powered devices, crystalline reactors, sonic emitters, atomic disassemblers — the aesthetic is whatever era the individual Etherite finds most inspiring, since the Ether doesn't care what century you're pretending to build for.

### Wonders

```
Rod Logic Computer (•• Wonder)
------------------------------
Background Cost: 3 | Primal Pool: 5-10

A hyperadvanced mechanical computer combining Etherite component design with Etheric materials science. Internal components include diamond lattices, organoplastic computation membranes, and ultrahard ceramic buffers — it does mechanically what most computers do electronically. Results display via a micromechanical color screen or a tactile patch on the user's skin. The Rod Logic Computer does not set off metal detectors, is immune to electromagnetic interference, and runs entirely on kinetic energy from being handled.

SPU Effect: Functions as a Focus equivalent — any Etherite who possesses one treats it as their primary instrument for Mind, Correspondence, and Data Sphere effects, gaining +1 die to rolls involving calculation, analysis, spatial mapping, or data retrieval. Structure 5, Armor 2 (advanced materials, compact design). The computer can run several pre-loaded programs simultaneously, each functioning as a stored one-use magical procedure at a specific Sphere level.
```

### Rotes

#### Locate Geospatial Nodal Point (Correspondence 3 / Prime 2)

A practical surveying technique for identifying Nodes — points where Primal Energy concentrations exist in the physical world — through careful measurement of etheric flux densities. The Etherite sets up a series of instruments around the suspected area and performs calibrated readings, identifying the Node's location, size, and approximate strength.

Mechanics: The mage rolls Intellect + Science against Difficulty 2 (Difficulty 3 for concealed or artificially suppressed Nodes). Each hit provides one detail about a Node within sensing range: location, size, current energy level, dominant resonance, or whether it's contested. This is explicitly a scientific procedure — the Etherite is performing measurements, not performing mysticism, and any instruments they're using are part of the effect.

## The Verbena

The Verbena are the Old Ways made flesh: witches, druids, blood-workers, and life-weavers who trace their practices to the pre-Roman shamanic traditions of Europe and beyond.
