Effect: Entropy 5/Life 3/Prime 3. Deception is automatic against all mundane examination and most supernatural methods. Only necromantic capability (Occult 4+ with specialization, or dedicated Life/Spirit effect) detects the ruse. The Field absorbs disposal attempts that would be permanently lethal: dissolving in acid, dismemberment, immolation.
[12-Hour Window] — After 12 hours, the operative returns to full function. She may be in a very difficult physical situation and will need to extract herself.
[Side Effect] — Operatives consistently report vivid hallucinations while under the Field: perceived interaction with spirits of the dead, distorted perception of surroundings. Currently classified as a design flaw under investigation.
```

```
Quantum State Ion Weapon, Mark I (QuStion Mark) — Background Cost 4, Enlightenment 5, Primal Energy 12
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Small hand-cannon that disrupts matter at a quantum level, superimposing multiple possible states and then collapsing them catastrophically.
Standard shot (1 Primal Energy): Prime 3 effect. Attack with Dexterity + Energy Weapons, Difficulty 6. 2 Aggravated damage per hit. Coincidental anywhere energy weapons are part of the Consensus.
Supercharged shot (3 additional Primal Energy): Prime 5 or Time 5 effect. Difficulty 9, 3 hits required. On success, removes the target from physical existence entirely. Whether removal is permanent is currently unknown.
[MMDS Origin] — This Device carries the standard MMDS prototype Paradox risk (see MMDS Prototype Rules below).
```

### MMDS Prototype Technology

Devices developed at the Multinational Military Development Site (Northern Territory, Australia) are not ready for field use. Most operate outside the Front Lines' Consensus tolerance. They are unstable in controlled laboratory conditions. In the field, they go wrong.

```
MMDS Prototype Rules
--------------------
Every MMDS prototype generates a permanent passive Paradox cost equal to half its Enlightenment rating (round up) in Paradox dice while in the operative's possession.
Paradox backlash checks triggered while carrying an MMDS prototype use Difficulty 5 instead of the standard Difficulty 6.
The Storyguide should treat MMDS prototypes as narrative time bombs. They are powerful. They are also looking for a reason to fail catastrophically.
```

```
Paradox Sink — Background Cost 10+, No Enlightenment (Implant)
--------------------------------------------------------------
A cybernetic implant or biomod that stores Paradox in an isolated Pattern-node rather than allowing it to accumulate in the operative's main Paradox pool. Prototype technology. Field testing incomplete.
Effect: Correspondence 2/Prime 5. Stores up to 10 Paradox (plus 2 per additional Background dot spent). While the sink holds Paradox, that Paradox cannot trigger backlash. Once full, the next Paradox accumulation causes the module to overheat: generates 5 additional Paradox and immediately backlashes.
Permanent side effect: Unsightly skin infections around the implant site. Spread at 1 Lethal damage per day of use. No operative has field-tested a Paradox Sink for more than 48 hours. Removal surgery triggers immediate backlash of all stored Paradox.
```

```
Surveillance Nanite Colony — Background Cost 6, Enlightenment 3, Primal Energy 15
------------------------------------------------------------------------
A single vial of nanites that fills a 200-meter-square area within 2 minutes of release. Infects everyone in the crowd. As a Trinket, one-use. As a full Device, reassembles over 72 hours after recall.
Effect: Correspondence 2. The colony relays all movement and speech from infected individuals back to the operative's receiver in real time.
Detection and removal: Occult 3+ to detect. Intelligence + Medicine to remove. Without removal, the colony disperses at the end of 72 hours with no ill effects to the host.
```

### Node Denial Device

The NDD is not available for requisition. Each major Union Construct has one, concealed from most of its own personnel. It is a last-resort scorched-earth system designed to permanently deny a Node to enemy forces by detonating it.

```
Node Denial Device — Not Available for Requisition, Enlightenment 8
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Attachment requires an extended action: Spirit 3/Forces 5/Matter 5/Prime 5, Difficulty 8, minimum 10 hits total.
Activation (safety systems): An extended activation roll at Difficulty 8, minimum 3 hits. The operator sets detonation time within the next hour.
Detonation effect: Forces 5/Matter 5/Prime 5 detonation equal to a small nuclear device, centered on the Node. There is no roll to survive within the blast radius. The Node is permanently destroyed.
One confirmed NDD is in the hands of a radical Templar Knights cell. The Union considers this a significant threat classification.
```

### Construct and Chantry Creation

The Operative's Dossier expands the Chantry/Construct creation system from M20, providing a point-based framework for building fully customized Constructs and Chantries in SPU chronicles. This system integrates with the Chantries rules in Chapter Ten. Point pool sizes are determined by the highest Chantry/Construct Background rating in the player group.

```
Construct/Chantry Rating by Point Pool
--------------------------------------
● (1 dot) — 10-20 points. Secure Squat or Safehouse: minimal protection, one or two reliable rooms.
●● (2 dots) — 21-30 points. Small Sanctuary or Auxiliary Office: modest established space, basic resources.
●●● (3 dots) — 30-70 points. Mystic Chantry or Enlightened Construct: full operational base, Node access possible.
●●●● (4 dots) — 71-100 points. Stronghold: well-defended, multiple departments or wings, significant resources.
●●●●● (5 dots) — 101+ points. Power Center: major factional presence, institutional authority, extensive reach.
```

No individual trait may be rated higher than twice the Construct's overall rating. Use the following table to allocate points. Access to communal resources requires a Presence + Status roll; Difficulty ranges from 4 (routine request from a member in good standing) to 9 (resource-intensive request from a member who has created problems for leadership).

```
Construct/Chantry Trait Costs
-----------------------------
Allies — 2 pts per dot: One exceptional ally per dot (Consors, Extraordinary Citizens, spirits, etc.)
Arcane/Cloaking — 2 pts per dot: -1 Difficulty per dot on rolls to find or investigate the Construct.
Backup — 2 pts per dot: Additional unawakened personnel available for support on call.
Cult/Sympathizers — 2 pts per dot: Personnel trained for ritual support or large-scale collaborative Procedures.
Enhancement — 4 pts per dot: Maximum Enhancement level the facility can install and maintain.
Elders/Management — 2 pts per dot: Power level and number of senior leadership figures.
Integrated Effects — 2 pts per dot: Anchored spells or effects permanently woven into the Construct's structure.
Library — 2 pts per dot: Communal research resource accessible to all members (functions as Library Background).
Node — 3 pts per dot: Weekly Quintessence surplus after Construct maintenance costs, available to members.
Reality Zone — 5 pts per dot: Strength of the local Reality Zone anchored to the Construct. Cannot exceed Construct rating.
Requisitions — 4 pts per dot: Maximum Requisition level that can be processed on-site without outside sourcing.
Resources — 3 pts per dot: Ready cash and liquid assets available to the Construct leadership for distribution.
Retainers — 2 pts per dot: Day-to-day operations staff (cooking, cleaning, logistics). Non-combatants.
Spies — 2 pts per dot: Informant network as per the Spies Background.
```

A Laboratory (separate Background) covers up to 500 square feet of personal working space. A Construct is a living institutional facility — larger, more complex, with communal resources and resident personnel. A high-rated Laboratory provides personal advantage; a high-rated Construct provides institutional advantage. Many serious Awakened characters maintain both.

Technocrat Personal Constructs: M20 standard rules suggest Technocrats cannot own personal Constructs, as the Union distrusts individual resource accumulation. Optional rule: A Technocrat in good standing may purchase Construct dots up to their Status Background dots. The Construct remains institutionally subject to review and revocation.

## Technocracy Reloaded — Advanced Devices, Bioware, and Wonders

The following material is drawn from Technocracy Reloaded and M20 Appendix II, covering the full catalog of mass-produced Gadgets, category-organized Technocratic Gear, cyberware and biomods, and the broader Wonder catalog available to Union operatives. All content has been converted to Storypath Ultra terminology. Items marked Enlightenment N/A can be operated by trained Extraordinary Citizens without Awakened ability. All Procedures listed are Blatant unless labeled as Adjustments.

### Section One: Additional Procedures

These Procedures supplement those listed under each Convention above and in the Operative's Dossier section. Adjustments are coincidental-level applications; Blatant Procedures carry Paradox risk and should be reserved for secured facilities or absolute necessity.

```
The Master's Edge (Mind 2; optionally +Entropy 1 or +Entropy 2)
---------------------------------------------------------------
Type: Adjustment (coincidental)  |  Enlightenment: 2+
Application: Social manipulation through maximized nonverbal influence.
SYSTEM: Roll Enlightenment vs. the target's Resolve (or Difficulty 4, whichever is higher). Target rolls Resolve vs. Difficulty 6 to resist; each hit cancels one of the operative's hits. Remaining hits reduce the Difficulty of social rolls using the targeted emotional state by 1 per hit (maximum -3). Fear applied: reduces Difficulty of Cunning + Leadership (Intimidation) rolls. Confidence applied: reduces Difficulty of Presence + appropriate social Skill. With Entropy 1 added: each hit on Enlightenment roll raises Difficulty of target's resistance roll by +1 (max +3). With Entropy 2 added: operative can split hits between Difficulty reduction for herself and Difficulty increase for target's resistance rolls.
```

```
All Triggers Locked (Forces 2 / Matter 2)
-----------------------------------------
Type: Procedure (vulgar)  |  Enlightenment: 2+
Application: Last-resort area suppression. Grabs all firearms in vicinity, fuses them into a single line, fires simultaneously.
SYSTEM: Requires at least 6 hits at Difficulty 7. Three turns of concentration: Turn 1 — grab all guns (Forces 2). Turn 2 — fuse into single line (Matter 2). Turn 3 — open fire. Effect: Fills surrounding area with gunfire for 3 turns. 7 dice lethal damage per turn to everyone within half-circle Blast Area 15. No aiming is possible. Wildly vulgar. Massive Paradox risk. For truly desperate situations only.
```

### Section Two: Gadgets

Gadgets are mass-produced, single-use or reusable items that employ Enlightened Science to achieve specific, predictable effects. Most can be operated by properly trained Extraordinary Citizens without requiring personal Enlightenment.

#### Two-Dot Gadgets

```
CLN-20 "Fun House Gas" — Chemical/Optical Disorientation
--------------------------------------------------------
Enlightenment 3  |  Primal Energy 10  |  Background Cost 2 pts. per grenade
Sphere Basis: Forces 2 (light refraction) + Entropy 2 (accident probability)
On detonation, refracts all light within 20 feet of burst point. ALL optical sensors in the area become entirely useless — human eyes, infrared, night vision, optical targeting systems. Effect persists 30-60 minutes unless dispersed by airflow. The Entropy 2 component significantly raises accident probability within the area. Does no direct damage. LIAMs are unaffected by CLN-20 optical distortion.
```

```
Spray Cams — Nanite Surveillance Film
-------------------------------------
Enlightenment 2  |  Primal Energy 10  |  Background Cost 4 pts.
Sphere Basis: Data 2
Functions as a standard spray-paint can. Coats surfaces with a nanite film containing millions of photosensitive and acoustically absorptive sensors. An entire wall becomes a continuous surveillance cluster that cannot be disrupted by destroying individual cameras. The cluster composites captured images into full 3D video with positional sound. Agent with the access code uses a Data 2 Adjustment to establish a Rank 1 Data connection to the location.
```

#### Three-Dot Gadgets

```
Bonding Facilitation Fluid — Molecular Fusion Agent
---------------------------------------------------
Enlightenment 3  |  Primal Energy 15  |  Background Cost 4 pts.
Sphere Basis: Matter 3 / Life 3
Applied to surfaces, the Fluid triggers a Matter 3/Life 3 Adjustment that fuses the molecular patterns of any two objects or creatures in contact. Can permanently bond two living beings, fuse an object to a surface, or merge disparate materials. The merger is irreversible without Matter/Life Procedures of equal or greater power.
```

```
Clear Cut — Monofilament Cutting Tool
-------------------------------------
Enlightenment 4  |  Primal Energy 10  |  Background Cost 4 pts.
Sphere Basis: Forces 2 / Matter 2
A nearly invisible filament deployed by tacking it to a surface. Activation generates a cutting edge capable of slicing through materials up to Hardness 6. Enlightenment roll vs. Difficulty 6; hits determine what materials the wire can cut (1-2 hits: soft materials; 3-4: standard metals; 5+: reinforced structures). Passive use as a tripwire: characters passing through unaware take Enhancement +3 lethal damage, Difficulty 7 Awareness roll to notice beforehand.
```

```
HIPSTer — Holographic Imaging Projection System Terrain-Reader
--------------------------------------------------------------
Enlightenment 3  |  Primal Energy 15  |  Background Cost 4 pts.
Sphere Basis: Data 2 / Forces 2
Provides two simultaneous capabilities: (1) All Attribute + Skill checks for surveillance, mapping, and target tracking gain +2 dice. (2) Provides real-time contextual environmental data, reducing Difficulty of tactical planning and coordination rolls by 1. Detects non-optical targets (thermal, sonic, RF signatures); unaffected by CLN-20 optical distortion. Transmits feed to other agents' ES-Phones or Construct terminals in real time.
```

```
LIAM — Limited Intelligent Autonomous Munition
----------------------------------------------
Enlightenment 3  |  Primal Energy 5  |  Background Cost 4 pts.
Sphere Basis: Forces 2/3 (mode-dependent)
Baseball-sized. Throw to deploy; internal thrusters guide it to the target area. Three activation modes:
MODE 1 — Timer (up to 30 sec): Concussive blast. Target makes Stamina roll Difficulty 7 or is stunned for 1 turn.
MODE 2 — Immediate force pulse: Non-lethal. Pushes all targets within 3m back 10 feet. Might + Athletics vs. Difficulty 6 to resist knockback.
MODE 3 — Immediate laser (Forces 3/Prime 2): Targeted lethal attack. Enlightenment roll vs. Difficulty 6; hits = lethal damage dice against single target.
```

```
Mightier Pen — Concealed Multi-Mode Weapon
------------------------------------------
Enlightenment 3  |  Primal Energy 5  |  Background Cost 4 pts.
Sphere Basis: Forces 2/3
Appears as a functional writing implement. Same three modes as LIAM. Passes routine inspection. Active only on deliberate activation gesture.
```

```
Pre-Designed Proficiency Packages (PDPPs) — Skill Boost Injectors
-----------------------------------------------------------------
Enlightenment 3-5  |  Primal Energy 5-25  |  Background Cost 3-12 pts. per use
Sphere Basis: Mind 3-5
Each PDPP provides a temporary boost to a specific Skill for the scene or session. Enlightenment 3 version: +1 die to designated Skill. Enlightenment 4 version: +2 dice. Enlightenment 5 version: +3 dice. Background Cost scales proportionally. Boosted knowledge fades at session end; agent retains no permanent skill improvement.
```

```
TMBL-WD — Tactical Matter/Energy Discharge
------------------------------------------
Enlightenment 5  |  Primal Energy 15  |  Background Cost 6 pts.
Sphere Basis: Forces 3 / Prime 2
Directed energy discharge. One-use per activation. Enlightenment roll vs. Difficulty 6; hits = lethal damage dice delivered to target. Each discharge costs 1 Primal Energy. The device recharges from a connected Primal Energy source (Construct terminal or SPECM module).
```

#### Four-Dot Gadgets

```
MP-34 Multipurpose Pacification Gas — Area Suppression Chemical
---------------------------------------------------------------
Enlightenment N/A  |  Primal Energy N/A  |  Background Cost 4 pts. per canister
Sphere Basis: Matter (crafting)
An Enlightened compound dispersed in enclosed spaces. All targets in affected area must make a Stamina roll (Difficulty 6) or fall unconscious for 1 hour. Each hit on the resistance roll reduces duration by 10 minutes. Agents with active Life 2/Prime 2 countermagick are unaffected. Does not affect supernatural beings immune to mundane chemical effects.
```

```
Zipper Ties — Self-Tightening Restraints
----------------------------------------
Enlightenment 4  |  Primal Energy 5  |  Background Cost 2 pts. per set
Sphere Basis: Matter 2
Matter 2 Adjustment embedded in polymer restraint bands. Tighten automatically when resistance is detected. Breaking requires Might roll Difficulty 9. Cutting requires a sharp tool (Artistry or Close Combat check). Once cut, the Zipper Ties are destroyed and cannot be reused.
```

### Section Three: Technocratic Gear by Category

#### Armor

```
Second Skin — Advanced Concealable Body Armor
---------------------------------------------
Enlightenment N/A  |  Primal Energy N/A  |  Background Cost 4 pts.
Armor Rating 2 vs. lethal damage; Rating 1 vs. aggravated. Flexible enough to wear under clothing; passes casual visual inspection. Does not impede movement or social rolls in normal conditions.
```

```
The Black Suit — Standard Operative Uniform
-------------------------------------------
Enlightenment N/A  |  Primal Energy N/A  |  Background Cost 6 pts.
Armor Rating 1 vs. lethal. RFID-blocking lining. Panic button transmits location to nearest Construct. The suit itself functions as an apparatus for Mind and Forces Adjustments. Reinforced seams rated for moderate physical stress.
```

```
Parkour Slippers — Adhesive Movement Enhancement
------------------------------------------------
Enlightenment N/A  |  Primal Energy N/A  |  Background Cost 6 pts.
Sphere Basis: Matter 3
Matter 3 Adjustments embedded in sole material provide gecko-grip adhesion to any surface. Wearer can move on vertical surfaces and ceilings at half normal movement speed. Reduces Difficulty of Athletics checks for wall-running, climbing, and precision movement by 2.
```

#### Weapons

```
LL/AP-15 "Finger Guns" — Concealed Micro-Weapon
-----------------------------------------------
Enlightenment N/A  |  Primal Energy N/A  |  Background Cost 6 pts.
Slips over one finger; completely concealable with no detection signature on standard scanners. Ranged attack: Enhancement +1 lethal, effective range 10 meters. Single-shot; reloading requires a prepared charge (1 turn).
```

```
Universal Neutralizer Device (UND) — Anti-Wonder Disruptor
----------------------------------------------------------
Enlightenment 4  |  Primal Energy 10  |  Background Cost 6 pts.
Sphere Basis: Prime 2 / Forces 2
Projects energy disrupting the Primal Energy powering Wonders and Devices. Line-of-sight range. Enlightenment roll vs. Difficulty 6; each hit deducts one hit from target Wonder's next activation roll. Wonders receive a soak roll (their Enlightenment rating vs. Difficulty 6) to resist disruption. Ineffective against mundane technology.
```

```
Variable Atmospheric Pharmacological Emitter (VAPE) — Chemical Delivery System
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Enlightenment 4  |  Primal Energy 20  |  Background Cost 6 pts.
Sphere Basis: Life 2 / Prime 2
Disperses a customizable pharmacological aerosol in a 10-meter radius cloud. Effect depends on loaded compound: Sedative — Targets make Stamina roll Difficulty 6 or fall unconscious for 1 hour. Hallucinogenic — As CLN-20 Fun House Gas but operates on the mind rather than optics. Stimulant — +1 die to Stamina and Might for target group for 1 hour. Swappable cartridges allow field reconfiguration between encounters.
```

```
NIMBY-50 — Dimensional Exile Weapon
-----------------------------------
Enlightenment 5  |  Primal Energy 25  |  Background Cost 13 pts.
Sphere Basis: Correspondence 4 / Dimensional Science 3
Fires at a target entity, sending it to a randomly determined non-Earth dimension. Enlightenment roll vs. Difficulty 7; hits = dimensional displacement force applied to target. Earth-native targets get Resolve + Esoterica vs. Difficulty 8 to resist. Specifically designed to be less effective against Earth-native targets. Highly effective against extra-dimensional entities of unknown origin. The target is not killed — it is displaced to a location guaranteed to be not Earth.
```

#### Surveillance and Intelligence

```
Operative Database (ODb) — Encrypted Intelligence Terminal

Forces 4 — Electric discharge; Enhancement +2 lethal against single target at close range. Mind 2 — Reduces Difficulty of observers' Awareness rolls to notice drone by -1.

### Section Four: Cyberware and Biotech

Cybernetic and biological enhancements modify a character's physical form. Each dot of the Enhancement Background requires either 1 permanent Paradox point or 1 Genetic Flaw (see Appendix V: Merits, Flaws, and Wonders — Genetic Flaws section). Non-Technocracy cybernetics automatically impose 1 Genetic Flaw per dot due to inferior quality control. Cyberware uses Enlightenment-based activation in most cases. Biomods are always-on passive systems requiring no activation roll. All enhancements impose the Construct social disadvantage at 3+ dots of Enhancement.

Biomods are biological modifications — genegineering, targeted viral agents, directed growth stimulation, and similar Life-based alterations. Unlike cyberware, biomods do not use Enlightenment or standard activation; they are always-on passive systems. Non-Technocracy biomods impose Genetic Flaws at Storyguide discretion.

### Section Six: Rules for Technocratic Equipment

The following rules govern how Technocratic Devices function within SPU mechanics.

#### Activation

Unless purchased as built-in Advantages, Devices require an activation roll. The player rolls the Device's Enlightenment rating as a dice pool. The Difficulty is typically 7, though the Storyguide may adjust for circumstances. The number of hits determines the effect's potency. Devices with Enlightenment N/A do not require an activation roll — they function automatically for any trained user.

#### Self-Contained Energy

Every cybernetic implant and Device has its own internal Primal Energy reserve equal to its listed Primal Energy value. Devices can recharge at Construct terminals (full recharge in 1 scene) or from portable SPECM modules (slower).

#### Paradox and Cyberware

Each dot in the Enhancement Background costs either 1 permanent Paradox point or 1 Genetic Flaw. Non-Technocracy cyberware automatically imposes 1 Genetic Flaw per dot regardless of the permanent Paradox option, reflecting the inferior quality of work outside the Union's laboratories. Biomod-related Paradox: mages with Enhancement 2+ accumulate 1 Paradox point per story session automatically. Genegineering-based mods impose Genetic Flaws (chronic ailments, behavioral tics, unusual allergies) rather than Paradox.

#### Extraction and Removal

Cybernetic enhancements are machines: they can be targeted magickally, may malfunction under extreme conditions, and can be forcibly removed. Extraction is a Life 3/Matter 3 Procedure or requires conventional surgery (Storyguide discretion). Removing an implant without proper medical support is agonizing and risks additional Genetic Flaws.

#### Non-Technocratic Cybernetics

Other factions (Society of Ether, Virtual Adepts, some Nephandi) produce their own cybernetic modifications. For every dot of Enhancement from non-Technocracy sources, the character suffers one automatic Genetic Flaw reflecting the instability of work outside Union protocols. Characters with mixed sources treat each source separately for this purpose.

---

Cross-references: Appendix S (Items and Gear) for additional Wonders and equipment stat blocks. Appendix F (The Nine Traditions) and Chapter 8 (Factions and the Social Game) for factional context. Chapter Ten (Chantries) for base Chantry rules that the Construct creation system above expands.

# Appendix H: The Phase Initiative System

## Design Overview

Standard initiative systems — roll a die, add a bonus, highest number goes first — work perfectly well, but they reduce the question of speed to a single moment at the start of a round. A lightning-fast martial artist and a lumbering giant might both roll well or poorly, and the system offers no mechanical way to express the fact that a truly fast combatant should not merely go slightly earlier, but should be able to act several times before a slow opponent draws breath.

The Phase Initiative System presented in this appendix replaces the standard initiative roll entirely. It uses a dice pool to generate Action Dice — one die for each potential action a character might take in a round — and the face value of each die determines which Phase of the round that action falls in. A character with a larger pool rolls more dice, which means more actions and, statistically, more actions that land in early Phases. A character who is genuinely faster than their opponent does not merely act first: they act repeatedly while their opponent struggles to respond.

This system also introduces Speed Factor, a rating that governs how many Action Dice an action consumes. Firing a pistol is fast; sustaining a full-auto burst across a panicked crowd is slow, devouring multiple dice and potentially carrying the action into the next round. Every choice has weight. Every action has a cost.

This appendix is optional and self-contained. The Storyguide and players should agree before a chronicle begins whether to use this system. It is best suited to chronicles where tactical positioning, weapon choice, and character speed are central to play, and where the players are comfortable with a slightly more mechanical combat structure in exchange for richer, more dynamic action sequences.

## Building Your Initiative Pool

At the start of each combat round, every combatant assembles their Initiative Pool. This pool represents the character's raw speed, perceptive awareness, and tactical instinct. Calculate it as the higher of these two options:

- Athletics + Cunning: physical speed and tactical reads — the warrior who moves before their brain catches up, the street fighter who reads body language as naturally as breathing.
- Empathy + Dexterity: perceptive quickness and fine motor precision — the telepath who feels the attacker's intention the moment it forms, the surgeon-duelist whose footwork is a dance.
Choose whichever combination yields the higher pool before rolling. You are not locked into one option from session to session — the higher pool always applies, and if a condition or Tag temporarily reduces one Attribute, recalculate.

Modifiers to your Initiative Pool apply before rolling. Common modifiers include:

- Heavy armor (full plate, ballistic vest with trauma plates): -1 to -2 dice. The weight and rigidity of serious protection slows your initial reaction.
- The Staggered condition: -1 die to Initiative Pool in addition to other Staggered penalties.
- The Blinded condition: -2 dice. Acting on incomplete information is profoundly disorienting.
- Alert or Prepared Tags from appropriate abilities: +1 or +2 dice. A character who prepared for this specific encounter has already begun moving.
- Surprise: A character who is completely unaware of the threat (not merely caught off guard, but genuinely blindsided) does not roll at all in the first round. They have no Action Dice and may not act until Round 2.
> EXAMPLE — BUILDING THE POOL
> 
> Nimara has Athletics 3, Cunning 3, Empathy 2, and Dexterity 4.
> Her Athletics+Cunning pool is 6. Her Empathy+Dexterity pool is 6. Equal — she uses 6.
> 
> She is wearing a light leather jacket (no penalty).
> She has the Quick-Draw Tag from her specialty, granting Enhancement 1 on initiative (handled in the Enhancement section).
> 
> She rolls 6 dice: results are 1, 2, 5, 5, 8, 9.
> Her Action Dice are at Phases 1, 2, 5, 5, 8, and 9 this round.
> She has two actions at Phase 5 — two dice landed on the same number.

## Reading Your Action Dice

Once you have rolled your Initiative Pool, every die result becomes an Action Die. The face value — from 1 to 10 — indicates which Phase of the round you may act in. You keep all dice; there is no target number, no success threshold, and no exploding dice on this roll. A 10 is simply Phase 10, the last phase of the round — not lucky, merely late.

This is the critical difference from most Storypath Ultra rolls: here, lower numbers are preferable. Acting in Phase 1 means you move before anyone else. Acting in Phase 10 means everyone else has already had their turn. Characters will sometimes wish they had rolled worse in the conventional sense — a pool full of 1s and 2s means acting early and often, which is exactly where you want to be.

Multiple dice showing the same number mean multiple actions in that Phase. Two dice at Phase 5 means you act twice when Phase 5 is called. This is unusual but not impossible, especially for characters with large pools who roll many dice.

There is no target number on this roll. Do not count hits (8+) — that mechanic applies to task resolution, not initiative. Every die result, from a 1 to a 10, is simply recorded as a Phase number. A 3 is Phase 3. A 7 is Phase 7. A result that would be a miss in combat is a perfectly good initiative die here.

### Trick Initiative — The Special Case of a 10

A result of 10 on an initiative die is a Trick Initiative die. Rather than being locked to Phase 10 (the last phase of the round, the least desirable position), a Trick Initiative die may be placed at any Phase the character chooses — declared freely after seeing all initiative results. The character effectively calls their shot: "I am acting at Phase 2 with this die," and the die takes that value for all purposes this round.

This represents exceptional speed, perception, or tactical instinct — the combatant who is so fast or so well-prepared that they can slot a reaction into exactly the moment they need. It is a meaningful advantage without importing the standard success-counting mechanic into initiative, keeping the two systems cleanly separate. If a character rolls multiple 10s, each may be placed independently at any Phase, including the same Phase as another die (producing two Trick Initiative actions at a chosen Phase).

> EXAMPLE — TRICK INITIATIVE
> 
> Vesper rolls her initiative pool and gets: 4, 6, 8, 10.
> The 10 is a Trick Initiative die. She can place it at any Phase she wishes.
> 
> She studies the other participants' dice (in a cooperative scene, all dice are
> revealed simultaneously). The biggest threat acts at Phase 3.
> 
> She places her Trick Initiative die at Phase 2 — moving her in just before that
> threat acts. Final dice: 2 (trick), 4, 6, 8.
> 
> Without the 10, she had nothing before Phase 4. The Trick Initiative
> bought her a critical early action at the exact moment she needed it.

#### Trick Initiative and Bank Tokens

A Trick Initiative die and a Bank Token are two distinct resources — one is a placed Phase die, the other is a valueless wildcard reserve — but they interact directly. When you roll a 10, you face a choice that must be made **at the start of the round, when initiative is declared**, before any Phases are called. The declaration is irrevocable once Phase 1 begins. Your three options:

- **Place it at any Phase 1–9.** The die joins your Phase lineup at the chosen position and functions as a normal Action Die from that point forward. A 10 placed at Phase 2 gives you an action at Phase 2 — as early as possible. A 10 placed at Phase 7 doubles your presence there. You choose based on where the round's action is likely to concentrate.
- **Convert it immediately into a Bank Token.** The 10 is not placed into any Phase; instead it becomes your Bank Token at the start of the round, giving you active defense readiness from Phase 1 onward — before you have had a single opportunity to bank a normal die mid-round. This token follows all standard Bank Token rules, including the same-phase restriction, carry cost, and Bank Limit.
- **Leave it at Phase 10.** The die acts as a normal Phase 10 action — the last possible Phase in the round. This is rarely the best choice, but it is valid. A character who wants a guaranteed final-Phase action (a finishing move, a dramatic last word, a reaction to the full shape of the round) may choose this deliberately.

Converting a 10 into a Bank Token this way follows all normal Bank Token rules: it counts against your Bank Limit (default 1), can be spent for offense or defense at any Phase from the current Phase forward, and can be carried to the next round at the standard pool −1 carry cost.

**If you roll multiple 10s**, you may split them freely between the two options. Two 10s: bank one (Bank Token) and place the other at Phase 2 (offensive timing). Three 10s with Combat Reserve (Bank Limit 2): bank two (both Bank Tokens) and place the third. You may not bank more 10s than your current Bank Limit allows — excess 10s beyond the limit must be placed as normal Phase dice.

The choice is made once, at initiative declaration, and is final. You cannot decide mid-round to convert a placed Trick die into a Bank Token, nor can you retroactively place a banked 10 back into the Phase sequence.

> WHY THIS MATTERS:
> 
> A normal Bank Token requires giving up a mid-round action — you bank when your Phase
> arrives, trading offense for defense readiness. A 10 converted to a Bank Token costs
> nothing from your Phase lineup. You kept every placed die AND arrived at Phase 1
> already holding a token.
> 
> This makes rolling 10s disproportionately valuable in the banking economy, which is
> intentional: exceptional reflexes or tactical instinct should translate into exceptional
> resource flexibility. The player who rolls two 10s faces a genuinely interesting decision
> — guaranteed early-round Bank Token vs. two perfectly-placed offensive actions — rather
> than an automatic "place both for maximum offense."
> 
> EXAMPLE — TRICK DIE CONVERTED TO BANK TOKEN:
> 
> Aldric rolls: 3, 5, 7, 10. He surveys the field: two opponents, both acting in Phase 4.
> He cannot act before Phase 3 normally, and Phase 3 is before either threat.
> 
> Option A: Place the 10 at Phase 2. He acts early and offensively, but arrives at Phase 4
> with no Bank Token — two attacks incoming and no active defense.
> 
> Option B: Convert the 10 to a Bank Token immediately. Final Phase dice: 3, 5, 7.
> He cannot act before the Phase 4 threats, but he has a Bank Token from the start.
> When the first Phase 4 attack arrives, he Parries (spends token). Riposte lands.
> The second Phase 4 attack hits undefended — but only one of two, and Aldric acted
> at Phase 3 before both of them.
> 
> The 10 bought him either a head start or a guaranteed defense. He chose the defense.
> A lesser roll would have given him no such option.

### Tiebreakers Within a Phase

When two or more characters have Action Dice in the same Phase, they must resolve who acts first. Apply the following tiebreakers in order:

- Pool size: The character who rolled the larger initiative pool acts first within the Phase. A character who rolled 6 dice acts before one who rolled 4 dice, even if both have a die showing Phase 3. This reflects the edge that superior speed and awareness provide even when two combatants react at nearly the same moment.
- Simultaneous: If the pool sizes are also equal, both characters act at exactly the same instant. Their actions are declared and resolved concurrently. Both attacks land (or miss) simultaneously. A sword swing that would have killed one fighter still connects even if that fighter's own attack was also killing their opponent — they die together, or both stagger back, or the exchange resolves in whatever dramatically appropriate way the Storyguide sees fit.

## The Phase Sequence

The round is divided into ten Phases, numbered 1 through 10. The Storyguide calls each Phase in ascending order: "Phase 1... Phase 2... Phase 3..." and so on. When your Phase number is called, you act — or you hold (see The Held Die, below). Phases with no actions simply pass without comment.

Within each Phase, the Storyguide calls on characters in pool-size order (largest pool first), resolving any simultaneous actions together. Once all actions in a Phase are resolved, the Storyguide calls the next Phase. This continues until Phase 10, at which point all Paradox dice accumulated during the round are rolled (if any), conditions are checked for duration, and a new round begins with a fresh initiative roll.

You do not roll new initiative dice between your actions within a round — your Action Dice for this round were all determined at the start. What you see at the beginning is everything you have to work with. This is what makes decisions to bank a die (reserving it for defense or a wildcard action) feel genuinely weighty: you are choosing flexibility over a guaranteed action, and the die you bank is a die you are not using offensively this round.

> EXAMPLE — READING THE PHASE SEQUENCE
> 
> The round has three participants:
>   Alaric:  Pool 5, dice at Phases 1, 3, 5, 7, 9
>   Viktor:  Pool 3, dice at Phases 2, 6, 9
>   Seraph:  Pool 4, dice at Phases 3, 5, 8, 10
> 
> Phase 1: Alaric acts. (Only Alaric has a die here.)
> Phase 2: Viktor acts.
> Phase 3: Both Alaric and Seraph act. Alaric (pool 5) goes before Seraph (pool 4).
> Phase 4: Empty. Nothing happens.
> Phase 5: Alaric acts, then Seraph acts.
> Phase 6: Viktor acts.
> Phase 7: Alaric acts.
> Phase 8: Seraph acts.
> Phase 9: Alaric and Viktor act simultaneously (pool 5 vs. pool 3 — Alaric goes first).
> Phase 10: Seraph acts. End of round: roll any accumulated Paradox dice.

## Enhancement and Initiative

Some abilities, Edges, specialties, or situational Tags grant Enhancement on initiative rolls. In most Storypath Ultra contexts, Enhancement adds automatic hits before you roll. In the Phase System, Enhancement works differently: each point of Enhancement allows you to shift one Action Die up to two Phases earlier (minimum Phase 1). You distribute Enhancement shifts freely after seeing your roll, before the round begins.

You can apply multiple Enhancement points to the same die (shifting it further back) or spread them across different dice (pushing several actions earlier). The only constraint is that no die may be shifted below Phase 1.

- Enhancement 1: Shift one die by up to 2 phases. A result of 7 becomes Phase 5. A result of 2 becomes Phase 1 (you cannot go to Phase 0 or below).
- Enhancement 2: Shift one die by up to 4 phases, OR shift two different dice by up to 2 phases each. A 9 becomes a 5, or a 7 becomes a 5 and an 8 becomes a 6.
- Enhancement 3: Shift one die by up to 6 phases (a 9 becomes Phase 3), OR one by 4 and one by 2, OR spread 2-phase shifts across three dice.
Enhancement on initiative represents genuine, exceptional speed — the martial artist who always finds the opening a heartbeat before anyone else reacts, the telepath who feels the attacker's intention the moment it crystallizes, the gunslinger whose draw is a blur that the eye cannot track. It is not merely being fast — it is being categorically faster.

> EXAMPLE — ENHANCEMENT ON INITIATIVE
> 
> Countess Vesper has Enhancement 2 on initiative from her Fencing Mastery Edge.
> She rolls 6 dice: 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9.
> 
> She decides to shift her 9 by 4 phases (to Phase 5) and her 7 by 2 phases (to Phase 5).
> Final Action Dice: 3, 5, 5, 5, 6, 8. She now has three actions at Phase 5.
> 
> Alternatively, she could shift her 8 by 4 (to Phase 4) and her 9 by 2 (to Phase 7):
> Final Action Dice: 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9. More spread, slightly earlier overall.
> 
> The choice is hers. She goes with the first option — three actions at Phase 5 is
> a devastating cluster if she can survive until then.

## The Held Die

Once per round, you may hold a single Action Die rather than use it when its Phase is called. To hold a die, physically move it to a corner of the table (separate from your active dice and your spent dice) when that Phase arrives. You may then trigger the held die at any point later in the round — between Phases, in direct response to another character's declared action, or at the start of any subsequent Phase you choose.

The held die retains its face value but is no longer bound to its original Phase. Its timing is now entirely in your hands. You choose the moment. This makes the held die one of the most powerful tactical tools in the Phase System — a reserved opportunity that can be deployed reactively, at the exact moment it will have the most impact.

Rules governing the held die:

- Only one held die at a time. You may never have two held dice simultaneously. If you wish to hold a new die while one is already held, you must first trigger or voluntarily discard the existing held die.
- Cannot pick up a replacement while holding. If one of your Phases arrives while you already have a held die, you may act normally (spending that die), use your existing held die (spending it and letting the current Phase die pass), or simply let the current Phase pass unused. You cannot hold the current die as a second held die.
- Expires at end of round. A held die not used by the end of Phase 10 is lost. The moment passed; the opportunity is gone.
- Cannot be spent for active defense. The Held Die is a timing tool for offensive or support actions, not a defensive reserve. Active defense requires a Bank Token (see Banking a Die, below). A character holding a die for precise timing and a character holding a Bank Token are doing two different things with two different resources.
- Triggers between phases. A held die can fire in the gap between Phase 4 ending and Phase 5 beginning — fast enough to intercept something that was just declared, before it resolves.
> EXAMPLE — THE HELD DIE
> 
> Kastor has action dice at Phases 3, 6, and 9. He uses Phase 3 to attack.
> 
> Phase 6 arrives. Instead of acting, Kastor holds his Phase 6 die — he moves it to the
> corner of the table. He has a suspicion the mage will try something in the next few phases.
> 
> Phase 7: The enemy mage (pool 4) begins casting a complex spell (SF 3), committing
> dice at Phases 7, 8, and 9. Kastor sees the casting begin.
> 
> Phase 8 is called. The mage is mid-cast, committed. Kastor triggers his held die
> immediately — attacking the mage to interrupt the spell. The attack lands.
> The mage must now choose: absorb the hit and continue casting, or abort the spell.
> 
> Without the held die, Kastor would have had to wait until Phase 9 to act.
> The held die bought him a full Phase of anticipation.

## Speed Factor

Not all actions require the same investment of time and effort. A quick jab with a knife is done in an instant. A sustained burst of automatic fire sweeping across a room, or the focused channeling of a complex magical working, demands sustained commitment — multiple moments of attention, physical effort, and concentration that lock a combatant into that action and prevent them from doing anything else.

Speed Factor (SF) is a rating assigned to every weapon, action type, and spell. SF is **inclusive of the declaring Phase**: when you declare an action at Phase T, it resolves at Phase T + SF − 1. An SF 1 action declared at Phase 3 resolves at Phase 3 — the same Phase, instantly. An SF 2 action declared at Phase 3 resolves at Phase 4 — the very next Phase. An SF 3 action declared at Phase 3 resolves at Phase 5.

Every Action Die that falls between Phase T and Phase T + SF − 1 (inclusive) is consumed by that action. Those dice are committed and cannot be used for anything else unless you choose to abort (see Aborting Actions, below).

The number of dice you actually lose depends on where your dice happened to land — not a flat count. The SF tells you exactly how many Phases wide the window is: SF 3 is a 3-Phase window, SF 5 is a 5-Phase window. A SF 3 action declared at Phase 2 resolves at Phase 4. Any of your dice at Phases 2, 3, or 4 are consumed. If your dice are at Phases 2, 6, 8, only the Phase 2 die is lost. If your dice are at Phases 2, 3, 4, all three are consumed. High initiative rolls can let you start an action and retain dice that a lower roll would lose.

> SF TIMING SUMMARY
> 
> SF is inclusive — it counts the current Phase as the first of the action's Phases.
>   SF 1 → resolves this Phase (instantaneous; zero Phase delay).
>   SF 2 → resolves next Phase.
>   SF 3 → resolves in 2 Phases.
>   SF N → resolves in N − 1 Phases after the declaring Phase.
> Formula: Resolve Phase = Declare Phase + SF − 1.
> Window: Declare Phase through Resolve Phase, inclusive. Width = SF Phases.
> 
> Example: SF 1, declare at Phase 3 → resolves at Phase 3. Window: {3}.
> Example: SF 2, declare at Phase 3 → resolves at Phase 4. Window: {3, 4}.
> Example: SF 5, declare at Phase 3 → resolves at Phase 7. Window: {3,4,5,6,7}.

> SF WINDOW EXAMPLE
> 
> Mara has Action Dice at Phases 1, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10.
> She casts a Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●● freeform spell.
> Freeform SF = sum of Sphere dots = 3+2 = SF 5.
> 
> She declares the spell at Phase 1.
> Resolve Phase = 1 + SF − 1 = 1 + 5 − 1 = Phase 5.
> Window: Phases 1 through 5 (inclusive). Width = 5 Phases.
> Action Dice in window: 1, 5. (Phases 2, 3, 4 have no dice, but they're in the window.)
> Those two dice are consumed. She retains Phases 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10.
> 
> Had she waited and declared at Phase 6, the spell would resolve at Phase 10 —
> just inside the round boundary, consuming her Phase 6, 7 dice.
> Declaring at Phase 1 here saved her three dice compared to a mid-round cast.

### The Cap Rule

A character's Speed Factor cost is capped at their total Initiative Pool size. A character cannot commit more dice — across this round and any cross-round overflow — than they rolled at the start of combat. Formally: total dice consumed by an action equals the minimum of SF and initiative pool size.

What this means practically: a character with an initiative pool of 2 can still fire a full-auto weapon (SF 3), but the cost is 2 dice, not 3. Their entire pool is consumed; they are done for the round. There is no cross-round debt when the cap triggers — the action simply costs everything they have, and it resolves at the final consumed die.

A character with a pool of 4 using a SF 3 weapon, however, pays 3 dice normally. The cap does not help them — their pool is large enough to absorb the full cost. Cross-round situations arise when a character with a pool large enough to absorb the SF begins the action late in the round, with fewer remaining dice than the SF requires. In those cases, the remainder carries to next round (see Cross-Round Actions).

> THE CAP RULE IN ACTION
> 
> Dmitri has an initiative pool of 2. He rolls dice at Phases 4 and 8.
> He wants to fire his assault rifle on full auto (SF 3).
> 
> Cap: min(SF 3, pool 2) = 2 total dice consumed.
> 
> Phase 4: First die committed. Full auto begins.
> Phase 8: Second (and final) die committed. Full auto resolves.
> Dmitri is done for the round — both dice are spent — but he got his burst off.
> No cross-round debt applies when the cap triggers.
> 
> If Dmitri had a pool of 4 and dice at Phases 2, 4, 8, 9:
> Full auto at Phase 4 costs 3 dice: Phases 4, 8, and 9 are committed.
> Phase 2 was already used (or is before his declaration). Phase 2 remains free if unused.

## Cross-Round Actions

When a character begins a high-SF action late in a round with fewer remaining Action Dice than the action's full cost (after applying the cap), the action crosses the round boundary. The committed dice from this round are consumed normally. The remaining SF cost carries into the next round, where it is satisfied by the first available Action Dice from the new round's initiative roll.

There is no pre-commitment of next round's dice. At the start of the next round, all combatants roll initiative fresh. When the Phase that satisfies the remaining SF cost arrives in the new round, that die is consumed — the action is ongoing, and this die represents the final moment of commitment. The effect of the action resolves at the start of that final Phase.

During all Phases between the start of the action and its resolution — whether within a single round or straddling two — the character is locked into the action. They cannot take other actions using committed dice, because those dice belong to the ongoing action. However, dice that have not yet been committed remain free. A character who begins full auto at Phase 7 with dice at Phases 7, 9, and one die from next round still has any dice earlier than Phase 7 (already spent) or after the commitment window free.

The resolution moment is always the start of the final committed Phase. Targets who are in the affected zone or area see the action building — they have had time to react, seek cover, or move — but when the final Phase arrives, the effect lands in full. The attack roll is made, the spell takes hold, the burst sweeps the room.

> EXAMPLE — CROSS-ROUND FULL AUTO
> 
> Elena has a pool of 3 and rolls dice at Phases 5, 7, and 9.
> She uses Phase 5 to move into a better position (SF 1). Phase 5 spent.
> 
> Phase 7: Elena declares full auto (SF 3). Pool is 3, so cap = min(3,3) = 3 dice total.
>   She has 2 remaining dice this round (Phases 7 and 9).
>   Phase 7 die committed. Phase 9 die committed. 2 of 3 cost paid.
>   1 die still owed — carries to next round.
> 
> Phase 8, Phase 9 of this round: Elena is committed. She sweeps the weapon
> across the zone. Targets can see what is happening and may seek cover.
> Elena cannot dodge or act otherwise with committed dice.
> 
> Round ends. New initiative is rolled.
> Elena rolls: Phases 1, 4, 8 next round.
> 
> Phase 1 (next round): Elena's first die is claimed by the ongoing action.
> The full auto resolves NOW. Elena rolls her attack against the zone.
> Targets in cover apply their cover penalty to defend.
> 
> Elena still has Phases 4 and 8 free this round — the cross-round cost
> only consumed her first die of the new round, not her entire new pool.

## Aborting Actions

At any point before the final committed die's Phase is called, you may abort an ongoing action. No roll is required — the decision is yours alone. Aborting is always available, but it carries a hard cost: all Action Dice committed so far are lost, consumed by the incomplete action. The Phase 7 die Elena spent beginning her burst is gone whether she finishes the burst or not.

The action fails completely. The burst goes wide. The spell unravels in a shower of stray energies. The great sword buries itself in the floor. No partial effect occurs; the investment simply evaporates. If the aborted action was a spell that had already accumulated Paradox dice for this round's casting index, those Paradox dice are still rolled at end of round. The Tapestry does not care about intent — only about the disturbance that was made.

Critically, dice that have not yet been committed are freed by an abort. If Elena committed Phases 7 and 9 to full auto, and she aborts at Phase 8 (before Phase 9 is reached), Phase 9 becomes free. She could use it to dodge, move, or make a quick attack. The abort is not costless — Phase 7 is gone — but it recovers the future investment.

The abort mechanic is the safety valve that makes cross-round actions tactically interesting rather than suicidal. A character who starts a big action is making a bet: they believe the action will be more valuable than any alternative they might take with those dice. When that bet looks wrong — when an unexpected threat arrives, when the situation changes, when taking a hit starts to look catastrophic — they can fold, losing what they've already spent but recovering what remains.

> EXAMPLE — ABORT DECISION
> 
> Rook has committed Phases 7 and 9 to a full auto burst. One more die owed next round.
> 
> Phase 8: An enemy Rook did not see flanks him and attacks.
> Rook has no Bank Token — he never banked a die this round. And Phases 7 and 9 are committed to the burst. He cannot actively defend regardless.
> 
> Rook's options:
>   (A) Eat the hit. Stay committed. The burst resolves next round as planned.
>       He has no Bank Token — no active defense regardless. He absorbs the damage.
>   (B) Abort. Lose Phase 7 (already spent) and Phase 9 (committed). Gain Phase 9 back
>       as a free die. Then immediately BANK that freed Phase 9 die, gaining a Bank Token.
>       Spend the fresh token to Dodge the flank attack.
> 
> He chooses (B). He aborts — Phase 7 die lost, Phase 9 freed. He immediately banks Phase 9,
> gaining a Bank Token in the same breath. He spends it to Dodge.
> The flanking attack is penalized by −2 dice. The burst is lost entirely.
> But Rook is alive to fight next round, with no token carried (he just spent it).
> 
> Note: the abort→bank→spend sequence is deliberate. When an abort frees a die, that die
> may be banked immediately — it does not have to be used as a normal action. This is one
> of the hidden advantages of aborting a committed action: you can convert future dice that
> would have been locked into the action into a defensive token right when you need it most.

## Banking a Die

At the heart of the Phase Initiative System's approach to defense is a single question: **when are you saving yourself for a reaction?** The answer is the Bank Token — a resource you deliberately hold back from the action economy and hold in reserve.

During the round, whenever a Phase is called and you have an Action Die showing that Phase number, you face a choice: act normally with that die, or **Bank** it. To Bank a die, remove it from your active dice and place a physical marker in front of you — a coin, a poker chip, a die turned face-down, a spare stone. This is your **Bank Token**.

The Bank Token has **no Phase number**. It is not "stored at Phase 3" or "waiting for Phase 7." It is a pure reserve — a unit of potential without a clock on it.

**Banking is announced when the Phase is called, before any actions in that Phase resolve.** You must commit to banking before the Phase's actions happen. You cannot watch the opponent act, then retroactively decide you would have preferred to bank.

> *Design Note:* Banking is the primary decision point of the system. It forces a real trade-off every round: "I am giving up an action now in exchange for the ability to react later." The character who banks early and correctly is not passive — they are reading the fight and positioning themselves to respond to what they cannot predict. A character who burns every die offensively without banking is gambling that nothing unexpected will happen before their next turn. Sometimes that gamble is right. When it is wrong, they find out with no active recourse.

### The Bank Token in Play

Once you hold a Bank Token, it can be spent in one of two ways:

**Offensive Use — Wildcard Phase:**
At any Phase from the current Phase forward, you may spend your Bank Token to perform any action you could perform with a normal Action Die. Declare the action during any open Phase as it is called, and spend the token in place of a die. The token acts exactly like a Trick Initiative die in this mode: flexible placement anywhere ahead of you in the round. **You cannot use this to return to a Phase that has already been called and completed.** Only current or future Phases are open.

> *Example — Wildcard offense:* Lucinda banked her Phase 4 die. Phases 4, 5, and 6 pass without incident. At Phase 8, an enemy turns to flee — Lucinda spends her Bank Token to attack at Phase 8 rather than waiting until her next Action Die at Phase 9. She was preserving the option to act anywhere in the round, and Phase 8 was the moment worth spending it on.

**Defensive Use — Reactive Defense:**
When an attack is declared against you and has not yet been resolved, you may spend your Bank Token to perform an active defense. You may declare Dodge, Parry, Shield Block, or any other active defense available to your character. The token is spent at the moment you declare the defense — before the attack roll is made.

> *Example — Reactive defense:* Viktor attacks Mira at Phase 5. Mira has no remaining Action Dice in Phases 1 through 5 — they were all spent on earlier actions. But she banked her Phase 2 die. When Viktor declares his attack, Mira immediately spends her Bank Token to Dodge. The token is gone; Viktor's attack pool takes a −2 die penalty.

**If you have no Bank Token, you cannot perform any active defense.** There is no fallback — no "sacrifice a future die," no emergency expenditure. Active defense is a planned capability, not a perpetual one. A character who spent their Bank Token on a wildcard offense in Phase 6 has no defense available in Phase 8. A character who never banked anything and carried no token from last round faces every attack with passive Soak, armor, and cover alone. This is intentional and consequential: the Bank Token system makes "I will be ready to react" a deliberate choice, not a default assumption.

### Spending the Token — Timing and Limits

The Bank Token is spent at the moment you declare its use. The outcome does not matter: if you spend it to Dodge and the dodge fails, the token is gone. If you spend it to Parry and you lose the contested roll, the token is gone. If you spend it for a wildcard offensive action and you roll zero hits, the token is gone. **Committing to spend commits the token.**

You may spend only **one Bank Token per incoming attack**, regardless of how many defensive options your character possesses. You cannot Dodge and Parry the same attack, nor spend two tokens on one swing.

```
BANK TOKEN — QUICK REFERENCE
------------------------------
HOW TO BANK: Announce when a Phase is called where you have a matching die.
             Banking occurs before any actions in that Phase resolve.
WHAT IT IS:  A valueless token. No Phase number. No expiration within the round.
SPEND (OFF): Act at any Phase from current Phase forward (wildcard timing).
SPEND (DEF): Declare active defense against any incoming attack.
NO TOKEN:    Cannot actively defend. Passive Soak, armor, and cover only.
LIMIT:       1 Bank Token maximum (default).
TOKEN COST:  Spent at declaration, regardless of outcome.
```

### Bank Timing: The Same-Phase Rule

A Bank Token created by banking a die at Phase X is **not available until Phase X+1 or later**. You cannot bank a die and spend the resulting token in the same Phase — banking is a preparation act, not a reaction. The token exists only for future Phases.

> *Why this matters:* Without this rule, a character could theoretically bank and spend simultaneously — banking at Phase 5 when an attack also resolves at Phase 5, then immediately spending the token to dodge that same attack. This collapses the real cost of banking: you would never need to plan ahead, because you could always react in the moment of the threat. The same-phase restriction ensures that banking is a forward-looking decision made before the danger arrives.

**The exception: using a current-Phase die directly for defense.** If you have an Action Die showing the current Phase number and an attack is declared against you, you may choose to use that die for defense instead of your intended action — without formally banking it. Declare "I am defending with my Phase X die" and spend it as you would a Bank Token: Dodge (attacker −2 dice, free zone step) or Parry (contested melee). The die is consumed. You lose your offensive action at that Phase, but you gain the active defense without the one-Phase delay that banking requires.

> *Design note:* This models the instinctive option of a fighter who was about to act and pivots instantly to defense instead. They had a die ready; they chose to spend it on survival rather than offense. It is not banking — there is no token, no carry, no future use. It is a direct trade: action for defense, happening now, with no setup required.

> **Example — Same-Phase die defense:**
> Mira has action dice at Phases 3, 5, 7. She has no Bank Token. At Phase 5, an enemy launches an attack. Mira declares that she is using her Phase 5 die for a Dodge instead of her intended casting. The die is spent: she cannot cast at Phase 5, but her attacker loses 2 dice and she steps one zone. Her remaining dice at Phase 7 are unaffected. Had she wanted to cast AND keep a defense option for Phase 5, she should have banked her Phase 3 die when Phase 3 was called — one Phase earlier, paying the cost in advance.

> **Example — Banking one Phase early:**
> Soren has dice at Phases 2, 4, 6, 9. He sees that his opponent acts at Phase 4 and he wants a defense ready. At Phase 2, he banks his Phase 2 die — creating a Bank Token that is available from Phase 3 onward. When Phase 4 arrives and the opponent attacks, Soren spends the token to Parry. The one-Phase gap between banking (Phase 2) and spending (Phase 4) is the planned gap, and it cost Soren his Phase 2 offensive action. If instead Soren had tried to bank at Phase 4 and immediately spend the token on Phase 4, the same-phase rule prevents it: the token is not available until Phase 5.

### Carrying the Bank Token Between Rounds

If the round ends with your Bank Token unspent, it **carries automatically into the next round**. You begin next round already holding a token — your defensive reserve from last round persisting into the new one.

**The Carry Cost:** At the start of any round in which you are carrying a Bank Token, roll **one fewer die** for Initiative. Your effective pool for that roll is reduced by 1. The physical metaphor is exact: you are still braced, still half-committed to guard — one portion of your attention and body is "holding" the previous round's reserve. You cannot roll at full engagement speed when part of your readiness is already spoken for.

> *Example — Carry cost:* Vesper has a pool of 7 and banked her Phase 3 die in Round 1. The round ends without her spending the token. Round 2: she rolls only 6 dice for Initiative, but she begins Round 2 already holding a Bank Token — ready to defend the moment an attack comes.

> If Vesper spends the token in Round 2 (for offense or defense), she rolls her full 7 dice in Round 3. The carry cost ends the moment the token is spent.

> If Vesper never spends the token and carries it again into Round 3, she rolls 6 dice in Round 3 as well. The cost persists as long as the carry persists. A character who carries a token indefinitely is paying for it every round in reduced Initiative — a real price for sustained defensive readiness.

```
CARRY-OVER — QUICK REFERENCE
------------------------------
CARRY:  Unspent Bank Token at round's end persists to next round automatically.
COST:   Roll Initiative pool − 1 die in any round you begin while carrying a token.
SPEND:  Spending the token (offense or defense) ends the carry cost.
        Next round you roll your full pool again.
MAX:    Cannot carry more tokens than your current Bank Limit (default: 1).
```

### The Bank Limit

By default, every character may hold a maximum of **1 Bank Token** at a time. You may not bank a second die if you are already holding a token. If a Phase arrives where you would bank a die but you are already at maximum, you must either spend your current token first or let that die pass unused — you cannot hold two tokens simultaneously under the default rules.

Some Tricks raise the Bank Limit (see **Combat Reserve**, below). A character with Bank Limit 2 may hold two tokens simultaneously — entering each round with two defense or wildcard opportunities. The carry cost at Bank Limit 2 is pool − 2 for any round in which two tokens are carried.

### The Held Die and the Bank Token — They Are Not the Same

The Bank Token **replaces** the old mechanic of sacrificing Action Dice for defense. The Held Die remains in the system but serves a different purpose.

**Held Die:** holds its original Phase number; allows you to choose exactly when within the current round to act; expires at round's end if unused; used for offensive or support timing.

**Bank Token:** has no Phase number; acts as a wildcard for offense or defense; can carry over between rounds at a pool cost; is the only resource that enables active defense.

A character may simultaneously hold one die (the Held Die) and one Bank Token. These are separate resources occupying separate roles. Having one does not grant the other.

> *Example — Held Die vs. Bank Token:*
>
> Hector has dice at Phases 2, 5, 8. He uses Phase 2 to attack. At Phase 5, he holds that die (setting it aside for precise timing later in the round — he wants to intercept an enemy spell the moment it begins casting).
>
> At Phase 6, an enemy attacks Hector. He has no Bank Token. The Held Die is not a defense resource — Hector cannot spend it to Dodge or Parry. He takes the hit.
>
> The Held Die fires at Phase 7, interrupting the enemy mage's casting exactly as Hector intended. The trade was deliberate: offensive timing over defensive cover.
>
> If Hector had instead **banked** his Phase 5 die (no Phase number, pure token), he would have had a Bank Token available at Phase 6 to Dodge. He would also have had Phase 8 remaining as a free action. The difference: the Held Die preserves Phase number and expires this round; the Bank Token is a wildcard that can carry over. Held Die = timing precision. Bank Token = defensive or wildcard flexibility.

---

## Dodge

Dodge is a purely reactive movement — ducking, rolling, stepping aside, throwing yourself clear. No attack roll of your own is required.

**Declare:** when an attack against you has been declared and before the attacker rolls.
**Cost:** spend your Bank Token. You must hold a Bank Token to declare a Dodge.
**Effect:** the attacker's pool suffers a −2 die penalty to the attack roll. You may also move up to one zone reflexively as part of the dodge — stepping behind cover, shifting out of a blast radius. This movement costs nothing extra.

A Dodge works against any attack: melee, ranged, or magical. It is the safest defensive option — no contested roll, just a token spent and a penalty applied. The risk is only the token itself: you may not Dodge again without another Bank Token.

```
DODGE — SUMMARY
---------------
WHEN:   Any incoming attack. Declare before the attack roll.
COST:   Spend your Bank Token. No token = cannot Dodge.
EFFECT: Attacker −2 dice. You may move one zone reflexively.
ROLL:   None required on your part.
NOTE:   Works vs. melee, ranged, and most magical attacks.
```

---

## Parry

A Parry actively deflects, redirects, or traps the incoming attack — catching a blade, absorbing a punch, using the attacker's momentum against them. It carries a contested roll: the ceiling is higher than Dodge (a successful Parry delivers an immediate riposte), but so is the floor (a failed Parry leaves you worse off than not trying, and your token is still gone).

**Declare:** when a melee or thrown-weapon attack has been declared against you and before the attacker rolls.
**Cost:** spend your Bank Token. You must hold a Bank Token to declare a Parry.
**Roll:** make a contested roll — your Melee (armed defense) or Athletics (unarmed) versus the attacker's attack pool. Both rolls are made simultaneously.

**If you win the contested roll:** the attack is completely deflected. No damage is taken. You deliver an immediate riposte — one automatic hit (1 point of damage before Soak) against the attacker. The riposte costs no additional token or die; it is the natural reward of a perfect deflection.

**If you lose the contested roll:** the attack lands normally. You take the hit as if you had never declared a Parry. The Bank Token is still gone. A failed Parry is strictly worse than not attempting one — you spent your defensive resource and absorbed the damage anyway. Characters who are not confident their combat pool exceeds the attacker's pool should strongly consider Dodging instead.

Parrying is only possible against melee attacks and some thrown weapons. You cannot Parry gunfire or most ranged magical effects. The Storyguide may permit Parrying against unusual attacks on a case-by-case basis — a mage catching a lightning bolt in a silver mirror, a Hermetic deflecting a telekinetic shove with a counterspell — but these exceptions are never assumed.

```
PARRY — SUMMARY
---------------
WHEN:   Melee attacks and some thrown weapons only. Declare before the roll.
COST:   Spend your Bank Token. No token = cannot Parry.
ROLL:   Your Melee or Athletics vs. attacker's attack pool.
WIN:    Attack deflected. Automatic riposte (1 automatic hit, no extra token).
LOSE:   Attack lands normally. Token still gone.
CAUTION: Failed Parry = token spent + full damage taken. Dodge if unsure.
```

---

## Shield Block

A Shield Block is an active defense that uses your shield's physical mass and Tags to absorb or deflect an attack. Unlike Dodge (which avoids) or Parry (which redirects), a Shield Block accepts the incoming force and reduces its impact through the shield's properties.

**Declare:** when an attack has been declared against you and before the attacker rolls.
**Cost:** spend your Bank Token. You must hold a Bank Token to declare a Shield Block.
**Invoke:** you may invoke one of your shield's Tags (such as [Block], [Reinforced], [Rigid], or [Ward]) to gain +2 dice on the shield-interposition roll, OR to activate a specific Tag effect as described in Appendix J.

**Roll:** Shield Skill (or Athletics if untrained) against a Difficulty set by the attack's force — typically the attacker's number of hits.

**If you succeed:** the shield interposes fully. Apply the shield's Soak value against the attack's damage. Hits that do not exceed the Soak are absorbed entirely. If the shield has an [Armored] or [Rigid] Tag, the Storyguide may rule the attacker suffers a Complication — a chipped blade, a jarred weapon hand, a sprained wrist from striking reinforced cover.

**If you fail:** the attack partially bypasses the shield. Apply half the shield's Soak (round down) and take the remainder as damage.

A Shield Block's Tags may be compelled as well as invoked. A heavy shield that invokes [Rigid] for its blocking force might be compelled to slow the defender's next action — the weight of the block carries momentum that limits the follow-through. Accept the Compel for 1 Momentum.

```
SHIELD BLOCK — SUMMARY
----------------------
WHEN:   Any melee or physical ranged attack. Declare before the roll.
COST:   Spend your Bank Token. No token = cannot Shield Block.
INVOKE: One shield Tag for +2 dice or a specific Tag effect (see Appendix J).
ROLL:   Shield Skill or Athletics vs. attacker's hits (Difficulty).
SUCCEED: Full shield Soak applied. Possible Complication on attacker.
FAIL:   Half Soak (round down) applied. Remainder lands.
COMPEL: Shield Tags may be compelled — accept the compel for 1 Momentum.
```

---

## Other Reactive Defenses

Tags, Tricks, and special abilities may grant additional reactive defense options beyond Dodge, Parry, and Shield Block. All of these options still require spending your Bank Token unless the ability explicitly states otherwise.

- A mage with Forces ●● and the [Wave-Deflector] Rote may invoke the Rote's Tags as a reactive barrier against ranged attacks — declaring it as an active defense and spending their Bank Token, just as any other defense.
- A character with the **Defensive Fighting** Trick may, once per round, perform a Parry without spending their Bank Token — the Trick absorbs the cost, and the token (if held) is preserved.
- A character tagged [Supernatural Reflexes] may perform one Dodge per scene without spending their Bank Token. The token (if held) is preserved; this free Dodge does not interact with the banking economy at all.

All such abilities still follow the declare-before-the-roll timing rule. Any ability that modifies or waives the Bank Token cost says so explicitly in its description. If an ability is silent on the cost, assume the standard Bank Token applies.

**No Bank Token, No Active Defense.** This is the fundamental rule that makes the banking economy meaningful. A character without a token — whether they never banked, spent it on offense, or used it on an earlier defense — has exhausted their active reactive capability. Passive Soak and armor remain. Worn armor, cover, and Hardiness still function. But Dodge, Parry, Shield Block, and similar active responses are unavailable until a new Bank Token is obtained. The next opportunity to bank is the next round.

---

## Initiative Tricks

Some Tricks interact directly with the Phase Initiative System, the Banking economy, or the action economy in ways that go beyond the standard rules. Two such Tricks are defined here; additional Tricks may appear in character creation resources and faction supplements.

---

### Combat Reserve

*Prerequisite: Athletics ●●● or any combat-focused Skill ●●●●.*

Your Bank Limit increases from 1 to **2**. You may hold up to two Bank Tokens simultaneously — carrying one from a previous round while banking a new one from the current round. Both tokens are tracked separately; each may be spent independently for offense or defense.

**Carry cost at two tokens:** if you begin a round carrying two Bank Tokens, roll Initiative pool − 2 dice for that round. The cost scales with what you hold. Carrying one token still costs pool − 1.

**Banking when already holding one token:** when your second die comes up and you wish to bank it, you may do so freely (provided you hold only one token). You may not exceed the two-token limit.

**Spending one of two tokens:** the remaining token stays in your possession. You are not suddenly reduced to a single token's carry cost — you continue paying pool − 2 until the second token is spent or the round ends.

> *Design Note:* Combat Reserve is a potent sustained-engagement advantage. A character who has built and maintained a two-token reserve enters each round with two reactive opportunities — enough to handle two separate attacks, or to act offensively at any moment and still defend once. The pool-reduction cost (−2 dice every round you sustain the double reserve) means aggressive defense eventually eats initiative advantage. A Combat Reserve character who never spends either token will, over many rounds, find their pool edge steadily shrinking.

> *Example:*
> Kira has a pool of 6 and Combat Reserve. Round 1: she banks her Phase 2 die. Round 1 ends, one token in hand. Round 2: she rolls 5 dice (pool 6 − 1 carry). She banks her Phase 3 die. She now holds two tokens. Round 3: she rolls 4 dice (pool 6 − 2 carry). But she has two wildcard defensive opportunities and can act at any open phase twice. In Round 3, an enemy attacks her at Phase 2. She spends one token to Parry — riposte hits. Down to one token. A second attacker strikes at Phase 5. She spends the last token to Dodge. Down to zero. Round 4: she rolls her full 6 dice again.

---

### Press the Advantage

*Prerequisite: Athletics ●● or Brawl ●●.*

Once per round, when you spend your **final Action Die** and **every attack you made this round hit**, you may immediately recover one previously spent die as a **Momentum Token**. Both conditions must be true simultaneously: pool is empty, and zero attacks missed.

The Momentum Token is not a Bank Token: it cannot be carried to the next round, does not count against your Bank Limit, and cannot be used for active defense. It can only be spent for an offensive or support action at the current Phase or any Phase that follows, within this round only.

**The condition in full:** count only attack rolls made with Action Dice this round. Support actions (movement, holding a die, banking) do not count toward or against the condition. If every attack roll produced at least one hit, the condition is met when the last die is spent. A single whiffed attack anywhere in the round — even an early one — breaks the chain and Press the Advantage does not trigger, regardless of what follows.

A die committed to an ongoing action (SF 2 or higher) counts as "spent" when its final committed Phase arrives. If that last committed Phase is also the last die in the pool, and all prior attacks hit, the Momentum Token appears at that moment.

Choose any die you spent this round to recover as the Momentum Token; it does not return at its original Phase number. It is a wildcard — one more action, usable anywhere from the current Phase forward.

> *Flavor:* You haven't just swung a lot — you've landed every blow. The opponent has absorbed hit after hit without a break to reset, and now they're off-balance, flinching, reacting rather than thinking. In that window of total dominance, you find one more opening that a lesser fighter would have missed entirely.

> *Example — trigger fires:*
> Damien has a pool of 4, with Action Dice at Phases 1, 3, 6, 8.
> Phase 1: attacks → 2 hits. ✓
> Phase 3: attacks → 1 hit. ✓
> Phase 6: attacks → 3 hits. ✓
> Phase 8: attacks — last die spent → 1 hit. ✓ All four attacks hit.
> **Press the Advantage triggers.** Damien recovers his Phase 1 die as a Momentum Token.
> Phase 9: he spends the token and attacks a fifth time. His opponent, who survived four
> strikes and expected a moment's respite, finds there is none.

> *Example — trigger fails:*
> Same Damien, same dice. Phase 1: attacks → 0 hits. ✗ Miss.
> Phase 3: attacks → 2 hits. Phase 6: attacks → 1 hit. Phase 8: attacks → 2 hits — last die spent.
> Three of four attacks hit — but one missed. **Press the Advantage does not trigger.**
> The chain broke at Phase 1. The opponent found a gap in Damien's assault and the
> momentum never fully built. He ends the round with an empty pool and no token.

> *Strategic Note — the resource puzzle:*
> Press the Advantage creates a genuine three-way tension each round:
>
> **Option A — Full offense, no bank:** attack every die, hit every time, earn the Momentum Token. Maximum pressure, zero defensive capability. One missed attack breaks it.
>
> **Option B — Bank one die:** trade one attack for a Bank Token. Now you have defense available, but you attacked one fewer time, raising the per-attack hit rate demand (you still need 100% on a shorter sequence). Press the Advantage still fires if all remaining attacks hit.
>
> **Option C — Bank one die, use Momentum Token offensively:** if you banked and still triggered Press the Advantage, you end the round with both a Momentum Token (spend it now) and a Bank Token (spend it next round for defense or a wildcard). The fighter who can bank, maintain a perfect hit record, and collect the Momentum Token is playing at a very high level — but it is achievable, and rewarding when it happens.
>
