> The condition is not just a timer on "pool hits zero." It is a meter of execution quality. Sloppy rounds go unrewarded. Precise ones compound.

---


## Movement in Combat

Movement is an action like any other — it costs Action Dice, has a Speed Factor, and must be declared and resolved within the Phase structure. Characters who choose to move sacrifice the opportunity to attack or cast in that Phase, but gain tactical positioning that may prove decisive in subsequent Phases.

Position is tracked using Storypath Ultra's own **Range Bands**, not a battle-grid and not an informally-defined "zone." Range Bands are narrative and relative — "Short range" between two human-scale combatants is a different absolute distance than "Short range" between a mage and a building-sized spirit, and the Storyguide should always frame the fiction first and let the band follow, not the reverse. This same band structure is also used to determine how far a Sphere effect can reach from its caster (see Chapter Four's Spell Parameter Scaling — Range Bands section); combat positioning and spellcasting range are governed by one shared table, not two separate ones, so a mage's sense of "how far away is that target" never has to shift depending on whether they're about to punch it or hex it.

### Range Bands

| Range Band | Typical Distance | What You Can Do From Here |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Close | 0–2 meters | Melee weapons, unarmed combat, grappling, most thrown-weapon minimums, and any effect or Tag that requires physical contact (see "Touch," Chapter Four). |
| Short | 3–20 meters | Thrown weapons, reach weapons, shotguns and SMGs at their best, close-quarters spellcasting. |
| Medium | 21–60 meters | Standard firearms range, most Forces/Life/Mind combat spells at their default casting parameters. |
| Long | 61–1,000 meters | Rifles with optics, sniping, heavy weapons, Correspondence-assisted targeting. |
| Extreme | 1,000+ meters, situational | Only specialized weapons, vehicles, or Sphere effects can act at this range — and only after paying to extend, in both the combat and spellcasting versions of this table. |
| Out of Range | Beyond any relevant weapon or effect's reach | No action can target something Out of Range without first closing distance — through movement, through magic, or by waiting for the target to come closer. This is a statement about a specific weapon or effect's reach, not a sixth band on the ladder above Extreme. |

Track Range Band per relevant relationship, not as a single global position. In a simple fight (a cabal against one cluster of enemies in an open room), it is usually enough to track a single shared band between "the party" and "the enemy group." In a complex fight with multiple fronts (defenders spread through a building, a flanking maneuver, a chase that spills into a firefight), track Range Band between whichever specific pairs of combatants actually matter to the scene — you do not need a full distance matrix between every combatant and every other combatant, only the ones the fiction is asking questions about. Each free hit on an attack or casting roll can be spent to extend that action's effective Range Band by one step (Close to Short, Short to Medium, and so on) — this "1 hit = 1 band" conversion is identical whether the hit is paying for a mundane weapon to reach further or a Sphere effect to reach further; see Chapter Four for how this applies to specific Spheres and effects.

### Movement Actions

**Reflexive Step — Free.** As part of any other action you take on one of your Phases (attacking, casting, defending), you may shift one Range Band — but only between the two pairs of bands Storypath Ultra allows this for reflexively: Close↔Short, or Short↔Medium. This costs no Action Die and represents the ordinary positioning that happens as part of doing anything at all in a fight — stepping back half a pace as you swing, closing the last couple of meters as you level a pistol. You may take one Reflexive Step per Phase in which you act; it does not stack with itself within a single Phase.

**Standard Move (SF 1).** Spend one Action Die to shift one Range Band in any direction relative to a chosen reference point — including the Medium↔Long and Long↔Extreme steps that a Reflexive Step cannot cover. This is your entire action for that die — you move and do nothing else in that Phase with that die.

**Sprint (SF 2).** Spend your current die and your next die in sequence to shift two Range Bands at a dead run (Medium to Close in one committed action, for instance). You cannot attack or cast on either committed die, though other free dice before or after the committed sprint are unaffected.

**Rush (SF equal to your attack's SF — no separate movement cost).** Adapted directly from the Storypath Ultra core Rush action: as part of declaring a melee attack, you may close one Range Band toward your target in the same motion — Medium to Close, or Short to Close. The attack suffers a Complication (+1 Difficulty) unless the target has spent a Bank Token this scene specifically to brace against exactly this (the Storyguide may rule a defender's declared Parry stance or a relevant Tag, such as [Sidestep] or [Braced], negates the Complication). You cannot Rush if you are already at Close range with your target — there is nothing left to close.

**Held Movement.** You may hold a die explicitly for reactive movement. When you trigger the held die as a movement, you can move in response to something that just happened — diving behind cover the moment a gun turns toward you, closing distance the moment an enemy's back is turned. Held movement triggers between Phases.

**Moving Target.** If you have shifted at least one Range Band during this round (by any of the above means), all attacks against you suffer a −1 die penalty for the rest of the round. You are harder to hit when you are not standing still. This applies even if you moved in an earlier Phase — the penalty lasts the round.

**Cover.** Moving into a position of cover requires spending an Action Die (folded into a Standard Move if the cover is one Range Band away, or as its own committed action if you are already at the right band and simply need to duck behind something present at your current position). Once in cover, attacks against you suffer an additional penalty based on cover quality: Light cover such as a car door or wooden crate: −1 die. Solid cover such as a concrete barrier or brick wall corner: −2 dice. Near-total cover such as a reinforced doorframe with only your weapon arm exposed: −3 dice.

Declaring movement as your action does not prevent you from making other actions with different dice in the same round. You might sprint in Phase 3 (spending Phases 3 and 5), then attack in Phase 7 with your remaining die. The sprint and the attack are separate actions on separate dice.

```
MOVEMENT — QUICK REFERENCE
---------------------------
REFLEXIVE STEP: Free. Close<->Short or Short<->Medium only. Once per Phase you act in.
STANDARD MOVE (SF1): 1 die. Shift 1 Range Band, any adjacent pair, any direction.
SPRINT (SF2): 2 dice (current + next). Shift 2 Range Bands. No attack/cast on committed dice.
RUSH (SF = attack's SF): Close 1 Range Band as part of a melee attack. +1 Difficulty unless defender braced.
HELD MOVEMENT: Hold a die, spend it later in the round for reactive repositioning.
MOVING TARGET: Shifted >=1 Range Band this round -> all incoming attacks at -1 die, rest of round.
COVER: Costs an Action Die to enter. Light -1, Solid -2, Near-total -3 to attackers.
```

> EXAMPLE — RANGE BANDS IN A FIREFIGHT
>
> Dex is at Medium range from a Syndicate gunman across a parking garage. On his Phase, he spends a Standard Move (SF 1) to shift to Short range, ducking behind a support pillar in the same action (the pillar counts as his destination, so this single die pays for both the move and entering cover). He is now at Short range, benefiting from Solid cover (−2 dice to the gunman's attacks) and the Moving Target penalty (−1 die, since he shifted a Range Band this round) — the gunman's shotgun (effective at Short) still threatens him, but at a combined −3 dice penalty. Two Phases later, Dex spends a Rush as part of his own attack, closing from Short to Close and tackling the gunman directly, accepting the Rush's +1 Difficulty Complication because he judges ending the fight in a grapple is worth it.

For running many identical, non-named NPCs moving and acting together without tracking each one's Range Band individually, see Appendix W's Speed Tier System and Group Rules — those tools apply on top of the positioning rules above, they do not replace them.

## Rates of Fire

Firearms and similar ranged weapons vary dramatically in how quickly they can be fired and sustained. The Speed Factor system handles this naturally — faster weapons have lower SF, slower weapons have higher SF — but the following table provides specific detail on how different firing modes affect both SF and the attack roll itself.

Full auto attacks that target a Range Band affect every target within that band simultaneously. Each target defends separately against the same attack roll result. Targets who are prone, in cover, or moving may apply appropriate penalties to the attacking roll or bonuses to their defense at Storyguide discretion. Full auto burns through ammunition at a prodigious rate — it is presumed that characters using this mode have sufficient ammunition for one burst; a second full auto in the same combat may require checking remaining ammunition.

Reloading a weapon is its own action, with its own Speed Factor. A standard magazine firearm reloads in SF 1 with the right preparation (speed loaders, pre-loaded magazines). A revolver reloads in SF 2 without a speed loader. A crossbow reloads in SF 3. These times assume combat conditions; a character taking their time outside of combat reloads in negligible time.

## Spellcasting and the Paradox Clock

Mages who cast spells in combat face one primary pressure: the Speed Factor of the working itself, which consumes Action Dice proportional to the complexity and power of the effect. Paradox is generated independently by each working, assessed on its own merits — Vulgar or Coincidental, witnessed or unwitnessed — with no additional penalty for casting multiple spells in the same round. The Paradox pool itself is the escalating pressure; a mage who hammers through three Vulgar workings in a round is not punished twice over — they simply add each working's Paradox to their pool, which grows large enough on its own.

Unlike mundane actions — which have fixed Speed Factors determined by the physical demands of the action — spells have variable Speed Factors that depend on the complexity of the working itself. A mage does not simply decide to "cast a standard spell." They decide to invoke Forces ●●● and Correspondence ●●, and the Tapestry extracts a price in time and concentration proportional to what is being demanded of it. More dots, more dice. More spheres, more juggling. The Phase Initiative System makes this cost viscerally felt.

### Paradox and Spellcasting

Each spell generates Paradox independently, assessed on its own merits at the moment it resolves. Casting multiple spells in a single round does not impose additional Paradox beyond what each spell individually earns. A Coincidental working generates no Paradox whether it is the first or fifth casting of the round. A Vulgar working with Sleeper witnesses generates its full Sphere-level dice whether preceded by zero or three other spells.

The natural growth of the Paradox pool is the escalating pressure. A mage who casts three Vulgar-with-Witnesses spells in a round adds all three workings' Paradox to their pool — which can easily reach 9 or more dice from raw spell activity alone, before any Botch or other modifier. The pool itself becomes the clock, ticking louder with each addition, and triggering automatically when it reaches 7 or more dice.

At end of each scene in which Vulgar magic was used, the Storyguide rolls the full Paradox pool and interprets results according to the Reading the Paradox Dice table in Chapter Five. There is no per-round roll in this system — Paradox accumulates across the scene and discharges at scene's end (or immediately upon reaching 7+ dice).

### Spellcasting Speed Factors

Every spell's Speed Factor is calculated from the Sphere dots required to cast it. There are two modes of casting, each with its own formula:

**Freeform Magic — SF = Sum of All Sphere Dots**

Add the dot ratings of every Sphere involved in the effect. A Forces ●●● bolt is SF 3. A Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●● working is SF 5 (3+2). A Mind ●●●● + Life ●●● combination is SF 7 (4+3). There is no +1 overhead penalty — the dot sum already reflects the inherent complexity of the working. The Tapestry does not add an additional surcharge; it simply mirrors the scope of what you're attempting.

This still reflects the reality of improvised magic: Forces and Correspondence do not naturally cooperate. The mage must hold every aspect of the working in mind simultaneously while willing the effect into existence. Freeform casting of complex multi-sphere effects is punishing in Phase Initiative — it is intended to be. Rotes exist precisely to avoid this cost. An SF 5 freeform effect is five Phases wide; an SF 2 Rote of the same working is only two Phases wide, freeing three extra dice.

**Rotes — SF = Highest Single Sphere Rating Minus One (Minimum 1)**

A Rote is a memorized, practiced pattern — a spell the mage has cast dozens or hundreds of times in controlled conditions. The mage has internalized the interplay between Spheres to the point where the secondary spheres thread themselves automatically, guided by habit and muscle memory of the will. Only the hardest, highest-rated sphere demands conscious attention — and even that attention is pre-loaded by repetition.

For a Rote, the Speed Factor equals the rating of the highest single Sphere required, minus one, with a minimum of 1. A Rote using Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●● has an SF of 2 (Forces 3 − 1 = 2). A Rote using Life ●●● + Mind ●● + Forces ● has an SF of 2 (Life 3 − 1 = 2). A Rote using Mind ●●●● + Life ●●● + Correspondence ●● has an SF of 3 (Mind 4 − 1 = 3). A single-dot Rote (any Sphere ●) has SF 1 (1 − 1 = 0, raised to minimum 1).

The same Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●● working costs SF 5 improvised (3+2 = 5) but only SF 2 as a Rote — a saving of 3 phases of window. In a round where a mage has 5 or 6 action dice, this difference determines whether the working leaves any dice free for other actions.

**Worked Examples of Spell SF**

Consider a mage with Forces ●●●, Correspondence ●●, and Mind ●●●, with Action Dice at Phases 1, 3, 5, 7, 9. Resolve Phase = Declare + SF − 1:

- Forces ●●● freeform (SF 3): Declared at Phase 1, resolves at Phase 3 (1+3−1). Window: {1,2,3}. Consumes dice at 1, 3. Retains 5, 7, 9. Fast enough to leave three dice free — a straightforward lightning bolt that doesn't dominate the round.
- Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●● freeform (SF 5): Declared at Phase 1, resolves at Phase 5 (1+5−1). Window: {1,2,3,4,5}. Consumes dice at 1, 3, 5. Retains 7 and 9. More expensive, but still leaves two dice. A freeform spatial/energy working at this cost is aggressive but viable.
- Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●● as Rote (SF 2): Declared at Phase 1, resolves at Phase 2 (1+2−1). Window: {1,2}. Consumes only the Phase 1 die (no die at Phase 2). Retains Phases 3, 5, 7, 9 — four dice free. This is the Rote advantage made concrete: the same spatial lightning bolt that costs SF 5 improvised costs SF 2 as a Rote, resolving in Phase 2 instead of Phase 5 and freeing four dice instead of two.
- Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●● + Mind ●●● freeform (SF 8): Declared at Phase 1, resolves at Phase 8 (1+8−1). Window: {1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8}. Consumes dice at 1, 3, 5, 7. Retains only Phase 9. A three-sphere freeform working eats nearly the entire round. As a Rote (SF 2, since highest Sphere is Forces/Mind ●●● = 3−1=2), this same working resolves at Phase 2 and costs only the Phase 1 die — transformative difference in action economy.

The minimum SF for any spell — even a trivial Forces ● cantrip — is 1. SF 1 means the spell goes off in the declaring Phase: declare and resolve happen simultaneously in that Phase, consuming only that one die.

```
SF AT A GLANCE — QUICK LOOKUP
-----------------------------
RESOLVE FORMULA: Resolve Phase = Declare Phase + SF − 1
  SF 1 → resolves same Phase (instantaneous)
  SF 2 → resolves 1 Phase later
  SF 3 → resolves 2 Phases later
  SF N → resolves N−1 Phases after declaration

SINGLE-SPHERE SPELLS (Rote SF = Dots − 1, min 1. Freeform SF = Dots.)
  Dots  | Rote SF | Rote delay | Freeform SF | FF delay | Example Effect
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  1     |    1    |  0 Phases  |      1      | 0 Phases | Minor cantrip (Forces 1: detect EM)
  2     |    1    |  0 Phases  |      2      | 1 Phase  | Moderate working (Life 2: heal bash)
  3     |    2    |  1 Phase   |      3      | 2 Phases | Significant (Forces 3: lightning bolt)
  4     |    3    |  2 Phases  |      4      | 3 Phases | Major working (Mind 4: full domination)
  5     |    4    |  3 Phases  |      5      | 4 Phases | Master-level (Life 5: raise the dead)

MULTI-SPHERE SPELLS
  Rote SF = highest dot − 1 (min 1). Freeform SF = sum of ALL dots.
  Spheres (config)         | Dot Sum | Rote SF | FF SF | Rote saves vs FF
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Forces 2 + Corr 1        |    3    |    1    |   3   | 2 Phases
  Forces 3 + Corr 2        |    5    |    2    |   5   | 3 Phases
  Life 3 + Mind 2          |    5    |    2    |   5   | 3 Phases
  Forces 4 + Life 3        |    7    |    3    |   7   | 4 Phases
  Forces 5 + Corr 4        |    9    |    4    |   9   | 5 Phases
  Life 5 + Mind 4 + Corr 3 |   12    |    4    |  12   | 8 Phases

KEY: Rote advantage is substantial. A Forces 5 + Corr 4 spell as a Rote
     resolves in SF 4 (3 Phases of delay); freeform takes SF 9 (8 Phases).
     With 10 Phases per round, SF 9 declared early still fits in one round;
     declared late it spills into the next. Rote SF 4 almost never spills.
```

A mage may choose to spend additional Action Dice on a single spell beyond its SF cost, representing deeper concentration and greater care. Each additional die spent this way reduces the spell's Paradox by 1 die (to a minimum of 0). The Tapestry rewards deliberate, careful casting: a mage who wants to hurl a vulgar Forces ●●● bolt with minimal Paradox blowback might commit 5 dice instead of 3 — burning 2 extra phases — to scrub 2 dice from the Paradox pool. This applies equally to any working regardless of how many spells have been cast this round.

Aborting a spell mid-cast follows the same rules as aborting any action. Committed dice are lost. Paradox dice already assigned to this casting are still rolled at end of round. A mage who begins a complex working and then thinks better of it does not escape the consequences — the disruption in the Tapestry has already occurred. A Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●● improvised working that is aborted after 3 of its 5 committed dice have been consumed still generates its full Paradox. The Tapestry noticed.

## Extended Combat Examples

The following five examples demonstrate the Phase Initiative System in full, covering the full range of mechanics: basic Phase flow and dodge, parry and riposte, full auto across a round boundary with an abort decision, mage casting under Paradox escalation, and a complex multi-combatant scene mixing all systems.

### Example One: The Schoolyard — Basic Phase Flow and Dodge

Alaric is a hardened combat veteran who has traded his mage robes for a long coat and a straight blade. He confronts a Technocratic operative known only as the Thug — a heavyset enforcer with brass knuckles and a willingness to use them.

Alaric has Athletics 3 and Cunning 4 — his pool is 7 dice. He rolls: 1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 8, 9. Seven action dice across seven different Phases. The Thug has Athletics 2 and Dexterity 2 — pool of 4. He rolls: 4, 4, 7, 9. Two actions at Phase 4, one at Phase 7, one at Phase 9.

Alaric glances at his roll. He knows the Thug has dice at Phase 4 and will act twice then. He decides to give up one early action in exchange for a defense token.

> ROUND 1 — ALARIC: Phases 1,2,3,5,7,8,9  |  THUG: Phases 4,4,7,9
> 
> Phase 1: Alaric acts. He attacks the Thug with his blade (SF 1). He rolls his combat pool
> and scores 3 hits. The Thug takes 3 damage. Solid opening.
> 
> Phase 2: Alaric acts again. He steps to the side to gain a flanking angle and attacks.
> 2 hits. The Thug staggers.
> 
> Phase 3: Phase 3 is called. Alaric has a die here — but instead of attacking,
> he BANKS it. He sets the die aside and places a coin in front of him: his Bank Token.
> He now holds Phases 5, 7, 8, 9 as remaining action dice, plus one Bank Token.
> 
> Phase 4: The Thug acts — twice, since two dice landed on 4.
>   First action: The Thug swings hard at Alaric.
>     -> Alaric declares a DODGE. He spends his Bank Token.
>     -> Token gone. The Thug's attack pool loses −2 dice. He rolls and gets 0 hits. Miss.
>   Second action: The Thug swings again immediately.
>     -> Alaric has no Bank Token. He cannot actively defend.
>     -> He decides to take it — the Thug is nearly done anyway.
>     -> 1 hit lands. Alaric takes minor damage.
> 
> Phase 5: Alaric attacks. 2 hits. The Thug is now badly wounded.
> 
> Phase 7: Alaric attacks. 3 hits. The Thug collapses, Incapacitated.
>   The Thug also had a Phase 7 die — but he is now down. It is wasted.
> 
> RESULT: Alaric attacked five times (Phases 1, 2, 5, 7, and still held 8 and 9 he never
> needed). He banked Phase 3 to get the Dodge at Phase 4 — giving up one attack for one
> guaranteed defense opportunity. Against the Thug's double-Phase-4, the Bank Token
> absorbed the more dangerous first swing; the second hit was acceptable given the Thug
> was nearly done. Alaric's pool advantage told the story — but the bank was the move
> that kept him standing clean through the Thug's best moment.

### Example Two: The Penthouse — Parry, Riposte, Enhancement, and Banking

Countess Vesper is an experienced duelist and covert operative. She has Empathy 3 and Dexterity 4, giving her a pool of 7. She also has Enhancement 1 on initiative from her Fencing Mastery Edge.

Before this confrontation there was a tense standoff in the corridor — enough time for both combatants to see each other and prepare. Vesper, knowing Blade will move first, made a deliberate decision: she banked one die during that preceding moment. She enters this round already carrying a Bank Token. The carry cost applies: she rolls only 6 dice this round instead of 7.

She rolls: 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 (six dice). She uses her Enhancement to shift the 7 to Phase 5, giving her: 3, 5, 5, 6, 8, 9 — two actions at Phase 5.

Her opponent is Blade, an Iteration X assassin with Athletics 4 and Cunning 3 — a pool of 7 as well, but no Enhancement and no Bank Token. Blade rolls: 2, 4, 5, 8.

> ROUND 1 — VESPER: Phases 3,5,5,6,8,9 + BANK TOKEN (carried)  |  BLADE: Phases 2,4,5,8
> 
> Phase 2: Blade attacks. He emerges from shadow and strikes at Vesper.
>   -> Vesper has no dice in Phases 1 or 2 — her earliest die is Phase 3.
>      Without a Bank Token, she would have NO defense here.
>      She spent the prior round carrying this token precisely for this moment.
>   -> Vesper declares a PARRY. She spends her Bank Token.
>   -> Token gone. Contested roll: Vesper's Melee (5 dice) vs. Blade's attack (7 dice).
>      Vesper scores 2 hits. Blade scores 1 hit. VESPER WINS.
>   -> Attack deflected. Vesper delivers a riposte — 1 automatic hit to Blade.
>      No extra token required. Her blade catches his forearm on the way past.
>   -> Vesper holds zero Bank Tokens. She must bank before the next attack.
> 
> Phase 3: Vesper's die arrives. She has a choice: attack, or bank.
>   -> She knows Blade has a die at Phase 4 — the next threat is one Phase away.
>   -> She BANKS her Phase 3 die. Token placed. She now holds a Bank Token again.
>      Remaining action dice: 5, 5, 6, 8, 9.
> 
> Phase 4: Blade attacks again, undeterred despite the riposte wound.
>   -> Vesper holds a Bank Token. She declares a PARRY and spends it.
>   -> Token gone. Contested: Vesper's Melee (5 dice) vs. Blade's attack (7 dice).
>      Vesper scores 1 hit. Blade scores 3 hits. BLADE WINS.
>   -> The parry fails. The attack lands. Vesper takes 3 damage. Token gone.
>      A failed Parry: she paid the Bank Token and still ate the hit.
>      She needed that riposte opportunity; at her pool, the risk was real.
> 
> Phase 5: Vesper has TWO dice here — Enhancement's gift. Blade also has a Phase 5 die.
>   -> Tiebreaker: Vesper rolled 6 dice this round; Blade rolled 7. Blade acts first.
>   -> Blade attacks: 1 hit. Vesper takes 1 more damage.
>      Vesper has no Bank Token. She cannot actively defend. She absorbs the hit.
>   -> First Vesper action: she attacks: 2 hits. Blade staggers.
>   -> Second Vesper action (her extra Phase 5 die): she BANKS it.
>      She did not need a second attack right now. Another token is better.
>      Bank Token placed. Remaining action dice: 6, 8, 9.
> 
> Phase 6: Vesper acts. She attacks with precision: 3 hits.
>   Blade is Staggered (−1 die on all actions until end of round).
> 
> Phase 8: Both Vesper (pool 6) and Blade (pool 7) act.
>   Tiebreaker: Blade (7) acts before Vesper (6).
>   -> Blade attacks (Staggered: −1 die): 0 hits. The wound to his forearm costs him.
>   -> Vesper attacks: 3 hits. Blade collapses, Incapacitated.
> 
> Phase 9: Vesper has her final action die and a Bank Token.
>   Nothing threatens her. She holds the die, watching. Round ends.
>   The Bank Token was unspent this round's second token — it carries to next round.
>   If a threat emerged, she had both an action AND a Bank Token in reserve.
> 
> RESULT: The key decision was banking in the corridor. Vesper's Phase 3 attack would have
> been a third hit — but a third hit on a healthy opponent is less valuable than having
> a defense when Blade's Phase 2 attack lands. The trade paid off immediately with the
> successful Parry and riposte. The failed Phase 4 Parry is the lesson most players learn
> early: a Bank Token spent on Parry is a gamble. If your pool cannot reliably beat the
> attacker's pool, Dodge (guaranteed −2 penalty, no contest) preserves more value.
> Vesper's second-token banking at Phase 5 shows the dynamic nature of the resource:
> the token is not just carried from round to round; it is built and spent within a round
> as the tactical picture evolves. End of round, she carries one token forward — ready
> for whatever comes through the penthouse door next.

### Example Three: The Loading Dock — Full Auto, Cross-Round, and Abort

Rook is a techno-mercenary with a pool of 4 (Athletics 3, Cunning 3). He rolls: 2, 5, 8, 9. He has an assault rifle capable of full auto (SF 3).

Courier is a fast-moving runner with a pool of 3 (Athletics 3, Cunning 2... wait, higher of that or Empathy 2 + Dexterity 4 = 6... let us say Courier has a pool of 5). She rolls: 1, 3, 6, 7, 10. She carries a light pistol (SF 1).

Watching from a catwalk is the Sniper — pool 2, dice at Phases 4 and 9. Bolt-action rifle (SF 3). Cap: min(3, 2) = 2 total dice. The Sniper's action will cost both dice.

> ROUND 1 — ROOK: 2,5,8,9  |  COURIER: 1,3,6,7,10  |  SNIPER: 4,9
> 
> Phase 1: Courier acts. She SPRINTS (SF 2) — commits Phase 1 and Phase 3.
>   She moves two zones toward the shipping containers on the far side of the dock.
> 
> Phase 2: Rook acts. He moves to a better firing position (SF 1). Phase 2 spent.
> 
> Phase 3: Courier's sprint Phase 3 die is CONSUMED by the sprint. She arrives
>   at the containers. She is now a moving target (-1 die on attacks against her).
> 
> Phase 4: Sniper fires (SF 3 capped at 2 — commits Phase 4 and Phase 9).
>   Phase 4 committed (firing begins). Phase 9 committed (resolution at start of Phase 9).
>   Courier is moving (-1 die) and in partial cover (-1 die). Sniper's pool: 2 dice,
>   minus 2 situational = 0 effective dice. The shot goes wide.
> 
> Phase 5: Rook acts. He begins full auto (SF 3). Window: Phase 5 through Phase 8 (5+3=8).
>   His remaining dice: Phases 5, 8, 9.
>   Dice in window [5–8]: "5" (consumed) and "8" (consumed).
>   The "9" die falls OUTSIDE the window (9 > 8) and remains free.
>   Full auto resolves at Phase 8. Rook still has Phase 9 available.
> 
> Phase 6: Courier acts (Phase 6 die). She ducks behind the containers (SF 1).
>   She is now in solid cover (-2 dice on attacks against her). She fires her
>   pistol at the Sniper from cover: 2 hits. The Sniper is hit and Staggered.
> 
> Phase 7: Courier acts (Phase 7 die). She sees Rook has been sustaining fire
>   since Phase 5. She has one Phase left this round (Phase 10).
>   She holds her Phase 7 die — something feels wrong.
> 
> Phase 8: Rook's Phase 8 die is consumed — the burst resolves NOW.
>   NEW DEVELOPMENT: As the burst fires, a second enemy drops from the catwalks!
>   The enemy attacks Rook during the resolution Phase. Rook has Phase 9 FREE
>   (it was outside his window), so he CAN dodge or respond.
> 
>   Rook faces a choice: absorb the hit and use Phase 9 offensively (he has no Bank Token
>   so he cannot actively defend anyway), or abort the burst, bank Phase 9, and spend
>   the fresh token to Dodge — but this costs him the entire burst.
> 
>   Full auto: He rolls his attack pool (Staggered: -1 die) against Courier's zone.
>   Courier is in solid cover: -2 dice. Total pool: reduced but still possible.
>   He scores 1 hit. Courier takes 1 damage despite cover.
> 
>   Sniper's bolt-action (window 4–9, Phase 9 is final die): Sniper is Staggered,
>   -1 die. Rolls at Courier. 0 hits. Cover saves her.
> 
> Phase 9: Rook has his free "9" die. He uses it to attack the flanking enemy.
>   2 hits. The flanker staggers back.
> 
> Phase 10: Courier triggers her HELD Phase 7 die (still valid — held dice
>   expire at end of round, and Phase 10 is still within the round).
>   She fires at the second enemy who attacked Rook: 2 hits. Enemy is wounded.
> 
> RESULT: The window rule proved its worth here. Under the old "consume N dice in sequence"
> model, Rook's "9" die would have been locked into the burst. Under the window model,
> the "9" fell outside the 5–8 window and remained free — letting Rook respond to the
> flanker. The burst still swept the zone. He walked away from both threats.

### Example Four: The Conclave Chamber — Dot SF, the Rote Advantage, and Banking

Seraphine is a Forces-and-Correspondence mage. Her Sphere ratings are Forces ●●● and Correspondence ●●. Her initiative pool is 5 (we keep it modest for this example). She rolls: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9. She has Enhancement 1 from her [Resonant Strike] Tag and shifts Phase 9 to Phase 7, giving final dice: 1, 3, 5, 7, 7. Two actions at Phase 7.

She knows a Rote called "Thunderstrike at Range" — Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●●. Improvised, this working costs SF 5 (3 + 2 = 5 sphere dots). As a practiced Rote, the SF equals the highest single Sphere rating minus one: Forces ●●● − 1 = **SF 2**. That three-Phase saving is, as we will see, the difference between a round consumed and a round dominated.

She faces a Technocratic Sentinel Construct — a hardened combat drone with a pool of 3 and dice at Phases 2, 6, 9. The Construct carries a sustained-beam laser projector (SF 5: it takes the entire charge cycle to deliver the burst). The scene is a Conclave chamber; witnesses are present, making vulgar magic Paradox-heavy.

> ROUND 1 — SERAPHINE: 1,3,5,7,7  |  CONSTRUCT: 2,6,9
> 
> SPELL SF REFERENCE FOR THIS ENCOUNTER:
>   Forces ● cantrip (improvised):  dots = 1. SF = 1. Minor kinetic shove.
>   "Thunderstrike at Range" Rote (Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●●):
>     - Improvised: dots = 3+2 = 5. SF = 5. Window = 5 Phases wide.
>     - As Rote: SF = highest Sphere − 1 = Forces ●●● − 1 = SF 2. Window = 2 Phases.
>   After spending Phase 1 on the cantrip, Seraphine has 4 dice remaining (3,5,7,7).
>   SF 5 improvised = impossible with 4 dice (would need 5 dice starting at Phase 1).
>   SF 2 Rote = uses 1–2 dice maximum, leaving 2–3 dice FREE. This is the entire point.
> 
> Phase 1: Seraphine acts. Forces ● cantrip, improvised. Dots: 1. SF: 1.
>   Resolve Phase = 1+1−1 = Phase 1. Fires this very Phase.
>   A focused kinetic shove hammers the Construct backward.
>   Casting pool: Forces 3 + Intellect 3 = 6 dice → 3 hits.
>   The Construct staggers. Structural integrity compromised.
>   Paradox: coincidental — +0 dice.
> 
> Phase 2: Construct fires its laser projector (SF 5).
>   Declare Phase = 2. Resolve Phase = 2+5−1 = Phase 6.
>   Window: Phases 2 through 6 (inclusive). Construct dice in window: Phase 2 and Phase 6.
>   Both consumed. The beam charges for four more Phases.
>   Seraphine sees the charging optics — she has time to react.
> 
> Phase 3: Phase 3 is called. Seraphine has a die here.
>   She reads the room: the laser resolves at Phase 6, and she has nothing before then
>   to stop it. She BANKS her Phase 3 die rather than attacking. Bank Token placed.
>   Remaining action dice: 5, 7, 7. Plus one Bank Token.
> 
> Phase 5: Seraphine acts. She initiates "Thunderstrike at Range" as a Rote.
>   Rote SF = 2 (Forces ●●● − 1 = 2). Declare Phase = 5.
>   Resolve Phase = 5+2−1 = Phase 6. Window: Phases 5 and 6 (inclusive).
>   Seraphine’s dice in window: Phase 5 only (she has no Phase 6 die).
>   ONE die consumed. Phase 5 spent. Rote resolves at Phase 6.
>   Paradox assessed at resolution (Phase 6) — see end of round.
>   Phases 7 and 7 remain FREE. She still has her Bank Token.
> 
>   CONTRAST: Improvised (SF 5) at Phase 5 → window {5,6,7,8,9} → would consume Phase 5,
>   Phase 7, and Phase 7 (second). THREE dice eaten. Only the Bank Token left.
>   No Phase 7 actions. No Bank Token for Phase 9. Completely committed for the round.
>   The Rote at SF 2 uses ONE die and leaves Seraphine with a full offensive remaining.
> 
> Phase 6: TWO things resolve simultaneously this Phase.
>   (a) The laser fires. Construct attacks Seraphine.
>       Pool: 3 dice (depleted). Rolls: 8, 2, 4. ONE hit.
>       Seraphine takes 2 damage. She absorbs it — she is saving the Bank Token for Phase 9.
>   (b) The Rote resolves.
>       "Thunderstrike at Range" fires. A bolt of kinetic force leaps the chamber,
>       guided by Correspondence through the pillars without requiring line of sight.
>       Casting pool: Forces 3 + Intellect 3 = 6 dice (Rote: no penalty) → 4 hits.
>       The Construct takes catastrophic damage. Barely functional.
> 
> Phase 7 (first die): Seraphine attacks — she has dice here because the Rote only cost SF 2.
>   She fires another Forces effect (cantrip, SF 1). Coincidental — +0 Paradox.
>   Rolls: 3 hits. Construct is critically damaged.
> 
> Phase 7 (second die): Seraphine has a choice — attack again or bank this die for a second token
>   dangerous) or bank this die for a second token (but she is already at Bank Limit 1).
>   She cannot hold two tokens. She attacks — another Forces cantrip. Coincidental — +0 Paradox.
>   She rolls: 2 hits. Construct functional but destroyed shortly.
> 
> Phase 9: Construct acts — its last die. Damaged to near-zero. Pool: 1 die.
>   It extends a weapon arm in a final attack at Seraphine.
>   Seraphine has her Bank Token. She spends it: DODGE.
>   Token gone. The Construct’s attack pool loses −2 dice. Pool: 1−2 = 0 effective dice.
>   The blow goes wild. Seraphine steps clear.
> 
> END OF ROUND: Roll Paradox.
>   Each working assessed independently:
>   Forces ● cantrips (×3) — coincidental: +0 Paradox each.
>   "Thunderstrike at Range" Rote — Vulgar with Sleeper witnesses: +2 × Forces ●●● = +6 dice.
>   Total Paradox pool: 6 dice.
>   Roll: 9, 5, 2, 4, 8, 1. TWO Paradox hits (9 and 8).
>   The lights in the chamber flicker. Every polished surface briefly shows Seraphine
>   wreathed in crackling electricity. The witnesses recoil in horror.
>   The [Witnesses] Tag on the scene is now hot — the Storyguide can Compel it.
> 
> WHAT IF SHE HAD TRIED IMPROVISED?
>   Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●● improvised = SF 5.
>   After the Phase 1 cantrip, she has 4 dice: Phase 3 (held), 5, 7, 7.
>   Committing Phase 5, 7, 7 covers 3 of the needed 5 dice — the spell bleeds into
>   Round 2, resolving on Round 2’s first two dice. During the bleed-over, she cannot
>   dodge, react, or do anything. The Phase 6 laser hits unopposed.
>   The Rote saved her the equivalent of 2 phases of exposure.
> 
> WHAT IF SHE HAD PUSHED A THIRD SPELL?
>   Her only free die was the held Phase 3. A third Vulgar working with witnesses
>   would add another +6 Paradox dice — bringing the pool to 12 before the round ends.
>   At 7+ dice the pool triggers immediately; Seraphine would be rolling Paradox mid-round.
>   Seraphine chooses restraint. The Tapestry is appeased — for now.

### Example Five: The Night Market — A Full Encounter

This example involves four participants in a running street fight. It demonstrates how all the systems interact simultaneously: mixed weapon types, spellcasting with dot-based SF, cross-round commitment, parrying, movement, and a Paradox roll.

Participants:

- Mira — Street mage, pool 5, dice: 1, 2, 5, 8, 9. Carries a combat knife (SF 1). Forces ●● and Entropy ●●● specialist. Enhancement 1 on initiative shifts her 9 to Phase 7. Final dice: 1, 2, 5, 7, 8. She knows one Rote: "Entropic Deterioration" (Entropy ●●● + Forces ●, Rote SF = highest = Entropy ●●● = 3).
- Jin — Martial artist, pool 6, dice: 1, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10. Unarmed (SF 1). Holds his Phase 10 die from the start — he always saves something.
- Operator Alpha — Technocratic gunman, pool 3, dice: 3, 7, 8. Semi-auto pistol (SF 1).
- Operator Beta — Technocratic heavy, pool 2, dice: 2, 9. Assault rifle on burst fire (SF 2).
> ROUND 1
> MIRA: 1,2,5,7,8  |  JIN: 1,3,4,6,8,10(held)  |  ALPHA: 3,7,8  |  BETA: 2,9
> 
> SPELL SF REFERENCE:
>   Forces ● bolt (improvised): dots = 1, SF = 1. Fast kinetic strike.
>   Forces ●● bolt (improvised): dots = 2, SF = 2. Harder hit, commits 2 dice.
>   "Entropic Deterioration" Rote (Entropy ●●● + Forces ●):
>     Improvised: dots = 3+1 = 4, SF = 4. Costs 4 dice.
>     As Rote: SF = highest sphere − 1 = Entropy ●●● − 1 = SF 2. Costs 2 dice.
> 
> Phase 1: Mira and Jin both act. Tiebreaker: Jin pool 6 vs. Mira pool 5 — JIN first.
>   Jin: Attacks Operator Alpha with a palm strike (SF 1). 2 hits. Alpha staggers.
>   Mira: Forces ● bolt (improvised, dots 1, SF 1) at Operator Beta.
     Commits Phase 1. Coincidental — +0 Paradox.
>     Casting pool: Forces 2 + Cunning 3 = 5 dice → 2 hits. Beta is struck.
>     Paradox pool: 0.
> 
> Phase 2: Beta acts (burst fire, SF 2) — commits Phase 2 and Phase 9.
>   Tiebreaker: Beta pool 2 vs. Mira pool 5 — MIRA acts first.
>   Mira acts at Phase 2: Forces ●● bolt (improvised, dots 2, SF 2).
>     Window: Phase 2 through Phase 4 (2+2=4). Dice in window [2–4]: only the "2" die.
>     The "5" die (5 > 4) is OUTSIDE the window and stays free.
     Cost: 1 die consumed. Vulgar, no witnesses — +2 Paradox dice (Forces ●●).
>     Casting pool: Forces 2 + Cunning 3 = 5 dice → 3 hits.
     Beta is hit hard. Paradox pool: 2 dice. Mira still has Phases 5, 7, 8 free.
>   Beta’s burst fire (SF 2) begins: window Phase 2 through Phase 9.
> 
> Phase 3: Jin acts. He attacks Alpha (SF 1). 3 hits. Alpha is Staggered.
>   Alpha also acts at Phase 3 (Staggered, -1 die). Tiebreaker: Jin 6 vs Alpha 3 — Jin first.
>   Jin strikes, then Alpha fires back: 1 hit lands on Jin. Minor damage.
> 
> Phase 4: Jin acts. Moves to Beta’s position — the burst rifle is more dangerous.
> 
> Phase 4: Forces ●● bolt resolves (window closes at Phase 4 = 2+2). Force wave tears into Beta.
>   3 hits land. Beta is badly hurt and Staggered.
> 
> Phase 5: Mira’s Phase 5 die is FREE — it was outside the bolt’s window [2–4].
>   She begins “Entropic Deterioration” Rote (Entropy ●●● + Forces ●).
>   Rote SF = 3. Window: Phase 5 through Phase 8 (5+3=8).
>   Dice in window [5–8]: “5” (consumed), “7” (consumed), “8” (consumed).
>   All three fall inside the window. No cross-round overflow needed.
  Vulgar, no witnesses — Entropy ●●● = +3 Paradox dice. Paradox pool: 2+3 = 5 dice.
> 
> Phase 6: Jin acts. He attacks Beta (SF 1). 2 hits. Beta is further Staggered.
> 
> Phase 7: Alpha acts at Phase 7 (Staggered, -1 die). He fires at Mira.
>   Mira’s Phase 7 die is COMMITTED to the Rote. She cannot dodge without aborting.
>   Alpha fires: 1 hit. Mira takes minor damage.
> 
> Phase 8: ROTE RESOLVES — window closes at Phase 8 (5+3=8). “Entropic Deterioration” fires.
>   Alpha acts at Phase 8 (his third die, Staggered). He fires at Jin.
>   Tiebreaker: Jin pool 6, Alpha pool 3 — Jin acts first.
>   Jin attacks Alpha (SF 1): 4 hits. Alpha is Incapacitated. Goes down.
>   Alpha’s Phase 8 die is wasted — he cannot act while Incapacitated.
> 
> Phase 9: Beta’s burst fire RESOLVES (final committed die: Phase 9).
>   Beta is badly Staggered and Jin is right next to him. He tries to fire anyway.
>   Beta’s pool: 2 dice, -1 Stagger = 1 die, -2 (Jin adjacent, close range bonus for Jin
>   to intercept). Jin has a Bank Token (he banked his Phase 5 die earlier in the round
>   when the situation was unclear). He spends the token to DODGE: −2 to Beta’s effective
>   roll. 1 die, −2 = 0 effective dice. Burst misses entirely.
>   His Held Phase 10 die remains — he can still act at Phase 10.
> 
> END OF ROUND 1 — PARADOX ROLL:
>   Each working assessed independently:
>   Forces ● cantrip — coincidental: +0 Paradox.
>   Forces ●● bolt — Vulgar without witnesses: +2 Paradox dice (Forces ●● = 2).
>   "Entropic Deterioration" Rote — Entropy ●●● Vulgar without witnesses: +3 Paradox dice.
>   since it’s SF-reduction not Paradox; the Rote’s practiced nature gives a feel of
>   Mira rolls 5 Paradox dice.
>   Results: 9, 8, 5, 3, 7, 2, 6. TWO Paradox hits (the 9 and 8).
>   Major Paradox event: The market street ripples. Entropy bleeds out of the Rote —
>   fruit rots in seconds, stalls collapse in accelerated decay, the crowd panics and
>   scatters. Mira takes 2 Paradox backlash damage and gains 1 Quiet point.
>   The [Witnesses] Tag and [Market Chaos] Tag both become hot.
> 
> ROUND 2 — ROTE ALREADY RESOLVED:
>   The Rote fired at Phase 8 of Round 1. No cross-round debt. Mira rolls fresh.
>   Beta’s assault rifle corrodes and jams spectacularly. It cannot fire again.
>   Beta is effectively disarmed and Staggered. He is no longer a serious threat.
> 
> RESULT: Alpha is down. Beta is disarmed and Staggered. Mira paid 2 Paradox hits
> for her aggression — three spells in one round is always a gamble.
> Jin is fresh, having used no magic and taken only minor damage.
> The market is a chaos zone. Round 3 would be cleanup — or escape.

## Quick Reference: Phase Initiative

```
PHASE INITIATIVE — QUICK REFERENCE
----------------------------------
INITIATIVE POOL: Higher of (Athletics + Cunning) OR (Empathy + Dexterity).
ROLL: Every die result (1-10) = Action Die for that Phase. Lower = earlier = better.
PHASES: 1 through 10, called in order. Act when your Phase is called.
TIES: Larger pool goes first. Equal pools = simultaneous.

ENHANCEMENT: Each point shifts one die up to 2 Phases earlier (min Phase 1).
HELD DIE: Hold ONE die. Trigger it anytime later this round. Expires at round end.

SPEED FACTOR: Cast at Phase T, resolves at Phase T + SF − 1. (SF is inclusive of the declaring Phase.)
  All Action Dice between T and T+SF−1 (inclusive) are consumed. Window = SF Phases wide.
  SF 1 at any Phase: resolves same Phase. SF 5 at Phase 1: resolves Phase 5; dice at Phases 1,5,6,7 consumed.
CROSS-ROUND: Unresolved at Phase 10? Carries into next round.
RESOLUTION: Effect triggers at the final consumed Phase.

ABORT: Lose all committed dice so far. Action fails. Paradox still owed.

BANKING: When a Phase is called and you have a matching die, you may Bank it instead of acting.
  Bank Token = valueless wildcard. Spend for offense (any Phase forward) or defense (any attack).
  SAME-PHASE RULE: A token banked at Phase X is NOT available until Phase X+1 or later.
  SAME-PHASE DEFENSE: Alternatively, use a current-Phase die directly for defense (no banking needed).
    — Declare "I defend with my Phase X die." Die consumed. Lose the offensive action; gain the defense.
  No Bank Token (and no current-Phase die used for defense) = no active defense. Passive Soak only.
  Carry unspent token to next round: roll Initiative pool −1 next round. Max bank: 1 (default).
ROLLED A 10 (declare at round start, before Phase 1):
  Option A — Place at any Phase 1–9: acts as a normal die there.
  Option B — Bank immediately: token available from Phase 1 onward.
  Option C — Leave at Phase 10: last-Phase action (rarely optimal, always valid).
DODGE: Spend Bank Token (or use current-Phase die). Attacker −2 dice. Move 1 zone free.
PARRY (melee only): Spend Bank Token (or use current-Phase die). Contested Melee vs. attack.
  Win = deflect + riposte (1 automatic hit). Lose = eat the attack AND resource still spent.

MOVEMENT: SF 1 (move 1 zone). SF 2 (sprint 2 zones). Moving target: attacker -1 die.
FULL AUTO: SF 3. +4 dice to attack. Zone effect. Cap applies to small pools.

SPELLCASTING PARADOX: Each working independent. Vulgar no witnesses: +1 die/Sphere lvl. Vulgar with Sleeper witnesses: +2 dice/Sphere lvl. Coincidental: +0.
FREEFORM SF = sum of ALL Sphere dots  (Forces ●●● + Corr ●● = 3+2 = SF 5).
ROTE SF    = highest single Sphere rating − 1 (Forces ●●● + Corr ●● Rote = SF 2).
Single-sphere freeform (e.g. Forces ●●●) = SF 3 (dot sum). Rote: SF 2 (3−1=2, min 1).
CASTING POOL: Sphere Skill + Casting Attribute (NOT Arete/Enlightenment). Arete/Enlightenment = cap only.
Spend Quintessence before rolling: +1 Enhancement die per point spent.
Max Quintessence spend per cast = Arete/Enlightenment ÷2, rounded up.

CONCENTRATION CHECK (struck while casting):
  Composure + Resolve vs. Difficulty = attacker hits.
  PASS: spell continues. FAIL: collapses, Quintessence lost,
        Paradox pool +1 die (not full amount).
```

---

# Appendix I: The Armor System

## Overview

The standard Storypath Ultra armor rules represent protection as a pool of extra Health boxes — durable enough in principle, but in practice a suit of full plate mail can be stripped to rubble by three well-placed unarmed attacks, and there is no mechanical difference between a blade scoring a direct hit and a blade bouncing off hardened steel. The system presented in this appendix replaces that model entirely.

Armor under these rules has two numeric values and a set of Tags that describe its nature, capabilities, and current condition. Ordinary attacks that cannot overcome the armor's protection do no lasting damage to it at all — they bounce off, exactly as they should. Powerful attacks with Enhanced force, armor-defeating weapon Tags, or magical energy physically degrade the armor's structure, burning through its Armor Points and pushing it toward worsening condition Tags. Sustained ordinary punishment accumulates as wear, slowly degrading the armor over multiple combats. The result is armor that feels durable and meaningful, that has a lifespan measured in serious battles rather than lucky strikes, and that rewards the investment of wearing serious protection.

This system integrates fully with the Storypath Ultra Tag economy. Armor properties are Tags that can be invoked for advantage or compelled against the wearer. Condition states — Dented, Compromised, Failing — are Tags that appear on the armor as damage accumulates and can be invoked by enemies who know how to exploit them. The armor's story is written in its Tags.

## The Two Core Values

### Soak

Soak is a flat number representing how much damage the armor absorbs before any reaches the wearer. Every time the character takes damage, subtract the armor's current Soak value from the damage roll's result before applying it to Health. If the damage after subtraction is zero or less, the armor absorbed the attack entirely — the wearer feels the impact but takes no Health damage. Soak does not reduce to zero through use in ordinary circumstances; it represents the armor doing its job.

Soak is affected by the armor's current condition Tag. A Dented armor has Soak reduced by 1. A Compromised armor has Soak reduced by 2. A Failing armor has Soak reduced by 3. Track the armor's current Armor Points to know which condition Tag applies and therefore what the effective Soak is at any moment.

Note that Soak applies to physical damage by default. Magical attacks (Forces blasts, Prime assaults, directed spirit attacks) bypass Soak unless the armor carries the Warded Tag. A warrior in full plate is very hard to hurt physically; a mage throwing lightning at them ignores the steel entirely unless the plate has been enchanted to resist magical force.

### Sturdy (Armor Points)

Sturdy represents the armor's structural integrity — how much punishment the armor itself can take before it begins to fail. Every armor has a Sturdy rating expressed as a number of Armor Points (AP). When AP is depleted to certain thresholds, the armor gains condition Tags that reduce its Soak and create vulnerabilities that enemies can exploit.

AP is not a fragile resource. In normal combat, ordinary attacks do not reduce AP at all — even if they exceed Soak and hurt the wearer, the armor structure remains intact. Only specific circumstances reduce AP:

- Enhanced attacks that deal any damage after Soak strip 1 AP from the armor.
- Attacks with the Armor-Piercing Tag strip 2 AP per hit, regardless of whether the attack is Enhanced.
- Attacks with the Devastating Tag that are also Enhanced strip 2 AP instead of 1.
- Magical attacks (those with the Magical Tag targeting physical matter, such as Forces or Prime effects used directly against the armor) strip 1 AP per hit regardless of Enhancement. Note: a magical attack that targets the wearer rather than the armor specifically does not strip AP.
- End-of-combat wear: if five or more non-Enhanced attacks exceeded Soak during a single combat encounter, strip 1 AP from the armor when the encounter ends. This represents the slow grinding punishment of sustained ordinary combat. A non-Enhanced attack that equals Soak exactly (zero damage to the wearer) does not count toward this tally — the armor absorbed it cleanly.

## The Condition Track

As AP is depleted, the armor passes through condition thresholds that apply negative Tags and reduce Soak. These thresholds are calculated as fractions of the armor's starting Sturdy rating. The exact AP values for each threshold are determined when the armor is built or purchased; they appear on the armor's stat block for easy reference during play.

Condition Tags are Tags in the full SPU sense: they can be invoked by enemies who are aware of the armor's damage and know how to exploit it, and they can be compelled by the Storyguide when the narrative calls for it. A character in Compromised armor who tries to bluff their way past a guard might find the Compromised Tag compelled — the guard notices the caved-in breastplate and draws the right conclusions. A character in Failing armor who is grappled might find the Tag compelled to force a dramatic structural failure at the worst possible moment.

When tracking AP in play, it helps to note the condition threshold values on the armor's stat block at the start of a session. The examples in the Building Armor section below demonstrate how to calculate and record these values.

## Repair

Armor can be repaired between combats, given the right tools, skills, and time. The quality of repair depends on the resources available.

- Field repair (basic tools, Crafts roll, one scene of work): Restore the armor to the top of its current condition threshold. A Compromised armor (AP at 30% of max) might be brought back to the very bottom of Compromised (just above 25%). It will not climb to Dented without a proper workshop. A successful field repair with three or more hits on the Crafts roll advances the armor one full condition tier.
- Workshop repair (proper facilities, Crafts roll, one day of work): Restore the armor to full AP. All condition Tags are removed. The armor is as good as it was before damage.
- Enchanted or supernatural armor may require magical repair. Life magic can repair Verbena natural-material armor. Forces or Matter magic (depending on the tradition) can restore enchanted metal. Iteration X technicians use specialized fabrication equipment. In each case, the roll uses the appropriate casting pool (Sphere Skill + Casting Attribute) rather than Crafts, using the same result thresholds as above.
- Destroyed armor cannot be repaired — only rebuilt from scratch, which is treated as crafting a new piece of armor.

## Building Armor

Every armor is defined by its Soak value, its Sturdy (AP) rating, and its set of descriptive Tags. When designing or acquiring armor, assign these values based on the armor's real-world (or fictional) properties and the level of protection it would realistically provide. The following guidelines establish the rating scale:

- Soak 1: Minimal protection. Padded clothing, a heavy coat, a light leather jacket. Offers meaningful resistance only against glancing blows.
- Soak 2: Moderate protection. Chainmail, a modern ballistic vest, a hardened leather cuirass. Stops most ordinary handgun rounds and light blades cleanly.
- Soak 3: Heavy protection. Full plate armor, military-grade body armor with trauma plates. Stops significant firearm rounds and heavy blows.
- Soak 4: Exceptional protection. Powered combat exoskeletons, magically reinforced plate, heavily engineered military systems. Very little gets through cleanly.
- Soak 5+: Reserved for supernatural armor, legendary artifacts, or Iteration X heavy platforms. Effectively impenetrable to ordinary weapons.
- Sturdy 4: Fragile or improvised. A few serious hits will finish it.
- Sturdy 6: Standard. Good armor for a few combats before needing attention.
- Sturdy 8: Durable. A full session of hard use before it starts degrading significantly.
- Sturdy 10–12: Military or exceptional. Multiple sessions before serious condition concerns arise.
Assign descriptive Tags based on the armor's material, construction, and purpose. Tags should reflect properties that will actually matter in play — an armor with fifteen Tags is not richer for it, merely unwieldy. Three to six Tags is the appropriate range for most armor. Choose Tags that create interesting invoke and compel situations in the stories you plan to tell.

### Example: Scuffed Leather Jacket

A battered motorcycle jacket worn by an urban street mage. Not purpose-built armor, but not nothing either.

```
SCUFFED LEATHER JACKET
----------------------
Soak 1   |   Sturdy 4   |   Flexible   |   Concealable

Condition Thresholds:
  Full: 4–4 AP  |  Dented: 3 AP (Soak 0)  |  Compromised: 2 AP (Soak —)  |  Failing: 1 AP  |  Destroyed: 0

Initiative: No penalty.
```

The Leather Jacket offers minimal but real protection. Its Soak 1 stops truly trivial hits — thrown objects, glancing fist strikes, minor blades — cleanly. Because it has only 4 AP, a single Enhanced attack that gets through Soak immediately pushes it to Dented (3 AP), where Soak drops to 0 and the jacket is effectively done as armor. It was never meant for serious combat.

Flexible can be invoked whenever the character needs to move freely — acrobatics, climbing, squeezing through gaps. It should never be compelled for rigidity. Concealable means the jacket looks like ordinary street wear; it cannot be identified as armor by casual observation, which is often its most important property.

> In play: Mira is wearing her leather jacket when a guard swings a baton at her.
> The baton hits for 2 damage. Soak 1 reduces it to 1. She takes 1 Health damage.
> This is a non-Enhanced hit that exceeded Soak — 1 tally mark.
> 
> Later, a technocrat fires a pistol at her with Enhancement (Armor-Piercing tag).
> The shot hits for 3 damage. Soak 1 reduces it to 2. She takes 2 Health damage.
> The Armor-Piercing tag strips 2 AP from the jacket: 4 → 2 AP.
> The jacket is now Compromised. Soak drops to —1... effectively 0. The jacket is done.
> The technocrat can invoke Compromised for Enhancement on their next shot.

### Example: Chainmail Hauberk

A well-maintained suit of interlocking steel rings, historically authentic, worn by a Verbena warrior-witch who refuses to modernize her combat approach.

```
CHAINMAIL HAUBERK
-----------------
Soak 2   |   Sturdy 6   |   Heavy (–1 initiative die)   |   Rigid   |   Archaic

Condition Thresholds:
  Full: 6–5 AP  |  Dented: 4 AP (Soak 1)  |  Compromised: 3 AP (Soak 0)  |  Failing: 1–2 AP (Soak —)  |  Destroyed: 0

Initiative: –1 die to pool.
```

Chainmail is excellent against slashing and moderate against piercing, but does very little against modern firearms — the interlocking rings were not designed for bullets. A Storyguide may rule that firearms with Armor-Piercing or Ballistic Tags treat chainmail's Soak as 1 rather than 2, since the rings simply do not stop rifle rounds cleanly.

Rigid can be invoked when defending against slashing attacks — the wearer can claim Enhancement by noting how the mail turns the blade. It is compelled when the character needs to move with genuine flexibility: swimming in chainmail is a serious undertaking, and squeezing through tight spaces may be impossible. Archaic resonates with pre-modern magical traditions; a Hermetic mage could invoke it during a ritual that calls on historical warrior-spirits, and a Verbena witch wearing it has an obvious connection to her tradition's warrior past. The Storyguide might compel Archaic when the armor's antiquated nature attracts unwanted attention — at a Technocracy checkpoint, chainmail is a red flag.

> The Verbena warrior Asha fights a Technocratic operative armed with a pistol.
> Three non-Enhanced pistol hits deal 3, 2, and 1 damage respectively.
>   Hit 1: 3 – Soak 2 = 1 damage to Asha. Tally: 1.
>   Hit 2: 2 – Soak 2 = 0. Armor absorbed completely. No tally.
>   Hit 3: 1 – Soak 2 = 0. Armor absorbed. No tally.
> 
> End of combat: 1 tally mark (below the threshold of 5). Chainmail is undamaged.
> 
> In the same fight, the operative fires once with Enhancement:
>   Enhanced hit: 4 damage – Soak 2 = 2 to Asha. AP: 6 → 5. Still in Full condition.
> 
> Asha invokes Rigid when the operative switches to a blade: Enhancement on her defense.

### Example: Full Plate Armor

A complete suit of articulated steel plate — gorget, pauldrons, breastplate, gauntlets, greaves, sabatons — representing the pinnacle of pre-firearms protective technology. An Akashic Brother might wear ceremonial Mongolian plate; a Hermetic Adeptus might have a suit enchanted by a predecessor.

```
FULL PLATE ARMOR
----------------
Soak 3   |   Sturdy 8   |   Heavy (–2 initiative dice)   |   Rigid   |   Archaic   |   Imposing

Condition Thresholds:
  Full: 8–7 AP  |  Dented: 6–5 AP (Soak 2)  |  Compromised: 4–3 AP (Soak 1)  |  Failing: 2–1 AP (Soak 0)  |  Destroyed: 0

Initiative: –2 dice to pool.
```

Full plate is genuinely formidable against ordinary physical attacks. An ordinary knife cannot reduce AP at all unless the five-tally wear threshold is reached, and even then it strips only 1 AP per five hits. It would take many combats of sustained knife-fighting to meaningfully degrade full plate. An Enhanced sword swing, however, immediately bites into AP.

Imposing creates dramatic invoke opportunities: roll to intimidate or project authority, invoke Imposing for Enhancement. A figure in full plate entering a room changes the room. But the Storyguide can compel Imposing whenever discretion is needed — a mage in full plate cannot slip through a crowd, attend a diplomatic function without incident, or pass through a metal detector.

The two-die initiative penalty is significant. In the Phase System, a character in full plate rolls two fewer dice for their initiative pool, meaning fewer actions and statistically later phases. A plate-armored warrior is hard to hurt but slow — opponents who understand the Phase System will try to exploit this, acting many times before the armored fighter gets a chance to respond. The armor's protection and its initiative cost are two sides of the same coin.

> Sir Aldric (full plate, Soak 3, Sturdy 8) faces a group of Technocratic operatives.
> 
> Round 1: Three non-Enhanced pistol hits land — 3, 4, and 2 damage.
>   3 – 3 = 0. Absorbed. No tally.
>   4 – 3 = 1. Penetrates. Tally: 1.
>   2 – 3 = 0. Absorbed. No tally.
> 
> Round 2: One operative fires with Enhancement. Pistol hit for 5 damage.
>   5 – 3 = 2. Penetrates. Enhanced: strip 1 AP. Sturdy 8 → 7. Still Full.
> 
> Round 3: An Enhanced shock baton hits for 4 damage.
>   4 – 3 = 1. Enhanced: strip 1 AP. Sturdy 7 → 6. Now DENTED (Soak drops to 2).
>   Attackers may invoke Dented for Enhancement on future attacks.
> 
> End of combat (after 3 rounds): 1 tally. Plate is at 6 AP — Dented.
> Field repair (Crafts roll, 3+ hits): restore to Full (8 AP). One scene of work.

### Example: Modern Ballistic Vest

A Level IIIA soft armor vest as worn by law enforcement and covert operatives — woven Kevlar and polyethylene laminate, rated for pistol rounds and shotgun pellets. An NWO operative or well-equipped Sleeper ally might wear one.

```
MODERN BALLISTIC VEST
---------------------
Soak 2 (3 vs. Ballistic attacks)   |   Sturdy 6   |   Ballistic   |   Concealable

Condition Thresholds:
  Full: 6–5 AP  |  Dented: 4 AP (Soak 1/2)  |  Compromised: 3 AP (Soak 0/1)  |  Failing: 1–2 AP  |  Destroyed: 0

Initiative: No penalty.
```

The Ballistic Tag means the armor is specifically optimized for firearm rounds. Against attacks with the Ballistic weapon Tag (handguns, rifles, shotguns), Soak is effectively 3 instead of 2. Against bladed weapons, it drops to 1 — Kevlar is not designed to stop edged weapons and a knife finds gaps in the weave easily. The Storyguide should apply this asymmetry actively.

Concealable means the vest can be worn under a business shirt or light jacket. It cannot be detected by casual observation. An operative wearing one looks like anyone else, which is often the point.

> Agent Torres (ballistic vest, Soak 2/3) is ambushed.
> 
> Pistol hit (Ballistic tag): 3 damage. Soak 3 (vs. Ballistic). 3 – 3 = 0. Absorbed.
> No tally. The vest catches the round cleanly.
> 
> Knife slash: 3 damage. Soak 1 (blade, Ballistic tag does not apply). 3 – 1 = 2 damage.
> Tally: 1. Torres takes 2 Health damage.
> 
> Enhanced shotgun blast (Ballistic + Devastating + Enhanced): 6 damage.
> 6 – 3 (Ballistic Soak) = 3 damage. Enhanced + Devastating: strip 2 AP. 6 → 4 AP.
> Vest is now DENTED (Soak drops to 2/1).

### Example: Hermetic Warding Robes

Ceremonial robes worn by a senior member of the Order of Hermes, embroidered with protective sigils and soaked in years of ward-casting. Physically unremarkable; magically potent. The robes have developed a Resonance that makes them subtly aware of threats.

```
HERMETIC WARDING ROBES
----------------------
Soak 1 (physical)   |   Soak 2 (magical — see Warded Tag)   |   Sturdy 5
Warded   |   Resonant (Order of Hermes)   |   Archaic   |   Enchanted

Condition Thresholds:
  Full: 5 AP  |  Dented: 4 AP  |  Compromised: 3 AP  |  Failing: 1–2 AP  |  Destroyed: 0

Initiative: No penalty.
```

The Warded Tag is the defining property of these robes. Magical attacks — Forces blasts, Prime assaults, directed Mind attacks, spirit-channeled harm — are reduced by Soak 2 rather than bypassing armor entirely. Against purely physical attacks, the robes offer only Soak 1 — a heavy sword swing still largely gets through.

Resonant (Order of Hermes) means members of the Order can invoke this Tag in magical contexts: casting a Warding Effect is enhanced by the robe's accumulated power, ritual protections are easier to maintain while wearing them, and Sympathetic connections to Hermetic places and artifacts are stronger. For a non-Hermetic mage attempting to wear these robes, the Tag may be compelled: the robes resist being used for Spheres outside their Resonance pattern, and in extreme cases, the Storyguide might rule that the Resonance creates active interference.

Enchanted means the armor can be affected by magical effects that target objects — a Correspondence ward placed on it extends to the wearer, for example — and that it may react to the presence of hostile magic in ways that alert the wearer. It also means that destroying the armor might release its stored magical energy unpredictably.

> The Hermetic Adept Seraphine faces a Technocratic Progenitor.
> 
> The Progenitor fires a bioengineered toxin (physical, no magic): 3 damage.
> Soak 1. 3 – 1 = 2. Physical Soak only. Seraphine takes 2 damage.
> 
> The Progenitor then activates a Life-pattern disruption device (Magical tag):
> 4 damage. Warded: Soak 2 applies. 4 – 2 = 2. AP check: Enhanced + Magical = 1 AP lost.
> Sturdy 5 → 4. Robes still in Full condition.
> 
> Seraphine invokes Resonant (Order of Hermes) while casting a defensive ward:
> Enhancement on her Warding roll. The robes pulse with silver light.

### Example: Verbena Bark-Woven Vest

A vest woven from strips of sacred oak bark, living moss, and braided human hair, constructed by a Verbena crafter-witch using Life magic. The vest is technically alive — it breathes, grows, and heals itself when given the chance.

```
VERBENA BARK-WOVEN VEST
-----------------------
Soak 2   |   Soak 3 (vs. Life/Spirit attacks)   |   Sturdy 7
Warded (Life, Spirit)   |   Resonant (Verbena)   |   Natural   |   Self-Repairing

Condition Thresholds:
  Full: 7–6 AP  |  Dented: 5 AP (Soak 1)  |  Compromised: 4–3 AP (Soak 0)  |  Failing: 1–2 AP  |  Destroyed: 0

Initiative: No penalty.
```

Self-Repairing is the vest's most unusual property. When the character rests in contact with living earth, natural vegetation, or a place of strong Life Resonance, the vest regains 2 AP per scene without any roll. Over the course of a night in the wilderness, a Failing vest can return to Compromised. No Crafts roll is required — the vest does this on its own, drawing on ambient Life energy. In an urban environment or a place of strong Technocratic Resonance, the Self-Repairing Tag is compelled: the vest cannot heal itself and may actually deteriorate slightly.

Natural can be invoked in wilderness contexts — the vest blends with natural surroundings (Enhancement on stealth in forests), and animals do not react negatively to the wearer. It is compelled in technological environments: airport security detects the unusual materials, Technocratic sensors flag it as anomalous, and anyone with science-based Pattern sight can see that this vest is actively alive, which is deeply unsettling.

> Asha's bark vest (Sturdy 7, currently at 3 AP — Compromised, Soak 0) has seen hard use.
> 
> She retreats to a forest glade to rest. Self-Repairing activates:
> Scene 1: +2 AP. Vest at 5 AP — back to Dented (Soak 1).
> Scene 2 (overnight): +2 AP. Vest at 7 AP — Full (Soak 2). Fully restored.
> 
> The next morning, Asha enters a Technocratic laboratory.
> Storyguide compels Natural: the lab's sterile environment suppresses the vest.
> Self-Repairing does not function here. The vest cannot heal itself in this place.
> Asha accepts the compel — but she's already at full AP, so it costs her nothing now.

### Example: Iteration X Combat Exoskeleton

A fully enclosed powered combat chassis produced by Iteration X — integrated servos, ablative ceramic-composite plating, integrated tactical display, and weapons hardpoints. Resembles a cross between powered armor and a robotic suit. Rare even within the Convention, issued only to front-line operatives on high-priority missions.

```
ITERATION X COMBAT EXOSKELETON
------------------------------
Soak 4   |   Sturdy 12   |   Powered   |   Reinforced   |   Technological   |   Imposing (Severe)

Condition Thresholds:
  Full: 12–10 AP  |  Dented: 9–7 AP (Soak 3)  |  Compromised: 6–4 AP (Soak 2)  |  Failing: 3–1 AP (Soak 1)  |  Destroyed: 0

Initiative: No penalty (Powered negates Heavy).
```

Powered means the exoskeleton bears its own weight using internal servos. The Heavy Tag is present but negated by Powered — the wearer does not suffer the initiative pool penalty. Remove the Powered Tag (system failure, severe damage, power cell destroyed) and the suit immediately becomes Heavy (–2 dice), as its full weight falls on the wearer.

Reinforced means that Armor-Piercing attacks strip only 1 AP instead of 2 against this armor. The suit is specifically engineered to defeat AP rounds — the ceramic composite layers are designed to defeat penetrators. This makes the exoskeleton significantly more durable against the weapons most likely to be used against it.

Imposing (Severe) means the suit is dramatically, unmistakably intimidating. Invoke for Enhancement on any intimidation, authority, or physical threat roll. It is compelled relentlessly whenever subtlety is needed — a two-meter armored figure cannot blend into a crowd, enter a civilian building without incident, or conduct any form of covert operation. The Convention deploys these suits knowing they sacrifice all subtlety for overwhelming force.

> Operative ANVIL (Iteration X exoskeleton, Soak 4, Sturdy 12) storms a Tradition chantry.
> 
> A Verbena warrior charges with an Enhanced spear strike (Armor-Piercing): 5 damage.
> 5 – 4 = 1. Enhanced + AP: normally 2 AP, but Reinforced reduces to 1. 12 → 11 AP.
> 
> A Virtual Adept fires an Enhanced railgun round (Armor-Piercing + Devastating): 8 damage.
> 8 – 4 = 4 damage. Enhanced + AP + Devastating: 2 AP (Devastating) but Reinforced
> makes it 1 AP for the AP component... Storyguide rules: Devastating + AP = 2 AP total,
> Reinforced reduces AP component by 1, Devastating is not reduced. Net: still 2 AP.
> 12 → 10 → 8 AP over the engagement. Exoskeleton approaches Dented (threshold: 9 AP).
> 
> After combat: 0 tally marks (non-Enhanced hits all bounced off Soak 4).
> Exoskeleton at 8 AP — Compromised (Soak 2). Needs workshop repair.
> 
> Operative ANVIL invokes Imposing (Severe) as the surviving Tradition members flee.
> Enhancement on intimidation. None of them stop running.

### Example: NWO Deep-Cover Suit

A tailored business suit that conceals woven ballistic panels and micro-reactive fiber that stiffens on impact. Standard issue for senior New World Order field operatives. Looks exactly like an expensive suit from any high-end clothier. Does not look like armor under any normal circumstances.

```
NWO DEEP-COVER SUIT
-------------------
Soak 2 (Ballistic 3)   |   Sturdy 5   |   Concealable   |   Ballistic   |   Technological   |   Authoritative

Condition Thresholds:
  Full: 5 AP  |  Dented: 4 AP (Soak 1)  |  Compromised: 3 AP (Soak 0)  |  Failing: 1–2 AP  |  Destroyed: 0

Initiative: No penalty.
```

Authoritative is a new Tag specific to NWO equipment. It can be invoked whenever projecting institutional authority — flashing credentials, speaking in the voice of someone who expects to be obeyed, presenting oneself as representing official power. The suit looks the part of someone who should not be questioned. It is compelled when the operative is clearly out of their context: a deep-cover suit at a Verbena ritual, a muddy wilderness survival scenario, or anywhere the suit's implicit message of institutional normalcy reads as threat rather than authority.

The suit is optimized for covert urban operation. Its low Sturdy (5 AP) means it cannot take sustained punishment — it is not designed to. An operative who finds themselves in extended combat in this suit is already having a very bad day. It exists to stop the bullet you didn't know was coming and to let you keep functioning long enough to get out.

### Example: Improvised — The Riot Shield

A police-issue polycarbonate riot shield, repurposed by an urban mage who grabbed whatever was available. Not worn — held. Provides protection only to the front and sides, and only when actively interposed.

```
RIOT SHIELD (IMPROVISED)
------------------------
Soak 1 (front/side only, when held)   |   Sturdy 3   |   Improvised   |   Portable   |   Shield

Condition Thresholds:
  Full: 3 AP  |  Dented: 2 AP (Soak 0)  |  Compromised/Failing: 1 AP (Soak 0)  |  Destroyed: 0

Initiative: No penalty, but requires a free hand. Cannot be used with two-handed weapons.
```

Portable means the armor only functions when actively interposed — the character must have a free hand and must declare they are using the shield when attacked from the front or sides. Rear attacks bypass the shield entirely. The Portable Tag is compelled when both hands are needed for something else.

Shield grants a specific bonus: when using the Parry action, the character may invoke Shield for Enhancement on the contested Parry roll. The shield is purpose-built for deflection, and it shows. Improvised is compelled whenever the situation calls for something more sophisticated — climbing with a riot shield in hand, for example, or attempting to operate a vehicle.

With only 3 AP, the riot shield degrades extremely quickly under any serious attack. A single Enhanced hit pushes it to Dented (Soak 0 — functionally useless). It exists to absorb one or two unexpected hits and then be discarded for something better.

## Quick Reference: Armor System

```
ARMOR SYSTEM — QUICK REFERENCE
------------------------------
SOAK: Flat damage reduction per hit. Reduced by condition Tags.
STURDY: Armor Points. Tracks structural integrity.

WHAT STRIPS AP:
  Enhanced attack (any damage after Soak): –1 AP.
  Armor-Piercing Tag (Enhanced or not): –2 AP.
  Devastating + Enhanced: –2 AP.
  Magical Tag (Forces/Prime vs. material): –1 AP regardless of Enhancement.
  End-of-combat wear: 5+ non-Enhanced hits exceeding Soak = –1 AP.
  Non-Enhanced hit equaling Soak exactly: NO tally, NO AP loss.

CONDITION THRESHOLDS (based on % of starting AP):
  Above 75%: Full. No condition tag.
  76%–51%: Dented. Soak –1. Attackers may invoke.
  50%–26%: Compromised. Soak –2. Enemies will invoke.
  25%–1%: Failing. Soak –3. Enhanced attacks exploit freely.
  0%: Destroyed. No Soak. No AP.

MAGICAL ATTACKS bypass Soak entirely unless armor has Warded Tag.

REPAIR: Field (Crafts roll) = restore to top of current tier.
Workshop (Crafts roll, 1 day) = full AP restored.
```

---

# Appendix J: Tags Reference

## How Tags Work in This System

Tags are the descriptive vocabulary of Storypath Ultra. Every character, object, location, and situation in the game can carry Tags — short, evocative phrases that describe meaningful properties. Tags do not merely flavor the fiction; they have mechanical teeth. A Tag can be invoked to add Enhancement dice to a relevant roll, or compelled against its bearer when the Tag's implications create a complication. The interplay of Tags is how the game's fiction and its mechanics remain inseparable.

This appendix catalogs all Tags used in the Mage: The Ascension Storypath Ultra conversion, organized by category. Some Tags are drawn directly from the core Storypath Ultra rules; others are new additions specific to this conversion, created to handle armor condition, magical properties, and the Phase Initiative System. Where a Tag is new to this conversion, it is noted as such.

Tags listed here are not exhaustive of all possible Tags in play. The Storyguide and players are always free to create new Tags on the fly when the fiction demands it. A character can acquire a Tag from a dramatic moment ("This is the sword that killed my mentor — Haunted by Memory"), and locations can carry Tags established by earlier events ("Scene of the Massacre — Soaked in Blood Resonance"). The entries in this appendix are the structured, rules-defined Tags that have specific mechanical definitions. Improvised Tags should be treated consistently: choose how they invoke and how they compel, and hold to that choice.

A note on variable Tags: some Tags carry a numeric value (Soak X, Sturdy X, Enhancement +X). These are written with the variable in the Tag name; the actual number is recorded on the character sheet, armor stat block, or weapon entry. Where the entry says "X" or "Rating," look to the specific item for the value.

## Combat Action Tags

Combat Action Tags describe properties of a specific attack or defensive action at the moment it occurs, rather than permanent properties of a weapon or armor. They arise from how an attack is made, what power is behind it, or what effect it is intended to achieve. These Tags are applied to individual attacks and interact with weapon and armor Tags to determine outcomes.

## Weapon Tags

Weapon Tags are permanent properties of a specific weapon that apply whenever that weapon is used. They describe the weapon's design, construction, and intended use. Multiple Tags on one weapon are common; a weapon's Tag set tells you what it is good at, what it is bad at, and where interesting narrative moments around it might arise.

## Armor Tags — Intrinsic Properties

Intrinsic armor Tags are permanent properties of the armor itself, present from the moment it is worn. They define what the armor is made of, how it was constructed, and what it was designed to do. Unlike condition Tags (which appear as armor is damaged), intrinsic Tags remain constant unless the armor is fundamentally altered. Most armor carries three to six intrinsic Tags.

## Armor Condition Tags

Condition Tags are not part of the armor's original construction — they appear as the armor's Armor Points are depleted through combat. Each condition Tag reduces the armor's effective Soak and creates an invocable vulnerability that enemies can exploit. Condition Tags are removed by successful repair. See Appendix I for the full armor system.

## Character Condition Tags

Character Condition Tags are states applied to a character as a result of injury, magical effects, environmental hazards, or combat outcomes. They modify what the character can do and how effectively they can do it. Most condition Tags have a defined duration or can be removed by specific actions. In the Phase System, some condition Tags specifically affect the initiative pool.

## Environmental and Situational Tags

Environmental and Situational Tags describe conditions of the scene rather than any individual character. They apply to everyone present and can be invoked by anyone whose action would benefit from the condition, or compelled against anyone whose action the condition would hinder. These Tags are established at the start of a scene or arise dynamically as events unfold.

## Magical Tags

Magical Tags describe properties of spells, magical effects, magical items, and the characters who wield them. They govern the Consensus's response to magic, the relationship between mages and their Resonance, and the specific nature of supernatural effects. Most Magical Tags interact directly with the Paradox system.

## Phase Initiative System Tags

These Tags are specific to the Phase Initiative System described in Appendix F. They modify how a character interacts with the Phase structure and are applied through abilities, circumstances, or armor properties.

## SPU Core Weapon Tags

The following tags are drawn directly from the Storypath Ultra core rules and apply to weapons in this game.
