They are distinct from the narrative weapon tags listed earlier (Brutal, Concealable, Reach, etc.), which describe a weapon's character and history. These SPU core tags describe specific mechanical capabilities — Tricks unlocked, structural interactions, and targeted effects. Both sets apply to the same weapons; a knife can be simultaneously Concealable (narrative tag) and Piercing (SPU core tag).

## SPU Core Armor Tags

These armor tags are taken from the Storypath Ultra core rules and supplement the conversion-specific intrinsic armor tags listed earlier. They describe physical construction properties and specific defensive capabilities.

## Tool Tags

Tools are items that are neither weapons nor armor — they exist to help characters do things other than fighting. Tool tags modify Enhancement, define scope, or grant special capabilities to non-combat items. See Appendix R for specific tool stat blocks and Appendix S for crafting tools as part of the Wonder system.

## Vehicle Tags

Vehicle tags define the specific capabilities of a vehicle beyond its base size, speed, handling, and injury ratings. Each vehicle can carry up to its Size in tags; iconic or Enlightened vehicles may carry more. See Appendix T for complete vehicle stat blocks and chase rules.

## Damage Type Tags

Damage type tags indicate what kind of energy or force a weapon delivers and what kind of material or protection an armor is rated against. When a weapon and armor share a damage type tag, the armor's Soak applies normally. When they do not share the relevant tag, the Soak from that armor type does not apply — the attack bypasses the protection entirely and damage goes directly to injury boxes.

Each weapon receives one free damage type tag and may acquire additional damage tags up to its tag limit. Each armor receives one free damage type tag (representing its primary protection rating) and may acquire additional tags up to its tag limit. This system is most important for games where opponents regularly wear armor — it gives mages a reason to think about which Sphere effect best defeats a given target's protection.

## Item and Wonder Tags

These tags describe properties of magical items (Wonders) and are applied during or after the crafting process. They distinguish magical items from mundane ones, define how an item interacts with the Consensus, and describe the nature of any spirit or awareness the item possesses. All Wonders automatically gain the Enchanted tag at minimum.

## Item Flaw Tags

Flaw tags appear on items as a result of failed milestone rolls during Wonder crafting (see Appendix S). Unlike standard tags, Flaws can only be compelled against the item's wielder — never invoked for advantage. They represent instabilities, spiritual resentments, or structural weaknesses baked into the item during creation. Flaws can be removed during crafting with the Quick Fix Trick (2 hits), but Flaws that survive to the final milestone become permanent features of the Wonder.

## Tag Design and Pricing — Getting the Scope Right

Tags are not free-form narrative labels with mechanical teeth bolted on. Each tag has a cost — measured in tag slots on an item — and the cost must reflect what the tag actually delivers. A tag that grants too much for its price slot creates items that are mechanically dominant without being interesting. A tag that is too narrow fails to generate meaningful invocations. Getting the scope right is the central skill in tag design.

The Storypath Ultra core establishes a hierarchy of tag value. Understanding this hierarchy is essential when creating new tags for the Mage setting — particularly for Wonders, which can carry tags well outside the standard weapon/armor/tool categories.

### The Tag Value Hierarchy

Each standard tag slot is worth approximately one of the following:

- +1 Enhancement to the item's primary action — the thing the item was designed to do. A weapon tag that boosts attack rolls. An armor tag that improves defense. A tool tag that helps the skill the tool was built for. This is the baseline.
- Access to one Trick — a specific mechanical option unlocked by the tag (Stun, Wound, Poison, etc.). The Trick's cost in hits determines whether it falls in the normal price range or pushes toward a double-cost tag.
- Reducing the cost of one specific Trick by 1 hit. Narrow in scope; standard price.
- Increasing the cost of one specific Trick against the item's bearer by 1 hit. Defensive benefit; standard price.
- One specific special action — moving after attacking, stealing rather than damaging, adding Enhancement to a narrowly defined non-primary use.
The rule for double-cost tags — tags that consume two tag slots instead of one — is stated directly in the SPU core:

**A tag counts as two tags when it is exceptionally good. This includes: (1) granting +1 Enhancement to a specific action that is not the item's primary use, (2) granting +2 Enhancement to any action (primary or otherwise), or (3) covering two or more distinct skill areas in a single tag.**

That last condition is the one most commonly violated in practice. A tag that says "invoke for Enhancement on Action A or Action B" is granting two benefits for one price. The correct response is either to narrow the tag to one action, or to declare it a double-cost tag and bill the item accordingly.

### Identifying Scope Creep

Scope creep in tag design looks like this: a tag description that contains the word "or" between two different action types, a tag that references two different Sphere categories, or a tag that applies to both offensive and defensive actions simultaneously. Any of these patterns is a signal that the tag is doing too much for a single slot.

Common examples of scope creep, and how to correct them:

| Overscoped Tag (1 slot claimed) | Correct Version |
| --- | --- |
| +2 Enhancement to attack rolls | +1 Enhancement to attack rolls (standard) — OR — counts as 2 tags for +2 |
| +1 Enhancement to Stealth OR Investigation | Pick one. Covering both action types = 2 tags. |
| +1 Enhancement to all social actions | Narrow to one type: Intimidation, Persuasion, etc. Covering all social = 2 tags. |
| +1 to casting AND +1 to relevant skill rolls | Two benefits = 2 tags. Declare it explicitly. |
| Invoke for combat OR for negotiation | A tag that works in two entirely different contexts = 2 tags. |

### Worked Example — The Hacker's Eye Tag

A Virtual Adept player wants to build a custom Cutting-Edge Device: a pair of smart glasses with an integrated Enlightened OS. The player proposes a tag called "Hacker's Eye" and describes it as: "Invoke for +2 dice on any Correspondence casting OR any Technology roll."

This tag has three separate problems:

- It grants +2 Enhancement (not the standard +1). That alone makes it double-cost.
- It covers Correspondence casting — a magical action type.
- It covers Technology rolls — a mundane skill action type. These are two entirely different action categories.
At its stated scope, the Hacker's Eye tag is worth approximately four tag slots: two for the +2 Enhancement, and two more for covering a secondary action type. Claiming it as a single tag would make the smart glasses dramatically overpowered for their item rank.

The correct approach is Option B: declare Hacker's Eye as a double-cost tag explicitly, reduce the Enhancement to +1 (which is all a double-cost tag normally earns for covering a secondary action), and accept that it consumes two of the item's tag slots. This makes the cost honest and the item balanced.

Here is what the corrected item looks like:

```
HACKER'S EYE SMART GLASSES — CORRECTED ITEM BUILD
-------------------------------------------------
Device  •  Cutting-Edge Rank  •  ●●●
Enlightenment: 3  |  Sphere: Data ●●●, Correspondence ●●
Background Cost: 6 pts.

Item Rank: Cutting-Edge. Maximum combined total: 5.

Properties allocated:
  Enhancement: +1 (specialized — Data and Technology actions only) ............. 1 point
  Tag: Concealable (they look like normal glasses) ............................ 1 point
  Tag: Technological .......................................................... 1 point
  Tag: Hacker's Eye [COUNTS AS 2 TAGS] ....................................... 2 points
  TOTAL ....................................................................... 5 points (at maximum)

HACKER'S EYE [counts as 2 tags]:
This tag covers two action categories and therefore consumes two tag slots.
  Invoke (Correspondence casting): +1 Enhancement on any Correspondence Effect while the glasses are active and the mage is using them as a focus.
  Invoke (Technology rolls): +1 Enhancement on any Technology-based action — hacking, device operation, electronic surveillance, systems analysis.
These are not separate +1 bonuses stacked together. Each invoke applies to one action of the appropriate type.
Compel: "The Hacker's Eye is always analyzing. You cannot look at something without cataloguing it. In a social setting, this reads as cold, distracted, and unsettling."

Why this works: The double-cost declaration is explicit and transparent. The item's Cutting-Edge budget (5 points) is fully spent. The Enhancement bonus on the primary item function (+1 specialized) is separate from the Hacker's Eye tag bonus — the tag covers a secondary action type at honest price. The glasses are genuinely useful without being dominant.

What was removed: The original proposal's +2 Enhancement was reduced to +1. That single reduction brings the tag from "four slots worth of benefit" to "two slots worth of benefit" — the correct price for what it delivers.
```

### The Narrowing Test

Before finalizing any tag, apply the narrowing test: ask what is the single most specific description of when this tag activates. If the answer contains "or," "and," or references more than one skill or action type, the tag needs either narrowing or a double-cost declaration.

A useful heuristic: a tag that an item's primary user would invoke more than once per scene is probably covering too much ground. Tags should be meaningful but not ubiquitous. A combat knife's Piercing tag triggers on successful hits that deal damage — not every scene, and not on every action. A pair of hacker's glasses whose Hacker's Eye fires on both casting and skill rolls might activate in nearly every scene, which suggests the tag is doing more work than its slot should allow.

Narrow tags make for better play. A narrowly scoped tag creates a specific moment of relevance — the player recognizes when it applies and makes an active choice to invoke it. A tag that applies to everything slides into background noise. The goal of the tag system is for each tag to be worth remembering and worth invoking, which means each tag needs a clear, specific identity.

> TAG NARROWING IN PRACTICE — THREE VERSIONS OF THE SAME IDEA
> 
> A Technocratic NWO operative wants their standard-issue earpiece to have a communication-enhancement tag. Three possible designs:
> 
> Version 1 — Overscoped (single tag claimed, multiple benefits):
>   "Command Voice: invoke for +2 Enhancement on social rolls OR communications rolls."
>   Problems: +2 Enhancement (double-cost alone), covers two action types. Worth 4 slots. Rejected.
> 
> Version 2 — Narrowed to single action (standard cost):
>   "Command Voice: invoke for +1 Enhancement on Intimidation actions when issuing orders via comms."
>   This is appropriately narrow. One action type (Intimidation), one context (via comms), +1 Enhancement.
>   Cost: 1 tag slot. Accepted.
> 
> Version 3 — Double-cost with explicit declaration (broader but honest):
>   "Command Voice [counts as 2 tags]: +1 Enhancement on both Intimidation actions AND Leadership actions when issuing orders via comms."
>   This covers two social skill types (Intimidation, Leadership) — that breadth costs 2 slots.
>   The item must have 2 tag slots available in its budget for this to work.
>   Cost: 2 tag slots. Accepted, if budget allows.
> 
> Version 2 is the better design for most items. Version 3 is appropriate when the item is thematically defined by this capability and the budget supports it. The operative's earpiece is a Mundane-rank Device (2 tag max) — Version 3 would consume both tag slots entirely on this one property, leaving no room for anything else. Version 2 leaves room for a second tag.

## Tag Creation Guidelines

This section is a complete reference for creating new tags from scratch — for characters, items, Wonders, situations, and environments. It consolidates the scope rules, pricing logic, activation cost structure, compel design principles, and common failure modes into one place. Whether you are designing a custom Wonder, building an NPC's signature ability, or giving a player's heirloom dagger a tag it has earned through play, these guidelines apply.

---

### Part One: What a Tag Is and Is Not

A tag is a descriptive label that has two mechanical functions: it can be **invoked** to gain an advantage, and it can be **compelled** to introduce a complication. That is the entire mechanical contract. A tag is not a passive stat bonus, not a floating pool of dice, and not a rule that simply fires whenever it is relevant. It is a resource — one that requires active engagement from both sides of the table to generate its value.

This means every tag must be designed with both functions in mind. A tag that can be invoked but never meaningfully compelled is a one-sided advantage. A tag that is always being compelled but never meaningfully invoked is a punishment, not a character element. Good tags are genuinely double-edged: the same quality that helps you in one context creates problems in another, and neither function feels forced.

The second thing a tag is not: a tag is not a description. [Blue Eyes] is a description. [Mesmerizing Gaze] is a tag — because it tells you something about when it invokes (+2 dice on an attempt to hold someone's attention), something about when it compels (the Storyguide can trigger it to make you unnervingly visible when you need to be anonymous), and something about the character's relationship to their own nature. The test is not "does this describe something real?" but "does this generate interesting mechanical and narrative moments at the table?"

---

### Part Two: The Four Tag Categories

Tags appear on four types of subjects, and each category has its own conventions.

**Character Tags** attach to a person — player character or NPC. They represent reputation, innate quality, training, supernatural connection, or significant history. Examples: [Former Soldier], [Known to the Consilium], [Speaks in Riddles], [Unbreakable]. Character tags are earned during character creation (via Paths and Backgrounds) or awarded by the Storyguide for significant narrative moments. They persist indefinitely unless the fiction changes them. A character who [Speaks in Riddles] and then undergoes a profound transformation that strips away her old mannerisms may lose the tag — or gain a new one: [No Longer Hiding].

**Item Tags** attach to a specific object — weapon, armor, tool, vehicle, Fetish, or Wonder. They define what the item does especially well, what constraints it operates under, and what complications it creates. Examples: [Precise], [Loud], [Reload 3], [Warded Against Spirits]. Item tags are set at item creation and do not change unless the item is significantly modified. An item that has been damaged in a dramatically significant way may gain a Flaw tag — [Cracked Blade], [Compromised Circuit] — that persists until repaired.

**Situation Tags** attach to a location, scene, or environmental condition. They are created and removed dynamically during play. Examples: [On Fire], [Crowd of Witnesses], [Contested Ground], [Ritual Space Active]. Situation tags are the Storyguide's most flexible tool for making environments mechanically alive. Any character can invoke a relevant situation tag; any character can be compelled by one. A [Crowd of Witnesses] tag can be invoked by a mage trying to blend in and disappear into the crowd, and compelled against the same mage when they want to cast Vulgar magic.

**Magical Effect Tags** attach to a specific active magical working or its sustained result. They describe the nature of the working in mechanically relevant terms. Examples: [Vulgar], [Witnessed], [Resonance: Entropy], [Sustained — Concentration Required]. Magical effect tags interact with Paradox, Sphere Defenses, and dispelling mechanics. When a Sphere Defense uses the same Sphere as an incoming attack, the interaction is partly governed by these tags.

---

### Part Three: Activation Modes

Every tag has an activation mode — the condition under which its mechanical function triggers. There are five:

#### Mode 1: Passive (Always-On)

The tag's benefit applies constantly, with no activation required and no resource cost. The tag simply describes a permanent truth about the subject.

**Examples:** [Armored 2] on a breastplate. [Waterproof] on a diving suit. [Fireproof] on an Awakened salamander spirit. [Enhanced Stamina 1] on a Life-augmented mage.

**When to use passive mode:** For physical properties of items that would be absurd to require activation for — you do not spend Momentum to make your armor stop a bullet. Also for constant supernatural properties that define what something is rather than what it does: a Paradox spirit's [Intangible] tag is always true, not activated.

**Pricing passive tags:** Passive tags are priced at their specific scope. A passive +1 to soak is worth one tag slot because it is always-on but narrow in effect. A passive +2 to soak is worth two tag slots. A passive benefit that applies to two different action types is worth two tag slots even if each component is modest — the constant availability of both effects is the premium.

**The passive trap:** Do not give passive tags broad scope. A tag that passively grants Enhancement on all social rolls is dominating — it fires in every social scene, every session, with no cost. The same benefit as an invoke-mode tag (1 Momentum for +2 dice, once per Spotlight) is a fair price for breadth. A passive tag should be narrow enough that it only fires in a specific subset of situations, so the constant-availability premium is balanced by genuine specificity.

> **Example — Passive tag designed correctly:**
> The [Surgical Grip] tag on a scalpel-form ritual knife. Invoke: not applicable — passive. Effect: when used in a Life-sphere medical working, treat the knife as a Dedicated Instrument (see Instruments), granting Enhancement +1 without requiring the mage to spend the normal dedication downtime. This applies only in Life medical workings with the knife in hand — a very specific intersection of conditions. The always-on benefit is real but narrow. One tag slot.

---

#### Mode 2: Standard Invoke (1 Momentum)

The player spends 1 Momentum when the tag is genuinely relevant to the current action and gains +2 dice. One invoke per tag per Spotlight. The Storyguide may grant a free invoke when the fiction particularly calls for it — no Momentum required.

This is the baseline activation mode. It is the mode assumed when a tag is described without specifying otherwise. It is the cheapest activation cost and the most common one in play.

**When to use standard invoke mode:** For character tags that represent cultivated skill or meaningful reputation. For item tags that describe a specific situational advantage. For anything where the benefit should be deliberate — the player should have to notice the relevance and choose to spend.

**The invoke constraint — once per Spotlight:** You cannot invoke the same tag twice on the same action or in the same Spotlight. If [Fast] applies to your attack, you get one +2 dice bonus for [Fast] this Spotlight, regardless of how fast the action actually is. This prevents invoke stacking on a single die roll.

**The free invoke:** The Storyguide can offer a free invoke at any time when the tag is dramatically perfect. "You're using your [Former Military Sniper] tag — this is exactly the kind of long-distance elimination you were trained for. Take the invoke, no cost." Free invokes are gifts from the fiction; they reward good positioning, clever setup, and Tags that were earned narratively rather than just purchased.

> **Example — Standard invoke tag designed correctly:**
> [Unnerving Precision] on a Euthanatos mage's character sheet. Invoke: spend 1 Momentum when making a social roll that benefits from projecting lethal certainty — negotiating from a position of "I can end this" rather than "please cooperate." Effect: +2 dice. Compel: the Storyguide introduces a moment where the mage's precision reads as cold rather than confident — a potential ally is frightened rather than impressed, or a normally cooperative NPC finds a reason to distance themselves. One character tag slot (free — character tags do not cost Advancement Points unless linked to a Background).

---

#### Mode 3: Once Per Scene (Special Effect)

The tag produces a specific effect — often replacing a roll entirely or generating an automatic result — that can only be triggered once per scene. This mode costs 1 Momentum to activate in most cases, or 0 Momentum if the Path or Wonder's design specifies it fires without cost.

This is the mode used for Path special abilities ("Once per scene, spend 1 Momentum to diagnose any illness by touch, no roll required") and for Wonders with limited activations. It is more powerful than a standard invoke because it bypasses the dice entirely — instead of +2 dice on a roll you might fail, you simply succeed at the specific thing the tag covers.

**The power-cost relationship:** A once-per-scene effect that replaces a roll is worth approximately one Momentum spend per activation. A once-per-scene effect that adds more than +2 dice (say, adds your full Sphere rating as bonus dice) costs 1 Momentum and carries a once-per-scene limit that together constitute the full price. A once-per-scene effect that also requires a Quintessence expenditure in addition to Momentum is in the territory of Wonder activation rather than tag activation.

**Pricing once-per-scene tags:** These are one-slot tags for narrow, specific effects. If the once-per-scene effect is broad — replacing any social roll, diagnosing any condition regardless of cause — it is a two-slot tag, because the breadth of what it covers compensates for the frequency limit. A once-per-scene tag that covers a very narrow specific circumstance can be modestly broad in what it produces, because the circumstance is the real constraint.

> **Example — Once per scene tag designed correctly:**
> [Witch's Mark] on Rosamund's Major Path. Activation: spend 1 Momentum. Effect: diagnose any natural illness or wound by touch, no roll required. Limit: once per scene. Compel: Rosamund's mark becomes visible under spiritual examination — a Storyguide can compel this tag to make her identifiable to entities or practitioners who can read such things, creating recognition she did not want. One Path special ability slot.

> **Example — Once per scene tag that is too broad (requires revision):**
> [Diagnostician's Eye]: spend 1 Momentum to diagnose any condition — illness, magical curse, spiritual possession, Awakened nature — without a roll, once per scene. This covers natural illness AND magical effects AND spirit states AND Awakened detection simultaneously. That is four distinct diagnostic domains with no limitation between them. Even once per scene, this is worth at least two slots because it fires across too many situation types. Corrected: split into [Witch's Mark] (natural illness/wounds) and [Spirit Sight] (spirit and Awakened detection), each costing one slot. The mage can have both, but each costs separately.

---

#### Mode 4: Once Per Session (Exceptional Effect)

The tag produces a significant effect usable only once per session. This is rarer than once per scene and reserved for effects with meaningful dramatic weight — things that should feel like turning points, not routine tools.

**When to use once per session:** When the effect is powerful enough that once per scene would be game-changing. Adding your full Sphere rating as bonus dice to any relevant roll. Automatically succeeding at any action in a specific category. Resisting a specific type of damage or effect entirely for one instance.

**Cost:** Once-per-session effects typically require 2 Momentum, or 1 Momentum plus a secondary cost (1 Willpower, 1 Quintessence), to activate. The frequency restriction does most of the pricing work — the activation cost is the secondary brake.

> **Example — Once per session tag:**
> [Avatar's Grace] as a special ability for a mage with Arete ●●●●●. Activation: spend 2 Momentum. Effect: treat one magical roll this session as if it were a Rote — apply no freeform difficulty penalty, apply the Rote Speed Factor. Limit: once per session. Compel: the Avatar's presence is visible to those who can see such things for the rest of the session, whether the mage wants it or not. This is a powerful effect — it removes a major casting obstacle for one roll — and the once-per-session limit is the primary constraint. Two Momentum ensures it feels costly.

---

#### Mode 5: Resource Cost (Quintessence or Willpower)

Some tags activate by spending a specific resource rather than Momentum. This is standard for Wonders (which spend Primal Energy / Quintessence) and for effects tied to personal essence (which spend Willpower).

**Quintessence-cost tags:** Used for Wonders whose effects are channeled magical energy. The Quintessence cost scales with the power of the effect: 1 Quintessence for a moderate effect (Enhancement +2 on a specific action, or a small automatic result), 2 Quintessence for a powerful effect (an automatic success on a challenging task, or an effect that would require multiple hits on a normal roll), 3+ Quintessence for dramatic Wonder-level effects (large-area attacks, permanent alterations, effects that require Sphere 4-5 to replicate with freeform magic).

**Willpower-cost tags:** Used for effects tied directly to the character's personal will and identity — effects that should feel like a significant personal expenditure, not just a tactical spend. Willpower is scarcer than Momentum and more personally meaningful. A Willpower-cost tag can be somewhat broader in scope than a Momentum-cost tag because the currency is more precious. Spending 1 Willpower to activate a tag that covers two action types is appropriate; spending 1 Momentum for the same breadth would require the double-cost declaration.

**Mixed costs:** Some activations require both a primary resource and Momentum. A Wonder that costs 1 Quintessence + 1 Momentum to activate has a meaningful total price — the Quintessence represents the magical energy expended, the Momentum represents the player's narrative investment in the moment. These mixed-cost activations should produce commensurately significant effects.

> **Example — Quintessence-cost tag on a Wonder:**
> A Hermetic Order mirror, enchanted as a scrying focus. Tag: [Correspondence Window]. Activation: spend 1 Quintessence. Effect: the mirror becomes a two-way sensory link to any location the mage has visited or has a sympathetic connection to. Duration: one scene. This is a significant effect — effectively a free Correspondence 2 working — but it requires a Wonder (Background cost), Quintessence expenditure per use, and has no combat application. The Quintessence cost and the item requirement together constitute the full price. No Momentum required: the Wonder's existence is the Momentum-equivalent investment.

> **Example — Willpower-cost tag on a character:**
> [Iron Will] on an Akashic Brother who has trained specifically to resist psychic intrusion. Activation: spend 1 Willpower when a mental or emotional Sphere effect would affect you. Effect: treat your Composure + Resolve as if it were 2 points higher for this resistance roll, and reduce the attacker's effective hits by 1 before applying effects. This covers all mental/emotional Sphere effects — broader than a standard invoke's single action type. The Willpower cost (scarce, meaningful, personal) justifies the breadth. Compel: [Iron Will] can be compelled to make the character emotionally rigid — unable to respond to a genuine appeal for trust or cooperation because the walls are always up.

---

### Part Four: Designing the Compel

Every tag must have a viable compel. This is not optional — a tag without a genuine compel is mechanically incomplete. If the Storyguide cannot think of how to compel a tag in the context of normal play, the tag is either too narrow (a pure mechanical bonus with no narrative texture) or too positive (it describes a trait with no downside).

**The compel structure:** A compel takes the tag's defining quality and finds a situation where that same quality creates a problem. The quality does not change — only the context around it makes it a problem rather than an asset.

| Tag | What makes it an asset | What makes it a problem (compel) |
|---|---|---|
| [Precise] | +2 dice on careful, targeted actions | You cannot respond to a situation that requires broad, improvised action — the need for precision becomes paralysis |
| [Loud] | Devastating presence; enemies know you are serious | You announce your position; Sleepers call the police; ambush impossible |
| [Known to the Consilium] | Opens doors, grants credibility | Creates accountability; cannot operate anonymously; rivals know where to find you |
| [Former Soldier] | Training fires in crisis situations | The training also fires in situations where it should not — threat assessment as paranoia, discipline as emotional distance |
| [Unbreakable] | Cannot be intimidated or broken | Cannot back down when backing down is the wise choice; a compel might represent the moment where walking away is the correct play and you literally cannot |

**The compel as invitation, not penalty:** The Storyguide offers a compel; the player accepts or refuses. If the player accepts, they gain 1 Momentum and deal with the complication. If they refuse, they spend 1 Momentum to maintain control of the scene. This means a well-designed compel should feel like a genuine offer — the player should be tempted to accept because the Momentum gain is valuable and the complication, while real, is dramatically interesting rather than simply punishing.

A compel that is purely punishing ("your tag fires and you lose an action for no interesting reason") is a bad compel. A compel that creates a problem the player would actually enjoy playing through is a good compel. The best compels create problems that connect to the character's deepest tensions — the very thing that makes them interesting.

**Designing compels for always-on passive tags:** Passive tags still need compels, but the compel targets the tag's descriptive reality rather than its activation. A [Surgical Grip] knife can be compelled when the mage needs to conceal the knife's nature — its obviously medical precision form makes it recognizable as a ritual instrument to anyone who knows what they are looking at. The compel does not deactivate the passive benefit; it generates a narrative complication alongside it.

> **Worked Example — Compel for a Wonder tag:**
> A Verbena mage carries an enchanted bone needle used for healing workings. Tag: [Drawn to Wounds]. Standard invoke: +2 dice on Life healing workings with the needle in hand. Compel: the Storyguide notices that a significant NPC in the scene has an old wound that has never fully healed — something the bone needle's resonance picks up on. "The needle pulls toward the wound. You feel the old hurt in your own hand, an echo of whatever injury this person carries. You can ignore it — spend 1 Momentum — or you can let it draw your attention to this person right now, gain 1 Momentum, and deal with the fact that you are suddenly very aware of a stranger's pain at the worst possible moment for that awareness." The player accepts: 1 Momentum gained. The scene now has a thread the Storyguide did not plan and the player chose to pull on. That is a working compel.

---

### Part Five: The Complete Tag Design Process

Follow these steps in order when creating a new tag.

**Step 1 — Name the quality.** What single characteristic does this tag describe? Name it in a way that is evocative and specific. Not [Good Fighter] (too vague) but [Relentless Pressure] or [Duelist's Economy]. Not [Smart] but [Reads the Room] or [Pattern Analyst]. The name should already suggest both its invoke and its compel.

**Step 2 — Write the invoke.** Complete this sentence: "I invoke this tag when _____, and the benefit is _____." The when should be specific enough that not every scene qualifies. The benefit should be either +2 dice, access to a specific Trick at reduced cost, or a clearly bounded special effect. If you cannot write the invoke sentence without using the word "or" between two different action types, the tag is covering too much ground.

**Step 3 — Determine the activation mode.** Is this passive (always true), standard invoke (1 Momentum), once per scene, once per session, or resource-cost? Match the mode to the power of the effect. Passive requires the narrowest scope. Once per session tolerates the broadest scope per activation. Resource-cost requires the benefit to match the currency's scarcity.

**Step 4 — Apply the cost hierarchy.** Count tag slots:

| What the tag delivers | Slot cost |
|---|---|
| Passive: +1 Enhancement to one specific action type | 1 slot |
| Passive: any benefit covering two action types | 2 slots |
| Invoke: +2 dice to one specific action type | 1 slot |
| Invoke: +2 dice to two action types ("or") | 2 slots |
| Invoke: +1 Enhancement to non-primary item action | 2 slots |
| Invoke: +2 Enhancement to any action | 2 slots |
| Once per scene: replace one specific roll entirely | 1 slot |
| Once per scene: broad effect across multiple domains | 2 slots |
| Access to one specific Trick | 1 slot |
| Reduce one specific Trick's hit cost by 1 | 1 slot |
| Increase one specific Trick's hit cost against bearer by 1 | 1 slot |

**Step 5 — Write the compel.** Complete this sentence: "The Storyguide can compel this tag when _____, and the complication is _____." If you cannot complete this sentence with a situation that would genuinely occur in normal play, the tag is too narrow or too uniformly positive. Revise either the name or the framing until a compelling compel becomes obvious.

**Step 6 — Apply the narrowing test.** Read the invoke text aloud. If it contains "or," "and," "any," or references more than one skill category, stop and narrow. Ask: what is the single most specific description of when this tag activates? That description should be the invoke. If the broader version is what you actually want, declare it a double-cost tag explicitly.

**Step 7 — Check for table feel.** Ask: will players remember this tag exists? Will it create memorable moments? A tag that fires in approximately one scene per session is calibrated correctly. A tag that fires constantly is too broad. A tag that almost never fires is too narrow. Target one clear, memorable invoke per session as the design goal.

---

### Part Six: Worked Examples — Full Design Process

#### Example A: A Character Tag for a Technocracy Operative

The NWO field agent concept: a specialist in social manipulation who has spent fifteen years running assets, breaking covers, and building false identities.

**Step 1 — Name the quality:** [The Mask Fits].

**Step 2 — Write the invoke:** "I invoke [The Mask Fits] when I am maintaining a false identity or persona under scrutiny — when someone is actively trying to see through the cover. +2 dice to any social roll made to sustain the deception."

**Step 3 — Activation mode:** Standard invoke, 1 Momentum.

**Step 4 — Cost:** One action type (maintaining a deception under scrutiny), +2 dice. One tag slot.

**Step 5 — Write the compel:** "The Storyguide compels [The Mask Fits] when the agent encounters someone from their actual past — someone who knew them before the masks, who calls them by their real name or references something only the real person would know. The mask slips. Not all the way, but enough. The agent gains 1 Momentum and has to navigate the moment where the person they were and the person they perform intersect dangerously."

**Step 6 — Narrowing test:** One action type. No "or." No breadth concerns. Passes.

**Step 7 — Table feel:** This fires in any scene involving sustained deception under pressure — approximately one meaningful scene per session for a spy-adjacent character. Players will remember it. The compel creates the single most dramatically interesting moment for this archetype. Approved.

---

#### Example B: An Item Tag for a Verbena Athame

The item concept: a ritual dagger that has drawn blood in hundreds of workings and carries the accumulated resonance of all that sacrifice.

**Step 1 — Name the quality:** [Blood Memory].

**Step 2 — Write the invoke:** "I invoke [Blood Memory] when performing a Life or Prime working in which the knife has already drawn blood this scene. +2 dice to the casting roll, as the accumulated resonance of past sacrifices amplifies the current working."

**Step 3 — Activation mode:** Standard invoke, 1 Momentum.

**Step 4 — Cost:** One action type (casting Life or Prime with the knife) — note this is two Spheres, which might suggest double-cost. However, the constraint "with the knife," "after drawing blood," and "this scene" together constitute a single, specific situation type rather than two separate action categories. The Spheres are contextual qualifiers, not separate action domains. One tag slot.

**Step 5 — Write the compel:** "The Storyguide compels [Blood Memory] when the knife is present in a scene where an innocent is harmed — the knife's resonance connects to the blood, regardless of whose it is or whether magic is involved. The mage feels every working the knife has ever participated in as a flash of memory and emotion. They gain 1 Momentum and must deal with the fact that they are momentarily somewhere else — not incapacitated, but rattled, present in two times simultaneously."

**Step 6 — Narrowing test:** The invoke specifies Life or Prime — two Spheres. Is this scope creep? No, because both Spheres are thematically unified by the knife's blood-and-sacrifice resonance. A ruling: if the two Sphere types share a tight thematic unity that the tag's name reflects, covering both in one invoke is acceptable at standard cost. If they are thematically unrelated, it is double-cost. [Blood Memory] invokes Life and Prime because blood is both a biological substance (Life) and a source of primal magical energy (Prime) — the unity is genuine. Passes.

**Step 7 — Table feel:** Fires when the mage is using their signature instrument in their signature style — reliably, but not constantly. The compel is emotionally rich. Approved.

---

#### Example C: A Situation Tag Created Mid-Combat

The scene: a warehouse fire started by a Nephandi's Entropy working. The Storyguide places a situation tag on the scene: [Spreading Fire].

**Invocable by:** Anyone who can use the fire to their advantage — pushing an enemy toward the flames, using the heat as cover for a freeform Forces working (the fire is already happening, making a fire effect Coincidental), or using the chaos to break pursuit.

**Compellable against:** Anyone in the scene. The Storyguide can compel [Spreading Fire] against a mage mid-working to force a Concentration check as a burning beam falls, against a character moving across the warehouse floor to impose a penalty or forced repositioning, or against anyone trying to have a careful conversation in a place that is actively becoming an oven.

**Removing the tag:** [Spreading Fire] can be removed from the scene by a successful Forces or Life/Matter working to contain it, by the fire consuming everything flammable in the area (which the Storyguide can compel to describe the warehouse becoming a total loss), or by the scene ending.

**Design note:** Situation tags do not cost tag slots — they are free Storyguide tools. Their value is entirely in how consistently they are invoked and compelled. A situation tag that nobody touches in play was a wasted declaration. The Storyguide should create situation tags that they fully intend to interact with mechanically at least twice before the scene ends.

---

### Part Seven: Common Mistakes in Tag Design

**Mistake 1 — The description tag.** A tag that describes something without generating mechanical moments. [Brown Hair] is the extreme example, but the mistake appears in more subtle forms: [Experienced Mage], [Member of the Chantry], [Has a Car]. These describe facts about the character but do not create invoke or compel opportunities. Every tag must answer: *when does the Storyguide pick this up and do something with it?*

**Mistake 2 — The pure bonus tag.** A tag designed as +Enhancement with no genuine compel, or with a compel so mild it is never worth the Storyguide's attention. [Very Good Aim] with compel "sometimes you miss" is a pure bonus tag. The compel must create narrative problems, not simply describe the action failing. Good compel for an aim-related tag: "Your precision requires time and stillness. In a chaotic, fast-moving situation, [Surgical Precision] can be compelled to mean you freeze for a moment trying to find the clean shot — and the situation does not wait."

**Mistake 3 — The universal tag.** A tag that applies to almost every scene. [Intelligent] or [Adaptable] or [Good Social Skills] — anything that would fire constantly. These feel good on the sheet and disappear into background noise at the table. Narrow them: not [Intelligent] but [Pattern Analyst: Social Structures], which invokes when reading organizational behavior and compels when the analysis is applied in a situation that required intuition rather than pattern-matching.

**Mistake 4 — The compel-immune tag.** Some tags feel impossible to compel because they describe purely physical properties. [Fireproof] on a wonder — when does the Storyguide compel that? Design answer: the [Fireproof] tag's compel is not about the fire, it's about what the fireproofing implies. "The Wonder is fireproof, which means it was designed for fire environments — and right now you need something delicate, precise, and subtle, and the hammer you are holding is emphatically not that." Every passive physical tag has a compel in what it implies about the object's nature and intended use.

**Mistake 5 — The undeclared double-cost tag.** The single most common practical mistake. A player proposes a tag that grants +2 dice on two different action types and the Storyguide approves it as a single-slot tag because both action types seem related. The item becomes more powerful than intended, the budget miscounts, and future items feel underpowered by comparison. Declare double-cost explicitly whenever the tag covers more than one action category, and track it clearly on the item sheet.

---

```
TAG CREATION — QUICK REFERENCE CHECKLIST
-----------------------------------------
1. NAME: Does the name evoke both invoke and compel without explanation?
2. INVOKE: One sentence. One action type. No "or." Benefit: +2 dice, Trick access, or bounded special effect.
3. MODE: Passive / Standard Invoke / Once Per Scene / Once Per Session / Resource Cost
4. COST (slot count):
   +2 dice, one action type = 1 slot
   +2 dice, two action types = 2 slots
   +2 Enhancement (any) = 2 slots
   +1 Enhancement to non-primary action = 2 slots
   Once-per-scene special effect, narrow = 1 slot
   Once-per-scene special effect, broad = 2 slots
5. COMPEL: One sentence. Same quality, different context, real problem. Tempting to accept.
6. NARROWING TEST: Read invoke aloud. If "or" appears between action types → narrow or declare double-cost.
7. TABLE FEEL: Fires ~once per session? Memorable moment? Compel is dramatically interesting?
```

## Quick Reference: Tag Interaction Summary

The following summary captures the most common Tag interactions at a glance for Storyguides managing fast-moving combat.

```
TAG INTERACTIONS — COMBAT QUICK REFERENCE
-----------------------------------------
ATTACK TAGS vs. ARMOR TAGS:
  Ballistic attack + Ballistic armor = Soak effectively +1.
  Ballistic attack + non-Ballistic armor = standard Soak.
  Magical attack + Warded armor = Soak applies (magical type must match).
  Magical attack + non-Warded armor = Soak bypassed entirely.
  Armor-Piercing attack + Reinforced armor = 1 AP stripped, not 2.
  Armor-Piercing attack + standard armor = 2 AP stripped.
  Enhanced attack + any armor = 1 AP stripped (if damage exceeds Soak).

CONDITION TAGS — WHO CAN INVOKE:
  Dented, Compromised, Failing: Attackers who know the armor is damaged.
  The armored character cannot invoke their own damage tags.

ENVIRONMENTAL TAGS — BOTH SIDES INVOKE:
  Darkness: attacker invokes if they have low-light vision; defender invokes for concealment.
  Cover: defender invokes; attacker may invoke to force movement.

MAGICAL TAGS — PARADOX TRIGGERS:
  Vulgar alone: base Paradox pool.
  Vulgar + Witnessed: +dice per witness group.
  Paradox-Scarred location: +1 die regardless of coincidental vs. vulgar.
  Tapestry-Stable: no Paradox, ever.
```

---

# Appendix K: Sanity — The Fraying Edge of the Awakened Mind

Mages see too much. That is the plain truth beneath every poetic account of Awakening, every description of the Spheres opening like flowers in the mind. To perceive the Tapestry as it truly is — infinite, alive, deeply indifferent, and occasionally actively malevolent — is to subject the human mind to pressures it was never designed to bear. Most Sleepers are protected from this by the consensus reality that mages struggle to transcend. The Awakened have no such protection. They bought clarity with sanity, and the ledger keeps running.

The Quiet Track presented in this appendix models the slow erosion of a mage's psychological grounding as they wade deeper into the numinous terrors of the World of Darkness. It is deliberately structured to parallel the Health track: a fixed-band system where your key mental attribute — Composure — determines how much punishment you can absorb before penalties begin, while fixed bands beyond that guarantee every character experiences the full range of psychological consequence. Like the Health track, the Quiet Track distinguishes between damage types, because not all horrors are equal. The shock of finding a murder scene is not the same wound as watching the murder happen in front of you. Watching a murder is not the same wound as looking into the eyes of something that should not exist under any conception of reality.

This system integrates with Storypath Ultra's Tag framework. Each Quiet band carries an implicit Tag that the Storyguide can invoke or compel. It also ties directly to the Paradox system: Paradox is the primary source of Void damage, the deepest and most permanent category of psychological harm. A mage who pushes their Paradox Pool recklessly is not merely risking backlash — they are chipping away at their own grip on shared reality, one Void mark at a time.

The defining feature of this system — the mechanic most worth understanding before anything else — is the Paradox Insulation effect that emerges as the Quiet Track fills. A mage deep in the Fracture band begins to partially slip out of the consensus Tapestry's reach. The Tapestry's corrective pressure loses purchase on a mind that no longer shares enough assumptions about reality to be effectively corrected. This is exactly what the Traditions warn about in their accounts of Marauders: the fully broken mage is Paradox-insulated precisely because there is no longer a coherent self for Paradox to punish.

## The Quiet Track — Band Structure

The Quiet Track uses the same four-band structure as the Health track, mirroring the SPU injury level design. Composure is to the Quiet Track what Stamina is to Health: it extends the width of the first and most forgiving band, giving psychologically resilient characters more room to absorb horror before the mechanical penalties begin. Every character, regardless of Composure, experiences all four bands as damage accumulates.

| Quiet Band | Boxes \| Penalty \| Description |
| --- | --- |
| STATIC | 2 + Composure boxes \| No mechanical penalty \| Perceptual glitches, minor hallucinations, occasional echoes of things not present. The mage is shaken but fully functional. Tag: [Static Perception] |
| DISTORTION | 2 boxes \| −1 die to all Social and Empathy rolls \| Reality noticeably warps around the mage. Sleepers sense wrongness. Social presence is compromised — the mage projects a subtle aura of wrongness that others feel without knowing why. Tag: [Distorted] |
| FRACTURE | 2 boxes \| −1 die to ALL rolls \| Internal experience diverges meaningfully from consensus. Active hallucinations begin. Some actions require a Composure + Resolve check (Difficulty 1) just to initiate. Paradox Insulation begins: reduce Paradox dice pool results by 1 (minimum 0 Void results). Tag: [Fractured] |
| FULL QUIET | 1 box \| Cannot take voluntary actions \| Complete break. The mage has retreated into their own interior reality. They respond to people who are not there; they cannot act on external reality in any coordinated way. They are effectively absent until rescued or the Quiet resolves spontaneously. Tag: [Quiet] |

Total Quiet track boxes = 7 + Composure. A character with Composure 1 has 8 Quiet boxes: three Static, two Distortion, two Fracture, one Full Quiet. A character with Composure 3 has 10 boxes: five Static, two Distortion, two Fracture, one Full Quiet. A character with Composure 5 has 12 boxes: seven Static, two Distortion, two Fracture, one Full Quiet. Record your band sizes on the character sheet at character creation.

Damage fills the Quiet Track from left to right across all bands in sequence. Filling all boxes in a band does not trigger a special event — the penalties are continuous and begin the moment you enter a band, not when you complete it. What matters is which band your current damage total has pushed you into. Filling the single Full Quiet box triggers an immediate Quiet episode.

## Quiet Damage Types

Three types of damage fill boxes on the Quiet Track: Shock, Trauma, and Void. They occupy the same boxes in the same left-to-right sequence — damage type determines how the damage was caused, how severe its psychological texture is, and how difficult it is to heal. The type does not determine which boxes it occupies; it fills the next available box regardless of type. What the type does determine is the nature of the experience in those boxes — a mage in the Distortion band filled with Shock damage has a qualitatively different experience than a mage in the same band filled with Void damage.

Mark damage types using the following notation in each box, exactly as Bashing (B), Lethal (L), and Aggravated (A) are marked on the Health track:

| Damage Type | Mark \| Cause \| Nature of the Wound |
| --- | --- |
| SHOCK | / (forward slash)   \| Ordinary human horror — viscerally disturbing but comprehensible within the frame of human experience \| Shock is the sharp intake of breath, the nausea, the jumpiness that follows a bad night. The mind has been pushed but not fundamentally altered. |
| TRAUMA | X (full cross)      \| Severe or sustained psychological damage — experiences that fundamentally challenge the character's sense of safety, morality, or the comprehensibility of reality \| Trauma is the wound that does not close easily: the recurring dream, the flinch at a sound, the moment of dissociation that appears without warning. |
| VOID | * (asterisk)        \| Cosmic fracture — contact with realities so fundamentally alien that the human mind has no category for them, or severe Paradox exposure that scars the pattern of the self \| Void damage is not merely psychological trauma. It is a structural change in how the mage's mind processes reality. Some Void damage may be permanent. |

### Shock — The Accessible Horror

Shock damage represents the impact of ordinary human horrors on the mage's psyche. These are experiences that are deeply disturbing, sometimes horrifying, but that remain within the frame of what human minds were built to eventually process: violence, death, loss, betrayal, the unexpected supernatural intrusion into a scene that felt safe.

Examples of events that deal Shock damage: discovering a mutilated corpse; witnessing unexpected violence; being the target of a serious physical assault; encountering a spirit or entity for the first time, without prior warning or preparation; experiencing a scene of profound emotional horror (discovering a loved one has been victimized); the minor supernatural disorientation of a first Paradox Cascade; witnessing another mage's Static or Distortion-level Quiet episode from a safe distance.

Shock damage is the most common type and the most recoverable. The mind was not built to be untouchable — it was built to absorb, process, and eventually integrate even difficult experiences. Shock respects that architecture. Given time and safety, it heals without scars.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Shock in context: Tobias and his cabalmate Sera investigate a crime scene that turns out to be far worse than the police report suggested — three victims, ritualistic markings, and evidence of something inhuman in the room. Tobias takes 1 Shock (finding the scene). Sera, who knows enough about the supernatural to recognize the markings as Nephandic, takes 2 Shock — her knowledge makes it worse, not better, because she understands what she is looking at. Neither character enters a new Quiet band; they are both in the Static range. Both are shaken, hyper-alert, prone to jumping at sounds. After a session away from heavy horror and a conversation with their Chantry elder, both clear their Shock boxes without a roll. The scene left marks, but no permanent scars.

### Trauma — The Sustainable Wound

Trauma damage represents deep psychological harm from experiences that cannot be easily integrated into a functional worldview: events that fundamentally challenge the mage's sense of safety, their moral framework, or their ability to trust their own perception of reality. Where Shock is a punch that leaves a bruise, Trauma is a wound that requires genuine attention to close.

Examples of events that deal Trauma damage: witnessing a murder directly, especially of someone the mage knows; being the instrument, even unwillingly, of serious harm to innocents; extended captivity with psychological abuse; sustained Paradox exposure across multiple scenes without recovery time; direct contact with a vampire's Domination or Presence powers that undermines the mage's sense of autonomous will; the specific horror of watching a trusted colleague cross the Threshold into Marauder behavior; surviving a Paradox Cascade that destroys something or someone meaningful.

Trauma requires active effort to heal. Time alone is not enough — the mind needs engagement, not just absence of further harm. Left unaddressed, Trauma boxes remain filled indefinitely. They do not worsen on their own, but they create a reduced baseline for absorbing further damage. A mage already carrying two Trauma boxes is much closer to the Distortion band than they feel, and the next bad night can push them over.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Trauma accumulation: Rhen, a Verbena with Composure 2, has a Quiet track of 9 boxes: four Static, two Distortion, two Fracture, one Full Quiet. Over the course of a difficult chronicle arc, he accumulates: 2 Shock (a particularly brutal confrontation) and 1 Trauma (he was forced to let an innocent die to complete a ritual). His track shows //X — three boxes filled, all in the Static band. He is shaken but not yet penalized. Then comes the breaking point: a scene where his mentor is killed in front of him by an NWO strike team. He takes 2 Trauma. His track now shows //XXX — five boxes filled. He has cleared the Static band and entered Distortion. His Social and Empathy rolls take −1 die. He's not broken, but he is noticeably off. Other mages who know him notice that something is wrong. Recovery will require real effort: at least two sessions of active therapeutic work.

### Void — The Cosmic Fracture

Void damage represents fundamental fractures in the mage's psyche from contact with realities so alien that the human mind has no category for them, or from exposure to Paradox severe enough to scar the pattern of the self. Void damage is not merely deep trauma — it is a structural change. A Void mark is a place where the mage's grip on consensus reality has been permanently loosened. The mind was not built to look at what caused this wound and remain intact. Something changed. Recovery is possible, but it is slow, difficult, and never certain.

Examples of events that deal Void damage: direct, sustained contact with an Outer God or entity of the Deep Umbra whose very existence violates the rules of comprehensible reality; time spent inside a Paradox Realm — the artificial pocket realities that severe Paradox creates — which deal 1 Void per day in the Realm; contact with a Nephandi's fully manifested Awakened pattern (their reality literally invades yours); witnessing a Convergence event (multiple Paradox storms merging); severe Paradox Catastrophe results that deal damage directly to the caster's pattern; a Seeking that goes catastrophically wrong and shatters rather than illuminates.

Void damage is recoverable, but the recovery is measured in story arcs rather than scenes or sessions. Some Void boxes — those earned from the most extreme exposures — may be ruled by the Storyguide as permanent: they represent changes so fundamental that recovery would require a story of its own, a full arc of extraordinary intervention, or possibly represent permanent alterations to who this mage is. Not all wounds can be healed. Not all changes can be reversed.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Void from Paradox Realm: Maya, a Dreamspeaker with Composure 3, has 10 Quiet boxes: five Static, two Distortion, two Fracture, one Full Quiet. Her track currently shows 3 Shock and 1 Trauma — four boxes, all in the Static band. A severe Paradox Catastrophe during a battle traps her in a Paradox Realm for what feels like days. Each subjective day in the Realm deals 1 Void. She experiences three subjective days before her cabal reaches her. When she emerges, she adds 3 Void marks: her track now reads ///* X ** — four Static boxes (3 Shock, 1 Void) and then the Trauma box, followed by two more Void marks that push her into the Distortion band. She is now at −1 die to all Social and Empathy rolls. The two Void marks in the Distortion band will require extraordinary intervention to clear — not ordinary therapy, but magical treatment or a significant story event. The Shock and Trauma marks are more manageable. But the damage done by the Paradox Realm is a different kind of wound entirely.

## Overflow and Escalation

Chronic low-level horror is genuinely dangerous. The overflow mechanic models the way sustained exposure to ordinary horror can become something deeper and harder to heal, even when no single event was catastrophic.

When all boxes on the Quiet Track are filled with at least Shock damage and more Shock arrives, the leftmost Shock box (the oldest, first-filled Shock mark) upgrades to Trauma: the / becomes an X. When all boxes are filled with at least Trauma damage and more Trauma arrives, the leftmost Trauma box upgrades to Void: the X becomes an *. The overflow result replaces the previous mark in the same box.

This means that a mage who repeatedly absorbs small Shock doses without recovering — the mage who keeps going back to the horrors, who never takes the downtime, who treats their psychological injuries the way some soldiers treat physical wounds, with contempt and willpower — will eventually find that their Shock has become Trauma. The mind does not forget. It reclassifies.

Similarly, a mage who accumulates too much Trauma without extraordinary intervention will find their Trauma converting to Void at the edges. This is the Traditions' fear made mechanical: the mage who pushes too hard, too often, against the worst things reality contains will eventually be changed by that contact. Not merely damaged. Changed.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Overflow in action: Vesper has Composure 2 and a Quiet track of nine boxes: four Static, two Distortion, two Fracture, one Full Quiet. Her track is filled: ////XXX*[Full Quiet empty]. All nine boxes have at least Shock. She takes 1 more Shock from a new horror. The overflow rule applies: the leftmost Shock mark (first Static box) upgrades to Trauma. Her track now reads: X///XXX* — she converted one Shock to Trauma because the track was full. The new Shock had nowhere to go without upgrading something. Now she takes 1 more Shock again the following session. Same thing: the leftmost remaining Shock upgrades to Trauma. Her track: XX//XXX* — two Shock converted to Trauma. Chronic horror, never fully healed between exposures, slowly degrades Shock into Trauma. If this continues until all boxes are Trauma, any further Trauma will begin converting to Void. The accumulation has a direction, and that direction is always toward deeper, harder-to-heal damage.

## How Damage Type Shapes the Experience

The number of boxes filled determines which Quiet band you are in and therefore which mechanical penalties apply. The damage types in those boxes determine the character of your experience within that band. Two mages can both be in the Distortion band and have identical mechanical penalties, but their Quiet experiences are qualitatively different based on what filled their tracks.

| Band \| Damage Type | Character of Experience |
| --- | --- |
| STATIC \| Mostly Shock | Jumpiness, hypervigilance, vivid and disturbing dreams. The mage is on edge. They startle easily. Colors seem slightly wrong in bad moments. This is ordinary human aftermath to extraordinary experience. |
| STATIC \| Mostly Trauma | Intrusive thoughts, emotional numbness, moments of dissociation. The mage is not just on edge — they are carrying weight. They avoid certain topics, certain places. Something is being carefully not thought about. |
| STATIC \| Mostly Void | Brief alien visions, derealization, moments where the mage is briefly looking at the world through something else's eyes. The Shock-level mechanics are identical, but the texture is deeply wrong. |
| DISTORTION \| Mostly Shock | Heightened paranoia, recurring nightmares, social awkwardness from hypervigilance and inability to mask distress. The −1 die to Social and Empathy rolls comes from being visibly shaken in a way others notice. |
| DISTORTION \| Mostly Trauma | Flashback intrusions during social interaction, difficulty trusting others, emotional blunting. Sleepers find the mage unsettling without knowing why — the body language of someone who has seen too much. |
| DISTORTION \| Mostly Void | Persistent minor hallucinations, double vision between material and Umbral realities, brief episodes where other things are perceptible through the mage. The wrongness is visible. Other supernaturals may react to it. |
| FRACTURE \| Mostly Shock | Emotional numbness alternating with overwhelming feeling. Avoidance behavior. The −1 die to all rolls comes from cognitive load — the mind is spending processing power managing damage, leaving less for everything else. |
| FRACTURE \| Mostly Trauma | Active dissociation, reality-testing impairment (the mage sometimes cannot tell whether a memory is real), intrusive reliving of traumatic events during quiet moments. Composure + Resolve checks to initiate sustained action. |
| FRACTURE \| Mostly Void | The mage is partly somewhere else. Spirits notice. Umbral entities are drawn to the crack in their pattern. Hallucinations are frequent and indistinguishable from reality without deliberate effort. The Gauntlet feels thin specifically around them. |
| FULL QUIET \| Any type | Complete Quiet episode. But the nature of the Quiet — its interior logic, its geography — is shaped by the dominant damage type. Shock Quiet is vivid but coherent, a distorted version of the mage's memories. Trauma Quiet is dangerous, built around the wound. Void Quiet is alien, potentially drawing other entities in. |

## Paradox and the Quiet Track

Paradox is the primary source of Void damage to the Quiet Track. This is by design: the mage who pushes too hard against the Tapestry is not merely risking backlash to their body or their immediate environment. They are risking their own grip on reality. The Tapestry pushes back, and when it pushes back hard enough, the wound is not physical — it is perceptual.

### Paradox Damage to the Quiet Track

| Paradox Event | Quiet Damage Dealt |
| --- | --- |
| Paradox Cascade (minor result) | 1 Shock — the moment of reality snapping back is jarring but survivable |
| Paradox Backlash targeting the mage directly | 1 Trauma — the corrective force of the Tapestry specifically against the mage's pattern |
| Paradox Catastrophe (major result) | 2 Void — direct damage to the mage's perceptual architecture |
| Time spent inside a Paradox Realm | 1 Void per subjective day inside the Realm — the artificial reality slowly overwrites the mage's sense of the real |
| Witnessing another mage's Full Quiet episode | 1 Shock to observers — contagious horror, even secondhand |
| Witnessing another mage's Fracture-band Quiet | 1 Shock to observers who lack previous experience with Quiet; experienced mages may roll Composure + Resolve at Diff 1 to resist |
| Nephandi pattern contact | 1–3 Void depending on the depth of contact — Nephandi reality actively invades |

The relationship between Paradox and the Quiet Track creates a feedback loop that the Traditions call the Spiral: a mage who accumulates Void damage enters the Fracture band, where Paradox Insulation begins to reduce Paradox consequences — but only because the mage is already so damaged that the Tapestry is losing purchase on them. The insulation is not protection. It is evidence of how far gone they are. A mage who is Paradox-insulated should feel no relief. The insulation means the damage is already done.

### Paradox Insulation

When a mage's Quiet damage has filled the Static band and at least one box of the Distortion band (meaning they have absorbed their Static reserve and are taking real penalties to Social and Empathy rolls), the Tapestry begins to lose its corrective grip on them. This is Paradox Insulation.

Full Paradox Insulation activates when the mage enters the Fracture band. At this stage, reduce the effective result count on Paradox dice pool rolls by 1 (minimum 0 Void/Cascade results). This does not reduce the Paradox dice pool itself — the Paradox is still generated, still exists — but the consequences of those dice are partially dampened because the mage's relationship with consensus reality is too fractured for the Tapestry's standard corrective responses to fully land.

At Full Quiet, Paradox Insulation is complete. Paradox dice results against the mage produce no mechanical effect. The Tapestry has, in effect, given up trying to correct something it no longer recognizes as a participant in consensus reality. The mage is beyond its reach. This is not salvation. This is the Marauder threshold.

> INLINE EXAMPLE — Insulation and its cost: Miriam is in the Fracture band, carrying 3 Void and 2 Trauma on her Quiet track. She casts a Vulgar spell with her Paradox Pool at 6 dice. Under normal circumstances, rolling 6 Paradox dice would be extremely dangerous — statistically likely to produce at least one Cascade. With Fracture-band Paradox Insulation, the result count is reduced by 1. A roll that would have produced 2 Cascade results now produces 1. A roll that would have produced 1 now produces 0. Miriam feels this as a strange flatness, a sense that something that should have hurt her... did not. She knows what that means. She has read enough Tradition accounts of Marauders to know that Paradox stops hurting you when you are already too broken to be Paradox's problem. It is not relief. It is a warning.

## Losing Quiet Track Stability — Trigger Events

### Shock Triggers

The following events deal 1 Shock to the Quiet Track unless the character passes a Composure + Resolve roll at Difficulty 1 (rolled after the event, not before — the mage experienced it). On a successful roll, the mage absorbs the experience without marking a Shock box; they are shaken but stable. On a failure, mark 1 Shock. The roll cannot be attempted if the character is already in the Fracture band; at that level of damage, ordinary Shock bypasses the roll entirely.

- Discovering a violent crime scene (first time finding this specific type of scene; repeat exposure at Storyguide discretion)
- Witnessing unexpected physical violence with lethal intent, even if the mage is not the target
- Experiencing the death of a minor NPC who the mage had meaningful contact with
- A supernatural entity manifesting in a way that is clearly not human, especially for the first time
- Being the target of a successful supernatural mental intrusion (Domination, Presence, Mind magic) — the violation of autonomous will, independent of content
- A Paradox Cascade result during a casting attempt
- Witnessing a Quiet episode (Static or Distortion band) in another mage
- Receiving significant Aggravated damage to the Health track — the body's pattern being torn is experienced psychologically as well as physically

### Trauma Triggers

The following events deal 1 Trauma directly, with no roll to resist. The mage experienced them; the damage is done. Extraordinary Composure or magical intervention may reduce subsequent boxes, but the first box cannot be rolled away. These events are categorically beyond what ordinary resilience can simply absorb in the moment.

- Witnessing the deliberate murder of a person the mage knows, in the mage's direct presence, with the mage unable to prevent it
- Being the direct instrument of serious harm to innocents, even unwillingly — the moral weight of having done it matters regardless of intent
- Extended captivity (more than a scene) involving deliberate psychological manipulation, isolation, or identity deconstruction
- Discovering that a trusted ally, mentor, or beloved figure is responsible for ongoing serious harm to others
- A Paradox Backlash directed specifically at the mage's pattern
- Time inside a Paradox Realm (plus the 1 Void per day; the initial entry also deals 1 Trauma from the disorientation of arrival)
- Surviving an encounter with a supernatural entity at the scale of a Umbrood Incarna or higher — the category of "too large for the mind" begins here
- A Seeking that reveals a personal truth the mage was fundamentally not ready to confront

### Void Triggers

Void damage is exceptional. It is not triggered by ordinary chronicle events. It occurs at the boundary of what mortal minds can survive as functional human beings. Void triggers are always Storyguide-called and should be used sparingly precisely because they are the hardest category of damage to recover from.

- Direct sustained contact with an Outer God or Deep Umbra entity whose nature is genuinely incomprehensible — not merely alien, but actively impossible under any framework the human mind possesses
- A Paradox Catastrophe result, dealing 2 Void in addition to any other effects
- Each subjective day spent inside a Paradox Realm beyond the initial Trauma
- Direct contact with a Nephandi's fully manifested pattern (their reality literally invades the mage's perceptual field)
- Witnessing a Convergence — multiple Paradox storms colliding, producing a reality rupture of catastrophic scale
- A Seeking that goes catastrophically wrong and shatters rather than illuminates — the Avatar turns on the mage
- Surviving Full Quiet and returning without intervention — some part of the experience remains, permanently

## Recovering from Quiet Track Damage

Recovery from Quiet Track damage is deliberate, slow, and categorically different by damage type. The architecture of recovery reflects the architecture of the wound: Shock heals because the mind was built to eventually integrate ordinary horrors; Trauma heals with active engagement and human connection; Void barely heals, because it represents structural changes that the ordinary processes of psychological recovery were not designed to address.

Recovery is always impossible during active chronicle crisis. A mage cannot clear Quiet damage while simultaneously being in danger, in pursuit, or in active supernatural engagement. Recovery requires actual downtime: a scene, a session, or an arc of genuine relative safety.

### Shock Recovery

Shock is the most naturally recoverable damage type. Given time and safety, the mind processes it without extraordinary intervention.

- Natural recovery: 1 Shock box clears automatically at the end of any session that contains a genuine downtime beat — a scene of rest, human contact, or ordinary life away from supernatural horror. No roll required. This represents the mind doing what it was built to do when given the opportunity.
- Accelerated recovery: A character with downtime available and deliberate effort (talking with allies, engaging in calming ritual practice, spending time in a place of genuine safety) can attempt Composure + Resolve at Difficulty 1. Each hit clears 1 additional Shock box that session.
- Maximum natural recovery: All Shock can clear in a single extended downtime period (one story beat of significant rest). Shock does not accumulate across sessions if the character is given proper recovery time.
> INLINE EXAMPLE — Shock recovery: Tobias ends a session with 3 Shock boxes filled. He is in the Static band, no penalties. The following session begins with a brief scene of him and his cabal at their Chantry, processing what happened. The Storyguide counts this as a genuine downtime beat. 1 Shock clears automatically. Tobias's player also rolls Composure + Resolve (4 dice, Difficulty 1) and gets 2 hits. 2 more Shock clear. All three boxes are gone before the session's main action begins. The horror that filled them was real; the healing was also real. He will remember what he saw. He just does not carry it forward as a mechanical wound.

### Trauma Recovery

Trauma requires active engagement to heal. Time alone is insufficient — the wound will remain exactly where it is, indefinitely, without treatment. Trauma recovery is a deliberate story commitment.

- Therapeutic recovery: A full scene of genuine psychological engagement — a trained therapist or counselor, a trusted Chantry elder with relevant experience, or a deeply personal conversation with someone who understands what the mage is carrying. The treating person rolls Presence + Medicine or Presence + Empathy against a Difficulty equal to how far into the Quiet Track the mage is (1 if still in Static band, 2 if in Distortion, 3 if in Fracture). Each hit clears 1 Trauma box. Three or more hits clears 2 Trauma boxes. Only one therapeutic session per in-game week produces mechanical benefit; subsequent sessions maintain stability but do not accelerate healing.
- Story-beat recovery: A significant in-character narrative moment of closure, confrontation, or acceptance directly related to the source of the Trauma — returning to a place, facing the person responsible, completing a task left unfinished — can clear 1 Trauma box at Storyguide discretion, without requiring a roll. This is a narrative reward for meaningful engagement with the wound.
- Mind magic: A Mind 3 effect specifically designed for psychological restoration can clear up to 2 Trauma boxes per casting session with a successful roll (Mind casting pool, Difficulty 2, 3+ hits required). Mind magic used for Trauma recovery generates Paradox as normal and risks dealing Shock to the patient if a Cascade occurs during treatment.
- Maximum per session: No more than 2 Trauma boxes can be cleared through any combination of methods in a single in-game week. Trauma has a floor beneath which it cannot be rushed.
> INLINE EXAMPLE — Trauma recovery: Rhen carries 3 Trauma marks and 2 Shock marks — all in the Static band. His cabal's ally, a Verbena healer named Anneth, spends a full scene in therapeutic conversation with him. Anneth's player rolls Presence + Empathy: 5 dice, Difficulty 1 (Rhen is still in the Static band). She gets 3 hits. Two Trauma boxes clear. Rhen's track now shows /X — 1 Shock, 1 Trauma remaining, from 5 boxes down to 2. The conversation was real, the healing was real, and the two remaining marks will take more time. But Rhen is no longer carrying the weight of three separate unprocessed Trauma events. A story beat later — Rhen returning to the location of his mentor's death to leave a meaningful object — the Storyguide calls a narrative resolution. 1 more Trauma box clears. Only 1 Shock remains, and that will clear naturally in the next downtime. Rhen has not forgotten. But he has integrated.

### Void Recovery

Void damage is the hardest to heal and the most uncertain in its prognosis. The wound is structural — not merely psychological, but perceptual and ontological. Recovery requires extraordinary intervention and significant time. Some Void damage may be permanent.

- Extraordinary magical intervention: Mind 4+ magic specifically designed to access and repair the fracture in the mage's perceptual pattern. Requires at least 3 hits on the casting roll against Difficulty 3. On success, 1 Void box clears. Only one such intervention per story arc produces benefit. The treating mage must have access to the patient's willing cooperation.
- Quintessence purification at a compatible Node: A Node with a Resonance that is explicitly harmonious with the mage's Avatar (not merely neutral; actively compatible) can provide re-anchoring. The mage spends a full scene in genuine meditative communion with the Node's essence. Roll Composure + Arete/Enlightenment against Difficulty 3. On 3+ hits, 1 Void box clears. On a failure, no change. On a Complication, the mage takes 1 Shock from the painful process of re-anchoring.
- Story arc breakthrough: A major narrative event directly addressing the source of the Void damage — not merely related to it, but a genuine moment of confrontation, understanding, or transcendence — may clear 1 Void box at Storyguide discretion. These are rare and cannot be planned; they must emerge from genuine story engagement.
- Maximum recovery: Only 1 Void box can clear per story arc through any means. Void heals at the pace of fundamental change, not session-to-session work.
- Permanent Void: The Storyguide may rule that specific Void boxes — those earned from the most extreme exposures, particularly extended Paradox Realm time or Outer God contact — cannot be cleared through any available means without a bespoke story arc of extraordinary intervention. Some mages carry permanent Void marks for the rest of their chronicle. This is not failure. It is consequence made flesh.
> INLINE EXAMPLE — Void recovery: Maya carries 3 Void marks from her time in the Paradox Realm, all in the Distortion and Fracture bands. She is taking −1 die to Social/Empathy rolls (Distortion) and −1 die to all rolls (Fracture).
