Her cabal finds a Verbena Node in rural Scotland whose Resonance is listed in Tradition records as deeply compatible with Dreamspeaker Avatar types. Maya spends a full scene in meditative contact with the Node's essence — the Storyguide describes it as sitting in the center of an ancient stone circle while rain soaks through her clothes, listening to something very old speak to something very deep in her. Her player rolls Composure (3) + Enlightenment (4) = 7 dice, Difficulty 3. Three hits. One Void box clears — the leftmost Void mark, the one in the Distortion band. Maya's −1 Social/Empathy penalty is gone. She is still in the Fracture band with two remaining Void marks, but one of the three has been addressed. Two more story arcs of this kind of work — if they can find compatible Nodes, if she survives — might bring her back to the Distortion band. The Fracture-band Void marks may be permanent. She does not know yet.

## Full Quiet — The Episode

When the single Full Quiet box fills, the mage enters a Quiet episode. This is not merely a severe mechanical penalty — it is a narrative event, a story beat, a moment when the mage's internal reality has become more real to them than the external one. The Storyguide takes primary narrative authority over the mage's experience for the duration of the episode. The player remains at the table and can describe their character's internal experience, but the character's external actions are driven by the logic of the Quiet, not the player's intentions.

The nature of the Quiet episode is shaped by the damage types that filled the track. A mage whose Full Quiet box was the last step in a track filled mostly with Shock experiences a Quiet that is vivid, internally consistent, and emotionally overwhelming — a distorted version of their memories, a place of grief or fear made literal. A mage whose track is filled mostly with Trauma experiences a Quiet that is darker and potentially dangerous — built around the wound, replaying the worst moments in unfamiliar combinations. A mage whose track is filled mostly with Void experiences something that may be genuinely alien: a Quiet whose interior logic follows rules the mage does not recognize, where entities from the Deep Umbra may find the crack in their pattern and push through.

The physical body of a mage in Full Quiet is present but distracted. They can still walk, eat when food is placed in front of them, and respond to physical stimuli — but they respond to things no one else can see, they do not initiate meaningful contact with the external world, and they cannot take any voluntary action in the material reality that others share. Allies who try to speak to them may occasionally get through — especially allies with Mind magic, or with a deep enough personal connection that the mage's name carries weight in their interior reality.

### Resolving Full Quiet

Full Quiet resolves through one of three paths:

- Rescue: Another mage enters the Quiet directly, using Mind 4 magic to project into the mage's subjective reality. This requires a successful casting roll against Difficulty 3 and a scene of navigation through the interior Quiet landscape — which the Storyguide controls and which may itself carry Shock or Trauma costs to the rescuing mage. On success, the rescuer guides the Quiet mage back to a point of re-anchoring. The Full Quiet box clears and the mage wakes into the Fracture band.
- Spontaneous resolution: A Quiet episode that is not fed by ongoing Void damage can resolve spontaneously over time — approximately one week per Void mark present when it began. A Shock-heavy Quiet might resolve in a week; a Void-heavy Quiet might take a month of the body being kept safe while the mind works through its own interior logic. Spontaneous resolution clears the Full Quiet box only. The damage that led there remains.
- Avatar Seeking: The Avatar, which continues to participate in consensus reality even when the mage's ego-self has retreated, may engineer a spontaneous Seeking as a last attempt to restore coherent Awakening. This is rare, extraordinary, and at the Storyguide's discretion. A successful Seeking clears the Full Quiet box and 1 additional Void mark. A failed Seeking (the mage cannot face what the Seeking requires) may extend the episode.
Regardless of resolution path, a mage who returns from Full Quiet begins recovery from within the Fracture band. The Full Quiet box has cleared; the damage beneath it remains. Full recovery from that point follows the normal rules. The experience of Full Quiet itself may deal additional Shock or Trauma boxes during the episode — the Storyguide should call these as they emerge from the narrative.

## The Marauder Threshold

A mage who enters Full Quiet with a track filled primarily with Void damage — especially Void marks earned from Paradox Realm exposure, Outer God contact, or Nephandi pattern contamination — is at risk of crossing the Marauder Threshold. This is not a defined box or a mechanical trigger; it is a Storyguide judgment about whether this particular mage, with this particular damage, has undergone a change that Full Quiet merely revealed rather than caused.

A Marauder is not a mage in Quiet. A mage in Quiet is lost but recoverable. A Marauder is a mage who has rebuilt a new, internally consistent relationship with reality — their own — and lost the capacity to operate within consensus reality in any meaningful way without that reality imposing itself on the world around them. The Tapestry around a Marauder bends. Things change in their presence, not because they intend it, but because their reality is louder than the shared one.

Crossing the Marauder Threshold is a chronicle-level event. The Storyguide should discuss it with the player. Some chronicles allow Marauder recovery arcs — stories of extraordinary scope, often involving the Avatar itself, other mages willing to risk themselves, and a confrontation with whatever is at the core of the mage's shattered worldview. Some chronicles do not. The system does not guarantee salvation. It only guarantees that the attempt is possible, if those who care about the mage are willing to pay the cost.

## Examples in Full

### Example One: The Long Slide

Rhen is a Verbena with Composure 2. His Quiet track is: four Static boxes, two Distortion, two Fracture, one Full Quiet. He begins the chronicle with an empty track.

Session 1: The cabal discovers the aftermath of a Nephandic ritual. Two Shock marks (finding the scene; recognizing the markings). Track: //

Session 2: Rhen is captured briefly by an NWO cell. During captivity, they attempt a psychological deconstruction interrogation. He takes 2 Trauma (sustained deliberate manipulation). Track: //XX — all four Static boxes filled.

Session 3: The cabal raids the NWO cell to extract him. During the raid, a sympathetic agent who helped him during captivity is killed in front of him by a superior. 1 more Trauma arrives. Overflow check: track is full of Shock/Trauma with all four Static boxes filled. The new Trauma mark pushes him into the Distortion band. His track: //XXX — two boxes in Distortion. He takes −1 die to Social/Empathy rolls.

Between sessions: Rhen spends a story beat at the Chantry, in conversation with his elder. The elder rolls Presence + Empathy at Difficulty 2 (Rhen is in Distortion). Four hits. Two Trauma boxes clear. Track returns to: //X — all in Static band, penalties gone.

Arc 2: Rhen deliberately seeks the source of the Nephandic ritual. The investigation goes wrong. He witnesses a full Nephandi pattern manifestation — 1 Void, plus 1 Trauma for the overall experience. Track: //X*T. He is in the Distortion band again (Void mark pushed him over the Static band boundary). −1 die Social/Empathy. The Void mark will require extraordinary intervention.

This is the long slide. Each arc leaves a mark. Recovery works, but it is not neutral — Rhen is accumulating scar tissue in his Quiet track, and the percentage of it that is Void rather than Shock is slowly increasing.

### Example Two: Paradox Insulation Emerging

Sera is a Virtual Adept with Composure 1, meaning her Quiet track is eight boxes: three Static, two Distortion, two Fracture, one Full Quiet. She tends to run high-Paradox operations and is careful about tracking her exposure.

Over three arcs, Sera accumulates: 5 Shock (various operational horrors, all manageable), 2 Trauma (a mission that cost two Sleeper allies their lives because of her choices), 1 Void (a Paradox Catastrophe during a desperate casting). Her track: /////XX* — eight boxes. She has filled her Static band (3 Shock), her Distortion band (2 Trauma — now at −1 die Social/Empathy), and her Fracture band (1 Void — now at −1 die all rolls, and Paradox Insulation is active).

In the final session, Sera pushes hard: a Vulgar Effect at high SF with her Paradox Pool sitting at 7 dice. Normally, seven Paradox dice is statistically brutal. With Fracture-band Paradox Insulation, the result is reduced by 1. A roll that might have produced 2 Cascades produces 1. She notices. She knows what that reduction means.

Sera's player: "She feels the Paradox roll happen, feels what should have hit her — and it almost doesn't. She stands in the middle of a ruined server room, Paradox dying around her, and she thinks: I used to feel that. What does it mean that I don't?"

The Void mark in her Fracture band will take an arc of extraordinary intervention to clear. The reduction in Paradox consequence is not a feature. It is a warning.

## Quick Reference: Quiet Track System

| Element | Rule |
| --- | --- |
| Track size | 7 + Composure boxes total |
| Static band | 2 + Composure boxes \| No penalty \| Tag: [Static Perception] |
| Distortion band | 2 boxes \| −1 die Social/Empathy \| Tag: [Distorted] |
| Fracture band | 2 boxes \| −1 die ALL rolls + Paradox Insulation begins \| Tag: [Fractured] |
| Full Quiet | 1 box \| Quiet episode begins, cannot act \| Tag: [Quiet] |
| Shock (/ mark) | Ordinary horror \| Clears 1/scene of safety, or Composure + Resolve Diff 1 |
| Trauma (X mark) | Severe horror \| Clears 1/week via therapy or story beat; Mind magic max 2/arc |
| Void (* mark) | Cosmic/Paradox fracture \| Clears 1/arc via extraordinary means; may be permanent |
| Overflow rule | Track full of Shock + more Shock → leftmost / upgrades to X; track full of Trauma + more → leftmost X upgrades to * |
| Paradox Cascade | 1 Shock to Quiet Track |
| Paradox Backlash | 1 Trauma to Quiet Track |
| Paradox Catastrophe | 2 Void to Quiet Track |
| Paradox Realm (per day) | 1 Void to Quiet Track (plus 1 Trauma on initial entry) |
| Paradox Insulation | Active at Fracture band: reduce Paradox result count by 1 (min 0). Complete at Full Quiet. |
| Full Quiet resolution | Via rescue (Mind 4+), spontaneous (weeks), or Avatar Seeking (rare). Returns to Fracture band. |
| Marauder Threshold | Storyguide judgment; Void-heavy Full Quiet; requires chronicle-level recovery arc if pursued |

# Appendix L: Bringing It All Together — A Complete Example of Play

This appendix walks through the complete Storypath Ultra system as applied to Mage: The Ascension. We build a character from scratch, establish his Avatar, choose his Essence, set up his Chantry, configure his Sanity track, and then run him through a three-round combat encounter against Technocracy operatives. Every mechanical decision is shown and explained in real time, using the SPU rules as defined in Chapters Two through Seven and the supporting appendices.

All die rolls are pre-determined for clarity. The Storyguide and player dialogue is presented as it might sound at an actual table, followed by italicized mechanical notes explaining exactly what is happening and why.

## Part One: Character Creation

Our character is Brother Aleksander “Sasha” Volkov, an Akashic Brotherhood mage. He is a former Spetsnaz sergeant who Awakened during a hostage crisis in Chechnya in 1996 — a moment when the only thing preventing a massacre was one soldier’s refusal to follow an illegal order, and the universe noticed. Since then he has studied at the Crossroads Dojo in Prague under the guidance of Sifu Aditi Chowdhury. Seven years post-Awakening, he is experienced, disciplined, and in the middle of an increasingly dangerous situation.

### Concept

Sasha is a warrior-monk in the truest sense — not a pacifist who happens to know martial arts, but a man who understands violence deeply enough to know when it is necessary and how to end it cleanly. His paradigm is Do: the Way. Every action, physical or magical, is an expression of alignment with the flow of the universe. When he heals, he is not injecting external energy into a body — he is removing the obstruction that prevents the body from healing itself. When he strikes, he is not attacking an opponent — he is returning a turbulent element to stillness. His Instruments are primarily his own body, breath, and focus, with secondary use of prayer beads and calligraphy for extended workings.

### Paths

Sasha’s Major Path is the Warrior’s Path (Akashic Brotherhood), granting him strong physical attributes and combat skills. His two Minor Paths are the Scholar’s Path (for Esoterica and magical knowledge) and the Survivor’s Path (for resilience and awareness).

```
Sasha Volkov — Akashic Brotherhood
----------------------------------
ATTRIBUTES
Might ●●●○○ (3)    Dexterity ●●●●○ (4)    Stamina ●●●○○ (3)
Presence ●●○○○ (2)    Manipulation ●●○○○ (2)    Composure ●●●○○ (3)
Intellect ●●●○○ (3)    Cunning ●●●○○ (3)    Resolve ●●●●○ (4)

SKILLS
Close Combat ●●●●○ (4)    Athletics ●●●○○ (3)    Esoterica ●●●○○ (3)
Awareness ●●●○○ (3)    Occult ●●○○○ (2)    Integrity ●●○○○ (2)
Survival ●●○○○ (2)    Medicine ●○○○○ (1)

ARETE ●●●●○ (4)  —  caps all Sphere ratings at ●●●●

SPHERE SKILLS  (Casting Attribute: Resolve ●●●●)
Mind ●●●○ (3)    Life ●●○○ (2)    Forces ●●○○ (2)    Correspondence ●○○○ (1)

DERIVED STATISTICS
Health: 7 + Stamina 3 = 10 boxes  (Bruised ×5 | Impaired ×2, −1 die | Critical ×2, −2 dice | Incap ×1)
Willpower: 7 (Resolve 4 + 3)  —  current pool: 7
Quintessence: 6 (Avatar ●●● + 3)  —  current: 5
Initiative Dice: Athletics 3 + Cunning 3 = 6
Arete: ●●●● (4)  —  Sphere cap, not a dice pool

SANITY TRACK: 8 / 10 (reduced from prior encounters)

ROTES
Iron Skin (Life ●●): Pool = Life 2 + Resolve 4 = 6 dice | SF = 2 | [Armored 2]
Thunder Palm (Forces ●●): Pool = Forces 2 + Resolve 4 = 6 dice | SF = 2 | +3 dmg, [Stun]
Spatial Whisper (Correspondence ●): Pool = Correspondence 1 + Intellect 3 = 4 dice | SF = 1 (Rote: 1−1=0 → min 1)
Mind’s Eye (Mind ●●●): Pool = Mind 3 + Resolve 4 = 7 dice | SF = 3 | sense/scan
```

A note on the casting pools: Sasha’s primary casting attribute is Resolve, which represents the focused, deliberate will that Akashic Do requires. This is why his pools are consistent across Spheres — he is not a spontaneous improviser but a disciplined practitioner who bends each Sphere through the same iron will. A Cult of Ecstasy mage might use Presence or Composure; a Virtual Adept might use Intellect. The choice of casting attribute is established with the Storyguide during character creation and reflects the character’s paradigm.

His Arete of ●●●● (4) means no Sphere can exceed 4 dots. He cannot attempt effects that require Sphere 5 until he completes a Seeking that raises his Arete to 5. Arete does not add dice to any pool — it is a cap and a gate.

## Part Two: Merits, Flaws, and Tags -- Finishing Touches

After building Sasha through Paths, the player spends his 7 Creation Points on Merits and optional extras. This section walks through every decision and shows exactly how each Merit functions as a Tag -- mechanically and narratively -- during the encounter in Part Six.

Recall: in Storypath Ultra, Merits are not separate mechanical systems. They are named Tags. A Tag has an Invoke condition (when you trigger it for +Enhancement dice) and often a Compel condition (when the Storyguide triggers it to create a complication). Merits happen to be Tags you paid for at character creation. Flaws are Tags that the Storyguide has standing permission to Compel -- they represent genuine weaknesses, not just flavour.

### Sasha's Creation Point Purchases

```
Sasha Volkov -- Step Five Creation Points
-----------------------------------------
STARTING POOL: 7 Creation Points

PURCHASE 1 -- Merit: Combat Veteran (3-dot) ... Cost: 4 CP
Tag definition: [Combat Veteran]
  INVOKE: +2 dice on any combat-adjacent Awareness or initiative roll (spotting ambushes, reading enemy positioning, assessing threat priority). Once per scene.
  COMPEL: Storyguide may trigger a "soldier first, mage second" reflex -- Sasha defaults to close combat or direct action instead of a more elegant magical solution. Costs 1 Momentum to override.
  Why Sasha has it: Seven years of post-Awakening life have not erased fifteen years of military muscle memory. When the shooting starts, his body knows what to do before his mind catches up.

PURCHASE 2 -- Merit: Iron Stomach (1-dot) ... Cost: 2 CP
Tag definition: [Iron Stomach]
  INVOKE: +1 die on Stamina-based rolls involving deprivation, harsh conditions, or resisting ingested substances (poisons, drugs, contaminated food). Once per session.
  COMPEL: Storyguide may describe Sasha as genuinely indifferent to food quality, comfort, or environmental conditions -- useful for characterisation and occasionally inconvenient when social situations demand otherwise.
  Why Sasha has it: A veteran of rough deployments learns not to be precious about what he eats or where he sleeps. This is as much a mark of character as it is physical resilience.

REMAINING: 1 CP unspent -- banked for session 2 (1 CP = 1 Background dot if spent then)

FLAWS: 0 purchased at creation. The player declined Flaws, accepting no bonus CP. The Storyguide has noted that Sasha's existing tags ([Unsettled] after his first encounter, [Bloodied] in heavy fights) function as situational Flaw-like Compels without requiring a formal Flaw purchase.
```

### How These Tags Work in the Encounter (Part Six)

Here is the precise mechanical effect of each Merit Tag during the Cold Storage encounter. Cross-reference with the round-by-round breakdown in Part Six to see these moments in context.

> MERIT TAG IN ACTION -- [Combat Veteran]
> 
> Trigger: Round 1, before action order is determined. Sasha's player declares he is reading the room.
> 
> PLAYER: "I want to invoke Combat Veteran. Sasha is assessing UNIT-7's attack pattern and Cross's positioning before he commits to a Phase."
> 
> STORYGUIDE: "That's exactly the kind of threat-assessment Combat Veteran covers. +2 Enhancement dice on your Awareness roll to read the tactical situation."
> 
> Roll: Awareness 3 + Cunning 3 + 2 Enhancement = 8 dice. Difficulty 1. Result: [5, 5, 4, 4, 3, 2, 2, 1] = 4 hits, 3 net. The Storyguide reveals UNIT-7's priority target (Cross, not Sasha), the NWO agent's suppressed weapon, and the freezer unit as cover. This information directly shapes Sasha's Round 1 plan.
> 
> Note: [Combat Veteran] is now spent for this scene. The Invoke is once-per-scene, not once-per-round. The player chose the right moment.

> MERIT TAG AS PASSIVE FLAVOUR -- [Iron Stomach]
> 
> Mechanical trigger: Not used in this encounter (no Stamina deprivation rolls, no toxins). The tag sits on the sheet, ready but unnecessary.
> 
> STORYGUIDE (post-encounter): "In the aftermath, Sasha finds a half-eaten protein bar in the jacket pocket of the unconscious NWO agent. He eats it without a second thought despite not knowing where it's been."
> 
> PLAYER (laughing): "Of course he does."
> 
> This is the Compel version: the Storyguide uses [Iron Stomach] as characterisation -- a moment that costs nothing mechanically but defines who Sasha is. No dice needed. No Momentum spent. Just a man who has stopped being precious about comfort.
> 
> If the protein bar had been doctored (a possible situation in a different scene), the Invoke would have applied: +1 die on the Stamina roll to resist the effects.

> COMPELLED CONDITION TAG -- [Unsettled] (acquired mid-encounter)
> 
> Context: Sasha gains [Unsettled] as a Paradox Backlash consequence at the end of the encounter. This is not a purchased Flaw -- it is a condition Tag, which functions identically.
> 
> INVOKE (player-triggered): "I want to invoke [Unsettled]. Sasha's psychological edge gives him a kind of hyper-clarity -- he can feel the wrongness in the room, which means he can feel what is wrong about Cross's cover story when she starts talking."
> 
> STORYGUIDE: "I'll allow it. +2 dice on your Integrity roll to detect her deception. The universe is whispering to you. Just not comfortably."
> 
> COMPEL (Storyguide-triggered, next session): "Sasha, I'm Compelling [Unsettled]. You've been in the Chantry for three hours and you can't sit still. Every sound makes you flinch. Sifu Chowdhury has noticed. She wants to talk, and you know exactly what she wants to say -- that you're running too hot. Do you spend 1 Momentum to push through it, or do you accept the Compel and let that conversation happen?"
> 
> PLAYER: "I accept. Let's have the scene."
> 
> This is the full tag loop: player invokes for mechanical advantage, Storyguide compels for narrative complication. The tag costs nothing to carry -- but it shapes every scene until it is cleared.

### A Note on Purchasing Flaws for Bonus CP

Sasha's player chose not to take Flaws at creation. Here is what it would have looked like if they had -- using a different mage as an example.

> SAMPLE FLAW PURCHASE -- Rosamund Caer-Gwyn (Verbena)
> 
> Rosamund's player takes the Flaw: Obsession (Blood Magic) as a 3-dot Flaw for +6 CP. This gives her 13 Creation Points instead of 7 -- enough for significantly more powerful Merits.
> 
> Tag definition: [Obsession: Blood Magic]
>   COMPEL CONDITIONS (any of the following triggers standing Storyguide permission to Compel):
>     -- When a non-blood solution to a magical problem is available, Rosamund reflexively reaches for blood first.
>     -- When under stress, her magic becomes visibly and unmistakably blood-based even when she intended something subtler.
>     -- Sleepers who witness her work react with visceral fear or disgust, not just surprise.
>   INVOKE: When Rosamund performs blood magic deliberately and correctly, +2 dice. The universe responds to her commitment.
> 
> STORYGUIDE: "The NWO agent is injured but cooperative. You could use Mind magic to calm him. You could use Life magic to speed-heal the rapport. What does Rosamund do?"
> 
> PLAYER: "She reaches for her ritual knife."
> 
> STORYGUIDE: "I'm Compelling [Obsession: Blood Magic]. Even though Mind 3 would be faster and cleaner, Rosamund's fingers go to her belt before she thinks about it. He sees the knife and his cooperation evaporates. You can spend 1 Momentum to use Mind instead, or accept the Compel and deal with a terrified informant."
> 
> PLAYER: "I accept. She tries to explain. She's not great at explaining."
> 
> This is why Flaws are worth CP: they create exactly this kind of scene. The Storyguide is not being arbitrary. The player already agreed, at character creation, that [Obsession: Blood Magic] could be used against them. Both sides get something -- the player got 6 extra CP and richer characterisation; the Storyguide gets meaningful dramatic material.

### The Creation Points Recap -- What Every Spend Buys

```
Step Five Creation Points -- Full Reference
-------------------------------------------
7 STARTING POINTS -- all characters begin with 7

WHAT YOU CAN BUY:
  1 CP = 1 dot in any Background (Avatar, Chantry membership, Allies, Resources, Contacts, etc.)
  2 CP = a 1-dot (minor) Merit Tag (Invoke: +1 die, once per scene or session)
  4 CP = a 3-dot (major) Merit Tag (Invoke: +2 dice, or a stronger passive effect)
  6 CP = a 5-dot (significant) Merit Tag (unique ability, permanent passive, or strong Invoke)
  2 CP = an extra Rote (beyond the 3 granted by your Major Path)
  2 CP = upgrade one Bond from 1 to 2 (or 2 to 3)
  1 CP = 1 dot of starting Quintessence (beyond base)

GAINING BONUS CP FROM FLAWS:
  1-dot Flaw = +2 CP  (minor Compel, situational)
  3-dot Flaw = +6 CP  (moderate Compel, regular)
  5-dot Flaw = +10 CP (major Compel, central to character)

CAP: No character may take Flaws worth more than +10 CP total. No single Flaw category may be taken more than once.

SASHA'S SPEND:
  [Combat Veteran] (3-dot Merit)   -- 4 CP
  [Iron Stomach]   (1-dot Merit)   -- 2 CP
  1 CP banked for session 2        -- 1 CP
  TOTAL SPENT: 7 / 7
```

## Part Three: The Avatar (Optional Rule)

Sasha has an Avatar (Appendix A applies). His Avatar rating is ●●● (3). It takes the form of a silent, elderly Mongolian warrior who appears in Sasha’s peripheral vision during moments of intense concentration. The Avatar never speaks, but its posture and expression convey approval, warning, or indifference with uncanny precision.

Avatar rating contributes to his maximum Quintessence: Avatar 3 + 3 = 6 maximum. It also means he will undergo Seekings at each Arete threshold — the Avatar will engineer the conditions.

## Part Four: Essence — The Pattern Avatar

Sasha’s Avatar Essence is Pattern (see Appendix C: Avatar Essences). This reflects his nature as a builder and maintainer of systems — military discipline, monastic practice, the patient development of skill over decades.

Pattern Essence Invoke: When Sasha acts to preserve, restore, or complete an existing structure (protecting his Chantry, healing a wounded ally, maintaining a vow), he may Invoke his Pattern Essence to add 2 dice to the roll as Enhancement.

Pattern Essence Compel: When Sasha faces a situation that requires improvisation, deviation from established method, or destroying something he has built, the Storyguide may Compel his Essence. He hesitates, double-checks, seeks a methodical solution when speed is required. He may resist the Compel by spending 1 Willpower.

## Part Five: The Chantry — The Crossroads Dojo (Optional Rule)

The Crossroads Dojo is a converted 17th-century building in Prague’s Malá Strana district. From the street, it is a legitimate dojo offering instruction in traditional martial arts. Within its consecrated interior, it is an Akashic chantry with three other mages in residence.

```
The Crossroads Dojo — Akashic Chantry
-------------------------------------
SIZE: Small (4 rooms, training hall, library, basement Node chamber)
MEMBERS: 4 mages (Sasha + 3 others)

CHANTRY TRAITS
Node Rating: ●● (2) — 2 Quintessence per hour for any resident, up to 2 per day
Library Rating: ●●● (3) — +1 die to Occult and Esoterica research rolls
Warding Rating: ●● (2) — +2 dice to detect supernatural intrusion

CHANTRY MERITS
[Consecrated]: Beings hostile to the Akashic tradition suffer -2 dice within; residents gain +1 to Willpower resistance rolls
[Hidden Node]: The basement Node is shielded from Technocratic scanning (Difficulty +2 to detect)
[Training Ground]: Residents who spend a full session practicing within the Dojo may mark 1 advancement tick on a physical skill

CHANTRY FLAW
[Known Location]: The Technocracy has the Dojo on a watch list. Not raided yet, but surveillance is active. This is why tonight’s encounter is happening.
```

## Part Six: The Sanity Track (Optional Rule)

Sasha begins this session with a Sanity of 8/10. He lost 2 Sanity in a prior session after witnessing a Paradox spirit consume a fellow mage — a psychologically shattering event that left him with a temporary [Unsettled] tag he has since cleared.

His current status: Sanity 8. No active Sanity tags. The Paradox Insulation from his Sanity rating means he absorbs the first Paradox die that would roll without effect — a small but real buffer.

Trigger to watch: If his Sanity drops to 7 during this encounter, he gains the [Unsettled] tag. If it drops to 5, [Fractured]. These will affect his ability to make clear decisions under pressure.

## Part Seven: The Encounter — Cold Storage

The scene: A refrigerated warehouse in Prague’s industrial district, 2:17 AM. Sasha has tracked a stolen Akashic artifact — a Dragon Pearl containing 8 Quintessence — to this location. What he doesn’t know: the Technocracy knows he’s coming.

Waiting for him are two operatives: Agent Cross (New World Order, Mind specialist) and UNIT-7 (Iteration X HIT Mark cyborg).

```
Agent Cross — NWO Operative
---------------------------
Dexterity ●●● (3)    Intellect ●●●● (4)    Resolve ●●● (3)
Close Combat ●● (2)    Firearms ●●● (3)    Esoterica ●●● (3)
Mind ●●● (3)  |  Arete ●●● (3)
Casting Pool (Mind): Mind 3 + Intellect 4 = 7 dice
Firearms Pool: Firearms 3 + Dexterity 3 = 6 dice
Health: 5  |  Willpower: 6  |  Quintessence: 4
Tags: [NWO Operative], [Mind Reader], [Sidearm: Suppressed Glock 17]
```

```
UNIT-7 — Iteration X HIT Mark
-----------------------------
Might ●●●●● (5)    Dexterity ●●●● (4)    Stamina ●●●● (4)
Close Combat ●●●● (4)    Athletics ●●● (3)
Health: 10  |  Armor: 2  |  Tags: [Armored 2], [Relentless], [Construct]
Attack Pool: Close Combat 4 + Might 5 = 9 dice (or Dexterity 4 for ranged)
Damage: +4 (hydraulic strike)
No Willpower, no Quintessence, no Paradox pool.
UNIT-7 cannot be reasoned with, frightened, or Compelled by social effects.
```

```
NEW RULES IN THIS ENCOUNTER — QUICK REFERENCE
---------------------------------------------

BOTCH RULE (Chapter 2 & 4):
  A roll Botches when: (1) zero hits, AND (2) more than half all dice show 1.
  For magic: Paradox pool 1s count toward the threshold alongside casting pool 1s.
  Botch = effect fails + 2 Paradox dice added immediately.
  Example: 6 casting + 3 Paradox = 9 total dice. Botch threshold: 5+ ones across all 9.

SPELL PARAMETER SCALING (Chapter 4):
  Range uses the same Close/Short/Medium/Long/Extreme bands as combat positioning
  (Appendix H). Default range = highest Sphere dot: ●=Close, ●●=Short(up to 20m),
  ●●●=Medium(up to 60m), ●●●●=Long(up to 1,000m), ●●●●●=Extreme(1,000m+).
  Extra free hits shift Range, Area, or Duration up one band each.
  [Touch]-tagged effects require Close range plus actual physical contact,
  regardless of hits spent. Sympathetic range (Correspondence ●●●+ with a real
  link) ignores this table entirely.
  Forces ●●● fireball: default Medium range (up to 60m). +1 hit = Long (up to 1,000m). +2 = Extreme (1,000+m).

These rules apply throughout this encounter. Watch for Mechanical Notes that reference them.
```

### Phase Initiative and Action Dice

Before Round 1, everyone draws Action Dice (AD). Each participant rolls their Initiative pool (Athletics + Cunning) and takes that many Action Dice to spend during the round. Spending 1 AD allows you to take one action in the current Phase.

Phases run from Phase 1 (highest priority) downward. Rote SF = highest Sphere dot − 1 (minimum 1); resolves at Phase 1 + SF. Freeform SF = sum of all Sphere dots; resolves at Phase 1 + SF. Regular attacks resolve in the Phase they are declared.

| Participant | Initiative Roll / Result |
| --- | --- |
| Sasha | Athletics 3 + Cunning 3 = 6 dice → rolls [4, 3, 3, 2, 2, 1] = 2 hits → 4 AD (2 base + 2 hits) |
| Agent Cross | 3 dice → rolls [4, 3, 1] = 1 hit → 3 AD |
| UNIT-7 | 3 dice → rolls [4, 2, 1] = 1 hit → 3 AD |

*Mechanical note: Action Dice represent moment-to-moment initiative. More AD = more actions, more flexibility. Sasha’s monastic conditioning gives him the edge here. Spending 2 AD for a complex action (like a rote + movement) is valid; spending 1 AD for a simple strike is also valid.*

### Round 1

Sasha enters through the loading bay. The warehouse is dark, cold, and full of industrial shelving. He has 4 AD. Cross has 3 AD. UNIT-7 has 3 AD.

**PHASE 1 — OPENING ACTIONS**

**PLAYER (Sasha): “Sasha immediately casts Iron Skin. He breathes out slowly, centering himself, and his skin takes on a faint golden sheen — chi hardening his body from the inside. He spends 2 AD: one for the rote, one to move to cover behind a heavy shelving unit.”**

Mechanical note: Iron Skin is a Rote (Life ●●). Rotes have a Speed Factor equal to the highest Sphere used minus 1 (minimum 1) — Life ●● Rote: SF 1 (2−1=1) in this case. SF 1 is inclusive, meaning the action resolves in the declaring Phase itself. Sasha declares it in Phase 1; it resolves in Phase 1 (same Phase — SF 1 = zero delay). He spends 1 AD for the cast (SF 1 = window of 1 Phase), plus 1 AD for the move. His casting pool is Life 2 + Resolve 4 = 6 dice. He chooses not to spend Quintessence this round, conserving resources. Difficulty is 1 (rote in non-stressful conditions; he has cover). He rolls [4, 4, 3, 3, 2, 1] = 3 hits. Iron Skin succeeds with 3 hits, granting him [Armored 2] — the tag absorbs 2 damage from each incoming hit. Sasha has 2 AD remaining. BOTCH NOTE: One 1 appeared in this roll. With 6 dice and 3 hits, no botch is possible — any hit prevents a botch entirely. If Sasha had rolled [1, 1, 1, 1, 2, 4] instead (0 hits, 4 ones out of 6 = 67%), that would be a Botch. At 0 Paradox dice, his threshold is 4 ones from 6 casting dice. The Iron Skin would invert — Life magic rebounds, and Sasha would take 1 Lethal as his own chi disrupts. The Paradox Pool gains 2 dice. He would begin Round 2 both injured and more susceptible to future botches.

**PHASE 2 — CROSS ACTS**

**STORYTELLER (Cross): “Cross is already scanning. Before Sasha even reached cover, she’s been probing the room with Mind — she felt him the moment he crossed the ward. She spends 1 AD to cast a Mind ●●● scan, trying to read his surface intentions.”**

Mechanical note: Cross’s scan is improvised Mind 3 magic (she doesn’t have a Rote for it — it’s dynamic). Improvised magic has an SF equal to total Sphere dots used = 3. Resolve Phase = Declare + SF − 1 = 2 + 3 − 1 = Phase 4. She declares in Phase 2; it resolves in Phase 4. Pool = Mind 3 + Intellect 4 = 7 dice, Difficulty 2 (opposed, warded area). She spends 1 AD (window covers Phases 2, 3, 4 — her only die in that range is the Phase 2 AD). Cross has 2 AD remaining.

**STORYTELLER (UNIT-7): “The HIT Mark doesn’t wait for orders. It detects the biometric signature of movement and closes at full speed, crashing through a shelving unit.”**

Mechanical note: UNIT-7 spends 1 AD to move and 1 AD to make a charge attack (declared this Phase, resolves this Phase since it’s a mundane action). Charge: Close Combat 4 + Might 5 = 9 dice, Difficulty 2 (Sasha is behind cover, granting him +1 Difficulty to the attack). The charge gives UNIT-7 +2 Enhancement dice = 11 dice total. Roll: [5, 5, 4, 4, 4, 4, 3, 3, 2, 1, 1] = 6 hits. After Difficulty 2, that’s 4 net hits. Damage: base 4 hits + 4 bonus damage from cyborg strength = 8 damage before defenses.

**PLAYER (Sasha): “Sasha sees it coming and rolls with it, deflecting the strike into the shelving rather than taking it clean. He’s using the momentum against the machine.”**

Mechanical note: Sasha defends actively — spending 1 AD (his third action of the round) for a dodge/parry. His defense pool: Close Combat 4 + Dexterity 4 = 8 dice (parry, using martial skill to redirect the strike). He is not spending Willpower here. Difficulty 2 (the HIT Mark is fast). Roll: [5, 4, 4, 3, 3, 2, 1, 1] = 3 hits. Net hits after Difficulty: 1. Defense reduces incoming damage by 2 per hit = 2 damage absorbed. Remaining damage: 8 - 2 = 6. Now apply [Armored 2] tag (Iron Skin resolves in Phase 3, but it was declared in Phase 1 — does it apply? RAW: the Rote’s protection begins at Phase 3. The attack happened in Phase 2. Iron Skin is NOT yet active.

Therefore: 6 damage hits Sasha. He marks 6 boxes on his Health track (10 total, 6 filled). He enters the IMPAIRED band (boxes 6–7, −1 die to all rolls). He is hurting but still fighting — the real crisis arrives in Rounds 2 and 3.

Tags triggered: Sasha gains the [Bloodied] condition tag from entering the Impaired band. Invoke [Bloodied]: “Fighting through pain” — once per scene, add 2 dice to a Resolve-based roll. Compel [Bloodied]: Storyguide may impose −1 additional die on one physical action where injury logically impedes movement. Note: the IMPAIRED band already applies −1 die to all rolls; this tag stacks on top when compelled.

### Round 2

Everyone draws new Action Dice for Round 2. Sasha is at 6/10 Health boxes, [Bloodied], IMPAIRED (â1 die to all rolls). Iron Skin is now active ([Armored 2]). Cross’s Mind scan is still resolving (Phase 5 of Round 1 — it has expired without hitting).

| Participant | New Initiative / AD |
| --- | --- |
| Sasha | 6 dice → [5, 4, 4, 3, 2, 1] = 3 hits → 5 AD |
| Agent Cross | 3 dice → [4, 3, 2] = 1 hit → 3 AD |
| UNIT-7 | 3 dice → [5, 4, 1] = 2 hits → 4 AD |

*Mechanical note: Sasha's Resolve-driven focus pays off here — despite being critically wounded, his initiative does not suffer a mechanical penalty from [Bloodied] until it is Compelled (the IMPAIRED band's -1 die applies to rolls, not to initiative draws). He has 5 AD, more than either opponent. This matters enormously: he can act early AND often.*

**PLAYER (Sasha): “Sasha is bleeding internally and knows it. He’s not going to survive another hit. He is going to throw everything at the HIT Mark right now. He spends 2 AD to cast Thunder Palm — all the Forces chi in his body directed through one strike. He’s going to try to overload the machine’s systems.”**

Mechanical note: Thunder Palm is a Rote (Forces ●●, SF 1 — highest dot 2, minus 1 = 1). Declared in Phase 1, resolves in Phase 2. Pool = Forces 2 + Resolve 4 = 6 dice. Sasha also invokes his [Bloodied] tag: “fighting through pain” — +2 Enhancement dice. Pool becomes 8 dice. He also spends 1 Quintessence for +1 Enhancement die = 9 dice total. Quintessence drops from 5 to 4. Difficulty 2 (UNIT-7’s [Construct] tag raises difficulty by 1 against bioelectric effects — but the Storyguide rules that electromagnetic disruption is not bioelectric; standard Difficulty 2). Roll: [5, 5, 5, 4, 4, 3, 3, 2, 1] = 5 hits. 3 net hits after Difficulty. Thunder Palm deals +3 bonus damage and applies [Stun] on 3+ hits.

Thunder Palm total damage: 3 net hits + 3 bonus = 6 damage. UNIT-7’s [Armored 2] tag absorbs 2 = 4 net damage. UNIT-7 has 10 Health; it marks 4 boxes (6 remaining). Additionally, [Stun] applies: UNIT-7 must spend 1 AD next Phase to shake off the electromagnetic disruption or suffer -2 dice to its next action. Sasha has 3 AD remaining.

BOTCH SCENARIO — THUNDER PALM: Sasha's pool was 9 dice (8 casting + 1 Quintessence) with 2 Paradox dice in reserve. His actual roll was [5,5,5,4,4,3,3,2,1] = 5 hits — nowhere near a botch. But suppose it had been [1,1,1,1,1,2,3,2,4] = 1 hit with 5 ones: still not a botch (1 hit prevents it). For a botch: 0 hits AND more than half the total dice (9 casting + 2 Paradox = 11 total, threshold = 6+ ones) showing 1s. A roll like [1,1,1,1,1,1,2,3,3] with 0 hits and 6 ones would qualify. Result: Thunder Palm inverts — the Forces chi misfires outward in a visible flash of kinetic energy (Vulgar in this Sleeper warehouse). Sasha takes 2 Lethal damage from the rebound, 2 Paradox dice are added immediately to the existing 2 (now 4 total), and the Storyguide makes a Paradox roll immediately. With 4 dice, even a modest result produces a Backlash. UNIT-7 gets a free action as the explosion gives it an opening. Sasha's desperate gamble becomes the fight's turning point in the worst way.

**PLAYER (Sasha): “While UNIT-7 is reeling, Sasha turns to Cross. He doesn’t have time to be subtle. He’s casting Spatial Whisper — Correspondence 1 — to pinpoint her exact location behind the crates. He needs to know where she is before she fires.”**

Mechanical note: Spatial Whisper is a Rote (Correspondence ●, SF 1 — minimum 1). Declared Phase 2, resolves Phase 3. Pool = Correspondence 1 + Intellect 3 = 4 dice, Difficulty 1 (simple location sense, no opposition). Roll: [5, 4, 3, 1] = 2 hits. Success: Sasha now knows Cross’s precise location. He marks this as a positioning advantage — the Storyguide grants him +1 Enhancement die on any directed action against Cross this round (he knows exactly where to aim). Sasha has 2 AD remaining. Sasha has now accumulated 2 Paradox dice from Round 1 and Round 2 combined: one from Iron Skin (Coincidental, minor), one from Thunder Palm (Coincidental, minor in an industrial area late at night with no witnesses). The Storyguide decides not to trigger Paradox yet — the pool sits at 2.

**PHASE 3 — ROTES RESOLVE, UNIT-7 RECOVERS**

Thunder Palm and Iron Skin both resolve this Phase. UNIT-7 spends 1 AD to shake off [Stun] (down to 3 AD). Cross fires.

**STORYTELLER (Cross): “Cross drops the scan — it’s pointless, he’s clearly hostile — and fires twice from behind the crates. Two shots, center mass.”**

Mechanical note: Cross spends 1 AD per shot = 2 shots. First shot: Firearms 3 + Dexterity 3 + 1 (positioning, suppressed) = 7 dice, Difficulty 2 (Sasha is at cover). Roll: [5, 5, 4, 4, 3, 2, 1] = 5 hits, 3 net hits after Difficulty 2. Damage: 3 hits + pistol rating 2 = 5 damage. Sasha’s [Armored 2] from Iron Skin absorbs 2 = 3 net damage. Sasha marks 3 more boxes (6+3=9). He is at 9/10 Health boxes — CRITICAL band (boxes 8–9, −2 dice to all rolls). One more clean hit ends the fight.

CRITICAL MOMENT: Second shot incoming. Sasha spends his final 2 AD to dodge. Defense pool: Close Combat 4 + Dexterity 4 = 8 dice, Difficulty 2. He also spends 1 Willpower: +2 Enhancement dice = 10 dice. Roll: [5, 5, 4, 4, 4, 3, 3, 2, 2, 1] = 5 hits, 3 net. Second shot’s result: [5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 1, 1] = 2 hits, 0 net after Difficulty 2 (defense negates entirely). Sasha barely avoids the second bullet. Willpower drops from 7 to 6.

**PLAYER (Sasha): “Sasha is done trading hits. One more hit and he’s down. He’s going to do something desperate. Can I attempt a dynamic Forces effect — not a Rote — to overload the warehouse power grid and cut the lights?”**

**STORYTELLER: “That’s improvised Forces ●● magic. What are you going for exactly?”**

**PLAYER: “I want to send a pulse of chi through the electrical system, triggering a breaker trip. No explosion, no fire — just darkness. UNIT-7 probably has thermal imaging but Cross doesn’t. It gives me a chance.”**

Mechanical note: The Storyguide rules this as a Coincidental Forces 2 improvised effect (cutting power to a building is plausibly explainable as an electrical fault). Improvised magic SF = total Sphere dots = 2. Declared end of Phase 3, resolves Phase 5. Pool = Forces 2 + Resolve 4 = 6 dice, Difficulty 2 (improvised, but Coincidental and well-conceived). Sasha has 0 AD left this round — he declared this as his final action. The Storyguide rules the declaration goes through before the AD runs dry since it is a reactive decision. PARAMETER SCALING NOTE: Forces ●● has default range Short (10m) and default area Single Target. Cutting power to the whole building requires Area: Building — that is 4 band steps from Single Target = 4 free hits. Sasha needs to either roll 6 hits on his Forces 2 pool (Difficulty 2 + 4 area hits = 6 total needed) or spend Quintessence. He rolls 5 hits, spending 2 to meet Difficulty, leaving 3 for area. Three band steps: Single → Small Group → Group → Large Group. That covers the floor he is on (about 30 people's worth of space). The power cut affects the entire floor — enough to black out the combat area and the corridor Cross is retreating through. To affect the whole building he would have needed 1 more free hit. The Storyguide rules the floor-level blackout is sufficient for the dramatic purpose.

Paradox pool note: Sasha now has 3 Paradox dice accumulated (the improvised effect adds 1 more). The Paradox roll does not happen yet — Paradox fires at the end of a scene or when a threshold is crossed. The pool sits at 3, which is notable but not yet triggering.

### Round 3

**PHASE 5 of Round 2 — The power goes out.**

**STORYTELLER: “The warehouse goes dark. Emergency lighting flickers on at 20% intensity — barely enough to see the floor. UNIT-7 switches to thermal without missing a beat. Cross, however, loses visual targeting. She’s firing at thermal blurs.”**

Mechanical note: Environmental tag applied: [Low Visibility]. For Cross: all Firearms rolls suffer -2 dice (firing at thermal blurs without imaging hardware). UNIT-7: unaffected (thermal imaging, [Construct] tag). Sasha: his Awareness 3 and Akashic training mean he fights by feel and sound — the Storyguide rules [Low Visibility] does not penalize him for Close Combat actions, only for ranged targeting. This is a significant tactical win.

Round 3 Initiative:

| Participant | AD |
| --- | --- |
| Sasha | 6 dice → [5, 5, 4, 3, 2, 2] = 3 hits → 5 AD |
| Agent Cross | 3 dice → [3, 2, 1] = 0 hits → 2 AD |
| UNIT-7 | 4 dice → [5, 4, 4, 2] = 3 hits → 5 AD |

**PLAYER (Sasha): “Before I declare — can Sasha attack Cross AND simultaneously use his other arm to check UNIT-7’s advance? He senses both their positions through Spatial Whisper. Both hands, two different opponents.”**

**STORYGUIDE: “That is Divided Attention. Both hands are live on different tasks simultaneously — no sequencing, no handoff between them. Both pools take −2 dice.”**

**PLAYER: “Let me check the math. Strike on Cross: Close Combat 4 + Dexterity 4 + 1 (spatial positioning) = 9 dice → 9 − 2 = 7 dice, Difficulty 2. Fending check on UNIT-7: Close Combat 4 + Dexterity 4 = 8 dice → 8 − 2 = 6 dice, Difficulty 2. Both are viable. I want to do it.”**

**STORYGUIDE: “Before we roll — I want to flag something. This is the third time tonight Sasha has used both hands for completely different simultaneous tasks: the Iron Skin while holding guard, the Thunder Palm while managing footwork against UNIT-7, and now this. That split-hand combat style is not an improvisation. It is who he is. Do you want to make it mechanically real with a Tag right now?”**

**PLAYER: “Yes. What does it look like?”**

**STORYGUIDE: “We call it [Ambidextrous]. The Invoke: when both Divided Attention actions are physical and both-hands-engaged — dual attacks, strike-plus-deflect, grapple-plus-strike, drive-plus-shoot — reduce the penalty from −2 to −1 on both pools. The split still costs you. You have just trained it so deeply the split costs you less. It does not apply when either task is magical or mental.”**

**PLAYER: “And the Compel?”**

**STORYGUIDE: “Your hands always want to be doing separate things. Put them in a situation requiring both hands to act as one coordinated unit — surgery, carefully lowering a fragile object, catching something breakable with both arms working together — and the dual-task reflex fires at the wrong moment. +1 Difficulty on that precision roll. It never hurts you in a fight. It hurts you in the quiet moments when cooperation matters more than speed.”**

**PLAYER: “I take it. And I invoke it immediately on this action.”**

Tag creation note: [Ambidextrous] was created collaboratively mid-encounter when a repeated behavioral pattern became mechanically significant. This is the intended use of the Tag system — when a character consistently demonstrates a defining capability the existing rules do not fully capture, the Storyguide and player name it together, frame an Invoke and a Compel, and make it real. The Tag costs nothing at creation except narrative recognition. It immediately becomes a two-edged tool: the player invokes it for benefit; the Storyguide may compel it for complication and offer a Momentum gain. Full Tag creation guidelines are in Appendix J.

[Ambidextrous] — Invoke: when both Divided Attention tasks are physical and both-hands-engaged, reduce the Divided Attention penalty from −2 to −1 on both pools. Does not apply when either task is magical, mental, or verbal. Compel: in a situation requiring both hands to act as a single coordinated unit (precision assembly, surgery, catching a fragile object), the trained dual-task reflex fires at the wrong moment: +1 Difficulty on the affected roll.

**PLAYER (Sasha): “Sasha is in trouble but the darkness helps. He closes on Cross — strike with his right arm, checking arm with his left to keep UNIT-7 off-balance.”**

Mechanical note: Divided Attention — two simultaneous physical actions, both hands engaged on different tasks. With [Ambidextrous] invoked: both pools take −1 instead of −2. Strike on Cross: Close Combat 4 + Dexterity 4 + 1 (Spatial Whisper positioning) = 9 dice → 9 − 1 = 8 dice, Difficulty 2. Fending check on UNIT-7: Close Combat 4 + Dexterity 4 = 8 dice → 8 − 1 = 7 dice, Difficulty 2. Without [Ambidextrous] the pools would have been 7 and 6 — the Tag recovered 1 die on each action.

Strike on Cross (8 dice, Diff 2): [5, 5, 5, 4, 4, 3, 2, 1] = 5 hits, 3 net. Damage: 3 hits + Unarmed +1 = 4 damage. Cross has no armor. She marks 4 boxes (1 Health remaining). The Storyguide has Cross take the [Bloodied] tag.

Fending check on UNIT-7 (7 dice, Diff 2): [5, 5, 4, 4, 3, 2, 1] = 4 hits, 2 net. Sasha is not attempting to damage UNIT-7 — the goal is disruption. 2 net hits: UNIT-7 is pushed back 1 meter and its next attack comes at +1 Difficulty as it recovers. Not devastating, but it bought Sasha a moment and kept both threats managed simultaneously.

**STORYTELLER (Cross): “Cross is badly hurt. She’s not going to make it through a second round of this. She spends both her remaining AD on a desperate gambit: a Mind ●●● compulsion, targeting Sasha directly. She’s going to try to make him believe UNIT-7 is his ally.”**

Mechanical note: This is improvised Mind 3 dynamic magic. SF = 3 (sum of 3 dots); declared Phase 3, resolves Phase 6 (next round would normally be Phase 6, so this effect does not resolve in Round 3 at all — it is still cooking). Cross’s pool: Mind 3 + Intellect 4 = 7 dice. She spends 1 Quintessence for +1 Enhancement = 8 dice. Difficulty: opposed roll (Sasha’s Resolve 4 + Composure 3 = 7 dice resistance). This is a future-Phase resolution — we note it and move on.

PARADOX NOTE: Cross is casting vulgar-adjacent (coercive mind control on a mage in a non-Sleeper environment — the Storyguide rules it as Vulgar since it fundamentally overrides autonomous will). Cross’s Paradox pool gains 2 dice. She has no Quintessence insulation. This will hurt her.

**STORYTELLER (UNIT-7): “The HIT Mark ignores Cross’s condition entirely and advances on Sasha. Full hydraulic strike.”**

Mechanical note: UNIT-7 has 5 AD, spends 2 (movement + strike). Attack: Close Combat 4 + Might 5 = 9 dice, Difficulty 2. Roll: [5, 4, 4, 4, 3, 3, 2, 1, 1] = 5 hits, 3 net. Damage: 3 hits + 4 strength bonus = 7 damage. [Armored 2] from Iron Skin absorbs 2 = 5 net damage to Sasha.

Sasha is at 9/10 Health boxes — CRITICAL band (−2 dice) — before this hit. 5 net damage would push him to 14, far past the Incapacitation threshold at box 10. This is the defining moment.

**PLAYER (Sasha): “Sasha has nothing left to dodge with. He does something he has only done once before: he channels all remaining chi into a single moment of absolute stillness. He’s trying to deflect the strike with a Force redirection technique — not a Rote, pure Do instinct. Can I attempt an improvised Forces ● response to reduce the damage?”**

**STORYTELLER: “That’s a defensive improvised Forces ● effect — partial redirection of kinetic force. One Sphere dot, SF 1. It would resolve this Phase if you declare now. You’re burning your last resources. What does it look like?”**

**PLAYER: “He meets UNIT-7’s strike with both forearms crossed. At the moment of contact, the force peels sideways — not stopped, but redirected. The shelving behind him buckles instead of his spine.”**

Mechanical note: Forces ● improvised (defensive). SF 1 (sum of 1 dot), resolves this Phase. Pool = Forces 2 + Resolve 4 = 6 dice, Difficulty 2. Sasha spends 1 Quintessence (drops to 3) and his last 2 AD. On 2 hits after Difficulty, the deflection reduces incoming damage by (hits) x 2 = up to 4 damage. Roll: [5, 5, 4, 4, 3, 2] = 4 hits, 2 net. Damage reduction: 2 x 2 = 4. Total damage to Sasha: 5 - 4 = 1. [Armored 2] absorbs the remaining 1. Net damage to Sasha: 0. He survives by the thinnest possible margin.

Paradox pool update: Sasha has now cast three times this scene (Iron Skin, Thunder Palm, Spatial Whisper) plus two improvised effects. His Paradox pool is at 4 dice. The Storyguide rules the scene is ending — Paradox fires.

### Scene Resolution: Paradox Fires

The fight is effectively over: Cross is down to 1 Health and collapses from shock. UNIT-7 calculates that its mission priority (capture, not kill) is compromised and withdraws, crashing through the loading bay door.

With the immediate threat gone, the accumulated Paradox fires. The Storyguide rolls Sasha’s 4 Paradox dice.

**STORYTELLER: “The air around Sasha shimmers. Reality is not happy with what just happened in a Sleeper warehouse, and it’s settling the bill. I’m rolling your 4 Paradox dice now.”**

Paradox dice results: [6, 4, 2, 1]. Two results of 4+ = 2 Paradox hits. Consulting the Paradox Backlash table (Chapter Five): 2 hits on a 4-die pool is a Moderate Backlash. The Storyguide selects from the table: [Resonance Burn] — the mage’s connection to his primary Sphere temporarily destabilizes. Sasha’s Forces Sphere drops to ● (1) for the remainder of the session as reality punishes him for the improvisational forcing. His Life and Mind Spheres are unaffected.

Paradox and Sanity: Per the Sanity system (Appendix J), a Moderate Backlash that directly affects a mage’s abilities costs 1 Sanity. Sasha drops from 8 to 7. This triggers the [Unsettled] tag. He is now operating at the edge of psychological stability.

**STORYTELLER: “Sasha, the backlash hits you like cold water. Your hands are shaking. The chi you’ve been channeling all night feels thin and distant, like a radio losing its signal. And underneath the physical exhaustion, there’s something else — a creeping wrongness, the feeling that the universe is watching you with something colder than indifference. You’ve gained the [Unsettled] tag.”**

**PLAYER: “Sasha stands very still in the dark for a long moment. Then he picks up the Dragon Pearl from the case Cross was guarding, and he leaves.”**

*The [Unsettled] tag persists until Sasha spends a scene in quiet meditation at the Chantry Node, recovers to full Quintessence, and the Storyguide determines the psychological wound has been processed. Until then: Invoke [Unsettled] when his raw edge provides instinctive insight. Compel [Unsettled] when the creeping wrongness makes him hesitate or act erratically.*

## Part Eight: Quick Reference -- Sasha’s Character Sheet Post-Encounter

```
Sasha Volkov — End-of-Session State
-----------------------------------
HEALTH: 9/10 boxes filled — CRITICAL band (−2 dice) — survived via Forces deflection
WILLPOWER: 6 / 7 (spent 1 in Round 2 for the critical dodge)
QUINTESSENCE: 3 / 6 (spent 2 during encounter)
PARADOX POOL: 0 (cleared — backlash has fired)
SANITY: 7 / 10  |  Status Tag: [Unsettled]

SPHERE CHANGES
Forces: temporarily ● (1) due to Resonance Burn — recovers next session
All other Spheres: unchanged

TAGS ACTIVE
[Bloodied]: persists until medical treatment or Life magic healing
[Unsettled]: persists until Chantry meditation recovery scene

MECHANICAL LESSONS FROM THIS ENCOUNTER
1. Arete/Enlightenment does not add dice. Casting pool = Sphere + Casting Attribute. Sasha’s Arete 4 matters because it lets him access Sphere 4 effects — not because it rolls.
2. Willpower is finite and precious. Sasha had 7 and spent 1 at the critical moment. Spending more earlier would have left him exposed at the end.
3. Quintessence enhances but depletes. Two spends of 1 Quintessence gave him edge on key rolls. Running dry would have applied [Strained].
4. Phase Initiative rewards preparation. Casting Iron Skin early (Round 1, Phase 1) meant it was active for Round 2. Reactive casting is always later.
5. Tags are the texture of the fight. [Armored 2], [Bloodied], [Stun], [Low Visibility], [Unsettled] — each one changed what was possible.
6. Paradox accumulates and fires. Four dice from five castings produced a Moderate Backlash. The Forces Sphere loss is not permanent, but it is significant and will affect future choices.
```

---

## Supplemental Example — Rote Use, Tagged Wonder, and a Tagged Complication

This example illustrates three things the Appendix L encounter did not: using a named Rote from Appendix N, activating a tagged Wonder (the Bulletproof Hoodie), and having that Wonder's tag compelled as a narrative complication.

**The Setup:** Esperanza (Verbena, Arete ●●●, Life ●●●, Prime ●●) is running an extraction. She has broken into a Technocracy front company and is carrying a wounded colleague, Danilo, toward the exit. Two Iteration X agents stand between her and the stairwell.

**Equipment:** Esperanza is wearing the Bulletproof Hoodie (Matter ●●●, Soak 4, Tags: [Flexible], [Natural], [Concealable], [Enchanted], [Ballistic]).

---

**ROUND 1 — PHASE INITIATIVE**

Esperanza rolls Athletics 2 + Cunning 4 = 6 dice. Results: 9, 8, 6, 4, 3, 1 → 2 hits + Enhancement [Resonant Strike] tag (she has a bone-handled athame invoked earlier) → acts at Phase 4 and Phase 6.

Iteration X Agent (IX-7) rolls 5 dice for Initiative. Results: 8, 7, 5, 3, 2 → 1 hit. Acts at Phase 5.

**DECLARE:** Esperanza declares her Phase 4 die: **Canticle of Rapid Mending** (Life ●●● Rote) on Danilo, who is at 4 Lethal damage. Rote SF = highest Sphere dot − 1 = 3 − 1 = SF 2. She will resolve at Phase 1 + SF 2 = Phase 3. Phase 4 die consumed.

She holds her Phase 6 die in reserve for a potential defensive reaction.

IX-7 declares a burst-fire suppression (Ranged 3 + Dexterity 4 = 7 dice, SF 2 for burst) targeting Esperanza.

**PHASE 3 (Rote resolves):**

CASTING: Esperanza rolls Life 3 + Composure 4 (her chosen casting attribute for biological empathy) = 7 dice. Difficulty 3 (Rote: highest Sphere dot). Results: 10, 9, 8, 5, 4, 2, 1 → 4 hits (10 = 2 hits). 4 hits ≥ Difficulty 3 → 1 Trick Hit.

EFFECT: Each hit heals 1 Lethal damage from the target (Rote effect as written in Appendix N). Danilo recovers 4 Lethal → he is now at 0 Lethal, fully functional.

TRICK HIT SPEND: Esperanza spends the 1 Trick Hit to remove the [Staggered] condition Danilo accumulated from his earlier injury. He can now run.

PARADOX: Coincidental? In a corporate lobby, a woman pressing her hands to a bleeding man's chest who then miraculously recovers — this is not plausibly deniable. Vulgar with witnesses? Yes — two Iteration X agents are watching. +2 dice × Life 3 = +6 dice to Esperanza's Paradox Pool. Her pool was at 2; it is now 8.

> Storyguide note: At 8 dice, Esperanza's pool is past the 7-die threshold. The Paradox roll triggers at the end of the scene — but not yet. She's pushed it to its edge.

**PHASE 5 (IX-7 fires):**

Burst fire, 7 dice, Difficulty 2 (medium range). Results: 10, 9, 7, 5, 3, 2, 1 → 4 hits. 4 Lethal base damage + 2 Trick Hits → spend both on Extra Damage: 6 Lethal incoming.

DEFENSE: Esperanza burns her stored Phase 6 die to trigger a Parry/Dodge (Athletics 2 + Dexterity 3 = 5 dice vs. 7 dice attack). Results: 9, 8, 5, 3, 1 → 2 hits. She cancels 2 of IX-7's 4 hits. Effective damage: 4 Lethal (after Trick Hit reduction).

ARMOR: Bulletproof Hoodie — Soak 4, [Ballistic] tag. The burst fire carries [Ballistic Damage], so Soak applies: 4 Lethal − 4 Soak = 0 damage reaches her health track. She takes nothing.

**THE WONDER TAG INVOKE AND COMPEL:**

Player: "I'm invoking [Concealable] on the Hoodie. I had this on when I walked into the building — security checked me twice and missed it. I want to narrate that IX-7 is briefly confused why the rounds aren't dropping me — it buys me a moment."

Storyguide: "Take your +2 dice on the next social/deceive action if you try to bluff past them. That works."

But then:

Storyguide: "The other agent — IX-3 — has been watching this whole time with his tactical sensors. He IDs the garment. [Concealable] Compel: the Hoodie's unusual construction just got flagged on his augmented sensor sweep. He now knows what you're wearing. Both agents shift to Armor-Piercing ammunition loads. Your Bulletproof Hoodie has exactly 8 Armor Points — but AP ammo strips 2 AP per hit. Accept the Compel?"

Player: "...Yes. I'll take it. That's fair and good drama."

Result: Esperanza gains 1 Momentum (from the Compel). The Hoodie's advantage has created exactly the kind of narrative tension tags are designed to generate.

---

**MECHANICAL LESSONS FROM THIS EXAMPLE:**

1. Named Rotes from Appendix N are plug-and-play. The Canticle of Rapid Mending entry provides the Difficulty, SF, Effect, and Paradox category immediately — no calculation required beyond rolling the pool.

2. Tagged Wonders have a full invoke/compel cycle. The Bulletproof Hoodie performed its primary function (Soak 4 negated a killing burst) and then generated a story consequence through its [Concealable] tag being compelled. The player received Momentum; the Storyguide received narrative material. Both outcomes served the game.

3. Paradox accumulates even when castings succeed. A successful Rote with full Coincidental framing would have added 0 Paradox. But Esperanza was witnessed performing an overtly miraculous healing. The pool hit 8 — she is one more Vulgar casting from a forced Paradox roll at 10+ dice. This is not a punishment; it is the system working as designed.

4. Phase Initiative rewards careful die management. Esperanza's stored Phase 6 die cancelled 2 hits and prevented all damage. Without the reserve, she would have taken 4 Lethal through the armor's remaining soak capacity — surviving, but hurt.

---

# Appendix M: The Cosmos — The Tellurian and Its Otherworlds

The universe that mages inhabit is not the universe most people believe in. Beneath the surface of consensus reality — beneath the agreed-upon world of matter and causality and measurable forces — exists a vast, layered structure of spiritual, conceptual, and ephemeral dimensions that mages call the Tellurian. This is the totality of existence: the physical world, the spiritual worlds that interpenetrate it, and the immeasurable deep spaces beyond, stretching outward into distances and abstractions that defy comprehension.

The Tellurian has been mapped — after a fashion. Any such map is immediately suspect, because the Otherworlds, as the spiritual dimensions are collectively called, are not fixed places. They change with every traveler's perception. What a Dreamspeaker sees in the Middle Umbra is not what a Void Engineer sees. What an Akashic Brother calls the High Umbra looks nothing like what a Verbena calls the same space. The structure described in this appendix represents a consensus cosmology — the rough agreement that mages have reached about what lies where and why, subject always to the caveat that the Otherworlds will ultimately do what they want regardless of anyone's cartography.

This appendix draws on the comprehensive survey of the Otherworlds compiled in Beyond the Barriers: The Book of Worlds, as well as the M20 core cosmological framework. It describes the major layers of the Tellurian in sequence, from the physical world outward to the farthest reaches of the Deep Umbra, with mechanical notes for each region and examples of what travelers have encountered there.

## The Physical World — The Skinlands / Gaia Realm

The physical world — sometimes called the Skinlands, sometimes the Gaia Realm, sometimes simply Earth — is the layer of existence that consensus reality defines as real. It is the world of matter, of causality that runs in one direction, of measurable forces and agreed-upon facts. Approximately six billion Sleepers live here, and the weight of their collective belief is the force that the Technocracy has spent centuries harnessing and directing. That weight is the Consensus: not a conspiracy exactly, but a gravitational field of agreed-upon assumption that makes the physical world stable, comprehensible, and resistant to magical disruption.

From a mage's perspective, the physical world is a surface. It is where they live and operate, but it is also the least interesting part of reality — or rather, the least visible part of what reality truly is. The mage who has Awakened sees the physical world differently than they did before: they see the Resonance that saturates places, the spiritual signatures that different locations carry, the faint impression of the Penumbra just behind everything they look at. The physical world is the top layer of an infinite depth chart.

It is also the battleground. The Ascension War is fought in parking lots and laboratories and conference rooms and university buildings, not in dramatic Umbral conflicts (though those happen too). It is fought over whose version of reality gets to be the consensus. Mages who forget this — who spend all their time in the Otherworlds — often return to find that the war has moved on without them.

## The Periphery — The Edge of Awareness

Before a mage or any sufficiently aware being crosses into the Otherworlds, they pass through a state of perception that the Verbena call the Sight and the Akashics call the First Awareness. Beyond the Barriers calls it simply the Periphery. This is not a place — it is a mode of perception, a heightened attunement to the spiritual layer of reality that exists alongside the physical without fully occupying it.

The Periphery brushes against everyone, Awakened or not. Brief moments of absolute certainty, a feeling of danger from a direction where no danger is visible, a fragment of precognition, an overwhelming sense that a place is wrong or right without any rational basis — these are all signs of the Periphery making contact. For Sleepers, these experiences are dismissed or rationalized. For mages, they are data.

Mechanically, any mage with Awareness ●● or higher, or any active Sphere of ● or higher, can perceive the Periphery with a Wits + Awareness roll. The Periphery reveals Resonance, the presence of active magic, the approximate location of spirits that have manifested or are pressing close to the physical world, and the condition of the Gauntlet in a given location. It does not grant travel; it grants perception.

## The Penumbra — The Skin of the World

The Penumbra is the immediate spiritual reflection of the physical world — the first layer that a mage actually enters when they cross the Gauntlet. It is sometimes described as the "shadow" of the physical world, or its spiritual skin, or the echo that the material world leaves in the spiritual dimension. All of these descriptions are partially right.

In the Penumbra, the physical world is still recognizable but distorted by truth rather than by appearance. A hospital in the Penumbra does not look like a hospital. It looks like what a hospital is: a place of pain, hope, fear, and, sometimes, grace. Those emotional and spiritual qualities saturate its Penumbral reflection. A corrupt police precinct where officers have been brutalizing the community for years appears in the Penumbra as what it is: a place of systematic cruelty. The physical facade is stripped away. What remains is Resonance.

The Penumbra always appears to be in twilight. During the day, the haze lightens and colors become more vivid; at night, the moon provides the strongest illumination, and the shadows develop a near-life of their own. Spiritual trails called Airts — the tracks left by spirits as they move — are visible in the Penumbra with a Perception + Awareness roll, and following an Airt is one of the more reliable ways to navigate.

Domains are the permanent features of the Penumbra: places where some powerful Resonance has crystallized into a semi-stable spiritual geography. A toxic waste dump becomes a Blight in the Penumbra — a Domain of corruption and sickness, housing mad spirits that feed on decay. A wilderness preserve or a genuine community center of human goodness becomes a Glen — a Domain of purity and vigor, sustaining healthier spirits. Domains grow and shrink based on what happens in their corresponding physical locations.

The Penumbra has three flavors, corresponding to the three great layers of the Near Umbra beyond it: a High flavor (closer to concepts and ideas), a Middle flavor (closer to spirits and elemental forces), and a Low flavor (closer to death and dissolution). A mage who enters the Penumbra will find themselves in one of these flavors based on how they entered and what they were reaching toward.

## The Gauntlet — The Barrier Between Worlds

The Gauntlet is the metaphysical membrane that separates the physical world from the Penumbra and, beyond it, the Umbra proper. It is not a place — it is a barrier, a resistance, a difficulty rating. Crossing it requires either the Spirit Sphere (the traditional approach), specific magical techniques, or extraordinary circumstances.

The Gauntlet's strength varies enormously by location. It is thinnest in places of strong natural or spiritual power: ancient wilderness areas, significant sacred sites, Nodes and ley line confluences. It is thickest in places that most embody consensus reality: cutting-edge research labs, sterile corporate environments, hospitals running on purely scientific paradigms, spaces designed specifically to eliminate anything irrational or metaphysical. In such places, crossing the Gauntlet can require extraordinary effort and significant magical power.

| Location Type | Gauntlet Strength (difficulty to cross) |
| --- | --- |
| Primordial wilderness, ancient sacred site | 2 (easy) |
| Rural countryside, old forest, active Node | 3 (moderate) |
| Suburban residential area | 4 (standard) |
| Urban city center, active commerce | 5 (difficult) |
| Cutting-edge research laboratory | 6 (hard) |
| Technocratic Construct or sterile facility | 7 (very hard) |
| Interior of a Null Zone (magic suppressed) | 8+ (may be impossible) |

Crossing the Gauntlet typically requires Spirit ●●, a Wits + Occult roll (difficulty = Gauntlet strength), and one or more rounds of concentration. The Garou call the act of crossing "stepping sideways." Mages may do it physically (they fully enter the Umbra, leaving their body behind), or astrally (their consciousness travels while their body remains in the physical world, connected by a silver cord). Physical travel allows them to bring materials; astral travel allows them to observe without being detected.

Only four worlds in the known Tellurian have Gauntlets in the Earthly sense: Earth, the Moon, Venus, and Mars. Most of the Otherworlds beyond the Horizon are accessible directly from the Deep Umbra without a Gauntlet barrier, though they have their own forms of resistance and difficulty of access.

## The Near Umbra — The Three Worlds

Beyond the Penumbra, the Otherworlds proper begin. The Near Umbra encompasses everything from the Penumbra out to the Horizon — the great barrier that separates the known spiritual worlds from the vast Deep Umbra beyond. Within this space are three great layers, each with its own nature, its own inhabitants, its own dangers, and its own gifts. These are the High Umbra, the Middle Umbra, and the Low Umbra, sometimes called the Three Worlds.

The three layers are not neatly stacked like geological strata. They overlap, interpenetrate, and bleed into each other in complex ways. A mage traveling through the Middle Umbra may suddenly find themselves at the edge of the High if they have been thinking too abstractly, or at the edge of the Low if something around them has become concerned with death. Navigation in the Near Umbra is as much a function of intention and mental state as of geography. Beyond the Barriers puts it this way: her intentions and knowledge lead her where she wants to go.

Zones — regions that cut across the layers, following their own rules — drift through the Near Umbra like bubbles in carbonated water, adding another layer of navigational complexity. These are described in their own section below.

### The High Umbra — The Astral Worlds

The High Umbra is the realm of thought made manifest — the world where ideas, concepts, archetypes, and abstract human consciousness have physical (or at least semi-physical) form. It is reached by going "up" from the Penumbra: not literally up in three-dimensional space, but up in terms of abstraction, in terms of moving from the felt and sensed world toward the purely conceptual. As Beyond the Barriers explains, travelers into the Astral reaches think their way there.

The High Umbra is divided into three ascending layers of abstraction, each more difficult to reach than the last:

**The Vulgate:**

The lowest and most accessible layer of the High Umbra, where everyday ideas and minor concepts have taken form. The Vulgate is densely populated with the spiritual residue of common human thought: beliefs that millions of people hold, cultural assumptions, collective memories, the emotional charge around ordinary concepts. A traveler in the Vulgate might find themselves walking through a realm that embodies "the neighborhood bar" or "the fear of examinations" or "the comfort of winter firelight." It is strange but comprehensible. Navigation here is easier than in the deeper Astral, though still requires knowledge of the High Umbra.

**The Spires:**

Rising above the Vulgate are the Spires — grand conceptual towers and mountains that represent significant, enduring ideas rather than the common currency of everyday thought. A Spire might embody War, or Love, or Scientific Progress, or Justice as an ideal. These are not small concepts sustained by one culture or one moment in history; they are structures built from millennia of human investment in an idea. Navigating the Spires means climbing — literally, in perceptual terms, through a series of caverns and halls and ascending staircases that represent increasingly pure expressions of the concept in question. The Spires become alien and challenging at their heights, comprehensible only to minds that genuinely understand the concept they embody.

**The Courts and Epiphanies:**

At the highest levels of the High Umbra lie the Courts — the domains of Umbrood that embody mythic or archetypal powers — and the Epiphanies, which are pure abstract concepts so fundamental that only the most advanced minds can even perceive them clearly, let alone navigate them. Courts include the domains of pantheons, the realms of mythic figures who have accrued enough belief to crystallize into Umbral reality, and the Afterworlds of various religious traditions: heavens, hells, and all the spaces between that human beings have dreamed into existence. At the highest reaches of the Epiphanies, the Oracles are said to dwell — if they exist at all — in fluid droplets of pure concept that spiral outward past the Horizon into the Deep Umbra. Mortals cannot visit these heights. Their minds are simply not comprehensive enough.

Traveling the High Umbra: Entering the Astral worlds requires either Spirit ●●● and leaving the body (conventional spirit travel), or Mind ●●●● or ●●●●● for astral projection. An explorer who enters through astral projection travels nude by perception — no material items cross the threshold, only the mental form. A silver cord connects them to their body, and when the cord snaps, they are lost. Those who enter physically through portals can bring material items, but the Astral resists physical mass and making such crossings tends to become increasingly difficult as one ascends.

When in the High Umbra, physical attributes diminish. Travelers use Social and Mental traits in place of Physical ones: Dexterity becomes Wits, Manipulation becomes Strength, Stamina becomes Intelligence. Damage is subtracted from Willpower rather than Health. A traveler whose Willpower reaches zero has their silver cord severed. They are not dead — but they are very, very lost.

> HIGH UMBRA REALMS OF NOTE (from Beyond the Barriers):
> 
> • The Great Hall: A vast nexus of doorways near the edge of the Vulgate,
>   built as a waystation by mages around 200 BCE. Sentinels guard it.
>   Through its doors, the early worldwalkers crossed to conceptual territory.
> 
> • The Inventium: A Spire-adjacent Realm embodying all human invention.
>   Reached through a footlocker entrance; stretches to infinity below.
>   Inhabited by the spirits of discarded inventions.
> 
> • The Eastern Court: Where Chinese folklore, Hong Kong film mythology,
>   and the Umbrood Heavens blend almost indistinguishably. A major Court
>   accessible from multiple adjacent Mythic Realms.
> 
> • The Afterworlds: Every religious tradition's concept of heaven, hell,
>   purgatory, or afterlife manifests as an actual place in the High Umbra,
>   sustained by the belief of its adherents. They are exactly as compelling
>   and exactly as literal as the faith that built them.

### The Middle Umbra — The Spirit Wilds

The Middle Umbra is the classic spirit world — the realm that the Garou call home, that the Dreamspeakers know better than anyone, and that most mages think of when they hear the word "Umbra." It is the world where nature, emotion, elemental forces, and the great spiritual presences of the world live and move and contest with each other.

As Beyond the Barriers describes it: the Middle Umbra reflects our emotions, our innermost feelings. It is not a place of logic or contrived literary fantasies. The Spirit Realm offers a chance for travelers to connect with their true nature. Experiences in the Middle Umbra tend to be symbolic, almost dreamlike, and if you hold your heart and soul open, you can find what you most desire there. The challenge comes when you try to bring it back.

The Middle Umbra is not a single landscape. It is an archipelago of discrete Realms — spiritual territories, each with its own rules, inhabitants, and Resonance — floating in a more or less amorphous expanse of spirit-stuff. The Garou claim there are at least thirteen major Realms in the Middle Umbra, plus many smaller Domains and contested territories. The following are those most relevant to Awakened travelers:

| Realm | Nature |
| --- | --- |
| Flux / The Radiance | The pure domain of the Wyld; constant change and creation; unformed potential. |
| The Scar | Ravaged territory dominated by the Weaver's crystallizing influence; order without life. |
| Battleground | A Realm locked in perpetual war; spirits of conflict, valor, and destruction. |
| Erebus | A dark reflection; associated with Wyrm influence; corruption and rot made manifest. |
| Pangaea | The primal Earth; dinosaurs; elemental forces before the Consensus tamed them. |
| The Legendary Realm | Where mythology and historical legend manifest; gods walk as they did in ancient faith. |
| The Aetherian Reaches | The sky realm at the top of the Middle Umbra; breathable heights; strange sky-spirits. |
| The Mirror Mazes | A Zone bordering Middle and High Umbra; labyrinthine; can be traveled to reach deeper. |

The Middle Umbra is traversed using the Spirit Sphere (●● to cross; ●●● to navigate reliably; ●●●● to interact with and command spirits). The Garou's method of "stepping sideways" physically deposits the traveler in the Middle Umbra. Mages may do the same. The landscape shifts constantly, and the Umbral Wind — a force of unknown origin and terrifying power — can blow travelers far off course. If the mists start to swirl and darken around an Umbral trail, Beyond the Barriers advises: seek refuge. Any nearby Realm or Domain should serve as shelter.

The Triat — the three great cosmic forces of Wyld (primal creation), Weaver (structure and pattern), and Wyrm (entropy and dissolution) — are more visible and more powerful in the Middle Umbra than anywhere else.
