It is a precise exploitation of three Sphere effects that, combined, create the impression of supernatural stealth: Forces ●● compresses local shadow into near-total blackness around the mage and eliminates sound (footsteps, clothing, breathing become inaudible within the darkness bubble); Correspondence ●●● moves the mage instantaneously from one shadow or darkened space to another within range; and the optional Mind ●●, when included, projects an aura of avoidance — a subtle psychic suggestion that witnesses' attention should not fix on that particular darkened corner.

The result is a signature entrance-and-exit style that senior Nephandi recognize as the mark of a practitioner who has fully internalized the darkness as a tool rather than simply a metaphor. They do not creep through shadows. They step through them. The combat implications are significant: appearing in a location where the mage was not a moment before denies a target the standard defensive preparation — the target's Defense dice are reduced by half (rounded up) against the first action following a Correspondence teleport. The darkness concealment also effectively removes the mage from sight between teleports, making sustained attacks against them extremely difficult in environments that have any usable shadow at all.

The full three-Sphere version — Forces ●●, Correspondence ●●●, Mind ●● — is more resource-intensive but makes witnesses not only fail to track the mage but actively avoid looking toward the mage's last known position. This is the version used for operations in populated environments where witnesses might otherwise raise an alarm. The simpler Forces ●●/Correspondence ●●● version suffices for environments where eliminating attention is less critical than speed.

Duration: the darkness compression and sound suppression last for one scene or until the mage ends the effect. Each teleport within the working costs one of the hits on the casting roll as a micro-expenditure; a roll of 5 hits provides 5 teleports, each of which can be used on any action during the scene. Any shadow large enough to step into is a valid destination, and the mage need not have visited the location previously as long as they can see or perceive the shadow through the working.

SF: 2 (rote). Casting pool: Dexterity + Correspondence.

Example — 2 hits: One teleport, from the mage's current shadowed position to another within forty meters. The Forces ●● darkness compression covers the departure and arrival. Anyone looking directly at either location sees shadow deepen briefly and then normalize. A sharp-eyed observer with Awareness 3+ gets a roll to notice something wrong; everyone else registers nothing.

Example — 4 hits: Four teleports available during the scene. With Mind ●●, the mage can move through a moderately populated space — a hotel lobby at 11pm, a museum at closing — without triggering any conscious attention. Security cameras capture a shape in shadows that the reviewing technician later categorizes as a lens artifact.

Example — 6 hits (Mind ●● variant): The mage moves through an occupied building, reaches a target, neutralizes them, and exits through a window — all without any witness in the building being able to give a coherent description of what they saw. Several witnesses recall "a feeling of dread" and one reports briefly feeling that they should not look at the east corridor, but none can explain why.

Coincidental when working in genuinely dark environments with minimal witnesses — the mage simply seems to step from shadow to shadow unnaturally quickly. Vulgar if the teleportation occurs in clearly lit conditions in front of witnesses.

---

### Abyssal Escape

Traditions: Nephandi (arch-practitioners, Descent Stage Three and above). Spheres: Correspondence ●●●●●, Spirit ●●●●●. SF: 4. Difficulty: 5 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Correspondence. Tags: [Transport], [Lasting], [Controversial].

The most catastrophic emergency protocol in the Nephandic arsenal. When faced with unbeatable opposition, a fully realized Arch-Nephandus who has mastered both Spheres to their highest level can tear a gateway directly through the Gauntlet and into the Qlippoth — or to another physical location across any distance — in a single desperate working. The Fallen use this when everything else has failed: when the ritual circle is broken, when allies are down, when the Traditions and Technocracy have combined forces and there is no conventional escape. They rip a hole in the world and walk through it.

The working requires at least 10 hits to succeed at all. Any result below 10 hits is a failure — not a partial success, not a lesser effect, an outright failure, which means whatever Paradox has accumulated from the (vulgar, heavily witnessed) escape attempt fires immediately with no benefit. At 10 hits exactly, the mage escapes to the intended destination — a deep Umbral Realm, the Qlippoth, a physical location across the world — one turn after casting, as the gateway opens, the mage steps through, and the gateway slams shut. At 15 hits, the mage can bring one other person with them. At 20 hits, the mage can hold the gateway open for a few moments to allow additional participants to follow.

The Paradox generated by Abyssal Escape is among the highest in any working in this document. It is always spectacularly vulgar — tearing reality produces visible phenomena of the most unambiguous kind — and the sustained Quintessential effort of Correspondence ●●●●● and Spirit ●●●●● combined with the distortion of the Gauntlet generates a Paradox Pool load that can destroy anyone except the most senior practitioners. Even those who succeed must manage the backlash: a 10-hit success escapes but typically arrives at the destination with 4–6 Paradox dice needing resolution on arrival.

Because this working almost always requires extended ritual to accumulate the necessary hit count safely, it is rarely available as an in-combat option. Most Arch-Nephandi who have survived to use this working did so because they prepared the ritual in advance — the components positioned, the Correspondences already mapped, the Umbral path already negotiated — so that when the moment came, the actual casting was compressed into a few turns of focused will rather than a multi-hour ritual. Practitioners who attempt improvised Abyssal Escapes generally do not arrive anywhere.

The working is documented because it explains certain historical mysteries: how particular arch-Nephandi disappeared from situations that should have been fatal, and why certain deep Umbral Realms bear the unmistakable Resonance signatures of long-term Fallen occupation.

SF: 4 (rote). Freeform SF equivalent: 9 (sum of Sphere dots minus 1 = 10 - 1 = 9). Casting pool: Resolve + Spirit (primary, as Spirit determines the Umbral pathfinding component). Must accumulate 10+ hits; almost always an extended ritual.

Example — 10 hits (minimum success): The mage tears an exit and steps through. The gateway closes. The assembled opposition finds an empty ritual space and a Gauntlet scar that takes three weeks to fully heal. The mage arrives somewhere deep and dark and far away. Whether they are safe there is a different question.

Example — 15 hits: The mage escapes and takes one ally — a critically injured associate who would not survive capture. Both arrive in the destination Realm, together. The ally's injuries do not improve in transit.

Example — Failed roll (9 or fewer hits): The gateway half-opens and then collapses. The accumulated Paradox for the attempt fires. The mage is still present. The Paradox consequences are determined by the Storyguide, but they are severe — this is one of the higher Paradox-risk workings in the game.

Vulgar in all circumstances. Among the highest-Paradox workings possible in the game system.


---

### Funhouse of Impossible Distances

Traditions: Order of Hermes (Wards and Measures), Virtual Adepts (Space Hacking). Spheres: Correspondence 3, Mind 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Correspondence. Tags: [Environmental], [Control], [Mental], [Ranged], [Controversial].

Named for Farandwee's legendary punishment — a paradox spirit known to enforce the proper use of spatial magic — this working recreates the entity's most feared weapon: a sealed zone of recursive space where all Correspondence workings become treacherous and the target's ability to perceive or navigate their surroundings is fundamentally compromised. The mage weaves a shell of distorted Correspondence around a defined area, typically a single room or enclosed corridor of up to ten meters per dimension. Within this shell, mirrors (real or conjured) multiply and refract spatial information until the inside of the enclosed space seems vastly larger than its physical boundaries — each wall leads to another reflective surface, each turn opens onto three apparent exits, and the sense of where one actually is in three-dimensional space collapses entirely.

The Mind 2 component is not optional — it handles the psychological dimension of the working, seeding the perceptual distortion into the target's actual subjective experience rather than simply manipulating the physical geometry. A target protected by a Mind shield that achieves more hits than the caster's roll perceives the effect as a sudden headache and strange visual noise rather than a genuine maze; they still cannot navigate effectively (the Correspondence distortion is real) but they know something is wrong and can attempt to counter it. Unshielded targets experience the maze as fully real and navigate accordingly — which means they navigate very badly.

Mechanically, the working applies a difficulty penalty of +2 to all Correspondence-based actions (teleportation, spatial sensing, gate opening) performed within the zone, and a difficulty penalty of +1 to all physical movement and navigation rolls. These penalties are cumulative with any existing Paradox penalties. The zone persists for one scene per hit, or until the mage dismisses it. The mage knows the true layout of the zone and moves through it without penalty. Allies designated at time of casting are similarly unaffected; everyone else is caught in it.

Against targets attempting to escape via Correspondence teleportation: a botch on the teleport roll while inside the zone sends the target to a random location within (Arete x 1 kilometer) rather than their intended destination. This is not additional magic — it is the natural consequence of attempting spatial manipulation inside a zone where spatial information is unreliable.

This rote sees occasional use by both defensive practitioners (protecting a chantry from spatial intrusion by enemy mages) and offensive ones (trapping a rival in a burning building where every exit appears to lead deeper in). The Order of Hermes developed the working after extensive study of Farandwee itself — several mages were taken for rides in the entity's actual maze and returned with detailed notes on the mechanisms involved. Virtual Adepts call their version "Space Hacking" and frame it as corrupting the underlying coordinate data of a location. Countering the effect requires Correspondence 3 to perceive its true spatial architecture and Prime 2 to begin dismantling the weave; the counter requires more hits than the original working.

Examples: With 3 hits, the zone persists for three scenes — a combat encounter, the aftermath, and the escape attempt. Enemies who enter the chantry's warded anteroom find that the door they came in through no longer seems to lead back to the street. With 6 hits, the entire interior of a three-story building is enclosed in the working. The building's physical architecture is intact; the spatial information the building provides to anyone inside is systematically wrong. Rescue workers sent in after the working expires still describe the interior as feeling subtly wrong for weeks. With 8 hits against a Correspondence 4 mage attempting to teleport away: the mage's escape attempt botches inside the distorted zone and they materialize three kilometers away in an unexpected location, buying the caster time to pursue through other means.

Coincidental when the zone is established before any target enters and has no obvious external manifestation. Vulgar when created in the direct presence of Sleeper witnesses, or when its spatial distortions are so severe that the geometry becomes physically impossible to explain (corridors that are longer inside than the building is wide, rooms that cannot exist at the physical coordinates they appear to occupy).

---

### Swipe — Spatial Erasure

Traditions: Cult of Ecstasy (Space-Dance), Virtual Adepts (Data Removal), Order of Hermes (Translocation with Oblivion). Spheres: Correspondence 3, Mind 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Correspondence. Tags: [Ranged], [Control], [Mental], [Controversial].

Inspired by study of the Paradox spirit Swipe — an entity that enforces the appropriate limits of spatial transgression by erasing mages from their current location and depositing them elsewhere — this working replicates the spirit's defining ability through deliberate application of Sphere magic. The mage selects a target within medium range (approximately 30 meters) and simultaneously displaces them in space (Correspondence 3) while editing the spatial memory of the target's current location from the minds of all nearby observers (Mind 2). The target vanishes. People who were present realize, within a few moments, that they cannot remember the target having been there at all — not that the target left, but that they were not present in the first place. This erasure from witness memory lasts approximately one hour per hit before fading naturally as the witnesses' actual memories re-integrate.

The target is displaced to a location within approximately ten kilometers, determined by the mage's intent. The mage can target a specific location (a park, a street corner, a building's exterior) or simply push the target in a general direction. A mage with Correspondence 4 can extend the displacement range substantially, and with Correspondence 5 can send the target essentially anywhere on Earth. Upon arrival, the target is disoriented — the strain of forced translocation scrambles effective functioning for one scene, applying a +2 difficulty penalty to all tasks until the target's cognitive and physical orientation stabilizes. The disorientation doubles if the mage achieves more than 6 hits on the casting.

The working does not injure the target in itself. It removes them, erases evidence of their recent presence, and leaves them somewhere inconvenient. The ethics of deploying this against an innocent bystander versus a pursuing threat differ considerably; most practitioners reserve it for the latter. The Paradox spirit Swipe presumably disapproves of its own methods being appropriated as deliberate technique, though there is no documented instance of Swipe appearing specifically in response to this working rather than other Correspondence transgressions.

Mages who have studied or encountered Swipe directly learn to construct the working with the spirit's own specific technique — the "swipe left" motion encoding the spatial fold in a single gestural focus. Virtual Adepts construct it as a data-removal algorithm targeting the target's location-data within the local Correspondence matrix. Cult of Ecstasy practitioners simply reach into the space the target occupies, feel where else they belong, and move them there.

Resisting the working: a target with Mind 2 or higher may attempt a contested roll to partially resist the memory-erasure component, preserving some fragments of the event in willing observers' minds. Fully resisting the translocation component requires Correspondence of equal or greater rating and an active, prepared ward against spatial displacement — an uncommon but viable defense among experienced mages who travel in contested territory.

Examples: With 4 hits, a hostile mage confronting the caster in a coffee shop vanishes mid-sentence while patrons momentarily lose track of where they were looking. When attention returns to the table, the hostile mage is simply not there — and no one quite remembers them being there. They materialize six kilometers away near the waterfront. The disorientation lasts the scene and they do not return before the caster has left. With 6 hits against a physically dangerous Sleeper threat: the threat vanishes and the spatial memory erasure affects multiple witnesses simultaneously. Law enforcement investigation later concludes the person was never at the scene. With 9 hits, combined with Correspondence 5: the target is removed to a location on another continent. The witnesses do not remember. The caster has approximately 48 hours before the situation must be managed in other ways.

Coincidental when applied subtly in crowds where distraction is already present. Vulgar when witnesses are looking directly at the target during the displacement, or when the spatial fold is dramatic enough to create visible distortion.


## Entropy — Rotes of Fate, Decay, and Probability

Entropy magic touches the hidden currents of fate, the fraying edges of patterns, and the statistical heartbeat of probability. Practitioners range from Euthanatos mages who ride the river of death and rebirth to Verbena witches who charm the luck of the harvest. All Entropy workings share a quality of riding what already exists — nudging probability rather than overriding it.

### Evil Eye

Traditions: Euthanatos, Verbena. Spheres: Entropy 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Tags: [Curse], [Subtle].

A focused glare and a muttered word of ill-will introduces subtle chaos into a target's probability field. The target suffers -1 die on all rolls for the scene (stacking to -3 maximum with repeated applications). The curse requires the mage to meet the target's gaze and complete the working within a moment of focus; breaking eye contact before finishing aborts the rote. The target feels nothing and notices nothing. Only a mage with Entropy 1+ or Prime 2+ can detect the curse — to everyone else, the afflicted person just seems to be having a run of bad luck.

Coincidental in all circumstances. The Evil Eye has been a staple of hedge magic for millennia; even Sleepers believe in it.

### Sense the Unraveling

Traditions: Euthanatos, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Entropy 1, Time 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Entropy. Tags: [Sensory], [Subtle].

The mage reads the subtle signs of entropy, decay, and structural weakness in all things around them, seeing damage not just as it exists now but as it has accumulated and will accumulate. They automatically detect: the current health levels of any examined person, the structural integrity (and likely failure points) of any object or building, whether any illness, addiction, or ongoing curse currently affects a target, and the rough age of any object or creature. With 3 or more hits, the mage can read the likely cause of existing damage — they know whether that cracked rib came from a fall or a fist. This rote is a standard diagnostic tool used by Euthanatos healers before deciding whether to help a target's condition or accelerate their passage onward.

Coincidental in all circumstances — acute observation of physical wear and condition is explicable.

### Gremlins

Traditions: Virtual Adepts, Sons of Ether. Spheres: Entropy 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Curse], [Technological], [Subtle], [Lasting].

The mage nudges the probability field around a machine, vehicle, electronic system, or complex device, introducing cascading micro-failures that no engineer can easily diagnose. The targeted system develops persistent, maddening problems: the engine that stalls at intersections, the server that drops connections at random, the weapon that jams in the moment it is needed most. Each hit on the casting inflicts one scene of unreliability — the Storyguide selects the most dramatically appropriate moment for failure. The Gremlins persist until the target system is extensively overhauled (a full workday of skilled repair), magically cleansed, or the duration expires.

Coincidental in virtually all circumstances — technology fails. Vulgar only if the failure is immediately and unambiguously supernatural (the machine literally assembles itself in reverse).

### Serendipity

Traditions: Verbena, Dreamspeakers, Cult of Ecstasy. Spheres: Entropy 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Boost], [Subtle], [Lasting].

Rather than pushing entropy onto others, the mage channels the flow of fate to smooth fortune for a chosen target (often themselves). The target gains +2 dice on one category of rolls (social, physical, or mental — chosen at casting) for the scene, as probability subtly bends in their favor: the cab arrives early, the guard looks the other way, the dice fall right. This rote does not guarantee success; it simply makes success more likely by nudging coincidence. With 4+ hits, the mage may choose two categories. The boost feels natural to the beneficiary — they think they are simply having a good day.

Coincidental in all circumstances.

### Fate's Warning

Traditions: Euthanatos. Spheres: Entropy 2, Time 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Entropy. Tags: [Sensory], [Subtle].

The mage reads the threads of fate binding a specific person or situation, perceiving whether the current trajectory moves toward fortune or doom. This does not provide specific prophetic visions — it is a directional sense, like a compass pointing toward catastrophe. The mage knows whether a planned action is likely to succeed or fail (better than 50-50 chance or worse), whether a person is marked for near-term danger, and whether a location carries recent violent Resonance. With 5 hits, the mage can share this reading with allies, granting +1 die to the relevant roll for everyone briefed on the warning. The Euthanatos call this reading "seeing the thread" — it is a core diagnostic skill taught early in their tradition.

Coincidental at all times.

### Doom

Traditions: Euthanatos. Spheres: Entropy 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Curse], [Damage], [Lasting].

The mage reaches into a target's pattern and accelerates the entropy already present, making existing damage catastrophically worse. Living targets with existing wounds find them worsening: all Bashing damage becomes Lethal; all Lethal damage becomes Aggravated. Objects begin visibly deteriorating, losing 1 point of Soak per round. The Doom lasts for (hits) rounds and cannot be resisted by mundane means — only Life 3+ magic or Entropy 3+ counter-magic can halt it. The Euthanatos view this rote not as murder but as acceleration of an inevitable process; they use it against those they have judged already condemned by karma or fate. Whether that justification holds weight depends entirely on the chronicle.

Vulgar with Witnesses — visible physical deterioration of a living person is undeniably supernatural.

### Pattern Rot

Traditions: Euthanatos. Spheres: Entropy 4, Life 3. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Entropy. Tags: [Damage], [Curse], [Lasting], [Penetrating].

A devastating working that attacks the fundamental coherence of a living being's pattern, causing their body to literally come apart at the molecular level. Each round after the first, the target must resist with Stamina + Fortitude (Difficulty 3) or suffer one level of Aggravated damage as tissues dissolve, bones soften, and biological processes fail. The rot persists until countered by Life 4+ or Prime 3+ magic, or until the mage consciously ends it (a reflexive action). Armor provides no protection — this is not a physical assault. Even at 1 hit, the effect is agonizing and visually apparent. At 5 hits, the rot progresses faster (two automatic levels of damage per round) and requires a greater magical counter. This rote is considered one of the most ethically compromised in the Euthanatos tradition and is rarely taught outside advanced initiates.

Vulgar with Witnesses in all circumstances.

---

### Spotting Flaws

Traditions: Akashayana, Euthanatos, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Entropy 1, Life 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Entropy. Tags: [Sensory], [Subtle].

The mage reads the structural weaknesses in an opponent's fighting posture and biology before committing to an attack — the stress fracture in old scar tissue, the knee that has been turned too many times, the moment of reach that exposes the liver, the micro-expression that precedes a feint. Entropy 1 reads probability flows and structural degradation patterns; Life 1 reads biological state and current vitality. Used as an opening action before combat, each hit reduces the difficulty of the mage's first three attack rolls by -1 (maximum -3 total reduction). Can also identify a specific anatomical vulnerability — the Storyguide notes one location on the target that, if struck, provides additional mechanical benefit (aggravated damage, stun, or forced morale check). Akashayana practitioners use this as a fundamental pre-combat assessment, learned before any martial application. The Euthanatos add to it a reading of the target's karmic trajectory — sometimes concluding that a target does not, in fact, need to be fought at all.

Coincidental in all circumstances — looks like exceptional observational skill and tactical awareness.

### The Luck Ward

Traditions: Euthanatos. Conventions: Syndicate. Spheres: Entropy 2, Prime 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Entropy. Tags: [Protective], [Lasting], [Environmental].

The mage embeds an Entropic probability field within a defined space — a room, a doorway, a vehicle — that actively shapes fortune against unauthorized intruders. Unlike a conventional ward that creates a barrier, the Luck Ward creates a field of arranged misfortune: the intruder's shoelace breaks as they prepare to run, a creak in the floorboard alerts an occupant, a surveillance camera's recording chip fails at the precisely wrong moment, or a convenient witness notices exactly what they shouldn't. The Ward does not directly attack — it arranges circumstances. The mage's casting roll triggers when an intruder acts against the warded space's interest; the hits from that trigger roll are then applied as difficulty increases or initiative penalties against the intruder's actions for that scene. Each activation draws on a reservoir of stored Quintessence (Prime 2 provides this reservoir — typically loaded with 3-5 points at creation). A ward that runs out of Quintessence becomes dormant but can be recharged. The Syndicate uses these extensively to protect financial documents and evidence trails.

Coincidental in all circumstances — bad luck has many mundane explanations.

### Blessings and Curses

Traditions: Euthanatos, Verbena, Celestial Chorus, Dreamspeakers. Spheres: Entropy 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Lasting], [Environmental].

The mage directs the flow of fortune toward or against a target person, place, or ongoing situation, shaping the probability currents that govern mundane events. At Entropy 3, the blessing or curse is general rather than specific — the mage can say "may fortune favor this endeavor" or "may misfortune dog his steps," but the exact form the fortune takes remains the Storyguide's domain. This is an essential feature of the technique: a blessing at this level is genuinely coincidental because it works through naturally occurring events rather than impossible direct intervention. A blessed merchant finds customers arriving when supply is favorable; a cursed politician finds journalists present when they blunder. Duration is one scene by default, extended by additional hits. Entropy 4-5 specifies exact misfortunes (vulgar). Adding Time 4 creates a contingency-triggered blessing or curse that fires when a particular event occurs. Verbena curse-workers use elaborate poppets and sympathetic foci; Celestial Chorus blessings are delivered through prayer and anointing; Chakravanti practitioners of the Euthanatos favor a simple direct gaze at the appropriate moment.

Coincidental when the blessing or curse manifests through natural circumstances. Vulgar when the mage specifies an exact and impossible outcome.

### Binding Oaths

Traditions: Euthanatos, Order of Hermes, Verbena. Spheres: Entropy 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Lasting], [Interaction].

The mage wraps probability itself into the structure of a sworn oath, creating a causal constraint that activates if the oath is broken. The target must consent to the oath willingly and consciously — coercion or deception invalidates the binding before it can take hold. The mage attunes their Entropy perception to the target's statement of intent, feeling the threads of probability knit around the spoken words. From that moment, the oath is a fact of the target's probability space: if they honor it, fortune nudges slightly in their favor in related matters; if they break it knowingly, a curse activates with an intensity scaled to the number of hits from the original casting. Simple oaths (one hit) carry a vague tide of bad luck; serious oaths (five or more hits) carry specific curses calibrated to the betrayal. The binding is detectable by anyone with Entropy 1 who examines the target with a Perception + Awareness roll. Euthanatos practitioners of the kurushige tradition consider Binding Oaths among their most sacred social tools, using them to establish contracts between disparate factions that neither side could otherwise trust to honor.

Coincidental when the curse manifests as general misfortune. Vulgar when the triggered doom is specific and impossible.


### Fate and Fortune — Probability Manipulation

Traditions: Euthanatos, Verbena. Conventions: Syndicate. Spheres: Entropy 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Subtle], [Environmental], [Boost].

The mage reads the current probability flows of a situation and applies gentle pressure to nudge small events in a desired direction — what mystics call "riding luck." This is the fundamental Entropy 2 working: not commanding fate but steering it, the way a skilled sailor steers a sailboat by adjusting to existing winds rather than creating new ones. The target element must already be present: the mage cannot conjure a taxi from thin air, but can arrange for a taxi that was already on the next block to turn this way. A door doesn't lock itself; a door that has a weak latch finds that latch failing at the convenient moment.

Each hit on the casting roll represents one successful probability nudge — a small event (a conversation distracted, a document misfiled, a wrong turn taken) that serves the mage's interests without requiring the impossible. Coincidental application requires that the arranged coincidence fall within the range of what could plausibly happen anyway; the Storyguide always controls the specific form the fortune takes. With 3 hits, the probability nudges are reliable and the mage can arrange multiple small events in sequence, creating a chain of convenient coincidences. With 5 hits, the nudge is powerful enough to reliably arrange events that are only improbable (not impossible) — a phone call arriving at exactly the right moment, a specific person walking through a door within five minutes.

Examples: A Syndicate operative nudges the probability of a building inspector's attention; the inspector skips the floor where the operative's assets are stored. A Verbena witch nudges the weather forecast; the outdoor ceremony benefits from the one-hour gap in an otherwise rainy day. An Euthanatos mage nudges the timing of a target's movements; the target takes the route that brings them past the mage's position.

Coincidental when the arranged events fall within normal probability ranges. Vulgar when the mage specifies an exact and inherently impossible outcome.

### Dangerous Quirks of Misfortune

Traditions: Euthanatos. Conventions: Syndicate. Spheres: Entropy 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Damage], [Environmental], [Subtle].

Where Fate and Fortune nudges probability toward the mage's benefit, Dangerous Quirks actively arranges for existing environmental hazards to become relevant at the worst possible moment for the intended target. The mage does not create the hazard from nothing — a speeding car, a weakened floorboard, a loose railing, a falling candle, a broken step, a gas leak near an open flame — but they reach into the probability flow and make it certain that the hazard manifests when the target is most exposed to it. The distinction from direct damage magic is important: the mage arranges the timing of an existing hazard, not the outcome.

This keeps the technique firmly coincidental: every arranged hazard could have occurred without magical intervention. The mage's hit roll establishes the reliability of the arrangement (one hit creates a probability nudge that is about 70% likely to succeed; five hits creates a near-certainty). The Storyguide determines the actual mechanical outcome of the hazard — the mage set the trap, not the result. This prevents the technique from being used to specify deaths or catastrophic outcomes directly. If the mage wants to specify that the speeding car will definitely strike the target, that is Entropy 4+ and becomes Vulgar. Dangerous Quirks remains coincidental precisely because it cannot guarantee outcomes — only opportunities.

Examples: With 2 hits, the mage arranges for a wet floor warning to be absent precisely when the target rushes through a lobby; the target may slip. With 4 hits, the mage arranges for the target's vehicle brakes to develop a problem that manifests during highway driving; the Storyguide determines the severity. With 5 hits against a target in a building with poor maintenance, the mage arranges a sequence of small environmental failures that cumulatively create a hazardous situation — none of which is individually unusual, but which converge on the target.

Coincidental in all circumstances when the hazard could have occurred naturally. Vulgar when the mage tries to specify the exact outcome rather than setting conditions.

### Spotting Lies — Entropy Method

Traditions: Euthanatos. Conventions: Syndicate. Spheres: Entropy 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Tags: [Sensory], [Subtle], [Interaction].

The mage uses Entropy perception to read the behavioral and probabilistic tells that accompany deception — the micro-expressions, vocal stress patterns, and behavioral inconsistencies that betray a conscious liar. Unlike the Mind 1 variant (which reads the mental static around lies), the Entropy method reads the external patterns: deception creates structural inconsistency in communication that Entropy 1 perceives as clearly as a crack in a bridge. A careful liar reduces these tells; a nervous or inexperienced one multiplies them. With Entropy 2-3, the mage can manipulate probability to create circumstances that make maintaining a lie harder. With Entropy 5, the mage overwhelms the liar's concentration until the truth simply breaks free.

Each hit on the casting roll provides clarity about the subject's current truthfulness: 1 hit detects the presence or absence of active deception; 3 hits identifies which specific statements were false; 5 hits provides the full texture of what the subject is hiding, including their emotional state around the deception. The technique cannot read what the truth is — only that deception is occurring and around what subject matter. Combined with Mind 2 (surface thought reading), the mage can often reconstruct the truth from the shape of the lie around it.

Examples: During an interrogation, an Euthanatos mage rolls 3 hits while the subject gives their account; she immediately knows which parts of the story were fabricated. With Entropy 3, she arranges coincidences (a phone rings at the wrong moment, an unexpected question from a companion) that break the liar's concentration and create gaps in the fabrication. With Entropy 5 against a hardened professional deceiver, the mage's working gradually erodes the liar's ability to maintain their story — within a scene, contradictions accumulate until the truth emerges.

Coincidental in all circumstances for Entropy 1-3 — appears as exceptional observational skill and fortunate timing.

### Tell-Tale Heart — Guilt and Confession

Traditions: Euthanatos, Order of Hermes. Conventions: NWO. Spheres: Entropy 2, Life 3, Mind 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Life. Tags: [Interaction], [Lasting], [Subtle].

A combined working that uses three Spheres as a sophisticated engine for extracting confession. Entropy 2 arranges circumstances to increase mounting pressure on the target — coincidences that remind them of what they've done, encounters with people they've wronged, documents that surface at inconvenient moments. Life 3 heightens anxiety through direct body chemistry manipulation — increasing cortisol, triggering fight-or-flight responses, disrupting sleep patterns. Mind 2 amplifies the psychological guilt response, turning what might be manageable discomfort into overwhelming emotional pressure. The three effects work synergistically: Entropy creates the external pressure, Life creates the internal physical experience of guilt, Mind makes it psychologically unbearable.

With sufficient hits, the target breaks down and confesses the truth themselves — the mage never needs to apply direct force, issue commands, or violate the target's will in any direct way. The target experiences the confession as arising entirely from their own conscience, which is both the technique's strength (no Mind-control Paradox) and its limitation (false positives exist — innocent people under extreme pressure sometimes confess to things they didn't do). The mage must apply this working over time; it is not an instant effect. Each scene of sustained application increases the probability of confession by one step.

Examples: With 3 hits applied to a guilty murderer over a scene, the target's anxiety rises to the level where they make mistakes, avoiding relevant topics, overexplaining themselves. With 5 hits sustained over a day, the target begins sleeping badly, their guilt manifesting as physical symptoms; by the end of the second day, the probability of voluntary confession is high. A false positive: an innocent person subjected to this working may confess to lesser things they have done wrong, overwhelmed by the accumulated guilt-pressure, confusing it for guilt about the specific matter the mage is investigating.

Coincidental in all circumstances — guilt, anxiety, and confession are natural psychological processes.

### Losing My Religion — Destroying Faith

Traditions: Nephandi, Euthanatos. Spheres: Entropy 5. SF: 4. Difficulty: 5 (Rote). Tags: [Lasting], [Mental], [Controversial].

The most spiritually invasive application of Entropy Sphere mastery — the deliberate unraveling of another person's core spiritual belief and sense of existential self-worth through metaphysical probability manipulation. This is not persuasion: the mage is not arguing the target out of their faith, but using Entropy 5 to attack the structural integrity of belief at a Pattern level, making it inherently less probable that the target can continue to hold the conviction that gives their life meaning. The working is deeply transgressive and generates intense dark Resonance in the caster.

Each hit on the casting roll reduces the target's temporary Willpower by one, as the foundation of their will — their belief in meaning — is eroded. If the target's Willpower reaches zero, their faith is broken: they experience a profound existential crisis, and all conviction-based Willpower expenditures are unavailable to them until faith is restored through significant personal work or magical intervention. Against mages, full success (typically 10 or more hits against a mage's Willpower) makes all of their subsequent magic Vulgar with witnesses — the mage has lost the conviction that underlies Coincidental magic, and the universe responds to their new uncertainty. True Faith adds +1 difficulty per dot and +2 additional required hits, as faith of that quality has its own metaphysical structural integrity.

This rote cannot be used on a large audience — faith is personal, and the Entropy working requires the mage to focus on the specific target's Pattern with a precision that precludes area effects. It is a deeply intimate and deeply harmful working. The Euthanatos study it theoretically to understand it; practical use is largely confined to Nephandi and dark-aspected mages who have decided the target's faith is itself a danger. Combining this rote with Mind torture or sustained physical breakdown can make the effect permanent. Recovery is possible through genuine spiritual renewal, counseling, and sometimes another mage's restorative working.

Examples: Against a True Faith holder with 3 dots in Faith, the mage needs to overcome the target's Willpower + 3 difficulty, requiring extra hits. With 6 hits, the target's Willpower is reduced to near zero and they enter acute spiritual crisis. With 10 hits against a non-mage target, the faith collapse is complete. Against an Awakened mage with 5 hits, the mage's next three castings are Vulgar with witnesses regardless of circumstance.

Vulgar in all circumstances at difficulty 9 — even without witnesses, this working generates Paradox.

### Witch's Vengeance

Traditions: Verbena, Euthanatos, Dreamspeakers, Order of Hermes, Cult of Ecstasy. Spheres: Entropy 4, Life 3, Time 3. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Entropy. Tags: [Damage], [Ranged], [Lasting], [Aggravated], [Interaction], [Controversial].

The oldest and most feared curse in the traditional mage's repertoire — not the quickest, not the cleanest, and not the most subtle, but the most complete. The mage constructs an effigy of the target: a poppet of wax and thread, a painted image, a carved idol, a digital file containing every piece of identifying information. The effigy is then subjected to the fate intended for the target: burned, stabbed, drowned, corrupted, aged, unraveled. The sympathetic link between the image and the person carries the Effect. The spell draws on Entropy 4 to impose accelerated decay and biological corruption on a complex living pattern; Life 3 to target the biological specifics of the victim rather than merely their Pattern; and Time 3 to carry the degradation forward through time — accelerating the biological clock, aging the target, collapsing what should have taken decades into hours or days.

The cultural variations are extensive. Euthanatos practitioners call this "the Good Death Forced" and apply it only to those whose karmic calculus justifies it — in their tradition, Witch's Vengeance without moral justification draws catastrophic personal Paradox. Verbena hedge-witches call it the "ill-wishing" and typically use Entropy-primary variants focused on misfortune rather than direct biological assault. Dreamspeakers use a spirit-sympathetic version that works through the target's spiritual connections rather than their physical pattern. Order of Hermes formalize it as a "Hermetic Hex" with precise sympathetic protocols and documented outcomes. The Cult of Ecstasy, improbably, have a pleasure-based inversion of the same mechanics — they use it to accelerate joy and creative excess in targets they want to help rather than harm, with results that are almost as disconcerting to the recipient.

The curse requires a genuine sympathetic link to the target — something physically connected (hair, blood, a cherished possession) or a digitally-assembled information portrait comprehensive enough to create a Pattern-link. Without the link, Entropy 4 cannot discriminate between the target and the environment; the Effect disperses. A Correspondence component (1 dot for every dot in the primary damage Sphere) extends the range to not require physical contact with the target — without it, the mage must be within touching distance at some point in the working, which creates obvious operational challenges when the target is a well-protected enemy. The spell is so overtly supernatural that no amount of paradigmatic camouflage renders it coincidental. Paradox is inevitable unless the mage is working in a dedicated Chantry or other space outside the Consensus.

Examples: With 4 hits over an extended ritual, the curse inflicts Entropy 4's pattern-decay Effect on the target: they experience accelerated biological corruption (illness, aging), losing 1 Stamina point per day until the curse is broken or runs its Duration. With 6 hits, the aging effect advances the target's biological clock by a decade, manifesting in rapid physical deterioration within hours of casting. With 8 hits and a Time 3 component, the mage specifies that a particular biological catastrophe (stroke, system failure, total pattern unraveling) occurs at a specific future moment, building toward it with horrible inevitability.

Vulgar in all circumstances, always. This is the most paradigmatically "magical" act in the traditional repertoire, and reality objects to it categorically.

### Devil's Advocate

Traditions: Nephandi (gilledians, aswadim). Spheres: Entropy ●●●●●, Mind ●●●. SF: 4. Difficulty: 5 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Entropy. Tags: [Mental], [Control], [Lasting], [Controversial], [Corrupt].

This working is among the most dangerous things a highly skilled Nephandus can do, precisely because it looks like nothing more than a very good argument. The practitioner engages the target in conversation — ideally sustained, ideally establishing a position of intellectual or social authority before the argument begins — and weaves two simultaneous Effects into the discourse. Mind ●●● plants the argument itself deep into the listener's processing, bypassing critical filters and encoding the ideas as though they were the listener's own conclusions. Entropy ●●●●● systematically erodes the underlying belief structures that would otherwise provide resistance: not attacking the target's ideas directly, but dissolving the epistemological foundations those ideas rest on. When the foundations dissolve, the new ideas do not feel like intrusions. They feel like realizations.

The working succeeds not through force but through patient philosophical demolition. The mage does not argue against the target's beliefs so much as cause those beliefs to crumble from within while simultaneously presenting an alternative that fills the resulting void. A target who begins the conversation as a committed Traditionalist can end it having reached conclusions she is certain are her own — conclusions that happen to align perfectly with what the Nephandus needed her to believe. This is not brainwashing. Brainwashing leaves residue. This is worse: it convinces. The target often becomes a passionate advocate for the position she was brought to, because she believes she arrived there through genuine reasoning.

The mechanical structure requires sustained social interaction. The Mind ●●● Effect is woven throughout a conversation that can range from a single long session to multiple meetings over days or weeks; an extended roll structure applies, with each session contributing to the total successes needed. The Entropy ●●●●● Effect requires the mage to have achieved at least initial rapport with the target — Entropy works on patterns, and the mage must perceive the target's belief-patterns before eroding them. The casting roll is made against the target's Willpower as difficulty (plus 2 if the target is an Awakened mage or a member of the Night-Folk, whose own reality-perception provides structural resistance to Conceptual Entropy).

Hits needed to overcome resistance depend on the depth of the conviction under assault:
- 1–2 hits: a casual preference or surface-level opinion
- 3–4 hits: a genuine but not deeply held conviction
- 5–6 hits: a strongly held philosophical or moral position
- 7+ hits: a core identity-level belief, something the target would previously have said was fundamental to who they are

The Entropy component is the key to the working's power and its terrible reputation. Entropy ●●●●● does not merely nudge probabilities — at this level, it can consume entire conceptual structures, leaving the target in a temporary state of radical philosophical openness during which the Mind ●●● component can introduce and anchor almost anything. A mage who botches the introduction step during this window installs whatever happened to be in the conversation at that moment — including accidental ideas, irrelevant details, or nothing at all, leaving the target in a state of permanent belief-collapse that no subsequent conversation will repair. This is not considered a success.

Because the working is entirely conversational, it is always coincidental. No one observing the conversation sees anything supernatural. What they see is someone having a long, engaging discussion that seems to leave the other participant thoughtful and changed. This is, in the usual way of these things, more frightening than something visible.

SF: 4 (rote). Freeform SF: 4 (highest Sphere 5 − 1 = 4 for rote purposes; freeform SF = Entropy 5 + Mind 3 − 1 = 7, but the rote advantage applies). Casting pool: Manipulation + Entropy, extended over social interaction.

Example — 3 hits across two conversations: The target, a junior Tradition mage, came in skeptical of Technocratic approaches and leaves mildly convinced that certain Union protocols have merit. Not a total conversion — but a crack. The Nephandus returns in a month to widen it.

Example — 6 hits across a week of increasingly intimate philosophical discussions: The target, a mid-level Verbena, began as a dedicated defender of the Council and ends the week having genuinely concluded that the Council's approach to the Ascension War is morally indefensible. She reaches this conclusion herself, by working through arguments she believes she constructed. She tells her closest friend about her crisis of faith. Her friend is alarmed. The Nephandus is already scheduling a follow-up visit.

Example — 9 hits, extended campaign against a senior Tradition mage: The target's core identity as an Awakened defender of human potential is systematically dissolved and replaced with a philosophical framework that justifies the Nephandi's goals. The process takes four months. The target Awakens on the other side of it as a gilledian — not through the Caul, but through having been argued there, step by careful step. This is considered one of the most complete victories a Devil's Advocate practitioner can achieve and is extremely rare.

Coincidental in all circumstances — what witnesses see is conversation.


---

### Hex — Stalking Misfortune

Traditions: Euthanatos (Kali's Mark), Verbena (Evil Threaded Wyrd), Batini (Ill Star Invoked). Spheres: Entropy 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Curse], [Lasting], [Subtle].

Named for the Paradox spirit Hex — an entity that inflicts precisely one or two botches upon a transgressing mage before departing — this working deliberately reproduces that entity's singular technique as a deployable curse. The mage reads the probability currents surrounding a target (Entropy 1 perception) and then selects a specific class of non-magical action within which the target will botch exactly once (Entropy 2 directional curse). Unlike a generalized bad-luck effect, this working is surgical: the mage designates a category of action (driving a vehicle, conducting a negotiation, making a physical attack, performing surgery) and the curse waits, latent and undetectable, until the next time the target performs an action of that type. At that moment, regardless of dice results, the action botches.

The curse does not announce itself. It arrives when it arrives. The target will experience their botch as ordinary catastrophic failure — a brake line that gives way at the wrong moment, a negotiation that collapses because of an absurd misreading, an attack that somehow hits an ally instead of an enemy. The magical origin is invisible to anyone without Entropy 2 perception applied specifically to the target's current probability envelope. Against a target so examined, the curse appears as a tight cluster of black probability — a snagged thread in the weave of their near future, wound around one specific type of action.

The curse expires after firing once (or twice, if the mage achieves 7 or more hits). It cannot fire more than once per scene, and it always departs after the second botch, following the Hex spirit's own behavioral constraints. The mage does not need to be present when the curse fires — it follows the target. Distance does not attenuate it. The only reliable defense is an Entropy-based ward or a sustained probability-monitoring Effect that would detect and snap the thread before the targeted action occurs; the latter requires Entropy 3 and active maintenance, which is impractical for most targets.

Euthanatos practitioners deploy this against targets in high-stakes situations where a single catastrophic failure carries significant consequences. A mage they intend to see removed might be hexed at the point of a combat they are known to be entering; the botch in that combat may be sufficient. Verbena witches use it as a curse in the older traditional sense — the evil eye made literal — and invoke it through ritual objects associated with the target. Batini practitioners tie it to astrological timing, selecting the moment of curse-application to align with the target's worst celestial configuration.

Apprentices sometimes attempt this rote as a form of pranking, cursing a social rival to botch their next public speaking engagement or romantic overture. Instructors who discover this use of the working tend to view it as a misappropriation of a technique that carries lethal potential in experienced hands, and respond accordingly.

Examples: With 3 hits, the curse is set on a Sleeper witness who intends to report the mage's activities to law enforcement. The next time that witness attempts to deliver their testimony, they suffer a spectacular and credibility-destroying botch — misidentifying the mage, contradicting their own prior statements, or producing evidence in a way that the court finds inadmissible. The case collapses. With 5 hits on a rival mage entering a contested situation: the botch fires on the rival's first offensive working, turning a combat-effective spell into a Paradox-generating failure that draws the Consensus's attention at the worst possible moment. With 7 hits (double-fire variant), the mage curses an enemy mage with two botches in the category "any magical working" — ensuring that two of the enemy's next workings will fail catastrophically, regardless of how well the dice otherwise favor them. The second botch may trigger after the enemy has recovered from the first and believes the danger is past.

Coincidental when applied through ritual prior to the target's action. Vulgar when applied in direct view of witnesses, or when the botch itself is so improbable that observers who know probability theory begin to suspect interference.


## Forces — Rotes of Energy and Physical Law

Forces magic commands the energies that underlie physical reality: heat, light, electricity, gravity, magnetism, sound, and kinetic force. It is among the most visually dramatic Sphere — Forces mages throw lightning and freeze rivers — and among the most likely to generate Paradox when used without restraint. The best Forces practitioners learn to work with environmental context, finding the line between "mage calls lightning" (Vulgar) and "mage encourages an already-gathering storm" (Coincidental).

### Read the Field

Traditions: Virtual Adepts, Sons of Ether. Spheres: Forces 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Tags: [Sensory], [Subtle].

The mage opens their senses to the full electromagnetic spectrum, perceiving radio signals, electronic transmissions, heat signatures, magnetic fields, and power-line hum. Any broadcasting or transmitting device within (Arete/Enlightenment x 100) meters is immediately located and its signal type identified (though not decoded without additional technology or Mind effects). The mage can also detect live electrical currents in walls, locate heat sources through barriers, and sense the direction of magnetic north precisely. Virtual Adepts use this rote constantly — they describe it as "turning on the real HUD." Sons of Ether call it "Etheric Field Awareness" and attribute it to sensitivity to Tesla's universal medium.

Coincidental in all circumstances.

### Flame Arrow

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Dreamspeakers, Verbena. Spheres: Forces 2, Prime 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Forces. Tags: [Ranged], [Damage], [Elemental].

The mage shapes raw energy into a directed projectile — a bolt of fire, a lance of electricity, a compression wave of kinetic force — and hurls it at a target in line of sight. The form varies by Tradition and Paradigm: Hermetic mages cast crackling lightning; Verbena witches throw witch-fire; Dreamspeakers call down spirit-shaped flame. Mechanics are identical regardless of form. Damage: (hits + 2) dice of Lethal, applied against Soak. The bolt travels at the speed of light and cannot be dodged conventionally — the target must use a magical defense or simply absorb the hit. Maximum range is visual line of sight; each 10 meters beyond 20 meters imposes -1 die on the attack roll.

Coincidental in environments where the energy type is plausible (electrical storms for lightning, wildfires for flame). Vulgar with Witnesses in most urban or calm conditions.

### Thunderclap

Traditions: Verbena, Sons of Ether. Spheres: Forces 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Area], [Damage], [Knockback], [Environmental].

The mage detonates a concussive shockwave of compressed air in a radius of (hits × 3) meters, centered on a point the mage can see. All targets in the area must resist with Strength + Athletics (Difficulty 2) or be knocked prone and pushed back (hits) meters. Those who fail also suffer 1 die of Bashing damage from the sonic impact. Everyone in the radius, including the mage if they are within it, is deafened for one round per hit — all hearing-based perception rolls fail, and shouted communication is impossible. Thunderclap is particularly useful for crowd control and tactical repositioning. Sons of Ether call it "Acoustic Pressure Discharge" and build special resonating chambers into their foci.

Coincidental in environments where sudden explosive sounds are plausible (construction zones, thunderstorms, heavy machinery). Vulgar in quiet locations.

### Wall of Force

Traditions: Sons of Ether, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Forces 3, Prime 1. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Forces. Tags: [Protective], [Barrier], [Lasting], [Environmental].

The mage erects an invisible barrier of coherent force fields that stops physical objects and energy (except the mage's own magic). The Wall has a Soak of 8 against physical attacks and persists for (hits) scenes. It occupies up to (hits × 2) square meters and can be shaped into walls, domes, floors, or any flat or curved surface the mage specifies. Projectiles, physical strikes, and even vehicles simply halt at the boundary. Pushed hard enough (a vehicle at high speed, a determined magical strike), the Wall can be broken: it absorbs up to (hits × 3) total damage before collapsing. The Wall is invisible to normal sight but visible to anyone with Prime 2+ or Correspondence 2+.

Vulgar with Witnesses when the Wall stops a visible attack and reveals itself. Coincidental only when the mage can frame it as an unusually strong wind, a sudden structural barrier, or something equally mundane.

### Call Lightning

Traditions: Verbena, Dreamspeakers. Spheres: Forces 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Ranged], [Damage], [Elemental], [Environmental].

The mage calls down a bolt of real lightning from storm clouds overhead — or, indoors, manifests a controlled electrical discharge. When used outdoors in overcast or stormy conditions, the bolt travels from the clouds to a target the mage designates within visual range. Damage: (hits + 4) dice of Lethal. The bolt can arc to one additional target within 3 meters of the primary target for half damage, with no separate roll required. Indoors, the working still functions but draws current from ambient sources (power lines, generators, the mage's own Quintessence) and deals the same damage with no arc. Targets struck by natural-seeming lightning must make a Composure roll (Difficulty 2) or be Stunned for one round.

Coincidental outdoors in overcast conditions — lightning during a storm is expected. Vulgar indoors or in clear-sky conditions.

### Veil of Darkness

Traditions: Sons of Ether, Virtual Adepts, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Forces 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Area], [Subtle], [Environmental], [Lasting].

The mage manipulates the light waves within a bounded area, creating a zone of absolute darkness — not merely dim light, but the complete absence of photons. Targets inside suffer all penalties for blindness (no attack or perception rolls that require sight; all physical actions suffer -3 dice; movement speed halved). Beings with genuine darkvision (Life 3+ enhancement, certain spirits, some vampires) see normally. Standard night-vision technology requires at least some ambient light and fails inside the Veil. The darkness fills a (hits × 3)-meter radius and persists for one scene or until the mage drops concentration. The mage can see through their own Veil at will.

Coincidental when power failures, smoke, or other mundane darkness events can be invoked as cover. Vulgar when the darkness appears with no obvious cause.

### Electromagnetic Pulse

Traditions: Virtual Adepts, Sons of Ether. Spheres: Forces 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Area], [Technological], [Damage], [Environmental].

The mage releases a controlled burst of electromagnetic radiation that disables all unshielded electronics within a radius of (hits × 5) meters. Vehicles stall (a restart requires a repair roll at Difficulty 2 — the mage's hits as a penalty), communications devices go silent, weapon electronics jam, and electronic locks disengage or freeze. The disruption lasts one scene; permanently damaging devices requires a second casting or achieving 5+ hits (which fuses circuits permanently). Faraday cages, military-hardened equipment, and specially warded devices (Sphere-enhanced technology, Technocratic constructs) are partially or fully immune. Living beings are unaffected. This rote is a Virtual Adept specialty for tactical disruption of Technocracy assets.

Coincidental when power surge events are plausible in the area. Vulgar in locations with no obvious electrical infrastructure.

### Freeze

Traditions: Sons of Ether, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Forces 2, Matter 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Forces. Tags: [Elemental], [Damage], [Environmental].

The mage rapidly extracts thermal energy from a target or localized area, dropping temperature to near-absolute-zero in seconds. Against a living target: (hits + 2) dice of Lethal damage as tissues crystallize, blood partially freezes, and extremities suffer instant frostbite. Against objects: matter becomes extremely brittle, reducing Soak by (hits) for one round before the material warms back to ambient temperature. Environmental use creates a frost zone up to (hits × 2) meters in radius, icing floors (all Athletics rolls to move suffer -2 dice), freezing water surfaces solid, and potentially damaging electronics and hydraulic systems. Targets in the frost zone who fail a Stamina roll (Difficulty 1) gain the Slowed condition for one round.

Coincidental in cold environments or when affecting only objects. Vulgar when visibly freezing a person solid in front of Sleepers.

---

### Blackout

Traditions: Virtual Adepts, Sons of Ether. Conventions: Syndicate. Spheres: Forces 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Environmental], [Lasting].

The mage disrupts electrical current in a defined area, plunging it into darkness and disabling powered systems. The simplest application — a room or building — requires Forces 2 and one or two hits; a city block requires Forces 3 and five or more hits; a city district or power grid requires Forces 5. The disruption is not sustained by the mage after the initial Effect: the mage arranges a probability of cascading failure and the mundane power grid does the rest. Backup generators and battery systems are unaffected unless the mage specifically targets them. In combat, a sudden Blackout equalizes fighters who have night-vision capability (natural or magical) with those who do not — skilled operators often trigger it the instant before stepping into a room. Adding Matter to the Effect allows the mage to permanently damage electrical infrastructure rather than merely interrupt it. Virtual Adepts treat this as basic systems intrusion; Sons of Ether describe it as electromagnetic field disruption via aetherial resonance dampening.

Coincidental when the blackout appears to be a plausible electrical failure. Vulgar when an impossibly precise or instant city-wide effect occurs.

### Elemental Assault

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Sons of Ether, Verbena, Akashayana. Spheres: Forces 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Damage], [Environmental].

The mage redirects existing elemental forces as weapons — hurling fire from an existing source, redirecting a river's current, propelling stones with atmospheric pressure, intensifying wind to a battering force. The key distinction from conjuration is that the element must already be present: fire must be burning, water must be flowing, wind must be blowing. No Prime energy is required because the mage works with what exists. Each hit adds to the force of the redirection (damage ratings for elemental types: fire and stone = Lethal; wind and water = Bashing; electricity = Lethal). At Forces 3+, the mage can sustain the assault across multiple turns without recasting, treating the redirected element as a controlled ongoing effect. The Akashayana understand this as "flowing with ki" — not fighting reality but steering it. Order of Hermes practitioners achieve the same result through formal thaumaturgy using elemental signatures and declared intents. Very useful in outdoor combat, industrial settings, or any location with environmental hazards.

Coincidental when working with existing environmental elements at moderate intensity. Vulgar when the redirection is extreme or physically impossible.

### The Storm-Cocoon

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Sons of Ether, Verbena. Spheres: Forces 3, Entropy 2, Prime 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Forces. Tags: [Protective], [Environmental], [Lasting].

The mage creates a ten-foot radius protective bubble of swirling elemental forces — howling winds, crackling electrical discharge, or a maelstrom of mixed energies — centered on themselves or a fixed point. Allies within the cocoon are shielded from incoming attacks: the swirling energy adds (hits × 2) dice to soak pools against ranged attacks and reduces the effective range of incoming projectiles (treat all ranged attacks as if from one range band further). The Entropy 2 component makes the cocoon's behavior unpredictable and difficult to target through — attackers who attempt to fire through the maelstrom face a +2 difficulty penalty in addition to the soak bonus. The cocoon moves with the mage if she concentrates; if she takes any action other than moving, it remains fixed at its current position. Duration is one scene or until the mage lets it lapse. The visual effect is dramatic: visible swirling wind, sparks, and occasional lightning suggest a figure at the center of a personal storm. In Verbena weather-witchery, this is called the Wind-Bride's Mantle.

Coincidental when experienced as an unusual weather pocket. Vulgar when the swirling elemental forces are clearly centered on a specific person.

### Creating Light

Traditions: Celestial Chorus, Sons of Ether, Virtual Adepts. Spheres: Forces 3, Prime 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Forces. Tags: [Environmental], [Lasting].

The mage conjures actual illumination where none exists — not by enhancing existing light (Forces 2) but by creating radiance from raw Quintessential energy shaped by Forces. The Prime provides the raw energy; the Forces component shapes it into the appropriate frequency of electromagnetic radiation. At Forces 2, the mage can only amplify, redirect, or dim existing light sources. Forces 3 with Prime 2 creates genuine light in total darkness. The light's color and quality matches the mage's paradigm: Celestial Chorus practitioners produce a warm golden-white that witnesses describe as beautiful and faintly warm; Sons of Ether create clinically neutral white light appropriate to a laboratory; Virtual Adepts produce selectable-spectrum illumination as one would dial through a LED controller. Intensity and area scale with hits (1 success: candlelight; 3 hits: room-filling; 5 hits: outdoor daylight equivalent). Conjured Sunlight — true UV-spectrum light capable of affecting photosensitive beings — requires Forces 4 and Prime 3, with Life 3 ensuring the biological component. Duration: one scene or sustained with concentration.

Coincidental when amplifying existing light. Vulgar when creating light from absolute darkness with no apparent source.

### Fireballs and Lightning

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Sons of Ether, Akashayana, Celestial Chorus. Spheres: Forces 3, Prime 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Forces. Tags: [Damage], [Aggravated].

The classic elemental attack — conjuring and hurling concentrated fire, lightning, or plasma at a target. Forces defines the medium and the damage; Prime 2 provides the Quintessential energy to conjure the element from nothing. The attack adds (hits) automatic damage dice in addition to rolled hits (treating the excess hits as automatic damage adds). Fire and lightning inflict Lethal damage at Forces 3; Aggravated damage requires Prime 3 in addition. Wind and concentrated sound inflict Bashing. The range and area of effect scale with hits (1 success: one target at short range; 5 hits: all targets within a ten-meter radius at medium range). Order of Hermes practitioners draw on classical elemental invocations with appropriate sigils and spoken formulae; Sons of Ether use hand-held plasma projectors whose "batteries" are Tass-charged capacitors; Akashayana chi-fire practitioners require no physical focus but must begin from a meditative stance. Sustained effects (walls of fire, fields of lightning) require additional hits allocated to Duration. Interactions: adding Matter 2 allows igniting fire-resistant materials; Spirit 2 makes the elemental attack capable of harming ephemeral beings.

Vulgar in all circumstances — elemental energy appearing from nothing is explicit impossibility.


### Invisibility and Silence

Traditions: Akashayana, Virtual Adepts, Sons of Ether. Conventions: NWO. Spheres: Forces 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Subtle], [Protective], [Lasting].

The mage bends light around themselves (or a target) to achieve visual invisibility, or suppresses sound waves to achieve silence — two applications of the same underlying Forces 2 working. Visual invisibility is the classic application: photons curve smoothly around the subject, creating the impression of an absence. The invisible subject can see normally only if Correspondence 1 is added to redirect some photons back to their eyes; without it, bending light around the self also blocks incoming light, leaving the mage effectively blind while invisible. Silence works by suppressing sound waves generated by the subject — footsteps vanish, breathing becomes inaudible, clothing stops rustling — and can be applied to a person or to an area.

Each hit on the casting roll determines the quality of the invisibility: 1 hit creates a blurring shimmer that passes casual glances; 3 hits creates genuine invisibility to all normal sight; 5 hits creates invisibility even to thermal imaging. The Silence component is more consistent — even 1 hit creates near-perfect acoustic suppression within the defined range, though extreme sounds (a gunshot, a shout) may bleed through with only 1 hit. Virtual Adepts conceptualize this as active camouflage running adaptive light-refraction algorithms; Sons of Ether describe it as aetherial refraction suits. NWO operatives use it routinely for infiltration; their paradigm frames it as advanced active camouflage technology. Akashayana shadow-step practitioners integrate it with movement training to achieve complete perceptual invisibility through a combination of Forces 2 and extraordinary chi-discipline.

Examples: With 2 hits (Forces 2, visual only), the mage is functionally invisible to casual observers but a trained professional looking carefully will notice the light distortion. Add Correspondence 1 and the mage can see normally while invisible. With 4 hits, the mage is invisible to all optical systems including cameras. With 5 hits and combined visual/silence effect, even an enhanced-senses observer has difficulty locating the mage by anything other than direct Pattern-sense or Spirit perception.

Vulgar in all circumstances — bending light around a person is explicit impossibility to Sleepers who look directly at where the mage should be.

### Shadowplay

Traditions: Hollow Ones, Akashayana, Euthanatos, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Forces 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Environmental], [Subtle], [Sensory].

The mage manipulates shadows and ambient light to create shapes, moving images, dramatic visual effects, and environmental mood. This is more artistic and expressive than purely tactical — though it has tactical applications. The mage can deepen shadows to near-impenetrable blackness in a specific area, create moving shadow figures that appear to be approaching (or fleeing), sculpt existing light into patterns, make their face appear shadowed and threatening even in full light, or create a dramatically lit environment that communicates meaning through visual framing. Shadowplay cannot create light where none exists (that requires Forces 3 / Prime 2), but it can work powerfully with existing illumination to achieve extraordinary effects.

Each hit on the casting represents a degree of precision and scope in the manipulation: 1 hit deepens ambient shadows in a room; 3 hits creates moving shadow figures of convincing complexity; 5 hits sculpts the entire lighting environment of a space into a specific visual statement that conveys information (a warning, an emotion, a representation of events) to anyone who witnesses it. Hollow Ones use Shadowplay extensively as both aesthetic expression and intimidation. Akashayana shadow-step practitioners use it to set up their disappearances — deep shadows provide cover for the physical technique. Euthanatos mages use it in ritual contexts, creating the visual atmosphere appropriate to ceremonies of death and transformation. Order of Hermes practitioners treat it as illumination engineering, using sigil-shapes in shadow to encode magical information.

Examples: With 2 hits, the mage creates dramatic chiaroscuro lighting in a room that makes their interrogation subject deeply uncomfortable without any visible magic. With 3 hits, the mage creates moving shadow figures that distract and divide an opponent's attention. With 5 hits, the mage creates a visual tableau in shadow that all present witnesses interpret as a warning or revelation — the shadow-shapes tell a story or convey a specific meaning.

Coincidental when the shadow manipulation is subtle and artistically plausible. Vulgar when shadow figures clearly move contrary to light sources or when shadows visibly disobey physics.

### Levitation and Objects in Flight

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Sons of Ether, Virtual Adepts, Akashayana. Spheres: Forces 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Transport], [Environmental].

The mage overcomes gravity for a target — themselves, another person, or an object — lifting and moving it through the air without physical support. Self-levitation requires Forces 2 for slow hovering; Forces 3 for directed flight at useful speed. Levitating another living person against their will requires adding Mind 3 (to prevent panic-induced interference with the working) or Life 2 / Prime 2 (to directly manage the target's biological interaction with the effect). Objects in flight scale with Forces rating and weight: person-sized objects (furniture, people) require Forces 3; vehicle-sized require Forces 4; building-mass objects require Forces 5.

Each hit on the casting roll represents precision and speed of movement: 1 hit achieves slow, unsteady levitation; 3 hits achieves directed flight at running speed with reasonable maneuverability; 5 hits achieves fast, precise aerial movement with full control. Dropped objects (deliberately released during flight) follow normal physical trajectories from their levitated height — a person levitated three stories up and then released falls three stories. Combined with Correspondence, the mage can control levitated objects at a distance. The Akashayana's chi-flight (Forces 2 / Life 2 / Prime 2) is a related technique but internally driven; standard levitation is an externally applied force that does not require the target's cooperation. Sons of Ether build gravity neutralizers into their equipment as standard devices.

Examples: With 2 hits (Forces 3), the mage lifts themselves slowly off the ground and hovers at ceiling height. With 4 hits, the mage directs their own flight at running speed, banking and turning with reasonable agility. With 5 hits levitating a car (Forces 4), the mage moves the vehicle precisely into a parking spot or into a wall.

Vulgar in all circumstances — people and objects do not float.

### Disrupting Bodily Functions

Traditions: Euthanatos, Sons of Ether. Conventions: Progenitors. Spheres: Forces 2, Life 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Life. Tags: [Damage], [Targeted].

The mage interferes with a target's biological processes through direct manipulation of the electromagnetic signals that run the nervous system. Forces 2 disrupts the electrical impulses underlying neural function — causing muscle cramps, sensory disruption, coordination failures, and pain without direct physical trauma. Life 3 directs this disruption specifically and aggressively at biological tissue, converting what would be a bashing interference into a direct aggravated attack on the target's Pattern. The distinction matters: Forces 2 alone produces involuntary muscle spasms, vertigo, and nausea (Bashing damage) without any visible mechanism; Life 3 combined produces Pattern-level disruption (Aggravated damage) that attacks the target's biological existence directly.

Each hit inflicts one die of damage (Bashing for Forces 2 alone; Lethal for Forces 2 + Life 3; Aggravated for Life 3 as direct Pattern attack). The target resists with Stamina + Fortitude (Difficulty 2 for Bashing; Difficulty 3 for Lethal/Aggravated). This technique requires line of sight and cannot be used at ranges beyond Medium without Correspondence. Progenitors frame it as "targeted bioelectric disruption"; Sons of Ether as "nerve cascade induction."
