Examples: With 10 hits against a standard vampire, the mage achieves a transformation lasting one scene — sufficient for a dramatic tactical moment and then a very serious problem when the Duration expires. With 15 hits, the transformation lasts a day, during which the furniture can be moved, stored, sat upon, or given to someone as a confusing gift. With 20 hits sustained across an extended ritual working with full Circle cooperation, the transformation becomes permanent — at which point the ethical questions become existential, and every Tradition member who learns of it will have opinions.

Vulgar in all circumstances, always, catastrophically. If you are seriously considering casting this rote in actual play, the Storyguide is entitled to describe the Paradox first, in full, before you commit.

### Beautify or Deform

Traditions: Nephandi (all sects). Spheres: Life ●●● (self only) or Life ●●●● (other targets). SF: 2 (Life ●●●), 3 (Life ●●●●). Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Tags: [Transform], [Lasting], [Control], [Corrupt].

Physical appearance is leverage. The Nephandi understand this with clinical precision and apply it accordingly. Beautify or Deform is one of the oldest workings in the Fallen repertoire: the magical reshaping of a person's physical form for strategic purposes, either enhancing attractiveness beyond natural limits or reducing a target's appearance to something that makes normal social functioning agonizing. The mage does not merely affect how someone looks. They affect how that person moves through the world: who opens doors for them, who trusts them on sight, who recoils from them in instinctive discomfort.

The Beautify variant enhances specific Attributes — typically Appearance, sometimes Charisma (where the enhancement extends to presence and bearing), and occasionally Strength (for practitioners running the working on their enforcers, aiming for physically imposing rather than conventionally attractive). Each Attribute dot raised by the working requires one hit on the casting roll. At standard Duration, the effect fades. To make the enhancement permanent, the casting must score at least four hits (for the caster themselves) or six hits (for another person), and the target must spend the equivalent experience points to "buy into" the new Attribute level — the magic provides the change, but the Pattern settles into it only when the investment is genuine. An unwilling target cannot be permanently beautified without their subsequent cooperation; the working simply reverts at Duration's end unless they accept the change.

A character who receives more than five dots of Appearance from this working gains, for the Duration, an aura of almost supernatural appeal — something beyond mere physical attractiveness that operates at the Pattern level. Sleepers in the vicinity feel a pull they cannot articulate. Even Awakened practitioners who know what they are looking at find themselves slightly more inclined to agree, cooperate, or hesitate before opposing. This is not a Mind Effect; it is the biological reality of a Pattern operating at an intensity that exceeds normal human range.

The Deform variant reduces Appearance through deliberate, precise, and merciless Pattern manipulation. The mage reaches into the target's physical form and adjusts the specifics of their features in directions that the local Consensus identifies as distressing or threatening. A target reduced to Appearance 1 gains the Repulsive Feature Flaw for the Duration; one reduced to Appearance 0 gains the Monstrous Flaw and becomes genuinely difficult to interact with for most Sleepers, who experience an instinctive revulsion that makes normal social exchange impossible. Fallen mages who use this as punishment take their time describing what they are doing, which is considered part of the casting.

The Inhuman Endurance variant replaces aesthetic transformation with stamina enhancement: Stamina is raised rather than Appearance, allowing the subject to sustain physical punishment that would incapacitate a normal person. This is used on enforcers, on captives that need to survive extended torment, and occasionally on the mage themselves when preparing for situations that will involve significant incoming damage. The variant has no cosmetic dimension but can also heal existing damage (two Health Levels of damage healed per additional hit dedicated to healing rather than Stamina raising).

The working is coincidental for subtle transformations — changes explainable by makeup, posture correction, surgical enhancement, or dramatic weight change. It becomes vulgar when the transformation is immediate, radical, or clearly impossible by mundane means.

SF: 2 (Life ●●●, self). SF: 3 (Life ●●●●, other). Casting pool: Resolve + Life.

Example — 2 hits, Life ●●●●, Beautify: A Nephandus raises an associate's Appearance by 2 for the scene before sending them in to seduce a target. The associate's appeal is heightened to something that reads as genuinely striking to everyone present. The seduction goes well. The associate's Appearance returns to baseline afterward.

Example — 4 hits, Life ●●●● (self), permanent: The mage raises their own Appearance by 3 permanently, committing the experience points for the change. What was merely attractive becomes something that makes first impressions extremely difficult to shake. Subsequent uses of Art of Seduction against targets who have met the mage are at -1 Difficulty.

Example — 5 hits, Life ●●●●, Deform: A target who refused to cooperate wakes the next morning with their Appearance reduced to 0. The change is not gradual. The Monstrous Flaw applies immediately. Their partner calls emergency services. Their employer receives a deeply disturbing phone call explaining they will not be coming in. The mage is aware of all of this through a maintained Correspondence scrying and finds it professionally satisfying.

---

### Constant Cravings

Traditions: Nephandi. Spheres: Life ●●●, Mind ●●● (or Mind ●●●● alone). SF: 2. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Life. Tags: [Control], [Damage], [Lasting], [Mental], [Corrupt].

Addiction is a weapon. The Nephandi have understood this for longer than modern pharmacology existed, and they have spent millennia refining the magical implementation. Constant Cravings implants a specific craving into a target's body and mind simultaneously — Life ●●● binds the working into the biological structures (neurochemistry, endocrine response, metabolic pattern) while Mind ●●● encodes the psychological dimension (the intrusive thought, the obsessive focus, the collapse of other sources of pleasure in the face of the single consuming need). The caster determines what the target craves. It can be a substance (a drug, a specific food, alcohol), a behavior (a compulsive act, a relationship dynamic, an activity), or a person (the mage themselves, an associate, anyone the mage has reason to want the target obsessively attached to). The craving, once installed, does not feel external. It feels like want — genuine, personal, urgent want.

The Duration is determined by hits on the casting roll, following the standard Duration chart: 3 hits sustains the working for a day; 4 hits for a week; 5 hits for a month; 6 hits for a year. For the entire Duration, the target experiences the craving as a continuous pressure. When they satisfy it, the pressure lifts briefly and then reasserts within hours. When they refuse it, the pressure builds — and at the mage's option (using the "Optional Dividing Successes Rule"), the pressure becomes physically painful: each minute of refusal inflicts one Health Level of Bashing damage, for as many minutes as additional hits were invested. An investment of 3 additional hits on top of the Duration roll means the craving inflicts pain for 3 consecutive minutes of abstinence — then resets when the need is met, then returns. A target in this condition has a very limited set of available choices.

The Mind ●●●● alone variant omits the biological anchoring but increases the psychological intensity. Without the Life component, the craving has no physical dimension — it is purely mental, entirely of the imagination — but this makes it in some ways more complete: the target craves something that may not be physically available at all, turning the working into an instrument of sustained, unrelievable frustration. A Mind ●●●● craving for something impossible (a dead person, a time in the past, an irreversible experience) traps the target in permanent, untreatable longing.

Breaking the working requires either its Duration to expire or an active magical intervention: Mind ●●●● to directly excise the enchantment from the target's consciousness, Life ●●●● to purge the biological anchoring, or both if the full combined working is present. A target who simply identifies that they have been enchanted and attempts willpower alone must roll Resolve + Composure against Difficulty 4 each day, accumulating 10 successes to break the working — an effort that itself requires sustained exposure to the craving's pressure throughout the attempt. Most targets never achieve it.

SF: 2 (rote). Casting pool: Resolve + Life.

Example — 3 hits (one day): A target involved in a negotiation is given a craving for a specific substance before the meeting. The craving is moderate but persistent, degrading their concentration throughout the session and making them slightly more susceptible to concessions. The mage is on the opposite side of the table and the Effect is perfectly coincidental — the target appears distracted, which is all the Effect produces in its limited-hit form.

Example — 5 hits (one month): A target who witnessed something they shouldn't has a craving for alcohol installed. The craving is intense enough that the target, who had been sober for three years, begins drinking heavily within a week. Their testimony, when the time comes, is not credible. The mage considers the month-duration working cheaper than the alternatives.

Example — 7 hits (one year, with 4 additional hits of pain-escalation): A mage being groomed for the Fallen is given a craving for a specific person — the Nephandus who is cultivating them. The craving makes separation from that person genuinely painful in a physical sense, and sustained separation produces Bashing damage. Over the year of its Duration, the craving effectively traps the target in proximity to their manipulator. By the time the Duration expires, the behavioral dependency has been independently reinforced enough that the magical working is no longer necessary. This is considered the elegant application of the rote.

Coincidental when the craving is for something the target could plausibly desire independently. Vulgar if the effect is contested in the moment by a target who perceives the intrusion.


---

### Sekpoli — Breath of Life

Traditions: Bata'a (Vodou healing invocation), Euthanatos (Prana-restoration), Dreamspeakers (Life-breath calling). Spheres: Life 3, Prime 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Life. Tags: [Healing], [Boost], [Lasting].

Sekpoli is the Vodou theological concept of the breath of life — the divine vitality that Mawu-Lisa, the Twin Parents and Breath of Life, breathes into each created being. The working that bears its name is a healing invocation in the tradition of the Bata'a and other African diaspora magical traditions, used by healer-mages when either they or their patients are critically depleted of life force, Quintessence, or both. It does not simply repair physical injury — it replenishes the underlying vitality that makes repair possible, addressing the energetic substrate (Prime 2) of the living pattern (Life 3) simultaneously.

The working produces two simultaneous effects: It heals biological damage according to the Life 3 component (restoring health levels as per standard healing workings — Bashing heals fully, Lethal requires more hits, Aggravated requires extended ritual), and it restores Quintessence to the target at a rate of 1 Quintessence per 2 hits on the casting roll. Unlike standard Life healing, the Sekpoli working specifically addresses targets in states of extreme depletion — mages who have burned through their Quintessence reserves, practitioners who have sustained Paradox wounds that have burned into their Pattern, or patients who are in shock states that ordinary Life magic heals mechanically but leaves energetically hollow. The Prime 2 component ensures that the healed pattern is energetically sustained, not merely structurally repaired.

The working carries cultural and spiritual weight. In Bata'a practice, it is an invocation to Mawu-Lisa directly — a request rather than a command, framed as prayer and conducted with the specific ritual materials associated with the Twin Parents (white offerings, eggs, prayers of gratitude, invocations of the divine breath). A Bata'a healer who performs this working disrespectfully or for trivial purposes risks the Twin Parents withdrawing their favorable Resonance from the practitioner's future workings. Other traditions accomplish the same mechanical effect through different paradigmatic framings: Dreamspeakers call upon ancestral breath-spirits; Euthanatos practitioners invoke the Prana-flow of the cosmic pattern; Verbena call it "kindling the fire" and place emphasis on the living spark rather than the divine breath. All achieve the same mechanical result, but the spiritual context differs substantially.

Non-mage targets can receive this working and experience the Quintessence restoration as a profound sense of physical renewed energy — patients in critical condition who receive the working describe it as returning from a very long distance. The Life 3 component heals the body; the Prime 2 component restores the will to use that body. Sleeper recipients do not gain the Quintessence (they have no Avatar to channel it through) but they experience the vitality of the energetic restoration in biological terms — recovery speeds, inflammation subsides, and pain reduces substantially beyond what the Life 3 healing alone would produce.

Practitioners who have studied Mawu-Lisa's nature directly (through Spirit workings or established pact) can extend the working's efficiency: the Twin Parents' blessing reduces the Quintessence cost of all subsequent Life and Prime workings performed that day by 1, as Their presence lingers in the healed pattern. This benefit requires genuine spiritual relationship with Mawu-Lisa rather than mere invocation of the name, and cannot be acquired by practitioners operating outside the African diaspora traditions without years of careful study and genuine relationship-building with the entity.

Examples: With 4 hits — the mage heals 2 Lethal health levels on a critically injured ally and restores 2 of the ally's spent Quintessence, simultaneously addressing the injury and the energetic depletion that made further healing difficult. With 7 hits — a mage who has burned through their entire Quintessence reserve to prevent a catastrophic Paradox outcome is restored to operational capacity: 3 Aggravated health levels healed over the extended ritual, and 3 Quintessence returned to their pattern. With 10 hits in an extended overnight ritual — a healer tends a community of injured practitioners after a major conflict, moving from person to person as the working sustains itself through night-long ceremony: each recipient receives proportional healing and Quintessence restoration, and the healer's Resonance with Mawu-Lisa remains elevated for the following week.

Coincidental when framed as prayer, herbal treatment, or energy work within a private ritual context. Vulgar when the healing is dramatic, immediate, and witnessed by Sleepers who see impossible physical restoration occurring before their eyes.

---

### Song of Returning Bones

Traditions: Bata'a (La Huesera's method), Dreamspeakers (Bone-calling rite), Verbena (Ancient British bone-raising). Spheres: Life 5, Prime 3, Spirit 3. SF: 4. Difficulty: 5 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Life. Tags: [Lasting], [Healing], [Summon], [Umbral].

Named for the working attributed to La Huesera — the Bone Woman, a powerful Umbral entity who collects the remains of forgotten beings and sings them back to life through weeks or months of dedicated spiritual labor — this is among the most demanding and ethically fraught workings in any healer's repertoire. It accomplishes genuine biological resurrection: the restoration of life to a body that has died, through the simultaneous engagement of Life 5 (complete biological reconstruction of the living pattern), Prime 3 (Quintessential reinvestment in the Pattern to sustain life), and Spirit 3 (recovery and re-integration of the departed soul or consciousness from the Spirit realm into the restored biological pattern).

La Huesera herself does not merely restore what was — she transforms what is restored. A wolf that died starving returns larger, healthier, a more capable hunter. A murdered woman returns with healed trauma, gleaming health, and often a life completely different from the one she left. The working does not simply turn back death; it seizes the moment of death as an opportunity and restructures the pattern around it, healing not just the biological failure but the circumstances that led to it. A practitioner who performs this working without understanding this transformative component will find that what they resurrect is not quite what they expected, and cannot be — the working burns away the conditions of death in the process of defeating it.

The working is extended ritual requiring between several hours and several days depending on the state of the remains and the time since death. The mage must have physical contact with the remains throughout. Fresh remains within hours of death require a single extended ritual (minimum 15 hits over 2-4 hours). Remains days or weeks old require accumulated hits over multiple sessions (minimum 25 hits). Remains months or years old are technically possible (La Huesera works on century-old bones) but require accumulated hits of 40 or more and sustained ritual lasting days. Remains that have been destroyed — cremated, dispersed, decomposed past recoverable tissue — cannot be worked upon at all, as Life 5 restores the pattern of what exists rather than reconstructing a pattern from nothing.

The spiritual component is the most uncertain: Spirit 3 allows the mage to reach into the Low Umbra and the Underworld to find the departed soul, but souls that have moved beyond the Low Umbra — those who have passed into deeper realms or been consumed by Wraith-like processes — may be impossible to recover. The soul must also be willing; a soul that has found peace, purpose, or resolution in its post-death existence cannot be compelled to return. A soul trapped in haunting existence, anchored to the world by unfinished business, is paradoxically easier to restore — it is already present in the near-Umbra, already seeking resolution, and the biological restoration gives it that resolution in the most direct possible form.

What returns is genuinely alive and genuinely themselves — their memories, personality, and identity intact. What changes is the body: restored from the state of death's trauma, it typically emerges healthier and more vital than at the point of death. The restoration burns away the specific biological vulnerabilities that the death exploited. A mage who died from a degenerative disease returns without that disease. Someone who died from violence returns with their wounds completely healed. Something about the pattern's passage through death and return cleanses certain damage from the biological record in ways that ordinary Life 5 healing cannot.

Examples: With 15 hits in a 3-hour ritual, a mage recently killed in combat is restored to life with all wounds healed. Their memories are intact through the moment of death. They are disoriented, exhausted, and profoundly affected by the experience, but alive. With 25 hits over two separate extended sessions spanning a week, the remains of a murder victim dead for six days are restored. The restored person does not remember their death — the working erases the trauma of dying from their experiential record. They return with a clarity of purpose about their unfinished life that was not present before. With 40 hits over four days of sustained ritual on behalf of a practitioner who died years prior, the working recovers a soul that has been present in the Low Umbra, refused passage by its own will. The body reconstructed from preserved remains; the soul, offered a restored biological vessel, accepts the return. What comes back carries deep knowledge of the Low Umbra that living practitioners do not possess.

Vulgar in all circumstances. The resurrection of the dead is among the most profound violations of the Consensus. This working generates significant Paradox regardless of preparation, and is typically only attempted in private by practitioners who have the Quintessence reserves and Paradox management capability to survive the attempt.


## Matter — Rotes of Substance and Form

Matter magic governs non-living physical substance — metals, stone, wood, glass, chemicals, and manufactured materials. It can analyze, reshape, transmute, create, and destroy inanimate material. Matter practitioners are often the most practical mages in the field: they can fix engines, open locks, dissolve barriers, and forge weapons without needing to explain anything to anyone.

### Analyze Matter

Traditions: Sons of Ether, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Matter 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Tags: [Sensory], [Subtle].

The mage reads the molecular composition of any substance they touch or closely examine, perceiving its exact chemical makeup, atomic structure, stress fractures, age, origin, and any magical alterations. Forgeries are immediately apparent — the mage knows that the "antique" was manufactured last decade, that the "gold" is plated brass, that the lock has been picked before by the wear on the tumblers. Hidden compartments, structural weaknesses in load-bearing elements, and the presence of toxins in food or drink are all readable at a touch. With 4 or more hits, the mage can identify the manufacturing source of any mass-produced item with enough specificity to narrow the factory or production run.

Coincidental in all circumstances.

### Shaping

Traditions: Sons of Ether, Order of Hermes, Virtual Adepts. Spheres: Matter 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Transform], [Craft].

The mage reshapes non-living matter without heat, tools, or any physical process, forming it into any desired configuration through pure magical will. Metal flows and sets like clay; stone carves itself; wood bends and joins. The mage can work at the pace of skilled craftsmanship without any physical effort. Complex mechanisms (clockwork, firearms, vehicles) require extended rolls; each hit on a single casting represents roughly one hour of skilled work. The final product is equivalent in quality to expert craftsmanship and is permanent. The Shaping cannot change the material — steel stays steel, wood stays wood — only the form. For transmutation between materials, see Transmute Matter below.

Coincidental when the shaping is small-scale and unobserved. Vulgar when material visibly reshapes itself in front of Sleepers.

### Transmute Matter

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Euthanatos. Spheres: Matter 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Transform], [Lasting].

The mage converts one material into another of similar structural complexity — iron into steel, lead into silver, copper into gold, wood into stone, plastic into ceramic. The conversion is permanent with 3 or more hits. Each hit affects approximately one kilogram of material; affecting a full suit of armor requires at least 5 hits. Cross-category transmutation (converting metal into an organic material, or synthetic into natural) requires Life 2 as well, as the mage must also restructure the pattern at a biological level. The legendary Hermetic goal of transmuting lead to gold is real and fully functional; Technocratic economists have noted with concern that gold from magical transmutation is indistinguishable from mined gold.

Coincidental when the transformation is hidden or gradual. Vulgar when visible material transforms in front of Sleepers.

### Forge Weapon

Traditions: Akashic Brotherhood, Order of Hermes, Verbena. Spheres: Matter 3, Prime 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Matter. Tags: [Enchant], [Combat], [Lasting].

The mage imbues a hand-crafted weapon — one that the mage or a trusted ally has physically forged or made — with preternatural sharpness, structural integrity, and supernatural striking power. The weapon's damage becomes Aggravated against supernatural creatures (vampires, spirits, Paradox manifestations, other mages). It never dulls, chips, or breaks under normal use; only magical assault or deliberate magical unmaking can damage it. The mage adds (Arete/Enlightenment) bonus dice on all attack rolls made with this specific weapon — the weapon has been keyed to the mage's pattern. The enchantment is permanent. A Forged Weapon is visually distinctive to those with Prime 1+ perception: it glows faintly with Quintessence.

Coincidental in ritual conditions with appropriate crafting tools and time. Vulgar when performed rapidly without proper framing.

### Acid Bath

Traditions: Sons of Ether, Euthanatos. Spheres: Matter 3, Forces 1. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Matter. Tags: [Damage], [Dissolve], [Targeted].

The mage converts ambient matter into a high-concentration corrosive agent, directing it against a target as a spray or pool. Against objects: (hits + 2) dice of damage, ignoring non-magical Soak — the material is chemically dissolved regardless of hardness. Against living targets: (hits + 2) dice of Lethal (Life patterns resist full dissolution but are still damaged). Armor struck by the acid permanently loses 1 point of Soak per 3 damage dealt; magical armor resists this degradation. The acid evaporates naturally within one scene, leaving only the damage it has already caused. This rote requires either a prepared acid focus or sufficient ambient matter to convert (standing in a room, near a vehicle, etc.).

Coincidental when the mage can frame a plausible acid source. Vulgar when acid manifests from thin air.

### Fortress

Traditions: Sons of Ether, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Matter 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Protective], [Barrier], [Environmental], [Lasting].

The mage reinforces the structural integrity of an existing building, vehicle, or large object, increasing its resistance to damage and penetration. The structure gains (hits × 2) additional Soak against all physical attacks; windows become as strong as quarter-inch steel plate; walls absorb explosive force without fracturing. The reinforcement is invisible from the outside — the structure looks unchanged. Duration: one scene per hit; extended with additional Quintessence (1 per additional scene). With 5 hits and permanent casting conditions (a prepared ritual space), the effect can be made persistent for up to a month before requiring renewal.

Coincidental in all circumstances — strengthened buildings show no external change.


### Synthesizing Materials

Traditions: Sons of Ether, Order of Hermes. Conventions: Progenitors, Syndicate. Spheres: Matter 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Transform], [Lasting], [Craft].

The mage converts one material into another of greater complexity or entirely different composition — not the simple elemental shift of Transmute Matter (lead to silver, wood to stone) but the synthesis of genuinely complex molecular structures. Matter 3 allows the synthesis of plastics, alloys, pharmaceutical-grade organic compounds, and industrial materials; Matter 4 allows exotic materials like superconductors, meta-materials, and substances with unusual physical properties; Matter 5 allows substances that violate conventional chemistry — truly impossible materials with paradigm-defying characteristics.

Synthesis differs from transmutation in that the mage is not merely changing what exists but composing a new substance with specific desired properties. A Son of Ether synthesizing an improved alloy is working as an extraordinary chemist-engineer, producing material with properties their mundane colleagues cannot match. Each hit on the casting adds to the quality and quantity of synthesized material: 1 hit produces a small sample of mediocre quality; 3 hits produces a significant quantity of high-quality material; 5 hits produces exceptional quantities of a flawless substance. Biologically active compounds (drugs, poisons, pheromones) require adding Life 2 to the casting, as the synthesis must account for how the substance interacts with biological systems. The Syndicate uses synthesis magic to produce controlled substances and materials that cannot be sourced through mundane channels.

Examples: With 3 hits (Matter 3), the mage synthesizes a pharmaceutical compound of medical quality — enough to treat several patients. With 5 hits using Matter 4, the mage synthesizes a superconducting material that functions at room temperature, a substance that doesn't exist in conventional science. With 7 hits including Life 2, the mage synthesizes a complex biological toxin with specific physiological targeting — effective against a narrow range of biological patterns.

Coincidental in a laboratory setting with appropriate equipment. Vulgar when complex materials materialize from thin air.

### Mechanical Wings and Flight Devices

Traditions: Sons of Ether, Virtual Adepts. Conventions: Iteration X. Spheres: Matter 2, Forces 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Matter. Tags: [Craft], [Transport], [Lasting].

The mage constructs or activates a mechanical flight device — a jetpack, an ornithopter, a gravity-plate, an aetherial lift harness, or any other physical device that achieves flight through technological rather than biological means. Matter 2 handles the device's material properties (the structural integrity, the mechanical systems, the energy conversion); Forces 2 provides the lift and thrust. If the power source is conventional (jet fuel, electric motors, compressed gas), no Prime is required. If the device runs on aetherial energy, chi batteries, Tass-charged capacitors, or other unusual power sources, Prime 2 is added.

The distinction between this and chi flight or levitation is paradigmatic: this rote creates a physical artifact rather than applying magic directly to the mage's body or to gravity. The device exists independently — it can be handed to someone else, stored when not in use, damaged and repaired. Sons of Ether are the preeminent practitioners of this technique, producing working ornithopters, aetherial dirigibles, and gravity-wave harnesses that function consistently within their paradigm. The technology is "real" in the sense that its mechanical principles are internally consistent, even when those principles don't match conventional physics. Duration is not a relevant concept — the device works as long as its mechanical and power systems are functional. Replenishing an unusual power source requires Prime 2; mechanical damage requires Matter 2 to repair.

Examples: A Son of Ether constructs an ornithopter flight harness over a scene of work (3 hits); it functions for weeks before the aetherial-compressed air reservoir needs recharging. An Iteration X operative activates a gravity-plate backpack (already prepared, requiring only 1 hit to power on) and achieves lift. A Virtual Adept builds a quadrotor drone with an unusual range extension.

Coincidental when the device uses conventional-seeming technology (it looks like a jetpack). Vulgar when the device's propulsion is clearly impossible by mundane physics.

### Altering Fundamental Properties

Traditions: Sons of Ether, Virtual Adepts, Order of Hermes. Conventions: Progenitors. Spheres: Matter 4, Prime 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Matter. Tags: [Transform], [Lasting].

The mage alters the fundamental physical properties of an object or substance at the Pattern level — not just its shape or composition (which Matter 2-3 handles) but its intrinsic physical characteristics: magnetic polarity, electrical conductivity, density, the rate at which it radiates heat, whether it can pass through other matter, its optical properties, or its chemical bonding behavior. Prime 4 is required because these are properties embedded in the Pattern itself, not surface characteristics that a simple transmutation can address.

Each category of property change requires separate hits: 1-2 hits for a minor property adjustment (making a metal non-conductive; reversing a material's magnetic polarity); 3-4 hits for a significant change (making solid matter partially permeable to physical objects, altering density by 50%, creating a material that is selectively transparent to specific frequencies); 5+ hits for a radical change (making matter completely intangible to physical force while remaining visible, altering a substance's chemical bonding so it can form molecules that don't exist in nature). The changes made with this technique are permanent if 5+ hits are achieved; temporary otherwise (one scene per hit). Undoing a permanent change requires matching the original hit count with another casting.

Examples: A Son of Ether alters the electrical conductivity of a specific material to create a room-temperature superconductor (3 hits; Matter 4 + Prime 4). An Order of Hermes alchemist permanently alters a lead bar's density and atomic structure, transforming it into a material that mimics gold at every level of physical analysis (4 hits — the legendary goal of alchemy). A Virtual Adept creates a material that is physically solid to living beings but permits free passage of electromagnetic radiation (5 hits).

Vulgar in all circumstances — matter acting against its fundamental nature.

### Penny Dreadful's Bright New Penny

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Sons of Ether, Verbena (antiques variant). Spheres: Matter 4, Prime 2, Time 2. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Matter. Tags: [Craft], [Lasting], [Transform], [Reaching].

The notorious itinerant mage called Penny Dreadful — whose full name and Tradition affiliation remain disputed across the records that mention her — developed this working in the middle period of her career as a solution to a practical problem: she had no money, frequently, and an eye for objects whose original value far exceeded their present condition. The initial form of the working — using Time to look backward to an object's original state, Matter to restore it, and Prime to fuel the reconstruction — was genuinely vulgar, because objects do not simply return themselves to pristine condition. Penny's refinement was to reframe the Effect entirely: rather than making trash become treasure, she learned to find treasure hidden in trash. The coincidental version doesn't restore an object — it finds an object that was already in fine condition, already original, already valuable, hidden inside or beneath the worn shell. Reality accepts this because finding things is mundane; making things is suspicious.

Matter 4 is required for full restoration from severely degraded conditions — Matter 3 handles repair of intact but damaged objects, but Matter 4 can reconstruct from near-nothing. Time 2 is essential to read the object's prior state accurately enough to reconstruct it faithfully, particularly for complex objects (clockwork mechanisms, illuminated manuscripts, precision instruments) where the original configuration matters as much as the materials. Prime 2 supplies the Quintessential energy needed to instantiate the new Pattern where the degraded one existed. The combined working is powerful enough to restore virtually any object to any prior state within its history — the mage cannot make the object newer than it ever was, but she can return it to the moment of its creation.

The rote has become a fixture in the wider magical community because its applications extend far beyond Penny Dreadful's original mercenary purpose. Verbena use it to restore ancient ritual objects whose texts have faded or whose physical forms have decayed beyond normal recovery. Sons of Ether use it to reconstruct experimental devices whose notes were lost. Order of Hermes archivists use it to restore damaged illuminated manuscripts and grimoires. The Syndicate has theoretical interest in a financial variant — restoring declined assets to peak value — that remains practically challenging because financial value is not purely material and Time + Matter alone cannot reconstruct it. Paradox for vulgar applications is typically material-level: objects that shouldn't be intact simply aren't, or are restored in a form the mage didn't intend.

Examples: With 3 hits, the mage restores a battered antique to its original pristine condition — the rust disappears, the broken mechanism reforms, the faded inscription becomes legible. With 5 hits, the mage reconstructs an object that has been destroyed, working from physical residue (ash, fragments, chemical residue) to recover the complete original. With 7 hits, the mage restores a genuinely ancient text that has decayed to fragments, including recovering the content of pages that no longer physically exist, reconstructing them from the manuscript's own temporal memory.

Coincidental when framed as finding a hidden antique or discovering an intact original beneath surface deterioration. Vulgar when the object is visibly observed to restore itself in front of witnesses.

### Grasping Terrors

Traditions: Nephandi, K'llashaa. Spheres: Matter ●●●; optionally with Prime ●● or Spirit ●●●. SF: 2 (Matter ●●● alone), with Prime or Spirit adds complexity but not rote SF (combined remains SF 2 as rote). Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Matter. Tags: [Damage], [Control], [Aggravated], [Controversial].

From nowhere, hands. Or claws. Or tentacles. Or something for which there is no category. They erupt from walls, from floors, from the surface of standing water, from compressed shadow, from any solid or semi-solid surface in the mage's environment, and they seize whatever they can reach. This is matter Magic operating at its most viscerally unpleasant: the material world itself is caused to grasp, hold, and tear.

The working uses Matter ●●● to animate and extrude matter from any available surface within range into grasping appendages. The mage does not summon limbs from another dimension — the limbs are made of the wall, or the floor, or the concrete, or whatever material is at hand, temporarily reshaped into forms that grip and hold. The texture, composition, and appearance of the appendages reflect the material from which they emerge: stone hands look like stone hands; shadow-matter produces something more difficult to describe; water extrudes tentacular shapes that clutch with surprising force. Whatever their material composition, they grip with Strength equal to one dot per hit scored on the casting roll, and they hold unless overcome by a Strength + Athletics roll against Difficulty equal to the hits scored.

Physical damage from the initial eruption and seizure is Bashing, based on hits scored. A target seized by the Grasping Terrors cannot move until they break free; an immobile target is substantially easier to affect with subsequent workings, which is often the actual purpose of this spell — not the damage it inflicts, but the control state it creates. A Fallen practitioner who has deployed Grasping Terrors typically follows immediately with whatever they actually intended to do while their target was held.

The Prime ●● optional variant makes the damage Aggravated — the appendages strike at Pattern level rather than merely physical level. This dramatically escalates the Paradox cost because Aggravated damage via Matter is unambiguously vulgar and visually spectacular. The Spirit ●●● variant channels the assault through Umbral assailants who exist simultaneously in the material world and the Penumbra, allowing the working to affect both physically manifested targets and Umbral beings with equal effectiveness. The Spirit variant also makes the grasping forces significantly more resilient: they cannot be simply broken free from by raw physical strength because their Umbral component reinforces them against purely physical escape.

The sheer visibility of this working — matter extruding improbably from surfaces to grab people is inherently reality-violating — means it is always vulgar with witnesses. Paradox accumulates significantly. Its shock value, however, is among the highest in any working in this section: even mages who have seen combat magic are genuinely disturbed by matter itself grabbing at them. The Shock mechanics apply to non-Awakened witnesses at Difficulty 3; Awakened witnesses may still take Shock at Difficulty 2 if the specific sensory quality of this working catches them unprepared.

SF: 2 (rote). Casting pool: Might + Matter.

Example — 2 hits, Matter ●●●: Stone hands extrude from a wall and seize a target's arm with Strength 2. The target is not held completely (insufficient strength) but takes 2 Bashing damage from the impact and is briefly unbalanced, losing their Defense dice on the Nephandus's next action. The mage uses that action to cast Art of Seduction without the usual difficulty of maintaining eye contact while dodging.

Example — 4 hits, Matter ●●●: The floor of a corridor erupts in multiple grasping shapes, seizing a fleeing target with Strength 4. The target is held completely. They cannot move, use weapons effectively, or dodge. The mage approaches at a walk. Every witness in the corridor is taking Shock checks.

Example — 5 hits, Matter ●●● + Prime ●●: The appendages emerge from darkness rather than any specific material surface and inflict Aggravated damage on contact, bypassing armor entirely and affecting the target at Pattern level. Against a mage with Mage Armor active, the Aggravated damage still partially bypasses it. Against a supernatural being whose specific defense relies on physical toughness, this is effectively the correct tool for the job. The Paradox cost for this variant is severe and is only deployed when the mage has determined that conventional engagement will fail.

Vulgar in all circumstances. Always generates Paradox with witnesses.


---

## Mind — Rotes of Thought and Consciousness

Mind magic touches the most private territory in existence: the internal world of another thinking being. Even the most benign Mind workings involve a level of intrusion that most people would find deeply troubling if they knew it was happening. For this reason, Mind practitioners develop strong ethical frameworks — or they abandon them entirely, which produces some of the most dangerous mages in the World of Darkness.

### Mental Shield

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Akashic Brotherhood. Spheres: Mind 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Tags: [Protective], [Subtle], [Lasting].

The mage erects a robust barrier around their own mind, resisting intrusion, reading, and influence. Any attempt to read, scan, influence, or control the mage's mind must first overcome this shield: the attacker must achieve more hits than the mage did on this casting roll, or the attempt fails entirely. With 3 or more hits on the shield roll, the mage also immediately becomes aware of any attempt to affect their mind (they sense the "push" against the barrier), and they can identify the approximate location of the mental attacker. The shield lasts one day per hit and is completely invisible and undetectable without Prime 2+ examination.

Coincidental in all circumstances.

### Read Surface Thoughts

Traditions: Akashic Brotherhood, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Mind 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Tags: [Sensory], [Subtle], [Mental].

The mage picks up on a target's active surface thoughts — whatever they are consciously focusing on in this moment. This is not deep telepathy; the mage receives fragments of current thinking (the target's anxiety about a meeting, a mental image of their home, the name they are rehearsing). No deeper memories, hidden intentions, or subconscious content is accessible. The target rarely notices unless they are specifically shielded (Mental Shield above) or have very high Willpower (8+, who feel a faint prickling sensation). Each hit increases the clarity and completeness of the surface reading; at 4 hits, the mage receives a fairly complete picture of the target's immediate conscious preoccupations.

Coincidental in all circumstances — the mage shows no external signs.

### Telepathy

Traditions: Akashic Brotherhood, Dreamspeakers, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Mind 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Communication], [Mental].

The mage establishes a two-way mental link with a target, allowing silent communication of thoughts, images, and emotional impressions across any distance within range. Willing communication with a willing target requires no roll — the link simply opens. With an unwilling target: the target resists with Willpower (Difficulty 2); each of the mage's hits above the target's resistance hits deepens the link. Once established (even against resistance), the link is two-way — the mage can hear the target's surface thoughts as well as send their own. Range: (Sphere rating × 100) meters without Correspondence; unlimited with Correspondence 2+. Duration: one scene.

Coincidental in all circumstances when established silently.

### Psychic Blast

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Euthanatos, Akashic Brotherhood. Spheres: Mind 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Damage], [Ranged], [Mental].

A focused lance of raw psychic energy strikes the target's mind directly, bypassing all physical armor. Damage: (hits + 1) dice of Bashing damage that cannot be Soaked by any physical protection. Mental defenses (Mental Shield, Willpower) can reduce the damage: for each point of Willpower the target burns (permanently expending it), they reduce the damage by 1 die. The attack is felt as a sudden, searing migraine — a white-hot pain that momentarily blanks the target's vision. Targets who suffer 3 or more levels of damage from a single Psychic Blast are Stunned for one round (they lose their next action). This rote requires line of sight and a target the mage can perceive; it cannot be used against someone the mage cannot detect.

Coincidental when the target collapses with no visible cause (it looks like a medical episode). Vulgar when psychic energy is visibly crackling between mage and target.

### Dream Walking

Traditions: Dreamspeakers, Cult of Ecstasy. Spheres: Mind 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Mental], [Transport], [Subtle].

The mage enters the dream of a sleeping target, moving through their dreamscape as a fully conscious actor in a world the target's unconscious has built. The mage perceives the dream accurately and can interact with it — speaking to dream-figures, examining symbolic environments, witnessing replayed memories filtered through the target's emotional lens. Information extracted from dreams is symbolically accurate: the target's deep fears, desires, and formative memories play out as narrative, requiring interpretation to extract factual content. With 3 or more hits, the mage can alter the dream's contents, planting suggestions, adjusting the emotional tone, or inserting false memories that the target will retain after waking (with the caveat that suggested memories feel slightly "off" and can be questioned by the target under stress).

Coincidental in all circumstances — the mage's body is asleep and shows nothing unusual.

### Mass Suggestion

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Cult of Ecstasy, Technocracy — New World Order. Spheres: Mind 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Control], [Area], [Mental].

The mage broadcasts a strong emotional impression or simple directional command to everyone within (hits × 5) meters. The suggestion must be simple and primal: "leave," "stop," "danger here," "trust this person," "nothing to see." Characters with Willpower 6 or less who are not already focused on a competing goal respond without a roll — they feel a sudden, apparently internal impulse and act on it. Characters with Willpower 7-9 may resist with a Willpower roll (Difficulty 1). Characters with Willpower 10 are immune. The mage does not target individuals; the broadcast affects everyone in range equally, including allies.

Coincidental when the suggestion produces ordinary-looking behavior. Vulgar when the affected crowd visibly moves in unison with no obvious cause.

### Memory Edit

Traditions: Euthanatos, New World Order, Akashic Brotherhood. Spheres: Mind 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Tags: [Control], [Mental], [Lasting], [Controversial].

The mage reaches into a target's long-term memory storage and rewrites it — removing events, altering details, implanting entirely false memories, or suppressing emotional responses associated with real memories. The editing is surgical: the mage can excise one specific event without disturbing surrounding memories, change a single participant in a memory to someone else, or construct an elaborate false history. False memories created this way are neurologically indistinguishable from real ones without Mind 4+ examination. The target will confabulate explanations for any gaps the editing creates. The effect is permanent; the target's natural mind will not shake it. Under extreme stress, the target may experience brief flashes of the suppressed truth (Storyguide discretion), but these feel like dreams or imagination.

Coincidental in all circumstances when the edited memory leaves the target behaving normally. Vulgar when the editing causes immediate visible behavioral change in front of Sleepers.

---

### The Old Mind-Push

Traditions: Akashayana, Virtual Adepts. Conventions: NWO. Spheres: Mind 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Mental], [Subtle], [Interaction].

The quintessential operative's tool — a precisely focused pulse of mental redirection that makes the mage unremarkable, forgettable, and simply not interesting to casual observers. At Mind 2, the technique discourages attention without compelling behavior: security guards find their gaze sliding past the mage; receptionists begin asking about someone else's badge; shopkeepers look up and look away. The effect is not invisibility and it is not compulsion — the target retains free will and may resist if they have specific reason to suspect something is wrong (Willpower roll at difficulty = mage's hits). Mind 3 actively steers thoughts away from the mage — not just discouraging attention but redirecting active investigation. Mind 4 overrides even determined professionals actively looking for exactly the mage's description. The NWO trains this as a standard operative technique; Akashayana practitioners frame it as projecting the chi-signature of a background element; Virtual Adepts conceptualize it as signal spoofing in the meat-space attention network. Particularly effective combined with Entropy 2 (which arranges circumstantial distractions) and the All-Access Pass compound rote.

Coincidental in all circumstances — mental influence is invisible and the results appear as ordinary human inattention.

### Predator's Eye

Traditions: Akashayana, Euthanatos. Spheres: Mind 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Mental], [Interaction].

The mirror of the Calming Glance — the mage projects focused emotional pressure that drives a target toward an extreme psychological state calibrated to the mage's tactical advantage. Fear makes opponents hesitate and break; rage makes them overcommit and leave openings; grief distracts and slows; lust creates vulnerability to social manipulation. The mage's hits set the difficulty of the target's Willpower roll to resist; if they fail, the imposed emotional state persists for one turn per hit and manifests mechanically as a -1 die penalty to all actions, with specific behavioral tendencies aligned to the emotion. The target experiences the emotion as genuinely arising from within — this is not perceived as external compulsion unless they have Mind protection or specifically suspect magical influence. The Akashayana use this in pre-combat assessment (Predator's Eye combined with Spotting Flaws provides a comprehensive tactical picture before the first blow is struck); Euthanatos practitioners use the fear variant in assassinations to create the internal landscape of finality they consider spiritually appropriate.

Coincidental in all circumstances — appears as charisma, intimidation, or persuasion.

### Victory in the Mind

Traditions: Akashayana, Euthanatos. Spheres: Mind 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Mental], [Boost], [Subtle].

The mage turns magical attention inward, amplifying combat focus, clearing the distractions of fear and doubt, and inducing the optimal mental state for a specific tactical context. This is the introspective counterpart to the externally-directed Predator's Eye: rather than pushing an emotional state at an opponent, the mage cultivates the ideal internal emotional and cognitive landscape for their own next actions. Each hit reduces the difficulty of the mage's next three combat actions by -1 (maximum reduction -3). Mind 2 achieves this through emotional amplification — fury, fierce calm, battle-joy, or whatever state the mage's tradition identifies as optimal. Mind 3 achieves the same result through direct cognitive optimization rather than emotional enhancement, which is mechanically identical but paradigmatically different. The Akashayana spend years learning to access this state instantly; many apprentices can only reach it through formal meditative ritual lasting ten minutes, while masters can trigger it in the split-second before a threat materializes. Interactions: combined with Battle Fury (Life 3 / Mind 3 / Prime 2), this rote's dice bonus stacks with that Effect's wound-penalty immunity.

Coincidental in all circumstances — the enhancement is entirely internal and produces no outward sign.

### Emotional Surge

Traditions: Cultists of Ecstasy, Akashayana, Marauders. Spheres: Mind 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Mental], [Interaction].

The mage projects a surge of intense specific emotion directly into a target's emotional experience — depression, euphoria, terror, fury, lust, or grief — in a wave powerful enough to temporarily overwhelm the target's own emotional baseline. Each hit reduces the target's temporary Willpower by one. If the mage's total hits exceed the target's current temporary Willpower, the imposed emotion takes over entirely and the target enters an irrational state dominated by that passion for the scene. The reverse application — the Sudden Chill — projects intense tranquility that can abort a fight, calm a panicking crowd, or break a character out of a berserk state. The difficulty of the Effect is modified by context: projecting an emotion that reinforces the target's current state (-1 to -3 difficulty) is far easier than projecting one that conflicts with it (+1 to +3 difficulty). The Cultists of Ecstasy view controlled application of Emotional Surge as a legitimate path to spiritual insight — forcing another to experience genuine joy, genuine grief, or genuine terror in a safe context can break psychological calcification. The technique is ethically fraught and its coincidentality depends entirely on how subtly it is delivered.

Coincidental when delivered subtly through a social encounter. Vulgar when the surge is so extreme that the target's behavior is obviously impossible without external cause.

### Psionic Strike

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Virtual Adepts. Conventions: NWO. Spheres: Mind 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Damage], [Mental].

The mage directs a focused bolt of raw psychic force into a target's consciousness — not an emotion, not a command, but a concussive discharge of mental energy that inflicts direct neurological damage. The attack uses the target's Willpower as difficulty rather than a standard physical defense. Each hit inflicts one die of Bashing damage (armor provides no protection; only Mind-based defenses or supernatural equivalents soak psychic force). Against mages and night-folk, the difficulty increases by +3 due to the strengthened mental architecture that Awakening or supernatural transformation provides. Unlike most Mind Effects, the Psychic Blast is inherently vulgar — it is a direct weaponization of psychic force with no plausible mundane explanation, and it manifests to observers with Spirit 2+ or Mind 1+ as a visible pulse of mental energy. The Psychic Shatter (Life 3 / Mind 3) is the aggravated-damage cousin of this rote, adding biological Pattern disruption to the mental assault — blood vessels rupture, neural pathways tear, and the damage inflicted cannot be soaked by physical means. Even survivors of a Psychic Shatter roll against Things Man Was Not Meant to Know.

Vulgar in all circumstances.

### Forging Memories

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Virtual Adepts. Conventions: NWO. Spheres: Mind 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Tags: [Mental], [Lasting], [Subtle].

The mage reaches into a target's memory structure and alters specific recollections — erasing events, replacing them with false narratives, or amplifying minor incidents into major memories. The simplest applications (Mind 4, one or two hits) erase a specific recent event from accessible memory: "you never saw me," "the last hour did not happen," "you were alone in this room." The classic NWO Men in Black application — the memory flash — falls in this category. More extensive operations (Mind 5, five or more hits) replace entire event-sequences with constructed false memories detailed enough to withstand questioning. Such operations may be deeply traumatic regardless of their content; minds are not designed to have their recordings overwritten, and recipients sometimes experience intrusive "glitches" where the true memory surfaces in dreams or under stress. Difficulties are adjusted by consent: willing targets are difficulty equal to Willpower; unwilling targets add +3; mages and night-folk add +3 or +5. Interactions: Time 4 combined with this rote creates a delayed memory-edit that triggers at a specified future moment.

Coincidental when framed as hypnosis or suggestion. Vulgar when the memory edit is dramatic, visible, or contested by a target who perceives the intrusion.


### Calming Glance

Traditions: Akashayana, Celestial Chorus, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Mind 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Mental], [Interaction], [Subtle].

The mage projects a focused pulse of calm and pacification directly into a target's emotional state — not compulsion, not command, but a genuine shift in the target's internal emotional experience toward de-escalation. Violence begins to feel unappealing; the anger or fear driving the confrontation loses urgency; the cognitive pathways associated with fighting feel less available. The target still has free will and can choose to continue the confrontation, but does so at increased psychological cost. Used well, Calming Glance can de-escalate a situation before it becomes physical without the target ever knowing they've been influenced.

The target resists with Willpower (difficulty = mage's hits); if they fail, their next three actions against the mage suffer a +1 difficulty penalty and they cannot make the first aggressive action in a conflict (if the conflict hasn't started yet). The technique is paradigmatically appropriate to traditions that emphasize restraint: Akashayana who view unnecessary violence as a failure of chi-mastery; Celestial Chorus who extend divine peace as an alternative to conflict; Order of Hermes who consider it base to resort to violence when the mind offers finer instruments. Combat use requires an action; pre-emptive social use is part of a scene of interaction. Multiple targets require Mind 3 and Area effect.

Examples: With 2 hits at the beginning of a confrontation, the mage establishes a threshold of pacification: the opponent must succeed on a Willpower roll before taking violent action. With 4 hits against a group, the mage creates general de-escalation across the entire encounter. With 5 hits deployed before a meeting, the mage establishes that the session will remain nonviolent — anyone who attempts violence does so at +2 difficulty for that action.

Coincidental in all circumstances — appears as charisma, presence, or simply a persuasive manner.

### War Dance

Traditions: Akashayana, Verbena, Dreamspeakers. Spheres: Mind 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Mental], [Boost], [Area].

The mage performs a ritually charged action — a dance, a chant, a battle cry, a war drum, a formal invocation — that projects a surge of martial focus and heightened combat readiness into nearby allies. The performance must be actual and sustained (not a moment's thought, but a genuine ceremonial action); the duration of the preparation determines the depth of the effect. Each hit on the casting grants participating allies +1 die on attack rolls (maximum +3) and reduces their fear-based penalties by the same amount. Duration: one scene or combat.

The Akashayana war dance is a formalized ki-projection ceremony taught within specific lineages; improper or disrespectful performance weakens the effect. The Verbena use bardic traditions, hunting songs, and the old battle-chants of their ancestral warrior cultures; the result is functionally identical but paradigmatically distinct. Dreamspeakers often invoke ancestral warrior spirits as part of the dance, creating a minor Spirit component to the working (though pure Mind 2 achieves the same mechanical result). The mage themselves can participate in the war dance's benefits but must maintain the performance to sustain the effect — if they stop dancing or chanting to engage directly in combat, the Effect immediately begins fading. Maximum 10 participants can be included without additional Sphere investment.

Examples: With 2 hits, the mage grants allies +1 to attack rolls for the combat. With 4 hits, the mage's performance grants +2 to attacks and effectively neutralizes fear-based morale effects for the entire scene. With 5 hits combined with Battle Fury (the mage is both the dancer and the combatant), the effect applies while the mage maintains a running chant during combat — at cost of -1 die to all physical actions while dual-tasking.

Coincidental in all circumstances — a charismatic battle speech or war dance has clear mundane precedent.

### Psychic Dueling

Traditions: Akashayana, Order of Hermes, Virtual Adepts. Spheres: Mind 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Tags: [Mental], [Damage], [Transport].

The mage projects their consciousness into the Astral Plane — the High Umbra, the realm of pure thought and mental reality — and engages an opponent there in direct mind-to-mind combat. This is the ultimate extension of Mind-based conflict, bypassing the physical entirely. Both participants must be capable of Astral Projection (Mind 4 minimum). An unwilling opponent can be drawn into Astral combat only if they are asleep or meditating (their psyche is more accessible in those states); drawing an aware and resistant opponent into Astral combat requires Mind 5. In the Astral, both combatants are represented by their mental self-conception — appearing as they see themselves, wielding constructs of pure thought as weapons and shields.

Astral combat uses Willpower pools instead of physical combat pools; damage inflicts Willpower loss rather than Health Level damage. A combatant reduced to 0 Willpower in the Astral is ejected back to physical consciousness — typically unconscious and disoriented for a scene, potentially suffering a permanent Willpower loss of 1 dot if the defeat was catastrophic. While projecting into the Astral, the physical body is catatonic and cannot defend itself. Damage to the physical body during projection causes the mage's consciousness to snap back immediately. Masters of Psychic Dueling develop Astral "weapon-forms" — mental constructs analogous to weapons, trained as rigorously as physical combat styles — which provide substantial advantages in the Astral environment.

Examples: With 4 hits and Mind 4, the mage establishes an Astral combat arena and draws a sleeping opponent's dream-self into direct conflict; the sleeping opponent participates in the duel as part of their dream, with no awareness that real Willpower is being risked. With 5 hits, the mage enters the Astral and attacks a projecting opponent directly, both mages' physical bodies lying vulnerable while their mental selves trade blows in a landscape of pure thought.

Coincidental when performed during meditation (the mage appears to be simply sitting quietly). Vulgar when the Astral combat is in progress — spirit-aware observers can perceive the struggle.

### The Mind-Altering Punch

Traditions: Akashayana, Euthanatos. Spheres: Mind 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Mental], [Damage], [Interaction].

A combined physical-mental technique that channels a specific emotional impulse through a physical strike — the blow delivers not just kinetic trauma but a focused mental message embedded in the impact: "Stay down," "Run," "You're afraid of me," "You've already lost." The target must succeed on a Willpower roll (difficulty = mage's hits) or the emotional impulse takes hold for three turns, manifesting as a specific behavioral tendency and a +1 difficulty penalty to actions contrary to the impulse. Physical damage is applied normally in addition to the mental effect; the two components are resolved simultaneously.

The emotional content must be chosen before the strike: the mage cannot retroactively decide what message to deliver after landing the blow. Context matters: delivering "Run" to someone whose comrades are all dead and who is actively retreating costs no additional Willpower resistance; delivering "Stay down" to a berserker at full health and adrenaline adds +2 to their resistance difficulty. The Akashayana view this as chi-communication — the chi channeled through the strike carries intention, which the target's own nervous system translates into emotional experience. Some practitioners have developed specific repertoires of strike-messages, refined through years of combat experience.

Examples: With 2 hits, the mage delivers a powerful strike combined with "Fall back" — the target must resist or hesitates for a turn before pressing the attack again. With 4 hits, the mage delivers "You've lost" to a target already injured; the resistance roll is at +1 difficulty (despair reinforcing the message), and failure produces genuine psychological submission for the remainder of the scene. With 5 hits, the mage delivers a multi-layered emotional message that overrides combat psychology entirely, producing non-aggression for the rest of the encounter.

Coincidental in all circumstances — appears as a particularly compelling combat technique.

### Heartbreak Slap — Psychic Shatter

Traditions: Akashayana, Euthanatos. Spheres: Life 3, Mind 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Life. Tags: [Damage], [Aggravated], [Mental].

A combined attack that simultaneously strikes the target's physical and mental patterns through a single directed blow or focused psychic assault. The Mind 3 component delivers a Psychic Blast — focused mental energy that inflicts Bashing damage bypassing physical armor. The Life 3 component adds biological Pattern disruption to the mental assault, converting the damage to Aggravated and causing genuine biological harm: blood vessels rupture, neural pathways tear, cellular breakdown occurs at the sites of impact. The combined attack inflicts damage that cannot be soaked by physical armor or normal Fortitude; only Prime-based shields or equivalent supernatural defenses provide protection.

Each hit inflicts one die of Aggravated damage. Targets who suffer three or more levels from a single application must resist with Willpower (difficulty 3) or suffer a temporary mental break lasting one scene (hallucinations, inability to concentrate, extreme emotional instability). Survivors of a full Heartbreak Slap at five or more hits roll against Things Man Was Not Meant to Know, as the experience of simultaneous physical and mental Pattern disruption is profoundly destabilizing to the psyche. The technique requires the mage to either touch the target (if using the physical strike variant) or have line of sight within Medium range (if using the purely psychic variant). Euthanatos practitioners use this as a terminal option against opponents they have judged beyond rehabilitation; Akashayana masters consider it among the most destructive techniques in their tradition and restrict its teaching to advanced initiates.

Examples: With 2 hits, the target suffers 2 dice of Aggravated damage and must make a difficulty 3 Willpower roll to avoid mental disruption. With 5 hits, the target suffers severe Pattern damage, likely Incapacitating them, and faces a Things Man Was Not Meant to Know roll. A target reduced to 0 Health Levels by this technique experiences not just physical death but simultaneous metaphysical fracturing that leaves even the possibility of ghostly afterexistence unclear.

Vulgar in all circumstances — visible or detectable psychic energy discharge during the attack.

### Stunning Nerve-Pinch

Traditions: Akashayana. Conventions: NWO. Spheres: Mind 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Mental], [Control], [Subtle].

The mage issues a direct command to specific nerve clusters, causing them to shut down temporarily without inflicting physical damage. This is the precision instrument version of the Mind-Altering Punch: instead of an emotional message, the mage transmits a specific neural command to a targeted area of the target's nervous system. The result is a stun or paralysis effect that leaves no lasting physical damage and can be calibrated to affect specific muscle groups (arm paralysis, leg paralysis, loss of fine motor control) or the entire voluntary nervous system (full body paralysis while consciousness is maintained).

The target resists with Willpower (difficulty = mage's hits); failure produces paralysis for one turn per hit in the area the mage designated. Unlike physical nerve strikes, the Mind 3 technique cannot be countered by physical armor or combat training — it bypasses the body entirely and acts directly on the nervous system's control signals. The NWO trains this as a "neural compliance technique" used to subdue targets without visible aggression; in their paradigm, it resembles a proprietary pressure-point application. Akashayana practitioners describe it as "speaking the chi-language of the body's command system."

Examples: With 2 hits, the mage paralyzes a target's arm for two turns — enough to prevent a weapon being raised. With 4 hits, the mage induces full-body paralysis for four turns while the target remains conscious; they can speak and observe but cannot move. With 5 hits, the mage's command overrides the target's autonomous nervous system as well, temporarily suppressing their Willpower-based resistance: subsequent rolls the target makes in the same scene are at -1 difficulty for the mage.

Coincidental when performed with apparent physical contact (pressure-point technique). Vulgar when paralysis is clearly not explicable by any physical mechanism.

### Knocking Sense Into Someone

Traditions: Akashayana, Virtual Adepts. Conventions: NWO. Spheres: Mind 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Tags: [Mental], [Lasting], [Controversial].

A powerful intervention into a target's mental state — either inducing or resolving altered psychological conditions. The mage can use this rote to: induce specific forms of temporary madness (hallucinations, specific phobias, compulsions, disconnection from consensus reality); erase the target's memories of recent events (up to the current scene with 2 hits; up to several days with 5 hits; more extensive retrograde amnesia requires Mind 5); shock someone out of an existing derangement or magical compulsion (same hit threshold as inducing the condition); or restore an otherwise-stable person to rational function after trauma. The technique requires either physical proximity (a touch or strike) or sustained focus within Close range.

Each application is contested against the target's Willpower (mage's hits must exceed the target's resistance hits). Causing madness in a stable person is the most difficult application; restoring sanity to someone already in acute mental distress is somewhat easier. The NWO calls this technique a "cognitive reset" or "attitude adjustment"; Virtual Adepts frame it as rewriting the operating system. Metaphysically insane beings (Marauders, certain fae and vampires) are largely immune even to Masters — their unreality has its own structural integrity. Applications are always permanent unless specifically targeted as temporary.

Examples: With 3 hits, the mage shocks a mentally compromised ally out of a magical compulsion. With 5 hits, the mage erases a Sleeper witness's memory of the past hour — they wake feeling like they blanked out, which is more plausible than clear memory of impossible events. With 5 hits used to induce madness, the target develops a specific hallucination or compulsion calibrated to the mage's intent.

Coincidental when shock treatment or extreme stress can explain the mental change. Vulgar when the mental alteration is obviously impossible to mundane explanation.

### Puppetry — Full Mind Control

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Nephandi. Conventions: NWO. Spheres: Mind 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Tags: [Control], [Mental], [Lasting], [Controversial].

The mage replaces a target's will with their own — commanding thoughts, directing impulses, suppressing personality, and dictating behavior. This is not suggestion or influence (which Mind 2-3 achieves) but actual occupation of the target's decision-making apparatus. The target's body performs the mage's will while their consciousness, if retained, observes helplessly from inside. Mind 4 commands single targets; Mind 5 rewrites them entirely or controls multiple targets simultaneously. The distinction matters: Mind 4 Puppetry issues commands the target fulfills; Mind 5 can replace the target's personality structure itself.

The technique requires the target to be within range (Close to Medium without Correspondence; any distance with Correspondence 3+). Resistance is by Willpower + any Mind protection: difficulty 3 against the mage's hits for an unwilling target; mages and Night-Folk add +3. Total command (the target performs any action, including ones against their core values, and will not remember it afterward) requires 10 hits. At lower thresholds: 2-3 hits = nudge the target toward a natural inclination; 5-7 hits = force specific actions the target would not otherwise take; 10 hits = complete override including memory suppression. The mage experiences the Puppetry as occupying a second perspective — they can receive perceptual information through the target's senses if they choose, which is extremely useful for intelligence operations.

Examples: With 3 hits, the mage nudges a guard toward looking the wrong way at the right moment. With 6 hits, the mage commands a target to perform a specific action they would not naturally do — open a locked door, hand over a document, leave a room. With 10 hits, the NWO operative has the target perform an entire sequence of actions across a scene and wake with no memory of any of it.

Coincidental when framed as social persuasion or hypnosis and the target's behavior remains plausible. Vulgar when the control is obviously absolute and the target behaves like a marionette.

### Instant Madness and Restored Sanity

Traditions: Marauders, Euthanatos, Akashayana, Celestial Chorus. Conventions: NWO. Spheres: Mind 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Tags: [Mental], [Lasting].

At Mind 4, the mage can instantly destroy or restore a target's mental coherence. Instant madness detonates the target's grip on consensus reality — hallucinations bloom, impulse control fails, the target may become violent, catatonic, or entirely disconnected from shared reality for as long as the Effect persists. The madness is not a specific derangement chosen by the mage (that requires more nuanced working) but a wholesale disruption of the target's mental architecture. Instant sanity restoration is the inverse: the mage shock-resets a mentally disrupted character to baseline function, including potentially providing temporary immunity to further mind-affecting effects while the restoration holds.

Both applications require the target to fail a Willpower roll against the mage's hits. Mages and Night-Folk with significant metaphysical protection add +3 to their resistance. Beings whose madness is fundamental rather than imposed — Marauders, certain supernatural creatures whose mental disruption is their nature rather than a condition — are immune even to Masters; their unreality is too thoroughly embedded in their Pattern to be addressed by this technique. Gradual induction of the same conditions through coincidental means (weeks of subtle Mind working, psychological conditioning, environmental manipulation) achieves the same results but is not this rote's domain.

Examples: With 4 hits directed at a lucid enemy mage, the target undergoes an acute psychotic break for the remainder of the scene. With 5 hits used in the restoration direction against a character suffering a temporary derangement, the target recovers and gains a +2 bonus against further mental destabilization for the scene. With 10 hits of instant madness directed at a non-mage target, the effect may become permanent without subsequent treatment.

Vulgar in all circumstances for instant application. Coincidental only when the condition change is gradual and attributable to natural causes.

### Psychic Pain Ward

Traditions: Order of Hermes. Conventions: NWO. Spheres: Mind 3, Prime 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Mind. Tags: [Protective], [Lasting], [Environmental].

The mage embeds a Psychic Blast (Mind 3) within a defined ward structure (Prime 2), creating a mental attack that fires automatically when an unauthorized person enters the warded space. Unlike a physical ward that blocks entry, the Psychic Pain Ward allows entry but punishes it: every unauthorized person who crosses the ward's threshold receives a burst of psychic pain (Bashing damage; number of dice equal to the mage's original hits). Physical armor provides no protection — this is a direct neural assault. The ward cannot distinguish between degrees of intent; anyone unauthorized who enters the space is attacked equally, whether they are a professional infiltrator or a maintenance worker who took a wrong turn.

Each activation of the ward costs 1 Quintessence from a reservoir stored in the ward at creation (typically loaded with 3-5 points using Prime 2 during setup). When the reservoir is exhausted, the ward becomes dormant and can be recharged by the mage at a Node or by spending Quintessence directly. The ward does not prevent simultaneous activations — a group of unauthorized entrants each trigger the ward independently.
