Vulgar only if the mage's physical state during the hayimn becomes alarming to Sleeper observers, or if the targets wake disturbed and attribute their dream disturbance to something other than ordinary sleep experience.


## Prime — Rotes of Quintessence and Magical Force

Prime magic interacts with Quintessence — the fundamental energy that underlies all magical activity, flows through Nodes, and sustains the patterns of living and non-living matter. It is the Sphere most concerned with the nature of magic itself: detecting it, channeling it, storing it, and unmaking it. Virtually every Tradition has Prime rotes, because working with Quintessence is a basic survival skill for an Awakened mage.

### Sense Quintessence

Traditions: All Traditions. Spheres: Prime 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Tags: [Sensory], [Subtle].

The universal Prime working taught to every mage who reaches Prime 1. The mage perceives the flow of Quintessence in their immediate environment: Nodes glow with concentrated energy; Tass shimmers with stored power; enchanted objects and active magical workings pulse with distinctive signatures; other mages's Resonance patterns are visible like fingerprints. The mage can estimate the Quintessence rating of any Node within range, identify whether an item has been enchanted and approximately when, and detect active magical effects even when their outward manifestation is invisible. Range: (Arete/Enlightenment x 50) meters for ambient flows; touch required for precise rating of specific objects. Duration: one scene.

Coincidental in all circumstances.

### Channel Quintessence

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Dreamspeakers, Verbena. Spheres: Prime 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Healing (mage)], [Boost].

The mage draws Quintessence from a nearby Node or piece of Tass, refilling their personal reserves. Each hit refills one point of Quintessence. The mage must be within (Arete/Enlightenment x 10) meters of an active Node or physically touching Tass. Without a Node or Tass, the mage can harvest ambient free Quintessence from the environment, but this yields only 1 point per 10 minutes of meditation and cannot be "rushed" by spending more hits. Drawing too aggressively from a small Node (attempting to take more than its available rating in a single day) risks damaging or temporarily depleting the Node; such damage lasts one week per point over-drawn.

Coincidental in all circumstances.

### Unweaving

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Euthanatos, Virtual Adepts. Spheres: Prime 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Dispel], [Counter].

The mage dismantles an existing magical effect, dissolving it back into free Quintessence. Each hit on the Unweaving roll removes one hit from the target effect; eliminating all of the original casting's hits dispels it entirely. This is a contested roll against the original caster's roll (or, for persistent effects, against a difficulty set by the Storyguide based on the working's power). Unweaving is the standard counter-magic technique, and most advanced mages have it prepared. It works on Rotes, Freeform effects, wards, enchantments, Paradox manifestations, and even some environmental magical phenomena (though Paradox is harder to unweave — the Storyguide adds 2 to the effective hits of any Paradox effect being resisted). Unweaving cannot be used to dispel the magic of Nephandi or Marauders without first achieving a Prime casting roll equal to their effective Arete/Enlightenment.

Coincidental when the dispelled effect had no visible presence. Vulgar when the mage is seen unmistakably disabling another mage's working.

### Prime Weapon

Traditions: Akashic Brotherhood, Order of Hermes, Verbena. Spheres: Prime 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Boost], [Combat], [Lasting].

The mage coats a weapon — or their own bare hands — with a sheath of raw Quintessence, making strikes capable of affecting beings that partially or fully exist outside the physical world. The weapon deals Aggravated damage to: spirits, Paradox manifestations, vampires, werewolves, and any being whose physical form is partially sustained by supernatural means. It deals normal Lethal damage to ordinary humans and animals. The Quintessential coating glows faintly in Prime-perception-enhanced sight — to normal observers, there is no visible change. The enhancement lasts for one scene and dissipates if the weapon leaves the mage's possession for more than a few seconds.

Coincidental in all circumstances.

### Enchant Item

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Akashic Brotherhood, Sons of Ether. Spheres: Prime 3 + relevant Spheres. SF: ... Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Enchant], [Lasting].

The mage imbues a mundane object with a persistent magical effect, creating a Talisman. The item stores one rote effect of the mage's choosing, keyed to the item's physical use (a sword that channels Flame Arrow when swung, a ring that maintains Mental Shield continuously, a watch that always shows accurate local time regardless of setting). Activating the stored effect later requires a Perception roll (Difficulty 1) rather than a full Casting roll, making Talismans useful for mages whose Casting pools are situationally weak. Each hit on the Enchantment adds one use per day before the item must recharge at a Node. Creating a Talisman requires a crafted physical vessel appropriate to the enchantment, ritual conditions, and the SF of both Prime 3 and the stored rote.

Coincidental in ritual conditions. Vulgar when performed without proper framing.

### Reality Anchor

Traditions: Order of Hermes. Spheres: Prime 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Tags: [Protective], [Lasting], [Environmental].

The mage establishes a zone of stabilized, reinforced reality around a location, suppressing Paradox buildup and making spontaneous Vulgar magic considerably more difficult. Within the Anchor's radius (hits x 5 meters): Paradox accumulates at half speed for everyone inside; any Vulgar magic cast by mages other than the one who established the Anchor suffers +2 to its effective Speed Factor for Paradox purposes; and spirits have difficulty manifesting (Spirit 3+ required to enter from the Penumbra rather than the usual Spirit 2). The Anchor does not prevent magic — it simply makes reality more "sticky." Duration: one scene per hit; permanent Anchors require Prime 5 and ritual work over several days.

Coincidental in all circumstances — the effect is invisible and produces no dramatic phenomena.

---

### Channeling Quintessence

Traditions: All Traditions. Spheres: Prime 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Subtle], [Lasting].

The fundamental Prime working — drawing Quintessential energy from external storage (personal Avatar reserves, Tass, Periapts, or actively flowing Nodes) and channeling it into magical workings or storing it for later use. Prime 1 allows the mage to see and perceive Quintessence flows, Nodes, Tass, and magical Resonance. Prime 2 allows active manipulation: drawing Quintessence from a Tass object absorbs its stored energy; drawing from a Node requires a roll (difficulty based on the Node's rating and accessibility); drawing from Tass held in the hand is automatic on a single hit. The channel can also run in reverse — pushing Quintessence into an object consecrates it, into an Effect powers it, into another mage or creature fuels their own reserves. Each turn of sustained channeling requires the mage to dedicate their attention to it; combat interrupts channeling unless the mage specifically builds in a shield against distraction. All Traditions perform this working, though their conceptual frameworks differ: Hermetics use formal invocations; Verbena bleed into chalices; Celestial Chorus pray; Virtual Adepts run energy transfer protocols.

Coincidental in all circumstances — Quintessence manipulation is essentially invisible.

### Quintessential Bolt

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Akashayana, Celestial Chorus. Spheres: Prime 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Damage], [Aggravated].

The mage condenses and projects raw Quintessential energy as a weapon — a bolt, blast, or focused discharge of the primal stuff of reality weaponized against a target's Pattern. The bolt inflicts Aggravated damage that bypasses armor (physical protection) and Fortitude, attacking the target's fundamental Pattern rather than their physical body. Costs one Quintessence per turn maintained as a sustained beam, or can be fired as a single pulse at no ongoing cost. The visual form is paradigm-dependent: Order of Hermes practitioners project crystalline white-gold beams; Akashayana chi-fire appears as blue-white flame; Celestial Chorus divine radiance manifests as blinding holy light that Nephandi and certain spirit-beings find physically agonizing. The bolt can be configured to harm specific target types: adding Spirit 2 allows it to damage ephemeral beings directly; adding Forces 3 makes it heat-based (affecting matter as well as Pattern); adding Life 3 makes it specifically devastating to biological patterns. Aggravated damage inflicted by the Quintessential Bolt cannot be soaked by any mundane means — only by Prime-based shields or equivalent supernatural defenses.

Vulgar in all circumstances.

### Rubbing the Bones

Traditions: Euthanatos, Order of Hermes, Dreamspeakers. Spheres: Prime 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Damage], [Mental].

The mage channels raw Prime energy directly into a target's Pattern through physical touch, disrupting their vital energies at the metaphysical level. The touch itself requires no particular force — a fingertip laid against the back of a hand, a palm pressed briefly against a shoulder. The Quintessential discharge causes Bashing damage that bypasses physical armor (it is not a physical blow). If the target fails a Willpower roll (difficulty = mage's hits from the Effect roll), they are paralyzed for one turn per hit — the disruption of their vital flow temporarily severs the connection between will and muscle. Unlike purely physical attacks, the paralysis from a failed Willpower save affects even supernatural beings that are immune to mundane stunning effects, provided they have a physical Pattern that Prime can interact with. Euthanatos practitioners use this as a "mercy tap" for delivering targets to unconsciousness without physical damage, and as a opening move to set up more severe workings. The physical touch requirement is a genuine constraint — this is a melee-range Effect. Against intangible or ephemeral targets, Spirit 2 must be added to allow the Prime discharge to interact with their non-physical pattern.

Vulgar when the energy discharge is visible. Coincidental when the touch produces no visible effect and the paralysis is mistaken for a nerve-pinch or pressure-point technique.

### Consecrating and Energizing Items

Traditions: All Traditions. Spheres: Prime 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Lasting], [Protective].

The mage invests a physical object with Quintessential energy, transforming it from a mundane item into a magical implement. At Prime 1, basic consecration attunes the item to the mage — it resists being used against her, and she can find it at any time by Pattern sense. At Prime 2, the consecration allows the item to inflict or soak Aggravated damage — a sword so charged wounds supernatural beings that resist mundane weapons; armor so charged provides protection against magical attacks. At Prime 3, the mage creates genuine Periapts (Quintessence storage vessels) or Artifacts (objects with specific built-in magical Effects). At Prime 4, the creation becomes a permanent self-sustaining Talisman. The process requires extended ritual: the mage must score a minimum number of hits over multiple sessions, spending personal Quintessence as material investment in the item. The item's existing quality limits what it can hold — a mass-produced factory knife cannot become a Talisman of the same magnitude as a hand-forged blade with a history. Living organisms can be consecrated as Charms (Prime 4 + Life 3), creating breathing Quintessence batteries that are among the most ethically complex items in magical tradition.

Coincidental when performed as a ritual with appropriate ceremony. Vulgar when the consecration produces visible energy phenomena in front of Sleepers.

### Heart's Blood Self-Sacrifice

Traditions: Verbena, Euthanatos, Celestial Chorus. Spheres: Prime 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Tags: [Subtle], [Lasting].

The mage converts personal life-force into Quintessential fuel, deliberately taking physical damage to generate magical energy. The sacrifice is simple and immediate: the mage deliberately inflicts one Health Level of damage on themselves (cutting the palm, pressing a blade against the forearm, striking stone with bare knuckles) — damage that bypasses soak entirely because it is intentional. One point of Quintessence is generated per Health Level sacrificed. The shed blood or vital energy serves as a powerful magical focus beyond merely generating Quintessence: it lowers the difficulty of the next Effect cast within the same scene by -1 to -3, depending on how much was sacrificed and how well it aligns with the working. Verbena blood-workers consider this the fundamental relationship between mage and magic — the cost validates the working, makes it real, roots it in flesh and will rather than mere intellectual exercise. Euthanatos practitioners use a more ceremonial variant involving ritual scarification according to tradition. Celestial Chorus practitioners sacrifice through extended fasting or a specific austerity. The technique is always performed privately — in public it is difficult to explain.

Coincidental within ritual context where the act appears as religious practice or self-discipline. Vulgar when obvious self-harm occurs in public.


### Tapping a Wellspring

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Celestial Chorus, Dreamspeakers. Spheres: Prime 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Tags: [Boost], [Lasting].

Where Channel Quintessence draws from stored sources (Nodes, Tass), Tapping a Wellspring draws from the emotional and spiritual energy produced by intense human experience — collective religious ecstasy, mass grief, moments of transcendent joy, the energy of extreme creative achievement, or any gathering where intense authentic human emotion creates a temporary local accumulation of free Quintessence. The mage's Resonance must be compatible with the energy source: a Celestial Chorus mage draws from religious fervor naturally; a Verbena draws from primal celebration; an Euthanatos draws from grief and memorialization. Drawing from an incompatible source is possible but reduces the effective yield by half.

Each hit on the casting roll draws 2 points of Quintessence from the surrounding emotional event. The drain is invisible to mundane observers and undetectable to most Awakened unless they have Prime 2+ and are specifically examining the mage. The wellspring is not depleted — human emotional energy generates itself continuously while the event is occurring. The technique can be used repeatedly during a single event. The energy drawn carries a slight Resonance taint matching the source; Quintessence drawn from grief has a different quality than Quintessence drawn from joy, which experienced Prime practitioners can detect.

Examples: An Euthanatos mage attends a large public funeral and draws 6 Quintessence (3 hits) from the ambient grief — enough to fuel a significant working. A Celestial Chorus choir-mage draws from their own congregation's devotional ecstasy during a particularly powerful service. A Verbena mage at a Beltane celebration draws from the collective joy of the festival.

Coincidental in all circumstances — the drawing process is completely invisible.

### Opening a Node

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Celestial Chorus, Dreamspeakers. Spheres: Prime 5. SF: 4. Difficulty: 5 (Rote). Tags: [Lasting], [Environmental].

The mage opens or activates a dormant Node — a place of concentrated Quintessential potential that, once awakened, produces Quintessence passively over time. A dormant Node exists but is not yet generating or channeling Quintessence; Opening activates it. A closed Node exists and generates Quintessence but has been sealed by deliberate magical effort or by trauma to its spiritual environment; Opening reopens it. The working can also be used in reverse — closing an active Node (protecting it from exploitation or denying access to enemies). Node ratings from 1 to 5 each require approximately 10 hits per dot to open or close (a 3-dot Node requires ~30 hits).

Extended ritual is typically required for anything larger than a 1-dot Node; the work takes hours to days of sustained effort at the location. The Node's nature and Resonance are not created by this working — Opening merely activates potential that was already latent. The mage's own Resonance affects the activation: Hermetics opening Nodes with formal invocations tend to impose Hermetic Resonance on the newly active Node; this is considered both a feature and a controversial practice by other traditions who consider it a form of spiritual colonization. A fully opened Node requires no maintenance; it generates Quintessence based on its rating independently.

Examples: A 1-dot Node requires 10 hits — achievable in a single extended ritual session. A 3-dot Node requires ~30 hits — several sessions of dedicated work. A 5-dot Node, already immensely significant, requires ~50 hits — typically a multi-mage ritual lasting a full day.

Coincidental when performed as a private ritual. Vulgar when the Node activation produces visible phenomena.

### Creating Tass

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Dreamspeakers, Verbena, Celestial Chorus. Spheres: Prime 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Tags: [Craft], [Lasting].

The mage condenses raw Quintessential energy from a Node or ambient flow into a physical substance (Tass) that stores it for later use. Tass can take any physical form the mage chooses: sacred water drawn from a Node-touched spring, crystals charged with Quintessential energy, specially prepared food (cakes baked at a Node site, blessed bread), charged incense, or any other paradigmatically appropriate physical carrier. Each hit on the casting creates 1 point of stored Quintessence within the Tass; the physical carrier determines how the stored energy is accessed and consumed.

Tass degrades over time if not properly stored — unprotected Tass loses 1 Quintessence per month; Tass stored in a Sanctum or appropriately consecrated vessel maintains its charge indefinitely. Tass can be traded between mages and serves as a form of magical currency in some traditions. The Hermetic tradition has developed extensive protocols for Tass creation and storage; the Virtual Adepts maintain databases of Tass quality and provenance. Creating Tass requires a Node or concentrated Quintessence source nearby; the mage cannot create Tass from personal reserves without losing that Quintessence permanently (there's no net gain).

Examples: With 3 hits drawing from a 4-dot Node, the mage creates a small crystal carrying 3 points of Quintessence. With 5 hits, the mage creates a significant piece of Tass — enough to fuel several moderate workings. The Verbena create Tass through harvest rituals aligned to seasonal Nodes; their Tass has a strongly naturalistic Resonance that makes it ideal for Life and Entropy workings.

Coincidental when performed as a ritual creation. Vulgar when Quintessential energy visibly condenses into physical form.

### Burnt Offerings

Traditions: Verbena, Celestial Chorus, Dreamspeakers, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Matter 2, Prime 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Matter. Tags: [Subtle].

The mage ritually destroys an object to release its bound Quintessence — fire is the most common method, but dissolution, burial, or any other form of genuine destruction serves equally. The basic technique (Prime 2 alone) destroys an item and extracts 1 point of Quintessence, regardless of the item's previous value. The more refined technique (Matter 2 + Prime 2) carefully deconstructs the object's Pattern to yield 1 Quintessence per hit — far more efficient but requiring greater skill. The object must be genuinely destroyed; the technique cannot extract Quintessence from an object the mage plans to keep.

The ritual context matters: objects with high personal significance or magical history yield more Quintessence than mundane objects of equivalent mass. Destroying a personal possession generates 1-3 extra Quintessence (Storyguide discretion based on significance). Destroying a magical item generates Quintessence equal to its Quintessence rating. The technique is a permanent and irreversible choice — once destroyed, the item's Pattern is dissolved. Verbena practitioners of blood-sacrifice traditions connect this to the Heart's Blood rote, viewing voluntary destruction of valued possessions as a parallel expression of the same magical principle.

Examples: With 2 hits (Matter 2 + Prime 2), the mage destroys a crafted object and extracts 2 Quintessence from its Pattern. With 4 hits destroying a significant personal possession, the mage generates 4 Quintessence plus any bonus from significance. Burning a minor Tass item releases its stored Quintessence directly without requiring this rote (the Quintessence was already stored); this technique is for generating Quintessence from non-Tass objects.

Coincidental in ritual contexts — burning offerings is an ancient and globally recognized practice.

### Bond of Blood — Quintessential Sharing

Traditions: Verbena, Euthanatos, Dreamspeakers. Spheres: Prime 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Lasting], [Interaction].

The mage establishes a voluntary Quintessential bond between two or more willing participants, creating a persistent link that allows them to share Quintessence across the connection. Both parties must be present and willing; coercion or deception invalidates the bond before it can form. The bond allows one participant to voluntarily channel Quintessence to another through an act of will — a useful backup for situations where a mage's reserves are depleted mid-working, or where one mage specializes in Quintessence generation and another in spending it. The bond is two-way: either participant can push Quintessence to the other.

Each hit on the casting determines the bond's depth: 1 hit creates a bond that permits transfer at a cost of 1 additional Quintessence per transfer (inefficient but functional); 3 hits creates an efficient bond with no transfer cost; 5 hits creates a deep bond that automatically balances the two participants' Quintessence pools if they are within range — excess from one flows naturally to the other. Duration is one scene by default; making the bond permanent requires additional hits and a physical focus (exchanged objects, a shared blood oath). The Verbena use blood exchange bonds extensively in working groups; Dreamspeakers create bonds through gift-giving and shared ceremony.

Examples: Three Verbena in a working group establish a Bond of Blood before a major working; throughout the ritual, any mage who runs low on Quintessence can draw from a partner without interrupting the working's flow. With 5 hits, the bond automatically maintains equal Quintessence levels between participants without any conscious transfer effort.

Coincidental when established through recognized ritual. Vulgar when Quintessential energy visibly flows between participants.

### Quintessential Blessings

Traditions: Celestial Chorus, Order of Hermes, Verbena, Dreamspeakers. Spheres: Prime 4, Entropy 2. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Prime. Tags: [Boost], [Lasting].

The mage suffuses a target with Quintessential vitality — filling their Quintessence pool completely and providing ongoing metaphysical benefits for the duration. At Prime 4, the target ignores wound penalties while the Quintessential sustenance holds their Pattern together; the penalty for being at three Health Levels above Incapacitated is suppressed, allowing the target to function at full effectiveness while badly injured. At Prime 5, the effect becomes more permanent — the target's Pattern is reinforced at a fundamental level, providing longer-lasting resilience. Adding Entropy 2 channels the Quintessential energy specifically through probability manipulation, making the blessed target more likely to succeed at challenges through statistical fortune as well as raw power.

Each hit on the casting determines the extent of the benefit: 1 hit refills the target's Quintessence pool to maximum; 3 hits also grants the wound-penalty immunity for the scene; 5 hits with Entropy 2 additionally provides +1 die to all of the target's rolls for the scene as fortune itself favors them. The blessing feels like genuine divine favor or natural luck to the recipient — and functionally it is, as the Quintessential sustenance and probability adjustment are real metaphysical effects. The Celestial Chorus performs this through laying-on-of-hands and formal blessing; Verbena through anointing and spoken charm; Dreamspeakers through invocation of helping spirits.

Examples: Before a critical ritual, the mage blesses all participants — their Quintessence pools are refilled and wound penalties suppressed for the working. A Celestial Chorus mage blesses a dying ally with 5 hits + Entropy 2 — the ally fights on at full effectiveness despite lethal wounds, feeling the genuine presence of divine favor sustaining them.

Coincidental when performed as recognizable religious blessing. Vulgar when the energy transfer is visible to Sleeper observers.

### Soulgem and Soulflower

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Verbena, Euthanatos. Spheres: Matter 4, Prime 4, Life 5. SF: 4. Difficulty: 5 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Life. Tags: [Craft], [Lasting].

The creation of extraordinarily powerful Periapts capable of storing large amounts of Quintessence — among the most valuable and demanding magical crafting projects in any tradition. A Soulgem is a mineral crystal infused with Prime energy at the Pattern level: Matter 4 is required to alter the mineral's fundamental atomic and molecular structure to make it capable of Quintessence storage at the required scale; Prime 4 actually loads and seals that storage. A Soulflower is a living plant that functions as a biological Quintessence storage system: Life 5 sustains the living Pattern in a state that maintains the Quintessential charge without biological degradation; Prime 4 loads and maintains the energy. Both require extended Great Work rituals lasting days to weeks.

A completed Soulgem stores 10-20 points of Quintessence (determined by hits scored during creation, approximately 2 points per hit above the base threshold). A Soulflower stores 8-15 points but has the advantage of being a living thing — it can slowly absorb ambient Quintessence over time if placed near a Node, effectively recharging itself. Both are permanent until deliberately destroyed. Destroying a Soulgem releases all stored Quintessence explosively (potentially damaging nearby mages if they are not prepared); a Soulflower releases its charge slowly as it dies naturally. The Euthanatos study these items academically but rarely create them, considering the intensive investment better directed toward direct action.

Examples: Creating a basic Soulgem (10 Quintessence capacity) requires approximately 10 hits over a week of ritual work, with the appropriate Sphere ratings throughout. A completed Soulgem is one of the most potent magical resources a mage can carry into dangerous situations. A 15-Quintessence Soulflower that recharges at 1 Quintessence per week at a Node represents a self-sustaining magical resource with significant long-term value.

Vulgar when the creation process involves visible energy phenomena. Coincidental as a completed, dormant item that shows no magical signs to Sleepers without Prime 1 examination.

### Additional Health Levels

Traditions: Verbena, Euthanatos, Celestial Chorus. Conventions: Progenitors. Spheres: Prime 5, Life 3. SF: 4. Difficulty: 5 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Prime. Tags: [Boost], [Protective], [Lasting].

The mage grants temporary additional Health Levels beyond the normal maximum — a Quintessential buffer that absorbs damage before the target's ordinary health track is engaged. These extra levels appear above the target's normal Incapacitated threshold; damage fills them first, and once they are exhausted, the target's ordinary health track resumes being affected. The extra levels do not heal existing damage; they add buffer capacity for incoming harm.

Each hit on the casting creates 2 temporary Health Levels. These levels persist for one scene (or until filled with damage). They do not regenerate — once damaged, they are used. The Quintessential sustenance requires 1 point of Quintessence per 2 additional Health Levels per scene; the mage must have the Quintessence available or the effect terminates. Prime 5 is required because supplementing the fundamental capacity of a living body's Pattern requires engagement at the deepest level of its Quintessential structure; Life 3 is required to integrate the additional capacity into a living organism's biological Pattern without causing rejection. Used on a non-living being or a construct, only Prime 5 is required (no Life component).

Examples: With 4 hits, the mage grants a target 8 additional temporary Health Levels — significant protection that can absorb multiple serious blows before the target's ordinary health is touched. A mage who grants themselves additional Health Levels before combat has an effective buffer that allows them to sustain hits that would otherwise reduce an unprotected practitioner to incapacitation.

Vulgar in all circumstances — biological impossibility.

---

### Pattern Absorption Ward

Traditions: Sons of Ether (Anti-Magic Field Generator), Virtual Adepts (Countermeasure Protocol). Conventions: Void Engineers (specialist application). Spheres: Prime 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Tags: [Protective], [Lasting], [Controversial].

Based on study of the Omniphage — a unique class of deep-Umbral entity capable of absorbing and consuming magical workings directed against it, converting the negated magical energy into additional Essence rather than allowing the magic to pass — this working creates a sustained Pattern-level absorption field around the practitioner or a designated target. When a magical working strikes the protected target, the ward intercepts the working's Quintessential component and converts it. The interception mechanism is Prime 4 operating at deep Pattern level: rather than simply blocking or deflecting incoming magic (as Mage Armor does), the ward reaches into the Quintessential architecture of the incoming working and unravels it, transforming the energy that would have caused the Effect into raw Quintessence that is then absorbed into the protected pattern.

Mechanically: when any magical working that can be countered (anything with a Prime component, which is to say any standard Sphere working) targets the protected being, the caster of this ward rolls their Prime dice pool against a difficulty of the attacking working's hits. Each success on the absorption roll eliminates one success from the incoming working, and each eliminated success generates 1 point of Quintessence that is absorbed into the ward's holder's Quintessence pool (up to their maximum). If the absorption roll achieves more successes than the incoming working has hits, the incoming working is negated entirely and the Quintessence generated by its negation is absorbed. If the absorption roll achieves fewer successes, the remaining hits of the incoming working proceed normally.

The ward is sustained, not instantaneous — it remains active for the scene in which it is cast and requires 1 point of Quintessence per hour of continuous operation beyond the first scene. It cannot be used offensively and does not generate any effect toward the source of the absorbed magic; it simply eats the magic and adds it to the protected pattern's Quintessence pool. This is notably different from pure countermagick, which reflects or disperses rather than absorbs. Against an Omniphage itself, this ward would produce an interesting mutual absorption situation that theorists have not resolved in practice.

The ward's conversion rate is efficient enough that a practitioner under sustained magical assault actually becomes more Quintessentially enriched as they absorb each incoming working — but this requires incoming attacks to sustain, and no practitioner would deliberately allow their enemy to attack them simply to gain Quintessence. The ward's real value is defensive: it transforms the vulnerability of being a magical target into a managed resource rather than a categorical threat.

Sons of Ether practitioners construct this as a physical device — an "Anti-Magic Field Generator" that they wear or carry, built around a specially prepared Prime-saturated core. The field generator is a Wonder rather than a continuous expenditure of magical effort. Virtual Adepts encode it as a countermeasure protocol that monitors incoming "magical data packets" and converts hostile packets to usable system resources. Both frame it consistently within their paradigms; the underlying Prime mechanics are identical.

Examples: With 6 hits on the initial casting, the ward is established. In the following combat scene, the mage is struck by three separate workings from opposing practitioners: a Forces 3 electrical attack (4 hits), a Mind 3 compulsion (5 hits), and an Entropy 3 Pattern-Rot targeting their biological structure (3 hits). The ward's absorption roll against the Forces attack achieves 5 successes — the attack is negated and the mage absorbs 5 Quintessence. The Mind attack is partially absorbed (3 of 5 successes negated; the remaining 2 hits still affect the mage at reduced severity). The Entropy attack is fully absorbed (4 successes against 3 hits). The mage ends the combat scene more Quintessentially resourced than they entered it, at the cost of still having received partial Mind effects. With 4 hits, the ward is borderline functional — it will absorb minor workings reliably but will fail to negate direct assaults from practitioners with high dice pools and strong hits counts.

Vulgar in circumstances where the absorption of visible magical attacks is observed by Sleepers. Coincidental when used defensively in private settings where the ward's operation cannot be distinguished from the attacker's magic simply failing.


## Spirit — Rotes of the Invisible World

Spirit magic bridges the physical world and the Penumbra — the spiritual echo of reality that exists alongside the material world. Spirit practitioners can see, speak with, summon, bind, and ultimately travel among the spirits that populate the Tellurian. The Dreamspeakers are the preeminent Spirit mages, but every Tradition has Spirit practitioners: Hermetic Solomonics who bind angelic and demonic intelligences, Verbena who propitiate nature spirits, and Celestial Chorus who commune with luminous beings they call angels.

### Spirit Sight

Traditions: Dreamspeakers, Akashic Brotherhood, Verbena. Spheres: Spirit 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Tags: [Sensory], [Subtle].

The mage perceives the Penumbra overlapping the physical world — spirits moving through the spiritual echo of their current location, the Gauntlet rating visible as a tangible shimmering barrier, and Resonance patterns clinging to locations and objects like spiritual stains. Spirits that are actively trying to hide must roll against the mage's hits or be immediately visible. Strong Resonance (a place of violence, a site of worship, an old Node) glows with distinctive coloration visible only through Spirit Sight. Duration: one scene. While maintaining Spirit Sight, the mage can hold conversations with any visible spirits by speaking aloud, though bystanders see only a mage talking to empty air.

Coincidental in all circumstances.

### Call Spirit

Traditions: Dreamspeakers, Order of Hermes, Verbena. Spheres: Spirit 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Summoning].

The mage sends a summons into the Penumbra, calling a specific spirit by name or type. Common spirits (city spirits, nature elementals, animal-spirits) typically respond within one scene; unique, powerful, or hostile spirits may require negotiation or multiple castings. The spirit is not compelled to obey the summons — only to appear and parley. The mage's hits set the difficulty of any follow-up Spirit rolls to command or negotiate with the summoned being. Spirits summoned against their will may be hostile; spirits summoned respectfully are usually willing to at least listen. The Dreamspeakers emphasize that all spirits are persons deserving of courtesy.

Coincidental when spirits manifest subtly (voices, flickering lights). Vulgar when full material manifestation occurs in front of Sleepers.

### Step Sideways — Umbral Crossing

Traditions: Dreamspeakers, Verbena, Akashic Brotherhood. Spheres: Spirit 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Transport], [Umbral].

The mage pushes through the Gauntlet, physically stepping out of the material world and into the Penumbra. The crossing takes one full combat turn (three seconds) of focused will. The difficulty of the crossing equals the local Gauntlet rating (typically 6-9 depending on location; Node sites are easier, urban cores are harder). Failure means the mage remains in the physical world; a dramatic failure may momentarily trap part of the mage's pattern between worlds (Bashing damage). Once in the Penumbra, the mage's body is absent from the physical world — they cannot affect it physically, and physical things cannot affect them. The mage can cross back using the same mechanics.

Vulgar with Witnesses — visible disappearance is unmistakably supernatural.

### Bind Spirit

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Dreamspeakers. Spheres: Spirit 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Control], [Lasting], [Controversial].

The mage constrains a spirit within a prepared vessel — a specially crafted object, a circle of power, a specific location, or (most controversially) a living host. The spirit cannot leave the binding without assistance from outside. This is a contested roll: the mage's Casting pool against the spirit's Willpower + Power rating. The binding lasts one day per hit; permanent binding requires Spirit 5. Solomonic mages of the Order of Hermes developed this rote; Dreamspeakers view it as ethically problematic and only use it as a last resort against dangerous entities. A bound spirit that is freed after long imprisonment may be extremely hostile.

Coincidental when the binding vessel is unobserved. Vulgar when the spirit's containment is visible to onlookers.

### Exorcism

Traditions: Dreamspeakers, Euthanatos, Celestial Chorus. Spheres: Spirit 3, Prime 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Spirit. Tags: [Banish], [Counter].

The mage forcibly ejects a spirit from a host, location, or object it occupies. This works against possessing spirits, spirits bound in places or items without their consent, and Paradox spirits that have attached to a mage's pattern. The Exorcism is a contested roll: the mage's Casting pool against the spirit's Rage or Willpower (whichever is higher). Each of the mage's net hits weakens the spirit's grip; achieving five total net hits forces it out entirely. Ancient, powerful spirits (Umbrood Lords, major Paradox manifestations) may require extended ritual lasting hours. A successfully exorcised spirit is expelled into the Penumbra; it is not destroyed and may return.

Coincidental when the exorcism appears to be prayer, ritual, or ceremonial activity. Vulgar when the spirit's departure is visibly dramatic.

### Spirit Pact

Traditions: Dreamspeakers, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Spirit 3, Prime 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Spirit. Tags: [Lasting], [Boost], [Alliance].

The mage negotiates a formal binding agreement with a willing spirit, establishing terms of mutual service. The mage offers something of value (Quintessence, a service, a regular offering at a specific location) and the spirit agrees to provide defined assistance (information from the Penumbra, a specific power used on the mage's behalf, protection of a location). The Pact is magically enforced — both parties feel its binding as a gentle constraint, and breaking it has consequences: the mage loses access to the agreed benefit and the spirit becomes hostile; the spirit loses whatever payment was promised and may suffer from the mage's displeasure. Well-maintained Pacts last indefinitely and are among the most stable magical resources a Dreamspeaker maintains.

Coincidental in all circumstances when the negotiation appears ritualistic or ceremonial.

---

### Ghost-Calling

Traditions: Euthanatos, Dreamspeakers, Hollow Ones. Spheres: Spirit 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Tags: [Summon], [Sensory].

The mage sends a summons into the Dark Umbra or the local haunting ground of a ghost — a deceased human consciousness that remains anchored to the living world through unresolved emotional attachments called Fetters. Spirit 2 catches the ghost's attention and invites it to manifest or communicate; it does not compel. If the mage possesses one of the ghost's Fetters (a personal belonging, a photograph, an object from the death scene), the summoning roll is made at -2 difficulty and the ghost is far more likely to respond cooperatively. Uncooperative ghosts can be commanded with Spirit 3 (pain) or bound with Spirit 4 (physical containment). The ghost, when it responds, typically manifests first as a cold spot, a sound, or an EMF fluctuation before achieving full communication. Full visible manifestation requires Spirit 3 from the ghost's own Charms or the mage's ongoing support. The Euthanatos use Ghost-Calling primarily for necromantic forensics — gathering testimony from the dead about circumstances of their deaths. The Hollow Ones approach it as theatrical communion with the departed. Dreamspeakers consider it a fundamental respectful communication practice with ancestral spirits.

Coincidental when performed as a private ritual. Vulgar when the ghost physically manifests in front of Sleepers.

### The Song of Flies

Traditions: Euthanatos, Hollow Ones. Spheres: Mind 2, Prime 1, Time 2, Matter 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Mind. Tags: [Sensory], [Subtle].

A forensic necromantic technique for reading the magical and emotional imprint of a site of death. The two core components work in concert. Mind 2 combined with Prime 1 reads the emotional Resonance of the death — how the person died, what they felt in those final moments, who was present, and what Quintessential energies were in play. Time 2 combined with Matter 1 provides postcognitive visions replaying the actual events as they occurred, not merely their emotional echo — the mage sees the scene play out with the fidelity of witness testimony rather than impression. Together, the rote gives investigators access to a full account of events surrounding a death, limited by how much time has passed (the Resonance fades over days to weeks) and how traumatic or magically charged the death was (intense deaths leave stronger, longer-lasting impressions). The name comes from the Euthanatos tradition: flies, being drawn to death, are said to carry the Song of the departed. A Hollow One who knows this rote might frame it as communing with the residual emotional recording of the space. Roll Perception + Investigation for detailed recovery of information.

Coincidental in all circumstances — appears as careful forensic examination of a scene.

### Animating the Dead

Traditions: Euthanatos, Verbena. Spheres: Life 2, Prime 2, Matter 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Life. Tags: [Summon], [Lasting], [Controversial].

The mage animates a corpse as an undead servitor — not a ghost (which is a spiritual entity) but a biological or skeletal puppet driven by Quintessential energy. Fresh flesh (Life 2 / Prime 2) produces a crude biological puppet that moves and acts but has no cognition; it can follow simple repetitive instructions but cannot handle unexpected situations. Dry bones or skeletal remains (Matter 2 / Prime 2) produce a more durable but even more limited construct — purely mechanical in its operation. The animated dead do not feel pain and do not tire, making them persistent if unimaginative assistants. Adding Spirit 2 invites a ghost to voluntarily inhabit the corpse; Spirit 4 locks an unwilling ghost inside, producing a more capable but deeply resentful entity. Entropy 3 or Time 3 slows the natural decay of animated flesh, extending the servitor's useful lifespan. The Euthanatos study this technique academically — understanding the line between life, death, and undeath is central to their philosophical tradition. Most practitioners do not use it casually, viewing it as a last resort when other options are exhausted. The act accumulates Jhor and dark Resonance regardless of intention.

Vulgar in all circumstances — a walking corpse is one of the most fundamental violations of Sleeper consensus.

### Warding Against the Dead

Traditions: Euthanatos, Celestial Chorus, Dreamspeakers, Verbena. Spheres: Spirit 2, Prime 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Spirit. Tags: [Protective], [Lasting], [Environmental].

The mage creates a spiritual barrier preventing ghosts and death-touched entities from manifesting, approaching, or using their powers within a defined space. Spirit 2 simply thickens the local Gauntlet against ghostly crossing — reducing the Dark Umbra's permeability at that location. The more comprehensive ward (adding Correspondence 2 for spatial definition and Entropy 2 for probability shaping) actively blocks ghostly Charms even from ghosts already present in the warded area, preventing manifestation, possession attempts, and the use of ghostly powers while within the boundary. The ward does not destroy ghosts — it contains or excludes them. Ghosts particularly resistant to Ward effects (those with high Willpower or specific Fetters inside the warded space) may push through temporarily; the ward's hits subtract from their efforts. Celestial Chorus wards are performed with incense and prayer; Dreamspeakers use sacred herbs, specific songs, and spirit-permission from local guardian spirits; Verbena press iron into the threshold and invoke the old bargains against the restless dead. Euthanatos practitioners use their intimate knowledge of death-Resonance to calibrate the ward's frequency precisely to the type of ghost they need to exclude.

Coincidental when performed as recognizable religious ritual. Vulgar when a visible spiritual barrier manifests in front of Sleepers.

### Wracking the Spirit

Traditions: Euthanatos, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Spirit 3, Prime 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Spirit. Tags: [Damage], [Aggravated].

The mage attacks a spiritual entity directly — disrupting the ephemeral coherence that holds a spirit's form together, inflicting pain and structural damage to its existence in the Penumbra or in the material world if it is manifested. Spirit 3 inflicts pain and disrupts spiritual cohesion (Bashing damage on the spirit's Pattern, resisted by the spirit's own rating). Adding Prime 2 allows the Prime energy to directly strike the spirit's Pattern, converting the attack to Aggravated damage that the spirit cannot ignore. The advanced application (Spirit 4 / Prime 2) creates Ephemeral Mutation — altering the spirit's nature, personality, or capabilities rather than simply damaging it, a technique used to rehabilitate corrupted spirits or weaken dangerous ones by changing what they fundamentally are. At Spirit 5 / Prime 5, the attack dissolves a spirit's essence entirely — true death for a spiritual entity, preventing reformation. This highest application is called Entropic Dispersal when the Entropy Sphere is used instead of Prime (Entropy 5 / Spirit 5). Only entities with Spirit perception (Spirit 1+) can see the attack as it targets a spiritual entity; to mundane observers, the mage appears to be gesturing at empty air.

Vulgar to anyone with Spirit perception. Coincidental to Sleepers who cannot perceive the target.


### Awakening the Spirit in Objects

Traditions: Dreamspeakers, Verbena, technoshamans. Spheres: Spirit 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Summon], [Lasting].

The mage rouses the slumbering spirit within a material object — awakening the intrinsic consciousness that animates all things, which most human traditions have forgotten exists. The awakened item develops a subtle "edge" and responsiveness: an awakened car runs better, seems to know the road; an awakened knife maintains its edge without sharpening; an awakened toaster produces perfect toast without setting adjustment. More significantly, the awakened item begins to exist in both the material and spiritual realms simultaneously — it can be brought into the Penumbra, and it can potentially affect ephemeral beings that would normally be intangible to mundane objects. The item develops a simple personality: preferences, moods, and the beginning of genuine awareness. This is an animistic paradigm-specific technique; a scientific materialist who does not believe objects have spirits cannot perform it.

Complex lifeforms are already awakened (their spirits are active); this rote applies only to objects considered "inanimate" by conventional thinking. Each hit on the casting determines the depth of awakening: 1 hit creates a subtle attunement (the item responds slightly better to its owner); 3 hits creates genuine awakening (the item has preferences, communicates through function and response, can perceive its environment in a limited way); 5 hits creates full awakening (the item has a distinct personality, can communicate with Spirit-sensitive mages through impressions, and can act to some degree independently in spiritual contexts). Duration: permanent once achieved. A fully awakened object can never truly "die" — it becomes a spirit-invested artifact that persists through ordinary physical damage as long as the spiritual component survives.

Examples: A Dreamspeaker awakens their vehicle — it develops a preference for certain roads, warns through subtle mechanical sounds when trouble approaches, and can navigate difficult terrain with unexpected competence. A technosamurai awakens their katana — it remembers every battle, communicates its readiness through weight and balance, and can strike ephemeral beings without additional magical enhancement. A Verbena awakens her spinning wheel — it spins itself under certain conditions and produces yarn of unusual quality.

Coincidental in private animistic ritual settings. Vulgar when the item visibly demonstrates its awakened consciousness in front of Sleepers.

### Invoking Spirit Possession

Traditions: Dreamspeakers, Voudoun practitioners, Euthanatos, Order of Hermes. Spheres: Spirit 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Tags: [Control], [Lasting], [Controversial].

The mage arranges for a spiritual entity to inhabit a physical vessel — whether the vessel is willing or not, and whether the spirit is willing or not. This is distinct from mere summoning (which calls a spirit to the area) and from ghost-calling (which addresses the dead specifically). Invoking Spirit Possession places the spirit within a material body: an object, an animal, or a person. Spirit 4 can invite any spirit to inhabit any vessel the spirit is compatible with, or can compel a sufficiently weakened spirit to inhabit a designated vessel. The inhabiting spirit then operates through the mage's purposes while the mage provides the physical access.

The vessel's own consciousness (if it has one) continues to exist alongside the possessing spirit in a state that ranges from cooperative to contested depending on the nature of the possession and the relative strengths of both consciousnesses. A person possessed by a powerful spirit with whom they have an existing relationship (Voudoun horse-riding of a chosen Loa) may experience the possession as transformative and consensual; a person possessed by an unwilling or hostile spirit may experience it as violation. Each hit on the casting determines the reliability and duration of the possession; contested possession (an unwilling spirit in an unwilling vessel) requires the most hits. The technique is the mechanism behind Voudoun possession, Hermetic Golem-spirit infusion, and certain Dreamspeaker healing practices where a healing spirit is invited to work through a practitioner's body.

Examples: With 3 hits (Spirit 4), a Voudoun practitioner invites a chosen Loa to ride them for a ceremony — the spirit possesses them with both parties' willing cooperation. With 5 hits, a Dreamspeaker compels a healing water-spirit to possess a patient, allowing the spirit's Charms to work directly on the patient's biological Pattern from within. With 6 hits against an unwilling spirit, an Euthanatos forces a wayward ghost into a prepared vessel, giving them negotiating leverage.

Vulgar in all circumstances where the possession is visible or demonstrably supernatural.

### Necromantic Travel — Walking Among the Dead

Traditions: Euthanatos, Hollow Ones. Spheres: Spirit 3, Life 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Life. Tags: [Transport], [Umbral].

A specialized variant of Umbral travel that takes the mage not into the ordinary Penumbra (which reflects the living world) but into the Dark Umbra — the realm of the dead, ghosts, and entropic dissolution. Standard Spirit 3 Umbral crossing takes a mage to the Penumbra; Necromantic Travel specifically navigates to the Dark Umbra, which requires knowledge of that realm's structure and access points. Life 4 is required to maintain the mage's biological Pattern during the crossing — the Dark Umbra is profoundly hostile to living things, and without Life-Sphere reinforcement, the journey causes biological damage at a rate of 1 die of Aggravated damage per scene of exposure.

While in the Dark Umbra, the mage's physical body enters a death-like state — slow heartbeat, minimal breathing, body temperature drops toward ambient. The mage is physically vulnerable during the journey. Returning requires either a successful Spirit casting to navigate back to the crossing point, or assistance from spirits who know the way. The Euthanatos consider the Dark Umbra one of the most significant testing grounds for their philosophical development — walking among the dead and returning with clarity about life's meaning is a central initiatory experience. Hollow Ones approach it as exploration of the most extreme of dark spaces.

Examples: With 3 hits, the mage crosses into the Dark Umbra for a scene and can interact with ghosts on their own terms — negotiating from the position of a visitor to their realm rather than a distant conjuror. With 5 hits, the mage maintains the crossing long enough to navigate deeper into the Dark Umbra toward older or more settled regions where powerful ghosts reside. A mage without Life 4 who attempts this journey accumulates 1 die of Aggravated damage per scene.

Vulgar in all circumstances — descending into the realm of the dead while physically alive is one of the most extreme magical acts.

### Self-Possession — Voluntary Ghost Inhabitation

Traditions: Euthanatos, Voudoun practitioners, Dreamspeakers. Spheres: Spirit 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Tags: [Summon], [Control], [Controversial].

The mage opens themselves voluntarily to a specific ghost's presence, allowing the deceased consciousness to inhabit their living body in a controlled co-inhabitation. This is distinct from being possessed involuntarily — the mage initiates the contact and maintains a Willpower contest for who controls the shared body at any moment. The ghost can speak through the mage's mouth (allowing communication with the non-Spirit-gifted), perform limited actions that require physical embodiment, or participate directly in ritual workings that would otherwise be impossible from the ghost's bodiless state.

The Willpower contest is ongoing: the mage rolls Willpower against the ghost's Willpower each scene; 10 net successes in the mage's favor establishes clear control; fewer net successes produce varying degrees of shared or contested control; the ghost winning outright temporarily overrides the mage's behavior. The technique is used to channel ghost knowledge, allow deceased individuals to say final words to the living, fulfill last requests that require physical action, or enable ghosts to participate in rituals designed to release them from anchoring Fetters. It is among the most intimate spiritual techniques available, as both consciousness experience the other's presence directly. Euthanatos ceremonial practice involves extensive preparation for self-possession events, including ritual cleansing and consent protocols.

Examples: With 3 hits, the mage establishes clear control while the ghost speaks and provides sensory input; the ghost can communicate through the mage's voice but cannot override the mage's actions. With 5 hits, the ghost can perform limited actions through the mage's body; the mage retains full control unless they deliberately yield a specific action. With a botch, the ghost briefly overpowers the mage's will — the ghost acts independently for a turn.

Vulgar when possession signs are visible. Coincidental in private ritual settings.

### Possessing Someone Else Through Spirit

Traditions: Euthanatos, Dreamspeakers, Nephandi. Spheres: Spirit 4, Mind 2. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Spirit. Tags: [Control], [Lasting], [Controversial].

A darker and more aggressive extension of Self-Possession — the mage uses Spirit 4 to open another person to ghostly inhabitation, whether or not that person consents. The mage does not themselves inhabit the target; rather, the mage becomes the intermediary that opens the target's Pattern to a specific spirit and then manages the resulting occupation. Mind 2 maintains influence over the target's mood and response during the process (preventing panic, disorientation, or involuntary resistance that would interfere with the possession). Full mental override of the target's body through a possessing spirit requires Mind 4 + Spirit 4.

Each hit on the casting determines how thoroughly the possession takes hold and how long it lasts. A willing target (perhaps someone who has agreed to serve as a vessel for a specific spirit in a ceremonial context) requires fewer hits and no contested roll. An unwilling target requires a full contested roll against their Willpower + any supernatural resistance traits. The possessing spirit's own Willpower and capabilities determine what it can do while inhabiting the target; the mage's role is enabling the possession, not directing the spirit's behavior during it. The ethical implications of forcing an unwilling possession on another person are severe; this technique is among the most morally compromising in any tradition's repertoire, and it is explicitly forbidden in most Dreamspeaker and Euthanatos ethical frameworks except in narrowly defined protective contexts.

Examples: In a ceremonial Voudoun context (fully willing participants), 3 hits enables clean possession with all parties cooperating. In a forced context against a non-mage with no supernatural resistance, 4 hits achieves full possession. Against a mage with Mental Shield and Spirit resistance, 7+ hits would be needed.

Vulgar in all circumstances — forced spiritual possession of another person.

### Creating a Body for a Spirit

Traditions: Dreamspeakers, Order of Hermes, Verbena. Spheres: Life 5, Prime 2. SF: 4. Difficulty: 5 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Life. Tags: [Craft], [Lasting], [Controversial].

The mage creates a physical vessel specifically designed to house a spiritual entity — an empty biological body that the spirit can inhabit and control. Life 5 handles the biological creation: constructing a fully functioning biological form without the natural process of gestation and development, an organism with all necessary systems but no animating mind or soul of its own. Prime 2 provides the Quintessential framework that makes the empty form compatible with spiritual inhabitation. The result is a biological body that looks and functions normally but has no consciousness of its own until a spirit inhabits it.

This is an extraordinarily demanding working requiring extended ritual (typically 25+ hits over multiple sessions) and philosophical complexity: what is the ethics of creating life specifically as a vessel for another consciousness? The body created is genuinely biological — it has biological needs (food, sleep, care), it can be wounded and killed, and if the inhabiting spirit departs, the body lingers as a mindless biological construct. Dreamspeakers who use this technique do so with extensive community deliberation about its appropriateness. Order of Hermes Solomonic practitioners use it within their tradition of spirit-binding, creating physical shells for spirits they wish to have as ongoing advisors. The Mind 5 / Spirit 5 variant displaces an existing person's mind and installs a spirit in their body — a far more ethically appalling approach.

Examples: A Dreamspeaker community council creates a body for an important ancestor spirit who wishes to participate in physical-world events — an intensive 30-hit ritual over three days. An Order of Hermes practitioner creates a body for a bound informant spirit, giving it physical presence in exchange for ongoing counsel. A corrupted mage creates a body for a Nephandic entity — an act of profound transgression.

Vulgar in all circumstances.

### Crafting a Golem

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Sons of Ether. Conventions: Progenitors. Spheres: Spirit 4, Matter 3, Prime 2. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Spirit. Tags: [Craft], [Summon], [Lasting].

The mage constructs a physical body from inanimate materials (clay, metal, stone, or biological tissue) and then binds a spirit within it to provide animation and motivation. This is the classical Golem — a constructed being animated by spiritual force. Matter 3 builds the physical body (without life, it is simply a constructed object at this stage); Prime 2 provides the Quintessential framework that makes the physical body compatible with spiritual inhabitation; Spirit 4 binds a willing or compelled spirit within, providing the animation that transforms the construction into a moving, acting entity. The resulting Golem obeys its creator's commands because the mage's Spirit 4 binding includes a compulsion toward the creator's authority.

Each 2 hits scored during creation provides 1 point of Durability (damage resistance) and 1 point of Structure (hit points equivalent). The Golem has the physical capabilities its construction allows: a stone Golem is very strong and very durable but slow and clumsy; a steel Golem is extremely strong and durable with better mobility; a biological Golem is faster but more fragile. The Golem's intelligence and capability depend on the spirit bound within it — a minor elemental provides instinct-level obedience; a more significant spirit provides genuine problem-solving within limited domains. Order of Hermes Golem-smithing is one of the tradition's most storied crafts, with documented lineages dating to medieval Prague.

Examples: With 6 hits creating a stone Golem (3 Durability, 3 Structure), the mage has a durable but slow guardian construct. With 10 hits creating a metal Golem (5 Durability, 5 Structure), the mage has a formidable combat construct. A biological Golem with 8 hits (4 Durability, 4 Structure) is faster and more capable of complex behavior due to the organic nervous system but requires food and can be affected by Life-Sphere magic.

Vulgar in all circumstances — a walking clay man is undeniably supernatural.

### Spiritual Imprisonment — The Gauntlet Cage

Traditions: Order of Hermes, Euthanatos, Dreamspeakers. Spheres: Spirit 4, Prime 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Spirit. Tags: [Control], [Lasting], [Counter].

The mage raises the local Gauntlet in a targeted area to an impassable degree — effectively creating a spiritual cage that traps a spirit between the physical world and the Penumbra, unable to cross in either direction. Prime 4 provides the Quintessential power to raise the Gauntlet to rating 10 in a defined space; Spirit 4 applies this thickening as a targeted binding rather than a broad environmental effect. The trapped spirit cannot manifest physically (too thick a Gauntlet), cannot retreat to the Penumbra (the same thickening applies in both directions), and cannot use powers that require crossing the threshold.

This is distinct from the Bind Spirit rote (Spirit 3), which traps a spirit in a vessel or circle — the Gauntlet Cage traps it in a location rather than a physical container. The spirit is constrained spatially rather than materially; the cage is the location itself, shaped into a prison by the thickened Gauntlet. The spirit's Willpower is the resistance roll; high Willpower spirits can sustain the Gauntlet-pushing for limited periods, but the cage requires them to overcome a rating 10 barrier with their Charms rather than the normal resistance. Duration is one scene per hit by default; permanent Gauntlet Cages require additional hits and ongoing Prime maintenance.

Examples: With 4 hits, the mage traps a significant ghost in a sealed room for a scene — enough time to negotiate or perform a binding ritual. With 6 hits, the cage lasts for several scenes, giving the mage time to prepare proper containment. With 8 hits and ongoing Quintessence investment, the cage becomes persistent — a permanent spiritual prison at a specific location.

Vulgar when the raised Gauntlet is detectable by Spirit-sensitive beings. Coincidental to Sleepers who cannot perceive the spiritual containment.

### Entropic Dispersal of Spirits

Traditions: Euthanatos, Nephandi. Spheres: Entropy 5, Spirit 5. SF: 4. Difficulty: 5 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Entropy. Tags: [Damage], [Aggravated], [Counter].

The ultimate anti-spirit working: the mage scatters a spirit's conceptual foundation — the core idea that holds its spiritual essence together — unraveling it entirely rather than merely damaging its ephemeral form. A spirit whose ephemeral form is destroyed can reform over time; a spirit whose conceptual foundation is Entropically dispersed cannot. The memories, Resonance, personality, and identity are broken apart and dissipated into the Quintessential flow as formless energy. True death for a spiritual entity.

Unlike Wracking the Spirit (which damages) or Spirit imprisonment (which contains), Entropic Dispersal is genuine termination. The working requires Entropy 5 to unravel the spirit's conceptual structure and Spirit 5 to access the spirit's deepest level of existence. It is contested: the spirit resists with its highest of Power, Finesse, or Resistance against the mage's hits. Each hit above the spirit's resistance strips away one layer of the spirit's coherence; achieving complete dispersal typically requires 5+ net hits. Major Umbrood, Celestines, and other entities of fundamental power are effectively immune — their conceptual foundation is too deeply embedded in the Tellurian to be unraveled by a single mage's working. Euthanatos consider this technique the "Final Gift" — appropriate only for spirits so corrupted or destructive that no other resolution serves.

Examples: With 5 net hits against a minor spirit, complete dispersal is achieved — the spirit ceases to exist. With 3 net hits against a significant spirit, severe damage is inflicted (equivalent to 5 Aggravated damage) but the spirit may ultimately reconstitute if it can escape. Against a major Umbrood lord, the technique may achieve only moderate damage regardless of hits — the entity's fundamental nature resists dispersal.

Vulgar in all circumstances — detectable as catastrophic spiritual destruction by any Spirit-perceiving observer.

### Awaken the Inanimate

Traditions: Dreamspeakers, Verbena, Euthanatos, Order of Hermes (as "Consecration of the Genius Loci"). Spheres: Spirit 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Tags: [Summon], [Lasting], [Environmental], [Craft], [Umbral].

Every object sleeps. Shamanic traditions across every continent have held this position, considered it axiomatically true, and built entire cosmological systems on the implications. The knife your grandmother used every day holds a memory; the forest that has stood for five centuries knows it exists; the house where your family was born and died has a resident presence that is not quite you and not quite nothing. The Dreamspeakers call this the animation of the world-soul; the Verbena call it the presence of the Old Ones in matter; the Order of Hermes calls it the genius loci and consecrates it with appropriate ritual. Whatever the name, the technique is the same: the mage speaks to the sleeping spirit within an object or place, and wakes it up.

Spirit 3 is required because the mage is not merely perceiving or communicating with a spirit (those being Spirit 1 and 2 respectively) but actively calling forth and animating a spiritual presence that was, before the mage's intervention, genuinely dormant. The dormant spirit of an object is not a spirit in the full sense — it has no agenda, no voice, no independent action — until woken. Once awakened, the object's spirit has presence in both physical and spiritual reality simultaneously. The awakened object exists in the Umbra as well as the material world; it may accompany its mage sideways into the spirit realm without being left behind. More practically, it performs its function better: an awakened knife holds an edge longer and seems almost eager to help its wielder; an awakened computer finds its own solutions to problems; an awakened car goes that extra mile when it matters.

The awakening works best when the mage has an existing relationship with the object or place. The spirit of a tool that has been used and cared for by a mage for years wakes more easily and more fully than the spirit of something purchased yesterday. Rousing a small personal object typically requires 5 hits over an extended ritual; a significant location or complex machine may require 20 hits or more. The mage invests part of their own Resonance in the process — the awakened spirit often reflects the mage's nature rather than the object's history. A Dreamspeaker who awakens her knife gives that knife something of her own guardian-spirit energy; a Verbena who awakens her grove makes it reflect her craft's particular flavor. This is not a flaw but a feature: the awakened object knows its mage and favors them specifically, while remaining merely animated for everyone else.

Examples: With 5 hits over an hour of ritual care and conversation, the mage awakens a personal weapon that will now refuse to cut its owner and will seem to find its way back to their hand when dropped. With 10 hits, the mage awakens a Chantry building that will alert its owner to intruders, resist unauthorized entry, and maintain a lower effective Gauntlet than the surrounding neighborhood. With 20 hits over days of ritual, the mage awakens a grove or major location as a sustained spiritual ally with its own ability to communicate information about what passes within it.

Coincidental when performed as extended ritual in private — the object simply seems to work better. Vulgar when the object manifests obviously impossible behavior in front of Sleeper witnesses.

---

### Death Fertility — Tzitzimitl's Blessing

Traditions: Dreamspeakers (Star-ancestor invocation), Bata'a (Guédé-aligned working), Euthanatos (Death-fertility current). Spheres: Life 4, Entropy 3, Spirit 3. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Life. Tags: [Healing], [Lasting], [Umbral], [Controversial].

The Tzitzimimeh are astral star-goddesses of the Mesoamerican tradition who embody a paradox of terrible beauty: they descend to consume mortal men, guide the dead to Mictlan, and simultaneously protect pregnant women and children with ferocious intensity. Their "Death Fertility" is not metaphor — it is a specific spiritual current they embody, in which death and the generation of new life are experienced as a single movement rather than opposites. This working channels that current deliberately, using the Tzitzimimeh's own spiritual signature (Spirit 3) to access an aspect of Life and Entropy that standard working-theory treats as incompatible: the enhancement of fertility and reproductive vitality through direct engagement with death-processes.

In practical terms: the working combines Entropy 3's pattern-dissolution capacity with Life 4's deep biological rewriting ability, channeled through a Spirit 3 invocation that calls upon the Tzitzimitl's aspect as death-midwife to serve as the third element. The Entropy component is not destructive in application — it dissolves specifically the biological impediments to fertility: blocked passages, hormonal dysregulation, genetic expression patterns that prevent viable reproduction, cellular-level damage from disease or aging, and the subtle Pattern degradation that accumulates in reproductive tissue over time. Life 4 simultaneously restructures the cleared biological terrain, rebuilding the reproductive system to a state of maximum vitality that the body's own pattern suggests but has failed to manifest. Spirit 3 provides the spiritual dimension that prevents the Entropy component from simply dissolving everything indiscriminately — the Tzitzimitl's aspect guides the dissolution to destroy only what should be destroyed.

The working has no direct healing component for conditions unrelated to fertility; it is a specialist working with a narrow biological focus. What it does within that focus is remarkably complete: it addresses male and female reproductive capacity equally (La Huesera's influence within this current does not distinguish between genders; she regards all bodies as "peoples"), heals damage from sexually transmitted infections, resolves the biological basis of certain pregnancy complications, and in rare cases of exceptional hits (12 or more accumulated over multiple sessions), can address genetic transmission of hereditary conditions that would pass to offspring.

The spirit component requires genuine invocation of the Tzitzimitl's aspect rather than mere Sphere mechanics — the working must begin with an appropriate spiritual approach (offerings, ceremony, acknowledgment of the paradox the entity embodies). A practitioner who approaches this working as purely mechanical technique will find the Entropy and Life components functional but the Spirit component inert, which produces an incomplete working that addresses biological impediments without the spiritual dimension that makes the cleared terrain truly fertile. Bata'a practitioners familiar with the Guédé Loa (who share this death-fertility current) can approach it through that spiritual relationship instead, substituting the Baron's aspect for the Tzitzimitl's.

Examples: With 8 hits over an extended two-session ritual — a patient who has been unable to conceive due to complex multi-system reproductive damage is restored to full reproductive capacity. The biological condition is genuinely resolved, not masked; subsequent medical examination reveals no underlying impediment. The practitioner works with the patient's own biological pattern's suggestion of what it would be without the damage — the Life 4 component reads that potential and builds toward it. With 12 hits over three sessions, combined with the genetic-condition aspect: the practitioner addresses a hereditary condition that the patient feared passing to offspring. The condition's genetic expression pattern is dissolved (Entropy 3) and the Life 4 component reconstructs the biological pattern without it. Whether this represents a genuine cure of genetic transmission or a Pattern-level intervention that does not propagate to the cellular DNA record is a question that Awakened geneticists debate. With 5 hits applied urgently during a complicated pregnancy: the specific biological threat to mother and child is dissolved and the pregnancy stabilizes. This is the most acute-care application of the working and requires the practitioner to have the requisite Spheres available on short notice, which limits its use to practitioners who specifically cultivate this working against need.

Vulgar in all circumstances — the Entropy component's engagement with life processes is intrinsically beyond the Consensus. Practitioners who work this in clinical contexts find that the spiritual invocation component provides some cover through the "miraculous recovery" framing that medical staff sometimes apply to unexplained positive outcomes.


### Pericarp Piercing — Exiting a Realm Without a Portal

Traditions: Dreamspeakers, Order of Hermes, Virtual Adepts. Spheres: Spirit 5. SF: 4. Difficulty: 5 (Rote). Tags: [Transport], [Umbral], [Lasting].

Every Horizon Realm, every Umbral Domain, every pocket reality that exists in the Three Worlds is bounded by a structure called the pericarp — the Realm Gauntlet, a membrane of condensed spiritual reality that separates the interior of the Realm from the mists of the Penumbra and the broader Umbral spaces beyond. The word "pericarp" comes from the botanical term for the wall of a seed — the hard outer layer that contains and protects the developing life within — and the metaphor is apt. A Realm is a living thing in spiritual terms, a concentrated reality shaped by its founders' intentions and sustained by the Quintessence flowing through its associated Node. The pericarp is its skin.

Most travelers enter and exit Horizon Realms through portals — deliberate gates built into the pericarp at specific locations, calibrated to accept or reject travelers based on conditions set by the Realm's founders. Portals are convenient, reliable, and controlled. They are also exactly what they appear to be: locks that the Realm's founders hold keys to. A traveler who has angered the Realm's inhabitants, been expelled, or whose only portal has been sealed behind them has a serious problem. A traveler who entered a Realm unintentionally — swept through an Anchorhead, pulled through a patron's summons, or simply wandered too far along a Moon Path — may not know where the portals are at all. In these circumstances, Pericarp Piercing is the only available exit that does not require the cooperation of the Realm's inhabitants.

Spirit 5 is required because the pericarp is not merely a thin spiritual membrane — it is the concentrated essence of the Realm's self-definition, the boundary at which the Realm's reality ends and the broader Umbra begins. Puncturing it deliberately against its natural structure requires the highest levels of Spirit mastery. The mage must perceive the pericarp (Spirit 1 allows this; at Spirit 5 the practitioner sees it in extraordinary detail), locate a workable weakness in its structure, and then apply targeted spiritual force to open a hole large enough to pass through.
