A rapid multi-Sphere assessment Procedure that gives the operative a comprehensive scan of a subject's or location's fundamental state in seconds. Life 1 reads biological status — health, current physical condition, species (including supernatural categories), and any life-threatening processes. Prime 1 detects magical activity, Quintessence residue, and whether the subject is Awakened (their Avatar's presence registers as a distinct signature). Matter 1 assesses structural integrity of physical objects and materials, identifying damage, hidden compartments, and material composition. Entropy 1 reads probability flows and structural weaknesses, flagging whatever is most likely to fail or cause problems in the immediate environment. Combined, the Pattern Scan takes two to three seconds of concentrated perception and provides the operative with a complete tactical picture of their environment that a mundane operative with sophisticated sensors could not replicate in hours. Progenitors use it in field medicine triage; Iteration X uses it for asset condition assessment; NWO operatives use it as a standard threat-assessment opening before engagement. The Procedure produces no external sign — to observers, the operative simply pauses and looks carefully at something.

Coincidental in all circumstances.

### Temporal Lag Compensation

Conventions: Iteration X, Virtual Adepts (allied). Spheres: Time 2, Life 2, Forces 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Sig. Sphere: Time. Tags: [Boost], [Lasting], [Subtle].

The operative's personal performance-optimization Procedure — a sustained compound Effect that minimizes the reaction-time gap between sensory input and motor response. Time 2 provides enhanced temporal perception: the operative experiences incoming events with a fractional-second lead time, as if watching a preview of the next moment. Life 2 optimizes the biological side: neural transmission speed is enhanced, muscle fibers are pre-loaded for rapid response, and the stress-hormone system is calibrated to support maximum performance without the tunnel vision and fine-motor degradation of panic. Forces 2 optimizes the interaction between the operative's body and their equipment — weapons handle as if extensions of the nervous system, vehicle controls respond with zero lag. The combined Effect grants +2 Initiative and reduces the difficulty of all physical reaction-based tests by -1 for the duration (one scene). Iteration X operatives use this as a pre-mission activation sequence performed in the two minutes before breach. Virtual Adept field operatives use an identical Procedure under the name "Input Lag Elimination." The procedure is invisible in operation — the operative simply seems to react faster and move more efficiently than their physical Attributes should allow, which is entirely consistent with exceptional training.

Coincidental in all circumstances.

---

## Extended Rotes — How Do You DO That?

The following rotes are drawn from the M20 How Do You DO That? sourcebook, a practical guide to Sphere magick organized by what a mage is trying to accomplish. Each entry below has been converted to SPU format: hits instead of successes, Difficulty instead of difficulty rating, Storyguide instead of Storyteller. SF equals the highest Sphere dot minus 1, minimum 1. These rotes were not duplicated in the main rote list above; they extend and complement that list with additional named workings from that text.

## Conjuration, Transformation, Shapechanging, and Modification

Life-Sphere arts of change represent some of the most versatile and demanding magick a willworker can master. From minor cosmetic alterations to wholesale transformation of complex organisms, these rotes cover the full spectrum of the Art of Change.

### Minor Shapechange (Self)

Spheres: Life 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Casting pool: Life Skill + Intellect or Awareness.

The mage alters her own form in a minor but significant way — growing claws, sharpening teeth, thickening skin, changing hair or eye colour, or adjusting her body mass within roughly the same proportions. The change is real and biological, not illusory. 1 hit achieves a simple cosmetic change; 2 hits achieves a meaningful physical alteration (claws that deal Strength +1 Lethal, or a tough hide that absorbs 1 point of bashing damage). The change lasts for the usual SPU Duration and requires one point of Quintessence to maintain in each subsequent scene. When the effect ends, the form returns to normal unless made permanent (see Permanent Changes).

Coincidental when the change is subtle and the mage is unobserved. Vulgar when dramatic physical changes occur in front of Sleepers — growing fangs or sprouting scales crosses the threshold regardless of setting.

### Major Shapechange (Self)

Spheres: Life 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Casting pool: Life Skill + Intellect or Cunning.

The mage transforms herself into a wholly different form — an animal, a wildly different human body, or something in between. The new form retains the mage's mind, memories, and Spheres, but gains the innate physical capabilities of the new shape (claws, gills, wings, etc.). Abilities that the animal would not naturally possess do not come automatically; additional Spheres must be built into the Effect at casting. 2 hits achieves a broadly similar shape (woman to large cat); 3 hits achieves a radically different form (woman to mist or stone). Each additional hit extends Duration by one step on the Duration chart. Each day spent in a radically different form costs 1 Willpower point unless the mage uses Life 5 mastery or has purchased the new form with 2 Experience.

Coincidental in secluded locations with no Sleeper witnesses. Vulgar in all other circumstances — wholesale transformation of a human into an animal is profoundly reality-defying.

### Forced Transformation (Other)

Spheres: Life 4 (minor transformation of another), Life 5 (radical). SF: 4. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). or 4. Casting pool: Life Skill + Cunning or Intellect, opposed.

The mage transforms another living being, imposing a new shape upon its Pattern. The target resists with Stamina + Fortitude against the mage's casting roll; if the target's resistance hits equal or exceed the mage's hits, the transformation fails or is only partial. 4 hits changes a target into a broadly similar form (man to boar); 5 or more hits changes a target into a radically different form (man to cobblestone or mist). The transformed being retains its original mind and memories unless the mage adds Mind effects to alter that as well. Making a transformation permanent requires 8 to 10 hits and a point of permanent Quintessence.

Vulgar in all circumstances against unwilling targets. Coincidental only when the mage is turning one animal into a similar animal in a private setting.

### Partial Transformation

Spheres: Life 3 (self), Life 4 (other). SF: 3. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). or 3. Casting pool: Life Skill + Intellect.

The mage transforms only a portion of a subject — grafting jaguar claws onto otherwise-human arms, growing a prehensile tail, sharpening senses to animal acuity, or adjusting one Attribute by up to 2 dots. This requires half the usual hits of a full transformation. 1 hit achieves a simple partial change on self (claws, heightened reflexes); 2 hits achieves a more complex partial change or applies the change to another character. Each unusual property built into the partial form costs 1 additional hit at casting. Attributes boosted this way return to normal when the Duration expires.

Coincidental when subtle and unobserved. Vulgar when the partial form is visibly inhuman in public.

### Multiple Forms

Spheres: Correspondence 4 / Life 4 / Mind 2. SF: 3. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Life Skill + Intellect.

The mage divides her consciousness and physical existence into multiple simultaneous creatures, each one a living entity sharing the original mage's mind and Spheres. Each hit beyond the base allows one additional creature-form (1 hit = two forms; 2 hits = three forms, and so on). Each separate form has the mage's full Health levels and Abilities. If any one form is killed, the mage endures; if all forms are destroyed, so is the mage. When reuniting, the mage suffers the accumulated damage of all destroyed forms simultaneously.

Vulgar in virtually all circumstances.

### Altering Characteristics

Spheres: Life 3 (self), Life 4 (other). SF: 3. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). or 3. Casting pool: Life Skill + Cunning or Intellect.

The mage boosts or reduces a subject's physical or social Attributes — Might, Stamina, Cunning, Presence, Awareness — by 1 dot per hit rolled. Mental Attributes can also be altered by modifying the subject's sensory organs or brain structure. Changes within the normal human range (1 to 5 dots) remain Coincidental if gradual; sudden dramatic changes or scores above 5 dots become Vulgar. Making changes permanent requires 4 to 6 hits for self, 8 to 10 hits for another, plus a point of permanent Quintessence.

Coincidental when changes are subtle and incremental. Vulgar when a subject visibly bulges with inhuman muscle or grows alien features.

### Conjuration from Nothing

Spheres: Matter 3 + Prime 2 (simple object); Matter 4 + Prime 2 (complex object). SF: 3. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Matter Skill + Intellect.

The mage conjures basic material from apparent nothingness — a rock, a length of rope, a functional tool — by channelling raw Quintessence through the Matter Sphere and shaping it into a coherent Pattern. 2 hits creates a simple, rough object; 3 hits creates a functional item; 4 or more hits creates a complex, precise item. Conjured matter lasts for the standard Duration and returns to nothing when it expires unless the mage makes the creation permanent with additional hits.

Coincidental when the object could plausibly have been retrieved from a bag. Vulgar when matter visibly materialises from thin air.

---

## Elemental Mastery

Forces and Matter combine with Prime and other Spheres to allow mages mastery over the raw elements — fire, water, earth, air, electricity, and the more exotic energies of the modern age.

### Elemental Kinetics — Basic Manipulation

Spheres: Forces 2 (energy), Matter 2 (solid or liquid). SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Forces or Matter Skill + Cunning or Awareness.

The mage moves, accelerates, decelerates, shapes, or redirects existing elemental matter or energy without creating or destroying it. A boulder rolls where directed; flames leap sideways; a stream changes course; lightning arcs to a new target. 1 hit achieves a minor adjustment (nudge, redirect); 2 hits achieves a meaningful movement or reshaping; 3 hits achieves a large and sustained elemental movement covering an area or lasting a scene. Weight and mass apply normally — moving a ten-tonne boulder still requires tremendous kinetic force even if the mage provides it magickally.

Coincidental for subtle redirections in private. Vulgar when large-scale elemental movement is witnessed without visible mechanism.

### Pyrokinesis — Mind-Fuelled Fire

Spheres: Mind 3 / Forces 2 (control existing fire); Mind 4 / Forces 3 / Prime 2 (conjure fire from nothing). SF: 3. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). or 3. Casting pool: Mind Skill + Cunning.

The mage commands fire through focused psychic will rather than pure elemental technique — a discipline associated with paradigms like Everything is Data or consciousness-focused mystical traditions. With Mind 3 / Forces 2, the mage controls, redirects, and shapes existing flames with great precision. With the full conjuration version, the mage manifests fire from sheer mental concentration. The Storyguide may require a Willpower point for especially large, sustained, or unusual conflagrations (setting metal on fire, controlling a wildfire). Damage and duration follow the same rules as Elemental Assault above.

Coincidental for subtle manipulation of existing fire. Vulgar when fire obviously obeys mental command or appears from nowhere.

### Mystic Physics — Altering Elemental Properties

Spheres: Forces 2 or Matter 2 (basic property change); Forces 3 or Matter 3 (radical). SF: 2. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). or 2. Casting pool: Forces or Matter Skill + Intellect.

The mage alters the intrinsic physical properties of a substance or energy — making water freeze in tropical heat, making metal soft as clay, reversing the polarity of an electrical field, or causing a light source to emit darkness. 1 hit alters a minor property in a small area; 2 hits alters properties significantly (all water in a room becomes acid, ambient temperature drops 20 degrees); 3 hits achieves dramatic property alteration over a large area. Permanently altering a substance's properties requires 5 or more hits and Prime 3.

Coincidental for changes attributable to technology or natural causes. Vulgar when properties visibly defy physics before Sleepers.

### Velocity Boost — Enhanced Projectiles

Spheres: Forces 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Forces Skill + Cunning.

The mage increases the velocity, accuracy, and kinetic impact of a projectile — an arrow, a thrown weapon, a bullet, a thrown bottle. Each hit lowers the Difficulty of the subsequent attack roll by 1, to a maximum reduction of 3. Additionally, each hit adds 1 point of base damage to the projectile's impact if the attack succeeds. The enhancement applies to a single projectile or a burst of fire from a single weapon.

Coincidental almost always — exceptional marksmanship is believable. Vulgar only if bullets curve visibly in mid-air.

### Summoning Storms — Large-Scale Weather Control

Spheres: Forces 4 (alter weather significantly), Forces 5 (summon major storm). Additional: Correspondence 2 or more (expand range). SF: 4. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). or 4. Casting pool: Forces Skill + Intellect, extended ritual.

The mage reaches out into atmospheric systems and draws weather into being or reshapes existing conditions. This is extended-roll magick — the Storyguide sets a target of 5 to 15 hits for modest weather changes (overcast skies, rain, a stiff wind) and 20 or more hits for major storms. Each roll takes 10 minutes of ritual concentration. Altering localised weather is Coincidental if conditions already favour change; conjuring snow on a cloudless midsummer day requires more hits and more Paradox. The storm, once called, follows natural patterns and may persist long after the spell's Duration.

Coincidental when conditions already favour change. Vulgar when a storm appears with no meteorological basis in front of observers.

### Creating a Realm

Spheres: Forces 5 / Matter 5 / Life 5 / Mind 5 / Spirit 3 minimum; actual requirements depend on desired features. SF: 4. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Casting pool: Highest relevant Skill + Intellect, extended Great Work.

The mage creates a new Horizon Realm — a pocket dimension with its own environment, physics, and inhabitants. Crafting the basic structure of a habitable Realm requires 100 or more hits in an extended ritual over days or weeks. Personalised Realms with complex ecosystems and self-sustaining physical laws may require 500 or more hits — typically a Great Work undertaken by a council of Masters. A Realm requires at least one associated Node for its energy source.

Inherently Vulgar by ordinary standards, but performed outside normal Paradox considerations once the Realm is established, since no Sleepers are present.

---

## Energy-Work — Prime Sphere Techniques

The Prime Sphere governs the raw Quintessential energy that underlies all reality. These rotes manipulate that energy directly — channelling it, storing it, weaponising it, and using it to empower or drain living beings and objects.

### Aura Reading

Spheres: Prime 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Casting pool: Prime Skill + Awareness.

The mage perceives the Quintessential aura of a person, place, or object. 1 hit detects the presence or absence of Quintessence and broad Resonance flavour; 2 hits identifies the flavour precisely (Entropic, Vital, Elemental, etc.) and notes recent magical activity; 3 hits reads the aura's full history — who has worked magick here, what Spheres were used, and what emotional signature persists. Residual traces last for a number of days equal to the original Effect's hits before fading.

Coincidental at all times — perceptual magick is invisible.

### Aura Alteration

Spheres: Prime 2 / Mind 1 (alter surface aura); Prime 3 / Mind 2 (create convincing false aura). SF: 2. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Casting pool: Prime Skill + Cunning.

The mage reshapes the visible Quintessential aura of herself or another being to display false information. 2 hits alters the surface impression for the scene; 3 hits creates a convincing false aura that passes casual inspection; 5 hits creates an aura deception that fools even an Awakened mage using Prime 3 to examine it, requiring an opposed roll to detect.

Coincidental — the alteration produces no visible effect.

### Energy Vampirism

Spheres: Prime 3 (willing transfer or unconscious drain); Prime 4 (unwilling extraction). SF: 3. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). or 3. Casting pool: Prime Skill + Cunning.

The mage siphons Quintessence from another living being's Pattern. With a willing donor, Prime 3 allows consensual transfer — sealed with a kiss, a blood oath, or a moment of shared ecstasy. With an unwilling target, Prime 4 rips Quintessence from the victim's Pattern; the target loses 1 die from all pools per 2 Quintessence drained until they recover through rest. Each hit on the casting roll transfers 2 Quintessence from target to mage.

Coincidental unless the drain collapses the victim. Vulgar when the target visibly crumples from vital energy extraction.

### Paradox Nullification

Spheres: Prime 4 / Entropy 3. SF: 3. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Casting pool: Prime Skill + Intellect, extended.

The mage siphons Paradox energy from her own Pattern or from a Paradox backlash in progress, dissolving the accumulated Unbelief before it can manifest. Each hit on the extended casting roll reduces the Paradox Pool by 1 point. The mage must maintain concentration throughout the process, which typically takes a scene of focused ritual activity.

Coincidental when performed using appropriate scientific or spiritual ritual framing. Vulgar when raw Paradox energy visibly dissipates in front of Sleepers.

### Disintegration and Purifying Flames

Spheres: Prime 4 (inanimate target); Prime 5 (living target). SF: 4. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). or 4. Casting pool: Prime Skill + Cunning.

The mage disperses the Quintessential Pattern of a target — not draining its energy but unravelling it entirely, returning the constituent energies to the world. Against inanimate objects, Prime 4 causes dissolution. Against living beings, Prime 5 is required. Each hit deals 2 levels of Aggravated damage to the target's Pattern. At 5 or more hits, the dissolution is total and the target cannot be resurrected by conventional means.

Vulgar in virtually all circumstances. Coincidental only in a context where destruction by fire or acid would be expected.

### Spotting the Afterglow — Magical Forensics

Spheres: Prime 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Casting pool: Prime Skill + Awareness.

The mage reads the residual Quintessential traces left by magickal Effects. 1 hit detects the presence of residual magickal activity within the scene; 2 hits identifies which Spheres were involved and how powerful the Effect was; 3 hits reconstructs the rough shape of the working — its intent, the number of castings, and the Resonance signature of the mage who performed it. This signature can then be used as a tracking tool via Correspondence.

Coincidental at all times.

---

## Enhanced Perceptions

### Basic Sphere Perceptions

Spheres: Any Sphere at Rank 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Casting pool: Sphere Skill + Awareness.

At Rank 1, every Sphere grants the mage perceptions beyond ordinary senses. Correspondence 1 provides a subtle directional sense of spatial relationships. Entropy 1 allows perception of decay, weakness, and structural flaws. Forces 1 reveals the flow of energies invisible to ordinary sight. Life 1 senses the vitality and biological state of living things. Matter 1 reveals the composition and structural integrity of materials. Mind 1 detects surface thoughts and emotional states. Prime 1 reveals Quintessential auras. Spirit 1 perceives ephemeral entities in the Penumbra. Time 1 senses the rough probability of near-future events. 1 hit activates the perception for the scene; 2 hits provides precise detail; 3 hits allows the mage to share these perceptions with others present.

Coincidental at all times — perceptual magick is invisible by definition.

### Sharing Perceptions

Spheres: Mind 2 + at least one other Sphere at Rank 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Casting pool: Mind Skill + Presence.

The mage broadcasts her extended Sphere perceptions to willing allies — allowing them to see Quintessential auras, sense nearby spirits, or feel the flow of Forces energies even without Sphere ratings of their own. 1 hit shares simple impressions with one ally for the scene; 2 hits shares clear perceptions with up to three allies; 3 hits extends the range to Medium distance and maintains the share even if the mage moves.

Coincidental at all times.

### Time-Spanning Visions — Prophecy and Hindsight

Spheres: Time 1 (immediate probability, one turn ahead); Time 2 (minutes to hours); Time 3 (days to years; hindsight of witnessed events); Time 4 (centuries; detailed hindsight without witnesses). SF: 1, 2, or 3. Difficulty: 1–4 (Rote — matches Sphere dot used). Casting pool: Time Skill + Awareness.

The mage perceives events that have not yet occurred or occurred before she was present. Time 1 gives an instinctive sense of immediate probabilities. Time 2 extends this to vague visions of the near future. Time 3 allows clear hindsight of events that have occurred in a location the mage can touch or perceive. Time 4 reaches into deeper history without requiring physical connection. Visions show the most probable outcome of current trends, not certainties. The Storyguide rolls a die secretly; on a 1, the vision is misleading. Each hit adds detail or extends the temporal range.

Coincidental when the mage meditates privately. Vulgar when visions manifest visibly or the mage accurately predicts specific future events in front of observers.

---

## Martial Arts — Arts of Supreme Mastery

The following rotes represent the meeting point between physical martial discipline and Sphere magick. Most require the mage to be personally engaged in combat or physical action.

### Slipstream — Combat Evasion

Spheres: Correspondence 1 or 2 (micro-positioning), or Entropy 2 (control probability of being hit), or Forces 2 (bend light or gravity), or Time 1 (calculate impact moment). SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Casting pool: Sphere Skill + Cunning or Dexterity.

The mage warps her immediate spatial, probabilistic, or temporal environment so that attacks consistently miss by the smallest possible margins. Each hit adds +1 Difficulty to the opponent's attack rolls against the mage for the scene. By spending hits into Duration, the mage can extend the protection for multiple turns. The Effect must be re-cast if she takes Incapacitated damage.

Coincidental as long as the mage appears to be exceptionally skilled at dodging. Vulgar if attacks visibly bend away from the mage's body or evasion is clearly impossible.

### Chi Flight — Gravity Defiance

Spheres: Forces 2 / Life 2 / Prime 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Forces Skill + Dexterity or Stamina.

Drawing on mastery of chi (Quintessential life-energy), the martial artist lightens her body against gravity, allowing her to run across water surfaces, balance on improbably thin supports, or briefly fly short distances up to Medium range in a single leap. Requires 1 point of Quintessence per scene of activation. While chi flight is active, the mage ignores falling damage for falls up to 10 metres per hit and can traverse precarious surfaces without penalty.

Coincidental in a paradigm-appropriate context (wuxia setting). Vulgar in a modern realistic setting when the mage's body visibly defies gravity in front of Sleeper witnesses.

### The Chi-Vampire Strike

Spheres: Prime 3 (drain Quintessence from opponent); Prime 5 (annihilate Pattern). SF: 4. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). or 4. Casting pool: Prime Skill + Cunning.

Advanced chi mastery allows the martial artist to drain vital energy from an opponent through physical contact. Prime 3 siphons Quintessence (2 points per hit) from the target; the victim feels exhausted and loses 1 die from all pools until the Quintessence is restored. Prime 5 targets the victim's Pattern directly, causing the target to literally come apart — at 5 hits, the victim suffers Pattern damage equivalent to severe burns and may require extended medical or magical treatment to survive.

Prime 3 drain: Coincidental — the victim simply feels exhausted. Prime 5 annihilation: Vulgar and unmistakable.

### Elemental Fury Chi — Flaming Hands, Stone Skin

Spheres: Life 3 / Prime 3 / Forces or Matter 3. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Casting pool: Life Skill + Stamina.

The martial artist channels elemental force through her body — fire erupts from her palms, stone hardens around her skin, electricity crackles through her fingertips. Life 3 alters the practitioner's physical form; Prime 3 fuses raw Quintessence with the elemental manifestation; Forces or Matter 3 provides the elemental component. Offensive use deals Strength +2 Aggravated damage and may ignite, fracture, or electrify surroundings. Defensive use soaks 2 levels of Aggravated damage per scene. Costs 1 Quintessence per turn of sustained use.

Vulgar in almost all circumstances outside settings with established elemental-martial expectations.

### The Pain-Shrug — Iron Body

Spheres: Life 3 (soak lethal and aggravated damage); or Mind 3 (ignore wound penalties). SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Casting pool: Life or Mind Skill + Stamina.

With Life 3, the mage soaks Lethal damage as if it were Bashing (reducing damage by 1 per hit), can soak Aggravated damage at 2 hits, and can raise her Stamina by 1 dot per additional hit (maximum 5 dots naturally, 2 hits per dot above 5). With Mind 3, she ignores wound-based penalties entirely until she actually dies. The two applications stack but must be cast separately. Quintessence cost: 1 point per scene of sustained soak-enhancement.

Coincidental as long as the mage appears inhumanly tough rather than clearly supernatural. Vulgar when the mage soaks gunfire visibly without injury.

### The Neo — Stopping Projectiles

Spheres: Forces 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Forces Skill + Dexterity or Cunning.

The martial artist raises an invisible field of kinetic disruption that deflects or stops incoming projectiles within her immediate reach. Each hit stops one category of incoming fire: 1 hit stops arrows and thrown objects; 2 hits stops a single firearm blast; 3 hits stops a burst of automatic fire; 5 hits stops any projectile attack physically within reach. The field must be re-cast each turn or maintained by spending 1 hit into Duration per additional turn. Without a chi component, the field costs no Quintessence.

Coincidental when dodges appear extremely skilled. Vulgar when projectiles visibly stop or deflect mid-air in front of Sleeper witnesses.

### Remote Command — War-Dance Leadership

Spheres: Mind 3 / Correspondence 1. SF: 2. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Casting pool: Mind Skill + Cunning or Presence.

The mage establishes a psychic command network with nearby allies, issuing real-time tactical orders and sharing situational awareness across a battlefield. Each hit includes one additional ally in the network (base = two allies at 1 hit; up to 50 allies at 10 hits). Within the network, the mage can mentally issue orders with zero delay, grant allies awareness of each other's positions, and coordinate attacks so that allied actions are not penalised for simultaneous manoeuvres. Maintaining the network while also acting aggressively requires a split pool.

Coincidental at all times — mental communication leaves no visible trace.

---

## Mystic Travel

### Speed — Accelerated Movement

Spheres: Forces 2 (kinetic acceleration); or Time 3 (temporal acceleration); or Life 3 (enhanced biological output). SF: 2. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). or 2. Casting pool: Forces or Life Skill + Stamina or Cunning.

The mage dramatically increases her movement speed. Forces 2 adjusts kinetic forces to propel the mage at up to triple normal speed; Life 3 enhances biological systems for similar results; Time 3 temporal acceleration allows the mage to take extra actions in a round (1 extra action per 2 hits). Sustained speed requires 1 Quintessence point per scene. Using Time 3 to move faster than three actions per round produces Paradox.

Coincidental when the speed is extraordinary but human-plausible. Vulgar when the mage moves at clearly superhuman velocity in front of Sleepers.

### Instant Fake IDs — Digital and Physical Identity Manipulation

Spheres: Matter 3 / Mind 2 / Correspondence 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Matter Skill + Cunning.

The mage creates a convincing false identity — rewriting data in official digital records, manufacturing physical documentation, and subtly adjusting appearance records. Matter 3 shapes the physical documents; Mind 2 ensures they feel emotionally credible to anyone who examines them; Correspondence 2 reaches into digital systems without physical access. 2 hits produces a basic ID that passes casual inspection; 4 hits passes professional verification; 6 hits produces a legend deep enough to withstand serious background investigation.

Coincidental when the documentation appears physically genuine. Vulgar if digital records are visibly rewritten in real time.

### Gates and Portals — Stable Spatial Connections

Spheres: Correspondence 4 (temporary gate); Correspondence 4 / Prime 3 (sustained gate); Correspondence 5 / Prime 4 (permanent gate). SF: 3. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). or 4 (depending on version cast). Casting pool: Correspondence Skill + Intellect, extended.

The mage opens a stable two-way portal between two locations, large enough for a person and their gear to pass through. Both locations must be known to the mage. A temporary gate lasts 1 scene per hit; a permanent gate requires Prime 3 enhancement and 5 or more hits. Anyone at either end can pass freely unless the mage adds Mind or Matter effects to restrict access. Unattended permanent gates attract spirits drawn to spatial anomalies.

Vulgar in all circumstances when open and visible. Coincidental only if the gate is concealed behind a mundane door or mirror.

### Everywhere at Once — Multiple Spatial Presence

Spheres: Correspondence 4 / Life 5 (duplicate physical presence); or Correspondence 5 (abstract spatial omnipresence). SF: 4. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). or 4. Casting pool: Correspondence Skill + Intellect.

The mage maintains simultaneous physical presence at multiple locations at once. The Correspondence 4 / Life 5 version creates physically separate bodies (see Multiple Forms). The pure Correspondence 5 approach allows the mage to perceive and act at multiple locations simultaneously. Each hit allows one additional simultaneous location. Actions at each location use the mage's full dice pool but suffer -1 die per additional location beyond the first.

Vulgar in virtually all circumstances.

---

## Necromancy — The Arts of the Dead

### The Vidare Mortem — Seeing the Road to Death

Spheres: Entropy 1 / Spirit 1. SF: 1. Difficulty: 1 (Rote). Casting pool: Entropy or Spirit Skill + Awareness.

The necromancer perceives the shadow of death upon living things — the visible mortality in a sick body, the near-future moment of a creature's natural death, the spiritual residue of violent death in a place. At 1 hit, the mage senses the presence of death energies nearby; 2 hits reveals the cause and approximate time of a recent death or a living being's likely natural lifespan; 3 hits allows the mage to perceive the Shadowlands overlay on the current location, seeing spirits of the dead as through gauze.

Coincidental at all times — a perceptual effect invisible to others.

### Necrosynthesis — Creating Undead Constructs

Spheres: Entropy 4 / Life 4 / Spirit 3 / Prime 2. SF: 3. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Entropy Skill + Intellect, extended Great Work.

The necromancer grafts spiritual essence, Quintessential energy, and animated biological matter into a self-sustaining undead construct with genuine autonomy — it thinks, learns, and develops over time. The process requires weeks of extended-roll work (target: 20 or more hits), the willing or captured spirit of a deceased person or creature, a suitable physical form, and substantial Quintessential investment. The resulting construct has its own Willpower and Resonance; it is loyal to its creator by default but not unconditionally so. Mages pursuing lichedom (merging their own spirit with a constructed undead body) follow this same path, requiring additional Prime and Mind effects.

Vulgar in all circumstances.

---

## Summoning, Binding, Bargaining, and Warding

### Binding Summoned Entities — The Contest of Wills

Spheres: Spirit 3 (bind willing or weakened spirit); Spirit 4 (bind resistant minor entity); Spirit 5 (bind powerful spirit). SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote)., 3, or 4. Casting pool: Spirit Skill + Cunning or Presence, opposed.

Having summoned an entity, the mage attempts to bind it within a ward, seal, or vessel — preventing its departure and compelling obedience. The spirit resists with Willpower against the mage's casting roll; if the spirit's hits equal or exceed the mage's hits, the binding fails and the spirit may retaliate. A successful binding lasts for the standard Duration extended by additional hits. An improperly bound spirit is unstable — it seeks weaknesses in its containment and may escape if the mage's attention lapses.

Vulgar when the binding is overtly supernatural in front of Sleepers. Coincidental in a private ritual setting.

### Minor Wards — Basic Protective Barriers

Spheres: Prime 2 + appropriate Sphere at Rank 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Prime Skill + Intellect.

The mage creates a ward — a magical barrier that prevents specific things from entering a defined area. A minor ward specifies one category of exclusion: spirits cannot cross, fire cannot enter, Correspondence-based spells cannot target within, and so on. Each hit extends the Duration by one step on the Duration chart. A ward can be penetrated by applying an opposed Effect with sufficient hits to exceed the ward's original hit count.

Coincidental when set as private ritual in a defined space. Vulgar when the ward visibly repels an entity in front of Sleeper witnesses.

### Major Wards — Comprehensive Spiritual Defences

Spheres: Spirit 3 / Prime 3 + additional Spheres for specific exclusions. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Casting pool: Spirit Skill + Intellect, extended.

The mage creates a comprehensive ward system that covers a building or large space, excluding all entities, magicks, or forces of a specified type. Major wards require extended rolls to set (target: 5 to 15 hits depending on area) and must be anchored to physical objects (sigils, salt lines, iron rings at cardinal points). A major ward persists for a number of days equal to hits without maintenance; with Prime 4, it can be made permanent. Wards can be stacked to exclude multiple categories, but each additional category requires a separate ward layer and another casting.

Coincidental when set as private ritual. Vulgar when the ward visibly reacts to entities in front of Sleepers.

### Mind-Barring — Mental Warding

Spheres: Mind 3 / Prime 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Mind Skill + Cunning.

The mage constructs a mental ward around her own consciousness — or that of a willing subject — that blocks or degrades all incoming Mind-Sphere Effects. Each hit adds +2 Difficulty to any Mind magick targeting the warded individual. The ward lasts for the standard Duration. Against an incoming Mind Effect whose caster matches or exceeds the ward's hits, the ward degrades rather than completely blocking. Effective also against psychic attacks from non-magickal supernatural sources.

Coincidental at all times — a purely internal effect.

### Traveling Wards — Portable Protection

Spheres: Prime 3 + appropriate defensive Sphere. SF: 2. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Casting pool: Prime Skill + Cunning.

The mage extends a ward around herself rather than a fixed location, carrying the barrier as she moves. The ward covers the mage and everything within arm's reach; expanding it to cover a group requires 1 additional hit per person included beyond the mage. A travelling ward against one category lasts for the standard Duration; maintaining it through combat costs 1 Quintessence per scene. The mage cannot cast other Effects through the ward without pausing the barrier for that instant.

Coincidental unless the ward visibly reacts to incoming attacks.

### Entropic Dispersal — Unravelling Constructed Things

Spheres: Entropy 3 / Matter 2 (unravel inanimate structures); Entropy 3 / Life 2 (weaken biological systems). SF: 2. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Entropy Skill + Cunning.

The mage accelerates the natural process of decay and structural collapse in a target — rotting wood in seconds, corroding metal, dissolving electronic systems, or rapidly degenerating a creature's biological integrity. Against inanimate objects, each hit inflicts 2 levels of structural damage. Against living beings, the mage inflicts 1 level of Aggravated damage per hit; the target resists with Stamina + Fortitude (Difficulty 3). Each turn of continued application costs 1 Quintessence.

Coincidental when decay appears natural or attributable to an unobvious source. Vulgar when a target visibly disintegrates in front of Sleepers.

### Spiritual Imprisonment — Soul Binding

Spheres: Spirit 4 / Entropy 3 / Prime 2 (trap a spirit in a vessel). SF: 3. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Spirit Skill + Cunning, opposed.

The mage traps a spirit or ghost within a prepared vessel — a sealed jar, an enchanted gem, a specially prepared relic, or a living body as a spiritual prison. The spirit resists with Willpower against the mage's casting roll; a margin of 2 or more hits in the mage's favour seals the binding. The imprisoned spirit can communicate through the vessel but cannot act independently. Spirits imprisoned for long periods develop intense Resonance that stains the vessel and its surroundings permanently.

Vulgar when the binding is overtly supernatural. Coincidental only in a ritual context where spiritual activities are expected.

---

## Time Distortion and Travel

### Rewinding Time — Local Temporal Reversal

Spheres: Time 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Casting pool: Time Skill + Intellect, extended.

The mage reverses local time, undoing events of the past few seconds to hours within the Effect's range. 1 hit rewinds approximately 3 seconds; 3 hits rewinds up to a minute; 5 hits rewinds up to an hour; 7 hits rewinds up to a day. The mage retains her memories of the undone events; other characters do not. This is one of the most Paradox-generating Effects in the Time Sphere, as it directly contradicts the flow of causality.

Vulgar in all circumstances.

### Time Immunity — Outside the Flow

Spheres: Time 4 / Entropy 3. SF: 3. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Casting pool: Time Skill + Stamina or Intellect.

The mage steps partially outside the flow of time, becoming immune to the effects of magical time manipulation, Entropic acceleration of aging, or temporal distortion Effects that target her specifically. While this Effect is active, time-based attacks against the mage must exceed the mage's hit count to affect her. The mage herself continues to age and act normally; this immunity applies only to externally imposed temporal effects.

Coincidental at all times — a purely internal effect invisible to observation.

---

## Uncanny Influence — Mind, Illusion, and Fate

### Emotional Surge — Psychic Influence

Spheres: Mind 2 (surface emotional suggestion); Mind 3 (deep emotional reshaping). SF: 2. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). or 2. Casting pool: Mind Skill + Presence.

The mage injects emotional content directly into a target's mind — sudden joy, crippling fear, melancholy, euphoria, or sudden attraction. The target resists with Willpower (Difficulty 3 for Mind 2; Difficulty 4 for Mind 3). Each hit on the mage's roll imposes the emotional state for one additional scene or increases its intensity. A natural-seeming emotional surge is coincidental; an artificial and contextually impossible emotion triggers Paradox. Mind 2 produces fleeting impressions; Mind 3 creates deep, sustained emotional states that can alter behaviour for days.

Coincidental when the emotion fits the context. Vulgar when the emotional change is obviously impossible given the situation.

### Sleep Spells — Induced Slumber

Spheres: Mind 3 (individuals); Mind 3 / Life 2 (biochemically enhanced sleep). SF: 2. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Mind Skill + Cunning.

The mage induces immediate sleep in a target or targets. The subject resists with Stamina + Fortitude (Difficulty 3 for a single target; add +1 Difficulty per additional target beyond the first). Each hit keeps the target asleep for one additional scene. The sleeper cannot be woken by normal means during the Duration — including fire alarms, loud noises, or physical shaking — though significant pain may allow a new Willpower roll (Difficulty 4) to wake. Adding Life 2 creates a biochemical component that mimics natural sleep chemistry, making the working nearly undetectable.

Coincidental when the target is already in a context where sleep is plausible. Vulgar when targets suddenly collapse in a combat zone or other clearly unnatural context.

### Influence From a Distance — Remote Mind Effects

Spheres: Mind 2 / Correspondence 2. SF: 1. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Mind Skill + Cunning.

The mage extends Mind-based Effects beyond arm's reach — emotional suggestions, memory inspections, surface thought-reading, and communication — to targets at any distance as long as she has a sympathetic connection or active Correspondence sensing of the target. Without Correspondence, Mind Effects are limited to Close range. With Correspondence 2, the mage can target anyone she can actively perceive at any distance; with Correspondence 3, anyone she has a strong sympathetic connection to.

Coincidental at all times — a purely perceptual and mental effect.

### Trigger-Conditioning — Post-Hypnotic Suggestion

Spheres: Mind 4. SF: 3. Difficulty: 4 (Rote). Casting pool: Mind Skill + Cunning, opposed.

The mage implants a post-hypnotic trigger in a target's mind — a hidden instruction that activates when a specified condition is met. The target resists with Willpower + Mind at Difficulty 3; if the mage's hits exceed the target's by 3 or more, the trigger is deeply embedded and can only be found and removed by another Mind 4 or higher Effect. When the trigger activates, the target performs the conditioned behaviour as if it were their own decision — unless they are in a situation clearly against their core values, in which case they may roll Willpower (Difficulty 5) to resist.

Coincidental if the session appears to be a therapeutic consultation. Vulgar when clearly performed as attack or coercion.

### Psychic Assault — Direct Mental Attack

Spheres: Mind 3 (pain and confusion); Mind 4 (targeted damage). SF: 3. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). or 3. Casting pool: Mind Skill + Cunning, opposed.

The mage attacks a target's mind directly — inflicting searing psychic pain, overloading the senses, inducing temporary madness, or directly damaging the target's mental Pattern. The target resists with Willpower + Mind (Difficulty 3 for Mind 3 variant; Difficulty 4 for Mind 4 assault). Each hit inflicts 1 level of Bashing damage from psychic shock (Mind 3) or 1 level of Lethal damage from actual Pattern damage (Mind 4). At 5 or more hits, the target must succeed on a Willpower roll (Difficulty 4) or suffer a temporary mental break lasting one scene.

Coincidental — invisible and undetectable without Prime or Mind perception. Vulgar only if the target visibly collapses in front of Sleepers who can attribute it to the mage.

### Overload — Sensory Shutdown

Spheres: Mind 3 / Forces 2. SF: 2. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). Casting pool: Mind Skill + Cunning.

The mage floods the target's senses and mind with overwhelming input — a blast of psychic white noise combined with a surge of raw Forces energy. The target resists with Stamina + Willpower (Difficulty 3). Each hit disables one of the target's primary senses for one scene and inflicts -2 dice to all actions as the nervous system struggles to compensate. At 5 or more hits, the target is effectively stunned — they can take no complex actions for (hits minus 4) turns.

Coincidental — no visible effect on the mage's side. Vulgar when the target visibly collapses or behaves erratically in circumstances that implicate the mage.

### Arts of Illusion — Sensory Deception

Spheres: Forces 2 (visual and auditory illusion); Mind 2 + Forces 2 (reinforced psychic illusion); Mind 3 / Forces 3 (total sensory immersion). SF: 2. Difficulty: 2 (Rote). or 2. Casting pool: Mind or Forces Skill + Cunning.

The mage creates sensory illusions — from simple visual tricks to comprehensive experiences that fool all five senses simultaneously. Forces 2 alone creates physical illusions (bent light, modified sound waves) that any observer can perceive. Mind 2 + Forces 2 creates illusions with a psychic reinforcement layer. Mind 3 / Forces 3 creates a total sensory environment indistinguishable from reality for the Duration. To disbelieve an illusion, the target rolls Intelligence + Awareness against Difficulty (3 + hits rolled by the mage) — a roll the Storyguide makes secretly.

Coincidental for simple light-bending and sound tricks. Vulgar when the illusion is obviously reality-defying or when the source is clearly identified.

### Conceptual Entropy — Eroding Ideas and Faith

Spheres: Entropy 4 / Mind 3. SF: 3. Difficulty: 3 (Rote). Casting pool: Entropy Skill + Cunning, extended opposed.

The mage attacks not the physical world but the conceptual one — eroding the structural integrity of a belief system, a plan, an ideology, or a mystical tradition. Applied against an individual, each hit reduces the target's confidence in a specified belief, lowering related Willpower rolls by 1 per hit for the Duration. Applied against a group or organisation (extended ritual targeting 10 to 20 hits), the effect can introduce systemic doubt that reduces group cohesion over weeks. Applied against a Paradigm, success totals of 50 or more can begin to shift local Consensus.

Coincidental when the doubt appears to arise naturally from events. Vulgar when the reality-eroding quality of the working is unmistakably supernatural.

---

These rotes represent named and mechanically specified techniques drawn from How Do You DO That?'s comprehensive treatment of Sphere magick. Additional applications — extended ranges, enhanced effects, combinatorial workings — follow the same underlying mechanics. The Storyguide has final authority over SF costs, Paradox generation, and Coincidental and Vulgar determinations for any application not specified above.

# Appendix O: Quick Reference — One-Page Cheat Sheets

Print these pages or keep the book open here. Everything a Storyguide and players need mid-session without hunting through chapters.

---

## Sheet 1 — Core Dice Rules

```
THE DICE POOL
-------------
BUILDING A POOL
  Pool = Attribute + Skill   |   Magic = Sphere Rating + Casting Attribute
  Enhancement dice: add to pool from Tags, Quintessence, Willpower, or circumstance.

ROLLING AND COUNTING HITS
  Roll all dice. Count dice showing 8 or higher.
  • 8 or 9 = 1 hit each.
  • 10   = 2 hits.

DIFFICULTY (0–5) — How many hits you need.
  0  Any hit succeeds (or automatic).    3  Hard — need 3 hits.
  1  Standard task.                      4  Formidable — need 4 hits.
  2  Challenging — need 2 hits.          5  Near-impossible — need 5 hits.

TRICK HITS — Hits scored ABOVE the Difficulty threshold.
  Spend Trick Hits on bonus effects: extra damage, wider area, longer duration, etc.
  Example: Difficulty 2 task, you roll 5 hits → 3 Trick Hits to spend.

COMPLICATIONS — Narrative disadvantages from failed or costly rolls.
  Tagged Complications (e.g. [Broken Arm]) impose -1 to -3 dice on relevant actions.

MOMENTUM — Shared resource (starts at 3).
  +1 Momentum when a player fails a roll.
  Spend 1 Momentum to: invoke a Tag (+2 dice), buy a bonus Minor Action, trigger a Trick,
  or assist an ally. Storyguide has a separate pool for NPCs and narrative twists.
```

---

## Sheet 2 — Magic & Casting

```
CASTING ROLL = Sphere Rating + Casting Attribute
------------------------------------------------
CASTING ATTRIBUTES BY SPHERE
  Correspondence  → Intellect or Cunning (navigation, spatial logic)
  Entropy         → Cunning or Intuition (pattern-reading, probability)
  Forces          → Cunning or Finesse (precise energy direction)
  Life            → Composure or Presence (biological empathy)
  Matter          → Intellect or Might (physical manipulation)
  Mind            → Resolve or Presence (mental projection and control)
  Prime           → Resolve or Intellect (raw magical awareness)
  Spirit          → Composure or Awareness (spiritual sensitivity)
  Time            → Cunning or Intellect (temporal reasoning)

ARETE (Traditions) / ENLIGHTENMENT (Technocracy) — A cap, NOT a dice pool. Range: 1–5.
  Max Sphere dots = your Arete/Enlightenment. Cannot raise a Sphere above it.
  Arete (Traditions) and Enlightenment (Technocracy) are the same stat — different name only.
  Arete/Enlightenment does NOT add dice to your casting pool.

QUINTESSENCE — Magical fuel. Max = Avatar + 3 (or Arete/Enlightenment + 1 without Avatar).
  Spend 1 Quintessence before rolling: +1 Enhancement die.
  Max spend per casting = Arete/Enlightenment ÷ 2 (round up).

ROTE vs. FREEFORM — WHICH TO USE?
  Rote: prepared working codified by the Tradition or Convention.
    Rote SF        = highest Sphere dot used − 1 (minimum SF 1).
    Rote Difficulty = highest Sphere dot used.
    Paradox: treat as Coincidental one step more often than freeform would be.
    Best for: reliability, avoiding Paradox, high-stress situations.
  Freeform: improvised working.
    Freeform SF        = sum of ALL Sphere dots used.
    Freeform Difficulty = highest Sphere dot + number of Spheres involved.
    Paradox: full Paradox risk, no category shift.
    Best for: novel effects, flexible response, high Arete or no matching Rote.

CASTING MODIFIERS (apply before rolling)
  Instruments (foci, tools, ritual space): +1 Enhancement.
  No instruments when foci required:       +1 Difficulty.
  Sympathetic link (hair, name, image):    −1 Difficulty.
  Willpower spend (once per roll):         +2 Enhancement.
  Quintessence spend (up to Arete÷2):      +1 Enhancement per dot.
  Rushing the casting (+1 Difficulty):     −1 SF (min SF 1).
  Duress (wounded, distracted, threatened): +1 Difficulty.
  Extended casting (ritual):               halve SF (round up), pool per round.

CASTING FORMULA EXAMPLES
  Forces ●●● + Correspondence ●● (two Spheres) as Freeform:
    Freeform SF = 3+2 = 5.  Difficulty = 3+2 = 5.  Pool = Forces 3 + Attribute.
  Same working as a Rote:
    Rote SF = 3−1 = 2.  Difficulty = 3.  Paradox shifted one step toward Coincidental.
  Single-Sphere: Life ●●● Freeform:
    SF = 3.  Difficulty = 3.  Pool = Life 3 + Attribute.

SUSTAINING ACTIVE EFFECTS
  Each effect you keep running: −1 die to ALL your other dice pools (stacks per effect).
  Dropping a sustained effect: free action on your Phase, no roll required.
  Taking damage while sustaining: Composure + Resolve roll, Difficulty = damage taken.
    Fail = effect drops; it does not misfire, it simply ends.
  Concentrating on 2+ sustained effects: each beyond the first adds −1 additional die.
  Sustained effects end automatically at end of scene unless explicitly extended.
  A Rote effect sustained past its listed SF requires a new roll at SF 1 to maintain.
```

---

## Sheet 3 — Paradox

```
PARADOX POOL & TRIGGERS
-----------------------
ADDING TO THE POOL
  Vulgar with Sleeper witnesses:    +2 dice per Sphere dot of highest Sphere used.
  Vulgar without witnesses:         +1 die per Sphere dot of highest Sphere used.
  Coincidental (any):               +0 dice. Pool unchanged.
  Rote advantage: shifts one step toward Coincidental.
    Vulgar w/ witnesses → Vulgar w/o witnesses (saves 1 die per dot).
    Vulgar w/o witnesses → Coincidental (saves Paradox entirely).
    Coincidental Rote → no Paradox at all.
  Botch (0 hits on casting roll): +1 die to pool regardless of category.
  Failed casting (hits < Difficulty, not a Botch): no Paradox, effect simply fails.

TRIGGERING THE ROLL
  Roll immediately when pool reaches 7+ dice (do not wait for end of scene).
  Roll at end of any scene in which pool has 1+ dice, even if under 7.
  Roll ALL pool dice. Every die face generates an effect simultaneously.
  After the roll: pool resets to 0 (all dice consumed by the Paradox event).

PARADOX DIE RESULTS (read EACH die individually — all results apply at once)
  1   — The Unraveling: last Vulgar effect this scene is partially undone.
          Multiple 1s: effect fully undone, AND one additional past Vulgar effect unravels.
  2   — Quiet Whisper: +1 box on the mage's Quiet Track.
  3   — Backlash Wound: mage suffers 1 Bashing damage (bypasses armor).
  4   — Sensory Bleed: vivid hallucination for the scene; −1 die to all actions taken.
  5   — Paradox Flare: mage's next casting this session is automatically Vulgar.
          Also adds +1 die to the freshly-reset pool immediately.
  6–7 — Backwash: current or most recent effect gains a twisted side-effect chosen by
          Storyguide. Each 6 or 7 result also removes 1 die from the pool (consumed/spent).
  8   — Paradox Drain: reality partially forgives — remove 2 dice from pool.
          If pool is already at 0 after the roll, this has no additional effect.
  9   — Spirit Touch: a Paradox Spirit is drawn and will manifest within 1–3 scenes.
          The mage is aware of its attention. It is not friendly.
  10  — Catastrophe (choose one at Storyguide discretion or player election):
          (a) 2 Aggravated damage to the mage.
          (b) Major Backlash — a significant narrative complication lasting a story arc.
          (c) +3 dice added to the freshly-reset pool (cascade risk).

QUIET TRACK (5 boxes total)
  1 box: Static — minor perceptual glitches, echoes, wrong-color shadows. No mechanical penalty.
  2 boxes: Distortion — −1 die on Empathy and Awareness rolls.
  3 boxes: Fracture — paradigm increasingly overrides reality; −1 die to all Social actions.
  4 boxes: Unraveling — −1 die to ALL actions; other characters begin questioning the mage's grip.
  5 boxes: Full Quiet — mage enters a Quiet episode. They are lost in their paradigm's reality.
             Storyguide controls NPC reactions; mage player narrates from inside the delusion.

CLEARING THE PARADOX POOL
  Meditating a full scene at a Node or Sanctum:     −1 die from pool.
  One full week of mundane, non-magical living:      −1 die from pool.
  Cleansing Ritual (Prime ●●, 1+ hours, mage aide):  −2 dice per successful ritual.
  Deliberate Expenditure:  voluntarily trigger the roll now; accept all results; pool → 0.
    Use when pool is high and a controlled burn is safer than an uncontrolled cascade.
  Quiet episode resolution:  Storyguide may reduce pool by 1–3 dice upon story completion.

CLEARING THE QUIET TRACK
  One month of normal life between stories: −1 box.
  Successful therapy or community healing ritual: −1 box per month of in-game time.
  Full Quiet episode resolves cleanly: −2 boxes (Storyguide discretion).
```

---

## Sheet 4 — Coincidental vs. Vulgar Decision Tree

```
IS THIS EFFECT COINCIDENTAL OR VULGAR?
--------------------------------------
Ask these questions in order. Stop at the first YES.

1. Could a reasonable Sleeper explain this as a lucky coincidence or natural event?
   YES → Coincidental. +0 Paradox dice.
   NO  → Continue to question 2.

2. Is the effect physically impossible by consensus reality standards?
   (Teleportation, visible energy bolts, levitation, resurrection, impossible healing...)
   YES → Vulgar. Continue to question 3.
   NO  → Coincidental.

3. Are Sleepers present who can directly observe the impossible effect?
   YES → Vulgar with witnesses. +2 dice to Paradox Pool.
   NO  → Vulgar without witnesses. +1 die to Paradox Pool.

BORDERLINE CASES (Storyguide call):
  "Improbable but deniable"      → Coincidental (cover story possible afterward).
  "Coincidental with a stretch"  → Coincidental, +1 Paradox if overdone this scene.
  "No cover story possible"      → Vulgar.

ROTE BONUS: shifts the boundary one step favorable.
  Vulgar with witnesses  → treat as Vulgar without witnesses.
  Vulgar no witnesses    → treat as Coincidental.
  Coincidental           → remains Coincidental (no additional benefit).
```

---

## Sheet 5 — Combat Quick Reference

```
SPOTLIGHT SYSTEM (DEFAULT) — USE THIS FIRST
-------------------------------------------
ROUND STRUCTURE
  1. Initiative: roll Athletics + Cunning at scene start.
  2. Highest Initiative takes first Spotlight, then descending order.
  3. Your Spotlight: one Major Action + any Minor Actions (speak, draw, step 1 pace).
  4. After all Spotlights: new round, same Initiative order.

ATTACK SEQUENCE
  Declare action. Roll Attribute + Skill (or Sphere + Attribute for magic).
  Hits ≥ Difficulty → attack lands. Excess hits = Trick Hits.
  Damage = base damage + Trick Hits spent on damage.
  Subtract target Armor. Mark remaining damage on Health track.

DEFENSE OPTIONS (your Spotlight choice)
  Active Dodge (Major Action): Athletics + Dexterity; each hit = +1 Difficulty to all attacks vs. you this round.
  Parry (Minor Action, melee only): Close Combat + Dexterity; each hit cancels 1 incoming hit.
  Cover (Minor Action, no roll): Partial +1 Difficulty, Heavy +2, Full = cannot be targeted.

DAMAGE TYPES
  Bashing (B)   — fists, blunt. Heals fastest.
  Lethal (L)    — blades, bullets, fire. Heals slower.
  Aggravated (A) — pattern attacks, some magic. Heals slowest; bypasses mundane Armor.

HEALTH TRACK — Band Structure (7 + Stamina boxes total)
  BRUISED   2+Stamina boxes | No penalty
  IMPAIRED  2 boxes         | −1 die to ALL rolls
  CRITICAL  2 boxes         | −2 dice to ALL rolls
  INCAP     1 box           | Cannot act

  Stamina 3 (10 boxes):  [B][B][B][B][B]  |  [I][I]  |  [C][C]  |  [X]
  Stamina 5 (12 boxes):  [B][B][B][B][B][B][B]  |  [I][I]  |  [C][C]  |  [X]
  Damage type in boxes: B=Bashing  L=Lethal  A=Aggravated (bypasses Armor)

COMMON TRICK HITS (1 Trick Hit each unless noted)
  Extra Damage   Disarm   Knockdown   Stagger (lose Minor Action)
  Wound (apply condition)   Setup (+1 die to ally's next roll)   Expose (lower cover)

ARMOR  (reduces Bashing and Lethal; does NOT reduce Aggravated)
  Light (+1 Armor)  Medium (+2, -1 Athletics/Stealth)  Heavy (+3, -2 Athletics/Stealth)
```

```
PHASE INITIATIVE (OPTIONAL — see Appendix H)
--------------------------------------------
WHEN TO USE: 6+ participants, or when spell-timing matters dramatically.

SPEED FACTORS (SF) — Higher = Slower to resolve
  SF 1 — Fast: unarmed strike, pistol single shot, light blade, simple movement,
          Forces 1 cantrip, drawing a weapon.
  SF 2 — Moderate: two-handed melee, rifle single shot, burst fire, climbing,
          Freeform spell totalling 2 dots (e.g. Forces 2 only); SF = 2. Rote: highest Sphere 3.
          Rote using highest Sphere 2 (e.g. Forces 2 Rote): SF 1 (2−1=1 = matches pistol).
  SF 3 — Slow: full auto fire, bolt-action reload+fire, heavy power attack,
          Freeform: Forces 3 alone = SF 3. Rote: highest Sphere 4 (4−1=3).
  SF 4+ — Very Slow: anti-materiel rifle, multi-Sphere freeform,
          e.g. Forces 3 + Corr 2 Freeform = SF 5 (3+2). Rote highest Sphere 5 (5−1=4).

SF WINDOW: Cast at Phase T resolves at Phase T+SF.
  ALL Action Dice that fall between Phase T and Phase T+SF (inclusive) are consumed.
  Example: SF 6, cast at Phase 1. Dice at Phases 1, 5, 6, 7 all consumed.
  (Phases 2,3,4 had no dice; 7 = 1+6.) You lose four dice, not six.

SF LOOKUP (Freeform = sum of dots; Rote = highest dot − 1, min 1)
  Single sphere: ●=SF1/2  ●●=SF2/3  ●●●=SF3/4  ●●●●=SF4/5  ●●●●●=SF5/6
  Two spheres (e.g. Frc3+Cor2): Rote=SF3  Freeform=SF5 (3+2)
  Three spheres (e.g. Frc3+Cor2+Lif2): Rote=SF3  Freeform=SF7 (3+2+2)

ROUND STRUCTURE
  1. DECLARE: everyone states their action simultaneously (before anyone acts).
  2. SF 1 resolves first (in Initiative order within the phase).
  3. SF 2 resolves next.
  4. SF 3 resolves. SF 4+ last.
  5. End of Round: sustained effects tick; new Round begins.

KEY RULE: If you are HIT while your action is still pending (higher SF),
  make a Concentration check (Composure + Resolve vs. attacker's hits).
  FAIL: your action collapses. A spell loses its Quintessence and adds
  reduced Paradox (1 die instead of full amount).
  PASS: your action completes normally despite being hit.

ROTE SF     = highest Sphere dot rating used (faster than freeform).
FREEFORM SF = sum of ALL Sphere dots used (slower than Rote SF; more Paradox risk).
```

# Appendix P: Character Sheet

## Overview

Appendix P contains a full two-page fillable character sheet formatted for Mage: The Ascension — Storypath Ultra Edition. The sheet includes fields for all Attributes, Skills, Paths, Spheres, Health Track (four bands: Bruised, Injured, Wounded, Incapacitated), Quiet Track (three bands: Stressed, Fractured, Broken), Paradox Pool, Arete/Enlightenment rating, Quintessence/Banality, Avatar rating, Paradigm/Practice/Instruments, XP log, and a dedicated section for Wonders and Focus Tags. The visual design uses bubble-dot notation for all dot-rated traits.

> **Agent note:** This appendix is a graphical layout artifact. Rule definitions for all traits on this sheet appear in their respective chapters. For Attributes and Skills see Chapter 3 (Character Creation); for Health Track mechanics see Chapter 7 (Combat); for Quiet Track see Appendix K; for Paradox Pool see Chapter 5 (Paradox); for Arete/Enlightenment see Chapter 3.

# Appendix Q: Quick Reference Card

## Overview

Appendix Q is a single-page condensed reference card intended for printing and use at the table. It covers: the core dice pool formula, hit thresholds and difficulty ratings, the botch condition, the Spotlight Initiative sequence, the five basic combat actions, Damage Type effects (Bashing/Lethal/Aggravated/Unsoakable), the Paradox trigger table, and the Sphere Difficulty ladder (Coincidental through Vulgar+Witness). All values are identical to those in Appendices F and O. See Appendix O (Text Quick Reference Sheets) for the full machine-readable version of these rules.

---

# Appendix R: Items and Gear

---

DESIGNER NOTE — BALANCE AND INTENT
=====================================

Every item in this appendix was built with the same underlying design philosophy: tags must cut both ways. An item that can only be invoked — never compelled — is a mechanical advantage with no dramatic cost, and that is bad game design. Every Enhancement an item grants should be matchable by a complication the Storyguide can introduce.

When reading item stat blocks, look at the compel line. If a tag's compel opportunity feels forced or trivial, that tag is doing less narrative work than it should. Players who understand this will choose items for their story implications as much as for their mechanical benefits — and those players will have better sessions.

A few specific principles baked into this appendix:

MUNDANE ITEMS ARE NOT SAFE. A [Concealable] firearm can be drawn unseen — and can be found in a pat-down. A [Long Range] sniper rifle extends reach dramatically — and takes a full Minor Action to set up, during which the sniper is a sitting target. Every tag has a cost hiding behind the benefit.

MAGICAL ITEMS GENERATE STORY. A Talisman with [Enchanted] produces no Paradox for the user to wield, but Paradox Spirits notice it. A Wonder with [Resonant (Verbena)] will whisper to Dreamspeakers and attract Garou attention. Magical items are not simply better mundane items — they are narrative hooks with extra steps.

BACKGROUND COST IS REAL COST. An item rated ●●●●● costs 13–15 Background points. Those are points not going into Contacts, Resources, Allies, or a Node. High-rated Wonders should feel worth that cost in play — not just in theory.

BALANCE IS CHRONICLE-LOCAL. Some items here (the Alanson R-25 Hardsuit, the Portable Hellgate, the Madness Grenade) are deliberately at the edge of what works in a normal chronicle. Before adding them to a game, ask whether the story you want to tell benefits from that level of power. The answer is sometimes yes. The item's compels are designed to create as much dramatic weight as its invokes. Ensure your table is comfortable with both before play.

For custom items built using the Crafting rules in Appendix S: the same principle applies. If you cannot write a compelling Compel for a tag you are adding, remove the tag.

---

The world of Mage: The Ascension is a world saturated with things — mundane objects that carry weight, history, and danger, and magical Wonders that reshape reality for those who know how to use them. This appendix catalogs both, organized into two broad sections: Mundane Items, which any character might carry, and Magical Items, Wonders crafted by Awakened minds and imbued with persistent supernatural power.

All items in this appendix are defined using the Storypath Ultra tag system. Tags are not decorative labels; they are mechanical and narrative properties that can be invoked for Enhancement or compelled by the Storyguide to create complications. Every tag listed here is defined in full in Appendix J: Tags Reference, including the new tags introduced specifically for this conversion. When an item carries a tag not previously in the Tags appendix, it has been added there as well — the two appendices are designed to be read together.

A note on item dot ratings: Magical items carry a dot rating (● to •••••) that reflects their Background point cost and overall power. A ● item costs 1–3 Background points and can be acquired fairly easily; a ••••• item costs 13–15 Background points and is a legendary artifact that may be unique in the world. Dot ratings are listed in each item's header for ease of reference.

Storytelling note: Items should generate stories. A mundane sniper rifle is not just a bundle of tags — it has a history, a previous owner, perhaps a name. A Talisman is not just a Quintessence battery — it may have Resonance that conflicts with the mage's own, or attract unwanted attention from other Awakened. The Storyguide is encouraged to add Tags to any item beyond those listed, reflecting the specific history of that particular object in this particular chronicle.

## Items and the 4-Band Health System

All items in this appendix deal damage that fills a character's 4-band Health Track — Bruised (boxes 1–3), Impaired (boxes 4–6), Critical (boxes 7–9), and Incapacitated (box 10). Understanding how weapon damage tags, Armor Soak, and the Piercing mechanic interact with this track is essential for correct play. The two key principles are: Soak reduces how fast the track fills, and band penalties apply based on total boxes filled regardless of how they were accumulated.

### How Soak and Damage Fill the Track

Every successful attack that clears Soak deals its remaining hits as boxes of health damage. Armor Soak is subtracted first — a Soak 3 vest absorbs 3 hits per attack before any reach the health track. If an attack deals 5 hits and armor Soaks 3, only 2 boxes are marked. As boxes accumulate, the character advances through bands and gains escalating penalties: –1 die in the Impaired band, –2 dice in the Critical band, and full incapacitation at 10 boxes. Healing reverses this by removing boxes, potentially restoring a lower band.

### Piercing — Armor Degradation, Not Band Bypassing

The Piercing tag does not skip health boxes or bypass the band system. Instead, it degrades the armor itself. Each successful hit from a Piercing weapon strips 1 Armor Point (AP) from the target's armor, reducing its Soak for all future attacks in that scene. A combat knife (Piercing) against a Kevlar vest (Soak 2, Sturdy 8 AP) begins stripping AP: after 8 Piercing hits connect, the vest's Sturdy track is exhausted and its Soak drops to 0. From that point, every subsequent attack from any source deals full damage to the health track.

Narrate Piercing as visible armor degradation: first small cuts, then deep gashes, then the knife finding flesh through ruined fabric. The Piercing mechanic rewards sustained engagement against armored opponents — patience and consistency matter more than raw power.

### Damage Type Tags and Band-Filling Efficiency

Armor must carry the same Damage Type tag as the attack for full Soak to apply. When there is a mismatch, Soak does not apply and the full attack damage goes directly to health boxes — potentially advancing the character through bands in a single action. This makes damage type matching one of the most consequential tactical decisions in the game.

- A pistol (Ballistic Damage) against leather motorcycle leathers (no Ballistic tag): no Soak. A 4-hit attack fills 4 health boxes directly, pushing an uninjured character deep into the Bruised band and toward Impaired in one action.
- The same pistol against a Kevlar vest (Ballistic tag, Soak 2): only 2 boxes filled. The character stays in the Bruised band and retains full dice pools.
- A fire breath effect (Elemental Damage) against military body armor (no Elemental tag): Soak 4 is irrelevant. The full attack fills health boxes as if the character were unarmored.
The health band system amplifies mismatched damage types: the faster a character fills boxes, the faster they reach Impaired and Critical, and each band threshold worsens their ability to respond. A mage who knows a target's armor type and chooses mismatched damage types can advance an opponent through bands dramatically faster than raw power would suggest.

### Status Conditions and Band Synergies

| Weapon Tag | Health Band Interaction |
| --- | --- |
| Piercing | Degrades armor AP rather than filling health boxes directly. Effective over multiple sustained hits; armor's Sturdy track determines how long until full exposure. |
| Deadly | On a successful attack, triggers the Aggravated Wound option: damage boxes filled by this attack cannot be healed in the same scene. Forces opponent to carry accumulated band penalties into subsequent scenes. |
| Stunning | Spend 2 hits to inflict the Stunned status condition regardless of current health band. Target loses next action die. Does not fill health boxes. |
| Brutal | Wound descriptions are severe and visible. Characters in the Impaired band from a Brutal source must make a Composure check or become Rattled — a social/psychological penalty that compounds the mechanical band penalty. |
| Wounding | Spend 1 hit to inflict Bleeding status: 1 additional health box per round until treated. Bleeding advances band position between actions, making it especially dangerous in the Critical band. |

### Tag Count Guidelines — Avoiding Overload

Items in this appendix carry between zero and four tags depending on quality and rank. The following soft caps apply — exceeding them is possible for exceptional items, but each tag above the guideline should be justified by a corresponding compel opportunity. Tags that cannot be compelled are padding.

| Item Type | Typical Tag Count  \|  Maximum |
| --- | --- |
| Mundane item (common) | 0–2 tags  \|  2 max |
| Mundane item (exceptional/Master Work) | 2–3 tags  \|  3 max |
| Wonder — Mundane rank | 1–2 tags  \|  2 max (within rank total of 3) |
| Wonder — Cutting-Edge rank | 2–3 tags  \|  3 max (within rank total of 5) |
| Wonder — Groundbreaking rank | 3–4 tags  \|  4 max (within rank total of 7) |

Some items in this appendix (notably the Alanson R-25 Hardsuit) carry more tags than these guidelines suggest. Those items are intentionally designed at the high end of their rank and carry correspondingly more compel opportunities. The Hardsuit's seven tags are balanced by seven narrative hooks the Storyguide can use against the operator. For custom items in your chronicle: if an item's tags cannot all be compelled at dramatically interesting moments, remove the ones that cannot.

Cross-reference: For detailed guidance on tag pricing and scope, including the "double-cost tag" rule for tags covering two distinct skill categories, see Appendix J: Tags Reference — "Tag Design and Pricing — Getting the Scope Right."

## Mundane Items

Mundane items are the equipment of the unAwakened world — firearms, blades, protective clothing, tools of trade and survival. They carry no magical charge and generate no Paradox. They are governed entirely by the SPU tag system: each item provides Enhancement to relevant actions and carries tags that define its specific capabilities and limitations.

A mundane item's tags were determined when it was designed and manufactured. A mage can carry a pistol without any magical implication; it fires bullets through wholly physical means. The interesting question is what happens when a mage combines a mundane item with magic — a topic addressed in the Crafting appendix (Appendix S). For now, these items stand on their own mechanical merits.

### Mundane Weapons

All weapons listed here provide +1 Enhancement to attack actions unless otherwise noted. Speed Factor values given are for Phase Initiative (see Appendix F). Damage type tags indicate what kind of armor is effective against each weapon — armor must carry the matching tag for its Soak to apply; otherwise damage goes directly to the character's injury track.

```
COMBAT KNIFE  •  Melee Weapon  •  SF 0
--------------------------------------
Enhancement: +1 to attack actions
Tags: [Concealable], [Piercing], [Edged Damage]

A standard fixed-blade fighting knife — military surplus, hunting knife, or custom-made. Small enough to conceal under a jacket, long enough to do serious work. The Piercing tag means successful attacks strip 1 Armor Point from the target's armor directly, bypassing Soak. Against light armor, a knife wielder who lands consistently will eventually open the target up.

Invoke: "My knife is Concealable — I drew it before the search found it." / "Piercing — I drive the blade between his ribs, stripping 1 AP from his vest."
Compel: "The knife is Concealable because it is small. At anything beyond arm's reach, it is useless."
Mage context: Many Tradition mages carry ritual knives — Verbena athames, Hermetic daggers — that may additionally carry the Arcane Attuned or Resonant tag. A mundane blade gains those properties through the Crafting system.
```

```
MACHETE / SHORT SWORD  •  Melee Weapon  •  SF 1
-----------------------------------------------
Enhancement: +1 to attack actions
Tags: [Brutal], [Edged Damage]

A heavy single-edged blade designed for chopping. The Brutal tag means the wounds are visibly dramatic — deep cuts, impressive blood loss, the kind of injury that breaks morale. Functionally adequate in combat and universally available without special permits in most jurisdictions.

Invoke: "Brutal wound — I invoke it to terrify the remaining guards. They hesitate."
Compel: "A machete in the middle of a fancy restaurant. Security is already calling the police."
```

```
SPEAR / STAFF  •  Melee Weapon  •  SF 1
---------------------------------------
Enhancement: +1 to attack actions
Tags: [Reach], [Versatile], [Blunt/Stabbing Damage (choose one at time of use)]

A pole weapon: spear for thrusting (Stabbing Damage), staff for striking (Blunt Damage). The Reach tag lets the wielder attack from slightly beyond normal melee distance and hold opponents at bay — anyone trying to close with a spear-wielder must either stay at the weapon's range or spend an action die forcing their way inside it. Versatile means the weapon works as both a ranged threat (hold distance) and a close threat (reverse grip).

Invoke: "Reach — I keep him from closing. He has to spend a die getting past the spear."
Compel: "A staff is seven feet long. You cannot bring it into the car or through the doorway."
Mage context: The Verbena favor the staff as a ritual implement. An Awakened staff often carries Resonant (Verbena), Arcane Attuned, and possibly Self-Repairing (if grown from a living branch).
```

```
BASEBALL BAT / CLUB  •  Melee Weapon  •  SF 1
---------------------------------------------
Enhancement: +1 to attack actions
Tags: [Brutal], [Stunning], [Blunt Damage]

The workhorse improvised-or-dedicated bludgeon. Brutal produces dramatic, visible wounds. Stunning gives access to the Stun Trick: for 2 hits, the target gains the Stunned condition. A stunned target loses their next action die entirely — the bat can functionally take a person out of one phase of combat entirely if the attack lands with sufficient hits. Against heavily armored targets, blunt damage may be resisted — check Appendix G for armor-specific vulnerabilities.

Invoke: "Stunning — 2 hits to activate. He is Stunned, loses his next die."
Compel: "Blunt Damage — his Kevlar vest is rated for this. Soak applies in full."
```

```
PISTOL (9mm / .45 ACP)  •  Ranged Weapon  •  SF 1
-------------------------------------------------
Enhancement: +1 to attack actions
Tags: [Concealable], [Ballistic Damage]

The baseline ranged weapon against which all mage Rote casting speed is calibrated. A pistol can hit targets up to medium range without any tag required (standard SPU ranged weapon default). It is concealable under a jacket and requires no special license to obtain in most modern settings, though the legality of carrying varies by jurisdiction.

Against armor: Ballistic Damage means the armor must carry the Ballistic tag for full Soak to apply. Unrated cloth armor (Flexible, Natural) applies only partial resistance; ballistic vests (Soak 2–3, Ballistic tag) fully apply their Soak.

Speed Factor 1 means the pistol fires at the same Phase as Forces ●● Rotes and faster than most improvised magic. This is intentional — mundane violence is fast, and magic must earn its speed through Rote practice.

Invoke: "Concealable — I had the pistol under my jacket at the checkpoint. They missed it."
Compel: "A pistol at long range — you need to close before this is effective."
```

```
REVOLVER (.357 / .44 Magnum)  •  Ranged Weapon  •  SF 1
-------------------------------------------------------
Enhancement: +1 to attack actions
Tags: [Concealable], [Brutal], [Ballistic Damage]

Heavier caliber than a standard pistol. The Brutal tag reflects the stopping power — wounds from large-caliber revolver rounds are dramatic and serious. The cylinder mechanism means no magazine to jam, though reloading is slower than a semi-automatic pistol.
```
