```
Invoke: "Brutal — the wound is severe. The target is shaken even if they have armor."
Compel: "Brutal and Ballistic — you fired in a crowd. The round went through the target and kept going."
```

```
SHOTGUN (Pump-Action)  •  Ranged Weapon  •  SF 1
------------------------------------------------
Enhancement: +1 to attack actions
Tags: [Brutal], [Blunt/Piercing Damage (buckshot vs. slug)]

Devastating at close range, ineffective at long range. A shotgun without the Long Range tag cannot effectively engage targets beyond close-to-medium range. Against targets in that range, the Brutal tag triggers for significant wound description and morale implications. Buckshot uses Blunt Damage (spreads impact); slugs use Piercing Damage (single concentrated mass, strips AP).

Invoke: "Brutal — at this range, the shotgun blast throws him off his feet."
Compel: "No Long Range tag. The target is beyond medium distance. The spread is useless at that range."
```

```
ASSAULT RIFLE (M4 / AK Pattern)  •  Ranged Weapon  •  SF 1 (single) / SF 2 (burst) / SF 3 (full auto)
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Enhancement: +1 to attack actions (single); +2 (burst); +4 (full auto)
Tags: [Automatic], [Long Range], [Ballistic Damage]

The standard military and paramilitary longarm. Single-fire SF 1 is identical to a pistol but reaches long range. Burst Fire (3–5 rounds) costs current action die plus next die, adds +2 Enhancement, and can target the same enemy or split across adjacent targets. Full Auto (10+ rounds) costs current and next two dice, adds +4 Enhancement, and creates a zone effect — all targets in the zone defend separately.

Not concealable. Not subtle. An assault rifle in an urban environment immediately establishes the situation as Vulgar for any magical actions taken in that scene — the Consensus is already being violated by the presence of visible military hardware.

Invoke (burst): "Burst Fire — I take the shot with +2 Enhancement to hit."
Invoke (full auto): "Full Auto — everyone in the hallway defends. +4 dice."
Compel: "Automatic means 30 rounds per magazine. You have fired 18. You have 12 left."
```

```
SNIPER RIFLE  •  Ranged Weapon  •  SF 2 (setup required)
--------------------------------------------------------
Enhancement: +2 to attack actions (specialized: only for buying off difficulty or damage Tricks)
Tags: [Long Range], [Deadly], [Piercing], [Ballistic Damage]

Precision weapon system — requires prone positioning, range estimation, wind calculation, and patience. The Enhanced +2 is specialized (see SPU expansion rules): it can only be used to buy off attack difficulty or to purchase damage-dealing Tricks. The Deadly tag means successful attacks can apply the Aggravated Wound status instead of normal damage — this represents the catastrophic physical trauma of a direct hit from a high-caliber round. Piercing strips 1 AP from armor in addition.

Setup requires at least 1 full Phase of preparation (treat as a committed action). Without setup, treat as a basic Long Range weapon at +1 Enhancement only.

Invoke: "Deadly — I trigger Aggravated Wound. The wound is incompatible with continued function."
Invoke: "Long Range — at 600 meters, I am outside any response their team can mount."
Compel: "You need a Phase to set up. In this ambush, you do not have that Phase."
Mage context: Sons of Ether operatives often enchant precision rifles with Matter ●● (enhanced tolerances, never misfires) and Correspondence ●● (ignore wind and atmospheric refraction). These become Invented weapons with additional tags.
```

```
TASER / STUN GUN  •  Ranged or Melee Weapon  •  SF 1
----------------------------------------------------
Enhancement: +1 to attack actions
Tags: [Concealable], [Stunning], [Versatile]

A conducted-energy weapon that disrupts muscle control. The Stunning tag gives access to the Stun Trick (2 hits: inflict Stunned). The Versatile tag means the taser works in melee (direct contact probe) or short range (barb-and-wire). Concealable — it fits in a pocket or holster without obvious profile. Against targets wearing insulated armor (rubber underlayer, certain Technocratic suits), the Storyguide may rule the Stunning effect is reduced or negated.

Invoke: "Stunning — 2 hits, he is Stunned. He loses his next action die entirely."
Compel: "You Stunned him, but you are now out of charge. The taser needs time to cycle."
```

```
CROSSBOW  •  Ranged Weapon  •  SF 2 (reload between shots)
----------------------------------------------------------
Enhancement: +1 to attack actions
Tags: [Silent], [Piercing], [Ballistic Damage]

A mechanically-loaded bolt-thrower. Silent means complications involving noise are ignored — a crossbow fired in a library does not trigger security sensors; a crossbow fired from a rooftop does not alert the target's colleagues below. Piercing strips 1 AP on a successful hit. The tradeoff: SF 2 includes the draw and lock cycle. After firing, the wielder must spend a full Phase reloading before firing again.

Invoke: "Silent — no one in the adjacent room heard the shot."
Compel: "You fired once. Reloading costs a full Phase. He is still standing and now he knows where you are."
Mage context: Verbena and Dreamspeaker mages occasionally use crossbows enchanted with Spirit-tipped bolts (Wounding against spirits) or Life-cursed bolts (Poisoned, delivering magical toxins). These become Talismans or Trinkets with additional tags.
```

```
THROWING KNIVES / SHURIKEN  •  Ranged/Melee Weapon  •  SF 1
-----------------------------------------------------------
Enhancement: +1 to attack actions
Tags: [Concealable], [Thrown], [Returning], [Edged Damage]

Small blades designed for throwing. The Returning tag applies when a mage has enchanted them with Correspondence ●● or Matter ●● to recall to the hand — mundane throwing knives do not return; mark the Returning tag as inactive unless enchanted. Concealable — a set of three fits in a jacket pocket or sleeve sheath. Used in melee, they function as combat knives. Thrown, they function as ranged weapons up to medium range.

Invoke: "Thrown — I put the knife in the guard's shoulder from here."
Compel: "Thrown means you now only have two knives. You need to retrieve the first or run low."
```

### Mundane Armor

Armor ratings are given as Soak (flat damage reduction) and Sturdy (Armor Points, the structural integrity pool that degrades under Enhanced and Armor-Piercing attacks). See Appendix G for the full armor degradation and repair system.

```
HEAVY CLOTHING / LEATHER JACKET  •  Light Armor
-----------------------------------------------
Soak 1  |  Sturdy 4  |  Initiative Penalty: None
Tags: [Flexible], [Natural], [Concealable]

A thick leather jacket, heavy denim, layered wool — mundane materials that absorb minor impacts and slow slashing weapons. Soak 1 is modest but real. The Flexible tag means it imposes no initiative penalty and works with acrobatics, sprinting, and stealth. Concealable because it looks like clothing, not armor.

Against Ballistic Damage: the Leather Jacket does not carry the Ballistic tag, so its Soak does not apply to firearm rounds. A bullet hits for full damage regardless of the jacket.

Invoke: "Flexible — my acrobatics roll is unimpeded. Enhancement on the tumble."
Compel: "Natural, Flexible — the blade slides along the leather and into the gap at the armpit."
Mage context: Verbena mages often wear cured leather armor made from ritually hunted animals. A Verbena hide jacket frequently carries Resonant (Verbena) and Natural in addition to base tags.
```

```
KEVLAR VEST (CONCEALABLE)  •  Medium Armor
------------------------------------------
Soak 2  |  Sturdy 8  |  Initiative Penalty: None
Tags: [Ballistic], [Concealable], [Rigid]

The standard concealed body armor of law enforcement and security professionals. The Ballistic tag means its Soak of 2 fully applies against Ballistic Damage attacks (pistol and rifle rounds). Against Edged Damage (blades), Rigid means the plate resists slashing but a dedicated thrust may exploit gaps — Storyguide may reduce effective Soak to 1 against targeted stabs. Concealable under a dress shirt or light jacket.

AP Degradation: Each Enhanced hit that exceeds Soak strips 1 AP; each Armor-Piercing attack strips 2 AP. A Kevlar vest subjected to repeated rifle fire across a combat scene will visibly degrade through the Dented → Compromised → Failing track.

Invoke: "Ballistic vest — invoke against the pistol round. Full Soak 2 applies."
Compel: "Concealed, which means it does not cover your arms or neck. That strike targeted your shoulder."
```

```
POLICE RIOT GEAR  •  Heavy Armor
--------------------------------
Soak 3  |  Sturdy 12  |  Initiative Penalty: –1 die
Tags: [Ballistic], [Rigid], [Heavy], [Imposing]

Full-body hard-shell protective equipment: helmet, chest plate, arm guards, leg guards. Soak 3 applies against Ballistic Damage, Edged Damage, and Blunt Damage (the Rigid tag means this armor resists all contact impacts). Heavy imposes –1 die to the Initiative Pool — the suit is cumbersome. Imposing means the wearer can invoke it for intimidation and authority displays; riot gear conveys institutional power.

Invoke: "Imposing — I invoke the riot armor. Enhancement on my attempt to make them disperse."
Compel: "Heavy means –1 initiative die. In the chase, you fall behind."
```

```
MILITARY BODY ARMOR (PLATE CARRIER + INSERTS)  •  Heavy Armor
-------------------------------------------------------------
Soak 4  |  Sturdy 16  |  Initiative Penalty: –2 dice
Tags: [Ballistic], [Rigid], [Heavy], [Reinforced], [Imposing]

The full tactical kit: front and back SAPI plates, side plates, ballistic helmet, and extremity protection. Soak 4 is exceptional for mundane protection. Reinforced means Armor-Piercing attacks strip only 1 AP instead of 2. Heavy at –2 dice is significant — characters in full plate carrier load-out are noticeably slower and cannot sprint as effectively. Paired with Imposing for authority projection.

Against magical attacks: Without the Warded tag, Soak does not apply to magical effects. A Forces ●● bolt from a mage bypasses all 4 Soak.

Invoke: "Reinforced — the AP round strips 1 AP, not 2. My plates are rated for this."
Compel: "–2 initiative dice. You are armored for a firefight, not a footrace."
```

```
MOTORCYCLE LEATHERS  •  Medium Armor
------------------------------------
Soak 2  |  Sturdy 6  |  Initiative Penalty: None
Tags: [Flexible], [Natural], [Rigid (partial)]

Purpose-built riding gear: thick cowhide with CE-rated armor inserts at joints. Flexible enough for full movement; the Rigid partial tag means the impact plates resist blunt trauma. The Natural tag makes it appropriate to Verbena and Dreamspeaker Resonance contexts. Against firearms, no Ballistic tag — the leather does not stop bullets.

Invoke: "Natural material — invoke in the forest context. Resonance-compatible with my Life working."
Compel: "You fell off the bike at 60mph. Even Flexible armor has its limits."
```

### Mundane Tools and Equipment

Tools in SPU provide Enhancement to specific actions and carry tags that further define their utility. Unlike weapons and armor, tools are rarely confrontational — they help characters do the things that are not fighting. The Storyguide should allow players to invoke tool tags creatively; a lockpick set might invoke Precise in a context where care and delicacy matter, even if the original design was for locks.

```
LOCKPICK SET  •  Tool
---------------------
Enhancement: +1 to Larceny, Security (physical locks)
Tags: [Concealable], [Exceptional]

A full set of picks, tension wrenches, and rakes in a leather fold wallet. Exceptional pushes the Enhancement to +2 for high-quality sets. Concealable — the wallet fits in a jacket pocket. Does not apply to electronic locks (see Hacking Kit).

Invoke: "Exceptional quality picks — +2 Enhancement on the tumbler lock."
Compel: "You have picks, not a bypass tool. This is an electronic keypad lock."
```

```
FIELD MEDICINE KIT  •  Tool
---------------------------
Enhancement: +1 to Medicine, First Aid
Tags: [Expendable], [Rugged]

A well-stocked trauma kit: pressure bandages, hemostatic gauze, tourniquets, airway management tools, IV supplies. Expendable means if it is ever lost or destroyed, the character recovers a replacement by the next scene without a roll (they just buy another). Rugged reduces relevant Complications by one step — field surgery under fire is already penalized; the Rugged kit means the penalties are less severe.

Invoke: "Rugged kit — reduce the Complication from operating under fire by 1 step."
Compel: "Expendable also means you are running low. This kit has treated three wounds. What is left?"
```

```
FORENSICS KIT  •  Tool
----------------------
Enhancement: +1 to Investigation, Science (crime scene analysis)
Tags: [Multiuse], [Exceptional]

Latent fingerprint powder and lifters, luminol, evidence bags, UV light, swabs, chemical reagent cards. Multiuse: the kit can be used for investigation at a crime scene or repurposed as a chemistry tool for identifying unknown substances (choose the second use; it is just as valid). Exceptional: +2 Enhancement when working a proper crime scene with adequate time.

Invoke: "Multiuse — I use the reagent cards to analyze what this substance is. Same Enhancement."
Compel: "You left luminol and fingerprint powder all over the crime scene. Investigators will know someone searched this."
```

```
LAPTOP COMPUTER  •  Tool
------------------------
Enhancement: +2 to Computer, Data, Research, Communication
Tags: [Exceptional], [Multiuse], [Fast]

A modern professional laptop: fast processor, solid-state drive, full wireless connectivity. Enhancement +2 reflects the genuine advantage a modern computer provides over working without one. Multiuse to an extraordinary degree — the same laptop handles hacking, financial research, communications, data analysis, and digital investigation. Fast: while not a vehicle tag, Fast here means the system processes queries rapidly, reducing time-based Complications on research actions.

Invoke: "Exceptional hardware — +2 Enhancement on the data analysis. This machine is fast."
Compel: "You need internet access for this. In this basement, you have no signal."
Mage context: Virtual Adept and Iteration X laptops are almost never mundane. A VA mage's laptop typically carries Enchanted, Technological, and possibly Arcane Attuned, with a direct interface to the Digital Web via Data ●●●.
```

```
OCCULT LIBRARY  •  Tool
-----------------------
Enhancement: +1 to Occult, Research (esoteric and supernatural topics)
Tags: [Huge], [Exceptional]

A serious collection: several hundred volumes on mythology, comparative religion, folklore, occultism, and supernatural phenomena. Huge means it cannot be moved without significant effort — it lives in the character's Chantry, sanctum, or library. Exceptional pushes Enhancement to +2 for topics the library directly covers.

The library is not exhaustive. It covers what it covers. For topics outside its scope (e.g., a Hermetic library consulted for Dreamspeaker spirit lore), the Enhancement may drop to +1 or may not apply at all.

Invoke: "Exceptional library — the Hermetic texts cover this ritual perfectly. +2 Enhancement on my research."
Compel: "Huge means it is here, in the Chantry. You are not in the Chantry right now."
```

```
SURVEILLANCE PACKAGE  •  Tool
-----------------------------
Enhancement: +1 to Investigation, Stealth (information gathering)
Tags: [Concealable], [Multiuse], [Technological]

Hidden cameras, audio bugs, GPS trackers, radio frequency scanners, and a signal receiver. Concealable because individual components are small and unremarkable. Multiuse: audio surveillance, visual surveillance, vehicle tracking, and signal interception are all different uses, all covered. Technological means the package can be disrupted by electromagnetic interference, Paradox events near Technocratic null zones, or a mage with Forces ●● and hostile intent.

Invoke: "Concealable — I planted the bug during the meeting. Nobody saw it go in."
Compel: "Technological — the Paradox event fried two of the cameras. You have blind spots."
```

```
CLIMBING GEAR  •  Tool
----------------------
Enhancement: +1 to Athletics (climbing, rappelling, vertical movement)
Tags: [Rugged], [Expendable]

Harness, rope, pitons, carabiners, and ascenders. Rugged reduces Complications from the environment — weather, surface texture, time pressure — by 1 step. Expendable means if the gear is lost (cut rope, abandoned pitons, dropped harness), it is recovered by the next scene.

Invoke: "Rugged gear — the rain has not made this impossible. Reduce the environmental Complication."
Compel: "Rappelling requires both hands. You cannot hold the climbing rope and return fire simultaneously."
```

# Appendix S: Crafting

A mage who can reshape reality should also be able to shape the objects within it. Crafting in Mage: The Ascension Storypath Ultra Edition covers two distinct practices: mundane crafting, the creation of ordinary items using skill and materials, and Enlightened crafting, the construction of Wonders — objects imbued with persistent magical power. The rules differ significantly because the underlying processes differ significantly. A blacksmith hammers metal; a Verbena mage who is also a blacksmith hammers metal while her will and her magic flow into the steel simultaneously.

This appendix covers both. It also addresses three related topics that arise naturally from the crafting framework: enchanting existing objects (adding magical properties to mundane items), Areas of Power (how Nodes and Vertices function as crafting resources), and Fetish binding (the specific and demanding art of capturing a spirit inside a physical vessel). Each of these deserves its own section because each presents distinct opportunities and risks.

Throughout this appendix, Storyguide notes are included to help run crafting as a satisfying narrative process rather than an abstract mechanical exercise. Crafting should feel like an event — it takes time, it has costs, it produces something that did not exist before, and it can fail in interesting ways.

## Mundane Crafting

Mundane crafting produces items that are physically exceptional but not supernaturally so — a perfectly balanced blade, a garment woven to unusual tightness, a mechanical device operating at the edge of its design envelope. These items may exceed what is commercially available, but they do not violate the Consensus. A Sleeper examining a mundane-crafted item would see excellent craftsmanship, nothing more.

Mundane crafting uses a single action, not the milestone system of Enlightened crafting. The character applies a relevant Attribute and Ability to a single roll, the result determines the item's quality, and additional hits can be spent on tags.

### The Mundane Crafting Roll

Choose the most appropriate Attribute from Cunning, Intellect, or Dexterity and pair it with a relevant Ability (Crafts, Medicine, Science, Technology, or another that fits the context). The base Difficulty is 1. Each hit on the roll can be used to:

- Apply one tag to the item (from the standard tag list in Appendix J and Appendix R)
- Increase the item's Enhancement by +1 (maximum +1 for standard mundane items; Master Work items may reach Enhancement +2 — see below)
- Improve quality in a narrative sense — the item is more beautiful, more durable, faster to use — without mechanical implication
```
MUNDANE CRAFTING AT A GLANCE
----------------------------

STANDARD CRAFTING (Difficulty 1):
  Enhancement: +1 maximum.
  Tags: 2 maximum.
  Time: Normal project duration.
  Materials: Standard.

MASTER WORK (Difficulty 2, double time, exceptional materials):
  Enhancement: +2 maximum.
  Tags: 3 maximum.
  Supernatural tags (Enchanted, Warded, Awakened, etc.): NOT available. Sphere magic required.
  Effect: The absolute peak of mundane craftsmanship — no mundane item exceeds these values.

Both use the same roll formula: Attribute + Crafts/Technology/Medicine/etc.
Each hit above Difficulty = 1 free allocation (Enhancement pip or tag).
```

Standard mundane items cap at two tags and Enhancement +1. However, a truly exceptional craftsperson — one who spends significant extra time and applies rare materials — can achieve Master Work quality. A Master Work item can reach Enhancement +2 and carry up to three tags, representing the absolute peak of mundane craftsmanship. Master Work requires: a Difficulty 2 roll instead of Difficulty 1, double the normal time investment, and access to exceptional materials (Storyguide determines availability). Even at Master Work quality, a mundane item cannot exceed Enhancement +2 or three tags, and cannot generate tags that require supernatural explanation: no Magical, no Warded, no Enchanted, no Awakened. These properties require Sphere magic.

> MASTER WORK EXAMPLE — MARCUS BRANDS A COMBAT KNIFE
> 
> Marcus (Iteration X, Technology ●●●●, Dexterity ●●●●) spends two weeks in a precision machining facility to create the finest mundane combat knife possible. He uses aerospace-grade steel, CNC-precision grinding, and custom ergonomic handle work.
> 
> Roll: Dexterity ●●●● (4 dice) + Crafts ●●●● (4 dice) = 8 dice, Difficulty 2 (Master Work). He took double time: +1 Enhancement on the roll = 9 dice.
> 
> He rolls 5 hits. 2 meet the Difficulty. 3 free hits.
> 
> Spend:
>   Free hit 1: Enhancement +1 (first rank, always available).
>   Free hit 2: Enhancement +2 (second rank, Master Work only).
>   Free hit 3: Precise tag (exceptional balance — the knife rewards accuracy).
>   Free hit 4 (of the remaining free hits after difficulty): Piercing tag (aerospace steel, honed to 10 nanometers — it finds gaps in armor).
>   Free hit 5: Concealable (knife profile is minimal — slides into a forearm sheath).
> 
> Result: A Master Work combat knife with Enhancement +2, tags Precise, Piercing, Concealable. This is as good as a mundane knife gets. When Marcus later enchants it with Matter ●●● (adding the Enchanted tag and raising its Piercing to strip 2 AP instead of 1), it becomes a Cutting-Edge Trinket building on an exceptional foundation.

### Time and Materials

Mundane crafting requires appropriate materials and a reasonable amount of time. The Storyguide sets both. A simple knife might take an hour with a portable forge; a fitted suit of armor might take weeks of work with proper facilities. The roll represents the culmination of the work, not a moment-by-moment process. If a character takes extra time (spending twice the normal time on a project), they gain Enhancement +1 on the crafting roll. If they rush (taking half the normal time), they face a Difficulty increase of 1.

> MUNDANE CRAFTING EXAMPLE — ROSAMUND ASHBY FORGES A RITUAL KNIFE
> 
> Rosamund (Verbena, Crafts ●●●, Dexterity ●●●) wants a ritual athame that exceeds the quality of anything she could buy. She spends a long weekend at her forge.
> 
> Roll: Dexterity + Crafts = 6 dice, Difficulty 1 (she took extra time: +1 Enhancement on the roll = 7 dice).
> 
> She rolls 4 hits.
> 
> She spends:
>   Hit 1: Applies Piercing tag (the blade is exceptionally sharp — it finds gaps in armor).
>   Hit 2: Applies Precise tag (the balance is perfect — it rewards accuracy over brute force).
>   Hit 3: Narrative quality improvement (the blade is visually beautiful — carved with Verbena knotwork).
>   Hit 4: Narrative quality improvement (the blade feels right in her hand — it is clearly hers).
> 
> Result: A combat knife with Enhancement +1, tags Concealable (base), Piercing, Precise. The knotwork and the sense of personal fit are narrative — they matter for Resonance when she later attempts to Enchant it, but they have no current mechanical tag.
> 
> This knife is not yet a magical item. When Rosamund adds Life ●● Enchantment later (see Enchanting Existing Items below), it becomes a Trinket with the Enchanted tag and an additional Effect. The mundane crafting work she did now means the final Wonder is stronger because of its foundation.

> MUNDANE CRAFTING EXAMPLE — TOMÁS FORGES A DREAMSPEAKER HUNTING BLADE
> 
> Tomás (Dreamspeaker, Crafts ●●, Dexterity ●●●) wants to forge a traditional hunting blade as a vessel for future spirit-binding. He has the skills but not Marcus-level precision — this is honest craft, not engineering. He works at a stone forge over two days, singing to the metal.
> 
> STANDARD ROLL (Difficulty 1): Dexterity ●●● (3) + Crafts ●● (2) = 5 dice.
> 
> He rolls: 9, 5, 4, 3, 1. One hit (the 9). Meets Difficulty with 0 free hits.
> Result: A functional hunting blade. No tags, no Enhancement. Competent work — it will hold an edge and serve as a vessel, but Tomás got nothing extra to spend.
> 
> --- SECOND ATTEMPT: MASTER WORK (Difficulty 2, double time, exceptional materials) ---
> 
> Tomás decides the blade deserves better. He sources iron from a meteorite fragment held by an elder smith (exceptional material), spends four days on the forge (double time = +1 Enhancement die to pool), and focuses his full craft on the work.
> 
> MASTER WORK ROLL (Difficulty 2): 5 dice + 1 (double time) = 6 dice.
> 
> He rolls: 10, 8, 6, 4, 3, 1. The 10 counts as 2 hits. Total: 3 hits. Difficulty 2 met. 1 free hit.
> 
> Spend:
>   Free hit 1: Piercing tag — the meteorite iron is dense and hard. The blade finds gaps in leather and ring armor naturally.
>   Enhancement: Tomás elects not to spend a hit on Enhancement — he only had 1 free hit and preferred the tag.
> 
> Result: A Master Work hunting blade. Enhancement +0 (he chose not to spend on it), Tag: Piercing, Tag: (none additional). Not the maximum of what Master Work can offer — Enhancement +2 and 3 tags — but this is what 1 free hit buys. The blade is exceptional mundane work: dense, balanced, spiritually significant by its materials. When Tomás binds a wolf-spirit into it later (see Fetish Binding), the Piercing tag carries forward into the Fetish version for free.
> 
> CONTRAST: If Tomás had rolled 5 hits (3 free hits after Difficulty 2):
>   Free hit 1: Enhancement +1.
>   Free hit 2: Enhancement +2 (Master Work ceiling reached).
>   Free hit 3: Piercing tag.
>   That blade would be Enhancement +2, Piercing — a genuinely exceptional weapon. The dice decide how much of the Master Work ceiling you actually reach.

> MASTER WORK SUCCESS — SEIJI FORGES AN AKASHIC TRAINING SWORD
> 
> Seiji (Akashic Brotherhood, Crafts ●●●, Dexterity ●●●●) spends four days forging a practice jian for Do training. He works with high-carbon steel from a trusted supplier and twice the usual time.
> 
> Roll (Master Work, Difficulty 2, double time): Dexterity ●●●● (4) + Crafts ●●● (3) + 1 (double time) = 8 dice.
> 
> Result: 5 hits. Difficulty 2 met. 3 free hits.
> 
>   Free hit 1: Enhancement +1.
>   Free hit 2: Enhancement +2 (Master Work ceiling).
>   Free hit 3: Balanced tag — the sword rewards precision; penalty dice from rushing or being off-balance do not apply on Full Defense.
> 
> The sword is Enhancement +2 with the Balanced tag. A second free hit could add Precise or Concealable, but Seiji has only three free hits and chooses Enhancement over extra tags. This is the ceiling of what mundane craftsmanship can produce.

### Mundane Crafting by Tradition and Convention

The approach a mage takes to mundane crafting reflects their Paradigm, even when no magic is involved. The work is the practice; the practice is the Paradigm.

| Tradition / Convention | Typical Mundane Crafting Focus |
| --- | --- |
| Verbena | Blades, leather, natural fiber garments, herbal preparations, ritual implements forged from personally harvested metals |
| Order of Hermes | Precision astronomical instruments, illuminated texts, alchemical apparatus, engraved ritual circles |
| Sons of Ether | Mechanical devices, custom scientific instruments, goggles and environment suits, steam-powered prototypes |
| Virtual Adepts | Custom hardware, modified electronics, encrypted communication devices, experimental software running on physical media |
| Akashic Brotherhood | Practice weapons designed for training, meditation tools, personally fitted training robes optimized for combat movement |
| Celestial Chorus | Religious objects of exceptional craftsmanship — icons, staves, holy water vessels |
| Dreamspeakers | Fetish vessels (the mundane container that will later be spirit-bound), ritual drums, natural jewelry |
| Euthanatoi | Precision poison apparatus, scalpels and medical tools, death-journey amulets |
| Cult of Ecstasy | Musical instruments, alchemical preparations for altered states, sensory-enhancing devices |
| Iteration X | Cybernetic components, precision weapons, composite armor, mechanical enhancement rigs |
| NWO | Surveillance equipment, social engineering tools, credentials and documentation |
| Progenitors | Biomedical apparatus, gene sequencing equipment, custom pharmaceutical preparations |
| Syndicate | Financial instruments, high-end fashion, surveillance tools designed as luxury goods |
| Void Engineers | Environmental suits, sensor arrays, propulsion systems for extreme environment exploration |

## Enlightened Crafting — Creating Wonders

Wonders — Talismans, Devices, Fetishes, Periapts, Trinkets, and all their cousins — are created through a milestone system. Unlike mundane crafting's single roll, Wonder creation requires three separate milestone actions, each representing a distinct phase of the work. Between milestones, time must pass. The Wonder is not rushed into existence; it grows into it.

Wonder crafting is inherently risky. Each failed milestone roll introduces a Flaw into the work. Flaws can be removed by later successful milestone actions, but only at the cost of using a hit for that purpose instead of adding positive properties. A Wonder with unresolved Flaws at completion is functional but problematic — it works, but something about it is wrong, and the Storyguide will compel those Flaws against the wielder at appropriate moments.

Wonder crafting also generates Paradox. Not much, in the crafting itself — the creation happens in private, away from Sleepers — but the Wonder, once it exists, is a permanent feature of the world that will generate Paradox each time it is used in a Witnessed context.

### Item Rank and the Milestone System

Every Wonder has an Item Rank, which determines how powerful it can be and how much time the crafting requires. There are three ranks: Mundane (the basic Wonder, simple single-Effect items), Cutting-Edge (multi-Effect or enhanced-power items), and Groundbreaking (the most powerful creations, rivaling legendary artifacts).

| Rank | Maximum Total (Enhancement + Tags + Effects)  \|  Time Between Milestones  \|  Max Tags  \|  Max Enhancement |
| --- | --- |
| Mundane Wonder | 3 combined  \|  One story beat (same scene or scene break)  \|  2  \|  +1 |
| Cutting-Edge | 5 combined  \|  One scene  \|  3  \|  +2 |
| Groundbreaking | 7 combined  \|  One session  \|  4  \|  +3 |

How to read the table: a Cutting-Edge Wonder can have a maximum combined total of 5 between its Enhancement bonus, number of tags, and number of Sphere Effects embedded in it. A Cutting-Edge weapon with Enhancement +2 and 2 tags and 1 Effect has a total of 5 (2 + 2 + 1) — at its maximum. Adding another tag would require it to be Groundbreaking rank.

### The Three Milestones

Each milestone is a crafting action requiring an Attribute + Ability roll at a set Difficulty. Success advances the project; failure either stalls it or introduces a Flaw. The three milestones represent different aspects of the Wonder's creation:

- Milestone 1 — Foundation: Establishing the physical and conceptual basis of the Wonder. The Storyguide sets Difficulty 1–2. Success allows the mage to allocate initial tags and Enhancement. Each hit above the Difficulty can also be spent on Tricks: Duplicate (produces multiple copies of one-use items), or Efficient Process (advance immediately to the next milestone without waiting).
- Milestone 2 — Infusion: The actual Sphere magic flows into the object. Difficulty equals the highest Sphere rating being embedded. Each hit can add an Effect (Sphere power the Wonder can produce), reduce embedded Sphere costs, or purchase Quick Fix (remove one Flaw from the project). Failure here introduces a Flaw.
- Milestone 3 — Completion: Binding the work into stable form. Difficulty 2 for Mundane rank, 3 for Cutting-Edge, 4 for Groundbreaking. Each hit can add one final tag, confirm an Effect, or purchase Multi-Purpose (add one additional Effect or Edge above the normal maximum — once per project). Failure at this stage introduces a Flaw that cannot be removed after completion.

### Milestone Outcomes — Failure, Partial Success, and Exceptional Success

Not every milestone roll succeeds cleanly. The outcome of each milestone roll determines both whether the project advances and whether it accrues damage in the form of Flaws. Understanding these outcomes prevents confusion about when Flaws appear and what the crafter can do about them.

| Outcome | What Happens |
| --- | --- |
| Botch (0 hits AND more than half all dice show 1 — see Botch Rule, Chapter 2) | The milestone fails catastrophically. A permanent Flaw is introduced — Quick Fix cannot remove it at any later milestone. If Paradox dice are in the pool, their 1s count toward the botch threshold alongside the casting pool. The crafter must wait the full time-between-milestones period before retrying. If this is Milestone 3, the Flaw is sealed into the completed item forever. |
| Failure (zero hits, no botch) | The milestone does not advance. No Flaw is introduced, but the time investment is wasted. The crafter must wait the full time-between-milestones period again before attempting the same milestone. Materials are not consumed. |
| Near-miss (1 or more hits, but below Difficulty threshold) | The milestone partially advances but a Flaw is introduced. The project continues to the next milestone; the Flaw can be removed with Quick Fix (2 hits) at any later milestone. If this is Milestone 3, the Flaw cannot be removed. |
| Success (meets Difficulty exactly) | The milestone completes normally. No Flaw. Free hits: zero (spent on meeting Difficulty). |
| Strong success (1–2 hits above Difficulty) | Milestone completes. Free hits can be spent on Tricks, additional tags, or Effects per the milestone type. |
| Exceptional success (3+ hits above Difficulty) | Milestone completes with excellence. Free hits spent normally, plus the crafter may reduce the time-before-next-milestone by one step (a scene wait becomes a story beat; a session wait becomes a scene). |

Note on near-miss: the "near-miss introduces a Flaw" rule applies when the roll generates at least 1 hit but falls short of the Difficulty. A Milestone 2 roll that generates 2 hits against Difficulty 3 is a near-miss — the Effect is embedded but the Flaw tag goes on the item simultaneously. This creates a tense decision for the crafter: press forward and accept the Flaw, hoping to Quick Fix it at Milestone 3, or delay the project and retry Milestone 2 (losing the time investment but starting with a clean slate). The clock of a Wonder project is almost always ticking.

### Milestone Botches — When the Crafting Catastrophically Fails

A Botch during a milestone roll is the worst outcome in crafting. It follows the standard Botch rule from Chapter Two: the roll produces zero hits and more than half the dice in the pool — casting pool and Paradox Pool combined — show 1. The permanence of a crafting Botch makes it qualitatively worse than a mundane Botch: a ruined sword can be reforged; a Wonder with a permanent Flaw is flawed forever unless the entire creation is abandoned.

The Paradox pool's contribution to the botch threshold is particularly dangerous during crafting. Mages often build significant Paradox during a single crafting session — one Vulgar effect early in a project can leave 3-4 Paradox dice sitting in the pool, and those dice are then present for every subsequent milestone roll. A mage who starts Milestone 3 with 4 Paradox dice has a dramatically higher botch risk than the same mage working clean.

| Botch at Milestone... | Consequence |
| --- | --- |
| Milestone 1 (Foundation) | The physical materials are ruined — cracked, warped, or spiritually contaminated. The mage must begin the entire project from scratch with fresh materials. The permanent Flaw is embedded in the ruined materials, not the new ones, so a fresh start truly resets. However, any rare or expensive materials are lost. |
| Milestone 2 (Infusion) | The Sphere energy embeds catastrophically. A permanent Flaw is added to the item — it cannot be removed by Quick Fix or by subsequent milestone rolls. The mage can attempt to continue to Milestone 3 with the permanent Flaw already present, or abandon the project. Abandonment means the partially-constructed Wonder is typically destroyed (the destabilized magic releases). |
| Milestone 3 (Completion) | The Wonder is completed — but with a permanent Flaw sealed into its final form. It cannot be revised, repaired, or unflawed without dismantling the Wonder entirely and starting over. However, it does function. Some mages choose to complete a Botch-3 Wonder because a flawed Wonder is better than none. |

> MILESTONE BOTCH EXAMPLE — JOACHIM'S DOOMED TALISMAN
> 
> Joachim (Order of Hermes, Arete ●●●, Forces ●●●, Prime ●●) is crafting a Cutting-Edge Talisman — a brass astrolabe that channels lightning. He has been doing Vulgar work all session and carries 3 Paradox dice.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 2 (Infusion — Difficulty 3 for Forces ●●●) ---
> 
> Casting pool: Forces ●●● + Intellect ●●●● = 7 dice.
> Paradox pool: 3 dice.
> Total dice for botch threshold: 10.
> Botch threshold: more than 5 ones across all 10 dice.
> 
> Casting pool roll: 1, 1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Zero hits. Three ones from casting pool.
> Paradox pool roll: 1, 1, 3. Two ones from Paradox pool.
> Total ones: 5. Threshold: more than 5 = at least 6. Five is exactly 5. NOT a Botch — but just barely.
> 
> Without Paradox: 3 ones from 7 dice = 43%. Not more than half. Still not a botch.
> If Paradox pool had been 4 dice and rolled three 1s: 3 + 3 = 6 ones across 11 dice. More than 5.5. BOTCH.
> 
> --- WHAT WOULD HAVE HAPPENED ON A BOTCH ---
> 
> The Forces energy discharges violently into the astrolabe. The brass warps. A permanent Backlash Flaw is embedded at Milestone 2 — every time the astrolabe channels lightning, there is a chance it discharges into the wielder instead of the target (Minor Complication on every activation; if not bought off, wielder takes damage).
> 
> Joachim has options:
>   A) Press forward to Milestone 3, knowing the Backlash Flaw will be permanent in the finished item.
>   B) Abandon the project. The astrolabe is unstable and will likely destroy itself within a day.
>   C) Attempt Milestone 2 again — but first he must wait the full between-milestone period, AND the Paradox Pool has now gained 2 more dice from the Botch, making the next attempt even more dangerous.
> 
> This is the death spiral of a botched Enlightened crafting project. The correct move was to spend a session reducing his Paradox Pool before beginning Milestone 2.
> 
> LESSON: Carry Paradox into crafting at your peril. A 3-die Paradox Pool on a 7-die casting pool raises your botch threshold from 4 ones needed to 6 ones needed across the combined 10 dice — and those extra dice actively want to show 1.

### Botch During Fetish Binding

Fetish Binding has its own three-step process (Step 1: Vessel Preparation, Step 2: Spirit Binding, Step 3: Binding Seal), and Botches during each step carry specific consequences:

- Step 1 Botch: The vessel is spiritually shattered. It cannot hold a spirit and must be replaced. Any Quintessence spent in the preparation is lost. The spirit is unaffected — it simply was not bound.
- Step 2 Botch: The binding fails catastrophically. The spirit tears free of any containment and is immediately hostile. Depending on the spirit's power, this may result in possession, attack, or simple flight — but it will not be willing to re-enter negotiations for at least a season. Paradox Pool gains 2 dice.
- Step 3 Botch: The binding seal fails. The spirit is trapped but furious — it gains the Rebellion Condition immediately (see Spirit Rebellion) and begins at Major Rebellion severity. The Fetish technically exists but is already in open revolt. Paradox Pool gains 1 die.

### Sphere Requirements for Wonders

The type of Wonder determines what Sphere rating is required to create it. The embedded Effects match the Wonder type. A mage cannot embed a Sphere Effect into a Wonder if their rating in that Sphere is insufficient to produce the Effect normally.

| Wonder Type | Sphere Requirements |
| --- | --- |
| Periapt / Matrix | Prime ●● minimum. Each dot above ●● allows storing +5 Quintessence. |
| Trinket | Any single Sphere at ●● minimum. Produces one repeatable Effect. |
| Charm / Gadget | Any single Sphere at ●. One-use Effect stored at the moment of creation. |
| Talisman | Two or more Spheres at ●● minimum. Multiple ongoing Effects. |
| Artifact / Invention | Highest Sphere at ●●● minimum. Complex inter-Sphere Effects. |
| Device | Relevant Sphere(s) at rating appropriate to Effects. Requires Enlightenment to activate. |
| Fetish | Spirit ●● to bind. The spirit provides the Effects; the mage provides the vessel. |
| Grimoire / Principia | Prime ●●● to create. The knowledge embedded requires Prime mastery. |

### Paradox and Wonder Crafting

Creating a Wonder does not generate significant Paradox during the crafting process itself, provided the work is done in private and at an appropriate pace. The Consensus does not observe the mage at their forge or workbench — it responds to public magical displays, not private practice.

However, the completed Wonder is a permanent node of magical potential in the world. When the Wonder is used in a Witnessed context, the Paradox implications of the Effect apply normally. A Talisman that produces a Forces ●●● lightning bolt is Vulgar every time it fires in front of Sleepers. The mage who wields it generates Paradox with each Witnessed use even if they have only Arete ● — the Effect happens regardless of the wielder's personal casting power, and the Consensus responds accordingly.

Certain Wonders carry permanent embedded Paradox: items that violate the Consensus simply by existing (like the Totem Tattoo, which is a permanently Vulgar magical alteration of a living body) accumulate a flat Paradox rating that applies at all times. These items are described individually in Appendix R.

### Paradox Triggers for Crafted Items — Quick Reference

The following table summarizes when a crafted Wonder generates Paradox in play. The crafting process itself generates no Paradox unless performed in public view. Paradox comes from use.

| Situation | Paradox Generated |
| --- | --- |
| Wonder used in private, no Sleeper witnesses, Coincidental Effect | None. Private use of coincidental magic does not accumulate Paradox. |
| Wonder used in private, no Sleeper witnesses, Vulgar Effect | 1 Paradox die. Vulgar effects always generate at least 1 die, even unwitnessed. |
| Wonder used with Sleeper witnesses, Coincidental Effect | None. Coincidental effects leave no Paradox trace even when observed. |
| Wonder used with Sleeper witnesses, Vulgar Effect | Full Paradox. Standard Vulgar Paradox calculation applies (see Chapter 5). |
| Item with permanent embedded Paradox (Totem Tattoo, etc.) | Paradox always present at the listed flat rating. This stacks with use-based Paradox. |
| Fetish with agitated bound spirit | +1 Paradox die on all uses until the spirit is appeased or rebound. The spirit's distress bleeds into the wielder's Paradox accumulation. |
| Wonder used in a Node or Vertex | –1 Paradox die on all uses (minimum 0). Node Resonance buffers the local Consensus. |
| Multiple Wonders active simultaneously | Each Wonder generates Paradox independently. Using three Wonders in one scene generates Paradox from all three separately. |

> WONDER CREATION EXAMPLE — ROSAMUND'S ATHAME (TRINKET)
> 
> Rosamund (Verbena, Arete ●●●, Life ●●●, Prime ●) wants to enchant her mundane-crafted ritual knife (from the earlier example) into a Trinket with Life ●● effects: natural healing by touch.
> 
> She plans a Mundane-rank Wonder (simple, single Effect). Target: Enhancement +1 (already achieved in mundane crafting), Enchanted tag, Wounded-Healing Effect (Life ●●). That is 1 Enhancement + 1 tag + 1 Effect = 3 combined. Within Mundane rank maximum.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 1: Foundation (Difficulty 1) ---
> Roll: Arete ●●● (3 dice) + Crafts ●●● (3 dice) = 6 dice, Difficulty 1.
> She rolls 3 hits.
> Spend:
>   Hit 1: Allocates the Enchanted tag to the knife (it is now a Wonder in progress).
>   Hit 2: Confirms Enhancement +1 carries forward from mundane crafting.
>   Hit 3: Advance immediately to Milestone 2 without waiting (Efficient Process Trick, 2 hits — but she only has 1 hit free after allocating Enhancement. She must wait between milestones).
> Result: Milestone 1 complete. She must wait one story beat before beginning Milestone 2.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 2: Infusion (Difficulty = highest Sphere = Life ●●● = 3) ---
> Roll: Arete ●●● (3 dice) + Crafts ●●● (3 dice) = 6 dice, Difficulty 3.
> She rolls 4 hits (3 to meet Difficulty, 1 free).
> Spend:
>   Free hit: Embeds Life ●● healing Effect (the knife, pressed to a wound, draws on the wielder's Quintessence to close lacerations and knit bruised tissue).
> No Flaw this milestone.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 3: Completion (Difficulty 2 for Mundane rank) ---
> Roll: Arete ●●● + Crafts ●●● = 6 dice, Difficulty 2.
> She rolls 2 hits (exactly meets Difficulty, 0 free hits).
> No additional properties added. No Flaws introduced.
> 
> Result: The athame is now a Trinket.
>   Enhancement: +1 (combat knife stats: Concealable, Piercing, Precise)
>   Tags: [Enchanted], [Concealable], [Piercing], [Precise]
>   Effect: Life ●● healing touch — spend 1 Quintessence per use; roll Arete vs. Difficulty 2; each hit heals 1 Bashing or converts 1 Lethal to Bashing on a touched target.
> 
> The knife has Resonance strongly tied to Rosamund personally. Another mage using it without her presence might find the Difficulty increased by 1 for the healing Effect — the knife knows its creator.

> WONDER CREATION EXAMPLE — ITERATION X DEVICE (CUTTING-EDGE)
> 
> Marcus, an Iteration X engineer (Enlightenment ●●●, Forces ●●●, Matter ●●●, Technology ●●●●), is building a miniaturized sonic disruption Device — a handheld tool that can shatter locks, disrupt communications equipment, and stun biological targets. He plans a Cutting-Edge Device.
> 
> Target properties:
>   Enhancement: +2 (specialized for disable/disrupt actions only)
>   Tags: [Concealable], [Technological], [Stunning]
>   Effects: Forces ●●● sonic disruption (breaks locks, disrupts electronics), Matter ●●● (shatters brittle materials), Life ●● stun (biological disruption)
> Total: 2 Enhancement + 3 tags + 3 Effects = 8. That exceeds Cutting-Edge maximum (5). He scales back.
> 
> Revised plan:
>   Enhancement: +2 (specialized)
>   Tags: [Concealable], [Technological]
>   Effects: Forces ●●● (handles locks and electronics), Life ●● (biological stun)
> Total: 2 + 2 + 2 = 6. Still over by 1. He removes the Life ●● Effect.
> 
> Final plan: Enhancement +2 (specialized), Tags: [Concealable], [Technological], Effect: Forces ●●● (sonic disruption). Total: 2 + 2 + 1 = 5. Exactly at Cutting-Edge maximum.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 1 (Difficulty 2, one scene required between milestones): 3 hits ---
>   Allocates Enhancement +2 (specialized) and Concealable tag.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 2 (Difficulty = Forces ●●● = 3): 2 hits ---
>   Hit 1 meets Difficulty. Hit 2: Embeds Forces ●●● sonic disruption Effect.
>   No Flaw. Adds Technological tag with the remaining hit.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 3 (Difficulty 3 for Cutting-Edge): 1 hit ---
>   Barely meets Difficulty. No free hits. No Flaw.
> 
> Final Device: Enhancement +2 (specialized — only for disable/disrupt actions), Tags: [Concealable], [Technological], [Enchanted].
> Effect: Forces ●●● sonic disruption — roll Enlightenment vs. Difficulty 2 (locks, communications equipment) or 3 (reinforced systems). Each hit = one destroyed or disabled system element. Cost: 1 Primal Energy per use.
> 
> The Stun application Marcus wanted did not make it into this version. He marks it as a planned upgrade — a second Cutting-Edge crafting project to add the Life ●● stun Effect to the existing Device, treating the upgrade itself as a Milestone 2 (infusion) into the existing foundation.

> WONDER CREATION — A PROJECT THAT GOES WRONG: HENRIQUE'S TALISMAN OF GUIDED ENTROPY
> 
> Henrique is a Euthanatoi mage (Arete ●●●, Entropy ●●●, Life ●●) working on a Cutting-Edge Talisman — a carved obsidian crescent intended to help him read the flow of death energy in a target and channel it into a precise strike. His design:
>   Enhancement: +2 (specialized — only on Entropy-aligned attack actions)
>   Tags: [Concealable], [Wounding]
>   Effect: Entropy ●●● (sense death resonance in targets; once per scene, add +2 dice to a single attack against a target whose death-state Henrique has read)
> Total build points: 2 + 2 + 1 = 5. Exactly at Cutting-Edge maximum.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 1 (Foundation — Difficulty 2): ROLL 6 dice → 4 hits ---
>   4 hits vs. Difficulty 2 = 2 free hits. Henrique allocates:
>     Free Hit 1: Concealable tag.
>     Free Hit 2: Wounding tag.
>   Enhancement +2 (specialized) is established as the foundation intent.
>   No Flaw. The obsidian takes shape cleanly. Henrique must wait one full scene before Milestone 2.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 2 (Infusion — Difficulty = Entropy ●●● = 3): ROLL 6 dice → 3 hits ---
>   3 hits vs. Difficulty 3. Hits equal Difficulty exactly — this is a Near-Miss.
>   A Near-Miss at Milestone 2 introduces a Flaw that can potentially be removed at Milestone 3.
>   The Storyguide determines the Flaw: Backlash. The obsidian crescent has accepted the Entropy infusion, but the death-resonance is unstable. When activated near targets who are not actually dying or wounded, the entropy backlashes into the wielder.
>   Henrique marks the item: Tags so far: Concealable, Wounding, Enchanted; Flaw: Backlash.
>   He must wait another scene before Milestone 3. He could attempt Quick Fix (see below).
> 
>   HENRIQUE'S DECISION — QUICK FIX:
>   Henrique does not want to carry this Flaw into completion. He attempts Quick Fix:
>   Quick Fix requires a Wits + Crafts roll vs. Difficulty 3 (same as the failed milestone) to stabilize the Flaw before the final milestone.
>   Henrique has Wits ●●● + Crafts ●● = 5 dice.
>   QUICK FIX ROLL: 5 dice → 2 hits. Difficulty is 3. He needs at least 3 hits to remove the Flaw entirely, or 1+ hits to reduce it from Moderate to Minor (in systems where Flaws have severity). He gets 2 hits — not enough to remove the Backlash entirely, but enough to reduce it.
>   Result: Backlash remains, but it is now a Minor Backlash (see Flaw table for Minor vs. standard severity). The Storyguide rules that Minor Backlash only triggers when the Effect is invoked and the roll botches — not on every single use.
>   Henrique proceeds to Milestone 3 with the reduced Flaw intact.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 3 (Completion — Difficulty 3 for Cutting-Edge): ROLL 6 dice → 3 hits ---
>   3 hits vs. Difficulty 3 = Barely meets Difficulty. No free hits.
>   With no free hits at Milestone 3, there is nothing left to spend on removing the remaining Flaw.
>   The crescent is completed with Minor Backlash still attached.
> 
> FINAL TALISMAN:
>   Enhancement: +2 (specialized — Entropy attack actions only)
>   Tags: [Concealable], [Wounding], [Enchanted]
>   Flaw: Minor Backlash (triggers only when the Entropy Effect is invoked and the activation roll botches)
>   Effect: Entropy ●●● — spend 1 Quintessence to read a target's death-resonance (Difficulty 2). On success, add +2 dice to one attack against that target before end of scene. Cost: 1 Quintessence per activation.
> 
> --- HOW THE FLAW PLAYS OUT IN COMBAT ---
> 
> Three sessions later. Henrique faces Samir, a Virtual Adept who has been interfering with the Euthanatoi's operations in São Paulo. Samir is at full health — uninjured, no death-resonance active. Henrique activates the crescent's Effect.
> 
> Phase 2: Henrique activates the Talisman. Roll Arete ●●● (3 dice) vs. Difficulty 2 to read Samir's death-resonance.
> ACTIVATION ROLL: 3 dice → result: 1, 1, 2. A botch. Zero hits, and at least two 1s — this triggers Minor Backlash.
> 
> Storyguide: "The crescent grows hot in your hand, Henrique. The entropy you're trying to read in Samir reflects back into you instead — you suddenly feel your own death-resonance flare. Take 1 point of Lethal damage as the Backlash strikes your cardiovascular system. The Effect does not activate."
> 
> Henrique takes 1 Lethal damage (entering his Bruised band but not yet Impaired), burns his Phase 2 action, and has no Entropy bonus for this combat. Samir, meanwhile, is unaffected and now knows Henrique just hurt himself trying to read him.
> 
> Phase 3: Henrique attacks normally — no Enhancement bonus, since the Effect failed. He rolls his base Dexterity + Melee pool (4 dice). Gets 2 hits. Samir soaks 1 via his Wards (Warded tag on his jacket absorbs 1). Samir takes 1 Lethal damage.
> 
> The Backlash did not end the fight, but it cost Henrique an action, inflicted 1 Lethal on himself, and removed the +2 dice advantage he was counting on. This is the correct weight for a Minor Flaw — consequential but not catastrophic, and most importantly it happened at exactly the wrong moment.
> 
> RECOVERY: At the end of the session, Henrique can attempt to repair the Flaw with a second Quick Fix roll (Wits + Crafts, Difficulty 3). If he gets 3+ hits, he can permanently remove Minor Backlash. He decides to try — and fails again with 2 hits. The crescent stays flawed. He may need another full Milestone 3 crafting session dedicated solely to Flaw removal, or he learns to live with it.

> WONDER CREATION — RUSHED CRAFTING WITH EFFICIENT PROCESS
> 
> Cassandra, a Verbena hedge-mage and herbalist (Arete ●●●, Life ●●●, Prime ●●), is three days from a critical confrontation with a Black Spiral Dancer pack. She wants to complete a Trinket — a knotted rowan-wood pendant that grants a bonus to Life-based Healing actions — before that fight. She does not have time for the standard milestone pacing.
> 
> Standard Trinket build:
>   Enhancement: +1 (Healing actions only)
>   Tags: [Warding (vs. corruption/bane effects)]
>   Effect: Life ●● — spend 1 Quintessence and touch a target to restore 1 Bashing or convert 1 Lethal to Bashing.
> Total: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3. Within Trinket maximum.
> 
> The problem: Milestone pacing requires at least one scene between each milestone. With three milestones, that is a minimum of two waiting intervals. Cassandra has three days but six scenes of expected play before the confrontation. She has just enough time — barely — if she uses Efficient Process.
> 
> EFFICIENT PROCESS (Trick, costs 2 hits from current milestone roll):
> When a mage uses Efficient Process, she compresses the waiting period between the current milestone and the next. Instead of waiting a full scene, she can begin the next milestone immediately — but the Difficulty of the next milestone increases by 1 to reflect the rushed, unset magical structure.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 1 (Foundation — Difficulty 1 for Trinket): ROLL 5 dice → 4 hits ---
>   4 hits vs. Difficulty 1 = 3 free hits. Cassandra allocates:
>     Free Hit 1: Enhancement +1 (Healing).
>     Free Hit 2: Warding tag.
>     Free Hit 3: Efficient Process — she compresses the wait and begins Milestone 2 immediately.
>   No Flaw. The rowan wood is smooth. Cassandra begins binding the Life essence that same afternoon.
> 
> EFFICIENT PROCESS COST: The next milestone's Difficulty increases by 1.
>   Milestone 2 would normally be Difficulty = Life ●●● = 3. Rushed: Difficulty becomes 4.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 2 (Infusion — Difficulty 4 because RUSHED): ROLL 5 dice → 3 hits ---
>   3 hits vs. Difficulty 4. She needed 4+ hits to succeed cleanly. This is a Near-Miss.
>   A Near-Miss at Milestone 2 while rushing: Flaw introduced. The Storyguide chooses Unstable.
>   The pendant's healing energy is real but poorly bound — under stress, it may discharge uncontrollably.
>   Flaw: Unstable. (Once per scene when the Effect is activated, the Storyguide may compel an Unstable trigger that creates an area Life effect centered on Cassandra, distributing the healing energy to everyone nearby — friend and foe alike, 1 Bashing restored to all — and depleting the Quintessence without the intended precision.)
> 
> Cassandra does NOT have enough time for a Quick Fix before Milestone 3 — she is already rushing. She decides to press forward.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 3 (Completion — Difficulty 2 for Trinket, NOT rushed): ROLL 5 dice → 4 hits ---
>   4 hits vs. Difficulty 2 = 2 free hits. Cassandra tries to use these free hits to remove the Flaw.
>   Removing a Flaw at Milestone 3 costs 2 free hits (same cost as adding a new Tag).
>   She has exactly 2 free hits. She spends both on Flaw removal.
>   FLAW REMOVAL ROLL: The Storyguide requires a secondary roll — Wits + Crafts (3 dice) vs. Difficulty 3.
>   SECONDARY ROLL: 3 dice → 1, 3, 5. Two hits. Close, but she needed 3. Flaw stays.
> 
>   Optional: Cassandra could spend her final free hit on adding a second Tag instead. But she only has 2 free hits total, and both went to the Flaw removal attempt. She does not have a second free hit — the attempt consumed them. She completes the item with Unstable.
> 
> FINAL TALISMAN:
>   Enhancement: +1 (Healing actions only)
>   Tags: [Warding], [Enchanted]
>   Flaw: Unstable
>   Effect: Life ●● — spend 1 Quintessence + touch to restore 1 Bashing or convert 1 Lethal to Bashing.
> 
> --- IN PLAY: THE CONFRONTATION ---
> 
> Night of the fight. Cassandra's packmate Declan takes two Lethal hits from a Dancer and drops into Critical (7 damage filled). Cassandra moves to Declan's side and activates the pendant.
> 
> She spends 1 Quintessence and touches Declan. Rolls Arete ●●● vs. Difficulty 2.
> HEALING ROLL: 3 dice → 4, 5, 6. Three hits — success. Normally: convert 1 Lethal to Bashing for Declan.
> 
> But the Storyguide invokes the Unstable Flaw: "The knot in the rowan wood pulses with uncontrolled Life essence. The healing energy radiates outward in a five-foot burst."
> 
> Effect: All living creatures within five feet each receive 1 Bashing healed/converted. This includes:
>   Declan: 1 Lethal converts to Bashing (good, he needed it).
>   Cassandra: 1 Bashing healed (minor but she wasn't the priority).
>   Dancer #2: 1 Bashing healed (the Dancer was at Impaired from the earlier fight — the healing pulls it back toward Bruised).
> 
> The Quintessence is spent. The Flaw just healed an enemy. The Dancer snarls at Cassandra: "You made me stronger, witch."
> 
> Cassandra can re-activate the pendant on her next action — but she has only 2 Quintessence left, and the fight is not over. The Unstable Flaw has made an already tense scene actively dangerous in a way that pure dice mechanics could not simulate.
> 
> AFTERMATH: After the fight (assuming they survive), Cassandra can attempt Flaw removal as a dedicated Wits + Crafts project outside of combat — a short scene (Difficulty 3, no milestone structure required, just a single roll). If she gets 3+ hits, the Unstable Flaw is permanently removed. The pendant is then a clean, working Trinket. Until then, she decides when she dares use it.

## Item Flaws

Flaws are introduced when a milestone roll fails. They represent imperfections in the crafting — magical instabilities, structural weaknesses, or behavioral quirks in a spirit-infused item. Flaws are tracked as Tags on the item. Unlike standard Tags, Flaws can only be compelled against the item's wielder — they cannot be invoked for advantage.

Each Flaw is a compellable property. The Storyguide should invoke Flaw compels at dramatically interesting moments — when they will create the most meaningful complications, not necessarily the most numerically damaging ones. A weapon that Backlashes should misfire when it would hurt the wielder most dramatically, not just randomly.

| Flaw | Effect (always compelled, never invoked) |
| --- | --- |
| Backlash | The item is unstable. Each time it is used as part of an action, it imposes a Minor Complication. If the Complication is not bought off, the item deals 1 damage to the wielder or creates a strange secondary effect at the Storyguide's discretion. |
| Fragile | The item is dangerously fragile. Each time it is used, it has a Minor Complication. If not bought off, the item breaks permanently. |
| Password | The item requires a phrase or codeword spoken aloud before it activates. This requires total concentration and takes up the character's action for one Phase. No password = no activation. |
| Unstable | Once per scene when used, the item imposes a Moderate Complication. If not bought off, the item creates an area effect centered on the user, determined by the Storyguide. |
| Weakened | The item is easier to destroy than normal. Any attack specifically targeting the item has the Sunder Trick at 1 fewer hit cost. |
| Hungry | A spirit-bound Fetish whose spirit is resentful or greedy. The item demands tribute — Quintessence, life force, or specific actions — at dramatic moments. Refusing the demand suppresses the Fetish's powers for the scene. |
| Resonance Conflict | The item's Resonance is in tension with the wielder's own Resonance or Tradition. Using the item in contexts where the conflict matters imposes –1 Enhancement on all effects. The item works better for someone else. |
| Witnessed | The item's effects are inherently Vulgar in ways that are difficult to conceal. Uses in the presence of Sleepers always generate at least +1 additional Paradox die, even if the effect would otherwise be coincidental. |

> FLAW EXAMPLE — THE BACKLASH BLADE
> 
> Henrique (Euthanatoi, Entropy ●●●) rushes through the creation of an Entropy-infused blade, cutting corners on Milestone 2. He fails the roll, introducing the Backlash Flaw.
> 
> The blade works — Entropy ●●● cuts through armor and produces necrotic wounds — but the Backlash Flaw means every time Henrique uses the blade in an attack, a Minor Complication appears on the action.
> 
> In play, the Storyguide waits for a dramatically significant moment: Henrique is fighting an Iteration X HIT Mark, he has the target at 9/10 damage, one more hit closes it. He swings. The Storyguide triggers the Backlash compel. The Complication: the blade's necrotic field turns partially inward. Henrique must spend a hit to buy off the Complication, or take 1 damage himself from his own weapon's corruption.
> 
> He had the hits to win the fight cleanly. Now he has to choose: buy off the Complication and possibly fail to finish the HIT Mark this Phase, or take the damage and close the fight.
> 
> That is a good Flaw — it creates a decision, not just a penalty.

## Enchanting Existing Items

A mage does not always build Wonders from raw materials. Sometimes the starting point is an existing mundane item with personal significance — a grandfather's watch, a sword carried through a critical battle, a car that has outlasted three owners. Enchanting an existing item follows a modified form of the Wonder creation process.

The mundane item's existing properties (including any tags applied during mundane crafting) carry forward without cost. The Enchanted tag is added as part of the Milestone 1 action at no hit cost. The mage begins with whatever foundation the item already represents — its physical quality, its tags, its history. This is one reason mages invest care in the mundane quality of objects they intend to eventually enchant: those mundane tags are free.

### The Enchanting Process

Enchanting an existing item uses the same three-milestone structure as Wonder creation. The differences:

- Milestone 1 (Foundation) begins from the existing item's properties. The Enchanted tag is applied automatically. The mage does not need to re-establish the physical properties; they already exist.
- Milestone 2 (Infusion) is where the Sphere magic is introduced. If the item has pre-existing mundane tags that are compatible with the intended Effect (a Precise blade being enchanted with Forces ●● for enhanced cutting), the compatible tags reduce the Difficulty of Milestone 2 by 1.
- Milestone 3 (Completion) binds the enchantment permanently into the object's physical and metaphysical structure.
Items with personal history have Resonance that accelerates or complicates enchanting. An item that has been owned by the mage for a year or more, used regularly, and has personal significance grants Enhancement +1 on all three milestone rolls. An item with incompatible Resonance (a Technocratic Device the mage is trying to enchant with Life magic) adds Difficulty +1 to Milestone 2.

> ENCHANTING EXAMPLE — ELIAS UPGRADES HIS JACKET
> 
> Elias (Order of Hermes, Arete ●●●, Forces ●●●, Matter ●●) has worn his leather riding jacket for four years. It has mundane tags: Flexible, Natural, Concealable, Soak 1, Sturdy 4. He wants to enchant it into a Trinket with Matter ●●● protection — bringing the Soak up to 4 and adding the Ballistic tag.
> 
> The jacket's four-year personal history grants Enhancement +1 on all milestone rolls.
> 
> Target: Add Ballistic tag, raise Soak to 4. Item Rank: Mundane Wonder (3 combined: +1 Enhancement over base already there, Ballistic tag = 1, Enchanted tag = 1, Matter Effect to raise Soak = 1... total = 3). Mundane rank.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 1 (Difficulty 1): Roll 4 dice (Arete 3 + Crafts 2 - 1, then +1 personal history = 6 dice). Rolls 3 hits.
>   Enchanted tag: free.
>   Hit 1: Allocates Ballistic tag.
>   Hit 2: Allocates the Soak-enhancement as the target Effect.
>   (Soak increase from 1 to 4 represents Matter ●●● fundamentally restructuring the jacket's fibers.)
> 
> --- MILESTONE 2 (Difficulty = Matter ●●● = 3): 6 dice (same pool + personal history). Rolls 4 hits.
>   3 hits meet Difficulty. Free hit: no additional allocation needed; the effect design is complete.
>   The Natural tag's compatibility with organic Matter magic (leather is natural material) reduces Difficulty by 1 = effective Difficulty 2. She had 4 hits vs. Difficulty 2 = 2 free hits.
>   He uses 1 free hit to pre-empt a potential Flaw (Quick Fix on nothing specific — held in reserve).
>   No Flaw introduced.
> 
> --- MILESTONE 3 (Difficulty 2): 6 dice. Rolls 3 hits.
>   Binding succeeds. Final allocation: confirm Soak 4, Ballistic, confirm Enchanted.
> 
> Result: The jacket is now a Trinket.
>   Soak 4  |  Sturdy 8 (raised from enchantment)  |  Initiative Penalty: None
>   Tags: [Flexible], [Natural], [Concealable], [Enchanted], [Ballistic]
>   Effect: Matter ●●● — the fibers resist both conventional and ballistic damage.
> 
> The jacket looks exactly as it always has. No casual observer sees armor. Mage Sight reveals the enchantment immediately — the Enchanted tag is visible to those with Prime ● or higher.

## Areas of Power — Nodes and Vertices

A Node (in Tradition terminology) or Vertex (in Technocratic terminology) is a location where free Quintessence flows from the Tellurian itself — a place where the membrane between material reality and the deeper patterns of existence is thin. Nodes are among the most strategically important locations in the Mage setting; factions fight over them, hide them, destroy them, and defend them at the cost of their lives.

For crafters, Nodes are irreplaceable resources. The free Quintessence they provide reduces crafting costs significantly. The Resonance they carry imprints itself on items created within them. And in sufficiently powerful Nodes, the Gauntlet is thin enough that Sphere effects during crafting are measurably more potent — Difficulty reductions that would otherwise require exceptional skill or time instead come naturally from the location.

### How Nodes Function in Crafting

Working within a Node provides three distinct crafting benefits:

- Quintessence Supply: The Node provides free Quintessence for enchanting. Each scene spent crafting within a Node of appropriate strength provides 1–5 Quintessence (Storyguide determines based on Node's dot rating) that can be spent directly on crafting costs without drawing from the mage's personal pool. This makes extended Talisman and Artifact creation dramatically more practical.
- Difficulty Reduction: For Milestone 2 (Infusion), crafting within a Node whose Resonance is compatible with the intended Wonder reduces Difficulty by 1. A Life-aspected Node reduces Difficulty for Life-magic enchantments. An Entropy-aspected Node reduces Difficulty for Entropy-infused work. Incompatible Resonance has no effect (it neither helps nor hurts).
- Resonance Bonding: Items created or completed within a Node absorb some of that Node's Resonance. This manifests as a free additional Tag representing the Node's nature. A knife completed in a Node of ancient violence gains the Brutal tag without a hit cost. An amulet completed in a healing spring's Node gains Self-Repairing. The Resonance Tag is an opportunity, not always an advantage — the Storyguide may sometimes compel it.

### Node Dot Ratings and Crafting Effects

| Node Rating | Quintessence per Crafting Scene  \|  Difficulty Reduction  \|  Resonance Tag Quality |
| --- | --- |
| ● (Minor) | 1 Quintessence  \|  None  \|  Weak resonance; tag has limited invoke potential |
| ●● (Modest) | 2 Quintessence  \|  None  \|  Clear resonance; standard tag invoke/compel |
| ●●● (Significant) | 3 Quintessence  \|  –1 Difficulty (Milestone 2 only)  \|  Strong resonance; tag invoked with +1 Enhancement |
| ●●●● (Major) | 4 Quintessence  \|  –1 Difficulty (all Milestones)  \|  Powerful resonance; tag may count as two tags for invoke purposes |
| ••••• (Prime) | 5 Quintessence  \|  –2 Difficulty (all Milestones)  \|  Extraordinary resonance; item begins play with Awakened tag |

### Controlling and Defending a Crafting Node

A mage or cabal that controls a Node controls a significant strategic resource. Other mages will want access to it. The most common sources of intergroup conflict in Mage chronicles are Node disputes — not philosophical disagreements about the nature of reality, but hard-nosed resource competition. A Node that produces 3–5 Quintessence per scene per crafter, sustained indefinitely, is worth more than most Wonders.

Nodes can be depleted by excessive use. The Storyguide tracks Node health — a heavily used Node without time to recover between uses may reduce its dot rating temporarily. Nodes can also be Tainted by Paradox events, by Nephandi influence, or by deliberate sabotage. A Tainted Node still provides Quintessence but its Resonance is corrupted, and items crafted in it gain the Resonance Tag of the Taint rather than the Node's original nature.

> NODE CRAFTING EXAMPLE — THE VERBENA GROVE
> 
> Rosamund's cabal controls a ●●● Life-aspected Node in an old-growth forest. Rosamund is crafting a Cutting-Edge Talisman (a braided hawthorn-and-iron torc with Life ●●● healing effects and Spirit ●● spirit-sight) over several sessions.
> 
> Benefits:
>   Quintessence Supply: 3 Quintessence per crafting scene. Over three milestones across three scenes, she gains 9 Quintessence total from the Node — enough to cover most of her personal Quintessence costs during infusion.
> 
>   Difficulty Reduction: Milestone 2 (Infusion of Life ●●● effects) has its Difficulty reduced by 1. Life ●●● = Difficulty 3, reduced to Difficulty 2. This is significant — a 1-point Difficulty reduction on a 6-die pool is roughly equivalent to +1–2 additional expected hits.
> 
>   Resonance Bonding: The torc is completed in a Life-aspected ancient-growth Node. It gains Self-Repairing as a free Tag — the torc will gradually restore its own integrity when in contact with living matter (worn against skin or pressed against a tree). It also absorbs a Nature Resonance Tag that Rosamund must manage: Verbena can invoke it, but in a Technocracy-heavy urban environment, the smell of old wood and green growth that clings to the torc may attract unwanted attention.
> 
> The three-milestone process:
>   Milestone 1 (Difficulty 1): Foundation — hawthorn and iron worked together. Enchanted, Natural, Resonant (Verbena) tags allocated. 2 hits. +3 QE from Node.
>   Milestone 2 (Difficulty 2, reduced from 3): Infusion — Life ●●● and Spirit ●● Effects embedded. 3 hits. 1 Free hit spent on the Spirit-sight Effect. +3 QE from Node.
>   Milestone 3 (Difficulty 3): Completion — Self-Repairing tag from Node Resonance bonding gained free. +3 QE from Node.
> 
> Final torc: Enhancement +1 to Life and Spirit magic rolls while worn. Tags: [Natural], [Enchanted], [Resonant (Verbena)], [Self-Repairing]. Effects: Life ●●● healing touch, Spirit ●● awareness.

## Fetish Binding

A Fetish is not merely an enchanted object — it is a prison, a partnership, or a relationship, depending on the mage's philosophy and the spirit's disposition. Binding a spirit into a physical vessel is among the most distinctive arts of the Dreamspeakers, though practitioners from every Tradition have developed their own versions of the practice. Verbena mages call them spirit-vessels and treat them with the same respect due any living agreement. Order of Hermes practitioners call them spirit-seals and approach the binding as a formal contract with binding terms. Technocratic operatives who work with bound spirits (primarily Void Engineers and certain Progenitor researchers) use clinical language — "contained entity deployment" — but the mechanics are identical.

The distinction between a Fetish and other Wonders is fundamental: the power of a Fetish is the spirit's power. The mage who holds the vessel commands access to whatever the spirit can do, but they do not understand it from the inside the way a mage understands their own Sphere work. This makes Fetishes simultaneously morepowerful and more dangerous than standard Wonders: more powerful because the spirit may have capabilities that exceed the mage's own Sphere ratings, more dangerous because a spirit that resents its binding has every incentive to cause the wielder harm, and does so from the inside of an item the mage is carrying.

Every Fetish has a spiritual awareness behind its power. When the mage activates a Fetish, they are not channeling their own Quintessence through a constructed Effect — they are requesting (or demanding, depending on binding strength) that the imprisoned spirit exercise its power on their behalf. This distinction matters enormously in play.

### Spirit Negotiation Before Binding

Before a spirit can be bound into a vessel, the mage must first locate and communicate with a suitable spirit, negotiate the terms of the binding, and prepare the physical vessel to accept spiritual habitation. This is a multi-stage process that begins well before any dice are rolled for the crafting itself.

The Storyguide determines the spirit's disposition based on the type of spirit and how the mage approaches it. A nature spirit that has been venerated and offered appropriate gifts is far more likely to accept a binding than one that has been summoned by force. The following table shows common spirit types and what they typically demand in exchange for voluntary binding.

| Spirit Type | Common Binding Demands |
| --- | --- |
| Nature spirit (animal, plant, weather) | Preservation of their associated habitat; periodic offerings (blood, earth, seasonal goods); promise that the vessel will be used in ways consistent with the spirit's nature (a wolf spirit in a war-axe is content; a wolf spirit in a surgical instrument is not). |
| Ancestor spirit (honored dead) | Memory — the mage must know and regularly recite the spirit's name and deeds. The vessel must be passed down within the spirit's lineage or trusted community. Betrayal of the family is grounds for rebellion. |
| Elemental (fire, water, earth, air) | Regular exposure to their element. A fire elemental in a torch-fetish must be lit at least once per moon. A water elemental in a vessel must be submerged monthly. Neglect = restlessness. |
| Urban spirit (city-fragment, street-soul) | Must be carried through its home neighborhood regularly. The fetish loses power if taken outside the city for more than a month. Thrives on human contact and activity. |
| Shade or ghost | Periodic contact with the living — the wielder must speak to the shade, update it on events in the world, treat it as a companion rather than a tool. Isolation drives shades erratic. |
| Gaffling (minor spirit servant) | Simplest demands: food-offerings of Quintessence once per scene used, or a specific physical offering. Gafflings are the most reliable Fetish spirits for this reason — low demands, low ambitions. |

Spirits that agree voluntarily to binding reduce the Difficulty of the Fetish Binding rolls by 1. Spirits that are coerced or trapped involuntarily increase the Difficulty by 1 and begin the binding with a Resentment Condition (see Spirit Rebellion below).

### Fetish Binding — The Three Steps

Fetish Binding does not use the standard Wonder milestone structure. It has its own three-step process, each requiring a roll, each with its own consequence for failure.

Step 1 — Vessel Preparation (Prime ●● minimum): The mage prepares the physical object to receive a spirit. This requires spending 1 Quintessence into the vessel and rolling Arete vs. Difficulty 2. Failure means the object cannot hold a spirit — the Quintessence is lost and the process must begin again with fresh materials. Exceptional success (3+ free hits) means the vessel has Resonance that actively attracts compatible spirits, reducing the time needed to locate one.

Step 2 — Spirit Binding (Spirit ●●● minimum): With the spirit present (willing or otherwise), the mage performs the binding ritual — usually a full scene of ceremony. Roll Arete vs. Difficulty 3 (voluntary spirit) or Difficulty 4 (coerced). Each hit beyond the first may be used to embed one Spirit Power the spirit can exercise through the vessel (the spirit's native abilities, not the mage's Spheres). Failure at this step releases the spirit and may anger it. A botch causes the spirit to immediately possess a nearby object or person if possible.

Step 3 — Binding Seal (Prime ●●● to prevent degradation): The mage seals the binding permanently. Roll Arete vs. Difficulty 3. Success means the Fetish is complete. Failure at this step means the binding is temporary — the spirit will work free after d10 days. A second attempt at Step 3 is possible, but each additional attempt gives the spirit one additional chance to resist (increase Difficulty by 1 per additional attempt).

> FETISH BINDING EXAMPLE — THE DREAMSPEAKER'S WOLF DRUM
> 
> Tomás, a Dreamspeaker (Arete ●●●, Spirit ●●●, Life ●●), wishes to create a war-drum Fetish housing a wolf-pack fragment spirit. He has located a suitable Gaffling — a minor wolf-spirit that haunts the hills outside his community — and spent three nights offering deer blood and howling at the moon. The spirit is amenable.
> 
> Step 1 — Vessel Preparation:
>   The drum is stretched elk hide over a cedar frame. Tomás spends 1 Quintessence and rolls Arete (3 dice) vs. Difficulty 2.
>   ROLL: 3, 4, 6 → 2 hits. Meets Difficulty. The drum hums with acceptance.
>
