> Step 2 — Spirit Binding (Voluntary — Difficulty 3):
>   Tomás performs the binding ceremony at a moonrise. Rolls 3 dice vs. Difficulty 3.
>   ROLL: 2, 5, 6 → 2 hits. Meets Difficulty (1 hit) with 1 free hit.
>   Base binding: the wolf-spirit is sealed into the drum.
>   Free hit: Tomás embeds one Spirit Power — Pack Sense. When the drum is beaten and 1 Quintessence spent, anyone who can hear the drum knows the location of all allies within 500 feet (wolf-pack awareness).
> 
> Step 3 — Binding Seal (Difficulty 3):
>   ROLL: 1, 5, 6 → 2 hits. Success. The binding is permanent.
> 
> FINAL FETISH — WOLF DRUM:
>   Tags: [Enchanted], [Resonant (Dreamspeaker/Wilderness)]
>   Spirit Power: Pack Sense — beat the drum and spend 1 Quintessence; all allies who can hear know each other's locations for one scene.
>   Spirit Demands: The drum must be beaten at least once per moon during a ceremony. Tomás must occasionally speak to the spirit within it — acknowledge the wolf.
>   Spirit Disposition: Content (voluntary binding, demands being honored).

### Spirit Rebellion

A spirit bound into a Fetish is not dead and not fully controlled. It can influence events from within its vessel. Rebellion occurs when:

- The wielder violates the binding terms (neglects demands, betrays stated purposes).
- The spirit was bound involuntarily and has not been appeased.
- The mage uses the Fetish in ways deeply contrary to the spirit's nature.
- An extended period of Paradox exposure destabilizes the binding.
Rebellion comes in three grades:

- Minor Rebellion: The Fetish simply fails to activate for one scene. No roll, no damage — the spirit goes silent. This is the spirit's warning.
- Moderate Rebellion: The Fetish activates, but against the wielder's interest. A weapon-fetish might choose an inopportune moment to discharge its power at the wielder. A healing fetish might draw Quintessence without the mage's consent. The Storyguide compels this as a Moderate Complication.
- Major Rebellion: The spirit attempts to fully reject the binding and escape. The mage must make a contested Spirit ●●● roll (Arete vs. the spirit's Resistance, typically 2–4 dice) to maintain the binding. Failure means the spirit tears free, destroying the vessel in the process, and the freed spirit is now hostile.
Resolution: Minor Rebellion can be resolved with appropriate offerings and a Spirit ●● roll vs. Difficulty 2 (one scene of ceremony). Moderate Rebellion requires Spirit ●●● and a Difficulty 3 roll (full scene ritual). Major Rebellion that fails its contested roll cannot be resolved — the Fetish is lost.

## Crafting Quick Reference

The table below summarises every crafting track in this appendix. Storyguides may photocopy or screenshot this page as a session reference.

| Crafting Type | Key Rules |
| --- | --- |
| MUNDANE — Standard | Difficulty 1. Enhancement +1 max. 2 tags max. Each hit above Difficulty = 1 free allocation. |
| MUNDANE — Master Work | Difficulty 2, double time, exceptional materials. Enhancement +2 max. 3 tags max. Same allocation rule. |
| Mundane Failure | Item not created; time and materials lost. No Flaw, no penalty — try again next scene. |
| Mundane Botch | 0 hits AND more than half all dice show 1. Item destroyed; materials lost; tool damage possible. |
| WONDER — Trinket | Point budget 3. Difficulty 1 (M1), Sphere rating (M2), 2 (M3). |
| WONDER — Cutting-Edge | Point budget 5. Difficulty 1 (M1), Sphere rating (M2), 3 (M3). |
| WONDER — Exotic | Point budget 8. Difficulty 2 (M1), Sphere rating (M2), 4 (M3). |
| WONDER — Legendary | Point budget 12. Difficulty 2 (M1), Sphere rating (M2), 5 (M3). |
| Milestone: Botch | 0 hits + half-dice are 1s → permanent Flaw sealed into item. Milestone counts as failed. |
| Milestone: Failure | 0 hits, no botch → stalls. No Flaw. Retry next scene. |
| Milestone: Near-miss | hits < Difficulty → Flaw introduced. Removable at a later milestone (costs 2 free hits). |
| Milestone: Success | hits = Difficulty → milestone complete, no free hits. |
| Milestone: Strong | hits > Difficulty by 1–2 → 1–2 free allocation hits. |
| Milestone: Exceptional | hits > Difficulty by 3+ → free hits AND next milestone wait reduced. |
| Flaw (Near-miss M2) | Removable at M3 for 2 free hits. If not removed, becomes permanent at M3 completion. |
| Flaw (Near-miss M3) | Permanent — cannot be removed post-completion. |
| Flaw (Botch, any) | Permanent — sealed regardless of remaining milestones. |
| Quick Fix (Trick) | Wits + Crafts vs. failed milestone Difficulty. Success removes or downgrades Flaw. |
| Efficient Process (Trick) | 2 free hits: compress wait between milestones. Next milestone Difficulty +1. |
| FETISH — Step 1 (Vessel) | Prime ●●. Arete vs. Difficulty 2. Prepares vessel to hold a spirit. |
| FETISH — Step 2 (Bind) | Spirit ●●●. Arete vs. Difficulty 3 (willing) or 4 (coerced). Free hits = Spirit Powers. |
| FETISH — Step 3 (Seal) | Prime ●●●. Arete vs. Difficulty 3. Locks spirit in permanently. |
| Voluntary spirit | Difficulty –1 on all three steps. |
| Coerced spirit | Difficulty +1 on all three steps; begins with Resentment Flaw. |

---

# Appendix T: Vehicles

Vehicles in Mage: The Ascension are rarely just transportation. A motorcycle is freedom and mobility in the Ascension War. A nondescript panel van is a surveillance platform, a mobile Chantry, or a getaway vehicle. A stolen ambulance is cover and emergency medical capacity simultaneously. A Technocratic Construct's field team always has a vehicle, and that vehicle is almost certainly not mundane.

This appendix covers vehicles using the Storypath Ultra vehicle framework, adapted for the Mage setting. All vehicles are defined by size, speed, handling, injury capacity, and tags. Mundane vehicles are presented first, followed by vehicles specifically modified by Mage-world factions. Vehicle combat and chase sequences close the appendix, with detailed examples.

A note on the Mage context: vehicles do not generate Paradox simply by being vehicles. However, a vehicle that has been enchanted — whose behavior violates what the Consensus accepts as normal — becomes a potential Paradox source. An enchanted car that drives itself, a motorcycle that becomes invisible, or a van that teleports between locations will generate Paradox in the same way any other Vulgar effect does. The tag Enchanted on a vehicle is therefore both a capability descriptor and a warning.

## How Vehicles Work

Every vehicle has the following traits:

- Size (1–5): Determines the vehicle's base injury capacity (Size + 1 injury boxes), tag limit (up to Size tags), and the scale at which it operates. A motorcycle is Size 1; an aircraft carrier is Size 5.
- Speed Advantage: The vehicle's base Speed Advantage. Higher values allow covering distances faster and winning chase rolls. The vehicle's Fast tags add to this.
- Handling: The base Complication that applies to all control rolls. A negative number is a bonus; a positive number is a penalty. Sports cars handle well; 18-wheelers handle poorly.
- Injury Boxes: (Size + 1) base. Damaged by successful attacks. Each injury box lost represents vehicle damage. At 0 injury boxes, the vehicle is disabled.
- Tags: Each vehicle can have up to its Size in tags (iconic vehicles can have more). Tags modify capability in specific ways.

### Vehicle Control Actions

Operating a vehicle requires a Dexterity + Drive (or Pilot) action when the situation is challenging — high-speed maneuvers, evasion, off-road driving, combat driving. The vehicle's Handling adds to or reduces the Difficulty of these rolls. In non-challenging situations (normal road driving, taxiing an aircraft), no roll is required.

In Phase Initiative, vehicle actions resolve at the driver's Phase. The vehicle's Speed Advantage adds to any roll to close distance, maintain position, or escape. Vehicle weapon attacks are made by the gunner (not the driver) at their own Phase unless the vehicle's weapons are AI-controlled.

### Vehicle Damage

Attacking a vehicle requires hitting the vehicle itself, not the occupants. Occupants inside an enclosed vehicle benefit from the vehicle's injury boxes as cover (treat as Cover (Solid) or Cover (Near-Total) at Storyguide discretion). Open vehicles (motorcycles, convertibles, open trucks) provide only partial cover — attacks directed at a motorcycle rider hit the rider directly.

When a vehicle reaches 0 injury boxes, it is disabled: an aircraft falls, a car stops functioning, a boat sinks. Occupants may take damage from the crash equal to the Storyguide's judgment based on speed and circumstances. Vehicles do not have a condition track like characters — they are either functional, damaged (50% or fewer injury boxes remaining), or destroyed (0 injury boxes). A damaged vehicle imposes –1 die to Drive rolls and any vehicle-mounted attack rolls.

### Vehicles, Phase Initiative, and Character Health Bands

Vehicle scenes integrate with the Phase Initiative system described in Appendix F. The driver acts at their Phase; passengers and gunners act at their own Phases independently. This creates multi-Phase vehicle encounters where a driver's evasive maneuver at Phase 3 changes the context for a gunner firing at Phase 1 next round. Speed Factor for vehicle-mounted attacks uses the character's own Speed Factor for the weapon being used — but adds a Complication equal to the vehicle's Speed for unstabilized firing.

When characters inside vehicles take damage (from attacks that pierce the vehicle's cover, from crashes, or from ramming), that damage fills their personal Health Track using the 4-band system normally. Vehicle cover reduces incoming damage first — subtract the vehicle's effective cover protection before applying remaining hits to the character's health boxes. For crashes and ramming, the Storyguide translates vehicle damage into character damage using the following guide:

| Vehicle Event | Character Health Band Impact (if inside vehicle) |
| --- | --- |
| Minor collision or controlled stop (1–2 vehicle injury) | Characters inside take 1–2 hits of Bludgeoning damage. Likely keeps characters in Bruised band. Seat-belted characters: halve damage (round down). |
| Significant collision (3–4 vehicle injury) | Characters inside take 3–4 hits of Bludgeoning damage, potentially reaching Impaired band. Airbag deployment (if present) reduces by 1 hit. Unbelted characters take full damage. |
| Severe crash or direct ram (5–6 vehicle injury) | Characters inside take 4–6 hits of Bludgeoning damage. Impaired or Critical band likely. Characters may be knocked unconscious (Storyguide discretion if in Critical band). |
| Vehicle destroyed (0 injury, high speed) | Characters inside take 6–8 hits. Critical band and potential Incapacitation. Life ●● healing magic or Progenitor intervention required to avoid long-term consequences. |
| Motorcycle crash at speed (any) | Rider takes damage equal to vehicle Speed as Bludgeoning hits (minimum 2). No vehicle cover — direct impact. Leather riding gear (Soak 2, Flexible) is the only mitigation. |

Magical mitigation: a mage with active Forces ●● can cushion a crash as a reflexive action (spending Quintessence), reducing crash damage by their Forces rating. Life ●●● Self-Healing can stabilize a character in the Critical band immediately after a crash. Matter ●●● can reinforce a vehicle in the moment of impact — this is a rare but possible use of reactive magic in vehicle scenes.

## Mundane Vehicles

These are the vehicles of everyday life in the modern world. Any character can access them through purchase, rental, theft, or borrowing. None carry supernatural properties, though any of them can be enchanted using the crafting rules in Appendix S.

### Ground Vehicles

```
MOTORCYCLE  •  Ground Vehicle  •  Size 1
----------------------------------------
Speed: 3  |  Handling: 0  |  Injury: 2
Tags: Maneuverable, Fast

The quintessential urban pursuit and escape vehicle. Size 1 means only the rider and a single passenger fit. Maneuverable and Fast provide real advantages in congested environments — a motorcycle can thread traffic that completely stops a car. The open design means the rider is not protected by the vehicle: all attacks against a motorcycle rider in combat hit the rider directly (treat vehicle as Light Cover only).

In Mage chronicles: Motorcycles are associated with freedom, speed, and survivability in Ascension War fieldwork. A mage on a motorcycle can escape almost anything through urban terrain if they use terrain creatively. The downside: if shot, the motorcycle provides minimal protection.

Invoke: "Maneuverable — I thread through the traffic jam. Speed Advantage applies. They cannot follow."
Compel: "Size 1. You cannot carry this equipment on a motorcycle. You need a different vehicle."
```

```
SEDAN (STANDARD CAR)  •  Ground Vehicle  •  Size 2
--------------------------------------------------
Speed: 2  |  Handling: 0  |  Injury: 3
Tags: (base only, standard car)

The workhorse vehicle. Four doors, room for four people plus cargo, reliable, unremarkable. No tags in its base form — a standard sedan is vanilla. Its advantage is exactly that: unremarkable. An NWO surveillance team driving a common sedan in a city neighborhood attracts no attention. A fleeing mage in a stolen Toyota is indistinguishable from anyone else fleeing in a stolen Toyota.

Base sedans have no tags to invoke or compel in the vehicle sense. The Storyguide may apply environmental tags (Difficult Terrain for off-road) or narrative tags from specific circumstances.

Invoke: "Unremarkable — the surveillance car blends. Nobody notices we are parked here for four hours."
Compel: "Standard handling means this car cannot take that corner at that speed without rolling."
```

```
SPORTS CAR  •  Ground Vehicle  •  Size 2
----------------------------------------
Speed: 3  |  Handling: –1 (bonus)  |  Injury: 3
Tags: Fast, Maneuverable

A performance-tuned vehicle: higher top speed than a standard car (Speed 3 vs. 2), better handling (–1 Complication on control rolls), and the Maneuverable tag for aggressive driving situations. Still Size 2 — four seats at most, limited cargo. Not subtle. A sports car in pursuit or fleeing a pursuit is the stuff of chronicle legend, but it attracts attention when parked and is memorable to any witness.

Invoke: "Sports car — Fast and Maneuverable. I take the mountain road at full speed. They will never keep up."
Compel: "A Ferrari parked in this neighborhood for six hours. Every person on the block has noticed it."
Mage context: Sons of Ether mages often tinker obsessively with sports cars, adding prototype engine components that push Speed to 4 or 5 before adding any actual magic. The car becomes an Invention before it becomes a Talisman.
```

```
SUV / PICKUP TRUCK  •  Ground Vehicle  •  Size 2
------------------------------------------------
Speed: 2  |  Handling: +1 (penalty for extreme maneuvers)  |  Injury: 3
Tags: All-terrain, Cargo (Size 1 equivalent payload)

Four-wheel drive, high clearance, significant cargo capacity. The All-terrain tag reduces Complications from off-road conditions by 1 step — a truck can go places a sports car cannot. Cargo allows hauling up to two passengers plus significant equipment or one additional small vehicle equivalent in payload. The handling penalty means it cannot match a sports car on hairpin turns.

In Mage chronicles: The most practical vehicle for carrying equipment into remote Nodes, transporting a cabal plus gear, or operating off-road. Dreamspeakers and Verbena mages who work outside cities favor trucks. The NWO's standard field van is a Size 2 vehicle of this type.

Invoke: "All-terrain — the dirt track is not a problem. I keep driving."
Compel: "Large vehicle in a tight parking structure. You cannot make that turn without scraping both walls."
```

```
PANEL VAN / CARGO VAN  •  Ground Vehicle  •  Size 2
---------------------------------------------------
Speed: 2  |  Handling: +1 (penalty)  |  Injury: 3
Tags: Cargo (large), Huge (relative to passengers — can conceal people and equipment)

The standard vehicle for anyone who needs to move things without those things being visible. Panel vans seat two in the cab and have a large enclosed cargo area — effectively a mobile room. This cargo area is the appeal: it can carry a full cabal plus equipment, serve as a mobile surgery or ritual space, conceal personnel during surveillance operations, or transport materials that should not be seen.

The Huge tag (relative application) means the van can carry items or groups that smaller vehicles cannot. Combined with appropriate Technocratic or Tradition modifications, vans become rolling command centers.

Invoke: "Cargo — I fit the entire ritual setup in the van. The Node is mobile."
Compel: "A white panel van following someone through a neighborhood at low speed. Every alert resident has already called this in."
```

```
MOTORCYCLE (HIGH-PERFORMANCE)  •  Ground Vehicle  •  Size 1
-----------------------------------------------------------
Speed: 4  |  Handling: –1 (bonus)  |  Injury: 2
Tags: Maneuverable, Fast, Fast (second time: Speed becomes 4)

A purpose-built racing or superbike pushed to its mechanical limits. Speed 4 makes this the fastest ground vehicle in practical use. The handling bonus compensates somewhat for the speed. Still Size 1 — still exposes the rider. At Speed 4 in combat or pursuit, the rider is moving fast enough that even dedicated pursuers will struggle to maintain visual contact in complex urban terrain.

Invoke: "Speed 4 — by the time they orient to my position, I am three blocks away."
Compel: "Speed 4 on a city street near a school. The accident you are about to cause is not hypothetical."
```

### Air Vehicles

```
HELICOPTER (LIGHT)  •  Air Vehicle  •  Size 2
---------------------------------------------
Speed: 3  |  Handling: 0  |  Injury: 3
Tags: Maneuverable, Hover

A small civilian helicopter — the kind available for charter or theft in any major metropolitan area. The Hover tag means it can maintain stationary position, useful for surveillance, extraction, or supporting operations on rooftops or in open areas. Maneuverable at medium scale. Requires Pilot skill to operate; helicopter controls are not intuitive.

Injury 3 means it absorbs three attacks before going down — helicopters are fragile relative to their apparent size. A single heavy weapon hit, a magical attack, or coordinated small-arms fire can bring one down. Going down in a helicopter is generally lethal unless the mage has Life or Forces effects ready.

Invoke: "Hover — I hold position above the rooftop. The team extracts while I wait."
Compel: "Helicopter over a city. Every person with binoculars, every camera, every witness. Paradox implications for any magical action below."
Mage context: Void Engineers use military-grade helicopters fitted with dimensional sensors. NWO helicopters are frequently Devices with surveillance Enhancements and possibly a pilot AI.
```

```
FIXED-WING AIRCRAFT (LIGHT PROP)  •  Air Vehicle  •  Size 2
-----------------------------------------------------------
Speed: 3  |  Handling: +1 (penalty)  |  Injury: 3
Tags: Fast (overland), Long Range (travel distances)

A small Cessna-type aircraft. Faster than a helicopter over distance (300+ mph cruise), less maneuverable (needs runway or very long strip to land). Long Range tag means it can cover continental distances within a scene or session. The handling penalty applies to evasive maneuvers and low-altitude flying.

Invoke: "Long Range — we reach the Node site in two hours. No one can follow by road in time."
Compel: "Fixed-wing aircraft needs a runway. There is no runway here. You cannot land."
```

```
COMMERCIAL JET  •  Air Vehicle  •  Size 3
-----------------------------------------
Speed: 4  |  Handling: +2 (significant penalty)  |  Injury: 4
Tags: Cargo (substantial), Long Range
Crew Required (flaw): requires pilot + co-pilot at minimum

Represents a private or small commercial jet. Size 3 means real injury capacity (4 boxes) and space for equipment, personnel, and even small vehicles in the cargo hold. The Crew Required flaw means a mage cannot simply steal and solo-pilot a commercial jet without either Correspondence magic, Time magic, or a Technocratic autopilot system. Useful as a chronicle resource when the cabal controls a Chantry with financial resources.

Invoke: "Long Range — intercontinental flight within the scope of a scene."
Compel: "Commercial jet filed a flight plan. It is tracked by air traffic control. You are not as anonymous as you think."
```

### Water Vehicles

```
SPEEDBOAT  •  Water Vehicle  •  Size 2
--------------------------------------
Speed: 3  |  Handling: 0  |  Injury: 3
Tags: Fast, Maneuverable

A high-performance recreational or pursuit boat. Faster than most alternatives on open water, nimble enough for river and coastal work, but limited to water environments. Size 2 means limited crew and cargo. Used by Void Engineers for coastal patrol, by coastal Verbena cabals for access to offshore Node locations, and by anyone needing to move fast across water without air traffic control noticing.

Invoke: "Fast on water — nobody catching this boat from shore by the time we clear the harbor."
Compel: "Speedboat only. You cannot go overland. The coastal road is faster for their ground team."
```

```
YACHT (PRIVATE)  •  Water Vehicle  •  Size 3
--------------------------------------------
Speed: 2  |  Handling: +1  |  Injury: 4
Tags: Cargo, Long Range, Self-Sustaining (with fuel)

A private sailing or motor yacht of 40–60 feet. Slower than a speedboat but capable of crossing oceans. Long Range covers intercontinental sea routes. Cargo capacity adequate for a full cabal plus significant equipment and supplies. Self-Sustaining means the yacht can provide food, water, and shelter for the crew for extended periods at sea.

Invoke: "Long Range — the yacht carries us to the Mediterranean Node without filing a flight plan or airport checkpoint."
Compel: "A yacht is large enough to attract coast guard and customs attention in many ports."
Mage context: Financially comfortable Tradition mages sometimes maintain yachts as mobile Chantries. The Syndicate definitely does. A yacht Chantry typically has an occult library (tool, Huge), a ritual space (appropriate to the Tradition), and possibly a Node anchor if the cabal has spent Background points on a Sanctum at sea.
```

```
SUBMARINE (SMALL)  •  Water Vehicle  •  Size 3
----------------------------------------------
Speed: 2  |  Handling: 0  |  Injury: 4
Tags: Submersible, Stealthy

A miniaturized submarine — the kind operated by military special forces or, in the Mage setting, Void Engineers conducting deep-sea operations. The Submersible tag means it operates both on and under the water surface. The Stealthy tag (+2 Enhancement on all actions to avoid detection) represents the acoustic isolation of a submarine at depth: surface radar and sonar struggle to locate it.

Size 3 means room for a small crew and specialized equipment. Not a practical vehicle for most chronicles, but when deep-ocean Nodes, Umbral access points beneath the sea, or dimensional research requires it, a submarine becomes suddenly very relevant.

Invoke: "Stealthy at depth — we are invisible to their surface ships and air coverage."
Compel: "Submersible but very slow. Their surface craft are already at the destination."
```

## Mage-Modified Vehicles

Any mundane vehicle can become something more through Enlightened crafting. A mage with appropriate Spheres can enchant a vehicle exactly as they would enchant armor or a weapon — through the milestone system described in Appendix S. Vehicle enchantments tend to be Cutting-Edge or Groundbreaking rank because the scope of changes mages typically want to make (invisible, indestructible, self-repairing, dimensionally accessible) push against Mundane rank limits quickly.

Vehicle enchantments also generate more Paradox in practice than armor or weapon enchantments. A Bulletproof Hoodie that is Matter-enchanted does not draw attention when it stops a bullet — the mage still got shot, still shows the reaction, still moves normally. But a car that becomes invisible, a motorcycle that parks itself in impossible spaces, or a van that sprouts additional rooms inside — these are obvious violations of the Consensus the moment a Sleeper witnesses them. Vehicle enchantments should be designed with coincidental appearance in mind.

### Mage-Specific Vehicle Tags

In addition to the standard vehicle tags (Fast, Maneuverable, All-terrain, etc.), enchanted vehicles may carry the following tags unique to the Mage context. These tags are not available on mundane vehicles — they require at least one dot in the appropriate Sphere to create.

| Tag | Effect  \|  Sphere Required  \|  Invoke / Compel |
| --- | --- |
| Resonant (Vehicle) | The vehicle carries strong magical Resonance tied to a Tradition, place, or purpose. Mages of compatible Resonance gain +1 Enhancement on all Sphere castings while inside. Incompatible mages suffer –1 Enhancement.  \|  Any Sphere ●  \|  Invoke: "Resonant with Verbena — I cast Life ●●● from inside. +1 Enhancement." Compel: "Resonant with Verbena — the Technocratic scanner detects unusual energy emanating from this vehicle." |
| Warded (Vehicle) | The vehicle's interior is warded against specific threat types (spirits, scrying, mental intrusion). The ward provides Cover (+2 dice) against covered threat types. The warding requires maintenance — if the ward Resonance is violated, it must be recast.  \|  Prime ●● + relevant Sphere ●●  \|  Invoke: "Warded against scrying — their Correspondence ●●● cannot locate us while we are inside." Compel: "The ward registers as a magical anomaly on their scanners. They know something is here even if they cannot see what." |
| Cloaked | The vehicle is invisible or disguised to targeted perception — Sleepers or specific magic users see what the enchantment wants them to see (a different make/model, an empty parking space, nothing at all). The Cloaked tag fails when the vehicle interacts physically with the perceived environment — a Cloaked car cannot drive through traffic without becoming visible.  \|  Correspondence ●● (misdirection) or Mind ●●● (illusion)  \|  Invoke: "Cloaked — the surveillance camera recorded nothing. We were invisible." Compel: "The Cloaked tag works on perception. The parking ticket on the windshield means someone touched the car. The cloak broke." |
| Awakened (Vehicle) | The vehicle has a degree of spiritual awareness — it responds to mental commands, recognizes its designated operator, and has rudimentary preferences. An Awakened vehicle will not harm its owner even when operated by others, and will make choices (route, speed, evasion) that prioritize the owner's survival.  \|  Spirit ●●● or Prime ●●●●  \|  Invoke: "Awakened — the bike knows I am in danger. It revs and positions itself between me and the shooter." Compel: "Awakened means it has opinions. It refuses to enter the Nephandi-tainted zone. You must park outside." |

### Enchanted Vehicle Paradox — Specific Examples

Mages who enchant vehicles frequently underestimate the Paradox risk. The following specific scenarios clarify when enchanted vehicle effects generate Paradox:

| Enchanted Vehicle Behavior | Paradox Generated |
| --- | --- |
| Self-driving enchanted car navigates normally in traffic, no witnesses who see anything strange | None. Coincidental — a car driving itself is unusual but modern technology can do this. No Consensus breach. |
| Self-driving car parks itself in a space that is clearly occupied, Sleeper witnesses | Paradox. The car demonstrably displaced another vehicle without visible mechanism. Vulgar in context. |
| Invisible motorcycle (Cloaked) traveling on empty road, no witnesses | 1 die Paradox. Vulgar effect regardless of witnesses — the Cloaked tag suppresses Paradox dice from witnesses but does not eliminate the baseline Vulgar die. |
| Invisible motorcycle drives through a crowd | Full Paradox. People feel and hear the motorcycle but cannot see it. Many witnesses. Vulgar and witnessed. |
| Expanded Interior van: Sleeper looks in the back — no anomaly visible (partition in place) | None. The partition maintains coincidental appearance. No Consensus breach without direct exposure to the spatial impossibility. |
| Expanded Interior van: Sleeper opens the back and sees a room larger than the van | Full Paradox. Direct observation of a spatial impossibility. Vulgar and witnessed. |
| Phase-shifting motorcycle passes through a wall on an empty street | 1 die Paradox (Vulgar, unwitnessed). If any Sleeper sees it: full Paradox. |
| Warded vehicle interior: scrying bounce (caster sees nothing) | None for the vehicle. The scrying caster may generate Paradox from a failed Vulgar working, but the ward itself produces none. |
| Resonant vehicle used for casting that is otherwise coincidental | None. The Resonant tag enhances casting but does not make it more Vulgar. |

### Common Vehicle Enchantments

The enchantments below represent completed Wonders in vehicle form. They are examples; mages with appropriate Spheres can create variations. Each enchantment lists the Spheres required and the tags added. All enchanted vehicles gain the Enchanted tag automatically.

```
SELF-DRIVING ENCHANTMENT  •  Vehicle Enchantment  •  ●●●
--------------------------------------------------------
Sphere: Correspondence ●●, Mind ● (for awareness of environment)
Tags Added: Enchanted, (effectively negates Crew Required for operation)
Background Cost: 6 pts. (integrated into vehicle)

A Correspondence-infused autopilot that allows the vehicle to operate without a driver. The vehicle does not think — it follows instructions given to it through a Mental interface (touch the steering wheel and visualize the destination) and navigates independently. It will not take creative evasive action; it follows optimal routes. The mage remains in the vehicle as a passenger.

In conjunction with a Mind ● awareness, the vehicle can recognize obstacles and respond. It will stop for red lights if the mage desires coincidental cover. It will not dodge gunfire without a driver taking the wheel.

Invoke: "Self-driving — I do not need to pilot. I can cast while the car drives."
Compel: "The enchantment follows instructions, not initiative. It parked itself in the fire zone while you were occupied."
```

```
EXPANDED INTERIOR (THE TARDIS EFFECT)  •  Vehicle Enchantment  •  ●●●●
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Sphere: Correspondence ●●●●
Tags Added: Enchanted, Cargo (vastly expanded), Self-Sustaining
Background Cost: 9 pts.

A Correspondence ●●●● enchantment that separates the interior spatial dimension of the vehicle from its exterior dimensions. The van appears to be a standard panel van from outside. Inside, the spatial volume is dramatically larger — a standard van becomes a mobile Chantry with a library, ritual space, living quarters for six, and equipment storage.

This is Vulgar if a Sleeper steps inside and notices the discrepancy. Mages working with the van must control who enters it. The Stealthily tag may accompany this enchantment in well-designed versions — the exterior appearance is designed to explain away any noise or light that might leak out.

Invoke: "Expanded Interior — the full cabal fits comfortably. The ritual requires a 15-foot circle and we have that."
Compel: "A Sleeper got in the van. They are now standing in a room significantly larger than the van. This is a problem."
```

```
PHASE-SHIFTING MOTORCYCLE  •  Vehicle Talisman  •  ●●●●
-------------------------------------------------------
Base: High-Performance Motorcycle (Size 1, Speed 4)
Sphere: Matter ●●●●, Correspondence ●●●
Tags Added: Enchanted, Awakened (barely), Stealthy
Background Cost: 10 pts.

Matter ●●●● allows the motorcycle to pass through solid materials of Size 1 or smaller (a wall, a closed gate, a parked car blocking an alley). During phase-shifting, the rider must also phase — they are pulled along with the bike. This is brief — maximum 2 seconds of physical phasing — but sufficient to pass through a wall and emerge on the other side. Correspondence ●●● allows the bike to reappear within 100 meters of any entrance it has previously mapped.

The Stealthy tag reflects that the bike runs silent when phase-shifting — no engine noise while passing through solid matter.

Vulgar when witnessed by Sleepers. A motorcycle that passes through a wall is not coincidental.

Invoke: "Phase-shift through the wall. I am through before the guard turns."
Compel: "Phase-shifting is Brief. You calculated wrong. You rematerialized inside the wall. This is bad."
```

## Technocratic Vehicles

Technocratic Constructs field specialized vehicles that blend advanced technology with Enlightened Science. Unlike Tradition enchantments, these vehicles are designed to appear as advanced but plausible technology — the Technocratic Paradigm keeps them coincidental even as they exceed mundane capability. An SMC vehicle is extraordinary, but nothing about it violates the Consensus. It is science, not magic.

```
MICON KATANA (SMC COMPACT)  •  Technocratic Vehicle  •  ●●●
-----------------------------------------------------------
Size: 1  |  Speed: 4  |  Handling: –2 (bonus)  |  Injury: 2
Tags: Enchanted (Invention), Technological, Fast, Maneuverable, Stealthy, Anti-Theft
Enlightenment: 3  |  Background Cost: 6 pts.

An Enlightened performance compact — looks like an aggressive sports car, drives like nothing commercially available. Speed 4, –2 handling bonus (exceptional), and the Stealthy tag (visual and acoustic signature reduction). The Anti-Theft tag means attempting to hotwire or otherwise steal the Katana without biometric authorization provides a Moderate Complication. Onboard systems include navigation linked to Technocratic traffic databases and passive surveillance equipment.

Invoke: "Maneuverable with –2 bonus — I take the intersection at full speed. No control check needed."
Compel: "Anti-Theft system logged the approach and flagged it. NWO knows someone tried to access this vehicle."
```

```
MAT-V SUPERVAN (SMC FIELD OPERATIONS)  •  Technocratic Vehicle  •  ●●●
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Size: 3  |  Speed: 2  |  Handling: 0  |  Injury: 6
Tags: Enchanted (Invention), Technological, Cargo, Armor (3 — three Armor tags), Anti-Theft, Authoritative
Enlightenment: 3  |  Background Cost: 6 pts.

The standard NWO field operations vehicle. Size 3 means substantial crew and equipment capacity. Three Armor tags provide a vehicle Armor rating of 3 — attacks against the van must overcome 3 points of damage absorption before dealing Injury. Against magical attacks without the Armor tag's type being matched, the armor may not apply (Storyguide discretion based on attack type). The Authoritative tag means the van projects institutional authority: law enforcement, federal agency, or corporate security, depending on how it is marked.

Interior equipment: NOT (Neuro-Optical Transmitter) capability, surveillance monitors, communications suite, and Enlightened medical equipment (Life ●●). The van is effectively a rolling Construct annex.

Invoke: "Armor 3 — the attack hits the van, not us. 3 damage absorbed."
Compel: "MAT-V is recognizable to anyone familiar with NWO operations. The Traditions have seen this vehicle before."
```

```
SPECTRE MARK IV (SMC PURSUIT)  •  Technocratic Vehicle  •  ●●●
--------------------------------------------------------------
Size: 2  |  Speed: 5  |  Handling: –2 (bonus)  |  Injury: 3
Tags: Enchanted (Invention), Technological, Fast, Fast (Speed 5), Maneuverable, Stealthy
Enlightenment: 4  |  Background Cost: 6 pts.

The Technocracy's dedicated pursuit vehicle. Speed 5 is the highest available in standard vehicle equipment. Two Fast tags stacked achieve this. The –2 handling bonus means control rolls almost never fail. Stealthy tag provides acoustic damping and optical signature reduction — the Spectre is difficult to hear at speed and nearly invisible on radar.

Used for high-priority target interdiction, agent extraction under fire, and situations where nothing on the road can catch or escape from it.

Invoke: "Speed 5 — nothing built conventionally catches this vehicle."
Compel: "The Spectre is specialized for speed and pursuit. It is not armored. One hit in the right place disables it."
```

```
SOW 18-WHEELER (SMC HEAVY TRANSPORT)  •  Technocratic Vehicle  •  ●●●
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Size: 4  |  Speed: 2  |  Handling: +3 (significant penalty)  |  Injury: 8
Tags: Enchanted (Invention), Technological, Cargo (Size 3 equivalent), Armor (2), Huge, Reinforced
Enlightenment: 3  |  Background Cost: 6 pts.
Crew Required (flaw): driver + one technician for full function

A heavy-transport vehicle built for moving Construct equipment, HIT Marks, or dimensional machinery across continental distances. Size 4 provides exceptional injury capacity (8 boxes). Armor 2 absorbs damage. Huge means it carries enormous payloads — other vehicles, dimensional gateway equipment, field hospitals. The handling penalty is severe: at +3 Complication, maneuvering the SoW in an urban environment is genuinely difficult.

Used by Iteration X for HIT Mark deployment, by the NWO for mobile detention, and by Progenitors for field laboratory transport.

Invoke: "Cargo Size 3 — we load the HIT Marks and the entire field kit. Everything fits."
Compel: "+3 Handling means this vehicle cannot navigate the city center effectively. Plan around it."
```

## Unique and Legendary Vehicles

Some vehicles in the Mage setting transcend the category of transportation entirely. They are Wonders in vehicle form — artifacts with Resonance, history, and sometimes personality. The examples below are not stat blocks so much as concepts; the Storyguide should develop them with full Talisman or Device mechanics as appropriate.

- The Sailing Chantry: A full-rigged sailing vessel (Size 4) whose hull is Enchanted wood grown from trees in a Life-aspected Node. The ship navigates the Horizon beyond the Gauntlet as easily as it sails physical water. Interior expanded through Correspondence to contain a library, ritual space, and workshop. The ship has an Awakened Tag — it is old enough to have absorbed the Resonance of fifty years of use.
- The Iron Golem-Cycle: An Iteration X creation — a motorcycle (Size 1) built around an awakened machine-spirit. The spirit can operate the cycle independently, can interface with digital systems within 100 yards, and is fanatically loyal to its designated operator. Tags include Awakened, Technological, Fast, Maneuverable, Anti-Theft, and Worn (the spirit refuses to accept another operator without a formal transfer protocol).
- The Dimensional Crawler: A vehicle that exists in multiple physical and metaphysical states simultaneously. A Sons of Ether custom-built crawler — visually resembling a oversized Victorian steam locomotive — that can navigate the Deep Umbra under its own power. Correspondence ●●●●● and Dimensional Science ●●●●● integrated. Tags include Enchanted, Awakened, Submersible (metaphysically — can submerge into the Umbra), Self-Sustaining, and Stealthy in Umbral space. If it is destroyed in the physical world, it rebuilds itself over the course of a year in the Umbra.

## Vehicle Combat and Chase Sequences

Vehicles transform combat in several ways: they move faster than people, they provide cover, they can be weaponized directly (ramming), and they introduce terrain in a dynamic way. Chase sequences specifically pit two or more vehicles against each other in a test of speed, skill, and resource management.

### Vehicle Combat — Basic Rules

In Phase Initiative (Appendix F), a vehicle acts at its driver's Phase. The vehicle's Speed Advantage applies to any roll involving closing distance, maintaining distance, or escaping. Occupants inside the vehicle can act at their own Phase — a passenger can shoot from a car window, cast a spell, or perform first aid on an injured ally, all at their own Phase separate from the driver's action.

Attacking from a moving vehicle imposes a Complication equal to the vehicle's Speed (representing the instability of firing from a moving platform). This Complication can be bought off with hits, mitigated by stabilizing (bracing against the vehicle, taking an aimed shot), or bypassed entirely by magic that does not require physical accuracy (an area effect, a targeted Mind attack).

Attacking a moving vehicle from outside it requires dealing with the Moving Target condition: –1 die to all attacks against any vehicle that has moved this round. Vehicles larger than Size 2 are harder to maneuver but easier to hit — the Moving Target penalty does not apply to Size 3+ vehicles in straight-line pursuit.

### Ramming

A vehicle can be used as a weapon directly. Ramming is a Dexterity + Drive action opposed by the target's evasion roll (if the target's driver is aware) or by Difficulty 2 for stationary or unaware targets. On a success, the ram deals damage equal to the attacking vehicle's Size to the target vehicle's injury track. The ramming vehicle also takes damage equal to half the target's Size (rounded up) — even winners take damage.

A ram from behind or at full speed adds the vehicle's Speed Advantage to the damage. A ram from a perpendicular angle uses normal Size damage. A pit maneuver or controlled fender-bender at low speed deals 1 damage regardless of Size, intended to spin the target out rather than destroy it.

When characters are inside rammed vehicles, use the vehicle event table above (Vehicles, Phase Initiative, and Character Health Bands) to determine occupant damage. A ram that deals 5 vehicle injury is a severe crash — occupants take 4–6 hits of health damage. A ram that deals 1 vehicle injury (pit maneuver) may spin the target but leaves occupants in the Bruised band at worst.

> RAMMING EXAMPLE — SIZE MISMATCH AND HEALTH BANDS
> 
> An NWO MAT-V Supervan (Size 3, Speed 2, Armor 3, Injury 6) executes a ram against Elias's Sedan (Size 2, Speed 2, Injury 3). The MAT-V driver is using a pursuit and capture tactic — they want to disable the Sedan without destroying its occupants.
> 
> Pit maneuver (low speed, 1 damage): The MAT-V nudges the Sedan's rear quarter. 1 injury to the Sedan (down to 2 boxes). Occupants take 1 hit Bludgeoning — barely Bruised band. The Sedan can still drive but is forced to turn.
> 
> Full-speed T-bone (Size 3 damage + Speed Advantage 2 = 5 damage): The MAT-V hits the Sedan from the side at full speed. Sedan takes 5 injury (down to –2, destroyed). MAT-V takes half Sedan Size = 1 injury (barely dented — Armor 3 absorbs most of it). Sedan occupants: severe crash. 4–6 hits of Bludgeoning damage. Elias (3 boxes of his Health Track already used from earlier damage) is now potentially in the Critical band.
> 
> The lesson: ramming scales with Size differential. A large Technocratic vehicle used as a ram is devastating to smaller targets — and the occupants of those targets suffer accordingly through the health band system.

### Chase Sequences

A chase is an extended conflict using vehicle stats and driver skill. Both pursuer and quarry establish their starting relative position (Close, Short, Medium, or Long range). Each round, both sides roll their Speed Advantage plus relevant Skill (Drive, Pilot). The winner adjusts relative position by one range band in their favor.

A pursuer wins by reaching Close range (they can now ram, board, or have occupants leap between vehicles). A quarry wins by reaching Long range and maintaining it for one additional round (they have successfully escaped).

Environmental factors apply as Tags: Difficult Terrain applies its standard effect. Pursuing in a Speed 3 vehicle against a Speed 5 vehicle is itself a Complication on the pursuer's roll equal to the Speed difference (2). A driver with the Prepared condition has Enhancement +2 on their first chase roll. Stealthy vehicles add Enhancement on their speed roll if the pursuer has not yet identified them.

> SIMPLE CHASE EXAMPLE — MOTORCYCLE VS. SPORTS CAR (3 ROUNDS)
> 
> Setup: A Cultist of Ecstasy (Drive ●●, Dexterity ●●●) on a standard Motorcycle (Speed 3, Maneuverable) is pursued by an NWO operative (Drive ●●●, Dexterity ●●●) in a Sports Car (Speed 3, Fast, Maneuverable). Both start at Short range.
> 
> Goal: Quarry wants to reach Long range and maintain it for 1 round. Pursuer wants to reach Close range.
> 
> --- Round 1 ---
> Quarry (motorcycle): Speed 3 + Drive ●● + Dex ●●● = Speed 3 + 5 dice = base. Rolls: 3 hits. +3 (Speed) = net 3 advantage.
> Pursuer (sports car): Speed 3 + Drive ●●● + Dex ●●● = Speed 3 + 6 dice. Rolls: 4 hits. +3 (Speed) = net 4 advantage.
> Pursuer wins by 1. Range closes from Short to Close.
> 
> --- Round 2 ---
> Quarry invokes Maneuverable: "I cut through the outdoor market — a car cannot follow." +1 Enhancement on chase roll. Rolls 5 hits. Net: 5 + 3 = 8.
> Pursuer: Difficult Terrain Complication +1 (attempting to follow through market). 5 dice vs. Complication 1: 3 hits free. Net: 3 + 3 = 6.
> Quarry wins by 2. Range moves from Close to Medium. The Maneuverable tag made the difference.
> 
> --- Round 3 ---
> Quarry: Dexterity + Drive = 5 dice. Rolls 4 hits. Net: 4 + 3 = 7.
> Pursuer: 6 dice. Rolls 5 hits. Net: 5 + 3 = 8.
> Pursuer wins by 1. Range closes from Medium back to Short.
> 
> Result: Even after 3 rounds, neither party has won. The chase continues into the next scene. The Maneuverable tag bought the quarry a crucial round — without it, they would likely be at Close range by Round 3. This is the typical rhythm of a chase: advantages and terrain matter as much as raw Speed.

> VEHICLE COMBAT EXAMPLE — THE PANHANDLE CHASE
> 
> The Setup:
> Elias (Order of Hermes, Forces ●●●, Arete ●●●) and his cabal have just extracted an important grimoire from an NWO safe house. They are in a standard Sedan (Speed 2, Handling 0, Injury 3). Two NWO operatives are pursuing in a MAT-V Supervan (Speed 2, Handling 0, Injury 6, Armor 3) and a Spectre Mark IV pursuit vehicle (Speed 5, Handling –2, Injury 3, Stealthy).
> 
> Round 1:
> Starting position: all vehicles at Short range of each other.
> 
> Elias (driving Sedan): Dexterity ●●● + Drive ●● = 5 dice. Speed 2. He rolls 3 hits.
> Spectre (pursuing): NWO driver at Dexterity ●●●● + Drive ●●●● = 8 dice, Speed 5. They roll 5 hits. Speed advantage over Elias: +3 automatic advantage.
> 
> The Spectre closes to Close range of the Sedan in Round 1. The MAT-V falls behind (Speed 2 = same as Elias but driver rolls worse).
> 
> Elias's passenger (Yara, Mind ●●●): At her Phase, she attempts a Mind ●●● confusion blast at the Spectre driver. Casting from a moving vehicle: Complication from Speed 2 vehicle (Complication +2). Roll: Arete ●●●● (4 dice) + Mind bonus (1 die) vs. Difficulty 2 + 2 Complication = Difficulty 4. She rolls 3 hits vs. Difficulty 4 — she does not meet Difficulty. The spell fails.
> 
> Round 2:
> The Spectre is now at Close range. The NWO shooter in the passenger seat fires at the Sedan.
> Attack roll: Dexterity ●●●● + Firearms ●●●● = 8 dice vs. Difficulty 1. Moving Target (–1 die on the attack): 7 dice. Rolls 4 hits. Ballistic Damage, no armor on Sedan.
> Sedan takes 4 injury (down to –1 on a 3-box vehicle — effectively disabled or barely running).
> 
> Elias: Forces ●●● Rote. SF = highest Sphere dot − 1 = Forces ●●● − 1 = SF 2.
> The Spectre has SF 5 (Speed advantage, already at Close range, acting this Phase).
> Elias activates his held Phase die (he saved one from Round 1).
> 
> Forces ●●● Rote — Elias channels a focused electromagnetic pulse through the Sedan's chassis at the Spectre's engine block. Coincidental in context (car electrical surge near damaged vehicle). Roll: Arete ●●● (3 dice). Hits needed: Difficulty 2 (Coincidental). Rolls 3 hits. Exceeds Difficulty by 1. Effect: Spectre's engine is disrupted. It must make a Difficulty 2 Drive roll to maintain control.
> 
> Spectre driver: Dexterity ●●●● + Drive ●●●● − Difficulty 2 = 6 dice vs. Difficulty 2. Rolls 1 hit. Barely maintains.
> But the Spectre loses momentum — its Speed Advantage advantage drops to +1 this round.
> 
> Round 3:
> Elias's Sedan is critically damaged (barely moving). He cannot outrun the Spectre on speed.
> Instead: Elias uses Forces ●●● Rote — SF 2 — to disrupt the Spectre's fuel system. Vulgar without witnesses (no Sleepers on this panhandle road). Roll: Arete ●●● vs. Difficulty 3 (Vulgar, no witnesses). Rolls 4 hits. 1 Paradox die (minimal — no witnesses, but Vulgar).
> 
> Spectre engine dies. The NWO pursuit vehicle coasts to a halt.
> 
> MAT-V is still a round behind at Short range. Elias's Sedan limps to a crossroads and turns off. The MAT-V driver must choose: pursue a barely-running Sedan they can probably catch, or stop to pick up their stranded colleagues.
> 
> They stop to pick up the Spectre team.
> 
> Elias escapes. Sedan is totaled. He has 1 Paradox die. The grimoire is safe.
> 
> Analysis: The chase worked because Elias used his Sphere advantage tactically — he could not outrun the Spectre, so he killed it instead. The Sedan being critically damaged was acceptable because the objective was the grimoire, not the car.

> EXAMPLE — MOBILE CHANTRY OPERATIONS (VAN IN THE FIELD)
> 
> The Setup:
> A cabal of four mages is running a field operation from a panel van they have enchanted with Expanded Interior (Correspondence ●●●●). Outside: a normal-looking white panel van parked in a commercial district. Inside: a comfortable ritual space with a research library, a medical bay, and workspace for four.
> 
> The van carries:
>   Size 2 vehicle (base stats), Enchanted (Correspondence ●●●● interior expansion), Cargo (vastly expanded), Technological (communications and surveillance), Anti-Theft (biometric locks).
> 
> In play:
>   Two cabal members are inside the van performing a ritual (Time ●●● working to trace an Entropy-coded message). Two are outside on foot surveillance.
> 
>   A Sleeper investigator begins nosing around the van after 20 minutes. He tries the back door. Anti-Theft: Moderate Complication. He cannot open it without triggering the alarm (which the cabal silences remotely via the ES-Phone app they have installed). He looks in the front window: ordinary van interior (the Expanded Interior is behind a partition that looks like a cargo van's standard equipment barrier).
> 
>   He notes the license plate and walks away. This is not over.
> 
>   Inside: the ritual is complete. The cabal packs up and the van drives itself (Self-Driving Enchantment, active) to the next position before the investigator can return with backup.
> 
> Analysis: The van as mobile Chantry works because the enchantments are layered to maintain coincidental appearance at every level. The one genuine Vulgar element — the Expanded Interior — is never directly witnessed by the Sleeper. Mages who enchant vehicles know to keep the outside mundane and the inside impossible.

> VEHICLE COMBAT EXAMPLE — WAREHOUSE DISTRICT CHASE
> 
> SETUP:
> Sasha is riding a reinforced motorcycle (Size 1, Speed 3, Handling 0, Injury 4 [modified +2], Tags: Maneuverable, Fast, Concealable). Two NWO agents pursue in a black pursuit van (Size 3, Speed 2, Handling 0, Injury 6, Tags: Armored 2, Heavy, Cargo). The van is slower but massive — a direct ram from it would be devastating.
> 
> PHASE INITIATIVE — ROUND 1:
> Sasha (Drive ●●●, Dexterity ●●●●): Initiative pool = 7 dice → rolls [9, 8, 7, 4, 3, 1, 10].
>   The 10 is a Trick Initiative die — Sasha places it at Phase 2, acting before the agents.
>   Action dice: Phases 1, 2(Trick), 3, 4, 7, 8, 9.
> NWO Driver (Drive ●●●, Dexterity ●●●): Initiative pool = 6 dice → rolls [8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 10].
>   The agents also roll a Trick die, placed at Phase 3.
>   Action dice: Phases 3(Trick), 4, 5, 6, 7, 8.
> 
> PHASE 2 — SASHA ACTS FIRST (TRICK DIE):
> Sasha uses his early action to thread the motorcycle through warehouse shelving — making himself a difficult target for the van.
> Action: Evasive Maneuver. Pool = Dexterity ●●●● (4) + Drive ●●● (3) = 7 dice. Difficulty 2.
> Roll: [10, 9, 8, 6, 5, 4, 2] → 5 hits, 3 net hits above Difficulty.
> Result: +3 free hits to defensive vehicle rolls this round. Sasha spends 1 AD (Trick die used).
> 
> PHASE 3 — AGENTS ACT (TRICK DIE):
> The pursuit van accelerates and attempts a ram. At Speed 2 vs. Sasha's Speed 3, the van has no Speed Advantage — Sasha is faster in a straight line. But the warehouse corridor is narrow.
> Action: Ram. Pool = Dexterity ●●● (3) + Drive ●●● (3) = 6 dice. Difficulty 3 (Sasha has evasive position).
> Roll: [9, 8, 7, 5, 4, 3] → 4 hits, 1 net hit above Difficulty.
> Ram damage = Attacker Size + Speed Advantage = 3 + 0 = 3 damage to the motorcycle.
> But Sasha's Evasive Maneuver granted +3 dice to vehicle defense rolls this round.
> Defensive Drive (Sasha): Pool = Dexterity 4 + Drive 3 + 3 (Evasive bonus) = 10 dice. Difficulty 3.
> Roll: [9, 8, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1] → 6 hits, 3 net. Reduces incoming damage by 3 hits = 3 absorbed.
> Net ram damage: 3 - 3 = 0. Sasha threads through just in time. The van clips a shelving unit instead.
> 
> PHASE 5 — PASSENGER SHOOTS FROM THE VAN:
> The NWO passenger leans out and fires a pistol at Sasha while in motion.
> Action: Pistol shot from moving vehicle. Pool = Firearms ●●● (3) + Dexterity ●●● (3) − 1 (moving vehicle penalty) = 5 dice. Difficulty 3 (Sasha is moving fast, evading).
> Roll: [9, 8, 7, 5, 3] → 3 hits, 0 net hits above Difficulty 3. The attack exactly meets Difficulty — minimal effect.
> Damage: 0 free hits + pistol base 2 = 2 damage. Motorcycle Injury: 4/4 → 2/4 remaining.
> The motorcycle is hit but functional. Sasha feels the impact.
> 
> PHASE 7 — SASHA RETURNS FIRE:
> Sasha draws a pistol one-handed and fires back over his shoulder.
> Action: Pistol from moving motorcycle (one-handed). Pool = Ranged Combat ●●● (3) + Dexterity ●●●● (4) − 2 (moving + one-handed) = 5 dice. Difficulty 3.
> Roll: [10, 9, 8, 6, 2] → 4 hits (the 10 counts as 2), 1 net hit above Difficulty.
> Damage: 1 free hit + pistol base 2 = 3 Lethal to the NWO driver.
> Driver takes 3 Lethal → enters the IMPAIRED band (−1 die). The van swerves.
> 
> PHASE 8 — VAN ATTEMPTS SECOND RAM:
> The driver, now Impaired (−1 die), pushes the van hard for another ram attempt.
> Action: Ram. Pool = Dexterity 3 + Drive 3 − 1 (Impaired) = 5 dice. Difficulty 3.
> Roll: [8, 7, 4, 3, 1] → 2 hits. Misses Difficulty 3. Failure — the van overshoots.
> The van skids wide. Sasha uses his faster Speed to create distance, exiting the warehouse through a side door.
> 
> END OF ROUND SUMMARY:
>   Sasha's motorcycle: 2/4 injury remaining (damaged but mobile).
>   NWO van: undamaged vehicle; driver in IMPAIRED band (−1 die for remaining combat).
>   Sasha: no personal damage taken (motorcycle absorbed all hits).
>   Sasha escapes into the street. The van cannot follow at speed without risking a collision.
> 
> MECHANICAL NOTES:
>   Ram damage = Attacker Size (not attacker size minus target size). Speed Advantage only adds when attacker is faster.
>   Defensive Driving can reduce ram damage like a dodge — roll Dexterity + Drive vs. the ram's Difficulty.
>   Vehicle injury is separate from character health. Riders only take personal damage from crashes and spills (see Vehicle Events table).
>   Shooting from a moving vehicle: −1 die penalty. One-handed shooting adds another −1 die. Stack modifiers sensibly.
>   Evasive Maneuver free hits carry forward to defensive rolls that round — they represent built-up positional advantage.

## Vehicle Quick Reference

| Vehicle | Size  \|  Speed  \|  Handling  \|  Injury  \|  Notable Tags |
| --- | --- |
| Motorcycle (standard) | 1  \|  3  \|  0  \|  2  \|  Maneuverable, Fast |
| Motorcycle (high-perf.) | 1  \|  4  \|  –1  \|  2  \|  Maneuverable, Fast, Fast |
| Sedan (standard) | 2  \|  2  \|  0  \|  3  \|  (base only, unremarkable) |
| Sports Car | 2  \|  3  \|  –1  \|  3  \|  Fast, Maneuverable |
| SUV / Pickup | 2  \|  2  \|  +1  \|  3  \|  All-terrain, Cargo |
| Panel Van / Cargo Van | 2  \|  2  \|  +1  \|  3  \|  Cargo (large) |
| Helicopter (light) | 2  \|  3  \|  0  \|  3  \|  Maneuverable, Hover |
| Fixed-Wing Aircraft | 2  \|  3  \|  +1  \|  3  \|  Fast (overland), Long Range |
| Commercial Jet | 3  \|  4  \|  +2  \|  4  \|  Cargo, Long Range. Crew Required |
| Speedboat | 2  \|  3  \|  0  \|  3  \|  Fast, Maneuverable |
| Yacht (private) | 3  \|  2  \|  +1  \|  4  \|  Cargo, Long Range, Self-Sustaining |
| Submarine (small) | 3  \|  2  \|  0  \|  4  \|  Submersible, Stealthy |
| SMC Micon Katana | 1  \|  4  \|  –2  \|  2  \|  Fast, Maneuverable, Stealthy, Anti-Theft |
| SMC MAT-V Supervan | 3  \|  2  \|  0  \|  6  \|  Armor (3), Cargo, Authoritative, Anti-Theft |
| SMC Spectre Mark IV | 2  \|  5  \|  –2  \|  3  \|  Fast (×2), Maneuverable, Stealthy |
| SMC SoW 18-Wheeler | 4  \|  2  \|  +3  \|  8  \|  Armor (2), Cargo, Huge, Reinforced. Crew Required |

```
VEHICLE COMBAT QUICK REFERENCE
------------------------------
VEHICLE ACTIONS (Phase Initiative):
  Driver acts at their Phase; passengers act at their own Phase.
  Speed Advantage adds to all distance-related rolls.

SHOOTING FROM MOVING VEHICLE:
  Complication = vehicle Speed. Buy off with hits or brace.
  Magic that does not require physical aim: no Complication from vehicle movement.

ATTACKING A MOVING VEHICLE:
  Moving Target: –1 die to all attacks against a vehicle that moved this round.
  Size 3+ vehicles in straight-line pursuit: no Moving Target penalty.

RAMMING:
  Attacker: Dexterity + Drive vs. Difficulty 2 (evasion) or target's roll.
  Damage to target = attacker's Size.
  Damage to attacker = half target's Size (round up).
  Full speed from behind: add Speed Advantage to damage.
  Pit maneuver: 1 damage, spin-out Complication instead.

CHASE SEQUENCE:
  Both sides roll Speed Advantage + Drive each round.
  Winner adjusts range by 1 band toward their goal.
  Pursuer wins at Close range. Quarry wins by reaching Long and holding 1 round.
  Speed difference = automatic Complication on slower vehicle's roll.

VEHICLE INJURY:
  Injury = Size + 1 boxes (base). Damaged by successful attacks on vehicle.
  At 0: disabled. Crash damage at Storyguide discretion based on speed.
  Enchanted self-repairing vehicles: restore injury as specified by enchantment.
```

# Appendix U: The Nephandi — Descent, the Caul, and the Fallen

*Source material: M20 Book of the Fallen*

The Nephandi are not simply mages who chose evil. That framing is too small, too tidy, too comfortable. The Fallen are mages whose inner spiritual core — their Avatar — has been inverted through a harrowing rite called the Caul, orienting them not toward Ascension but toward its opposite: the dissolution of self, the corruption of others, and ultimately the extinction of existence itself. They are broken vessels containing Qlippothic energy in human form, and they have, in most cases, chosen to be exactly that.

This appendix covers the mechanics and frameworks Storyguides need to portray the Nephandi as genuine threats and, if the chronicle calls for it, as playable antagonists-turned-protagonists. It covers the three paths by which mages Fall, the Descent track that measures how far a Nephandus has gone, the mechanical perks and deformities the Caul leaves behind, the paradigms that justify the Fallen worldview, the practices through which their magic flows, and a selection of named mortal servants who do the Nephandi's dirty work in the daylight world. Named Nephandic antagonists with full stat blocks appear in Appendix D: Entities and Antagonists.

## The Three Paths of Falling

Nephandi do not all arrive at the Caul by the same road. The Book of the Fallen identifies three distinct categories of Fallen mages, each shaped by the circumstances of their corruption. Mechanically, all three function identically once initiated — same casting system, same Sphere Skills, same Enhancement mechanics, same relationship with Paradox (though that relationship is complicated, as detailed below). The distinction matters for roleplay, backstory, and the Storyguide's understanding of what drives a particular Nephandus.

### Barabbi: The Worst Turncoats

A barabbi (singular: barabbus) is a mage who once belonged to another faction — Tradition, Technocracy, Disparate, or independent — and who chose to abandon that allegiance for the Fallen Path. The word itself carries contempt from both sides: Council mages and Technocrats use it as an epithet for betrayers, while even some Nephandi regard the barabbi with a certain wariness, knowing that someone who turned once can turn again.

The motivations are as varied as the people: perceived betrayal by the old group, a need for extremity that the original faction could not satisfy, a crisis of faith that resolved in the worst possible direction, abuse within the group that the Fallen were happy to weaponize, or simply the slow accumulation of compromises until the barabbus realized there was nothing left worth preserving. In many cases, the original group didn't meet her needs — she felt shoved aside, denied credit, blamed unjustly, or simply starved of the extreme focus she craved. What drew her to the original life was deepened, twisted, and intensified by her Nightside conversion.

A barabbus often remains inside her original group, poisoning it from within. This is arguably more dangerous than an openly Fallen mage. A barabbi Tradition mage has access to the Chantry, knows the cabal's resources and weaknesses, and can engineer crises that look like accidents. A barabbi Technocrat can redirect Union resources, falsify reports, and arrange for colleagues to suffer accidents in the field. The Fallen prize barabbi operatives for exactly this reason.

Mechanically, a barabbi retains all Sphere Skills and Backgrounds from her previous life. She does not lose her rotes or her Chantry affiliation immediately; she loses them when she is exposed or when she chooses to reveal herself. Her Resonance tag shifts over time toward Qlippothic descriptors: Cold, Consuming, Inverted, Entropic. This shift is gradual but detectable by mages with Prime 2 or Awareness 3+, which is part of why deep cover barabbi work so hard to maintain their Shroud the Soul concealment.

> Example — Barabbi in the Chronicle

### Widderslainte: The Caul-Born

The word widderslainte comes from a twisted perversion of "proper health" in Gaelic. These are the mages who came out of the womb already disposed to Fall. In the cosmology of Mage, reincarnation is a given. Most souls eventually return to the Great Wheel of birth, death, and rebirth. Nephandic Avatars which reincarnate as widderslainte might be the ghost-like consciousnesses of Nephandi who escaped from their hells and returned to mortal life as new human beings — or they could be alien consciousnesses, demonic urge-spirits, broken Avatar shards, or other scraps of metaphysical debris.

Whatever their origins, these troublesome souls spend enormous energy trying to drive their new incarnation back into the Nephandic fold. A widderslainte child may display disturbing tendencies — not evil by adult reckoning, since children lack the development to be abusers in the meaningful sense, but deeply unsettling. As she grows, the inner pull intensifies. She may Awaken "normally" and then be guided toward the Caul by what feels like her own instincts but is in fact the will of a pre-existing Fallen Avatar pushing through her choices.

Mechanically, a widderslainte has a Caul-darkened Avatar from the moment of Awakening. She does not need to seek out a Nephandus or undergo the Caul as an external rite; her passage is internal, accomplished through a series of dark Seekings that the Fallen Avatar engineers. These Seekings look like nightmares made real: the widderslainte faces the worst thing she has done or could do, and the Avatar demands she embrace it rather than transcend it. Each successful dark Seeking advances her along the Descent track (see below).

Unlike barabbi, widderslainte have no previous loyalty to discard and no habits from a "good" life to unlearn. Unlike Caul-Born (below), they sometimes genuinely struggle against their nature — which makes the best widderslainte story one of sympathetic tragedy rather than villainy. A widderslainte who resists her Avatar is a mage in constant internal warfare, burning resources every session to hold the thing inside her at bay. This can be a compelling player character arc if the chronicle supports it. The Storyguide should consult with the player beforehand and establish at what point, if ever, the character might stop resisting — and what that means for everyone else at the table.

### Caul-Born: Pure by Design

Orphan mages — those who Awaken without factional affiliation — have the greatest statistical tendency to Fall. Sometimes called the Caul-Born, these are people who either Awaken directly through the Cauls themselves or Fall into the Cauls very shortly after Awakening. The Traditions and Technocracy race to claim unaffiliated Awakened partly to prevent exactly this outcome.

"Caul-Born" is not a phrase you will hear from Council mages or Technocrats. Among the Fallen, those who Awakened on the Nephandic Path from the beginning are considered pure in their devotions — or at least as pure as the Fallen get. Unlike barabbi, they have no previous loyalty to discard. Unlike widderslainte, they do not grow up conflicted about who and what they are. Caul-Born Nephandi are who they are because they want to be that way. Their Arts are Black Arts by design, not modification, and their beliefs are held sincerely. Thus, Caul-Born mages maintain bone-deep loyalty to their Fallen cause. With very few exceptions, they hold very few regrets.

Mechanically, Caul-Born Nephandi begin play with Qlippothic Resonance already present in their aura (see Merits and Flaws below). They cannot claim the Innocuous Aura merit without substantial in-chronicle work, because their passage through the dark was their entry into existence as a mage. They compensate with genuine fanaticism: Caul-Born receive a free reroll on any Resolve + Composure check to resist coercion, seduction away from the Fallen Path, or social engineering that would compromise their Nephandic identity. They chose this. They are not going back.

## The Descent Track

Where a Traditions mage has a Path — the journey toward Ascension — a Nephandus has the Descent: the inverse journey that ends not in transcendence but in perfect union with the Void or (for the most ambitious) the consumption of it. The Descent Track is an optional subsystem for chronicles that feature Nephandi as ongoing characters, whether as primary antagonists with recurring arcs or as player-controlled fallen mages.

The Descent Track has five stages. Each stage represents how deeply a Nephandus has invested in Qlippothic existence, how much of her humanity has been traded away, and how much raw Abyssal power she has access to. Advancing the track requires completing dark Seekings, which function like standard Seekings but mirror them in their demands: where a Seeking challenges a mage to transcend a limitation, a dark Seeking demands that she embrace one.

```
The Descent Track — Five Stages
-------------------------------
STAGE ONE — Novitiate: The Caul has been survived. The mage knows what she is. Her Resonance has begun to darken. She gains access to Nephandic Merits (see below). She is, from the perspective of other mages, a dangerous unknown.

STAGE TWO — Initiate: She has committed her first major atrocity in service to the Fallen cause and survived the consequences. Her Resonance is now unmistakably Qlippothic to any mage with Prime 2+ who reads her aura without concealment. She gains one additional Sphere Trick that functions only within the Qlippothic resonance of her practice.

STAGE THREE — Corrupted: She has completed a dark Seeking and returned from the Qlippothic Realms. She may now enter the Qlippoth voluntarily using Spirit 3+, though the journey is never safe. Her humanity rating (tracked separately at the Storyguide's discretion) has declined visibly. Mortals who cannot name their unease with her nevertheless feel it.

STAGE FOUR — Condemned: She has attained what the Fallen call Qlippothic Mastery — the ability to draw on Abyssal energies directly as a supplemental power source. Once per session, she may reduce the SF of a vulgar working by 2 by tapping this reservoir. The Storyguide decides what this looks like: spontaneous physical corruption, weeping darkness from the casting mage's hands, an absence of light that witnesses feel in their teeth.

STAGE FIVE — Leviathan's Threshold: The Nephandus stands at the edge of the Void. She has done things that cannot be undone. At this stage, she may attempt the Ritual of Leviathan — a catastrophic working that either achieves Assumption (absorption into the Void, removing her from play permanently in a dark apotheosis) or Consummation (she devours the Leviathan essence and becomes something no longer fully human: a Dark Master in embryo, an NPC in the Storyguide's hands).
```

Redemption is theoretically possible at Stages One through Three. The mage must repudiate the Fallen Path through a genuine Seeking — not a dark one, but a luminous one — that costs her one Background dot permanently for each Stage she wishes to retreat. The process is brutal, often traumatic, and frequently fatal. The Qlippothic Avatar does not release its grip gently. Redemption at Stage Four requires a Seeking that the Storyguide should treat as a major chronicle climax. Redemption at Stage Five is, by most accounts, not possible. The Fallen say this smugly. They are probably right.

> Example — Using the Descent Track

## Nephandic Merits and Flaws

The following Merits and Flaws are available only to Nephandic characters who have gone through the Caul, and they arise from that passage. Point totals are listed for reference, but for Storyguide-controlled Nephandi, dramatic appropriateness matters more than balance.

### Shadow Appeal — 3-point Supernatural Merit

Every human being has a repressed shadow — a collection of things about themselves they refuse to acknowledge, either because those things feel too awful or too good to admit. Mages who have traveled through the Qlippothic depths develop a frightening degree of insight into these shadows, and a fiendish gift for exploiting them.

Mechanically: This Merit subtracts 2 from the Difficulty of any Social roll the Nephandus makes when exploiting someone's shadow aspect — the egotist's need for validation, the lonely person's hunger for connection, the guilty person's terror of exposure. This deduction can lower Difficulty beyond the standard -3 modifier limit, giving the Fallen an inhuman edge. The Merit does not work on people who have no shadow (extremely unlikely) or on mages who have undergone successful Seeking against their shadow self.

### Innocuous Aura — 5-point Supernatural Merit

Most Nephandi have been visibly marked by their Caul passage. This mage has somehow escaped such deformations of the soul. Her aura shows no flickers of Qlippothic essence, making her an exceptional infiltrator and double agent. She can move in Tradition Chantries, Technocracy Constructs, and ordinary social environments without triggering the instinctive unease most Fallen provoke. Aura readings at Prime 2 reveal a normal — if perhaps unusually intense — mage aura. Only Prime 4+ reads the concealed Qlippothic core.

### Abyssal Mastery — 7-point Supernatural Merit

The Fallen mage with this Merit has aligned herself so deeply with the Void that its rigors hold no threat for her. She can endure the cold vacuum of deep space and the Deep Umbra without life support, food, water, or breathable atmosphere. No matter how long she spends in the darkest Umbral reaches, she retains her mortal form upon return. Additionally, she may use Spirit 5 to escape directly into the Qlippoth — a feat most Masters cannot successfully attempt — reaching any Qlipha she has previously attained.

### Saint of the Pit — 7-point Supernatural Merit

She has gone where few Nephandi have gone and come back speaking of it. Her reputation among infernal entities is such that all but the most powerful Goetic powers and dark totems defer to her commands. Rules-wise, this Merit lowers the Difficulty of all Social rolls by 3 whenever the Nephandus wants to compel obedience, drive a bargain, demand a favor, or frighten an infernal entity. The greatest arch-demons and Qlippothic godheads remain immune, though they treat her with respect. In the mortal world, any character with three or more dots in Occult, Lore (Nephandi), or relevant Esoterica has heard of this mage. What they do with that knowledge is their problem.

### Qlippothic Radiance — 1 to 5-point Supernatural Flaw

The Shells have left a discernible mark that this character cannot conceal. Anyone who understands Qlippothic energies detects the marks of passage on her aura automatically. Other characters feel distinctly uncomfortable in her presence even without being able to name why. The severity scales with how far into the Qlippoth she has traveled: 1-point means sensitive mages notice, 3-point means everyone with Awareness 2+ notices, 5-point means mundane mortals feel sick to their stomachs in her presence and animals flee without prompting.

### Abyssal Lunatic — 3-point Supernatural Flaw

The Caul passage cracked something in this mage that never fully healed. She is not merely strange — she is fractured in ways that occasionally override her own control. Once per session, the Storyguide may trigger a Sanity check (see Appendix K) against a Difficulty equal to her current Descent Stage + 2. On a failure, she acts on Qlippothic instinct rather than conscious choice for a number of rounds equal to the margin of failure. She does not become catatonic; she becomes something worse: utterly, genuinely herself, stripped of any civilized veneer she maintains for operational purposes.

### Infernal Investments — Variable Merit (Flaw Bundled)

The Nephandus has bound herself to an infernal patron — a Goetic demon, a Qlippothic entity, or a representative of Those Who Dwell Behind the Stars. This relationship grants her access to Investment Traits (see below) but comes with mandatory Demonic Investment Flaws that reflect the cost of the relationship.

Optional Rule: Infernal Pacts. A Storyguide who wants to make Infernal Investments a concrete mechanical system can treat them as a structured pact with specific terms. The demon provides Investment Traits worth up to (its Might score × 2) points. In return, the Nephandus owes Service, Moth into Flame, or Hellfire — one Flaw per two Investment Traits received. Service means the demon can call on the Nephandus for specific tasks at any time, and refusing costs a Willpower point and adds a Paradox die. Moth into Flame means the Nephandus is compelled to investigate anything that looks like a path to greater power, even obvious traps. Hellfire means she has internalized some of the infernal resonance: when she botches a vulgar working, one Paradox die is replaced by a Hellfire die that deals unsoakable Aggravated damage to the nearest sentient being — which may be her.

```
Sample Investment Traits (Merit side)
-------------------------------------
Luciferian Charm (+3): Difficulty on all resistance rolls against this mage's social influence is increased by 2 for anyone who would find her physically attractive.
Occult Awareness (+2): She gains a free reroll on any Perception + Awareness roll involving supernatural phenomena.
Infernal Key (+4): Once per session she may open any lock, electronic or mechanical, by spending a Willpower point. No roll required.
Regeneration (+5): She heals one Health band of Bashing or Lethal damage per round without spending Quintessence.
Malevolent Focus (+4): Her combat spells deal +1 damage die that bypasses armor. This bonus applies only to spells cast with explicitly harmful intent.
```

## Nephandic Paradigms

Like all mages, Nephandi justify their existence through paradigmatic frameworks. These are not merely rationalizations — they are internally consistent worldviews that, from the inside, feel like the truth. When a Storyguide portrays a Nephandus, she should know which paradigm drives this particular mage, and how that mage would explain her choices to someone she was trying to recruit. The following paradigms are common among the Fallen. Each can serve as a Tag (Invoke and Compel) for Nephandic characters in the SPU system.

```
All Power Comes From Sin
------------------------
Invoke: When using magic explicitly framed as forbidden, transgressive, or explicitly evil, reduce Difficulty by 1. The mage leans into the devil's compact without hesitation.
Compel: When presented with a "good" or "virtuous" solution to a problem, the mage must at minimum question it, and ideally cannot participate without feeling her power drain. She has committed to the sinful path; walking the straight road costs a Willpower point per scene.

Description: Magic is evil. This mage knows it and accepts it. Other mages who claim righteousness while working magic are hypocrites. This paradigm supports ritual magic, blood sacrifice, Goetic invocation, and anything that the cultural mainstream would recognize as "dark occultism." The mage employs it without apology.
```

```
Cosmic Horror is the Only Truth
-------------------------------
Invoke: When dealing with entities from the Deep Umbra, Qlippothic Realms, or Those Who Dwell Behind the Stars, reduce Difficulty by 1. This mage understands what lies beyond, and that understanding is practical.
Compel: She cannot genuinely comfort someone about cosmic meaning or mortality. She believes the universe is hostile indifference made conscious, and this bleeds through in any prolonged human interaction. Social rolls to establish trust over multiple sessions suffer +1 Difficulty as people sense the abyss behind her eyes.

Description: The universe is vast, ancient, indifferent, and actively hostile to human consciousness. Not metaphorically hostile — literally so. The things in the dark do not hate us; hate requires acknowledging us, and they do not. This mage has seen enough of the Qlippothic Realms to know this is true. She works with the darkness not because it is good but because it is real, and the only power worth having is aligned with what is real.
```

```
We Are Stormtroopers of the Abyss
---------------------------------
Invoke: When acting on direct orders from a Nephandic superior or in service to an explicit Fallen agenda, reduce Difficulty by 1. The mission matters; personal hesitation does not.
Compel: Without a chain of command, this mage struggles. In leaderless situations, she defaults to the most extreme available action rather than the most effective one. She is a weapon, not a strategist.

Description: She is not an individual pursuing personal enlightenment. She is a soldier in a war that predates humanity and will outlast it. Her purpose is the advancement of the Fallen agenda, the expansion of Qlippothic territory in the Consensus, and the weakening of the Traditions and Technocracy. She is disciplined, efficient, and completely without mercy. This is perhaps the most dangerous Nephandic paradigm because it is the most militarized.
```

```
People Are Shit
---------------
```
